text:
We analyze the transmitted flux in a sample of 17 QSOs spectra at
5.74<zem<6.42 to obtain tighter constraints on the volume-averaged neutral
hydrogen fraction, xHI, at z~6. We study separately the narrow transmission
windows (peaks) and the wide dark portions (gaps) in the observed absorption
spectra. By comparing the statistics of these spectral features with Lyalpha
forest simulations, we conclude that xHI evolves smoothly from 10^{-4.4} at
z=5.3 to 10^{-4.2} at z=5.6, with a robust upper limit xHI<0.36 at z=6.3. The
frequency and physical sizes of the peaks imply an origin in cosmic underdense
regions and/or in HII regions around faint quasars or galaxies. In one case
(the intervening HII region of the faint quasar RD J1148+5253 at z=5.70 along
the LOS of SDSS J1148+5251 at z=6.42) the increase of the peak spectral density
is explained by the first-ever detected transverse proximity effect in the HI
Lyalpha forest; this indicates that at least some peaks result from a locally
enhanced radiation field. We then obtain a strong lower limit on the foreground
QSO lifetime of tQ>11 Myr. The observed widths of the peaks are found to be
systematically larger than the simulated ones. Reasons for such discrepancy
might reside either in the photoionization equilibrium assumption or in
radiative transfer effects.

In this work, we investigate a massive, filamentary structure at redshift z=0.55 and the star formation histories of galaxies at lower redshifts. We employed the data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey to identify and study the filamentary structure, measuring its mass and luminosity through the use of spectral analysis tools. Additionally, we analyzed the star formation histories of galaxies at redshifts below z=1, using data from the Cosmic Assembly Near-infrared Deep Extragalactic Legacy Survey. We applied a Bayesian spectral fitting methodology to derive the star formation rates of these galaxies.

The motivation for this research stems from the desire to better understand the large-scale structure of the universe and the processes driving galaxy evolution. The filamentary structure we studied is of particular interest, as it is among the most massive known structures in the universe. Additionally, investigating the star formation histories of galaxies can provide insights into the mechanisms that drive the formation of new stars in galaxies.

The problem we aim to address in this work is to broaden our understanding of the physical processes that shape the formation and evolution of galaxies. By studying these massive structures and the star formation histories of galaxies, we can gain insights into the interactions between galaxies and their environment, the role of gas accretion in galaxy formation, and the influence of mergers and collisions on the evolution of galaxies. Overall, this work contributes to our knowledge about the formation and evolution of galaxies and the structure of the universe at large.

In this work, we consider the Interval Conjectures for level Hilbert functions, which aim to provide a way to analyze the levels of homogenization of a given ideal. The problem we tackle is to generalize the conjectures for arbitrary ideals, which have so far been only proven for monomial ideals. Our results show that the Interval Conjectures hold for all radical ideals, which is a significant extension of the previously known results, and provide a way to compute the levels of a given ideal in a simple and straightforward manner. 

To achieve these results, we use combinatorial and algebraic techniques, such as the theory of Gröbner bases and the combinatorial description of level Hilbert functions. We also introduce a new concept, called the associated graded module of an ideal, which allows us to simplify the computations and relate the Interval Conjectures to other important concepts in commutative algebra, such as the depths of an ideal. 

Our work contributes to the understanding of the levels of homogenization of ideals, which is a fundamental concept in algebraic geometry and computational algebra. Our results have potential applications in various fields, such as cryptography, coding theory, and optimization, where algebraic techniques are used to solve complex problems.

How do blogs cite and influence each other? How do such links evolve? Does
the popularity of old blog posts drop exponentially with time? These are some
of the questions that we address in this work. Our goal is to build a model
that generates realistic cascades, so that it can help us with link prediction
and outlier detection.
  Blogs (weblogs) have become an important medium of information because of
their timely publication, ease of use, and wide availability. In fact, they
often make headlines, by discussing and discovering evidence about political
events and facts. Often blogs link to one another, creating a publicly
available record of how information and influence spreads through an underlying
social network. Aggregating links from several blog posts creates a directed
graph which we analyze to discover the patterns of information propagation in
blogspace, and thereby understand the underlying social network. Not only are
blogs interesting on their own merit, but our analysis also sheds light on how
rumors, viruses, and ideas propagate over social and computer networks.
  Here we report some surprising findings of the blog linking and information
propagation structure, after we analyzed one of the largest available datasets,
with 45,000 blogs and ~ 2.2 million blog-postings. Our analysis also sheds
light on how rumors, viruses, and ideas propagate over social and computer
networks. We also present a simple model that mimics the spread of information
on the blogosphere, and produces information cascades very similar to those
found in real life.

Recently, the iterative approach named linear tabling has received
considerable attention because of its simplicity, ease of implementation, and
good space efficiency. Linear tabling is a framework from which different
methods can be derived based on the strategies used in handling looping
subgoals. One decision concerns when answers are consumed and returned. This
paper describes two strategies, namely, {\it lazy} and {\it eager} strategies,
and compares them both qualitatively and quantitatively. The results indicate
that, while the lazy strategy has good locality and is well suited for finding
all solutions, the eager strategy is comparable in speed with the lazy strategy
and is well suited for programs with cuts. Linear tabling relies on depth-first
iterative deepening rather than suspension to compute fixpoints. Each cluster
of inter-dependent subgoals as represented by a top-most looping subgoal is
iteratively evaluated until no subgoal in it can produce any new answers. Naive
re-evaluation of all looping subgoals, albeit simple, may be computationally
unacceptable. In this paper, we also introduce semi-naive optimization, an
effective technique employed in bottom-up evaluation of logic programs to avoid
redundant joins of answers, into linear tabling. We give the conditions for the
technique to be safe (i.e. sound and complete) and propose an optimization
technique called {\it early answer promotion} to enhance its effectiveness.
Benchmarking in B-Prolog demonstrates that with this optimization linear
tabling compares favorably well in speed with the state-of-the-art
implementation of SLG.

In this study, we present the results obtained from the VLT-FLAMES survey of massive stars in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). Our primary focus is on the wind properties and evolution of hot massive stars, where we explore the relationship between stellar mass, metallicity, and wind strength. The motivation for this research comes from the need to understand the impact of massive stars on their environment and how they contribute to the chemical enrichment of galaxies.

We analyzed a sample of over 800 massive stars in the LMC, covering a broad range of spectral types, luminosities, and masses. Using high-quality spectra obtained with the VLT-FLAMES spectrograph, we measured the wind parameters of each star, including the wind terminal velocity, mass-loss rate, and clumping factor. We then compared our findings with theoretical predictions and observational constraints from other galaxies.

Our key findings indicate that the strength of stellar winds in massive stars is strongly correlated with metallicity and luminosity. Moreover, we observed a clear dependence of the clumping factor on stellar X-ray emission, suggesting that X-rays might play a crucial role in the formation of wind density inhomogeneities. Our study provides new insights into the properties and evolution of massive stars and contributes to the broader field of galactic archaeology.

While most useful information theoretic inequalities can be deduced from the
basic properties of entropy or mutual information, up to now Shannon's entropy
power inequality (EPI) is an exception: Existing information theoretic proofs
of the EPI hinge on representations of differential entropy using either Fisher
information or minimum mean-square error (MMSE), which are derived from de
Bruijn's identity. In this paper, we first present an unified view of these
proofs, showing that they share two essential ingredients: 1) a data processing
argument applied to a covariance-preserving linear transformation; 2) an
integration over a path of a continuous Gaussian perturbation. Using these
ingredients, we develop a new and brief proof of the EPI through a mutual
information inequality, which replaces Stam and Blachman's Fisher information
inequality (FII) and an inequality for MMSE by Guo, Shamai and Verd\'u used in
earlier proofs. The result has the advantage of being very simple in that it
relies only on the basic properties of mutual information. These ideas are then
generalized to various extended versions of the EPI: Zamir and Feder's
generalized EPI for linear transformations of the random variables, Takano and
Johnson's EPI for dependent variables, Liu and Viswanath's
covariance-constrained EPI, and Costa's concavity inequality for the entropy
power.

We present a study on the relationship between galaxy morphologies and environment within the Abell 901/902 supercluster, using data from the COMBO-17 survey. Our sample consists of 363 galaxies with spectroscopic redshifts in the range 0.16 <z< 0.23. We classify the galaxies into four morphological types: early-type, bulge-dominated, disk-dominated and irregular, using the Sérsic profile index and the visual inspection of the images. We investigate the dependence of the fractions and distributions of these morphological types on environmental parameters, such as galaxy density, cluster-centric distance, and local galaxy surface density. We find that early-type galaxies are more prevalent in high-density regions and closer to the cluster center, whereas disk-dominated and irregular galaxies are more uniformly distributed throughout the supercluster. Our results suggest that the galaxy morphologies in the Abell 901/902 supercluster are strongly influenced by the environment, in agreement with current models of galaxy formation and evolution that emphasize the role of gravitational interactions and mergers in shaping galaxy properties. Our study provides a valuable contribution to the understanding of the complex interplay between galaxy morphologies and the large-scale structure of the universe.

Due to its relatively small distance (725 pc), the Cepheus A East
star-forming region is an ideal laboratory to study massive star formation
processes. Based on its morphology, it has been suggested that the flattened
molecular gas distribution around the YSO HW2 may be a 350-AU-radius massive
protostellar disk. Goal of our work is to ascertain the nature of this
structure. We have employed the Plateau de Bure Interferometer to acquire
(sub-)arcsecond-resolution imaging of high-density and shock tracers, such as
methyl cyanide (CH3CN) and silicon monoxide (SiO), towards the HW2 position. On
the 1-arcsecond (about 725 AU) scale, the flattened distribution of molecular
gas around HW2 appears to be due to the projected superposition, on the plane
of the sky, of at least three protostellar objects, of which at least one is
powering a molecular outflow at a small angle with respect to the line of
sight. The presence of a protostellar disk around HW2 is not ruled out, but
such structure is likely to be detected on a smaller spatial scale, or using
different molecular tracers.

A charge injection technique is applied to the X-ray CCD camera, XIS (X-ray
Imaging Spectrometer) onboard Suzaku. The charge transfer inefficiency (CTI) in
each CCD column (vertical transfer channel) is measured by the injection of
charge packets into a transfer channel and subsequent readout. This paper
reports the performances of the charge injection capability based on the ground
experiments using a radiation damaged device, and in-orbit measurements of the
XIS. The ground experiments show that charges are stably injected with the
dispersion of 91eV in FWHM in a specific column for the charges equivalent to
the X-ray energy of 5.1keV. This dispersion width is significantly smaller than
that of the X-ray events of 113eV (FWHM) at approximately the same energy. The
amount of charge loss during transfer in a specific column, which is measured
with the charge injection capability, is consistent with that measured with the
calibration source. These results indicate that the charge injection technique
can accurately measure column-dependent charge losses rather than the
calibration sources. The column-to-column CTI correction to the calibration
source spectra significantly reduces the line widths compared to those with a
column-averaged CTI correction (from 193eV to 173eV in FWHM on an average at
the time of one year after the launch). In addition, this method significantly
reduces the low energy tail in the line profile of the calibration source
spectrum.

In this study, we present a new measurement of the Hubble Constant using Cepheid parallaxes observed with the Hubble Space Telescope. Our motivation stems from the current tension between direct measurements of the Hubble Constant using Cepheid variables and the cosmic microwave background, which may imply the need for new physics beyond the standard cosmological model. We take advantage of the long baseline of Hubble observations to calibrate the period-luminosity relations of Cepheids in nearby galaxies using trigonometric parallaxes. From there, we extrapolate these relations to more distant galaxies, allowing us to determine accurate distances and thereby the Hubble Constant. 

Our final result of 73.3 ± 1.8 km/s/Mpc is in excellent agreement with other Cepheid-based measurements, but is slightly lower than that inferred from the cosmic microwave background. These results suggest that, regardless of the potential need for new physics, there is still important work to be done in understanding the systematic errors in cosmological measurements. 

Our methodology employs a Bayesian hierarchical modeling framework that accounts for systematic uncertainties, such as metallicity effects and the dependence of the period-luminosity relation on the wavelength range used for observations. We also incorporate a step-wise approach to outlier rejection, allowing for robust and consistent results. This new measurement highlights the continued potential of Cepheid-based distance measurements to improve our understanding of the universe.

In the set of 236 GRB afterglows observed by Swift between January 2005 and
March 2007, we identify 30 X-ray light-curves whose power-law fall-off exhibit
a steepening ("break") at 0.1-10 day after trigger, to a decay steeper than
t^{-1.5}. For most of these afterglows, the X-ray spectral slope and the decay
indices before and after the break can be accommodated by the standard jet
model although a different origin of the breaks cannot be ruled out. In
addition, there are 27 other afterglows whose X-ray light-curves may also
exhibit a late break to a steep decay, but the evidence is not that compelling.
  The X-ray emissions of 38 afterglows decay slower than t^{-1.5} until after 3
day, half of them exhibiting such a slow decay until after 10 day.
  Therefore, the fraction of well-monitored Swift afterglows with potential
jet-breaks is around 60 percent, whether we count only the strongest cases for
each type or all of them. This fraction is comparable to the 75 percent of
pre-Swift afterglows whose optical light-curves displayed similar breaks at ~1
day.
  The properties of the prompt emission of Swift afterglows with light-curve
breaks show the same correlations (peak energy of GRB spectrum with the burst
isotropic output and with burst collimated output) as previously found for
pre-Swift optical afterglows with light-curve breaks (the Amati and Ghirlanda
relations, respectively). However, we find that Ghirlanda relation is largely a
consequence of Amati's and that the use of the jet-break time leads to a
stronger Ghirlanda correlation only when the few outliers to the Amati relation
are included.

In our work titled "Magnetic Fingerprints of sub-100 nm Fe Nanodots", we aim to investigate the magnetic properties of nanoscale iron (Fe) dots. The problem lies in understanding how the magnetic properties of Fe nanodots change as their size decreases to below 100 nm. Such small sizes are of interest in the field of spintronics, where nanodots are used in spin valves and magnetic storage media. Our work attempts to solve this problem by investigating the magnetic behavior of sub-100 nm Fe nanodots using magnetometry and microscopy techniques.

Key ideas in our methodology include the use of electron beam lithography, sputtering, and annealing to fabricate sub-100 nm Fe nanodots. We use magnetic force microscopy (MFM) to image the magnetic domain structure of the nanodots and measure their magnetic moment using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM). By correlating the magnetic moments of the nanodots with their size and shape, we aim to determine the characteristic magnetic fingerprint of Fe nanodots.

Our results show that the magnetic moments of sub-100 nm Fe nanodots are significantly different from their bulk counterparts due to the presence of magnetic domains. The magnetization curves of the nanodots exhibit a single domain state for sizes below 100 nm. We also observed an increase in coercivity with decreasing size, indicating that the magnetic properties of Fe nanodots are significantly influenced by their size and shape. Our study contributes to advancing the understanding of magnetic nanoparticles and their potential for future applications in spintronics and magnetic data storage.

We consider the Kalman - Yakubovich - Popov (KYP) inequality \[
\begin{pmatrix} X-A^* XA-C^*C & -A^*X B- C^*D\cr -B^*X A-D^* C & I- B^*X B-D^*D
\end{pmatrix} \ge 0 \] for contractive operator matrices $ \begin{pmatrix}
A&B\cr C &D \end{pmatrix}:\begin{pmatrix}\mathfrak{H}\cr\mathfrak{M}
\end{pmatrix}\to\begin{pmatrix}\mathfrak{H}\cr\mathfrak{N} \end{pmatrix}, $
where $\mathfrak{H},$ $\mathfrak{M}$, and $\mathfrak{N}$ are separable Hilbert
spaces. We restrict ourselves to the solutions $X$ from the operator interval
$[0, I_\mathfrak{H}]$. Several equivalent forms of KYP are obtained. Using the
parametrization of the blocks of contractive operator matrices, the Kre\u{\i}n
shorted operator, and the M\"obius representation of the Schur class
operator-valued function we find several equivalent forms of the KYP
inequality. Properties of solutions are established and it is proved that the
minimal solution of the KYP inequality satisfies the corresponding algebraic
Riccati equation and can be obtained by the iterative procedure with the
special choice of the initial point. In terms of the Kre\u{\i}n shorted
operators a necessary condition and some sufficient conditions for uniqueness
of the solution are established.

In this study, we investigate the mechanism of high-harmonic generation (HHG) in diatomic molecules using a quantum-orbit analysis of the interference patterns. The motivation behind this research stems from the fact that HHG has become an important source of ultrafast light pulses for a wide range of applications. However, understanding the mechanisms of HHG in molecules remains a significant challenge for both theoretical and experimental studies. 

To address this challenge, we have developed a methodology that combines quantum-orbit analysis with molecular orbital theory to investigate the interference patterns of high-order harmonics in diatomic molecules. Our analysis reveals that the interference patterns are highly sensitive to the internuclear distance of the molecule, which in turn determines the degree of constructive and destructive interference between the HHG emissions from each atom in the molecule. 

Our results demonstrate that the quantum-mechanical interference effects play a crucial role in the HHG process in diatomic molecules. Our study provides insights into the HHG mechanism that can be useful for developing novel light sources for high-resolution imaging and spectroscopy.

In our research, we investigate the sensitivity of solar off-limb line profiles to electron density stratification and the velocity distribution anisotropy. The motivation behind our work is to understand the influence of the solar atmosphere's physical properties on spectral line profiles. To achieve this, we employed a novel methodology that combines numerical simulations and observational data. 

First, we simulated the solar atmosphere using the radiative transfer code RH, implementing a 1D model with 16 layers. Next, we constructed line profiles using the NEMO code, which accounts for the solar limb darkening and instrumental broadening effects. We then compared the line profiles from our simulations to those observed with the Interface Region Imaging Spectrograph (IRIS). We varied the electron density stratification and velocity distribution anisotropy parameters, and we observed how they impact the line profiles.

Our work addresses a crucial problem in solar physics: the need to understand the mechanisms that shape the solar atmosphere's spectral lines. By exploring how electron density stratification and the velocity distribution anisotropy influence off-limb line profiles, we contribute to the greater understanding of solar plasma properties. In conclusion, our research provides new insights into the physics governing the solar atmosphere and has implications for advancing our knowledge of the Sun and its relationship to the Earth's climate.

We detected X-ray emission from the 50-kyr-old pulsar J1809-1917 and resolved
its pulsar wind nebula (PWN) with Chandra. The pulsar spectrum fits PL+BB model
with the photon index of 1.2 and the BB temperature of 2 MK for n_{H}=0.7\times
10^{22} cm^{-2}. The luminosities are(4\pm 1)\times 10^{31} ergs s^{-1} for the
PL component (in the 0.5-8 keV band) and ~1\times 10^{32} ergs s^{-1} for the
BB component (bolometric) at a plausible distance of 3.5 kpc. The bright inner
PWN component of a 3''\times12'' size is elongated in the north-south
direction, with the pulsar close to its south end. This component is immersed
in a larger (20''\times40''), similarly elongated outer PWN component of lower
surface brightness. The elongated shape of the compact PWN can be explained by
the ram pressure confinement of the pulsar wind due to the supersonic motion of
the pulsar. The PWN spectrum fits a PL model with photon index of 1.4\pm0.1 and
0.5-8 keV luminosity of 4\times10^{32} ergs s^{-1}. The compact PWN appears to
be inside a large-scale (~4'\times4') emission more extended to the south of
the pulsar, i.e. in the direction of the alleged pulsar motion. To explain the
extended X-ray emission ahead of the moving pulsar, one has to invoke strong
intrinsic anisotropy of the pulsar wind or assume that this emission comes from
a relic PWN swept by the asymmetrical reverse SNR shock. The pulsar and its PWN
are located within the extent of the unidentified TeV source HESS J1809-193
whose brightest part is offset by ~8' to the south of the pulsar, i.e. in the
same direction as the large-scale X-ray emission. Although the association
between J1809-1917 and HESS J1809-193 is plausible, an alternative source of
relativistic electrons powering HESS J1809-193 might be the serendipitously
discovered X-ray source CXOU J180940.7-192544.

Our research focused on investigating the occurrence and properties of emission and absorption noise in the fractional quantum Hall effect. Our motivation for this research was to shed more light on the interaction between electronic states in these exotic systems. The fractional quantum Hall effect involves the collective behavior of strongly interacting electrons in a two-dimensional electron gas subject to a strong magnetic field. 

We utilized advanced measurement techniques to study the observable products of emission and absorption noise in these systems. Our primary methodology was to measure the current and voltage correlations between various quantum Hall edge channels. In particular, we focused on the noise properties of the fractional edge states arising in the vicinity of the filling factor of 5/2. 

We aimed to solve the problem of understanding the underlying mechanisms responsible for the noise activity in these systems. To this end, our work involved detailed analysis of the measured data and comparison with theoretical models. Our results have provided insights into the dynamical processes and correlations between electrons in fractional quantum Hall systems. The understanding gained through our research has practical implications for the development of future electronic devices that rely on the fractional quantum Hall effect.

In this work, we examine the application of the Kullback-Leibler distance to quantify the amount of information filtered from multivariate data. Our methodology involves analyzing the distribution of data in two different datasets and calculating the entropy of each dataset. We then use the Kullback-Leibler distance to measure the amount of information lost in the process of transforming one dataset into the other. This distance measures the divergence between the actual probability distribution of the data and the probability distribution of the data under an alternative hypothesis.

The problem we aim to solve is to provide a measure of the amount of information lost in the transformation of raw data. This is important because the quality of information obtained from multivariate data is dependent on the degree of information retained after filtering. The results obtained from our experiment demonstrate that the Kullback-Leibler distance is an effective metric for evaluating the amount of information extracted from multivariate data. 

In conclusion, the Kullback-Leibler distance offers a unique perspective on measuring the information filtered from multivariate data. By quantifying the divergence between two probability distributions, we can determine the amount of information retained. The implications of our findings are significant for researchers and practitioners analyzing multivariate data, as it provides an objective measure of information quality for decision making.

We analyze a recently proposed method to create fractional quantum Hall (FQH)
states of atoms confined in optical lattices [A. S{\o}rensen {\it et al.},
Phys. Rev. Lett. {\bf 94} 086803 (2005)]. Extending the previous work, we
investigate conditions under which the FQH effect can be achieved for bosons on
a lattice with an effective magnetic field and finite onsite interaction.
Furthermore, we characterize the ground state in such systems by calculating
Chern numbers which can provide direct signatures of topological order and
explore regimes where the characterization in terms of wavefunction overlap
fails. We also discuss various issues which are relevant for the practical
realization of such FQH states with ultracold atoms in an optical lattice,
including the presence of the long-range dipole interaction which can improve
the energy gap and stabilize the ground state. We also investigate a new
detection technique based on Bragg spectroscopy to probe these system in an
experimental realization.

In this study, we present the results of our analysis of the near-infrared spectra of the black hole X-ray binary, A0620-00. Black hole X-ray binaries provide an ideal environment to study the strong gravitational effects of black holes on their companion star. They also provide crucial insights into the formation and evolution of binary systems. The near-infrared region of the electromagnetic spectrum is particularly useful in these studies because it allows us to probe the innermost regions of the system, where the black hole exerts the strongest gravitational forces.

Our goal was to analyze the near-infrared spectra of A0620-00 and decipher the properties of the system. Using high-resolution spectra obtained from the Gemini Near-Infrared Spectrograph (GNIRS), we were able to extract several important features of the system. We identified the presence of a hot accretion disk around the black hole, which emits intense near-infrared radiation. We also detected the signature of the companion star, a low-mass red dwarf, which is orbiting the black hole every 7.75 hours.

Our findings provide important insights into the physics of black hole X-ray binaries and their evolution. Our study also highlights the usefulness of near-infrared observations in studying these systems. Further studies in this field will undoubtedly shed more light on the mysteries of black holes and their interactions with their surroundings.

In this study, we explore the absorption spectroscopy of individual single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) as a method to understand their electronic and optical properties. Absorption spectra of SWCNTs can provide critical information on their electronic band structure and exciton binding energy, essential parameters for developing nanotube-based electronics and optoelectronic devices. However, the presence of multiple chiralities and diameters in typical SWCNT samples complicates analysis of their absorption spectra. To overcome this hurdle, we used a customized spectroscopy system with submillisecond acquisition times to measure absorption spectra of individual SWCNTs with high spectral resolution. By calculating the absorption peak position and intensity, we identified the chiral indices and diameter of individual SWCNTs, revealing a dependence of exciton binding energy on their diameter. Furthermore, we characterized the excitonic transitions of a single SWCNT, which exhibited highly asymmetric absorption lineshapes beyond simple Lorentzian functions, indicating the presence of strong electron-electron interaction. Our results provide key insights for understanding the optical and electronic properties of SWCNTs and for designing nanotube-based devices with new functionalities.

In this paper, we address the problem of power allocation for the Vertical Bell Labs Layered Space-Time (V-BLAST) system. The V-BLAST architecture is a multiple-input-multiple-output (MIMO) communication system that uses spatial diversity to increase data rates in wireless communication networks. In this architecture, each antenna transmits a different part of the data, which are then combined at the receiver side to recover the original data. 

In this work, we propose an optimization algorithm to find the optimum power allocation between different antennas in the V-BLAST architecture. Our algorithm aims to maximize the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the received signal while satisfying a power constraint. We formulate the problem as a non-convex optimization problem and use the gradient-based approach to solve it. 

The key idea of our research is to incorporate optimization techniques to improve the performance of MIMO systems. Our approach is to solve the power allocation problem for V-BLAST architecture using mathematical optimization. To the best of our knowledge, our approach is the first to provide an optimal power allocation solution for the V-BLAST architecture. 

The motivation for our research is to enhance the overall performance of wireless networks by improving the efficiency and reliability of the data communication systems. The proposed algorithm can be used to allocate power and resources for different antennas in a wireless network, making it an essential tool for optimizing wireless communication systems. Our results show that the proposed algorithm provides significant performance improvement compared to other existing power allocation algorithms, making it a promising method for future research in this area.

In this paper, we explore the feasibility of testing string theory with Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) experiments. While the Standard Model of particle physics has been highly successful in explaining the particle physics interactions up to the TeV scale, it falls short of providing a consistent framework for incorporating gravity. String theory, on the other hand, is a theoretical framework that aims to reconcile quantum mechanics and gravity at all energy scales.

To test string theory using CMB, we focused on three key predictions of the theory. First, string theory predicts that the universe has extra dimensions beyond our four visible dimensions, which could manifest themselves as distortions in the CMB. Second, the theory predicts the existence of cosmic strings - topological defects formed during the universe's early phase transitions - that could produce gravitational waves affecting the CMB. Finally, string theory predicts the presence of specific patterns in the CMB that could arise from the so-called "brane inflation".

We simulated CMB maps with these predictions and compared them with the latest Planck data. Our results suggest that the presence of extra dimensions would affect the overall CMB power spectrum, while cosmic strings could leave imprints in the CMB temperature and polarization maps. We also found that brane inflation could leave detectable signatures in the CMB temperature and polarization maps in the form of specific patterns.

In conclusion, we demonstrate that CMB experiments can provide a powerful tool for testing string theory predictions and potentially provide indirect evidence for the existence of extra dimensions, cosmic strings, and brane inflation.

We investigate statistical properties of LRGs in a sample of X-ray selected
galaxy clusters at intermediate redshift ($0.2\le z\le0.6$). The LRGs are
selected based on carefully designed color criteria, and the cluster membership
is assessed via photometric redshifts. As clusters and LRGs are both viewed as
promising tracer of the underlying dark matter distribution, understanding the
distribution of LRGs within clusters is an important issue.
  Our main findings include:
  1. The halo occupation distribution of LRGs inside our cluster sample is
$N(M) = k\times (M/10^{14})^{a}$ where $a=0.620\pm 0.105 $ and $k=1.425\pm0.285
$ assuming a Poisson distribution for $N(M)$.
  2. The halo occupation distribution of LRGs ($N(M)$) and the satellite
distribution of LRGs ($N-1(M)$) are both consistent with being Poisson. To be
more quantitative, we find $Var(N)/<N>= 1.428\pm 0.351$ and $Var(N-1)/<N-1> =
1.823 \pm 0.496$
  3. The radial profile of LRGs within clusters when fitted with a NFW profile
gives a concentration of $17.5^{+7.1}_{-4.3}$ ($6.0^{+3.2}_{-1.9}$) including
(excluding) BLRGs (Brightest LRGs).
  We also discuss the implications of these observations on the evolution of
massive galaxies in clusters.

In this work, we report the detection of a rare supersoft outburst event during a Suzaku observation of the supernova remnant 1E0102.2-7219. This remnant is known to have been formed by the explosion of a massive star, which likely left behind a compact object such as a neutron star or a black hole. We were interested in studying the X-ray emission from the remnant, particularly at energies of less than 1 keV, where the emission is expected to be dominated by soft X-rays. 

During our Suzaku observation, we discovered a transient source that appeared to be coincident with the remnant. The source displayed a steep soft X-ray spectrum and a rapid rise and decline in flux over a timescale of a few days. These observations are consistent with the signature of a supersoft outburst, which is a rare phenomenon thought to be caused by a thermonuclear runaway on the surface of a white dwarf or a neutron star. Our discovery of this event in connection with 1E0102.2-7219 provides new insights into the nature of the compact object in the remnant, as well as the physical conditions in the surrounding medium. 

Our results represent a significant step forward in understanding the complex processes that drive supernova explosions and the subsequent evolution of the remnants they leave behind. This work highlights the potential of Suzaku and other observatories to detect and study rare astronomical events, and underscores the importance of continued exploration of our dynamic universe.

We introduce a family of rings of symmetric functions depending on an
infinite sequence of parameters. A distinguished basis of such a ring is
comprised by analogues of the Schur functions. The corresponding structure
coefficients are polynomials in the parameters which we call the
Littlewood-Richardson polynomials. We give a combinatorial rule for their
calculation by modifying an earlier result of B. Sagan and the author. The new
rule provides a formula for these polynomials which is manifestly positive in
the sense of W. Graham. We apply this formula for the calculation of the
product of equivariant Schubert classes on Grassmannians which implies a
stability property of the structure coefficients. The first manifestly positive
formula for such an expansion was given by A. Knutson and T. Tao by using
combinatorics of puzzles while the stability property was not apparent from
that formula. We also use the Littlewood-Richardson polynomials to describe the
multiplication rule in the algebra of the Casimir elements for the general
linear Lie algebra in the basis of the quantum immanants constructed by A.
Okounkov and G. Olshanski.

In this paper, we investigate the positivity and almost positivity properties of biharmonic Green's functions under Dirichlet boundary conditions. We establish that the biharmonic Green's functions are positive in the interior of the domain and for radial functions, they are almost positive on the boundary. Our key idea is to use an integral representation for biharmonic Green's functions and apply a series of transformations to obtain a simpler expression for the positivity of the Green's functions. We also introduce a fractional operator which allows us to study almost positivity. The methodology employed involves the use of integral representation, transformation techniques, and properties of fractional integrals and derivatives.

The problem this work attempts to solve is important in many areas of physics, engineering, and applied mathematics where biharmonic equations arise. The positivity and almost positivity properties of biharmonic Green's functions are crucial for ensuring the stability and well-posedness of boundary value problems. Our findings have significant implications in the study of elasticity, fluid mechanics, and electrostatics.

In conclusion, our work provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for the positivity and almost positivity of biharmonic Green's functions under Dirichlet boundary conditions, and can be used to derive new results in various application areas.

In this work, we investigate the merging timescale of luminous red galaxies (LRGs) and the fate of their associated dark matter halos. The motivation for this research stems from the need to understand the assembly processes of galaxies and the hierarchical growth of structure in the Universe. By studying the merging timescale of LRGs, we hope to shed light on the efficiency and frequency of galaxy mergers, which are thought to be major drivers of galaxy evolution.

Our key idea is that by analyzing the spatial distribution and clustering properties of a sample of LRGs, we can place constraints on the typical timescale of their merger events. We use a combination of observational data from the Baryon Oscillation Spectroscopic Survey and simulations from the IllustrisTNG project to compare our theoretical predictions with the observed clustering of LRGs.

Our methodology involves modeling the dark matter halo occupation distribution and using it to predict the number density and clustering of LRGs over a range of merging timescales. We then compare our model predictions with the observed clustering statistics to infer the most likely merger timescale.

The problem that our work attempts to solve is to understand how and when LRGs merge, and what happens to their associated dark matter halos during the merging process. By placing constraints on the merging timescale of LRGs, we aim to provide insight into the efficiency and frequency of galaxy mergers, which are important drivers of galaxy evolution.

Residual correlations between decay products due to a combination of both
correlations between parents at small relative velocities and small decay
momenta are discussed. Residual correlations between photons from pion decays
are considered as a new possible source of information on direct photon
fraction. Residual correlations in $p\gamma$ and $p\Lambda$ systems due to
$p\Sigma^0$ interaction in final state are predicted based on the $p\Sigma^0$
low energy scattering parameters deduced from the spin-flavour SU$_6$ model by
Fujiwara et al. including effective meson exchange potentials and explicit
flavour symmetry breaking to reproduce the properties of the two-nucleon system
and the low-energy hyperon-nucleon cross section data. The $p\gamma_{\Sigma^0}$
residual correlation is concentrated at $k^* \approx 70$ Mev/$c$ and its shape
and intensity appears to be sensitive to the scattering parameters and
space-time dimensions of the source. The $p\Lambda_{\Sigma^0}$ residual
correlation recovers the negative parent $p\Sigma^0$ correlation for $k^* > 70$
Mev/$c$. The neglect of this negative residual correlation would lead to the
underestimation of the parent $p\Lambda$ correlation effect and to an
overestimation of the source size.

A new generation of radio telescopes are currently being built with the goal
of tracing the cosmic distribution of atomic hydrogen at redshifts 6-15 through
its 21-cm line. The observations will probe the large-scale brightness
fluctuations sourced by ionization fluctuations during cosmic reionization.
Since detailed maps will be difficult to extract due to noise and foreground
emission, efforts have focused on a statistical detection of the 21-cm
fluctuations. During cosmic reionization, these fluctuations are highly
non-Gaussian and thus more information can be extracted than just the
one-dimensional function that is usually considered, i.e., the correlation
function. We calculate a two-dimensional function that if measured
observationally would allow a more thorough investigation of the properties of
the underlying ionizing sources. This function is the probability distribution
function (PDF) of the difference in the 21-cm brightness temperature between
two points, as a function of the separation between the points. While the
standard correlation function is determined by a complicated mixture of
contributions from density and ionization fluctuations, we show that the
difference PDF holds the key to separately measuring the statistical properties
of the ionized regions.

The identification of basaltic asteroids in the asteroid Main Belt and the
description of their surface mineralogy is necessary to understand the
diversity in the collection of basaltic meteorites. Basaltic asteroids can be
identified from their visible reflectance spectra and are classified as V-type
in the usual taxonomies. In this work, we report visible spectroscopic
observations of two candidate V-type asteroids, (7472) Kumakiri and (10537)
1991 RY16, located in the outer Main Belt (a > 2.85 UA). These candidate have
been previously identified by Roig and Gil-Hutton (2006, Icarus 183, 411) using
the Sloan Digital Sky Survey colors. The spectroscopic observations have been
obtained at the Calar Alto Observatory, Spain, during observational runs in
November and December 2006. The spectra of these two asteroids show the steep
slope shortwards of 0.70 microns and the deep absorption feature longwards of
0.75 microns that are characteristic of V-type asteroids. However, the presence
of a shallow but conspicuous absorption band around 0.65 microns opens some
questions about the actual mineralogy of these two asteroids. Such band has
never been observed before in basaltic asteroids with the intensity we detected
it. We discuss the possibility for this shallow absorption feature to be caused
by the presence of chromium on the asteroid surface. Our results indicate that,
together with (1459) Magnya, asteroids (7472) Kumakiri and (10537) 1991 RY16
may be the only traces of basaltic material found up to now in the outer Main
Belt.

We present a model improving the two-angle model for interphase chromatin
(E2A model). This model takes into account the cylindrical shape of the histone
octamers, the H1 histones in front of the nucleosomes and the vertical distance
$d$ between the in and outgoing DNA strands. Factoring these chromatin features
in, one gets essential changes in the chromatin phase diagram: Not only the
shape of the excluded-volume borderline changes but also the vertical distance
$d$ has a dramatic influence on the forbidden area. Furthermore, we examined
the influence of H1 defects on the properties of the chromatin fiber. Thus we
present two possible strategies for chromatin compaction: The use of very dense
states in the phase diagram in the gaps in the excluded volume borderline or
missing H1 histones which can lead to very compact fibers. The chromatin fiber
might use both of these mechanisms to compact itself at least locally. Line
densities computed within the model coincident with the experimental values.

Within a half-parsec from the Galactic center (GC), there is a population of
coeval young stars which appear to reside in a coherent disk. Surrounding this
dynamically-cool stellar system, there is a population of stars with a similar
age and much larger eccentricities and inclinations relative to the disk. We
propose a hypothesis for the origin of this dynamical dichotomy. Without
specifying any specific mechanism, we consider the possibility that both
stellar populations were formed within a disk some 6 Myr ago. But this orderly
structure was dynamically perturbed outside-in by an intruding object with a
mass ~10^4 Msun, which may be an intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH) or a dark
stellar cluster hosting an IMBH. We suggest that the perturber migrated inward
to ~0.15-0.3pc from the GC under the action of dynamical friction. Along the
way, it captured many stars in the outer disk region into its mean-motion
resonance, forced them to migrate with it, closely encountered with them, and
induced the growth of their eccentricity and inclination. But stars in the
inner regions of the disk retain their initial coplanar structure. We predict
that some of the inclined and eccentric stars surrounding the disk may have
similar Galactocentric semimajor axis. Future precision determination of their
kinematic distribution of these stars will not only provide a test for this
hypothesis but also evidences for the presence of an IMBH or a dark cluster at
the immediate proximity of the massive black hole at the GC. (abridged)

A general Hamiltonian theory for the adiabatic motion of relativistic charged
particles confined by slowly-varying background electromagnetic fields is
presented based on a unified Lie-transform perturbation analysis in extended
phase space (which includes energy and time as independent coordinates) for all
three adiabatic invariants. First, the guiding-center equations of motion for a
relativistic particle are derived from the particle Lagrangian. Covariant
aspects of the resulting relativistic guiding-center equations of motion are
discussed and contrasted with previous works. Next, the second and third
invariants for the bounce motion and drift motion, respectively, are obtained
by successively removing the bounce phase and the drift phase from the
guiding-center Lagrangian. First-order corrections to the second and third
adiabatic invariants for a relativistic particle are derived. These results
simplify and generalize previous works to all three adiabatic motions of
relativistic magnetically-trapped particles.

In this paper, we present our work on the kinetic theory of two dimensional point vortices, which has been motivated by the need to understand the behavior of such vortices in various fluid dynamics applications. To this end, we have developed a BBGKY-like hierarchy for the point vortices system, and derived the kinetic equation for the one particle distribution function. In order to obtain this result, we have used a well-known technique in the field of plasma physics, where we have treated the velocity of each point vortex as a random variable. 

Our results show that the kinetic equation for the one particle distribution function corresponds to the kinetic equation for a two-dimensional ideal gas. By analyzing the moments of this equation, we have also derived the evolution equations for the moments of the vorticity field. We have further used the formalism of the kinetic theory to derive the equation for the energy spectrum of the point vortices, which shows that the energy cascades from the large scales to small scales similar to the enstrophy cascade in two-dimensional turbulence. 

Overall, our work provides an analytical foundation for understanding the dynamics of point vortices in two-dimensional systems, which has implications for a wide range of applications in fluid dynamics and geophysics.

The large quantity and high quality of modern radio and infrared line
observations require efficient modeling techniques to infer physical and
chemical parameters such as temperature, density, and molecular abundances. We
present a computer program to calculate the intensities of atomic and molecular
lines produced in a uniform medium, based on statistical equilibrium
calculations involving collisional and radiative processes and including
radiation from background sources. Optical depth effects are treated with an
escape probability method. The program is available on the World Wide Web at
http://www.sron.rug.nl/~vdtak/radex/index.shtml . The program makes use of
molecular data files maintained in the Leiden Atomic and Molecular Database
(LAMDA), which will continue to be improved and expanded. The performance of
the program is compared with more approximate and with more sophisticated
methods. An Appendix provides diagnostic plots to estimate physical parameters
from line intensity ratios of commonly observed molecules. This program should
form an important tool in analyzing observations from current and future radio
and infrared telescopes.

We report the studies of ultrafast electron nanocrystallography on
size-selected Au nanoparticles (2-20 nm) supported on a molecular interface.
Reversible surface melting, melting, and recrystallization were investigated
with dynamical full-profile radial distribution functions determined with
sub-picosecond and picometer accuracies. In an ultrafast photoinduced melting,
the nanoparticles are driven to a non-equilibrium transformation, characterized
by the initial lattice deformations, nonequilibrium electron-phonon coupling,
and upon melting, the collective bonding and debonding, transforming
nanocrystals into shelled nanoliquids. The displasive structural excitation at
premelting and the coherent transformation with crystal/liquid coexistence
during photomelting differ from the reciprocal behavior of recrystallization,
where a hot lattice forms from liquid and then thermally contracts. The degree
of structural change and the thermodynamics of melting are found to depend on
the size of nanoparticle.

In this study, we explore the possibility of discerning new properties of atomic nuclei using non-linear methods, such as fractal and recurrence quantification analysis. We aim to solve the issue of limited understanding of the complexity of the internal structure of atomic nuclei, particularly within the context of unstable isotopes. Our methodology involves the analysis of alpha decay data from various atomic nuclei, focusing on the measurement of fractal dimension and recurrence quantification features. 

Our key idea is that the internal structure of nuclei may follow non-linear, complex patterns that can be better understood with the use of advanced mathematical techniques. The motivation for our research stems from the potential impact that gaining a better understanding of the properties of atomic nuclei can have on fields such as nuclear physics, astrophysics, and nuclear medicine, to name a few. By exploring new methods of analysis, we hope to unveil previously unknown properties of atomic nuclei and further our understanding of the fundamental building blocks of matter. This study could also pave the way for future research in the field of nuclear physics, providing new insights and avenues of investigation.

During the last three decades, evidence has mounted that star and planet
formation is not an isolated process, but is influenced by current and previous
generations of stars. Although cool stars form in a range of environments, from
isolated globules to rich embedded clusters, the influences of other stars on
cool star and planet formation may be most significant in embedded clusters,
where hundreds to thousands of cool stars form in close proximity to OB stars.
At the cool stars 14 meeting, a splinter session was convened to discuss the
role of environment in the formation of cool stars and planetary systems; with
an emphasis on the ``hot'' environment found in rich clusters. We review here
the basic results, ideas and questions presented at the session. We have
organized this contribution into five basic questions: what is the typical
environment of cool star formation, what role do hot star play in cool star
formation, what role does environment play in planet formation, what is the
role of hot star winds and supernovae, and what was the formation environment
of the Sun? The intention is to review progress made in addressing each
question, and to underscore areas of agreement and contention.

In this work, we present a novel approach to limit in-degree distribution in growing networks with arbitrary out-degree. The motivation behind this research is to better understand the behavior of complex networks and how their topological properties evolve over time. Specifically, we aim to provide a way to control the in-degree distribution of a growing network, which is essential for modeling many real-world systems such as social networks, citation networks, and biological networks. Our approach is based on the idea of preferential attachment, where new nodes are connected preferentially to highly connected existing nodes. We show that by introducing a damping factor to the preferential attachment rule, we can effectively limit the in-degree distribution without compromising other important network properties such as the power-law degree distribution. Moreover, we demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing methods in terms of controlling the in-degree distribution while preserving other important features of the network. Overall, this work provides a useful tool for researchers studying complex networks and could have practical applications in fields such as epidemiology, finance, and transportation.

In this work, we investigate the dynamic phase transition in the two-dimensional kinetic Ising model using the conjugate field and fluctuation-dissipation relation. The motivation for our research is to understand the behavior of the dynamic phase transition in the Ising model, which is a paradigmatic model for statistical physics. The problem that our work attempts to solve is how to describe the behavior of the system near the critical point, where the correlation length becomes infinite.

To achieve this goal, we use the conjugate field method to derive the equation of motion for the order parameter and the fluctuation-dissipation relation to compute the critical exponents. We find that the behavior of the order parameter is governed by a non-linear diffusion equation, which exhibits both stationary and travelling wave solutions. We also find that the critical exponents can be obtained from the scaling behavior of the order parameter near the critical point.

Our methodology involves analytical calculations and numerical simulations of the Ising model using Monte Carlo methods. Our key idea is to use the conjugate field method to derive the equation of motion and the fluctuation-dissipation relation to compute the critical exponents. Our results provide insight into the behavior of the dynamic phase transition in the Ising model and can be used to understand other systems with similar behavior.

In this paper, a family of low-density parity-check (LDPC) degree
distributions, whose decoding threshold on the binary erasure channel (BEC)
admits a simple closed form, is presented. These degree distributions are a
subset of the check regular distributions (i.e. all the check nodes have the
same degree), and are referred to as $p$-positive distributions. It is given
proof that the threshold for a $p$-positive distribution is simply expressed by
$[\lambda'(0)\rho'(1)]^{-1}$. Besides this closed form threshold expression,
the $p$-positive distributions exhibit three additional properties. First, for
given code rate, check degree and maximum variable degree, they are in some
cases characterized by a threshold which is extremely close to that of the best
known check regular distributions, under the same set of constraints. Second,
the threshold optimization problem within the $p$-positive class can be solved
in some cases with analytic methods, without using any numerical optimization
tool. Third, these distributions can achieve the BEC capacity. The last
property is shown by proving that the well-known binomial degree distributions
belong to the $p$-positive family.

We propose a 4+1-dimensional action that is a candidate for realising a
standard-model-like effective theory for fields dynamically localised to a
domain-wall brane. Our construction is based in part on the conjecture that the
Dvali-Shifman mechanism for dynamically localising gauge bosons works correctly
in 4+1-d. Assuming this to be so, we require the gauge symmetry to be SU(5)in
the bulk, spontaneously breaking to SU(3)xSU(2)xU(1) inside the domain wall,
thus dynamically localising the standard model gauge bosons provided that the
SU(5) theory in the bulk exhibits confinement. The wall is created jointly by a
real singlet-Higgs field eta configured as a kink, and an SU(5) adjoint-Higgs
field chi that takes nonzero values inside the wall. Chiral 3+1-dimensional
quarks and leptons are confined and split along the bulk direction via their
Yukawa couplings to eta and chi. The Higgs doublet and its colour triplet SU(5)
partner are similarly localised and split. The splittings suppress
coloured-Higgs-induced proton decay and, because of the different localisation
profiles, the usual SU(5) mass relation m_e = m_d does not arise. Localised
gravity is generated via the Randall-Sundrum alternative to compactification.

In this work, we aim to investigate the building up of the disk galaxy M33 and the evolution of the metallicity gradient. Our goal is to shed light on the mechanisms responsible for the creation of the disk structure and on the way in which the chemical composition of the gas is modified during its evolution. 

In order to achieve this, we have analyzed a sample of young star clusters in M33 using high resolution spectroscopy. Our results indicate that there is a clear correlation between the metallicity of the clusters and their distance from the center of the galaxy. This suggests that the metallicity gradient in M33 has been established over a long period of time through a combination of gas accretion and star formation.

Furthermore, we have developed a model that takes into account the effects of accretion, star formation, and outflow on the chemical evolution of the galaxy. Our analysis shows that the observed metallicity gradient can be reproduced by our model, providing a solid theoretical framework for the observed phenomenon.

Overall, our work provides new insights into the formation and evolution of M33, and more broadly into the mechanisms driving the evolution of disk galaxies. Our approach, which combines observational data and theoretical modeling, represents a powerful tool for understanding the complex processes taking place in these systems.

In this work, we present deep radio images of the Cygnus OB2 region, where two unidentified TeV sources, HEGRA and Whipple, have been detected. The motivation for the research lies in the need to identify possible counterparts to these high-energy sources. Since the emission mechanisms of TeV sources are not fully understood, the search for counterparts in other wavelengths, such as radio, can provide valuable information about their nature and origin.

We conducted observations with the Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope, resulting in maps with a sensitivity of 20 micro-Jansky at 325 MHz and 12 micro-Jansky at 610 MHz. We detected several compact radio sources within the TeV source regions, including one that coincides with the reported position of a ROSAT X-ray source. We also resolved extended emission in the northern HEGRA source, which shows a complex structure with multiple clumps and filaments.

Overall, our results provide new insights into the nature of the TeV sources in the Cygnus OB2 region, suggesting that they could be associated with supernova remnants or pulsar wind nebulae. The deep radio images presented in this work will serve as a valuable resource for future studies of this region and other TeV sources.

This work is part of the NUGA survey of CO emission in nearby active
galaxies. We present observations of NGC4569, a member of the Virgo Cluster. We
analyse the molecular gas distribution and kinematics in the central region and
we investigate a possible link to the strong starburst present at the nucleus.
70% of the 1.1x10^9 Msolar of molecular gas detected in the inner 20" is found
to be concentrated within the inner 800 pc and is distributed along the large
scale stellar bar seen in near-infrared observations. A hole in the CO
distribution coincides with the nucleus where most of the Halpha emission and
blue light are emitted. The kinematics are modelled in three different ways,
ranging from the purely geometrical to the most physical. This approach allows
us to constrain progressively the physical properties of the galaxy and
eventually to emerge with a reasonable fit to an analytical model of orbits in
a barred potential. Fitting an axisymmetric model shows that the non-circular
motions must be comparable in amplitude to the circular motions (120 km/s).
Fitting a model based on elliptical orbits allows us to identify with
confidence the single inner Lindblad resonance (ILR) of the large scale bar.
Finally, a model based on analytical solutions for the gas particle orbits in a
weakly barred potential constrained by the ILR radius reproduces the
observations well. The mass inflow rate is then estimated and discussed based
on the best fit model solution. The gravitational torques implied by this model
are able to efficiently funnel the gas inside the ILR down to 300 pc, although
another mechanism must take over to fuel the nuclear starburst inside 100 pc.

The effect of the electron density stratification on the intensity profiles
of the H I Ly-$\alpha$ line and the O VI and Mg X doublets formed in solar
coronal holes is investigated. We employ an analytical 2-D model of the large
scale coronal magnetic field that provides a good representation of the corona
at the minimum of solar activity. We use the mass-flux conservation equation to
determine the outflow speed of the solar wind at any location in the solar
corona and take into account the integration along the line of sight (LOS). The
main assumption we make is that no anisotropy in the kinetic temperature of the
coronal species is considered. We find that at distances greater than 1 Rsun
from the solar surface the widths of the emitted lines of O VI and Mg X are
sensitive to the details of the adopted electron density stratification.
However, Ly-$\alpha$, which is a pure radiative line, is hardly affected. The
calculated total intensities of Ly-$\alpha$ and the O VI doublet depend to a
lesser degree on the density stratification and are comparable to the observed
ones for most of the considered density models. The widths of the observed
profiles of Ly-$\alpha$ and Mg X are well reproduced by most of the considered
electron density stratifications, while for the O VI doublet only few
stratifications give satisfying results. The densities deduced from SOHO data
result in O VI profiles whose widths and intensity ratio are relatively close
to the values observed by UVCS although only isotropic velocity distributions
are employed. These density profiles also reproduce the other considered
observables with good accuracy. Thus the need for a strong anisotropy of the
velocity distribution (i.e. a temperature anisotropy) is not so clear cut as
previous investigations of UVCS data suggested. ...

We study to which extent planetesimal accretion is affected by the perturbing
presence of a compagnon star. We concentrate on one crucial parameter: the
distribution of encounter velocities within the planetesimal swarm. We
numerically explore the evolution of this parameter taking into account the
secular perturbations of the binary and friction due to the very likely
presence of gas in the disk. $<\Delta v>$ maps are derived, for planetesimals
of different sizes, for a total of 120 binary configurations (eccentricity eb
and separation ab). We identify for each case 3 different accreting behaviours.
1) In regions where no significant dV increase is observed, ``classical''
(i.e., single-star) accretion is possible. 2) In regions where $dV>v_{ero}$,
the threshold velocity above which all impacts are eroding, no accretion is
possible and planet growth is stopped. 3) In between these 2 limiting
behaviours, a large fraction of binary configurations leads to significant dV
increase, but still below the erosion threshold. In this intermediate case,
planetesimal growth can occur, but proceeds slower than in the single-star
case, possibly following the so-called type II runaway groth mode.

Aims:The Gaia astrometric survey mission will, as a consequence of its
scanning law, obtain low resolution optical (330-1000 nm) spectrophotometry of
several million unresolved galaxies brighter than V=22. We present the first
steps in a project to design and implement a classification system for these
data. The goal is both to determine morphological classes and to estimate
intrinsic astrophysical parameters via synthetic templates. Here we describe
(1) a new library of synthetic galaxy spectra, and (2) first results of
classification and parametrization experiments using simulated Gaia
spectrophotometry of this library. Methods:We have created a large grid of
synthetic galaxy spectra using the PEGASE.2 code, which is based on galaxy
evolution models that take into account metallicity evolution, extinction
correction, emission lines (with stellar spectra based on the BaSeL library).
Our classification and regression models are Support Vector Machines (SVMs),
which are kernel-based nonlinear estimators. Results:We produce a basic library
of about 4000 zero redshift galaxy spectra covering the main Hubble types over
wavelength range 250 to 1050 nm at a sampling of 1 nm or less. It is computed
on a regular grid of four key astrophysical parameters for each type and for
intermediate random values of the same parameters. An extended library
reproduces this at a series of redshifts. Initial results from the SVM
classifiers and parametrizers are promising, indicating that Hubble types can
be reliably predicted and several parameters estimated with low bias and
variance. Comparing the colours of our synthetic library with Sloan Digital Sky
Survey (SDSS) spectra we find good agreement over the full range of Hubble
types and parameters.

Significant alignment and signed-intensity anomalies of local features of the
cosmic microwave background (CMB) are detected on the three-year WMAP data,
through a decomposition of the signal with steerable wavelets on the sphere.
Firstly, an alignment analysis identifies two mean preferred planes in the sky,
both with normal axes close to the CMB dipole axis. The first plane is defined
by the directions toward which local CMB features are anomalously aligned. A
mean preferred axis is also identified in this plane, located very close to the
ecliptic poles axis. The second plane is defined by the directions anomalously
avoided by local CMB features. This alignment anomaly provides further insight
on recent results (Wiaux et al. 2006). Secondly, a signed-intensity analysis
identifies three mean preferred directions in the southern galactic hemisphere
with anomalously high or low temperature of local CMB features: a cold spot
essentially identified with a known cold spot (Vielva et al. 2004), a second
cold spot lying very close to the southern end of the CMB dipole axis, and a
hot spot lying close to the southern end of the ecliptic poles axis. In both
analyses, the anomalies are observed at wavelet scales corresponding to angular
sizes around 10 degress on the celestial sphere, with global significance
levels around 1%. Further investigation reveals that the alignment and
signed-intensity anomalies are only very partially related. Instrumental noise,
foreground emissions, as well as some form of other systematics, are strongly
rejected as possible origins of the detections. An explanation might still be
envisaged in terms of a global violation of the isotropy of the Universe,
inducing an intrinsic statistical anisotropy of the CMB.

We investigate the transport properties of ballistic quantum wires in the
presence of Zeeman spin splittings and a spatially inhomogeneous Rashba
interaction. The Zeeman interaction is extended along the wire and produces
gaps in the energy spectrum which allow electron propagation only for spinors
lying along a certain direction. For spins in the opposite direction the waves
are evanescent far away from the Rashba region, which plays the role of the
scattering center. The most interesting case occurs when the magnetic field is
perpendicular to the Rashba field. Then, the spins of the asymptotic
wavefunctions are not eigenfunctions of the Rashba Hamiltonian and the
resulting coupling between spins in the Rashba region gives rise to sudden
changes of the transmission probability when the Fermi energy is swept along
the gap. After briefly examining the energy spectrum and eigenfunctions of a
wire with extended Rashba coupling, we analyze the transmission through a
region of localized Rashba interaction, in which a double interface separates a
region of constant Rashba interaction from wire leads free from spin-orbit
coupling. For energies slightly above the propagation threshold, we find the
ubiquitous occurrence of transmission zeros (antiresonances) which are analyzed
by matching methods in the one-dimensional limit. We find that a a minimal
tight-binding model yields analytical transmission lineshapes of Fano
antiresonance type. More general angular dependences of the external magnetic
field is treated within projected Schroedinger equations with Hamiltonian
matrix elements mixing wavefunction components. Finally, we consider a
realistic quantum wire where the energy subbands are coupled via the Rashba
intersubband coupling term and discuss its effect on the transmission zeros.

In this study, we investigate the impact of cosmic rays on population III star formation. Our results suggest that cosmic rays can significantly affect the formation of these early, massive stars. To arrive at these findings, we simulated the collapse of primordial gas clouds using a three-dimensional, radiation-hydrodynamic code with a detailed chemical network. We included the effects of cosmic rays, and our simulations showed that cosmic rays heat and ionize the gas in the collapsing protostellar clouds, which leads to a delay in the gravitational collapse and fragmentation of the gas. The delay in the fragmentation of the gas is crucial for the formation of massive stars that have long lifetimes and, as such, are important contributors to chemical enrichment in the early Universe. 
This study seeks to solve the ongoing questions of how population III stars formed and what factors contributed to their development. Our work aimed to show that cosmic rays played an essential role in the creation of these early stars, a concept that has not previously been explored in detail. The findings of this study have broad implications for our understanding of early star formation and the evolution of the Universe.

We consider constraints on the planetesimal population residing in the disks
of AU Microscopii, Beta Pictoris and Fomalhaut taking into account their
observed thicknesses and normal disk opacities. We estimate that bodies of
radius 5, 180 and 70 km are responsible for initiating the collisional cascade
accounting for the dust production for AU-Mic, Beta-Pic and Fomalhaut's disks,
respectively, at break radii from the star where their surface brightness
profiles change slope. Larger bodies, of radius 1000km and with surface density
of order 0.01 g/cm^2, are required to explain the thickness of these disks
assuming that they are heated by gravitational stirring. A comparison between
the densities of the two sizes suggests the size distribution in the largest
bodies is flatter than that observed in the Kuiper belt. AU Mic's disk requires
the shallowest size distribution for bodies with radius greater than 10km
suggesting that the disk contains planetary embryos experiencing a stage of
runaway growth.

In this work, we investigate the isospin violation in the decays $\phi, J/\psi, \psi^\prime \to \omega \pi^0$ via hadronic loops. The investigation is motivated by the importance of understanding the symmetry violation in hadronic interactions, which plays a crucial role in various theoretical models and experimental studies. The aim of this research is to provide a quantitative prediction for the isospin violation in the studied decays using chiral perturbation theory and dispersion relations.

Our analysis is based on the effective Lagrangian approach, which allows us to calculate the decay amplitudes in terms of the relevant low-energy constants and hadronic loops. The hadronic loops contribute significantly to the amplitude, and our analysis includes the contributions from the $\rho$ and $f_2$ resonances.

We find that the isospin violation in the studied decays is relatively small, with the branching ratio for the isospin-violating mode being below 1%. Our results are in good agreement with the existing experimental data and provide insights into the symmetry breaking in hadronic interactions.

In conclusion, our work contributes to the understanding of isospin violation in hadronic decays and provides a quantitative prediction for the isospin violation in $\phi, J/\psi, \psi^\prime \to \omega \pi^0$ via hadronic loops.

In this study, we investigate the gamma-ray albedo of the moon using data collected by the Lunar Prospector spacecraft. The motivation for this research is to better understand the interaction between the moon and cosmic rays and to provide a baseline for future studies of the moon's surface.

Our analysis reveals that the gamma-ray albedo of the moon varies significantly with latitude and is highest near the poles. We also observe a correlation between the albedo and the surface composition of the moon, with higher albedo values associated with areas containing higher concentrations of elements such as aluminum, silicon, and titanium.

To obtain these results, we analyzed data from the Lunar Prospector Gamma-Ray Spectrometer and used Monte Carlo simulations to model the interaction of cosmic rays with the moon's surface. Our methodology allowed us to account for the effects of the moon's topography and composition on the gamma-ray signal.

These findings have important implications for future studies of the moon, as they provide a better understanding of the response of the moon's surface to cosmic rays and can be used to improve models of lunar elemental composition. It also highlights the importance of studying cosmic rays and their interaction with celestial bodies to better understand the evolution of our solar system.

In this work, we present analytic models of plausible gravitational lens potentials that aim to solve the problem of accurately predicting the gravitational lensing phenomenon. The gravitational lensing phenomenon, where light from a distant object is bent and magnified by an intervening massive object, is one of the striking predictions of Einstein's theory of general relativity. It has become a powerful tool for testing cosmological models and probing the nature of dark matter. However, modeling the gravitational potential of the lensing object accurately remains a challenging problem in astrophysics. 

Motivated by the need for more accurate models, we developed a new framework for creating analytic models of plausible gravitational lens potentials. We focused on mass distributions that are observationally viable and physically motivated. Our method allows us to reconstruct the mass profile of a lensing object from the observed lensing signal and estimate the statistical uncertainties in our model. 

Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework in modeling a wide range of gravitational lens potentials. Our method outperforms existing techniques in terms of accuracy and computational efficiency. We also provided insights into the degeneracies and biases in gravitational lens modeling and highlighted the importance of careful model selection. Overall, the analytic models presented in this work can aid in the interpretation of current lensing data and guide future observations.

In this paper, we explore the question of whether advanced potentials are anomalous. The concept of advanced potentials has been developed in recent years in relation to the theory of quantum mechanics. According to this theory, the quantum state of a system is described by a wave function, and the evolution of this wave function over time can be characterized by two different processes: the well-known process of wave function collapse and the less familiar process of advanced potentials.
