measurements be complementary as well. At first sight it seems, however, that
these measurements can be jointly performed with arbitrary precision: they
pertain to different systems (the center of mass of the box and an internal
clock, respectively). But this is deceptive. As we show by explicit
calculation, although the relevant quantities are simultaneously measurable,
they develop non-vanishing commutators when calculated back to the time of
escape of the photon. This justifies Bohr's qualitative arguments in a precise
way; and it illustrates how the details of the dynamics conspire to guarantee
the requirements of complementarity. In addition, our calculations exhibit a
``fine structure'' in the distribution of the uncertainties over the
complementary quantities: depending on \textit{when} the box measurement is
performed, the resulting quantum description of the photon differs. This brings
us close to the argumentation of the later EPR thought experiment.

We report the detection of the millisecond pulsar B1257+12 with the Chandra
X-ray Observatory. In a 20 ks exposure we detected 25 photons from the pulsar,
with energies between 0.4 and 2.0 keV, corresponding to the flux F_X=(4.4+/-
0.9)*10^{-15} ergs s^{-1} cm^{-2} in this energy range. The X-ray spectrum can
be described by a power-law model with photon index Gamma = 2.8 and luminosity
L_X \approx 2.5*10^{29} ergs s^{-1} in the 0.3--8 keV band, for a plausible
distance of 500 pc and hydrogen column density N_H=3*10^{20} cm^{-2}.
Alternatively, the spectrum can be fitted by a blackbody model with kT ~ 0.22
keV and projected emitting area ~2000 m^2. If the thermal X-rays are emitted
from two symmetric polar caps, the bolometric luminosity of the two caps is 2
L_bol ~ 3*10^{29} ergs s^{-1}. We compared our results with the data on other
30 millisecond pulsars observed in X-rays and found that the apparent X-ray
efficiency of PSR B1257+12, L_X/Edot ~ 3*10^{-5} for d=500 pc, is lower than
those of most of millisecond pulsars. This might be explained by an unfavorable
orientation of the X-ray pulsar beam if the radiation is magnetospheric, or by
strong asymmetry of polar caps if the radiation is thermal (e.g., one of the
polar caps is much brighter than the other and remains invisible for most part
of the pulsar period). Alternatively, it could be attributed to absorption of
X-rays in circumpulsar matter, such as a flaring debris disk left over after
formation of the planetary system around the pulsar.

We present an analysis of new Suzaku data and archival data from XMM-Newton
of the cluster of galaxies Sersic 159-03, which has a strong soft X-ray excess
emission component. The Suzaku observation confirms the presence of the soft
excess emission, but it does not confirm the presence of redshifted OVII lines
in the cluster. Radial profiles and 2D maps derived from XMM-Newton
observations show that the soft excess emission has a strong peak at the
position of the central cD galaxy and the maps do not show any significant
azimuthal variations. Although the soft excess emission can be fitted equally
well with both thermal and non-thermal models, its spatial distribution is
neither consistent with the models of intercluster warm-hot filaments, nor with
models of clumpy warm intracluster gas associated with infalling groups. Using
the data obtained by the XMM-Newton Reflection Grating Spectrometers we do not
confirm the presence of the warm gas in the cluster centre with the expected
properties assuming the soft excess is of thermal origin. The observed
properties of the soft excess emission are consistent with the non-thermal
interpretation. While the high density of relativistic electrons associated
with the peak of the soft emission in the cluster centre might have been
provided by an active galactic nucleus in the central cD galaxy, the underlying
population might have been accelerated in diffuse shocks.

We present the latest developments of the MadGraph/MadEvent Monte Carlo event
generator and several applications to hadron collider physics. In the current
version events at the parton, hadron and detector level can be generated
directly from a web interface, for arbitrary processes in the Standard Model
and in several physics scenarios beyond it (HEFT, MSSM, 2HDM). The most
important additions are: a new framework for implementing user-defined new
physics models; a standalone running mode for creating and testing matrix
elements; generation of events corresponding to different processes, such as
signal(s) and backgrounds, in the same run; two platforms for data analysis,
where events are accessible at the parton, hadron and detector level; and the
generation of inclusive multi-jet samples by combining parton-level events with
parton showers. To illustrate the new capabilities of the package some
applications to hadron collider physics are presented:
  1) Higgs search in pp \to H \to W^+W^-: signal and backgrounds.
  2) Higgs CP properties: pp \to H jj$in the HEFT.
  3) Spin of a new resonance from lepton angular distributions.
  4) Single-top and Higgs associated production in a generic 2HDM.
  5) Comparison of strong SUSY pair production at the SPS points.
  6) Inclusive W+jets matched samples: comparison with the Tevatron data.

In this work, we calculate the decay rates of the OZI-forbidden processes
$J/\psi(\Upsilon)\to \pi\pi, \rho\pi$ at the order of the leading-twist
distribution amplitude. The process of $J/\psi(\Upsilon)\to \pi^+ \pi^-$
violates isospin conservation and the amplitude is explicitly proportional to
the isospin violation factor $m_u-m_d$, our numerical results on their decay
rates are consistent with the data. The process $J/\psi(\Upsilon)\to \rho\pi$
violates the hadronic helicity conservation and should be suppressed, as
indicated in literature, its decay rate can only be proportional to $m_q^2$ at
the order of leading twist. Our theoretical evaluation confirms this statement
that the theoretical evaluation on $\Gamma(J/\psi(\Upsilon)\to \rho\pi)$ is
almost one order smaller than the data unless the model parameters take certain
extreme values. It may imply that the sizable branching ratio of
$J/\psi(\Upsilon)\to \rho\pi$ should be explained by either higher twist
contributions or other mechanisms.

In this work, we present phase-resolved measurements of stimulated emission in an optically pumped laser system. The problem we aim to solve is to investigate the dynamics of stimulated emission with high temporal resolution, which is crucial for understanding the underlying physical processes that govern laser operation. Stimulated emission is a fundamental phenomenon in laser science and technology, and its precise measurement is essential for the development of new laser sources and applications. 

Our methodology involves using a fast photo-detector to perform time-resolved measurements of the laser output signal, along with a phase-locked loop circuit to extract the phase information of the signal. This allows us to obtain phase-resolved data of the stimulated emission in a laser system, which provides insights into the dynamics of the laser cavity and the mode locking process. 

The motivation for this research stems from the importance of developing novel laser systems for applications ranging from material processing to bioimaging. By refining our understanding of the stimulated emission process, we can improve the efficiency and stability of laser sources, enabling better performance and wider application of laser technology. Our results provide valuable insights into the dynamics of stimulated emission, paving the way for the development of advanced laser systems.

We present a mathematically justifiable, computationally simple, sample
eigenvalue based procedure for estimating the number of high-dimensional
signals in white noise using relatively few samples. The main motivation for
considering a sample eigenvalue based scheme is the computational simplicity
and the robustness to eigenvector modelling errors which are can adversely
impact the performance of estimators that exploit information in the sample
eigenvectors.
  There is, however, a price we pay by discarding the information in the sample
eigenvectors; we highlight a fundamental asymptotic limit of sample eigenvalue
based detection of weak/closely spaced high-dimensional signals from a limited
sample size. This motivates our heuristic definition of the effective number of
identifiable signals which is equal to the number of "signal" eigenvalues of
the population covariance matrix which exceed the noise variance by a factor
strictly greater than 1+sqrt(Dimensionality of the system/Sample size). The
fundamental asymptotic limit brings into sharp focus why, when there are too
few samples available so that the effective number of signals is less than the
actual number of signals, underestimation of the model order is unavoidable (in
an asymptotic sense) when using any sample eigenvalue based detection scheme,
including the one proposed herein. The analysis reveals why adding more sensors
can only exacerbate the situation. Numerical simulations are used to
demonstrate that the proposed estimator consistently estimates the true number
of signals in the dimension fixed, large sample size limit and the effective
number of identifiable signals in the large dimension, large sample size limit.

In this work, we investigate the electrodynamics of the Josephson vortex lattice (JVL) in high-temperature superconductors (HTS). We aim to understand the behavior of JVLs under various external conditions, including magnetic fields and temperature fluctuations. Our study reveals that the JVLs in HTS exhibit rich and complex behaviors that cannot be explained solely by the conventional understanding of HTS. Specifically, we observe the emergence of novel vortex states and dynamical phenomena that are unique to JVLs in HTS.

To investigate these phenomena, we developed a theoretical framework based on the Ginzburg-Landau equations, which incorporate the effects of magnetic fields, temperature variations, and supercurrent flow. Our approach allows us to simulate various JVL configurations for different conditions and to study their dynamic behavior under the action of external perturbations.

Our results highlight the significant role of vortex-vortex interactions in the collective behavior of JVLs and their response to the applied magnetic fields, leading to the emergence of distinct vortex lattice structures. Moreover, we demonstrate the dependence of the JVL dynamics on the strength of the external magnetic field and the temperature of the system.

In summary, this work sheds light on the complex electrodynamics of JVLs in HTS, offering new insights into the behavior of these systems under various conditions. Our study contributes to the understanding of HTS materials and may provide new avenues for the development of novel technological applications.

In this paper, we present a watermark embedding and detection technique for digital images using the least significant bit (LSB) algorithm. The motivation for this research stems from the increasing need for secure and protected digital content, especially in fields such as copyright protection and data authentication. Our proposed technique involves embedding a watermark image into the lower bit planes of the host image, using a secret key to ensure its secure transmission. The detection process involves extracting the watermark from the watermarked image by analyzing its LSB values. The effectiveness of our approach is evaluated through various performance metrics such as peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR), structural similarity index (SSIM) and normalized correlation (NC). We also perform robustness analysis to verify the resilience of the method to common attacks such as image compression, filtering and cropping. We validate the robustness results using the normalized correlation index (NCI). Our experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves higher robustness and efficiency compared to existing techniques. This method can be applied to a variety of content, including images, videos, and audios, to provide secure and tamper-proof data protection.

Using high-resolution UV spectra of 16 low-z QSOs, we study the physical
conditions and statistics of O VI absorption in the IGM at z < 0.5. We identify
51 intervening (z_{abs} << z_{QSO}) O VI systems comprised of 77 individual
components, and we find 14 "proximate" systems (z_{abs} ~ z_{QSO}) containing
34 components. For intervening systems [components] with rest-frame equivalent
width W_{r} > 30 mA, the number of O VI absorbers per unit redshift dN/dz =
15.6(+2.9/-2.4) [21.0(+3.2/-2.8)], and this decreases to dN/dz = 0.9(+1.0/-0.5)
[0.3(+0.7/-0.3)] for W_{r} > 300 mA. The number per redshift increases steeply
as z_{abs} approaches z_{QSO}, and some proximate absorbers have substantially
lower H I/O VI ratios. The lower proximate ratios could be partially due to
ionization effects but also require higher metallicities. We find that 37% of
the intervening O VI absorbers have velocity centroids that are well-aligned
with corresponding H I absorption. If the O VI and the H I trace the same gas,
the relatively small differences in line widths imply the absorbers are cool
with T < 10^{5} K. Most of these well-aligned absorbers have the
characteristics of metal-enriched photoionized gas. However, the O VI in the
apparently simple and cold systems could be associated with a hot phase with T
~ 10^{5.5} K if the metallicity is high enough to cause the associated broad Ly
alpha absorption to be too weak to detect. We show that 53% of the intervening
O VI systems are complex multiphase absorbers that can accommodate both lower
metallicity collisionally-ionized gas with T > 10^{5} K and cold photoionzed
gas.

We present an exploration of the Peculiar Motions in the region of the Ursa Major Supercluster of galaxies. The motivation for this research stems from the desire to improve our understanding of the large-scale structure of the Universe. Specifically, we aimed to investigate deviations from the expected Hubble flow, to determine the velocity fields of galaxies, and to understand the dynamics of this particular region. We can conclusively state that Ursa Major contributes an overall motion of approximately 320 km/s towards the dipole and 70 km/s towards the great attractor. This work represents a significant milestone towards the understanding of the larger scale clustering of galaxies and their evolution over time. 

The study process involves the accumulation and curation of a comprehensive sample assembly of objects in Ursa Major. The selection criteria included distance from the Galactic equator, a neutral hydrogen flux density above 20 Jy, and radial velocity between 400 km/s and 1000 km/s. We used a comprehensive observation suite of the CO(1-0) spectral line which allowed for the gathering of highly accurate velocity values of galaxies in the sample while imposing a negligible effect on the overall mass. Our work contributes significantly to the shaping of our understanding of the distribution of matter in the Ursa Major Supercluster.

The tilt modulus of a defective Abrikosov vortex lattice pinned by material
line defects is computed using the boson analogy. It tends to infinity at long
wavelength, which yields a Bose glass state that is robust to the addition of
weak point-pinning centers, and which implies a restoring force per vortex line
for rigid translations about mechanical equilibrium that is independent of
magnetic field. It also indicates that the Bose glass state breaks into pieces
along the direction of the correlated pinning centers if the latter have finite
length. The critical current is predicted to crossover from two dimensional to
three dimensional behavior as a function of sample thickness along the
correlated pinning centers in such case. That crossover notably can occur at a
film thickness that is much larger than that expected from point pins of
comparable strength. The above is compared to the dependence on thickness shown
by the critical current in certain films of high-temperature superconductors
currently being developed for wire technology.

An impressive success of spintronic applications has been typically realized
in metal-based structures which utilize magnetoresistive effects for
substantial improvements in the performance of computer hard drives and
magnetic random access memories. Correspondingly, the theoretical understanding
of spin-polarized transport is usually limited to a metallic regime in a linear
response, which, while providing a good description for data storage and
magnetic memory devices, is not sufficient for signal processing and digital
logic. In contrast, much less is known about possible applications of
semiconductor-based spintronics and spin-polarized transport in related
structures which could utilize strong intrinsic nonlinearities in
current-voltage characteristics to implement spin-based logic. Here we discuss
the challenges for realizing a particular class of structures in semiconductor
spintronics: our proposal for bipolar spintronic devices in which carriers of
both polarities (electrons and holes) contribute to spin-charge coupling. We
formulate the theoretical framework for bipolar spin-polarized transport, and
describe several novel effects in two- and three-terminal structures which
arise from the interplay between nonequilibrium spin and equilibrium
magnetization.

In this study, we investigate the possible association between extragalactic radio sources and the WMAP cold spot, a puzzling feature in the cosmic microwave background radiation. The motivation for this research stems from the desire to better understand the origin of the WMAP cold spot, which is inconsistent with the standard cosmological model and could potentially challenge our current understanding of the universe. 

To address this problem, we conducted a statistical analysis of the positions of extragalactic radio sources and compared them with the location of the WMAP cold spot. Our results reveal a significant excess of radio sources at the position of the cold spot, indicating a possible connection between these two phenomena. 

Furthermore, we performed a detailed analysis of the properties of the identified radio sources, including their brightness and redshift, and found that the excess is mostly driven by a population of faint radio sources at high redshifts. These results suggest that the WMAP cold spot might be caused by large-scale structures in the universe, such as cosmic voids or supervoids, which can produce a lack of hot gas and hence a deficit in the CMB temperature.

Overall, our study provides new insights into the origin of the WMAP cold spot and highlights the potential of extragalactic radio sources as a valuable tracer of large-scale structures in the universe.

In this research, our motivation was to investigate the possibility of obtaining the spacetime metric from cosmological observations. The spacetime metric is an essential concept in General Relativity, which describes the curvature of spacetime caused by the presence of matter and energy. Despite its significant role in cosmology, the spacetime metric is technically challenging to measure. Our key idea was to explore if the metric could be inferred from observable quantities such as the cosmic microwave background radiation, matter distribution, and gravitational lensing.

To accomplish this goal, we combined various cosmological datasets and used statistical techniques to reconstruct the spacetime metric. We employed the Bayesian framework to derive probabilistic results and quantified uncertainties and confidence intervals. Our methodology also included testing the robustness of our findings against different assumptions made in the analysis.

Our results indicated that it was possible to obtain the spacetime metric using this approach. We found a high level of concordance between the metric inferred from our analysis and the one predicted by General Relativity. Our findings open promising avenues for future applications in cosmology and astrophysics.

In conclusion, our work demonstrates a novel and rigorous approach to obtain the spacetime metric from cosmological observations. We expect that our methodology and results will contribute to future research in cosmology and improve our understanding of the nature of the universe.

In this work, we investigate the magnetic, magneto-thermal, and magneto-transport properties of SmMn2Si2-xGex compounds to gain insights into their potential applications in spintronics and magnetic refrigeration. The substitution of Si by Ge in SmMn2Si2-xGex is expected to alter its electronic structure, leading to changes in its magnetic and transport properties. 

Our experiments show that with an increase in Ge substitution concentration, the temperature dependence of magnetization shifts towards lower temperatures, indicating a reduction in the Curie temperature. We also observed an increase in the magneto-thermal response of the compounds, which is related to the magnetic entropy change at the Curie temperature. Moreover, the electrical resistivity decreases with increasing substitution, indicating an enhancement in the metallic character of the compounds.

These results demonstrate that SmMn2Si2-xGex compounds exhibit interesting magneto-thermal and magneto-transport properties. Furthermore, the reduction in the Curie temperature and enhancement of electrical conductivity provide potential avenues for the development of spintronic and magnetic refrigeration applications. This research provides insights into tailoring the properties of SmMn2Si2-xGex compounds by Ge substitution for their potential use in future spintronics and magnetic cooling technologies.

In this study, we investigated the longitudinal impedance and wake from X-ray free-electron laser (XFEL) undulators and their impact on current-enhanced self-amplified spontaneous emission (SASE) schemes. The motivation for this research stemmed from the desire to optimize the performance of XFELs and improve the quality of the X-ray pulses they generate.

In particular, we focused on the effects of the longitudinal impedance of the vacuum chamber and the wakefields generated by the charged particles in the undulator on the XFEL beam. We used a combination of theory, simulations, and experiments to analyze these effects and develop strategies for mitigating them.

Our results show that the longitudinal impedance and wakefields have significant effects on the beam quality and the generation of short X-ray pulses in current-enhanced SASE schemes. Specifically, we found that they can lead to beam degradation, bunch lengthening, and instabilities. However, we also demonstrated that these effects can be mitigated by careful design of the vacuum chamber and undulator and by tuning the XFEL beam parameters.

Overall, our study provides important insights into the importance of considering the longitudinal impedance and wake from XFEL undulators in the design and operation of these advanced X-ray sources.

This White Paper briefly reviews the present status of the muon (g-2)
experiment and the physics motivation for a new effort. The present comparison
between experiment and theory indicates a tantalizing $3.4 \sigma$ deviation.
An improvement in precision on this comparison by a factor of 2--with the
central value remaining unchanged--will exceed the ``discovery'' threshold,
with a sensitivity above $6 \sigma$. The 2.5-fold reduction improvement goal of
the new Brookhaven E969 experiment, along with continued steady reduction of
the standard model theory uncertainty, will achieve this more definitive test.
  Already, the (g-2) result is arguably the most compelling indicator of
physics beyond the standard model and, at the very least, it represents a major
constraint for speculative new theories such as supersymmetry or extra
dimensions. In this report, we summarize the present experimental status and
provide an up-to-date accounting of the standard model theory, including the
expectations for improvement in the hadronic contributions, which dominate the
overall uncertainty. Our primary focus is on the physics case that motivates
improved experimental and theoretical efforts. Accordingly, we give examples of
specific new-physics implications in the context of direct searches at the LHC
as well as general arguments about the role of an improved (g-2) measurement. A
brief summary of the plans for an upgraded effort complete the report.

We investigate the Dbar-N interaction at low energies using a meson-exchange
model supplemented with a short-distance contribution from one-gluon-exchange.
The model is developed in close analogy to the meson-exchange KN interaction of
the Juelich group utilizing SU(4) symmetry constraints. The main ingredients of
the interaction are provided by vector meson (rho, omega) exchange and
higher-order box diagrams involving D*N, D\Delta, and D*\Delta intermediate
states. The short range part is assumed to receive additional contributions
from genuine quark-gluon processes. The predicted cross sections for Dbar-N for
excess energies up to 150 MeV are of the same order of magnitude as those for
KN but with average values of around 20 mb, roughly a factor two larger than
for the latter system. It is found that the omega-exchange plays a very
important role. Its interference pattern with the rho-exchange, which is
basically fixed by the assumed SU(4) symmetry, clearly determines the
qualitative features of the Dbar-N interaction -- very similiar to what happens
also for the KN system.

In this paper, we investigate the isophotal structure and dust distribution in a sample of radio-loud elliptical galaxies. Our methodology involves the analysis of optical images obtained from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) and the inclusion of radio data from the Faint Images of the Radio Sky at Twenty-Centimeters (FIRST) survey. We present the results of our analysis based on the dust morphology, dust-to-star ratio, and radio properties of the sample galaxies. 

Our findings reveal that the radio-loud elliptical galaxies in our sample exhibit complex dust morphologies that differ from those observed in radio-quiet ellipticals. The observed dust-to-star ratios are relatively low, indicating that the dust in these galaxies is not strongly associated with the stellar populations. Additionally, we find that the majority of the sample galaxies have extended radio emission, suggesting the presence of active galactic nuclei (AGN). 

The primary problem our work attempts to solve is understanding the relationship between the AGN activity and the dust distribution in radio-loud elliptical galaxies. This study contributes to a better understanding of the complex interplay between the AGN, the dust, and the star formation in the radio-loud elliptical galaxies. The results obtained from our analysis can also aid in constraining evolutionary models for these galaxies and provide essential insights into their formation and evolution.

By combining high-resolution (HST-WFPC2) and wide-field ground based (2.2m
ESO-WFI) and space (GALEX) observations, we have collected a multi-wavelength
photometric data base (ranging from the far UV to the near infrared) of the
galactic globular cluster NGC1904 (M79). The sample covers the entire cluster
extension, from the very central regions up to the tidal radius. In the present
paper such a data set is used to study the BSS population and its radial
distribution. A total number of 39 bright ($m_{218}\le 19.5$) BSS has been
detected, and they have been found to be highly segregated in the cluster core.
No significant upturn in the BSS frequency has been observed in the outskirts
of NGC 1904, in contrast to other clusters (M 3, 47 Tuc, NGC 6752, M 5) studied
with the same technique. Such evidences, coupled with the large radius of
avoidance estimated for NGC 1904 ($r_{avoid}\sim 30$ core radii), indicate that
the vast majority of the cluster heavy stars (binaries) has already sunk to the
core. Accordingly, extensive dynamical simulations suggest that BSS formed by
mass transfer activity in primordial binaries evolving in isolation in the
cluster outskirts represent only a negligible (0--10%) fraction of the overall
population.

We identify plain evaporation of ions as the fundamental loss mechanism out
of a multipole ion trap. Using thermalized negative Cl- ions we find that the
evaporative loss rate is proportional to a Boltzmann factor. This thermodynamic
description sheds new light on the dynamics of particles in time-varying
confining potentials. It specifically allows us to extract the effective depth
of the ion trap as the activation energy for evaporation. As a function of the
rf amplitude we find two distinct regimes related to the stability of motion of
the trapped ions. For low amplitudes the entire trap allows for stable motion
and the trap depth increases with the rf field. For larger rf amplitudes,
however, rapid energy transfer from the field to the ion motion can occur at
large trap radii, which leads to a reduction of the effective trapping volume.
In this regime the trap depth decreases again with increasing rf amplitude. We
give an analytical parameterization of the trap depth for various multipole
traps that allows predictions of the most favorable trapping conditions.

In this work, we investigate the spheroidal and torsional modes of quasistatic shear oscillations in solid globe models of nuclear physics and pulsar astrophysics. The main goal of this research is to expand our understanding of the seismic properties of compact stars. Specifically, we aim to shed light on how differences in the internal structure and composition of such stars might impact their observed oscillation patterns.

To achieve this, we employ a combination of analytical techniques and numerical simulations. Our analysis is grounded in the equations governing linearized perturbations of the fluid and solid components of the star, which we solve using appropriate boundary conditions. In particular, we explore the effects of different density profiles, equation of state models, and geometries on the excitation and damping of these oscillations.

Our findings suggest that the presence of a solid crust, as well as variations in the core composition, can significantly affect the evolution of spheroidal and torsional modes in compact stars. These results have far-reaching implications for our ability to accurately infer the properties of pulsars and other compact objects based on their observed oscillations. Overall, our work contributes to a deeper understanding of the rich dynamical behavior of compact stars and their possible astrophysical origins.

In this study, we investigated the dominant aerosol processes during high-pollution episodes over Greater Tokyo. This type of pollution is a significant concern as it poses a significant threat to human health and the environment. Understanding the dominant processes involved in aerosol formation can assist in the development of effective strategies to mitigate this issue.

We carried out measurements of aerosols, their precursors, and meteorological parameters, using a mobile survey vehicle and a fixed-site monitoring station. Our results showed that during high-pollution episodes, the dominant aerosol processes were secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation, particle resuspension, and long-range transport.

Our findings suggest that SOA formation contributed a significant fraction of aerosols during high-pollution episodes, indicating a need for more effective control of volatile organic compound emissions. Particle resuspension played a significant role in the formation of coarse particulate matter, indicating the need for improved street cleaning practices. Lastly, long-range transport from outside the region was found to contribute a small but significant fraction to the local pollution levels.

Overall, this study provides important insights into the dominant aerosol processes involved in high-pollution episodes over Greater Tokyo, highlighting the need for targeted and effective pollution control strategies.

In this study, we investigate the interplay between thermal percolation and jamming upon dimer adsorption on binary alloys. The motivation for this research stems from the need to understand the complex mechanisms involved in the formation of nanostructures on alloy surfaces. We aim to shed light on how the adsorption of dimers on binary alloys can lead to the formation of jammed islands, and how this process is influenced by thermal fluctuations in the system. 

To achieve this, we employ Monte Carlo simulations to investigate the dynamics of dimers on the surface of binary alloys. Our results reveal that the thermal percolation threshold is lower for the system with higher surface adatom mobility, leading to greater island connectivity. Furthermore, we find that the morphology of the islands is significantly affected by the concentration of dimers on the surface. 

This work extends the understanding of the interplay between thermal percolation and jamming on surfaces of binary alloys in a new direction. The results obtained may contribute to the development of new strategies for controlling the formation of nanostructures on alloy surfaces at the atomic scale.

In this study, we aimed to investigate the role of beta-Dystroglycan processing in the anchorage of Utrophin/DP116 in normal and MDX mouse Schwann cell membrane. Dystroglycan is a transmembrane protein that plays a critical role in linking the cytoskeleton to the extracellular matrix. Its cleavage into alpha-Dystroglycan and beta-Dystroglycan has been linked to several diseases, including muscular dystrophy. Utrophin/DP116 is a dystrophin homologue that has been reported to act as a substitute for dystrophin in dystrophic muscles. However, the role of Utrophin/DP116 in Schwann cells is not well understood. Using western blot analysis and immunocytochemistry, we demonstrated that the processing of beta-Dystroglycan significantly affected the anchorage of Utrophin/DP116 in the Schwann cell membrane. Furthermore, we found that the processing of beta-Dystroglycan was altered in MDX mouse Schwann cells compared to normal cells, which might explain the reduced Utrophin/DP116 anchorage in MDX mice. Our results suggest that the processing of beta-Dystroglycan plays an essential role in the stability of the Schwann cell membrane and could be a potential target for therapeutic interventions in dystrophic neuropathies.

We present the first measurement of the AGN fraction in high-redshift
clusters of galaxies (z~0.6) with spectroscopy of one cluster and archival data
for three additional clusters. We identify 8 AGN in all four of these clusters
from the Chandra data, which are sensitive to AGN with hard X-ray (2-10keV)
luminosity L_{X,H} > 10^43 erg/s in host galaxies more luminous than a rest
frame M_R < -20 mag. This stands in sharp contrast to the one AGN with L_{X,H}
> 10^43 erg/s we discovered in our earlier study of eight low-redshift clusters
with z=0.06-0.31 (average z~0.2). Three of the four high-redshift cluster
datasets are sensitive to nearly L_{X,H} > 10^42 erg/s and we identify seven
AGN above this luminosity limit, compared to two in eight, low-redshift
clusters. Based on membership estimates for each cluster, we determine that the
AGN fraction at z~0.6 is f_A(L_X>10^42;M_R<-20) = 0.028 (+0.019/-0.012) and
f_A(L_X>10^43;M_R<-20) = 0.020 (+0.012/-0.008). These values are approximately
a factor of 20 greater than the AGN fractions in lower-redshift (average z~0.2)
clusters of galaxies and represent a substantial increase over the factors of
1.5 and 3.3 increase, respectively, in the measured space density evolution of
the hard X-ray luminosity function over this redshift range. Potential
systematic errors would only increase the significance of our result. The
cluster AGN fraction increases more rapidly with redshift than the field and
the increase in cluster AGN indicates the presence of an AGN Butcher-Oemler
Effect.

A new kinematic and dynamic study of the halo of the giant elliptical galaxy,
NGC 5128, is presented. From a spectroscopically confirmed sample of 340
globular clusters and 780 planetary nebulae, the rotation amplitude, rotation
axis, velocity dispersion, and the total dynamical mass are determined for the
halo of NGC 5128. The globular cluster kinematics were searched for both radial
dependence and metallicity dependence by subdividing the globular cluster
sample into 158 metal-rich ([Fe/H] > -1.0) and 178 metal-poor ([Fe/H] < -1.0)
globular clusters. Our results show the kinematics of the metal-rich and
metal-poor subpopulations are quite similar. The kinematics are compared to the
planetary nebula population where differences are apparent in the outer regions
of the halo. The total mass of NGC 5128 is found using the Tracer Mass
estimator (Evans et al. 2003), to determine the mass supported by internal
random motions, and the spherical component of the Jeans equation to determine
the mass supported by rotation. We find a total mass of (1.0+/-0.2) x 10^(12)
Msun from the planetary nebulae data out to a projected radius of 90 kpc and
(1.3+/-0.5) x 10^(12) Msun from the globular clusters out to a projected radius
of 50 kpc. Lastly, we present a new and homogeneous catalog of known globular
clusters in NGC 5128. This catalog combines all previous definitive cluster
identifications from radial velocity studies and HST imaging studies, as well
as 80 new globular clusters from a study of M.A. Beasley et al. (2007, in
preparation).

We investigate the orbit-crossing time (T_c) of protoplanet systems both with
and without a gas-disk background. The protoplanets are initially with equal
masses and separation (EMS systems) scaled by their mutual Hill's radii. In a
gas-free environment, we find log (T_c/yr) = A+B \log (k_0/2.3). Through a
simple analytical approach, we demonstrate that the evolution of the velocity
dispersion in an EMS system follows a random walk. The stochastic nature of
random-walk diffusion leads to (i) an increasing average eccentricity <e> ~
t^1/2, where t is the time; (ii) Rayleigh-distributed eccentricities
(P(e,t)=e/\sigma^2 \exp(-e^2/(2\sigma^2)) of the protoplanets; (iii) a
power-law dependence of T_c on planetary separation. As evidence for the
chaotic diffusion, the observed eccentricities of known extra solar planets can
be approximated by a Rayleigh distribution. We evaluate the isolation masses of
the embryos, which determine the probability of gas giant formation, as a
function of the dust and gas surface densities.

In this paper, we present a dynamical computation of the coefficients of electroweak chiral Lagrangian (EWCL) in the context of one-doublet and topcolor-assisted technicolor (TC2) models. We use the operator product expansion (OPE) to evaluate the coefficients in terms of the vacuum expectation values (VEVs) of the composite operators. Our approach combines chiral perturbation theory (ChPT) for the low-energy dynamics and heavy quark effective theory (HQET) for the heavy quark contributions. We calculate the leading-order contributions and work with the two-loop level in ChPT. We also take into account the mass splittings between the top quark and the other quarks. Our results show that the EWCL coefficients are sensitive to the composite operator VEVs, and they can provide information about the dynamics of the strongly interacting sector. 

The motivation for this study is to explore the properties of the electroweak symmetry breaking sector and its relationship with the strong interaction sector in the standard model. In particular, we investigate the validity of the one-doublet and topcolor-assisted technicolor models and their predictions for the electroweak chiral Lagrangian coefficients. Our approach provides a rigorous method for evaluating the coefficients and can be extended to other models with similar composite operators. Our results demonstrate the importance of the EWCL coefficients as probes of the underlying dynamics of the electroweak symmetry breaking sector.

The protoplanetary disk that surrounded the early Sun was a crucial environment where planet formation took place. Jupiter and Saturn were the most massive planets in the disk that affected the dynamics of the gas and the dust in the environment. This study aimed to understand the role of Jupiter and Saturn in shaping the protoplanetary disk and the implications of their dynamics on planet formation. 

We used numerical simulations to model the interactions between Jupiter, Saturn, and the disk of gas and dust. The simulations were based on the hydrodynamic equations and the physical properties of the disk, such as viscosity, temperature, and pressure. Our simulations revealed that Jupiter and Saturn had a significant impact on the disk's structure, affecting its density, temperature, and flow patterns. Additionally, Jupiter and Saturn also influenced the formation of planetesimals, which are the building blocks of planets. 

Our findings shed light on the early stages of planet formation and the mechanisms that shaped the Solar System. Specifically, Jupiter's and Saturn's positions and their migration had an essential role in the formation of the terrestrial planets and the asteroid belt. Our study provides a fundamental insight into the dynamics of protoplanetary disks and planet formation that could be applied to other star systems.

We present phase-resolved spectroscopy of the short period cataclysmic
variable WZ Sge obtained with the Hubble Space Telescope. We were able to
resolve the orbital motion of a number of absorption lines that likely probe
the environment near the accreting white dwarf. The radial velocities derived
from simultaneous fits to 13 absorption lines indicate an orbital velocity
semi-amplitude of K_UV = 47 +/- 3 km/s. However, we find that the phase zero is
offset from the white dwarf ephemeris by +0.1. Our offset and velocity
amplitude are very similar to constraints derived from optical emission lines
from the quiescent accretion disk, despite the fact that we are probing
material much closer to the primary. If we associate the UV amplitude with K_1,
our dynamical constraints together with the K_2 estimates from Steeghs et al.
(2001) and the known binary inclination of i=77+/-2 imply 0.88<M_1<1.53 M_sun,
0.078 < M_2 < 0.13 M_sun and 0.075<q=M_2/M_1<0.101. If we interpret the mean
velocity of the UV lines (-16+/-4 km/s) as being due to the gravitational
red-shift caused in the high-g environment near the white dwarf, we find
v_grav=56+/-5 km/s which provides an independent estimate on the mass of the
primary of M_1=0.85+/-0.04 M_sun when coupled with a mass-radius relation. Our
primary mass estimates are in excellent agreement and are also self-consistent
with spectrophotometric fits to the UV fluxes despite the observed phase
offset. It is at this point unclear what causes the observed phase-offset in
the UV spectra and by how much it distorts the radial velocity signature from
the underlying white dwarf.

In this study, we present a successive sub-grouping method for multiple sequence alignments (MSAs) as an efficient tool to analyze sequence data. MSAs are widely used in various research fields, including evolutionary biology, comparative genomics, and structural biology. However, accurate analysis of MSAs remains challenging because of their size and complexity. In particular, MSAs can contain large numbers of sequences with varying lengths, structures, and similarities, which makes it difficult to apply existing methods to optimize the alignment.

Our proposed method involves iteratively removing sequences that have minimal similarities with the remaining sequences in the alignment until all sequences have been clustered into subgroups with a pre-defined similarity threshold. The key idea of our method is rooted in the observation that the accuracy of MSAs generally improves when sequences with low similarity are separated from highly similar ones. Profiling the sequence alignment this way improves computational efficiency, increases the accuracy of multiple sequence alignment, and makes the method applicable to a wide range of sequence datasets.

We tested our successive sub-grouping algorithm on various sequence datasets and compared the results to existing multiple sequence alignment algorithms, demonstrating that our method generates highly accurate MSAs in a fraction of the time compared to other methods. Consequently, our method offers an efficient tool for analyzing large and complex sequence datasets in a variety of research fields.

The dynamics of many socioeconomic systems is determined by the decision
making process of agents. The decision process depends on agent's
characteristics, such as preferences, risk aversion, behavioral biases, etc..
In addition, in some systems the size of agents can be highly heterogeneous
leading to very different impacts of agents on the system dynamics. The large
size of some agents poses challenging problems to agents who want to control
their impact, either by forcing the system in a given direction or by hiding
their intentionality. Here we consider the financial market as a model system,
and we study empirically how agents strategically adjust the properties of
large orders in order to meet their preference and minimize their impact. We
quantify this strategic behavior by detecting scaling relations of allometric
nature between the variables characterizing the trading activity of different
institutions. We observe power law distributions in the investment time
horizon, in the number of transactions needed to execute a large order and in
the traded value exchanged by large institutions and we show that heterogeneity
of agents is a key ingredient for the emergence of some aggregate properties
characterizing this complex system.

The very nature of the solar chromosphere, its structuring and dynamics,
remains far from being properly understood, in spite of intensive research.
Here we point out the potential of chromospheric observations at millimeter
wavelengths to resolve this long-standing problem. Computations carried out
with a sophisticated dynamic model of the solar chromosphere due to Carlsson
and Stein demonstrate that millimeter emission is extremely sensitive to
dynamic processes in the chromosphere and the appropriate wavelengths to look
for dynamic signatures are in the range 0.8-5.0 mm. The model also suggests
that high resolution observations at mm wavelengths, as will be provided by
ALMA, will have the unique property of reacting to both the hot and the cool
gas, and thus will have the potential of distinguishing between rival models of
the solar atmosphere. Thus, initial results obtained from the observations of
the quiet Sun at 3.5 mm with the BIMA array (resolution of 12 arcsec) reveal
significant oscillations with amplitudes of 50-150 K and frequencies of 1.5-8
mHz with a tendency toward short-period oscillations in internetwork and longer
periods in network regions. However higher spatial resolution, such as that
provided by ALMA, is required for a clean separation between the features
within the solar atmosphere and for an adequate comparison with the output of
the comprehensive dynamic simulations.

In this work, we investigate the cohomology classes of central type groups that are both non-degenerate and bijective. Our goal is to better understand the structure of these types of groups and the properties of their cohomology classes. Our approach involves a combination of algebraic and geometric methods, including the use of cohomology theory and group cohomology.

Our main result is the characterization of central type groups that admit non-degenerate and bijective cohomology classes, which we prove through a careful analysis of the algebraic and geometric properties of these groups. Specifically, we show that such groups have a strong connection between their algebraic structure and their geometry, which allows us to identify and classify their cohomology classes.

Our work addresses an important problem in the field of algebraic topology, which is to understand the cohomology classes of groups in a rigorous and systematic way. By characterizing central type groups with non-degenerate and bijective cohomology classes, we provide a deeper insight into the properties of these groups and their role in algebraic topology. Our work opens the way to a variety of future research directions, including the study of higher-dimensional cohomology classes and the construction of new families of groups with interesting cohomology properties.

Dynamic properties of molecular motors that fuel their motion by actively
interacting with underlying molecular tracks are studied theoretically via
discrete-state stochastic ``burnt-bridge'' models. The transport of the
particles is viewed as an effective diffusion along one-dimensional lattices
with periodically distributed weak links. When an unbiased random walker passes
the weak link it can be destroyed (``burned'') with probability p, providing a
bias in the motion of the molecular motor. A new theoretical approach that
allows one to calculate exactly all dynamic properties of motor proteins, such
as velocity and dispersion, at general conditions is presented. It is found
that dispersion is a decreasing function of the concentration of bridges, while
the dependence of dispersion on the burning probability is more complex. Our
calculations also show a gap in dispersion for very low concentrations of weak
links which indicates a dynamic phase transition between unbiased and biased
diffusion regimes. Theoretical findings are supported by Monte Carlo computer
simulations.

In this paper, we investigate the implications of the Bianchi model of anisotropic cosmology for the observed Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) polarization. Our research is motivated by the persistent CMB anomalies that have been observed and remain unexplained by the standard cosmological model. We aim to study whether an anisotropic model, specifically the Bianchi model, can explain these anomalies.

To achieve this goal, we employ the formalism of dynamical systems to analyze the behavior of the model's dynamics. We analyze the behavior of the CMB polarization anisotropy for different orientations of the Bianchi model, incorporating the most recent Planck data to obtain a detailed understanding of the various parameters involved.

We find that the Bianchi model predicts polarization anisotropies that fit the observed data better than the isotropic case. Additionally, we show that the Bianchi models with the most acceptable levels of anisotropy require a non-zero rotation of the observer's frame, which has significant implications for the interpretation of observations.

Our work suggests that anisotropic Bianchi models may provide a viable alternative to the standard cosmological model for explaining the unexplained CMB anomalies, although more investigation is warranted.

Performance of object-oriented database systems (OODBs) is still an issue to
both designers and users nowadays. The aim of this paper is to propose a
generic discrete-event random simulation model, called VOODB, in order to
evaluate the performances of OODBs in general, and the performances of
optimization methods like clustering in particular. Such optimization methods
undoubtedly improve the performances of OODBs. Yet, they also always induce
some kind of overhead for the system. Therefore, it is important to evaluate
their exact impact on the overall performances. VOODB has been designed as a
generic discrete-event random simulation model by putting to use a modelling
approach, and has been validated by simulating the behavior of the O2 OODB and
the Texas persistent object store. Since our final objective is to compare
object clustering algorithms, some experiments have also been conducted on the
DSTC clustering technique, which is implemented in Texas. To validate VOODB,
performance results obtained by simulation for a given experiment have been
compared to the results obtained by benchmarking the real systems in the same
conditions. Benchmarking and simulation performance evaluations have been
observed to be consistent, so it appears that simulation can be a reliable
approach to evaluate the performances of OODBs.

We investigate the embedding of brane anti-brane inflation into a concrete
type IIB string theory compactification with all moduli fixed. Specifically, we
are considering a D3-brane, whose position represents the inflaton $\phi$, in a
warped conifold throat in the presence of supersymmetrically embedded D7-branes
and an anti D3-brane localized at the tip of the warped conifold cone. After
presenting the moduli stabilization analysis for a general D7-brane embedding,
we concentrate on two explicit models, the Ouyang and the Kuperstein
embeddings. We analyze whether the forces, induced by moduli stabilization and
acting on the D3-brane, might cancel by fine-tuning such as to leave us with
the original Coulomb attraction of the anti D3-brane as the driving force for
inflation. For a large class of D7-brane embeddings we obtain a negative
result. Cancelations are possible only for very small intervals of $\phi$
around an inflection point but not globally. For the most part of its motion
the inflaton then feels a steep, non slow-roll potential. We study the
inflationary dynamics induced by this potential.

In this study, we aim to investigate the luminous red galaxy (LRG) population in clusters at redshifts $0.2\leq z \leq 0.6$. The motivation behind this research stems from the need to understand the evolution of galaxy populations in clusters over cosmic time. LRGs are a particularly interesting subset of galaxies given their high stellar masses and strong clustering bias. By understanding the properties and behavior of LRGs in clusters at different redshifts, we can gain insight into how the cluster environment affects galaxy evolution. 

We used data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) and the Baryon Oscillation Spectroscopic Survey (BOSS) to identify galaxy clusters in the target redshift range and to select samples of LRGs and other galaxies in these clusters. We then analyzed the clustering properties of these samples, using a two-point correlation function. 

Our results indicate that LRGs in clusters at $0.2\leq z\leq 0.6$ are more strongly clustered than other galaxy types in the same clusters. This finding is consistent with previous studies and suggests that LRGs are more likely to reside in the centers of clusters. However, we also find that the clustering bias of LRGs in these clusters is weaker than that of LRGs in nearby clusters. This may be due to environmental effects on the evolution of LRGs in cluster environments at different redshifts. 

Overall, our study provides insight into the evolution of LRG populations in clusters over cosmic time and highlights the importance of investigating the clustering properties of galaxy populations in different environments.

In this work, we investigate the advantages of considering four-dimensional composite Higgs models over the commonly studied three-dimensional ones. The key idea is to assume that the Higgs particle is a composite object, which has been formed as a bound state of a fundamental particle and a new strong interaction. Composite Higgs models are a promising alternative to the Standard Model, and they can account for the origin of the Higgs mass without introducing additional particles. 

Our methodology consists of a detailed analysis of the effects of introducing an extra dimension, which can modify the properties of the heavy spin-1 particles, called vector resonances, that appear in composite Higgs models. We show that the inclusion of a fourth dimension can result in a richer phenomenology, with the possibility of new resonance structures and different coupling patterns among the particles. Additionally, we study the consequence of these additional resonances on the Higgs production and their potential to be observed in the Large Hadron Collider.

The problem that our work attempts to solve is to improve the predictive power of composite Higgs models while maintaining their simplicity and elegance. Our motivation for the research is to search for potential signals of new physics beyond the Standard Model that can be observed in present and future experiments. Overall, our work suggests that four-dimensional composite Higgs models offer a fascinating perspective on the nature of the Higgs boson and can have a broad impact on particle physics.

Recent data gathered and triggered by the SWIFT satellite have greatly
improved our knowledge of long-duration gamma ray bursts (GRBs) and X-ray
flashes (XRFs). This is particularly the case for the X-ray data at all times.
We show that the entire X-ray observations are in excellent agreement with the
predictions of the `cannonball' model of GRBs and XRFs, which are based on
simple physics and were published long before the launch of SWIFT. Two
mechanisms underlie these predictions: inverse Compton scattering and
synchrotron radiation, generally dominant at early and late times,
respectively. The former mechanism provides a unified description of the
gamma-ray peaks, X-ray flares and even the optical `humps' seen in some
favourable cases; i.e. their very different durations, fluxes and peak-times
are related precisely as predicted. The observed smooth or bumpy fast decay of
the X-ray light curve is correctly described case-by-case, in minute detail.
The `canonical' X-ray plateau, as well as the subsequent gradual steepening of
the afterglow to an asymptotic power-law decay, are as foretold. So are the
chromatic and achromatic properties of the light-curves.

We present new observational results obtained for the Galactic non-thermal
radio source G328.4+0.2 to determine both if this source is a pulsar wind
nebula or supernova remnant, and in either case, the physical properties of
this source. Using X-ray data obtained by XMM, we confirm that the X-ray
emission from this source is heavily absorbed and has a spectrum best fit by a
power law model of photon index=2 with no evidence for a thermal component, the
X-ray emission from G328.4+0.2 comes from a region significantly smaller than
the radio emission, and that the X-ray and radio emission are significantly
offset from each other. We also present the results of a new high resolution (7
arcseconds) 1.4 GHz image of G328.4+0.2 obtained using the Australia Telescope
Compact Array, and a deep search for radio pulsations using the Parkes Radio
Telescope. We find that the radio emission has a flat spectrum, though some
areas along the eastern edge of G328.4+0.2 have a steeper radio spectral index
of ~-0.3. Additionally, we obtain a luminosity limit of the central pulsar of
L_{1400} < 30 mJy kpc^2, assuming a distance of 17 kpc. In light of these
observational results, we test if G328.4+0.2 is a pulsar wind nebula (PWN) or a
large PWN inside a supernova remnant (SNR) using a simple hydrodynamic model
for the evolution of a PWN inside a SNR. As a result of this analysis, we
conclude that G328.4+0.2 is a young (< 10000 years old) pulsar wind nebula
formed by a low magnetic field (<10^12 G) neutron star born spinning rapidly
(<10 ms) expanding into an undetected SNR formed by an energetic (>10^51 ergs),
low ejecta mass (M < 5 Solar Masses) supernova explosion which occurred in a
low density (n~0.03 cm^{-3}) environment.

We present a new approach to derive irreducible metric variations of the action terms associated with the sixth-order gravity theory. Our method aims to obtain the approximated stress-energy tensor of the theory by using the metric variations. 

We start by constructing the most general polynomial action of sixth-order gravity that respects the field equations and has a well-defined variational principle. We then consider variations of the action with respect to the metric and obtain a set of equations that need to be solved to determine the irreducible components of the metric variations. We use a decomposition of the metric that naturally emerges from the analysis of the field equations to simplify the computations. 

Our approach differs from previous works in that we obtain a closed-form expression for the metric variations, which allows us to derive the approximated stress-energy tensor of the theory without resorting to numerical simulations. This result represents a significant advancement towards the understanding of the properties of sixth-order gravity and its connection with the classical theories of gravitation. 

Our motivation for this work is to shed light on the properties of higher-order gravity and to provide a tool for the analysis of astrophysical systems that are subject to strong gravitational fields. The method presented here can be applied to study a variety of problems in gravitational physics, such as black hole thermodynamics and the dynamics of compact stars.

In this study, we investigate the intrinsic fraction of Broad Absorption Line Quasars (BALQSOs), which are a subclass of active galactic nuclei that exhibit broad, blueshifted absorption lines in their spectra. The current understanding is that BALQSOs are a rare type of quasar, but there is evidence to suggest that this rarity may be an observational bias caused by the difficulty in identifying them. Our study uses data from the Two-Micron All-Sky Survey (2MASS) to identify a large sample of BALQSOs that have previously gone undetected or misclassified due to biases in previous surveys.

We analyze the 2MASS data and find that the intrinsic fraction of BALQSOs is much higher than previously thought, with around 30% of quasars exhibiting broad absorption lines. This result significantly changes our understanding of the frequency of BALQSOs and has important implications for models of active galactic nuclei and their evolution.

Our research is motivated by the need to better understand the properties and incidence of different types of quasars, which are among the most luminous and distant objects in the universe. Accurate measurements of the intrinsic fraction of BALQSOs are essential for developing a comprehensive picture of the evolution and behavior of these objects and will inform future studies of the quasar population as a whole.

In this study, we investigated the presence of black-body components in the spectra of Gamma-Ray Bursts (GRBs) using a comprehensive analysis of observational data from the Burst and Transient Source Experiment (BATSE). The GRBs are powerful astronomical phenomena, which signal the catastrophic destruction of massive stars. Our study aimed to understand the nature of the emission mechanisms in GRBs by analyzing the spectra and identifying the presence of black-body radiation. To achieve this, we employed statistical analysis techniques and generated a large data set that was analyzed using various statistical models.

Our work attempts to solve one of the major questions in the field of astrophysics - the origin of the relativistic outflows and their connection with the observed spectra of GRBs. The issue of whether the emission is non-thermal or thermal in nature has long been debated in the field. Our study provides evidence that the black-body component is present in the GRB spectra, which indicates that thermal emission plays a significant role in the observed radiation.

The results obtained from our analysis suggest that the thermal component present in the GRB spectra is consistent with emission from the jet photosphere. Our findings provide new insight into the physical mechanisms taking place in GRBs and challenge the current understanding of their emission mechanisms. The discovery of the black-body component opens up new avenues for studying the properties of GRBs, and it has significant implications for the field of astrophysics.

In this study, we investigate the formation and collapse of quiescent cloud cores induced by dynamic compressions. The motivation for our research is to better understand the key physical processes that trigger the formation and collapse of quiescent cloud cores. These cores are crucial in the formation of stars, planets, and other astronomical objects. Identifying the factors that contribute to the formation and collapse of these cloud cores, as well as understanding the dynamics of their evolution, will ultimately improve our understanding of the universe and how it has evolved over time.

Our key approach in this work involves numerical simulations, which allow us to investigate the behavior of cloud cores under dynamic compression conditions. We employ a three-dimensional, magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) simulation model to study the effect of compressing a pre-existing cloud core. Our simulation setup includes a hydrostatic initial equilibrium cloud that is subjected to a pulse-driven shock compression. By tracking the evolution of the cloud core under these conditions, we are able to analyze the mechanisms that drive its formation and collapse.

The problem that this work attempts to solve is the lack of a comprehensive understanding of the physical processes that govern the formation and collapse of quiescent cloud cores. By studying these processes within the framework of our simulation model, we aim to develop a more detailed understanding of these phenomena and their role in the formation of astronomical objects. Our findings will be relevant to researchers studying the evolution of the universe and the conditions that led to the formation of stars and other celestial bodies.

We show that the surface gravity of a transiting extrasolar planet can be
calculated from only the spectroscopic orbit of its parent star and the
analysis of its transit light curve. This does not require additional
constraints, such as are often inferred from theoretical stellar models or
model atmospheres. The planet's surface gravity can therefore be measured
precisely and from only directly observable quantities. We outline the method
and apply it to the case of the first known transiting extrasolar planet, HD
209458b. We find a surface gravity of g_p = 9.28 +/- 0.15 m/s, which is an
order of magnitude more precise than the best available measurements of its
mass, radius and density. This confirms that the planet has a much lower
surface gravity that that predicted by published theoretical models of gas
giant planets. We apply our method to all fourteen known transiting extrasolar
planets and find a significant correlation between surface gravity and orbital
period, which is related to the known correlation between mass and period. This
correlation may be the underlying effect as surface gravity is a fundamental
parameter in the evaporation of planetary atmospheres.

We present a study on the kinematic evolution of strong MgII absorbers and their relationship with galaxy evolution. Strong MgII absorbers are a class of quasar absorption lines that arise from dense gas clouds, and are considered a useful probe of the interstellar medium (ISM) and circumgalactic medium (CGM) of galaxies at moderate redshifts. Our work aims to address the question of whether strong MgII absorbers arise from outflows or inflows. 

We perform a detailed analysis of the kinematic properties of 248 strong MgII absorbers at z ~ 0.5 - 1.5 using high-resolution spectroscopic data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey. We find that strong MgII absorbers are not randomly distributed in velocity space, but instead exhibit ordered kinematic patterns that vary with redshift. Our results suggest that at higher redshifts, strong MgII absorbers tend to arise from outflows, while at lower redshifts they preferentially trace inflows toward galaxies. We investigate possible correlations between MgII absorption and galaxy properties, such as star formation rate, metallicity, and environment.

Our work provides new insights into the kinematic properties and evolution of the CGM and ISM of galaxies over cosmic time. We employ various statistical methods, including clustering and principal component analysis, to extract the key kinematic features of MgII absorbers. Our findings support the idea that MgII absorbers arise from a diverse range of physical processes, including galactic winds, accretion, and merger events. Our study lays the foundation for future investigations of the link between MgII absorption and galaxy evolution, which will require larger and more comprehensive datasets.

Secular variations of the geomagnetic field have been measured with a
continuously improving accuracy during the last few hundred years, culminating
nowadays with satellite data. It is however well known that the dynamics of the
magnetic field is linked to that of the velocity field in the core and any
attempt to model secular variations will involve a coupled dynamical system for
magnetic field and core velocity. Unfortunately, there is no direct observation
of the velocity. Independently of the exact nature of the above-mentioned
coupled system -- some version being currently under construction -- the
question is debated in this paper whether good knowledge of the magnetic field
can be translated into good knowledge of core dynamics. Furthermore, what will
be the impact of the most recent and precise geomagnetic data on our knowledge
of the geomagnetic field of the past and future? These questions are cast into
the language of variational data assimilation, while the dynamical system
considered in this paper consists in a set of two oversimplified
one-dimensional equations for magnetic and velocity fields. This toy model
retains important features inherited from the induction and Navier-Stokes
equations: non-linear magnetic and momentum terms are present and its linear
response to small disturbances contains Alfv\'en waves. It is concluded that
variational data assimilation is indeed appropriate in principle, even though
the velocity field remains hidden at all times; it allows us to recover the
entire evolution of both fields from partial and irregularly distributed
information on the magnetic field. This work constitutes a first step on the
way toward the reassimilation of historical geomagnetic data and geomagnetic
forecast.

In this study, we investigate the contribution of double white dwarfs (DWDs) in the foreground of the future Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (LISA) gravitational wave (GW) detector. Motivated by the need to accurately predict the observed signal in LISA, we tackle the challenge of characterizing the properties of DWDs and their GW emission. DWDs are promising sources of GW emission due to their high merger rates and strong signals. We employ a synthetic population synthesis approach, combining a binary stellar evolution code with a Monte Carlo simulation, to generate a population of DWDs, from which we calculate their properties and GW signals. We find that DWDs make a significant contribution to the LISA GW foreground, producing an overlap with other expected source populations. Moreover, our results suggest that LISA may detect tens of thousands of DWDs in a few years of observation, providing crucial information about the population and evolution of binary systems in the Galaxy. Our study highlights the importance of including DWDs in GW data analysis and paves the way for future studies on the astrophysics of compact binaries.

In this study, we present a thorough analysis of the eclipsing binary system U Geminorum. Our main objective is to test methods to determine the orbital parameters of binary star systems using observations obtained from multiple observatories. The system consists of two stars that orbit each other in a period of approximately 1.93 days. The primary star is a K0-type dwarf, while the secondary star is a larger, more luminous F5-type dwarf. The system also exhibits intense chromospheric activity, which makes it an excellent target to test methods for determining the mass, radius, and luminosity of binary stars.

To achieve our goals, we obtained multi-band photometric observations of U Geminorum using six different observatories. We applied various techniques, including light-curve modeling and spectral analysis, to derive the physical parameters of the system. Our analysis yielded accurate measurements of the mass, radius, and luminosity of the two stars, as well as the inclination and position of the orbit.

Our study has significant implications for the determination of the fundamental parameters of binary stars, which play a crucial role in several astrophysical fields, such as stellar evolution and Galactic structure. The results of our analysis demonstrate that multi-band photometry combined with spectroscopic observations can provide reliable estimates of the orbital parameters of binary star systems.

We present a study on the temporal evolution of step-edge fluctuations under electromigration conditions. This phenomenon is of great interest in the field of nanoscale electronics, as it can result in failure of interconnects and devices. The problem that our work attempts to solve is understanding the underlying mechanisms of step-edge fluctuations and how they evolve over time. Our key ideas involve using in-situ scanning electron microscopy to observe the changes in step-edge fluctuations during electromigration experiments. We also perform numerical simulations to complement our experimental observations. Our methodology involves studying samples of thin-film interconnects fabricated using copper on a silicon substrate. 

Our results show that the fluctuations at the step edges begin by exhibiting small-amplitude standing waves. As the electromigration continues, these fluctuations evolve into more complex patterns, such as propagating waves and even kinks. We also observe that the fluctuations become more pronounced as the electromigration progresses. Our simulations provide insights into the fundamental mechanisms that govern the observed behaviors. Overall, our study provides a fundamental understanding of the temporal evolution of step-edge fluctuations under electromigration conditions.

This paper investigates the use of evolutionary optimisation techniques to
register a template with a scene image. An error function is created to measure
the correspondence of the template to the image. The problem presented here is
to optimise the horizontal, vertical and scaling parameters that register the
template with the scene. The Genetic Algorithm, Simulated Annealing and
Particle Swarm Optimisations are compared to a Nelder-Mead Simplex optimisation
with starting points chosen in a pre-processing stage. The paper investigates
the precision and accuracy of each method and shows that all four methods
perform favourably for image registration. SA is the most precise, GA is the
most accurate. PSO is a good mix of both and the Simplex method returns local
minima the most. A pre-processing stage should be investigated for the
evolutionary methods in order to improve performance. Discrete versions of the
optimisation methods should be investigated to further improve computational
performance.

High dispersion near-infrared spectra have been taken of seven
highly-evolved, variable, intermediate-mass (4-6 Msun) AGB stars in the LMC and
SMC in order to look for C, N and O variations that are expected to arise from
third dredge-up and hot-bottom burning. The pulsation of the objects has been
modelled, yielding stellar masses, and spectral synthesis calculations have
been performed in order to derive abundances from the observed spectra. For two
stars, abundances of C, N, O, Na, Al, Ti, Sc and Fe were derived and compared
with the abundances predicted by detailed AGB models. Both stars show very
large N enhancements and C deficiencies. These results provide the first
observational confirmation of the long-predicted production of primary nitrogen
by the combination of third dredge-up and hot-bottom burning in
intermediate-mass AGB stars. It was not possible to derive abundances for the
remaining five stars: three were too cool to model, while another two had
strong shocks in their atmospheres which caused strong emission to fill the
line cores and made abundance determination impossible. The latter occurrence
allows us to predict the pulsation phase interval during which observations
should be made if successful abundance analysis is to be possible.

In this article, we aim to explore the concept of continuous tensegrities and its applications in engineering and architecture. The motivation behind our research stems from the need to develop structures with enhanced stability and flexibility, while also minimizing material usage. To this end, we seek to investigate the properties of continuous tensegrities and explore their potential as structural components. 

One of the main problems that our work attempts to solve is the challenge of designing structures that can withstand dynamic loads and deformations. Conventional rigid structures are often limited in their ability to adjust to changing external conditions, whereas tensegrities can adapt and respond to different loads and forces. Our research investigates the ways in which continuous tensegrities can be used as modular building blocks for the construction of complex and adaptable structures.

The key ideas and methodology for our research include a review of the existing literature on tensegrities, the use of computational simulations to explore different designs, and physical experiments to test the structural properties of the tensegrities. We will also consider the potential applications of continuous tensegrities in various fields, including robotics, aerospace engineering, and construction.

Overall, our research aims to contribute to the development of innovative and sustainable building practices, by exploring the potential of continuous tensegrities as a versatile and adaptive structural system.

In this work, we investigate the effects of lepton flavour violation (LFV) on chargino production at the linear collider. The Standard Model (SM) predicts that LFV is highly suppressed, and thus is not observable in current experiments. However, it is possible that new physics beyond the SM may introduce LFV. Chargino production is a key process for discovering new physics, as it is sensitive to the properties of both the chargino and neutralino sectors.

Using a simplified model framework, we study the impact of LFV on the chargino production cross section and kinematic distributions. We find that LFV can significantly alter the chargino production rates and distributions, with deviations from the SM expectations reaching up to a factor of three. We also examine the sensitivity of the study to the input parameters of the model, and demonstrate that the results are robust within reasonable variations of the parameters.

Our work contributes to the broader field of searches for new physics at colliders, by investigating the potential implications of LFV on chargino production. This study sheds light on the importance of considering potentially beyond-the-SM processes in experimental analyses. Overall, the results presented here highlight the potential of future linear collider experiments to probe the LFV and chargino sectors, and provide valuable input for future experimental design and data analyses.
