In this work, we investigate the beta function of gauge theories at two loops using differential renormalization. We begin by discussing the key ideas and methodology involved in the study of gauge theories, which are fundamental in our understanding of the Standard Model of particle physics. The beta function describes the dependence of the coupling strength on the energy scale of the theory and plays an essential role in the renormalization group flow of the theory. Our goal is to calculate the beta function of gauge theories using the differential renormalization approach, which is a perturbative method involving assigning a finite value to divergent integrals.

To achieve our objective, we derive the two-loop contribution to the beta function in the differential renormalization framework. We show that the differential renormalization method provides a consistent way to define the beta function, avoiding issues that arise with traditional renormalization methods. Our results open up new avenues for investigating the renormalization group properties of gauge theories and their behavior at higher loop orders.

The motivation for our research stems from the need for a better understanding of the fundamental principles that govern the behavior of gauge theories at higher energy scales. The differential renormalization approach is a promising method for calculating higher-order loop contributions in a more reliable and consistent manner, which can lead to novel insights into the properties of gauge theories. Overall, our work provides a significant contribution to the field of particle physics by advancing our understanding of gauge theories beyond the current state-of-the-art calculations.

Soil bulk density affects water storage, water and nutrient movement, and
plant root activity in the soil profile. Its measurement is difficult in field
conditions. Vibration-induced conductivity fluctuation was investigated to
quantify soil bulk density with possible field applications in the future. The
AC electrical conductivity of soil was measured using a pair of blade-like
electrodes while exposing the soil to periodic vibration. The blades were
positioned longitudinally and transversally to the direction of the induced
vibration to enable the calculation of a normalized index. The normalized index
was expected to provide data independent from the vibration strength and to
reduce the effect of soil salinity and water content. The experiment was
conducted on natural and salinized fine sand at two moisture conditions and
four bulk densities. The blade-shaped electrodes improved electrode-soil
contact compared to cylindrical electrodes, and thereby, reduced measurement
noise. Simulations on a simplified resistor lattice indicate that the
transversal effect increases as soil bulk density decreases. Measurement of dry
sand showed a negative correlation between the normalized conductivity
fluctuation and soil bulk density for both longitudinal and transversal
settings. The decrease in the transversal signal was smaller than expected. The
wet natural and salinized soils performed very similarly as hypothesized, but
their normalized VICOF response was not significant to bulk density changes.

We analyze the possibility of probing non-standard neutrino interactions
(NSI, for short) through the detection of neutrinos produced in a future
galactic supernova (SN).We consider the effect of NSI on the neutrino
propagation through the SN envelope within a three-neutrino framework, paying
special attention to the inclusion of NSI-induced resonant conversions, which
may take place in the most deleptonised inner layers. We study the possibility
of detecting NSI effects in a Megaton water Cherenkov detector, either through
modulation effects in the $\bar\nu_e$ spectrum due to (i) the passage of shock
waves through the SN envelope, (ii) the time dependence of the electron
fraction and (iii) the Earth matter effects; or, finally, through the possible
detectability of the neutronization $\nu_e$ burst. We find that the $\bar\nu_e$
spectrum can exhibit dramatic features due to the internal NSI-induced resonant
conversion. This occurs for non-universal NSI strengths of a few %, and for
very small flavor-changing NSI above a few$\times 10^{-5}$.

In the context of supersymmetric compactifications of type II supergravity to
four dimensions, we show that orientifold sources can be compatible with a
generalized SU(3) x SU(3)-structure that is neither strictly SU(3) nor static
SU(2). We illustrate this with explicit examples, obtained by suitably
T-dualizing known solutions on the six-torus. In addition we prove the
following integrability statements, valid under certain mild assumptions: (a)
for general type II supergravity backgrounds with orientifold and/or D-brane
generalized-calibrated sources, the source-corrected Einstein and dilaton
equations of motion follow automatically from the supersymmetry equations once
the likewise source-corrected form equations of motion and Bianchi identities
are imposed; (b) in the special case of supersymmetric compactifications to
four-dimensional Minkowski space, the equations of motion of all fields,
including the NSNS three-form, follow automatically once the supersymmetry and
the Bianchi identities of the forms are imposed. Both (a) and (b) are equally
valid whether the sources are smeared or localized. As a byproduct we obtain
the calibration form for a space-filling NS5-brane.

The fast changing reality in technical and natural domains perceived by
always more accurate observations has drawn attention on new and very broad
class of systems with specific behaviour represented under the common wording
complexity. From elementary system graph representation with components as
nodes and interactions as vertices, systems are shown to belong to only three
states : simple, complicated, and complex, the main properties of which are
discussed. The first two states have been studied at length over past
centuries, and last one finds its origin in the elementary fact that when
system performance is pushed up, there exists a threshold above which
interaction between components overtake outside interaction. Then system
self-organizes and filters corresponding outer action, making it more robust to
outer effect, with emergence of new behaviour not predictable from only
components study. Examples in Physics and Biology are given. Three main classes
of complexity behaviour are distinguished corresponding to different grades of
difficulty to handle their dynamics. The interest of using complex state
properties in man-made systems is stressed. Important issues concentrating on
the difficult balance between relative system isolation when becoming complex
and required delegation of corresponding new capability from (outside) operator
are discussed. This implies giving the system some intelligence in an adequate
frame between new augmented system state and supervising operator, with
consequences on canonical system triplet {effector-sensor-controller} to be
reorganized in this new setting. Moreover, entering complexity state opens the
possibility for function to feedback onto structure, ie to mimic at technical
level the invention of Nature over Her very long history.

In this research, we investigate the variable radio-to-X-ray spectrum of magnetar XTE J1810-197. The magnetar is known for the sudden bursts of radio emission that are observed during its quiescent state. The motive behind this study is to comprehend the underlying mechanism behind the radio emission and its association with X-ray spectrum emission. We aim to investigate the correlation between the radio and X-ray flux, and their spectral characteristics.

We employed the X-ray observatory Chandra and the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array (VLA) radio telescope to conduct multi-wavelength observations over a period of 8 years. Our primary focus was to trace the emission of the magnetar at different wavelengths and analyze the variation in its flux. We also studied the spectral evolution of the magnetar and compared it with theoretical models. In order to determine the correlation between the radio and X-ray flux, we employed a cross-correlation analysis.

The key idea behind this research is to comprehend the physical process that leads to the radio and X-ray emission from magnetars.  This study provides significant insight into the relationship between the two types of radiation, which could lead to a better understanding of the physical process behind the generation of radio and X-rays emission in magnetars.

In this work, we present a new method for directly determining the surface gravities of transiting extrasolar planets. Determining the surface gravity of exoplanets is a crucial step in the characterization of these planets, as it can provide insight into the planet's atmospheric structure, composition, and evolution. However, current methods for determining surface gravity rely on theoretical models and assumptions, which can lead to large uncertainties. Our new method uses only the observed transit light curve and the planet's mass, eliminating the need for assumptions about the planet's atmosphere or interior. 

Using a sample of ten transiting exoplanets with well-measured masses, we demonstrate the validity and precision of our method. We find that our method produces surface gravity measurements with uncertainties of less than 5%, significantly lower than the typical uncertainties obtained from current methods. 

Our method is based on the idea that the transit light curve shape is sensitive to the planet's surface gravity. Specifically, shallow transits (with low impact parameter) are indicative of high surface gravity, while deep transits (with high impact parameter) correspond to lower surface gravity. We use a Bayesian framework to combine information from the transit light curve and the planet's mass to infer the surface gravity. 

In conclusion, our new method provides a valuable tool for accurately determining the surface gravities of transiting exoplanets and can contribute to our understanding of these fascinating worlds.

In the modern Bayesian view classical probability theory is simply an
extension of conventional logic, i.e., a quantitative tool that allows for
consistent reasoning in the presence of uncertainty. Classical theory
presupposes, however, that--at least in principle--the amount of evidence that
an experimenter can accumulate always matches the size of the hypothesis space.
I investigate how the framework for consistent reasoning must be modified in
non-classical situations where hypotheses form a continuum, yet the maximum
evidence accessible through experiment is not allowed to exceed some finite
upper bound. Invoking basic consistency requirements pertaining to the
preparation and composition of systems, as well as to the continuity of
probabilities, I show that the modified theory must have an internal symmetry
isomorphic to the unitary group. It thus appears that the only consistent
algorithm for plausible reasoning about a continuum of hypotheses on the basis
of finite evidence is furnished by quantum theory in complex Hilbert space.

In our work titled "Random Access Broadcast: Stability and Throughput Analysis," we aim to provide a comprehensive analysis of the stability and throughput of a random access broadcast protocol. The motivation behind this research stems from the need to improve the performance of wireless networks, especially under heavy traffic conditions. The protocol under consideration effectively utilizes random access to transmit packets to multiple receivers simultaneously. However, the unpredictability and randomness of the protocol raises questions about its stability and efficiency. 

We conducted simulations to study the stability of the protocol and its ability to sustain high network utilization levels without causing packet losses. Our analysis shows that the protocol is highly stable and significantly outperforms other prominent protocols in terms of packet delivery rate and network utilization. We also investigate the tradeoffs between system stability and packet rates, under varying levels of interference and noise. 

The key idea behind our analysis is to model the random access protocol as a Markov chain and employ stochastic processes to quantify its probability distributions. We use these distributions to compute the probability of collision and packet losses, which are essential metrics for assessing protocol stability. Our results demonstrate the efficacy of our methodology in predicting the stability and throughput of the protocol. Overall, our work provides an in-depth understanding of the stability and performance tradeoffs of random access protocols, which can aid in the design and deployment of efficient wireless networks.

Mesh numbering is a critical issue in Finite Element Methods, as the
computational cost of one analysis is highly dependent on the order of the
nodes of the mesh. This paper presents some preliminary investigations on the
problem of mesh numbering using Evolutionary Algorithms. Three conclusions can
be drawn from these experiments. First, the results of the up-to-date method
used in all FEM softwares (Gibb's method) can be consistently improved; second,
none of the crossover operators tried so far (either general or problem
specific) proved useful; third, though the general tendency in Evolutionary
Computation seems to be the hybridization with other methods (deterministic or
heuristic), none of the presented attempt did encounter any success yet. The
good news, however, is that this algorithm allows an improvement over the
standard heuristic method between 12% and 20% for both the 1545 and 5453-nodes
meshes used as test-bed. Finally, some strange interaction between the
selection scheme and the use of problem specific mutation operator was
observed, which appeals for further investigation.

We present the results of a large-scale survey of neutron(n)-capture elements
in Galactic planetary nebulae (PNe), undertaken to study enrichments from
s-process nucleosynthesis in their progenitor stars. From new K band
observations of over 100 PNe supplemented by data from the literature, we have
detected the emission lines [Kr III] 2.199 and/or [Se IV] 2.287 $\mu$m in 81 of
120 objects. We determine Se and Kr elemental abundances, employing ionization
correction formulae derived in the first paper of this series. We find a
significant range in Se and Kr abundances, from near solar (no enrichment) to
enhanced by >1.0 dex relative to solar, which we interpret as self-enrichment
due to in situ s-process nucleosynthesis. Kr tends to be more strongly enriched
than Se; in 18 objects exhibiting both Se and Kr emission, we find that [Kr/Se]
= 0.5$\pm$0.2.
  Our survey has increased the number of PNe with n-capture element abundance
determinations by a factor of ten, enabling us for the first time to search for
correlations with other nebular properties. As expected, we find a positive
correlation between s-process enrichments and the C/O ratio. Type I and bipolar
PNe, which arise from intermediate-mass progenitors (>3-4 solar masses),
exhibit little to no s-process enrichments. Finally, PNe with H-deficient
Wolf-Rayet central stars do not exhibit systematically larger s-process
enrichments than objects with H-rich nuclei. Overall, 44% of the PNe in our
sample display significant s-process enrichments (>0.3 dex). Using an empirical
PN luminosity function to correct for incompleteness, we estimate that the true
fraction of s-process enriched Galactic PNe is at least 20%.

We present the first multi-epoch study that includes concurrent mid-infrared
and radio interferometry of an oxygen-rich Mira star. We obtained mid-infrared
interferometry of S Ori with VLTI/MIDI at four epochs between December 2004 and
December 2005. We concurrently observed v=1, J=1-0 (43.1 GHz), and v=2, J=1-0
(42.8 GHz) SiO maser emission toward S Ori with the VLBA at three epochs. The
MIDI data are analyzed using self-excited dynamic model atmospheres including
molecular layers, complemented by a radiative transfer model of the
circumstellar dust shell. The VLBA data are reduced to the spatial structure
and kinematics of the maser spots. The modeling of our MIDI data results in
phase-dependent continuum photospheric angular diameters between about 7.9 mas
(Phase 0.55) and 9.7 mas (Phase 1.16). The dust shell can best be modeled with
Al2O3 grains using phase-dependent inner boundary radii between 1.8 and 2.4
photospheric radii. The dust shell appears to be more compact with greater
optical depth near visual minimum, and more extended with lower optical depth
after visual maximum. The ratios of the SiO maser ring radii to the
photospheric radii are between about 1.9 and 2.4. The maser spots mark the
region of the molecular atmospheric layers just beyond the steepest decrease in
the mid-infrared model intensity profile. Their velocity structure indicates a
radial gas expansion. Al2O3 dust grains and SiO maser spots form at relatively
small radii of 1.8-2.4 photospheric radii. Our results suggest increased mass
loss and dust formation close to the surface near the minimum visual phase,
when Al2O3 dust grains are co-located with the molecular gas and the SiO maser
shells, and a more expanded dust shell after visual maximum. Silicon does not
appear to be bound in dust, as our data show no sign of silicate grains.

In this study, we present a technique for on-line condition monitoring using computational intelligence. The primary focus of our work is to develop a reliable and efficient system that can predict an impending fault in an industrial machine. We believe that an early diagnosis of the potential fault can prevent costly downtime, thereby increasing productivity and equipment life cycle. The proposed system welds a combination of signal processing and machine learning techniques to train a multi-resonant support vector machine model to detect anomalies in vibration signals. The high efficiency of the proposed method was made possible by optimising the system's parameters using firefly, bat and particle swarm optimisation algorithms. We benchmarked the performance of our approach using a dataset composed of vibration signals collected from a gas turbine engine. The results from our experimental evaluation show the system's high accuracy and precision in detecting machine faults. We further validated our approach using a real-time simulation of the gas turbine's operation. We can confidently conclude that our method will provide a timely and accurate diagnosis of the machine's health status, establishing its superiority over traditional fault detection techniques.

In this study, we analyzed the DNA base CG frequency distribution in Takifugu rubripes (Puffer fish) genome to identify a universal spectrum. The motivation behind this research was to uncover a pattern or trend in the CG frequency distribution that could be generalized to other organisms and help in understanding the genomic evolution of species. We used the Python programming language to extract and analyze the CG frequency data from the genome. Our results showed that the CG frequency distribution in Takifugu rubripes genome follows a universal spectrum, which means that it is independent of the region in the genome or the size of the genome. This discovery indicates that there is a fundamental mechanism that governs the CG frequency distribution in genomes across different species, which could be influenced by the functional constraints and the mutational processes in the genome. These findings suggest that future studies need to consider the universal spectrum when analyzing and comparing genomic data across species. Overall, this study emphasizes the importance of a fundamental understanding of genomic evolution and the use of computational methods to uncover hidden patterns in the genome.

In this work, we investigate the enhanced Kondo effect in an electron system dynamically coupled with local optical phonon. The Kondo effect is a quantum mechanical phenomenon that arises when a magnetic impurity is introduced into a conducting material. This introduction leads to the formation of a bound state of the impurity electron and a conduction electron, which in turn produces a significant increase in the material's electrical resistivity at low temperatures. Our study provides a mechanism for enhancing the Kondo effect in such a system by dynamically coupling electrons with local optical phonons.

To accomplish this, we used a self-consistent iterative perturbation approach, which allows us to account for the nonlinear electron-phonon coupling and electron-electron correlation effects. Our calculations revealed that the dynamic coupling between the electrons and local phonons significantly enhances the Kondo temperature, leading to a considerable increase in the critical value of the exchange interaction for the formation of the Kondo state. Our results suggest that local phonons can improve the Kondo physics in materials, which could impact various fields, including spintronics and quantum computing.

The motivation for our research is to understand the Kondo effect in electron systems, which is a fundamental phenomenon in condensed matter physics. Moreover, the ability to enhance the Kondo effect can have practical applications in different technological fields. Our study attempts to solve the problem of limited Kondo temperatures in materials by proposing an effective method to boost the Kondo effect through dynamic coupling with phonons.

In this work, we investigate the electrical properties of epitaxial multiferroic thin films using impedance spectroscopy. The study of multiferroics, which exhibit both ferroelectric and magnetic properties, has been of great interest due to their potential in the development of novel devices that can perform multiple functionalities in a single material. However, the understanding of the fundamental electrical properties that govern their behavior is still incomplete. 

To overcome these challenges, we have utilized impedance spectroscopy to investigate the electrical conductivity, dielectric constant, and impedance behavior of multiferroic thin films. Our results indicate that the electrical conductivity is strongly dependent on the frequency and temperature, which suggests the presence of charge carrier hopping among the different crystal planes. Additionally, the dielectric constant exhibits a strong dependence on the temperature, which indicates the presence of a paraelectric-to-ferroelectric transition. An interesting finding of our study is the observed frequency-dependent impedance behavior associated with the charging and discharging of the surface and grain boundaries, respectively. 

Our methodology involved the fabrication of high-quality epitaxial multiferroic thin films using pulsed laser deposition, followed by the characterization of their electrical properties using impedance spectroscopy. Overall, our results provide important insights into the fundamental electrical properties of multiferroic thin films, which can be used to guide the development of new multifunctional devices.

In this work, we present the results of an imaging survey for extrasolar planets around 45 close, young stars using SDI at the VLT and MMT. The main motivation behind this research was to detect and characterize potential young exoplanets in the early stages of formation while still embedded in their natal disks. Our survey covered a total of 45 stars, all within approximately 100 parsecs from Earth, with a detection sensitivity of less than 10 Jupiter masses at separations ranging from 0.1 to 5 arcseconds.

The results obtained from our imaging survey revealed no undiscovered planets around our target stars, but a total of seven previously known companions were re-detected. In addition, we estimated the mass limits for any undetected planets around our target stars.

Our primary methodology involved using the SDI technique to filter out the bloated point-spread-function of the central stars, allowing us to detect fainter companion objects. While this technique is not a perfect solution, we have achieved a significant improvement over traditional imaging.

Overall, our imaging survey has provided valuable new data on young exoplanets and their environments, adding to the growing body of research in this field. Our results are also relevant to future direct imaging missions such as the James Webb Space Telescope.

In this work, we investigate the dynamics of size-selected gold nanoparticles using ultrafast electron nanocrystallography. The study focuses on understanding the structural changes that occur in gold nanoparticles during laser-induced melting and subsequent resolidification on ultrafast timescales. This dynamic behavior of nanoparticles is relevant for applications such as catalysis and nanoelectronics, where the sizes of particles play a critical role in their properties.

To investigate this phenomenon, we utilized a combination of femtosecond laser pulses and transmission electron microscopy. We employed a size selection method to obtain monodisperse nanoparticles with diameters ranging from 2 to 15 nm. The particles were then irradiated with femtosecond laser pulses, and the structural changes were probed on ultrafast timescales using ultrafast electron nanocrystallography. The electron diffraction patterns were analyzed to obtain information about the structural changes and the melting and resolidification processes occurring in the nanoparticles.

Our research provides insights into the dynamics of size-selected gold nanoparticles and sheds light on the fundamental processes of melting and resolidification in nanoparticles. Understanding these processes is essential for designing and optimizing nanoparticle-based devices and technologies.  Overall, this work contributes to the fundamental understanding of the properties of nanomaterials and provides a basis for the development of new applications in the field of nanoscience.

In this work, we match the WMAP 3-yrs results with the Cosmological Slingshot Primordial Spectrum. Our aim is to demonstrate how this approach is critical for cosmological parameter estimation. The Cosmological Slingshot Primordial Spectrum is a theoretical model for the primordial spectrum of perturbations, based on the slingshot mechanism operating in a contracting Universe. We use the Planck likelihood code and the MultiNest algorithm to perform parameter estimation. We also make use of a Bayesian evidence analysis to test the consistency of the model with the data.

Our results show that this approach provides an excellent fit to the data. We obtain constraints on the cosmological parameters that are consistent with previous studies. We find that the model's predictions match the data for the overall amplitude of the primordial spectrum, as well as for its spectral index.

The problem our work attempts to solve is the precise measurement of cosmological parameters using observations of the cosmic microwave background radiation. The motivation for this research is to improve our understanding of the Universe's origins and evolution. A more accurate measurement of cosmological parameters can provide insights into the nature of dark matter, dark energy, and the overall structure of the Universe.

In this paper, we present a novel approach to ensure probabilistic anonymity in scheduling problems without sacrificing efficiency and feasibility. Our approach is based on the concept of admissible schedulers, which are schedulers that satisfy a set of probabilistic constraints while ensuring that the set of feasible schedules is not empty. We show that if we can construct an admissible scheduler for a given scheduling problem, then we can also construct a probabilistically anonymous scheduler with high probability. 

Our methodology involves formulating the scheduling problem as a mathematical optimization problem and using tools from convex optimization and probability theory to devise a randomized algorithm for constructing admissible schedulers. We also provide a theoretical analysis of the algorithm and show that the resulting schedulers satisfy the required probabilistic constraints with high probability. 

Our motivation for this research stems from the increasing importance of privacy in modern computing systems. Many real-world scheduling problems involve sensitive data such as personal information or trade secrets, and it is essential to ensure that such data is not compromised during the scheduling process. Our approach offers a practical and efficient solution to this problem by balancing the competing goals of privacy, efficiency, and feasibility in a principled and mathematically rigorous way.

We present the results of multiwaveband polarimetric observations of 15 active galactic nuclei (AGN) at high frequencies, aimed at studying their correlated polarization behavior. Our observations were carried out with the Australia Telescope Compact Array (ATCA) at 5.5, 9, 17, and 22 GHz, using the Compact Array Broadband Backend (CABB) in polarimetric mode. Our methodology involved the simultaneous measurement of all four Stokes parameters to obtain accurate polarization data. We analyzed the polarization properties of each AGN, including polarization angle and percentage, and investigated the degree of polarization alignment among the different frequencies. 

Our work aimed to contribute to the understanding of the physical processes that drive AGN activity, particularly in relation to the magnetic fields around the supermassive black hole at their centers. Our observations allowed us to detect correlated polarization behavior among sources, such as the presence of aligned polarization angles and the variability of the polarization behavior across different frequency bands. We found that the degree of alignment between different frequencies varied considerably among our sample, indicating the importance of considering multiple frequency observations when studying AGN polarization. The results obtained shed new light on the connection between the magnetic field and the accretion flow in AGN, allowing for further insights into the processes responsible for the emission observed in these sources.

In this paper, we investigate Littlewood-Richardson (LR) polynomials, which are a family of symmetric functions that appear in the study of Schubert calculus, representation theory, and algebraic geometry. The central problem we address is to understand the combinatorial structure and algebraic properties of LR polynomials and to compute them efficiently. The motivation for this research stems from their numerous applications in diverse areas of mathematics, physics, and computer science, including quantum computation, statistical mechanics, and combinatorial optimization.

To achieve our goals, we employ a variety of techniques from algebraic geometry, combinatorics, and representation theory, such as Schur-Weyl duality, Pieri's formula, and crystal bases. We also develop new methods that exploit the interplay between LR polynomials and other important families of symmetric functions, such as Schur, Kronecker, and Macdonald polynomials. Our work sheds light on various open problems and conjectures related to LR polynomials and provides new insight into their structure, properties, and applications.

In summary, our research contributes to the ongoing efforts to understand and develop the rich theory of symmetric functions and their interactions with other fields of mathematics and beyond. We hope that our results will stimulate further research in this fascinating area and benefit the scientific community as a whole.

In this work, we present the results of recent surveys carried out toward the Galactic Center region with the aim of uncovering the nature of the Galactic Center lobe. Our analysis of the data obtained from the Chandra, XMM–Newton, and NuSTAR telescopes reveals the presence of extensive, faint, and diffuse X-ray emission extending across ∼100 pc in the central region of our Galaxy.

The morphology of this emission suggests the presence of a large-scale outflow driven by the supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way. The observed characteristics of this outflow point to a highly energetic and structured phenomena whose origin and driving mechanism constitute one of the most pressing issues in modern astrophysics.

Our survey focused on the study of spectral and timing properties of this emission, aiming at constraining the physical processes underlying the outflow and its interaction with the surrounding environment. Through careful analysis of our data, we were able to identify the main emission mechanisms at work and determine their relative importance.

Our work provides new insights into the nature of the Galactic Center lobe and contributes to our understanding of the formation and evolution of the Milky Way and other galaxies. It also constitutes an important stepping stone towards the development of a comprehensive model for the Galactic Center region, which promises to greatly enhance our understanding of the complex astrophysical processes occurring in this region of the Universe.

In this work, we explore the possibility that nature could exhibit Sweet Spot Supersymmetry (SSS). This theoretical framework allows for a soft selection of low energy scales in a natural way. It is particularly interesting to solve the hierarchy problem, which is the apparent unnaturalness of the Higgs boson mass, much lighter than the Planck scale. We study a minimal setup of SSS that combines a D-term contribution from an extra U(1) gauge symmetry with the mirage mediation mechanism for supersymmetry breaking. The key idea behind this model is that the U(1) gauge symmetry scale and the supersymmetry breaking scale could coincide at a sweet spot defined by a ratio of order one. By analyzing the renormalization group equations leading to the Higgs mass and the dark matter relic density, we find that our minimal SSS model can account for a Higgs boson mass close to the observed value and a dark matter density consistent with the latest Planck results. Additionally, we show that this scenario can be testable at the current and future LHC experiments, making it a promising candidate for solving the hierarchy problem and for exploring new physics beyond the Standard Model.

In this paper, we explore the relationship between quantisation and reduction in semisimple groups. Our focus is on discrete series representations, which have been shown to play a crucial role in the study of geometric quantisation. Our primary result is that quantisation commutes with reduction for these representations. This result has significant implications for the study of quantisation theory, which seeks to understand how to turn classical systems into quantum ones.

Our motivation for this research stems from a desire to better understand the underlying principles of quantisation theory. Although there has been substantial progress in this area in recent years, many open questions remain. In particular, the relationship between quantisation and reduction is not fully understood, and our work seeks to shed light on this topic.

To prove our main result, we rely on a combination of algebraic and geometric techniques. The key idea is to use the Howe duality theorem to establish a connection between the representation theory of semisimple Lie groups and their associated symmetric spaces. We then use this connection to show that quantisation commutes with reduction for discrete series representations. Our methodology is rigorous and relies on a careful analysis of the relevant mathematical objects.

In summary, our work provides new insights into the relationship between quantisation and reduction for semisimple groups. Our results have important applications in the study of quantisation theory and should be of interest to researchers in the field.

In this study, we investigate the stellar mass assembly of galaxies over the last 10 billion years using data from three surveys: the Spitzer Wide-area Infrared Extragalactic (SWIRE), the VIMOS VLT Deep Survey (VVDS), and the Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope Legacy Survey (CFHTLS). Our primary objective is to understand the evolution of the red sequence of galaxies, which consists of quiescent galaxies with older stars. We focus on the redshift range between z=2 and z=1, where there is evidence for a significant build-up of galaxies on the red sequence. To analyze the data, we measure the stellar mass and star formation rate of galaxies and assign them to different populations based on their colors. We also fit a simple model to the data to estimate the fraction of galaxies on the red sequence at different redshifts. Our results show a clear increase in the red sequence fraction between z=2 and z=1, with most of the growth occurring in the first billion years after the Big Bang. The observed trend is consistent with theoretical models that predict the rapid assembly of galaxies through mergers and the suppression of star formation in the most massive systems. Overall, our study provides new insights into the formation and evolution of galaxies and the underlying physical processes that govern their properties.

In this work, we explore the real and complex beta-deformation of the N=4 planar super Yang-Mills theory from the perspective of integrability. The beta-deformed theory is an extension of the standard N=4 super Yang-Mills, allowing to break its full superconformal symmetry to a smaller subgroup. This symmetry breaking is controlled by a single complex parameter, beta, which governs the interactions among the gauge fields and their supersymmetric partners. 

We present a systematic analysis of the spectral problem associated with the integrability of the beta-deformed theory. We employ a combination of algebraic, analytic and numerical methods to compute the spectrum of anomalous dimensions of single trace gauge invariant operators to high order in the deformation parameter beta. Moreover, we compare the real and complex beta-deformations and explore their similarities and differences. 

Our motivation is twofold: first, we are interested in studying the integrability of the beta-deformed theories, which is a powerful tool to unveil their properties at any value of the coupling constant. Second, we aim to shed light on the emergence of complex structures in supersymmetric gauge theories and their possible connections with complex geometry and string theory. 

Our results show that the real and complex beta-deformations have different properties, related to their different symmetries and the nature of the deformation parameter. We also find evidence for a quantum spectral curve structure underlying the anomalous dimensions, which is a key ingredient of exact integrability. Our work provides a solid basis for further studies of the beta-deformed theories and their relation to other areas of mathematical physics.

In our study, we investigate the strong atom-field coupling for Bose-Einstein condensates in an optical cavity on a chip. The aim of our research is to investigate the interaction between confined atoms and light in the optical regime. We have obtained results demonstrating the production of a confined cloud of atoms, or Bose-Einstein condensate, in an optical resonator. The confinement of the atoms inside the resonator creates a strong coupling between the light and the atoms, leading to a variety of interesting phenomena.

 Our key idea is to use a cavity on a chip to confine light and atoms in the same small volume. This enables us to study the interaction between atoms and light in a new regime, where the confinement is sufficiently tight to create ultra-strong coupling.

Our methodology involves employing a unique combination of microfabrication techniques and laser cooling methods. We use a specifically designed optical resonator, which employs a two-dimensional mirror array to localize light in the resonator. We study the system experimentally using fluorescence and absorption imaging of the cloud of atoms as well as cavity transmission measurements.

 Our study aims to contribute to the development of a new paradigm for understanding light-matter interactions in the ultra-strong coupling regime for potential applications in quantum sensing and metrology. The work represents an attempt to solve the problem of investigating the ultra-strong light-matter coupling regime in Bose-Einstein condensates and promises to pave the way for exciting prospects in the fields of quantum computing and quantum metrology.

In this study, we investigate the near-IR dwarf galaxy population of the Coma Cluster using Spitzer IRAC. Our primary motivation is to uncover the faint, low surface brightness (LSB) dwarf galaxies in the cluster that have been previously undetected due to their low luminosity and surface brightness. These galaxies could have formed early in the universe's history and provide valuable insights into the conditions present during the early stages of galaxy formation. Furthermore, they could also help us understand the formation and evolution of the Coma Cluster itself.

To identify these LSB dwarf galaxies, we analyze images obtained with Spitzer IRAC at 3.6 and 4.5 microns. Our methodology involves using a combination of color-magnitude and color-color diagrams to identify candidate LSB galaxies. We then conduct follow-up observations to validate and confirm their existence.

Our work attempts to solve the problem of incomplete galaxy catalogs of the Coma Cluster. Previous studies have only focused on the bright, high surface brightness galaxies, while ignoring the faint and LSB galaxies. Our study uses Spitzer IRAC to uncover the LSB dwarf galaxy population, and we anticipate that our results will greatly enhance our understanding of galaxy formation and the Coma Cluster.

We show that the Kullback-Leibler distance is a good measure of the
statistical uncertainty of correlation matrices estimated by using a finite set
of data. For correlation matrices of multivariate Gaussian variables we
analytically determine the expected values of the Kullback-Leibler distance of
a sample correlation matrix from a reference model and we show that the
expected values are known also when the specific model is unknown. We propose
to make use of the Kullback-Leibler distance to estimate the information
extracted from a correlation matrix by correlation filtering procedures. We
also show how to use this distance to measure the stability of filtering
procedures with respect to statistical uncertainty. We explain the
effectiveness of our method by comparing four filtering procedures, two of them
being based on spectral analysis and the other two on hierarchical clustering.
We compare these techniques as applied both to simulations of factor models and
empirical data. We investigate the ability of these filtering procedures in
recovering the correlation matrix of models from simulations. We discuss such
an ability in terms of both the heterogeneity of model parameters and the
length of data series. We also show that the two spectral techniques are
typically more informative about the sample correlation matrix than techniques
based on hierarchical clustering, whereas the latter are more stable with
respect to statistical uncertainty.

In this work, we present the Anderson-Yuval-Hamann (AYH) representation of the nonequilibrium two level system (TLS) in the Coulomb gas framework. The problem we attempt to solve is the description of the dynamics of a TLS driven out of equilibrium by a time-dependent electric field. The key idea behind our approach is to represent the TLS as a four-dimensional complex field on the Keldysh contour, which allows us to map the problem to a two-dimensional Coulomb gas in imaginary time. 

Our methodology involves the application of the functional integral formalism, where we derive the partition function of the system and obtain an effective Lagrangian describing the dynamics of the AYH field. We then perform a saddle point approximation to compute the expectation value of the field and obtain the occupation probability of the TLS. 

The motivation for our research lies in the need to better understand the nonequilibrium dynamics of quantum systems, which play a crucial role in modern technology such as quantum computing and sensing. Our work provides a rigorous mathematical description of the TLS under time-dependent driving, which can be further extended to more complex systems. Overall, our results contribute to the development of a better understanding of the nonequilibrium quantum mechanics and its potential applications.

The rich set of interactions between individuals in the society results in
complex community structure, capturing highly connected circles of friends,
families, or professional cliques in a social network. Thanks to frequent
changes in the activity and communication patterns of individuals, the
associated social and communication network is subject to constant evolution.
Our knowledge of the mechanisms governing the underlying community dynamics is
limited, but is essential for a deeper understanding of the development and
self-optimisation of the society as a whole. We have developed a new algorithm
based on clique percolation, that allows, for the first time, to investigate
the time dependence of overlapping communities on a large scale and as such, to
uncover basic relationships characterising community evolution. Our focus is on
networks capturing the collaboration between scientists and the calls between
mobile phone users. We find that large groups persist longer if they are
capable of dynamically altering their membership, suggesting that an ability to
change the composition results in better adaptability. The behaviour of small
groups displays the opposite tendency, the condition for stability being that
their composition remains unchanged. We also show that the knowledge of the
time commitment of the members to a given community can be used for estimating
the community's lifetime. These findings offer a new view on the fundamental
differences between the dynamics of small groups and large institutions.

In this work, we present a novel approach for visual data mining of genomic databases using immersive graph-based exploration. Our motivation for this research stems from the explosion of genomic data generated by high-throughput sequencing technologies, which makes it increasingly difficult for researchers to sift through and interpret large amounts of data. Our approach visualizes the data in a 3D graph-based representation that allows users to immerse themselves in the data and explore it interactively. The results we obtained using this approach show that our visualization tool is effective in identifying novel associations between genes, pathways, and diseases.

The key idea behind our approach is to leverage the power of human perception and pattern recognition to facilitate knowledge discovery in large genomic datasets. Our methodology involves preprocessing the data using a combination of clustering and dimensionality reduction techniques, followed by graph construction and layout using force-directed algorithms. The resulting 3D graph is then fed into a virtual reality environment that enables users to interact with the data and explore it in a natural and intuitive way.

In conclusion, our visual data mining approach using immersive graph-based exploration represents a powerful tool for genomic data analysis and has the potential to accelerate discovery in the field of genetics and genomics.

We investigate colour selection techniques for high redshift galaxies in the
UKIDSS Ultra Deep Survey Early Data Release (UDS EDR). Combined with very deep
Subaru optical photometry, the depth (K_AB = 22.5) and area (0.62 deg^2) of the
UDS EDR allows us to investigate optical/near-IR selection using a large sample
of over 30,000 objects. By using the B-z, z-K colour-colour diagram (the BzK
technique) we identify over 7500 candidate galaxies at z > 1.4, which can be
further separated into passive and starforming systems (pBzK and sBzK
respectively). Our unique sample allows us to identify a new feature not
previously seen in BzK diagrams, consistent with the passively evolving track
of early type galaxies at z < 1.4. We also compare the BzK technique with the
R-K colour selection of Extremely Red Objects (EROs) and the J-K selection of
Distant Red Galaxies (DRGs), and quantify the overlap between these
populations. We find that the majority of DRGs, at these relatively bright
magnitudes are also EROs. Since previous studies have found that DRGs at these
magnitudes have redshifts of z ~ 1 we determine that these DRG/ERO galaxies
have SEDs consistent with being dusty star-forming galaxies or AGN at z < 2.
Finally we observe a flattening in the number counts of pBzK galaxies, similar
to other studies, which may indicate that we are sampling the luminosity
function of passive z > 1 galaxies over a narrow redshift range.

We present our research on the Optimal Electrostatic Space Tower, also known as the Mast or New Space Elevator. Our motivation springs from the need for more cost-effective and sustainable transportation options, particularly in the context of space exploration and exploitation. The idea of a space elevator has been around for over a century, but has been impeded by technological challenges, particularly in terms of material strength and energy requirements. Our approach deviates from traditional designs, which involve tall towers anchored to the ground, instead proposing a tower which extends outward from a central hub into space, with a pivot point at the hub. In order to facilitate the motion of climbers up and down the tower, we employ electrostatic charging of the tower and climbers. This allows for reduction in energy usage, through the transfer of electricity produced by solar panels on the tower. We utilize a combination of mathematical modeling, material simulations, and experimentation to validate our design and demonstrate its feasibility. Our work solves the problem of excessive energy consumption in previously attempted designs, provides a cost-effective option with increased safety and convenience, and advances the field of space technology.

In this study, we sought to identify network communities with a higher resolution than previously achieved. The motivation for this research stems from the need for more accurate network analysis methods tailored to the complex structures of real-world networks. Specifically, we aimed to resolve fine-grained communities that could not be identified by existing methods. To accomplish this, we proposed a community detection algorithm that maps vertices to a high-dimensional space, enabling the resolution of closely interconnected and overlapping communities within a network. The effectiveness of our approach was demonstrated through experiments on synthetic and real-world networks. The results showed that our method achieves higher resolution and accuracy, identifies communities missed by existing methods, and provides a more detailed understanding of network structures. Moreover, our approach is scalable, and its computational complexity is linear with the number of edges in the network. In conclusion, this study presents a novel community detection algorithm that can reveal fine-grained network communities not identified by existing methods, thus offering new opportunities for network analysis in various fields.

We investigate the influence of noise on a graph state generation scheme
which exploits a mirror inverting spin chain. Within this scheme the spin chain
is used repeatedly as an entanglement bus (EB) to create multi-partite
entanglement. The noise model we consider comprises of each spin of this EB
being exposed to independent local noise which degrades the capabilities of the
EB. Here we concentrate on quantifying its performance as a single-qubit
channel and as a mediator of a two-qubit entangling gate, since these are basic
operations necessary for graph state generation using the EB. In particular,
for the single-qubit case we numerically calculate the average channel fidelity
and whether the channel becomes entanglement breaking, i.e., expunges any
entanglement the transferred qubit may have with other external qubits. We find
that neither local decay nor dephasing noise cause entanglement breaking. This
is in contrast to local thermal and depolarizing noise where we determine a
critical length and critical noise coupling, respectively, at which
entanglement breaking occurs. The critical noise coupling for local
depolarizing noise is found to exhibit a power-law dependence on the chain
length. For two qubits we similarly compute the average gate fidelity and
whether the ability for this gate to create entanglement is maintained. The
concatenation of these noisy gates for the construction of a five qubit linear
cluster state and a Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger state indicates that the level
of noise that can be tolerated for graph state generation is tightly
constrained.

Observations were made of the optical afterglow of GRB 051028 with the Lulin
observatory's 1.0 m telescope and the WIDGET robotic telescope system. R band
photometric data points were obtained on 2005 October 28 (UT), or 0.095-0.180
days after the burst. There is a possible plateau in the optical light curve
around 0.1 days after the burst; the light curve resembles optically bright
afterglows (e.g. GRB 041006, GRB 050319, GRB060605) in shape of the light curve
but not in brightness. The brightness of the GRB 051028 afterglow is 3
magnitudes fainter than that of one of the dark events, GRB 020124. Optically
dark GRBs have been attributed to dust extinction within the host galaxy or
high redshift. However, the spectrum analysis of the X-rays implies that there
is no significant absorption by the host galaxy. Furthermore, according to the
model theoretical calculation of the Ly$\alpha$ absorption to find the limit of
GRB 051028's redshift, the expected $R$ band absorption is not high enough to
explain the darkness of the afterglow. While the present results disfavor
either the high-redshift hypothesis or the high extinction scenario for
optically dark bursts, they are consistent with the possibility that the
brightness of the optical afterglow, intrinsically dark.

In this work, we present astrophysical supplements to the All Sky Compiled Catalogue of 2.5 Million Stars (ASCC-2.5) by providing the radial velocities of around 55000 stars and the mean radial velocities of 516 Galactic open clusters and associations. The aim of this study is to enhance the ASCC-2.5 catalogue by providing additional information that helps to improve our understanding of the Milky Way's kinematics and dynamics.

We obtained the radial velocities using the spectrum of the stars measured by several high-resolution spectrographs, including the Large Sky Area Multi-Object Fiber Spectroscopic Telescope (LAMOST). Our results indicate that the open clusters present an asymmetric velocity distribution, which is an indication of the Galactic disk's non-axisymmetric shape. Moreover, the cluster's mean velocities are used to study the Local Standard of Rest velocity, which is relevant to investigate the vertical and radial motions of the disk.

Our work provides crucial information that can be used to investigate the Milky Way's formation and evolution. It can help us understand the Galactic structure and formation history, allowing us to create a more precise model for our Galaxy. Overall, our methodology is based on analyzing and interpreting large datasets, which are essential in modern astrophysics.

Recently Supernova 2006gy was noted as the most luminous ever recorded, with
a total radiated energy of ~10^44 Joules. It was proposed that the progenitor
may have been a massive evolved star similar to eta Carinae, which resides in
our own galaxy at a distance of about 2.3 kpc. eta Carinae appears ready to
detonate. Although it is too distant to pose a serious threat as a normal
supernova, and given its rotation axis is unlikely to produce a Gamma-Ray Burst
oriented toward the Earth, eta Carinae is about 30,000 times nearer than
2006gy, and we re-evaluate it as a potential superluminous supernova. We find
that given the large ratio of emission in the optical to the X-ray, atmospheric
effects are negligible. Ionization of the atmosphere and concomitant ozone
depletion are unlikely to be important. Any cosmic ray effects should be spread
out over ~10^4 y, and similarly unlikely to produce any serious perturbation to
the biosphere. We also discuss a new possible effect of supernovae, endocrine
disruption induced by blue light near the peak of the optical spectrum. This is
a possibility for nearby supernovae at distances too large to be considered
"dangerous" for other reasons. However, due to reddening and extinction by the
interstellar medium, eta Carinae is unlikely to trigger such effects to any
significant degree.

In this work, we explore the concept of entropy power inequalities, which provide a fundamental limit on the amount of information that can be transmitted over a noisy channel. Our approach is based on information theory, which offers a powerful framework for analyzing the capacity of communication systems in the presence of noise.

Our main contribution is to provide rigorous proofs of several important entropy power inequalities, which have been previously established through heuristic arguments or numerical simulations. Specifically, we focus on the classical Shannon entropy power inequality and its extensions to more general settings, such as Gaussian channels with feedback or discrete memoryless channels with side information.

To achieve this goal, we use a combination of techniques from probability theory, statistical physics, and convex analysis. We exploit the duality between information divergence and convex functionals, which allows us to rewrite the entropy power inequalities as optimization problems over suitable function classes. We also develop new inequalities and inequalities for related problems, such as information inequalities and moment inequalities.

Our work is motivated by the need for a deeper understanding of the fundamental limits of communication systems, which is critical for the design of efficient and robust communication protocols. Our results provide new insights into the structure of entropy power inequalities and their connections with other areas of information theory, such as channel coding and error-correcting codes.

We present the first results of a Spitzer IRAC (Infrared Array Camera)
wide-field survey of the Coma cluster. The observations cover two fields of
different galaxy densities; the first is a 0.733 deg^2 region in the core of
the cluster (Coma 1), the second a 0.555 deg^2 off-center region located ~57
arcmin (1.7 Mpc) south-west from the core (Coma 3). The observations, although
short 70-90 s exposures, are very sensitive; we detect ~29,200 sources at 3.6
micron over the total ~1.3 deg^2 survey area. We construct 3.6 micron galaxy
luminosity functions (LFs) for each field using selection functions based on
spectroscopic redshifts. At the bright end, the LFs are well modeled by a
traditional Schechter function; [M^star (3.6 micron), alpha_1] = [-25.17,
-1.18] and [-24.69, -1.30] for Coma 1 and Coma 3 respectively. However, at the
faint end (M(3.6 micron) > -20.5), there is a steep increase in the LF slope in
both fields indicative of large numbers of red dwarf galaxies. The reality of
this population is examined using optical counterparts in SDSS data with
optical color filtering (g-r<1.3). The steep increase can be modeled with a
power-law function, with slopes of alpha_2 = -2.18 (Coma 1) and alpha_2 = -2.60
(Coma 3). A qualitative comparison with optical (B- and R-band) LFs shows that
we are likely to be observing a larger population of dwarf galaxies in the
near-IR, which may be a low-surface-brightness (LSB) population that IRAC is
particularly sensitive to, or a population too red to be detected in existing
optical surveys down to R~20.

We considered diffusion-driven processes on small-world networks with
distance-dependent random links. The study of diffusion on such networks is
motivated by transport on randomly folded polymer chains, synchronization
problems in task-completion networks, and gradient driven transport on
networks. Changing the parameters of the distance-dependence, we found a rich
phase diagram, with different transient and recurrent phases in the context of
random walks on networks. We performed the calculations in two limiting cases:
in the annealed case, where the rearrangement of the random links is fast, and
in the quenched case, where the link rearrangement is slow compared to the
motion of the random walker or the surface. It has been well-established that
in a large class of interacting systems, adding an arbitrarily small density
of, possibly long-range, quenched random links to a regular lattice interaction
topology, will give rise to mean-field (or annealed) like behavior. In some
cases, however, mean-field scaling breaks down, such as in diffusion or in the
Edwards-Wilkinson process in "low-dimensional" small-world networks. This
break-down can be understood by treating the random links perturbatively, where
the mean-field (or annealed) prediction appears as the lowest-order term of a
naive perturbation expansion. The asymptotic analytic results are also
confirmed numerically by employing exact numerical diagonalization of the
network Laplacian. Further, we construct a finite-size scaling framework for
the relevant observables, capturing the cross-over behaviors in finite
networks. This work provides a detailed account of the
self-consistent-perturbative and renormalization approaches briefly introduced
in two earlier short reports.

Studies of the effects of environment on galaxy properties and evolution
require well defined control samples. Such isolated galaxy samples have up to
now been small or poorly defined. The AMIGA project (Analysis of the
interstellar Medium of Isolated GAlaxies) represents an attempt to define a
statistically useful sample of the most isolated galaxies in the local (z <
0.05) Universe. A suitable large sample for the AMIGA project already exists,
the Catalogue of Isolated Galaxies (CIG, Karachentseva 1973; 1050 galaxies),
and we use this sample as a starting point to refine and perform a better
quantification of its isolation properties. Digitised POSS-I E images were
analysed out to a minimum projected radius R > 0.5 Mpc around 950 CIG galaxies
(those within Vr = 1500 km s-1 were excluded). We identified all galaxy
candidates in each field brighter than B = 17.5 with a high degree of
confidence using the LMORPHO software. We generated a catalogue of
approximately 54 000 potential neighbours (redshifts exist for 30% of this
sample). Six hundred sixty-six galaxies pass and two hundred eighty-four fail
the original CIG isolation criterion. The available redshift data confirm that
our catalogue involves a largely background population rather than physically
associated neighbours. We find that the exclusion of neighbours within a factor
of four in size around each CIG galaxy, employed in the original isolation
criterion, corresponds to Delta Vr ~ 18000 km s-1 indicating that it was a
conservative limit. Galaxies in the CIG have been found to show different
degrees of isolation. We conclude that a quantitative measure of this is
mandatory. It will be the subject of future work based on the catalogue of
neighbours obtained here.

We characterize the crystalline silicate content and spatial distribution of
small dust grains in a large sample of protoplanetary disks in the
Taurus-Auriga young cluster, using Spitzer Space Telescope mid-infrared
spectra. In turn we use the results to analyze the evolution of structure and
composition of these 1-2 Myr-old disks around Solar- and later-type young
stars, and test the standard models of dust processing which result in the
conversion of originally amorphous dust into minerals. We find strong evidence
of evolution of the dust crystalline mass fraction in parallel with that of the
structure of the disks, in the sense that increasing crystalline mass fraction
is strongly linked to dust settling to the disk midplane. We also confirm that
the crystalline silicates are confined to small radii, r < 10 AU. However, we
see no significant correlation of crystalline mass fraction with stellar mass
or luminosity, stellar accretion rate, disk mass, or disk/star mass ratio, as
would be expected in the standard models of dust processing based upon
photo-evaporation and condensation close to the central star,
accretion-heating-driven annealing at r < 1 AU, or spiral-shock heating at r <
10 AU, with or without effective radial mixing mechanisms. Either another
grain-crystallizing mechanism dominates over these, or another process must be
at work within the disks to erase the correlations they produce. We propose one
of each sort that seem to be worth further investigation, namely X-ray heating
and annealing of dust grains, and modulation of disk structure by
giant-planetary formation and migration.

In this study, we present a novel cascade model for particle concentration and enstrophy in fully developed turbulence with mass loading feedback. The primary goal of this research is to understand the complex dynamics of turbulent flow and its impact on the transport of particles in a fluid medium. The study aims to solve the problem of how to accurately predict the concentration and motion of particles in turbulent flow fields.

The key ideas of the cascade model involve the description of the particle concentration and enstrophy as a function of the Kolmogorov scale. The model incorporates feedback between the density of particles and the flow characteristics to predict the behavior of particles in turbulent flow. Furthermore, we use statistical analysis and data-driven modeling to experimentally validate the proposed methodology.

The motivation behind this research is driven by the need to understand the transport of particles in turbulent fluids to improve industrial processes and environmental remediation. For instance, predicting the motion and concentration of pollutants in water bodies is crucial in the design of effective water treatment and purification systems. Moreover, the study can also aid in the design of new drug delivery systems that leverage the fluid dynamics of the human body.

In conclusion, the proposed cascade model provides a framework for predicting the behavior of particles in complex turbulent flow fields. The combination of statistical analysis and data-driven modeling provides a holistic approach to understanding the complex dynamics of fluid-particle interactions.

[Abridged] We have studied the optical spectra of 28 O- and early B-type
stars in the Large Magellanic Cloud, 22 of which are associated with the young
star-forming region N11. Stellar parameters are determined using an automated
fitting method, combining the stellar atmosphere code FASTWIND with the
genetic-algorithm optimisation routine PIKAIA. Results for stars in the LH9 and
LH10 associations of N11 are consistent with a sequential star formation
scenario, in which activity in LH9 triggered the formation of LH10. Our sample
contains four stars of spectral type O2, of which the hottest is found to be
~49-54 kK (cf. ~45-46 kK for O3 stars). The masses of helium-enriched dwarfs
and giants are systematically lower than those implied by non-rotating
evolutionary tracks. We interpret this as evidence for efficient
rotationally-enhanced mixing, leading to the surfacing of primary helium and to
an increase of the stellar luminosity. This result is consistent with findings
for SMC stars by Mokiem et al. For bright giants and supergiants no such
mass-discrepancy is found, implying that these stars follow tracks of modestly
(or non-)rotating objects. Stellar mass-loss properties were found to be
intermediate to those found in massive stars in the Galaxy and the SMC, and
comparisons with theoretical predictions at LMC metallicity yielded good
agreement over the luminosity range of our targets, i.e. 5.0 < log L/L(sun) <
6.1.

In this work, we investigate the transmission properties of a spin-split quantum wire with local Rashba interaction. By using a two-terminal scattering method combined with the Landauer-Büttiker formalism, we show that the transmission probability of spin-up and spin-down electrons can be strongly modulated by varying the Rashba coupling strength and the length of the wire. Our results demonstrate that the spin splitting effect significantly affects the overall transmission, leading to a spin-dependent energy filtering and selective propagation of spin-polarized electrons. By analyzing the transmission spectra and the wave function profiles, we identify the emergence of resonant transmission peaks and localized states in the wire, which can be attributed to the interplay between the Rashba coupling and the spin-orbit interaction.

Our study aims to shed light on the fundamental physics of spin-dependent transport in mesoscopic systems and provide a possible route for designing efficient spintronic devices. Our key idea is to exploit the spin-splitting effect induced by the Rashba interaction to create a tunable spin filter, which can enhance the spin-polarization and reduce the decoherence in spintronic circuits. To achieve this goal, we employed a comprehensive theoretical approach that combines analytical calculations with numerical simulations based on the tight-binding model. Our methodology allows us to capture the subtle effects of the Rashba coupling on the electronic structure and transport properties, and provides a quantitative understanding of the spin-dependent phenomena observed in the system.

In this study, we present a systematic investigation of the final masses of gas giant planets. Our aim is to shed light on the underlying physics that governs the formation and evolution of giant planets, which pose intriguing challenges in astrophysics. We examine a large number of simulations that consider various initial conditions and explore different formation scenarios of planetary systems. First, we apply a series of statistical methods to quantify the distribution of final masses and explore the correlations with other physical parameters such as disk properties, stellar mass, and metallicity. Our results demonstrate that the final masses follow a universal distribution that is independent of the initial conditions and the formation history. Secondly, we identify two distinct regimes of planet formation that correspond to different dominant mechanisms of gas accretion. Finally, we use a comprehensive suite of numerical codes to validate our findings and investigate the sensitivity of our results to numerical methods. Our work provides crucial insights into the origin of gas giant planets, and lays the groundwork for further studies on the broader question of planetary systems formation.

Close pre-main-sequence binary stars are expected to clear central holes in
their protoplanetary disks, but the extent to which material can flow from the
circumbinary disk across the gap onto the individual circumstellar disks has
been unclear. In binaries with eccentric orbits, periodic perturbation of the
outer disk is predicted to induce mass flow across the gap, resulting in
accretion that varies with the binary period. This accretion may manifest
itself observationally as periodic changes in luminosity. Here we present a
search for such periodic accretion in the pre-main-sequence spectroscopic
binary UZ Tau E. We present BVRI photometry spanning three years; we find that
the brightness of UZ Tau E is clearly periodic, with a best-fit period of 19.16
+/- 0.04 days. This is consistent with the spectroscopic binary period of 19.13
days, refined here from analysis of new and existing radial velocity data. The
brightness of UZ Tau E shows significant random variability, but the overall
periodic pattern is a broad peak in enhanced brightness, spanning more than
half the binary orbital period. The variability of the H-alpha line is not as
clearly periodic, but given the sparseness of the data, some periodic component
is not ruled out. The photometric variations are in good agreement with
predictions from simulations of binaries with orbital parameters similar to
those of UZ Tau E, suggesting that periodic accretion does occur from
circumbinary disks, replenishing the inner disks and possibly extending the
timescale over which they might form planets.

In this work, we explore the possibility of shrinking binary and planetary orbits by using Kozai cycles in conjunction with tidal friction. Our hypothesis is that this mechanism can significantly reduce the semimajor axis of the orbit and lead to merger or destruction of the system. 

Our study is motivated by the observed abundance of close binaries and hot Jupiters, which are thought to form farther away and migrate inwards. The Kozai cycle is a well-known mechanism for increasing the eccentricity of an orbit while conserving the total angular momentum of the system. When combined with tidal friction, this process can also decrease the semimajor axis by removing energy from the orbit and producing a positive feedback loop. 

To test our hypothesis, we use numerical simulations of two-body and three-body systems with different masses, initial parameters, and tidal coefficients. We also investigate the impact of additional effects such as stellar or planetary rotation, magnetic fields, and oblateness. Our results show that Kozai cycles with tidal friction can indeed shrink the orbit of a binary or planetary system, but the outcome depends strongly on the initial conditions and the specific form of the tidal dissipation model. 

Overall, our work sheds light on the complex interplay between gravitational perturbations, dissipative processes, and orbital evolution. Our findings have implications for the formation and evolution of compact objects, the detection of gravitational waves, and the habitability of exoplanets.

In this study, we investigate the origin of the galaxy mass-metallicity relation and its implications for Galactic outflows. Our research challenges the conventional hierarchical galaxy formation model with  analytical models and hydrodynamical simulations. We found that the galaxy mass-metallicity relation can be explained by the balance between inflow, outflow, and star formation. Stellar-driven winds and supernova feedback also play an important role in regulating the mass-metallicity relation.

Our methodology involves running hydrodynamical simulations with different initial conditions and feedback prescriptions. We then compare the results with analytical models derived from the continuity equation and chemical evolution theory. We focus on reproducing the observed galaxy mass-metallicity relation and its variation with redshift and local environment.

By understanding the origin of the galaxy mass-metallicity relation, we can also better understand the nature of Galactic outflows. Our work suggests that outflows are efficient at removing metals from star-forming regions and enriching the surrounding intergalactic medium. However, the mass-loading factor of the outflows is highly dependent on the galaxy potential well and feedback efficiency. Therefore, the interplay between inflow, outflow, and feedback is crucial in shaping the chemical evolution of galaxies.

Overall, our study provides key implications for future observational and theoretical studies of galaxy evolution and star formation.

In this work, we investigate the decoherence of many-spin systems in Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. Our goal is to understand the underlying mechanisms responsible for decoherence and to explore the possibility of inducing a quantum dynamical phase transition. 

We start by characterizing the molecular systems under investigation using state-of-the-art experimental techniques. This allows us to develop a theoretical framework for describing the complex interactions between the spins and their environment. We also use numerical simulations to explore the dynamics of the system under different conditions.

Our results show that decoherence in many-spin systems can be attributed to various factors such as magnetic field inhomogeneity, spin-spin and spin-environment interactions, and thermal relaxation processes. Furthermore, we demonstrate that an environmental perturbation can induce a phase transition from a coherent to an incoherent regime, resulting in a loss of quantum information.

The findings presented in this work provide a comprehensive understanding of the decoherence mechanisms in many-spin systems and may have implications for the design of quantum information processing devices. They also highlight the importance of environmental effects in NMR spectroscopy and the potential for using perturbations to induce quantum phase transitions. Further studies aimed towards practical applications are warranted in this field.

Avalanches, or Avalanche-like, events are often observed in the dynamical
behaviour of many complex systems which span from solar flaring to the Earth's
crust dynamics and from traffic flows to financial markets. Self-organized
criticality (SOC) is one of the most popular theories able to explain this
intermittent charge/discharge behaviour. Despite a large amount of theoretical
work, empirical tests for SOC are still in their infancy. In the present paper
we address the common problem of revealing SOC from a simple time series
without having much information about the underlying system. As a working
example we use a modified version of the multifractal random walk originally
proposed as a model for the stock market dynamics. The study reveals, despite
the lack of the typical ingredients of SOC, an avalanche-like dynamics similar
to that of many physical systems. While, on one hand, the results confirm the
relevance of cascade models in representing turbulent-like phenomena, on the
other, they also raise the question about the current state of reliability of
SOC inference from time series analysis.

We study the chiral transformation properties of all possible local
(non-derivative) interpolating field operators for baryons consisting of three
quarks with two flavors, assuming good isospin symmetry. We derive and use the
relations/identities among the baryon operators with identical quantum numbers
that follow from the combined colour, Dirac and isospin Fierz transformations.
These relations reduce the number of independent baryon operators with any
given spin and isospin. The Fierz identities also effectively restrict allowed
baryon chiral multiplets. It turns out that the chiral multiplets of the
baryons are equivalent to their Lorentz representation. For the two independent
nucleon operators the only permissible chiral multiplet is the fundamental one
$(\frac12,0)\oplus(0,\frac12)$. For the $\Delta$, admissible Lorentz
representations are $(1,\frac12)\oplus (\frac12,1)$ and
$(\frac32,0)\oplus(0,\frac32)$. In the case of the $(1,\frac12)\oplus
