(\frac12,1)$ chiral multiplet the $I(J)=\frac32(\frac32)$ $\Delta$ field has
one $I(J)=\frac12(\frac32)$ chiral partner; otherwise it has none. We also
consider the Abelian ($U_A(1)$) chiral transformation properties of fields and
show that each baryon comes in two varieties: 1) with Abelian axial charge +3;
and 2) with Abelian axial charge -1. In case of the nucleon these are the two
Ioffe's fields; in case of the $\Delta$, the $(1,\frac12)\oplus (\frac12,1)$
multiplet has Abelian axial charge -1 and the $(\frac32,0)\oplus(0,\frac32)$
multiplet has Abelian axial charge +3.

In this paper, we investigate the unitary representations of wavelet groups and their applicability in encoding iterated function systems in solenoids. Our approach to finding these representations follows the framework pioneered by Kari and Nekrashevych. We first construct a contracting auto-homeomorphism that generates the wavelet group, and then use this to generate a family of unitary representations. Using these representations, we show that it is possible to define a measurable encoding of the iterated function systems of self-similar sets in solenoids.

The motivation for this research comes from the need to better understand the structure of wavelet groups and their relationship to fractal geometry. Specifically, we are interested in the properties of the wavelet group that make it an efficient tool for encoding iterated function systems. Our work attempts to solve the problem of finding a suitable encoding for these systems in solenoids. 

The methodology we employ involves a careful analysis of the wavelet group, including its automorphism group and the properties of its associated unitary representations. We also use the theory of solenoids to provide a geometric interpretation of the encoding we develop. Ultimately, our work provides a new way of understanding the relationship between wavelet groups and fractal geometry, and may have implications for the development of new compression algorithms and other applications.

The layered structure and the rheological properties of anti-wear films,
generated in a rolling/sliding contact from lubricants containing zinc
dialkyldithiophosphate (ZDTP) and/or molybdenum dialkyldithiocarbamate (MoDTC)
additives, have been studied by dynamic nanoindentation experiments coupled
with a simple modelling of the stiffness measurements. Local nano-friction
experiments were conducted with the same device in order to determine the
evolution of the friction coefficient as a function of the applied pressure for
the different lubricant formulations. For the MoDTC film, the applied pressure
in the friction test remains low (<0.5 GPa) and the apparent friction
coefficient is high ($\mu$ > 0.4). For the tribofilms containing MoDTC together
with ZDTP, which permits the applied pressure to increase up to a few GPa
through some accommodation process, a very low friction domain appears (0.01 <
$\mu$ < 0.05), located a few nanometers below the surface of the tribofilm.
This low friction coefficient is attributed to the presence of MoS2 planes
sliding over each other in a favourable configuration obtained when the
pressure is sufficiently high, which is made possible by the presence of ZDTP.

We compare the use of galaxy asymmetry and pair proximity for measuring
galaxy merger fractions and rates for a volume limited sample of 3184 galaxies
with -21 < M(B) -5 log h < -18 mag. and 0.010 < z < 0.123 drawn from the
Millennium Galaxy Catalogue. Our findings are that:
  (i) Galaxies in close pairs are generally more asymmetric than isolated
galaxies and the degree of asymmetry increases for closer pairs. At least 35%
of close pairs (with projected separation of less than 20 h^{-1} kpc and
velocity difference of less than 500 km s^{-1}) show significant asymmetry and
are therefore likely to be physically bound.
  (ii) Among asymmetric galaxies, we find that at least 80% are either
interacting systems or merger remnants. However, a significant fraction of
galaxies initially identified as asymmetric are contaminated by nearby stars or
are fragmented by the source extraction algorithm. Merger rates calculated via
asymmetry indices need careful attention in order to remove the above sources
of contamination, but are very reliable once this is carried out.
  (iii) Close pairs and asymmetries represent two complementary methods of
measuring the merger rate. Galaxies in close pairs identify future mergers,
occurring within the dynamical friction timescale, while asymmetries are
sensitive to the immediate pre-merger phase and identify remnants.
  (iv) The merger fraction derived via the close pair fraction and asymmetries
is about 2% for a merger rate of (5.2 +- 1.0) 10^{-4} h^3 Mpc^{-3} Gyr^{-1}.
These results are marginally consistent with theoretical simulations (depending
on the merger time-scale), but imply a flat evolution of the merger rate with
redshift up to z ~1.

We present an investigation of the thermoelectric properties of the strongly correlated model for Na$_x$CoO$_2$. Our work attempts to address the fundamental research question regarding thermoelectric properties that arise from the complex interplay of electronic correlation and band structure in strongly correlated materials. Our motivation for this research is to enhance our understanding of the link between electronic correlation and thermoelectric effects, with the ultimate goal of designing new materials with improved thermoelectric performance. We employ a numerically exact dynamical mean-field theory approach to study the thermoelectric transport properties of the Na$_x$CoO$_2$ model. We utilize a gauge-invariant approach to extract the transport coefficients that are central to the study of the Seebeck effect and the electrical conductivity. Our main result reveals an enhancement of the Seebeck coefficient in the strongly correlated regime, as the electronic correlation increases. Furthermore, we find that the electrical conductivity decreases as the electronic correlation increases, which is consistent with the generalized Drude scenario for strongly correlated materials. Our work provides new insights into the relationship between electronic correlation and thermoelectricity, which should inform the design and discovery of new materials with optimized thermoelectric performance.

We present the results of a search for $B^{+}\to\gamma \ell^{+}\nu_{\ell}$,
where $\ell = e, \mu$. We use a sample of 232 million $B\bar{B}$ pairs recorded
at the $\Upsilon(4S)$ with the BaBar detector at the PEP-II $B$ Factory. We
measure a partial branching fraction $\Delta\mathcal{B}$ in a restricted region
of phase space that reduces the effect of theoretical uncertainties, requiring
the lepton energy to be between 1.875 and 2.850GeV, the photon energy to be
between 0.45 and 2.35GeV, and the cosine of the angle between the lepton and
photon momenta to be less than -0.36, with all quantities computed in the
$\Upsilon(4S)$ center-of-mass frame. We find $\Delta\mathcal{B}(B^{+}\to\gamma
\ell^{+}\nu_{\ell}) = (-0.3^{+1.3}_{-1.5} ({stat}) \pm 0.6 ({syst}) \pm 0.1
({th})) \times 10^{-6}$, assuming lepton universality. Interpreted as a 90%
C.L. Bayesian upper limit, the result corresponds to $1.7\times10^{-6}$ for a
prior flat in amplitude, and $2.3\times10^{-6}$ for a prior flat in branching
fraction.

We present new Faraday rotation measures (RMs) for 148 extragalactic radio
sources behind the southern Galactic plane (253o < l < 356o, |b| < 1.5o), and
use these data in combination with published data to probe the large-scale
structure of the Milky Way's magnetic field. We show that the magnitudes of
these RMs oscillate with longitude in a manner that correlates with the
locations of the Galactic spiral arms. The observed pattern in RMs requries the
presence of at least one large-scale magnetic reversal in the fourth Galactic
quadrant, located between the Sagittarius- Carina and Scutum-Crux spiral arms.
To quantitatively compare our measurements to other recent studies, we consider
all available extragalactic and pulsar RMs in the region we have surveyed, and
jointly fit these data to simple models in which the large-scale field follows
the spiral arms. In the best-fitting model, the magnetic field in the fourth
Galactic quadrant is directed clockwise in the Sagittarius-Carina spiral arm
(as viewed from the North Galactic pole), but is oriented counter- clockwise in
the Scutum-Crux arm. This contrasts with recent analyses of pulsar RMs alone,
in which the fourth-quadrant field was presumed to be directed
counter-clockwise in the Sagittarius- Carina arm. Also in contrast to recent
pulsar RM studies, our joint modeling of pulsar and extragalactic RMs
demonstrates that large numbers of large-scale magnetic field reversals are not
required to account for observations.

The Hamiltonian formalism has proven to be a powerful tool for studying the dynamics of physical systems, including those in cosmology. In this work, we apply the Hamiltonian approach to the Friedmann cosmology model, which describes the large-scale evolution of the universe. Our goal is to obtain a Hamiltonian formulation of the model and investigate its quantization. 

The motivation for this research is twofold. Firstly, it allows us to study the quantum properties of the universe, providing a theoretical framework for understanding phenomena such as the cosmic microwave background or the formation of galaxies. Secondly, it allows us to test the validity of fundamental physical theories, such as general relativity, in the high-energy regime where they are expected to break down.

The key ideas of our approach involve identifying the canonical variables of the Friedmann model, constructing the corresponding Hamiltonian, and imposing appropriate quantization conditions. Our methodology involves combining analytical calculations with numerical simulations, such as solving the Schrodinger equation for the system's wave function.

Our main results include the derivation of a Hamiltonian formalism for the Friedmann model, as well as preliminary calculations for its quantum properties. We discuss the implications of our findings for the broader field of cosmology, highlighting the potential for future research in this area.

In this paper we study the confluence of two regular singular points of the
hypergeometric equation into an irregular one. We study the consequence of the
divergence of solutions at the irregular singular point for the unfolded
system. Our study covers a full neighborhood of the origin in the confluence
parameter space. In particular, we show how the divergence of solutions at the
irregular singular point explains the presence of logarithmic terms in the
solutions at a regular singular point of the unfolded system. For this study,
we consider values of the confluence parameter taken in two sectors covering
the complex plane. In each sector, we study the monodromy of a first integral
of a Riccati system related to the hypergeometric equation. Then, on each
sector, we include the presence of logarithmic terms into a continuous
phenomenon and view a Stokes multiplier related to a 1-summable solution as the
limit of an obstruction that prevents a pair of eigenvectors of the monodromy
operators, one at each singular point, to coincide.

This work focuses on investigating the interface width and bulk stability requirements of deeply quenched liquid-gas systems in the context of simulation. The motivation behind this research comes from the fact that deeply quenched liquid-gas systems exhibit complex behavior, which makes it challenging to simulate and understand their properties. A major problem that this work attempts to solve is the accurate simulation of these systems, which is crucial to understand their properties and behavior.

We conducted simulations of deeply quenched liquid-gas systems using a Lennard-Jones fluid model with different interface widths and bulk stabilization methods. Our findings suggest that the interface width plays a crucial role in determining the stability of the system. We found that using a narrow interface width leads to greater instabilities in the bulk of the system, resulting in the formation of voids. On the other hand, a larger interface width stabilizes the system, resulting in the formation of a dense and stable bulk. Furthermore, our results indicate that the bulk stabilization method used also affects the stability of the system.

In conclusion, our work highlights the importance of considering interface width and bulk stability when simulating deeply quenched liquid-gas systems. It provides insights into how these factors influence the behavior and properties of such systems. The results obtained from this work will contribute to the development of more accurate simulations of deeply quenched liquid-gas systems, leading to a better understanding of their behavior and properties.

We report the detection of five Jovian mass planets orbiting high metallicity
stars. Four of these stars were first observed as part of the N2K program and
exhibited low RMS velocity scatter after three consecutive observations.
However, follow-up observations over the last three years now reveal the
presence of longer period planets with orbital periods ranging from 21 days to
a few years. HD 11506 is a G0V star with a planet of \msini = 4.74 \mjup in a
3.85 year orbit. HD 17156 is a G0V star with a 3.12 \mjup planet in a 21.2 day
orbit. The eccentricity of this orbit is 0.67, one of the highest known for a
planet with a relatively short period. The orbital period for this planet
places it in a region of parameter space where relatively few planets have been
detected. HD 125612 is a G3V star with a planet of \msini = 3.5 \mjup in a 1.4
year orbit. HD 170469 is a G5IV star with a planet of \msini = 0.67 \mjup in a
3.13 year orbit. HD 231701 is an F8V star with planet of 1.08 \mjup in a 142
day orbit. All of these stars have supersolar metallicity. Three of the five
stars were observed photometrically but showed no evidence of brightness
variability. A transit search conducted for HD 17156 was negative but covered
only 25% of the search space and so is not conclusive.

The region of MSSM Higgs parameter space currently excluded by the CDF
Collaboration, based on an analysis of ~1 fb^-1 of integrated luminosity, is
less than the expected sensitivity. We analyze the potential implications of
the persistence of this discrepancy within the MSSM, assuming that the soft
supersymmetry-breaking contributions to scalar masses are universal, apart from
those to the Higgs masses (the NUHM model). We find that a light heavy MSSM
Higgs signal in the unexcluded part of the sensitive region could indeed be
accommodated in this simple model, even after taking into account other
constraints from cold dark matter, electroweak precision observables and B
physics observables. In this case the NUHM suggests that supersymmetric
signatures should also be detectable in the near future in some other
measurements such as BR(B_s -> mu+ mu-), BR(b -> s gamma) and (g-2)_mu, and M_h
would have to be very close to the LEP exclusion limit. In addition, the dark
matter candidate associated with this model should be on the verge of detection
in direct detection experiments.

In our research, we investigate the inspiral of massive black holes during the merger of gas-rich galaxies. Galaxy mergers are known to be instrumental in shaping the structure and evolution of galaxies. The dynamical interaction between the two black holes during the merger process plays a crucial role in determining the final outcome. Also, the presence of gas in the merging galaxies can trigger episodes of intense star formation, and also fuel the central black holes, leading to growth and accretion episodes. We aim to study this problem to understand better the interplay between the black holes and the surrounding gas and stars.

We use numerical simulations to track the dynamics of the black holes and gas during the merger. Our findings show that the presence of gas can significantly influence the evolution of the binary black hole system, leading to a faster inspiral timescale and enhancing the probability of mergers. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the interaction between the binary black holes and gas can lead to significant excitation of non-axisymmetric modes in the disk, leading to the formation of spiral arms and potentially triggering inflows towards the center.

Our research has significant implications for understanding the evolution of galaxies and their black hole population. Understanding the merger process of black holes and gas-rich galaxies can help us interpret the observed properties of galaxies and the growing supermassive black holes at their centers.

We study the processes $e^+ e^-\to K^+ K^- \pi^+\pi^-\gamma$,
$K^+K^-\pi^0\pi^0\gamma$ and $K^+ K^- K^+ K^-\gamma$, where the photon is
radiated from the initial state. About 34600, 4400 and 2300 fully reconstructed
events, respectively, are selected from 232 \invfb of \babar data. The
invariant mass of the hadronic final state defines the effective \epem
center-of-mass energy, so that the $K^+ K^- \pi^+\pi^-\gamma$ data can be
compared with direct measurements of the $e^+ e^-\to K^+K^- \pipi$ reaction; no
direct measurements exist for the $e^+ e^-\to K^+ K^- \pi^0\pi^0$ or $\epem\to
K^+ K^- K^+ K^-$ reactions. Studying the structure of these events, we find
contributions from a number of intermediate states, and we extract their cross
sections where possible. In particular, we isolate the contribution from $e^+
e^-\to\phi(1020) f_{0}(980)$ and study its structure near threshold. In the
charmonium region, we observe the $J/\psi$ in all three final states and
several intermediate states, as well as the $\psi(2S)$ in some modes, and
measure the corresponding branching fractions. We see no signal for the Y(4260)
and obtain an upper limit of
$\BR_{Y(4260)\to\phi\pi^+\pi^-}\cdot\Gamma^{Y}_{ee}<0.4 \ev$ at 90% C.L.

In our academic work, we addressed the 1874 controversy between Camille Jordan and Leopold Kronecker to better understand the debates surrounding the foundations of mathematics and the emergence of abstract algebra as a discipline. The motivation behind this research was to investigate the views and arguments presented by both mathematicians and to highlight the implications of such a controversy for the development of mathematical thought.

We used a historical and analytical methodology to examine the different aspects of the controversy. Our research focused on understanding the arguments presented by Jordan and Kronecker and how they related to the emerging field of abstract algebra. We analyzed the main ideas and concepts presented by both mathematicians, emphasizing the role that mathematical abstractions played in their reasoning.

Through our study, we discovered that the controversy between Jordan and Kronecker revealed significant differences in their views on mathematical abstraction and the nature of mathematical entities. The debate centered on Kronecker's objections to Jordan's use of abstract algebraic structures to solve problems in number theory. Jordan saw abstraction as essential for progress in mathematics, while Kronecker believed that it would lead to confusion and abstraction from physical reality.

Our research provides a deeper understanding of the controversy and its impact on the development of abstract algebra. It also sheds light on the different perspectives on mathematical abstraction held by Jordan and Kronecker, highlighting the fundamental role that abstraction plays in mathematical reasoning and the ongoing debates surrounding its use in the field.

In this work, we present a detailed study of the quantum electrodynamics (QED) calculation of the 2p1/2-2s and 2p3/2-2s transition energies and the ground-state hyperfine splitting in lithium-like scandium. The motivation for this study is the need to develop accurate theoretical models for interpreting experimental data from high-precision spectroscopic measurements of highly charged ions. The calculation is carried out within the framework of the Breit-Pauli Hamiltonian, which includes the leading relativistic and QED effects. We use a sophisticated computational approach based on finite basis sets and a perturbation theory expansion of the transition operators to obtain extremely accurate predictions for these atomic properties. Our calculated values for the transition energies and hyperfine splitting are in excellent agreement with experimental measurements and with the most advanced theoretical predictions available in the literature. The key ideas and methodology behind our calculation involve a careful treatment of relativistic and QED effects, including the incorporation of higher-order corrections to the one-electron Dirac wave functions and the use of an optimized basis set. These results provide valuable information for the interpretation of experimental data and the benchmarking of theoretical models in the realm of high-precision spectroscopy.

We present a study of cool stars in hot places, motivated by the need to better understand stellar atmospheres and the physical processes that govern their behavior. Our aim is to investigate the properties of cool stars in close proximity to hot environments such as giant planets, brown dwarfs, and white dwarfs, with a particular focus on the effects of ionizing radiation and proximity on these stars. 

Our work attempts to solve the challenges of accurately characterizing the spectra and spectral energy distributions of cool stars in such extreme conditions, and to assess the impact of this on our ability to study the atmospheres of these stars. We also aim to shed light on the validity of current models and the degree of accuracy of past observations, which may be affected by these hot environments.

To achieve this, we utilize various methods, including radiative transfer modeling, synthetic spectral analysis, and observations of cool stars in hot environments. Our methods allow us to compare real observations with theoretical models and to test the validity of current understanding of the behavior of cool stars at close range to hot objects. 

Our findings have important implications for future observations of cool stars in extreme environments, and for our understanding of the physical processes that govern the behavior of stars in these conditions. We predict that our research will inform larger studies of cool stars in hot places and deepen our understanding of the complex interplay between these stars and their surroundings.

We examine whether or not it is possible to derive the field strength
distribution of quiet Sun internetwork regions from very high spatial
resolution polarimetric observations in the visible. In particular, we consider
the case of the spectropolarimeter attached to the Solar Optical Telescope
aboard Hinode. Radiative magneto-convection simulations are used to synthesize
the four Stokes profiles of the \ion{Fe}{1} 630.2 nm lines. Once the profiles
are degraded to a spatial resolution of 0\farcs32 and added noise, we infer the
atmospheric parameters by means of Milne-Eddington inversions. The comparison
of the derived values with the real ones indicates that the visible lines yield
correct internetwork field strengths and magnetic fluxes, with uncertainties
smaller than $\sim$150 G, when a stray light contamination factor is included
in the inversion. Contrary to the results of ground-based observations at
1\arcsec, weak fields are retrieved wherever the field is weak in the
simulation.

In our work, we investigate the phenomenon of strain localization in amorphous solids through a shear transformation zone (STZ) model. Amorphous solids are materials that lack long-range order in their atomic structure, making them inherently prone to strain localization and ultimately leading to failure. Our study aims to understand the mechanisms and conditions that lead to strain localization in amorphous solids, potentially leading to the development of more robust materials.

Our simulations show that the STZ model accurately captures the strain localization behavior observed in amorphous solids. We find that strain localization occurs due to the accumulation of plastic deformation in localized regions, resulting in shear bands. We also observe that the number of STZs plays a crucial role in determining the onset of strain localization.

Our study addresses a fundamental question in the field of materials science and provides insights into the behavior of amorphous solids under deformation. The development of localized deformation is a significant issue affecting the practical applications of amorphous solids for structural uses. By identifying the mechanisms causing strain localization, our work provides a starting point for developing strategies to design amorphous solids that are less susceptible to deformation. Our findings offer a crucial contribution to the field of materials science and could lead to better understanding of this topic in future research.

We present a new computer program designed for the fast non-LTE analysis of interstellar line spectra. Our program, called "FASTANAL," is based on modern numerical techniques for solving complex systems of coupled non-linear equations and incorporates a range of physical processes relevant to interstellar gas dynamics. The main problem addressed by our work is the need for efficient and accurate tools to analyze the large amounts of data acquired in modern astronomical surveys. Traditional non-LTE analysis methods are often too slow and computationally expensive to handle these data sets, hindering progress in this field. The motivation behind our research is therefore to develop a fast and flexible software package that can handle a wide range of data types and provide high-quality spectral analysis in a timely manner. We present the results of several benchmark tests using simulated data, as well as a proof-of-concept analysis of real spectral data obtained from the interstellar medium. Our program is freely available for download and can be used in a variety of astronomical applications. We anticipate that FASTANAL will be a valuable tool for future astronomical surveys and will enable new discoveries in a variety of fields.

In this work, we investigate the adiabatic quantum dynamics of a random Ising chain crossing its quantum critical point, motivated by the need to understand better the behavior of random quantum systems at criticality. Specifically, we study the time evolution of the system under a slowly varying transverse field, which drives the ground state from the ordered phase, through the quantum critical point, to the disordered phase. We use a combination of numerical techniques, including exact diagonalization and matrix product state simulations, to explore the properties of the system during the adiabatic evolution. Our main results show that the quantum critical point manifests as a smooth but rapid change in the dynamics, characterized by a scaling of the adiabatic energy gap, as well as universal critical exponents governing the scaling of the entanglement entropy and fidelity susceptibility. Moreover, we observe that the random disorder induces strong finite-size effects, causing the transition to occur at a non-universal value of the transverse field. Overall, our work provides insight into the behavior of random quantum systems at criticality, highlighting the importance of finite-size effects and offering potential applications in quantum information and computation.

In this work, we present rigorous mathematical results concerning the periodic oscillation of an adiabatic piston. The motivation for this research stems from the importance of understanding the behavior of adiabatic processes in thermodynamics, which has implications in fields such as engineering and physics. Specifically, we consider a piston that moves adiabatically in a cylinder, undergoing periodic oscillations due to external forces. Our aim is to rigorously prove the existence, uniqueness, and stability of the oscillatory solutions to this problem using analytical methods.

To achieve these results, we utilize a combination of dynamical systems theory, perturbation methods, and variational techniques. We construct a rigorous mathematical framework that allows us to analyze the stability and asymptotic properties of the solutions. Our main result establishes that, for small enough perturbations of the initial conditions, the system exhibits periodic oscillations with an amplitude that decays exponentially over time.

Our work contributes to the existing body of literature on adiabatic processes and provides insight into the dynamical behavior of complex systems. The results obtained can be extended to other physical systems that exhibit periodic oscillations, thus opening up avenues for future research on the topic.

In this work, we investigate the dynamics of localization phenomena for hardcore bosons in optical lattices. We study the behavior of these systems under the effect of disorder and an external potential and examine how the interplay between these two factors affects the localization properties of the bosons.

Our methodology involves the use of analytical and numerical techniques to investigate the properties of the hardcore bosons in the optical lattices. We explore the phase diagrams of the system and identify the critical points where the localization phenomena occur. Furthermore, we utilize the concept of entanglement entropy to characterize the properties of the states and examine how entanglement properties evolve in the presence of disorder.

The motivation for this research stems from the recent advancements in the experimental techniques for controlling and manipulating ultracold atoms in optical lattices. In particular, we are interested in understanding the properties of hardcore bosons, which are a model system for the strong interactions between particles.

The problem this work attempts to solve is to provide a comprehensive understanding of the localization phenomena for hardcore bosons in optical lattices under the effect of external potential and disorder. This study may have implications for the design and control of quantum systems and may contribute to the development of new technologies based on ultracold atoms.

The set of solutions of random constraint satisfaction problems (zero energy
groundstates of mean-field diluted spin glasses) undergoes several structural
phase transitions as the amount of constraints is increased. This set first
breaks down into a large number of well separated clusters. At the freezing
transition, which is in general distinct from the clustering one, some
variables (spins) take the same value in all solutions of a given cluster. In
this paper we study the critical behavior around the freezing transition, which
appears in the unfrozen phase as the divergence of the sizes of the
rearrangements induced in response to the modification of a variable. The
formalism is developed on generic constraint satisfaction problems and applied
in particular to the random satisfiability of boolean formulas and to the
coloring of random graphs. The computation is first performed in random tree
ensembles, for which we underline a connection with percolation models and with
the reconstruction problem of information theory. The validity of these results
for the original random ensembles is then discussed in the framework of the
cavity method.

We present a numerical method for spatially 1.5-dimensional and
time-dependent studies of accretion disks around black holes, that is
originated from a combination of the standard pseudo-spectral method and the
adaptive domain decomposition method existing in the literature, but with a
number of improvements in both the numerical and physical senses. In
particular, we introduce a new treatment for the connection at the interfaces
of decomposed subdomains, construct an adaptive function for the mapping
between the Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto collocation points and the physical
collocation points in each subdomain, and modify the over-simplified
1-dimensional basic equations of accretion flows to account for the effects of
viscous stresses in both the azimuthal and radial directions. Our method is
verified by reproducing the best results obtained previously by Szuszkiewicz &
Miller on the limit-cycle behavior of thermally unstable accretion disks with
moderate viscosity. A new finding is that, according to our computations, the
Bernoulli function of the matter in such disks is always and everywhere
negative, so that outflows are unlikely to originate from these disks. We are
encouraged to study the more difficult case of thermally unstable accretion
disks with strong viscosity, and wish to report our results in a subsequent
paper.

The study entitled "The Galactic Central Diffuse X-ray Enhancement: A Differential Absorption/Emission Analysis" explores the mysterious X-ray diffuse emission at the Galactic Center of the Milky Way. This research was motivated by the lack of a clear understanding of the origin of the Galactic Central diffuse X-ray emission, which could provide crucial information about the physical processes and the distribution of gas around the center of the galaxy. The problem this work attempts to solve is to identify the various components that contribute to the diffuse X-ray emission and differentiate them using spectral analysis.
 
The research utilized data from the Chandra X-ray Observatory and XMM-Newton telescopes, and a new method of differential absorption and emission analysis to investigate the diffuse emission from the Galactic Center's hot plasma and its surrounding neutral and molecular gas. The results revealed that the X-ray emission originates from a combination of plasma, Fe I Kα, and molecular Fe XXV emission. The plasma coexists with molecular clouds and exhibits a complex distribution pattern, indicating the interplay between various physical processes in the Galactic Center's environment.

In summary, this study provides a more detailed characterization of the Galactic Central diffuse X-ray enhancement. Our findings offer valuable insights into the physical processes in the central region of the Milky Way, which will contribute to a better understanding of the evolution and dynamics of galaxies.

GRB051022 was undetected to deep limits in early optical observations, but
precise astrometry from radio and X-ray showed that it most likely originated
in a galaxy at z~0.8. We report radio, optical, near infra-red and X-ray
observations of GRB051022. Using the available X-ray and radio data, we model
the afterglow and calculate the energetics of the afterglow, finding it to be
an order of magnitude lower than that of the prompt emission. The broad-band
modeling also allows us to precisely define various other physical parameters
and the minimum required amount of extinction, to explain the absence of an
optical afterglow. Our observations suggest a high extinction, at least 2.3
magnitudes in the infrared (J) and at least 5.4 magnitudes in the optical (U)
in the host-galaxy restframe. Such high extinctions are unusual for GRBs, and
likely indicate a geometry where our line of sight to the burst passes through
a dusty region in the host that is not directly co-located with the burst
itself.

Motivated by heavy ion collision experiments, we study the hydrodynamic
properties of non-Abelian systems. These issues arise in condensed matter
physics in the context of transport of spins in the presence of spin orbit
coupling: the Pauli Hamiltonian governing the leading relativistic corrections
in condensed matter systems can be rewritten in a language of SU(2) covariant
derivatives, where the role of the non-Abelian gauge fields is taken by the
physical electromagnetic fields. Taking a similar perspective as Jackiw and
coworkers, we show that non-abelian hydrodynamical currents can be factored in
a non-coherent 'classical' part, and a coherent part requiring macroscopic
non-abelian quantum entanglement. Non-abelian flow being thus a much richer
affair than familiar hydrodynamics, permits us to classify the various spin
transport phenomena in in condensed matter physics in a unifying framework.In
semiconductor spintronics, the absence of hydrodynamics is well known, but in
our formulation it is directly associated with the fact that non-abelian
currents are only covariantly conserved.We analyze the quantum mechanical
single particle currents of relevance to mesoscopic transport with as highlight
the Aharonov-Casher effect, where we demonstrate that the non-abelian transport
structure renders it much more fragile than its abelian counterpart, the
Aharonov-Bohm effect. We subsequently focus on spin flows protected by order
parameters, of which the spin-spiral magnets and the spin superfluids are
important examples. The surprising bonus is that the presence of an order
parameter, being single-valued, restores hydrodynamics. We demonstrate a new
effect: the trapping of electrical line charge, being the 'fixed frame'
non-Abelian analogue of the familiar magnetic flux trapping by superconductors.

As decentralized computing scenarios get ever more popular, unstructured
topologies are natural candidates to consider running mix networks upon. We
consider mix network topologies where mixes are placed on the nodes of an
unstructured network, such as social networks and scale-free random networks.
We explore the efficiency and traffic analysis resistance properties of mix
networks based on unstructured topologies as opposed to theoretically optimal
structured topologies, under high latency conditions. We consider a mix of
directed and undirected network models, as well as one real world case study --
the LiveJournal friendship network topology. Our analysis indicates that
mix-networks based on scale-free and small-world topologies have, firstly,
mix-route lengths that are roughly comparable to those in expander graphs;
second, that compromise of the most central nodes has little effect on
anonymization properties, and third, batch sizes required for warding off
intersection attacks need to be an order of magnitude higher in unstructured
networks in comparison with expander graph topologies.

In this study, we aim to determine the optimal window for the 2MASS dipole. The 2MASS dipole is a variation in the cosmic microwave background that is used to measure the velocity of the Sun with respect to the reference frame of the Universe. This measurement is essential for accurately determining the cosmic microwave background spectrum and testing for deviations from the standard model of cosmology. However, determining the optimal window for the 2MASS dipole is a non-trivial task. 

Our key idea is to use a Bayesian approach to estimate the optimal window. This method involves creating a model for the dipole signal and comparing it to observed data. We use a Markov Chain Monte Carlo approach to sample the posterior distribution of the model parameters, which allows us to estimate the optimal window on the basis of the evidence.

Our methodology involved analyzing the data from the WMAP and Planck missions and comparing it to our model for the dipole signal. We found that the optimal window for the 2MASS dipole is between 50 and 80 degrees, which is consistent with previous studies.

In conclusion, our study presents a new approach for determining the optimal window for the 2MASS dipole. Our model for the dipole signal allowed us to estimate the optimal window on the basis of the evidence. Our results will assist in refining measurements of the cosmic microwave background spectrum and testing the standard model of cosmology.

The geometric constructions are elaborated on (semi) Riemannian manifolds and
vector bundles provided with nonintegrable distributions defining nonlinear
connection structures induced canonically by metric tensors. Such spaces are
called nonholonomic manifolds and described by two equivalent linear
connections also induced in unique forms by a metric tensor (the Levi Civita
and the canonical distinguished connection, d-connection). The lifts of
geometric objects on tangent bundles are performed for certain classes of
d-connections and frame transforms when the Riemann tensor is parametrized by
constant matrix coefficients. For such configurations, the flows of
non-stretching curves and corresponding bi-Hamilton and solitonic hierarchies
encode information about Ricci flow evolution, Einstein spaces and exact
solutions in gravity and geometric mechanics. The applied methods were
elaborated formally in Finsler geometry and allows us to develop the formalism
for generalized Riemann-Finsler and Lagrange spaces. Nevertheless, all
geometric constructions can be equivalently re-defined for the Levi Civita
connections and holonomic frames on (semi) Riemannian manifolds.

We present MadGraph/MadEvent v4, a pioneering web application that streamlines the workflow of automating matrix element calculations, event generation and LHC simulation. Our goal is to provide particle physicists with a singular, immersive platform to facilitate scientific research while also reducing their learning curve to implement simulations and experiments.

Our application solves the problem of long and arduous computational processes, which hinder the efficiency and productivity of researchers. Our approach avoids the technicalities and intricacies of particle physics, providing an easy-to-use interface through which users can submit their computations remotely and retrieve their data. 

We have obtained impressive results with our application, including improved accuracy in the prediction of Higgs boson mass values and the real-time generation of Feynman diagrams, thus providing a practical and pedagogical tool for educational purposes.

Our methodology involved the integration of high-performance computing, making our application an outstanding solution for academic researchers and professionals in the field. Our key idea was to create a user-friendly interface that would make scientific research accessible to anyone, regardless of their technical background.

In conclusion, MadGraph/MadEvent v4, the new web generation, provides an innovative and powerful solution for automating matrix element calculations and generating LHC simulations. Our cutting-edge application is pioneering the field by providing researchers, students and professionals with improved accuracy, real-time Feynman diagrams generation and a simplified workflow, thus enhancing scientific research productivity, accuracy and efficiency.

Aims: We analyze the available information on the star BD+43 3654 to
investigate the possibility that it may have had its origin in the massive OB
association Cygnus OB2.
  Methods: We present new spectroscopic observations allowing a reliable
spectral classification of the star, and discuss existing MSX observations of
its associated bow shock and astrometric information not previously studied.
  Results: Our observations reveal that BD+43 3654 is a very early and luminous
star of spectral type O4If, with an estimated mass of (70 +/- 15) solar masses
and an age of about 1.6 Myr. The high spatial resolution of the MSX
observations allows us to determine its direction of motion in the plane of the
sky by means of the symmetry axis of the well-defined bow shock, which matches
well the orientation expected from the proper motion. Tracing back its path
across the sky we find that BD+43 3654 was located near the central, densest
region of Cygnus OB2 at a time in the past similar to its estimated age.
  Conclusions: BD+43 3654 turns out to be one of the three most massive runaway
stars known, and it most likely formed in the central region of Cygnus OB2. A
runaway formation mechanism by means of dynamical ejection is consistent with
our results.

We consider processes with second order long range dependence resulting from
heavy tailed durations. We refer to this phenomenon as duration-driven long
range dependence (DDLRD), as opposed to the more widely studied linear long
range dependence based on fractional differencing of an $iid$ process. We
consider in detail two specific processes having DDLRD, originally presented in
Taqqu and Levy (1986), and Parke (1999). For these processes, we obtain the
limiting distribution of suitably standardized discrete Fourier transforms
(DFTs) and sample autocovariances. At low frequencies, the standardized DFTs
converge to a stable law, as do the standardized sample autocovariances at
fixed lags. Finite collections of standardized sample autocovariances at a
fixed set of lags converge to a degenerate distribution. The standardized DFTs
at high frequencies converge to a Gaussian law. Our asymptotic results are
strikingly similar for the two DDLRD processes studied. We calibrate our
asymptotic results with a simulation study which also investigates the
properties of the semiparametric log periodogram regression estimator of the
memory parameter.

We report on a deep search for CO(J=3-2) line emission from the host galaxy
of GRB 980425 with the Atacama Submillimeter Telescope Experiment (ASTE). We
observed five points of the galaxy covering the entire region. After combining
all of the spectra, we obtained a global spectrum with the rms noise level of
3.3 mK in T_mb scale at a velocity resolution of 10 km s^-1. No significant
emission was detected, though we find a marginal emission feature in the
velocity range corresponding to the redshift of the galaxy. We derive 3 sigma
upper limits on the global properties: the velocity-integrated CO(3-2)
intensity of I_CO(3-2) < 0.26 K km s^-1 by adopting a velocity width of 67 km
s^-1; the H_2 column density of N(H_2) < 3 x 10^20 cm^-2; the molecular gas
mass of M(H_2) < 3 x 10^8 M_sun, by assuming a CO line luminosity to H_2
molecular gas mass conversion factor of X_CO = 5.0 x 10^20 cm^-2 (K km
s^-1)^-1; and the star formation rate of SFR < 0.1 M_sun yr^-1, based on the
Schmidt law. The SFR is consistent with the previous results of H_alpha and
mid-IR observations, thereby suggesting that there is no significant obscured
star formation in the host galaxy of GRB 980425. This result implies that there
is a variety of GRB hosts with regard to the presence of obscured star
formation.

We present an analysis of the Lyman alpha forests of five quasar spectra in
the near UV. Properties of the intergalactic medium (IGM) at an intermediate
redshift interval (0.9 < z < 1.9) are studied. The amount of baryons in the
diffuse photoionised IGM and the warm-hot intergalactic medium (WHIM) are
traced to get constraints on the redshift evolution of the different phases of
the intergalactic gas. The baryon density of the diffuse IGM is determined with
photoionisation calculations under the assumption of local hydrostatic
equilibrium. We assume that the gas is ionised by a metagalactic background
radiation with a Haardt & Madau (2001) spectrum. The WHIM is traced with broad
Lyman alpha (BLA) absorption. The properties of a number of BLA detections are
studied. Under the assumption of collisional ionisation equilibrium a lower
limit to the baryon density could be estimated. It is found that the diffuse
photoionised IGM contains at least 25% of the total baryonic matter at
redshifts 1 < z < 2. For the WHIM a lower limit of 2.4% could be determined.
Furthermore the data indicates that the intergalactic gas is in a state of
evolution at z=1.5. We confirm that a considerable part of the WHIM is created
between z=1 and z=2.

Our work investigates the infrared (IR) spectroscopy of HCOOH in interstellar ice analogues, with the goal of understanding the formation and evolution of complex organic molecules in cold regions of the interstellar medium. Interstellar ices play a critical role in the formation of molecules that later form planets and life. Formic acid (HCOOH) is a key molecule in the chemical inventory of the interstellar medium and has been detected in several star-forming regions. Hence it is important to study its infrared spectrum, which can reveal important insights into its chemical structure and formation mechanisms in the ice matrix. 

Using a cryogenic Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer, we measured the IR spectra of HCOOH in laboratory-made interstellar ice analogues under astrophysically relevant conditions. We observed the formation of HCOOH in the ice mixture and confirmed through a series of experiments that it is formed via hydrogenation of CO ice. Our results suggest that HCOOH formation proceeds via reactive surface sites in the CO-dominated ice matrix, which implies that other hydrogenated species (e.g., formaldehyde, methanol) could also be produced via the same mechanism. 

Our work provides important insights into the chemistry of the interstellar medium and the formation and evolution of complex organic molecules in cold regions. These findings could have significant implications for understanding the origins of life and the habitability of other planets.

In our study, we present an analysis of an unusually brilliant transient event observed in the galaxy Messier 85. Using data from the Panoramic Survey Telescope and Rapid Response System (Pan-STARRS) and the Las Cumbres Observatory Global Telescope (LCOGT), we have constructed a detailed light curve of the event and performed spectroscopic observations with the Keck Telescope to determine its origin. 

Our analysis reveals that the transient event is consistent with a Type Ia supernova. However, the event is unusually bright, with an estimated peak absolute magnitude of -20.7, which is more luminous than most Type Ia supernovae. We also find that the host galaxy, Messier 85, has a relatively low star formation rate, indicating that the progenitor system may have originated from an older stellar population.

The motivation for our research is to understand the properties and origins of luminous transients in galaxies. Although Type Ia supernovae are well-studied events, the unusually high luminosity of this transient event presents a unique challenge. Our analysis provides insights into the physics and progenitor systems of these rare events, and may have implications for cosmological distance measurements.

Overall, our study highlights the importance of multi-wavelength observations and spectroscopic analysis in characterizing transients, and provides valuable insights into the properties of supernovae in low star formation rate galaxies.

Recently, considerable progress has been made in understanding finite-size
scaling in equilibrium systems. Here, we study finite-size scaling in
non-equilibrium systems at the instance of directed percolation (DP), which has
become the paradigm of non-equilibrium phase transitions into absorbing states,
above, at and below the upper critical dimension. We investigate the
finite-size scaling behavior of DP analytically and numerically by considering
its steady state generated by a homogeneous constant external source on a
d-dimensional hypercube of finite edge length L with periodic boundary
conditions near the bulk critical point. In particular, we study the order
parameter and its higher moments using renormalized field theory. We derive
finite-size scaling forms of the moments in a one-loop calculation. Moreover,
we introduce and calculate a ratio of the order parameter moments that plays a
similar role in the analysis of finite size scaling in absorbing nonequilibrium
processes as the famous Binder cumulant in equilibrium systems and that, in
particular, provides a new signature of the DP universality class. To
complement our analytical work, we perform Monte Carlo simulations which
confirm our analytical results.

High resolution molecular line observations of CS, HCO+, C18O and N2H+ were
obtained toward the starless globule FeSt 1-457 in order to investigate its
kinematics and chemistry. The HCO+ and CS spectra show clear self-reversed and
asymmetric profiles across the face of the globule. The sense of the observed
asymmetry is indicative of the global presence of expansion motions in the
outer layers of the globule. These motions appear to be subsonic and
significantly below the escape velocity of the globule. Comparison of our
observations with near-infrared extinction data indicate that the globule is
gravitationally bound. Taken together these considerations lead us to suggest
that the observed expansion has its origin in an oscillatory motion of the
outer layers of the globule which itself is likely in a quasi-stable state near
hydrostatic equilibrium. Analysis of the observed linewidths of CO and N2H+
confirm that thermal pressure is the dominant component of the cloud's internal
support. A simple calculation suggests that the dominant mode of pulsation
would be an l = 2 mode with a period of 0.3 Myr. Deformation of the globule due
to the large amplitude l = 2 oscillation may be responsible for the
double-peaked structure of the core detected in high resolution extinction
maps. Detailed comparison of the molecular-line observations and extinction
data provides evidence for significant depletion of C18O and perhaps HCO+ while
N2H+ may be undepleted to a cloud depth of about 40 magnitudes of visual
extinction.

In this study, we investigate the mass assembly history of spheroidal galaxies, specifically focusing on whether newly-formed systems arise via major mergers. The main problem this work attempts to solve is understanding the dominant formation mechanism of spheroidal galaxies and its relation to the merging history of these systems. 

Using a sample of 88 spheroidal galaxies at redshifts between z = 1.6 and 0.3, we examine their mass assembly histories through galaxy stellar mass growth rates. We also employ high-resolution simulations to compare with these observations. 

Our results reveal that the majority of these galaxies attained half of their stellar mass by redshift z ~1 through a quiescent mode of star formation. We find evidence for both minor and major mergers contributing to the mass assembly histories of these spheroidal galaxies, but major mergers do not appear to be the dominant contributors. 

Overall, our findings suggest that a combination of various physical processes, such as cold gas accretion and minor mergers, likely produce spheroidal galaxies and that major mergers play a limited role in spheroidal galaxy formation. This work provides crucial insight into the formation mechanisms of spheroidal galaxies and sets the stage for further studies aiming to comprehensively investigate their assembly histories.

We study the influence of the choice of transport coefficients (viscosity and
resistivity) on MHD turbulence driven by the magnetorotational instability
(MRI) in accretion disks. We follow the methodology described in paper I: we
adopt an unstratified shearing box model and focus on the case where the net
vertical magnetic flux threading the box vanishes. For the most part we use the
finite difference code ZEUS, including explicit transport coefficients in the
calculations. However, we also compare our results with those obtained using
other algorithms (NIRVANA, the PENCIL code and a spectral code) to demonstrate
both the convergence of our results and their independence of the numerical
scheme. We find that small scale dissipation affects the saturated state of MHD
turbulence. In agreement with recent similar numerical simulations done in the
presence of a net vertical magnetic flux, we find that turbulent activity
(measured by the rate of angular momentum transport) is an increasing function
of the magnetic Prandtl number Pm for all values of the Reynolds number Re that
we investigated. We also found that turbulence disappears when the Prandtl
number falls below a critical value Pm_c that is apparently a decreasing
function of Re. For the limited region of parameter space that can be probed
with current computational resources, we always obtained Pm_c>1. We conclude
that the magnitudes of the transport coefficients are important in determining
the properties of MHD turbulence in numerical simulations in the shearing box
with zero net flux, at least for Reynolds numbers and magnetic Prandtl numbers
that are such that transport is not dominated by numerical effects and thus can
be probed using current computational resources.

In this work, we investigate the behavior of the fluctuation dynamo and turbulent induction in low magnetic Prandtl number regimes. The magnetic Prandtl number represents the ratio between the viscous and magnetic diffusion timescales and is a fundamental parameter governing the behavior of magnetohydrodynamic systems. We perform numerical simulations of a three-dimensional turbulent flow with an imposed magnetic field, exploring the behavior of small-scale magnetic fluctuations and their amplification through turbulent motions. Our results reveal that the efficiency of the dynamo process in generating magnetic fields is significantly enhanced in low magnetic Prandtl number regimes, where the fluctuations play a crucial role in the magnetic field amplification. We also observe a strong correlation between the dynamo process and turbulent energy production, with the latter playing a fundamental role in the magnetic energy growth. These findings shed new light on the fundamental dynamics of magnetohydrodynamic turbulence and provide new insights into the mechanisms behind astrophysical phenomena such as galactic magnetic fields. Our study contributes to the on-going effort to understand the complex interplay of fluid turbulence and magnetic fields in various physical systems.

In this work, we focused on testing the radiative neutrino mass generation mechanism at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC). We proposed a novel methodology, which allowed us to investigate the impact of a heavy neutrino and a charged scalar in the generation of radiative neutrino masses at the LHC. Our simulations involved heavy neutrino's decay into a lighter neutrino and the charged scalar, which subsequently decayed into a neutrino and a photon. We then investigated the modulation of the invariant mass of the photon-neutrino system, which provided us with a conclusive signature of the radiative neutrino mass generation mechanism. 

Our results showed that the proposed methodology was efficient in testing the mechanism at higher energies than previous experiments, which were carried out at the electroweak scale. We found that the presence of a heavy neutrino enhanced the significance of the signature, allowing us to increase the sensitivity to the mass of the radiative neutrino. Additionally, we demonstrated that the proposed methodology was complementary to other experimental approaches, such as the direct search for heavy neutrinos.

Overall, our work aimed to solve the longstanding problem of testing the radiative neutrino mass generation mechanism at higher energies than previous experiments. We believe that the proposed methodology could pave the way for new studies at the LHC, allowing us to further explore the electroweak sector of the Standard Model.

We present a study of the stratified dust distribution in the GG Tau circumbinary ring based on ALMA observations. Our results show that the dust distribution in the ring is largely influenced by the binary stars and the large-scale pressure gradients caused by the stellar illumination. We find that the dust particles in the inner and outer regions of the ring have different vertical structures due to the different heating sources from the binary stars. We also investigate the impact of the dust dynamics on the ring evolution and find that the dust particles are subject to radial drift, which could lead to the accumulation of planetesimals and the formation of planets. 

The study attempts to solve the open question of the dust evolution in the GG Tau circumbinary ring. Previous models have not fully accounted for the stratification and dynamics of the dust particles, leading to discrepancies between observations and simulations. Our work fills this gap by providing a comprehensive analysis of the dust distribution and dynamics using high-resolution ALMA observations. 

The motivation for this research stems from the unique properties of the GG Tau system, which contains multiple protoplanetary disks and potential sites for planet formation. Understanding the dust evolution in the circumbinary ring is fundamental for constraining the mechanisms of planet formation and the formation history of planetesimal belts. Our findings have implications for the wider study of binary star systems and their role in shaping the architecture and diversity of exoplanetary systems.

In this work, we study the high-energy emission properties of jets and blazars in the context of the Extragalactic Background Light (EBL) using data from the upcoming Gamma-ray Large Area Space Telescope (GLAST) and Energetic X-ray Imaging Survey Telescope (EXIST). The EBL is a diffuse radiation field that pervades the universe and can interact with gamma rays, leading to gamma-ray attenuation and absorption. Jets and blazars, on the other hand, are astrophysical sources that release highly energetic particles along their axes. They are known to emit gamma rays up to tens of TeV, and their emission properties can be influenced by the EBL. Our main motivation is to understand the interplay between these two astrophysical phenomena.

We employ a multi-wavelength observational approach to study the spectral and temporal behavior of jets and blazars across different energy bands. Additionally, we carry out theoretical modeling to interpret our observations and extract physical information about the sources. Our results suggest that the EBL has a significant impact on the gamma-ray emission from blazars, which can be used to infer the EBL density. We also find evidence for spectral variability and flaring activity in several sources, which can provide insights into the acceleration and emission mechanisms of high-energy particles in jets and blazars. Finally, we discuss the implications of our findings for future surveys with GLAST and other high-energy observatories.

In this work, we investigate the derivations and skew derivations of the Grassmann algebras, which have applications in algebra, topology, geometry, and physics. Our motivation for this research stems from the fact that these algebras provide a natural framework for studying multi-vector fields and exterior differential forms on manifolds. Moreover, derivations and skew derivations play a fundamental role in the study of the geometry and topology of these spaces.

The problem that this work attempts to solve is to provide a comprehensive study of the properties and structure of the spaces of derivations and skew derivations of the Grassmann algebras. Specifically, we aim to determine the dimensionality and the algebraic structure of these spaces, and to investigate their relationships with other fundamental objects in mathematics such as Lie algebras and Lie groups.

To achieve our goals, we develop a systematic and rigorous approach based on the algebraic and geometric properties of the Grassmann algebras and their tensor products. We also employ the techniques of differential geometry and algebraic topology to explore the topological and geometric aspects of our results. Our key ideas and methodology involve the use of graded and filtered structures, multilinear algebra, Lie theory, homological algebra, and cohomology theory.

Our work contributes to the understanding of the algebraic and geometric properties of the Grassmann algebras and their applications to mathematics, physics, and engineering. It provides a foundation for future research on multi-vector fields, differential forms, and their derivatives in a unified and systematic way.

Consider a $N\times n$ random matrix $Y_n=(Y_{ij}^{n})$ where the entries are
given by $$ Y_{ij}^{n}=\frac{\sigma_{ij}(n)}{\sqrt{n}} X_{ij}^{n} $$ the
$X_{ij}^{n}$ being centered, independent and identically distributed random
variables with unit variance and $(\sigma_{ij}(n); 1\le i\le N, 1\le j\le n)$
being an array of numbers we shall refer to as a variance profile. We study in
this article the fluctuations of the random variable $$ \log\det(Y_n Y_n^* +
\rho I_N) $$ where $Y^*$ is the Hermitian adjoint of $Y$ and $\rho > 0$ is an
additional parameter. We prove that when centered and properly rescaled, this
random variable satisfies a Central Limit Theorem (CLT) and has a Gaussian
limit whose parameters are identified. A complete description of the scaling
parameter is given; in particular it is shown that an additional term appears
in this parameter in the case where the 4$^\textrm{th}$ moment of the
$X_{ij}$'s differs from the 4$^{\textrm{th}}$ moment of a Gaussian random
variable. Such a CLT is of interest in the field of wireless communications.

In this study, we present a high-resolution ultraviolet spectroscopic analysis of the white dwarf central star of Sh 2-216 using FUSE and HST. Sh 2-216 is a young, compact H II region located in the Galactic plane, known to harbor several massive young stellar objects. The central star of Sh 2-216 has previously been studied using optical spectroscopy; however, there are no existing ultraviolet observations of this object.
Our analysis revealed the presence of several emission features, including C IV, N V, O VI, and Si IV, as well as absorption lines from neutral and singly-ionized metals. We utilized photoionization models to analyze the emission line ratios and determined the properties of the ionized gas surrounding the white dwarf central star.
The motivation for this research stems from the need to understand the properties and formation mechanism of Sh 2-216, as well as the role of the central star in shaping the ionized nebula. Furthermore, this study contributes to the limited number of ultraviolet observations of white dwarf central stars, which can provide valuable insights into their evolution and atmospheres. Our results demonstrate the importance of ultraviolet observations in the study of young compact H II regions and white dwarf central stars.

We derive the CMB radiative transfer equation in the form of a multipole
hierarchy in the nearly-Friedmann-Robertson-Walker limit of homogeneous, but
anisotropic, universes classified via their Bianchi type. Compared with
previous calculations, this allows a more sophisticated treatment of
recombination, produces predictions for the polarization of the radiation, and
allows for reionization. Our derivation is independent of any assumptions about
the dynamical behaviour of the field equations, except that it requires
anisotropies to be small back to recombination; this is already demanded by
observations.
  We calculate the polarization signal in the Bianchi VIIh case, with the
parameters recently advocated to mimic the several large-angle anomalous
features observed in the CMB. We find that the peak polarization signal is ~
1.2 micro K for the best-fit model to the temperature anisotropies, and is
mostly confined to multipoles l<10. Remarkably, the predicted large-angle EE
and TE power spectra in the Bianchi model are consistent with WMAP observations
that are usually interpreted as evidence of early reionization. However, the
power in B-mode polarisation is predicted to be similar to the E-mode power and
parity-violating correlations are also predicted by the model; the WMAP
non-detection of either of these signals casts further strong doubts on the
veracity of attempts to explain the large-angle anomalies with global
anisotropy. On the other hand, given that there exist further dynamical degrees
of freedom in the VIIh universes that are yet to be compared with CMB
observations, we cannot at this time definitively reject the anisotropy
explanation.

In this study, we present our findings from experimenting with recursive queries in both database and logic programming systems. Our motivation lies in the fact that recursive queries have been shown to be powerful tools in handling complex data structures and relationships. However, previous research has mainly focused on exploring recursive queries in either database or logic programming systems, with limited comparison studies on their performance and effectiveness. 

As such, our work aims to solve this problem by conducting an in-depth experimental analysis that compares the performance and scalability of recursive queries implemented in two popular systems: MySQL and Prolog. We used real-world datasets, such as social networks and genealogy databases, to evaluate the systems' ability to handle complex relationships and large amounts of data. 

Our results showed that although both systems allowed for the implementation of recursive queries, there were significant differences in their performance and scalability. Specifically, MySQL demonstrated better performance in handling large datasets, while Prolog was better suited for handling complex relationships. These findings provide valuable insights for practitioners and researchers seeking to implement and optimize recursive queries in database and logic programming systems. Overall, our study contributes to the growing body of research exploring the use of recursive queries in data-intensive applications and highlights the importance of comparing different implementation approaches.

In this paper, we present precision measurements of large scale structure using Type Ia supernova surveys, with the motivation of understanding the nature of dark energy. To achieve our goal, we simulated future observations from the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) and Large Synoptic Survey Telescope (LSST). We used a sample of over 1600 Type Ia supernovae, and our analysis method utilized a joint likelihood function, which included constraints from cosmic microwave background, baryon acoustic oscillations, and Type Ia supernovae.

Our results show that combining DESI and LSST surveys improves the precision of the dark energy equation of state, w, by almost 30%. The current constraints on w are consistent with a cosmological constant, but new surveys will be able to place stronger bounds on alternative models. Additionally, we found that combining multiple probes of dark energy, such as Type Ia supernovae and baryon acoustic oscillations, significantly improves the constraints.

Our work highlights the importance of precise measurements of large scale structure, especially when it comes to understanding the nature of dark energy. In the future, the combination of DESI and LSST will provide us with a comprehensive view of the universe and help us to better understand the mysterious dark energy that drives cosmic acceleration.

We consider an application of the mathematical formalism of quantum mechanics
(QM) outside physics, namely, to game theory. We present a simple game between
macroscopic players, say Alice and Bob (or in a more complex form - Alice, Bob
and Cecilia), which can be represented in the quantum-like (QL) way -- by using
a complex probability amplitude (game's ``wave function'') and noncommutative
operators. The crucial point is that games under consideration are so called
extensive form games. Here the order of actions of players is important, such a
game can be represented by the tree of actions. The QL probabilistic behavior
of players is a consequence of incomplete information which is available to
e.g. Bob about the previous action of Alice. In general one could not construct
a classical probability space underlying a QL-game. This can happen even in a
QL-game with two players. In a QL-game with three players Bell's inequality can
be violated. The most natural probabilistic description is given by so called
contextual probability theory completed by the frequency definition of
probability.

Our work aims to provide a detailed kinetic study of the ring opening of cycloalkanes, which is a fundamental step in the preparation of various organic chemicals. However, the reaction mechanism of this process is still debated and its kinetics remain poorly understood. To solve this problem, we performed CBS-QB3 calculations, which are considered highly accurate for computing reaction energies and rates, to investigate the ring opening of cycloalkanes.

Our key idea was to use computational methods to uncover the reaction mechanism and rate constants of this important organic transformation. We first performed systematic scans of the potential energy surface of ring opening reactions in different cycloalkanes, including cyclobutane, cyclopentane, and cyclohexane, and identified the rate-determining step and other important intermediates. We then calculated the rate constants using transition state theory and examined the effects of different solvents, temperatures, and substituents on the reaction rates.

The motivation for our research is twofold. On one hand, a detailed understanding of the reaction kinetics can help optimize the process parameters and design better catalysts for the ring opening of cycloalkanes, which is a crucial step in the production of polymers, fuels, and pharmaceuticals. On the other hand, our study provides a theoretical benchmark for future experimental investigations of this reaction, and offers insights into the general principles governing the reactivity of cyclic molecules.

Experimental and theoretical studies are presented related to the
ground-state magneto-optical resonance prepared in Cesium vapour confined in an
Extremely Thin Cell (ETC, with thickness equal to the wavelength of the
irradiating light). It is shown that the utilization of the ETC allows one to
examine the formation of a magneto-optical resonance on the individual
hyperfine transitions, thus distinguishing processes resulting in dark (reduced
absorption) or bright (enhanced absorption) resonance formation. We report on
an experimental evidence of the bright magneto-optical resonance sign reversal
in Cs atoms confined in the ETC. A theoretical model is proposed based on the
optical Bloch equations that involves the elastic interaction processes of
atoms in the ETC with its walls resulting in depolarization of the Cs excited
state which is polarized by the exciting radiation. This depolarization leads
to the sign reversal of the bright resonance. Using the proposed model, the
magneto-optical resonance amplitude and width as a function of laser power are
calculated and compared with the experimental ones. The numerical results are
in good agreement with the experiment.

In this work, we probe the structure of gamma-ray burst (GRB) jets with the steep decay phase of their early X-ray afterglows. The steep decay phase is a prominent feature of GRB afterglows that appears within the first few minutes after the burst. Our aim is to use this phase to infer information about the geometry and composition of the GRB jet.

The problem we attempt to solve is that the structure of GRB jets is poorly understood. GRBs are among the most powerful explosions in the Universe, yet we know little about the mechanisms that power them. By probing the structure of GRB jets, we hope to shed light on these mechanisms and gain a better understanding of the physics of these explosive events.

To achieve this, we analyze data from a sample of Swift X-ray Telescope (XRT) GRB afterglows. We fit the early X-ray light curves with a model that takes into account the geometry and composition of the jet. Our analysis reveals a wide range of jet structures and compositions, indicating that GRB jets are complex and varied.

In conclusion, our work sheds new light on the structure of GRB jets and provides insight into the physics of these explosive events. The results highlight the need for more detailed observations and modeling to fully understand these phenomena.

In this work, we investigate the phenomenology of the next-to-minimal supersymmetric standard model (nMSSM) from both collider experiments and cosmological observations. Our main motivation for conducting this research is the need to explore the potential of the nMSSM to explain dark matter and the hierarchy problem. We examine several scenarios in which the nMSSM can accommodate viable dark matter candidates and present constraints on the model parameters from the LHC and other experiments. We also perform a comprehensive study of the cosmological implications of the nMSSM, specifically focusing on the evolution of dark matter abundance.

To achieve these results, we adopt a phenomenological approach, combining numerical simulations and theoretical calculations. We use the latest experimental data from the LHC and estimate the expected sensitivities of future experiments. We also implement a Monte Carlo analysis of the parameter space of the nMSSM to determine the regions that are consistent with observed dark matter abundance and other cosmological constraints.

Overall, our study reveals that the nMSSM can provide a viable explanation for dark matter, but also highlights the need for more precise measurements and future discoveries to fully assess its potential. The results presented here can guide future experimental and observational efforts in probing supersymmetry and its role in cosmology.

In this Paper we present high angular resolution imaging of 3
intermediate-mass (IM) stars using the Plateau de Bure Interferometer (PdBI).
In particular we present the chemical study we have carried out towards the IM
hot core NGC 7129--FIRS 2. This is the first chemical study in an IM hot core
and provides important hints to understand the dependence of the hot core
chemistry on the stellar luminosity. We also present our high angular
resolution (0.3") images of the borderline Class 0-Class I object IC1396 N.
These images trace the warm region of this IM protostar with unprecedent detail
(0.3"\sim200 AU at the distance of IC1396 N) and provide the first detection of
a cluster of IM hot cores. Finally, we present our interferometric continuum
and spectroscopic images of the disk around the Herbig Be star R Mon. We have
determined the kinematics and physical structure of the disk associated with
this B0 star. The low spectral index derived from the dust emission as well as
the flat geometry of the disk suggest a more rapid evolution of the disks
associated with massive stars. In the Discussion, we dare to propose a possible
evolutionary sequence for the warm circumstellar material around IM stars.

Our work on "The Distance Geometry of Music" explores a novel approach to analyzing musical compositions using geometric concepts. We address the central problem of quantifying the relationship between different musical pieces and identifying patterns and structures that exist across musical genres. To achieve this, we use distance geometry, a branch of mathematics that deals with distances and shapes in geometric space.

Our research reveals that musical pieces have a fundamental geometric structure that is governed by the underlying musical motifs and themes. Through analysis of the distribution of musical intervals, we show that different musical genres can be characterized by distinct geometric signatures, allowing us to classify music based on inherent structural properties. Our approach represents a new paradigm for music analysis and has important implications for fields such as music psychology, music theory, and music production.
