appear to be invariant with respect to changes in the Mach number. For
instance, at Mach 6 the slope of the power spectrum of v is -1.69, and the
exponent of the third-order structure function of v is unity. We also directly
measure the mass dimension of the "fractal" density distribution in the
inertial subrange, D_m = 2.4, which is similar to the observed fractal
dimension of molecular clouds and agrees well with the cascade phenomenology.

In this study, we investigate the characteristics of the transition region using Hinode/EIS data and its diagnostic capabilities. The transition region marks the significant shift in temperature from the chromosphere to the coronal temperature. It is a complex and dynamic region where highly active phenomena occur that profoundly influence the solar atmosphere and significantly affect the space environment, including the Earth's climate. 

We apply several diagnostic techniques to explore the transition region features, including spectral line intensity ratios, line profile asymmetries, and outflows. Our approach enhances our understanding of the processes occurring in the transition region and how they interplay with the surrounding atmospheric layers. We detect strong evidence of explosive events and plasma ejections occurring in the transition region. Additionally, we determine the differential emission measure of the region and their correlation with the dynamics of the transition region. Our findings suggest that the processes occurring in the transition region play a crucial role in the heating and dynamics of the coronal atmosphere.

Our research addresses the problem of understanding the dynamism and complexity of the transition region and the processes that occur within it. This knowledge is significant in the study of the solar atmosphere and its influence on space weather and its potential impacts on the Earth's climate. Our findings may also provide further insight into the coronal heating problem, which seeks to understand how the corona is heated to several million degrees in contrast to the cooler temperatures of the solar surface.

A method is given for solving an optimal H2 approximation problem for SISO
linear time-invariant stable systems. The method, based on constructive
algebra, guarantees that the global optimum is found; it does not involve any
gradient-based search, and hence avoids the usual problems of local minima. We
examine mostly the case when the model order is reduced by one, and when the
original system has distinct poles. This case exhibits special structure which
allows us to provide a complete solution. The problem is converted into linear
algebra by exhibiting a finite-dimensional basis for a certain space, and can
then be solved by eigenvalue calculations, following the methods developed by
Stetter and Moeller. The use of Buchberger's algorithm is avoided by writing
the first-order optimality conditions in a special form, from which a Groebner
basis is immediately available. Compared with our previous work the method
presented here has much smaller time and memory requirements, and can therefore
be applied to systems of significantly higher McMillan degree. In addition,
some hypotheses which were required in the previous work have been removed.
Some examples are included.

Numerical simulations suggest that active galactic nuclei (AGNs) play an
important role in the formation of early-type galaxies by expelling gas and
dust in powerful galactic winds and quenching star formation. However, the
existence of AGN feedback capable of halting galaxy-wide star formation has yet
to be observationally confirmed. To investigate this question, we have obtained
spectra of 14 post-starburst galaxies at z~0.6 to search for evidence of
galactic winds. In 10/14 galaxies we detect Mg II 2796,2803 absorption lines
which are blueshifted by 490 - 2020 km/s with respect to the stars. The median
blueshift is 1140 km/s. We hypothesize that the outflowing gas represents a
fossil galactic wind launched near the peak of the galaxy's activity, a few 100
Myr ago. The velocities we measure are intermediate between those of luminous
starbursts and broad absorption line quasars, which suggests that feedback from
an AGN may have played a role in expelling cool gas and shutting down star
formation.

This paper aims to investigate the role of geometry and topology in relativistic cosmology. We utilize the fundamental principles of general relativity and topology to understand the structure and evolution of the universe at a large scale. We propose that the topology of the universe plays a critical role in determining its geometry.

Our methodology involves both analytical and numerical techniques to obtain accurate results. We start by employing the standard cosmological model, which assumes that the universe is homogeneous and isotropic on large scales. We then explore the implications of different topologies, including flat, open, and closed universes, in determining the geometry of the universe.

Furthermore, we investigate the fundamental properties of cosmic strings and their impact on the topology of the universe. We analyze both the observational and theoretical evidence for the existence of cosmic strings and the implications for the topology of the universe.

The results obtained indicate that the topology of the universe has a significant impact on its geometry and evolution. The cosmic microwave background (CMB) radiation provides observational evidence that supports the idea of a flat universe with a topology that is consistent with the standard cosmological model. However, our analysis also suggests that the existence of cosmic strings could lead to a non-trivial topology, which would have important implications for the evolution of the universe.

Overall, this work contributes to our understanding of the interplay between geometry and topology in relativistic cosmology and highlights the importance of topology in determining the properties and evolution of the universe.

We present an autonomous distributed admission control scheme for IEEE 802.11 DCF (Distributed Coordination Function) that aims to improve network performance by reducing channel contention and collisions. Our research is motivated by the fact that current admission control schemes are centralized and unable to adapt to the dynamic changes in network traffic, leading to congestion and reduced network throughput. To address this challenge, our proposed scheme employs a distributed approach using a lightweight algorithm that allows nodes to dynamically control their admission requests based on network conditions. 

To evaluate the effectiveness of our scheme, we conducted simulations using NS-3 and compared its performance against other existing schemes. Our results show that our proposed scheme outperforms other schemes by achieving higher network throughput, lower latency, and reduced packet loss under different traffic loads. Moreover, our scheme was able to adapt to changes in network conditions, providing users with fair access to network resources.

In summary, our work proposes a novel distributed admission control scheme for IEEE 802.11 DCF networks that addresses the dynamic changes in network traffic, leading to improved network performance. Our approach provides flexibility, adaptability, and fair resource allocation to network nodes, as demonstrated by our simulation results.

We present a systematic study of exclusive charmless nonleptonic two-body B
decays including axial-vector mesons in the final state. We calculate branching
ratios of B\to PA, VA and AA decays, where A, V and P denote an axial-vector, a
vector and a pseudoscalar meson, respectively. We assume naive factorization
hypothesis and use the improved version of the nonrelativistic ISGW quark model
for form factors in B\to A transitions. We include contributions that arise
from the effective \Delta B=1 weak Hamiltonian H_{eff}. The respective
factorized amplitude of these decays are explicitly showed and their penguin
contributions are classified. We find that decays B^-to a_1^0\pi^-,\barB^0\to
a_1^{\pm}\pi^{\mp}, B^-\to a_1^-\bar K^0, \bar B^0\to a_1^+K^-, \bar B^0\to
f_1\bar K^0, B^-\to f_1K^-, B^-\to K_1^-(1400)\etap, B^-\to b_1^-\bar K^{0},
and \bar B^0\to b_1^+\pi^-(K^-) have branching ratios of the order of 10^{-5}.
We also study the dependence of branching ratios for B \to K_1P(V,A) decays
(K_1=K_1(1270),K_1(1400)) with respect to the mixing angle between K_A and K_B.

In this work, we investigate the impact of spatially distributed ionisation sources on the temperature structure of HII regions using numerical simulations. We focus on HII regions that are ionised by clusters of massive stars, which have been observed to exhibit complex temperature structures that are not well understood. The main motivation for our research is to gain a better understanding of how ionisation sources influence the temperature distribution within HII regions, which can help us interpret observational data and better constrain star formation models.

Our approach involves using the radiation hydrodynamics code, RAPTOR, to simulate the evolution of HII regions ionised by different distributions of massive stars. We vary the number and location of ionising sources to investigate how this affects the temperature structure of the HII regions. We also consider the impact of different stellar populations, such as those with varying masses and ages.

Our main finding is that the distribution of ionising sources strongly influences the temperature structure of the HII region, with denser clusters resulting in higher temperatures. We also find that the location of the ionising sources can lead to temperature gradients within the HII region. Our results demonstrate the importance of considering the spatial distribution of ionising sources when interpreting observations of HII regions.

In this study, we investigate the nature of high-energy gamma-rays produced in cosmic-ray interactions with interstellar gas and radiation fields, and their role in shaping the TeV-band spectrum of the RX J1713.7-3946 supernova remnant. Our research is motivated by the need to understand the origin of the diffuse gamma-ray emission observed in the sky, which is believed to be a significant component of the cosmic-ray energy budget. Additionally, we aim to shed light on the complex interplay between cosmic rays and their environment, and the mechanisms responsible for their acceleration and propagation. 
 
Using the Fermi Large Area Telescope (LAT), we have analyzed seven years of gamma-ray data and performed a detailed spectral and morphological study of the RX J1713.7-3946 region. Our results show that the diffuse gamma-ray emission is consistent with the expected contribution from cosmic-ray interactions, with the proton-proton interaction being the dominant process in the TeV-band. Furthermore, our analysis reveals a significant correlation between the gamma-ray and radio synchrotron emissions, suggesting a common origin for both phenomena.  
 
In summary, our work provides compelling evidence for the production of high-energy gamma-rays in cosmic-ray interactions with the interstellar medium, and sheds light on the origin and nature of the diffuse gamma-ray component in our Galaxy. These findings have important implications for our understanding of the cosmic-ray population and their impact on the surrounding environment.

Gravitational waves (GWs) from the inspiral of a neutron star (NS) or
stellar-mass black hole (BH) into an intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH) with
mass between ~50 and ~350 solar masses may be detectable by the planned
advanced generation of ground-based GW interferometers. Such intermediate mass
ratio inspirals (IMRIs) are most likely to be found in globular clusters. We
analyze four possible IMRI formation mechanisms: (1) hardening of an NS-IMBH or
BH-IMBH binary via three-body interactions, (2) hardening via Kozai resonance
in a hierarchical triple system, (3) direct capture, and (4) inspiral of a
compact object from a tidally captured main-sequence star; we also discuss
tidal effects when the inspiraling object is an NS. For each mechanism we
predict the typical eccentricities of the resulting IMRIs. We find that IMRIs
will have largely circularized by the time they enter the sensitivity band of
ground-based detectors. Hardening of a binary via three-body interactions,
which is likely to be the dominant mechanism for IMRI formation, yields
eccentricities under 10^-4 when the GW frequency reaches 10 Hz. Even among
IMRIs formed via direct captures, which can have the highest eccentricities,
around 90% will circularize to eccentricities under 0.1 before the GW frequency
reaches 10 Hz. We estimate the rate of IMRI coalescences in globular clusters
and the sensitivity of a network of three Advanced LIGO detectors to the
resulting GWs. We show that this detector network may see up to tens of IMRIs
per year, although rates of one to a few per year may be more plausible. We
also estimate the loss in signal-to-noise ratio that will result from using
circular IMRI templates for data analysis and find that, for the eccentricities
we expect, this loss is negligible.

The measured low initial sticking probability of oxygen molecules at the
Al(111) surface that had puzzled the field for many years was recently
explained in a non-adiabatic picture invoking spin-selection rules [J. Behler
et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 94, 036104 (2005)]. These selection rules tend to
conserve the initial spin-triplet character of the free O2 molecule during the
molecule's approach to the surface. A new locally-constrained
density-functional theory approach gave access to the corresponding
potential-energy surface (PES) seen by such an impinging spin-triplet molecule
and indicated barriers to dissociation which reduce the sticking probability.
Here, we further substantiate this non-adiabatic picture by providing a
detailed account of the employed approach. Building on the previous work, we
focus in particular on inaccuracies in present-day exchange-correlation
functionals. Our analysis shows that small quantitative differences in the
spin-triplet constrained PES obtained with different gradient-corrected
functionals have a noticeable effect on the lowest kinetic energy part of the
resulting sticking curve.

The magnetic and thermodynamic properties of spin-1/2 Heisenberg diamond
chains are investigated in three different cases: (a) J1, J2, J3>0
(frustrated); (b) J1, J3<0, J2>0 (frustrated); and (c) J1, J2>0, J3<0
(non-frustrated). The density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) technique is
invoked to study the properties of the system in the ground state, while the
transfer matrix renormalization group (TMRG) technique is applied to explore
the thermodynamic properties. The local magnetic moments, spin correlation
functions, and static structure factors are discussed in the ground state for
the three cases. It is shown that the static structure factor S(q) shows peaks
at wavevectors $q=a\pi /3$ (a=0,1,2,3,4,5) for different couplings in a zero
magnetic field, which, however in the magnetic fields where the magnetization
plateau with m=1/6 pertains, exhibits the peaks only at q=0, $2\pi /3$ and
$4\pi /3$, which are found to be couplings-independent. The DMRG results of the
zero-field static structure factor can be nicely fitted by a linear
superposition of six modes, where two fitting equations are proposed. It is
observed that the six modes are closely related to the low-lying excitations of
the system. At finite temperatures, the double-peak structures of the
susceptibility and specific heat against temperature are obtained, where the
peak positions and heights are found to depend on the competition of the
couplings. It is also uncovered that the XXZ anisotropy of F and AF couplings
leads the system of case (c) to display quite different behaviors. In addition,
the experimental data of the susceptibility, specific heat and magnetization
for the compound Cu$_{3}$(CO$_{3}$)$_{2}$(OH)$_{2}$ are fairly compared with
our TMRG results.

In this research project, we examine the potential impact of a fifth large-scale space-time dimension on the conservation of energy in a four-dimensional Universe. Our motivation stems from the hypothesis that a fifth dimension may exist, and if it does, it could have significant consequences on the laws of physics. 

We approached the problem by considering hypothetical scenarios and equations that described the behavior of energy in a four-dimensional space-time compared to a five-dimensional one. We found that the conservation of energy in a four-dimensional Universe was affected by the presence of a fifth dimension. Specifically, the conservation of energy was no longer absolute but instead proportional to the energy available in the fifth dimension. 

Our results hold important implications for our understanding of the fundamental laws of physics and the conservation of energy, as well as for research in fields such as cosmology and particle physics. We also pose new questions for further investigation, such as the nature and effects of higher-dimensional spaces on our physical world. 

This work attempts to address a problem posed by the hypothesis of a fifth dimension and its potential consequences for the conservation of energy. By providing mathematical models and analysis, this research contributes to ongoing scientific discourse and helps to push the boundaries of our current understanding of the Universe.

In this paper we demonstrate a technique of utilizing magnetically trapped
neutral Rb-87 atoms to measure the magnitude and direction of stray electric
fields emanating from surface contaminants. We apply an alternating external
electric field that adds to (or subtracts from) the stray field in such a way
as to resonantly drive the trapped atoms into a mechanical dipole oscillation.
The growth rate of the oscillation's amplitude provides information about the
magnitude and sign of the stray field gradient. Using this measurement
technique, we are able to reconstruct the vector electric field produced by
surface contaminants. In addition, we can accurately measure the electric
fields generated from adsorbed atoms purposely placed onto the surface and
account for their systematic effects, which can plague a precision
surface-force measurement. We show that baking the substrate can reduce the
electric fields emanating from adsorbate, and that the mechanism for reduction
is likely surface diffusion, not desorption.

Supernova 1987A in the Large Magellanic Cloud provides a spectacularly
detailed view of the aftermath of a core-collapse explosion. The supernova
ejecta initially coasted outward at more than 10% of the speed of light, but in
1990 were observed to decelerate rapidly as they began to encounter dense
circumstellar material expelled by the progenitor star. The resulting shock has
subsequently produced steadily brightening radio synchrotron emission, which is
resolved by the Australia Telescope Compact Array (ATCA) into an expanding
limb-brightened shell. Here we present 15 years of ATCA imaging of Supernova
1987A, at an effective angular resolution of 0.4 arcsec. We find that the radio
remnant has accelerated in its expansion over this period, from approx 3600
km/s in 1992 to approx 5200 km/s at the end of 2006. The published diameters of
the evolving X-ray shell have been ~15% smaller than the corresponding radio
values, but a simultaneous Fourier analysis of both radio and X-ray data
eliminates this discrepancy, and yields a current diameter for the shell in
both wave-bands of approx 1.7 arcsec. An asymmetric brightness distribution is
seen in radio images at all ATCA epochs: the eastern and western rims have
higher fluxes than the northern and southern regions, indicating that most of
the radio emission comes from the equatorial plane of the system, where the
progenitor star's circumstellar wind is thought to be densest. The eastern lobe
is brighter than and further from the supernova site than the western lobe,
suggesting an additional asymmetry in the initial distribution of supernova
ejecta.

Three-dimensional (3D) chromatin structure is closely related to genome
function, in particular transcription. However, the folding path of the
chromatin fiber in the interphase nucleus is unknown. Here, we systematically
measured the 3D physical distance between pairwise labeled genomic positions in
gene-dense, highly transcribed domains and gene-poor less active areas on
chromosomes 1 and 11 in G1 nuclei of human primary fibroblasts, using
fluorescence in situ hybridization. Interpretation of our results and those
published by others, based on polymer physics, shows that the folding of the
chromatin fiber can be described as a polymer in a globular state (GS),
maintained by intra-polymer attractive interactions that counteract
self-avoidance forces. The GS polymer model is able to describe chromatin
folding in as well the highly expressed domains as the lowly expressed ones,
indicating that they differ in Kuhn length and chromatin compaction. Each type
of genomic domain constitutes an ensemble of relatively compact globular
folding states, resulting in a considerable cellto- cell variation between
otherwise identical cells. We present evidence for different polymer folding
regimes of the chromatin fiber on the length scale of a few mega base pairs and
on that of complete chromosome arms (several tens of Mb). Our results present a
novel view on the folding of the chromatin fiber in interphase and open the
possibility to explore the nature of the intra-chromatin fiber interactions.

Non-perturbative corrections and modular properties of four-dimensional type
IIB Calabi-Yau orientifolds are discussed. It is shown that certain
non-perturbative alpha' corrections survive in the large volume limit of the
orientifold and periodically correct the Kahler potential. These corrections
depend on the NS-NS two form and have to be completed by D-instanton
contributions to transform covariantely under symmetries of the type IIB
orientifold background. It is shown that generically also the D-instanton
superpotential depends on the two-form moduli as well as on the complex
dilaton. These contributions can arise through theta-functions with the dilaton
as modular parameter. An orientifold of the Enriques Calabi-Yau allows to
illustrate these general considerations. It is shown that this compactification
leads to a controlled four-dimensional N=1 effective theory due to the absence
of various quantum corrections. Making contact to the underlying topological
string theory the D-instanton superpotential is proposed to be related to a
specific modular form counting D3, D1, D(-1) degeneracies on the Enriques
Calabi-Yau.

In this work, we investigate the behavior of self-dual critical Z(N) spin systems in the presence of the spontaneously broken Z(2) symmetry, commonly known as spontaneous symmetry breaking. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that this type of symmetry breaking is a fundamental phenomenon in many physical systems, including magnetism, superconductivity, and particle physics. Using the powerful tools of Conformal Field Theory (CFT), we derive and analyze the critical exponents of the Singular Limit of Entanglement (SLE) curves in these systems. Specifically, we focus on the critical behavior of the so-called restricted solid-on-solid (RSOS) model and its connection to the self-dual criticality of the 3-state Potts model.

Through a detailed analysis of the SLE curves, we obtain precise CFT predictions for the anomalous dimensions, the central charge, and the conformal blocks of the RSOS model. Our results provide strong evidence for the universality of the self-dual criticality and shed new light on the critical behavior of the Z(N) spin systems. The methodology we use involves a combination of numerical simulations, CFT calculations, and analytical techniques such as the Coulomb gas formalism and the conformal bootstrap equations. Our findings pave the way for future research on the critical properties of self-dual Z(N) spin systems and their applications in condensed matter physics, statistical mechanics, and quantum field theory.

In this study, we present efficient simulations of early structure formation and reionization, aimed at investigating the key physical processes that shaped the high-redshift Universe. Our work attempts to solve the computational challenge posed by the need for large-scale simulations that capture the complex interplay between gravity, hydrodynamics, radiative transfer, and non-equilibrium chemistry. The key innovation of our approach is a hybrid technique that combines particle-based N-body simulations with grid-based hydrodynamics and radiative transfer solvers. This hybrid approach allows us to accurately follow the formation of the first galaxies and the reionization of the intergalactic medium, while also maximizing computational efficiency.

Our simulations show that the early Universe was a highly dynamic and evolving system, with the first stars and galaxies appearing by redshifts as early as 20. Our results shed light on the role of feedback processes, such as supernovae explosions and ionizing radiation, in regulating the formation of structures and driving the reionization of the Universe. Moreover, our simulations enable us to make predictions for a range of observables, such as the 21-cm signal, that can be used to test cosmological models and constrain the properties of dark matter and dark energy. Our study thus provides a powerful tool for exploring the physics of the early Universe and its observational signatures.

In this study, we investigate the closed bosonic string field theory at quintic order II, focusing on marginal deformations and effective potential. Our research aims to address the limitations in existing string theories by developing a deeper understanding of the dynamics of the closed bosonic string field. 

The motivation for our study is the need for a comprehensive and unified framework for studying all string theories. Although string theory has become the leading candidate for a unified fundamental theory of physics, it faces significant conceptual and technical challenges, such as the existence of different string theories and the identification of a vacuum state. We believe that understanding the dynamics of the closed bosonic string field theory can contribute to a better understanding of string theory in general.

Our results show that the effective potential of the closed bosonic string field is invariant under marginal deformations, which is a novel finding. We also demonstrate that the effects of marginal deformations can be captured through the variation of the effective potential. Furthermore, our analysis reveals the existence of a duality symmetry that relates two different marginal deformations. These findings contribute to our understanding of the closed bosonic string field theory and may have implications for other areas of physics.

Overall, our research provides a deeper insight into the dynamics of the closed bosonic string field theory, which may contribute to a broader understanding of string theory.

We present the first measurement of a rapid increase in the Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) fraction in high-redshift clusters of galaxies. The motivation for this research stems from the need to understand the role of AGNs in the formation and evolution of clusters of galaxies. AGNs release large amounts of energy in the form of radiation and jets, which can have a significant impact on the surrounding environment, affecting galaxy formation and evolution.

Using data from the Spitzer Adaptation of the Red-sequence Cluster Survey, we identified 36 clusters at redshifts 0.8<z<1.5 and measured the AGN fraction in each cluster. We found a significant increase in the AGN fraction with increasing redshift, with a factor of ~3 increase between redshifts 0.8 and 1.5. This result is consistent with previous observations of AGN evolution in the field, and indicates that the cluster environment may play an important role in triggering AGN activity.

The problem this work attempts to solve is the lack of information on the evolution of AGNs in high-redshift clusters of galaxies. Our measurement of the rapidly increasing AGN fraction in these clusters provides new insight into the mechanisms driving AGN activity in these environments and may help to inform models of galaxy evolution.

The TACTIC Atmospheric Cherenkov Imaging Telescope is an innovative solution to a longstanding problem in the field of astrophysics. Our research aims to improve the detection and analysis of very-high-energy (VHE) gamma rays, which originate from astrophysical sources such as pulsars, supernovae, and active galactic nuclei. The key challenge in detecting these elusive particles is their low flux, which requires sophisticated instruments with high sensitivity and resolution. 

Our team of researchers has developed the TACTIC telescope, which employs the atmospheric Cherenkov imaging technique to observe VHE gamma rays. The telescope consists of a mirror dish, a camera system, and data acquisition and analysis software. The dish focuses Cherenkov light emitted by air showers, generated when gamma rays interact with the Earth's atmosphere. The camera system records the images of these showers and analyzes them to identify gamma ray events. 

The motivation for developing the TACTIC telescope is twofold: first, to advance our understanding of the universe by detecting and studying VHE gamma rays and their sources, and second, to improve the technology and methodology of gamma ray astronomy. Our research is important for the broader scientific community, as it contributes to ongoing efforts to understand the fundamental physics of the universe.

In this study, we investigate the photospheric environment of a solar pore with a light bridge. The motivation for this research is to understand the characteristics of the magnetic fields that create and maintain these structures on the solar surface. Pores are dark and compact regions on the photosphere that are associated with weak magnetic fields. The presence of a light bridge, however, implies that there is a stronger magnetic field in the surrounding region. The problem this work attempts to solve is to understand how these two kinds of magnetic fields interact with one another in the formation and evolution of these structures.

Using high-resolution observations taken with the Solar Dynamics Observatory, we analyze the photospheric magnetic field in and around a pore with a light bridge. We use the magnetic field information to compute various parameters that characterize the photospheric environment, such as magnetic flux, magnetic inclination, and magnetic shear. Our results indicate that the magnetic field in the region of the light bridge is more complex than the magnetic field in the surrounding pore, with multiple magnetic polarities and a higher degree of magnetic shear. These findings imply that the light bridge is a site of magnetic reconnection, where the magnetic fields of opposite polarities interact and release magnetic energy. Our work provides valuable insights into the physical processes that govern the generation and evolution of solar magnetic structures.

In this work, we investigated the remnant evolution after a carbon-oxygen white dwarf merger. The problem we addressed is the lack of understanding of the fate of the remnant of such a merger, which is important to understand the evolution of binary systems and the production of various astronomical phenomena, including supernovae type Ia.

Our key idea is to model the remnant evolution using a one-dimensional hydrodynamical code that considers the effects of nuclear burning, convective mixing, and heat transport. We considered different models with different initial conditions and masses to explore the parameter space of the problem.

Our methodology involved numerical simulations and data analysis to obtain the density, temperature, and composition profiles of the remnant at different times after the merger. We also calculated the nuclear burning energy release, the convective mixing time, and the mass loss rate of the remnant.

Our results show that the remnant of the carbon-oxygen white dwarf merger can undergo a series of phases, including thermonuclear runaway, convective mixing, and mass loss, depending on its initial conditions and mass. The energy release from nuclear burning can lead to the formation of high-velocity outflows that could explain the observed properties of some peculiar supernovae. Our study provides new insights into the remnant evolution after a carbon-oxygen white dwarf merger and their significance in astrophysics.

This is a sequel to \cite{li-qva1} and \cite{li-qva2} in a series to study
vertex algebra-like structures arising from various algebras such as quantum
affine algebras and Yangians. In this paper, we study two versions of the
double Yangian $DY_{\hbar}(sl_{2})$, denoted by $DY_{q}(sl_{2})$ and
$DY_{q}^{\infty}(sl_{2})$ with $q$ a nonzero complex number. For each nonzero
complex number $q$, we construct a quantum vertex algebra $V_{q}$ and prove
that every $DY_{q}(sl_{2})$-module is naturally a $V_{q}$-module. We also show
that $DY_{q}^{\infty}(sl_{2})$-modules are what we call
$V_{q}$-modules-at-infinity. To achieve this goal, we study what we call
$\S$-local subsets and quasi-local subsets of $\Hom (W,W((x^{-1})))$ for any
vector space $W$, and we prove that any $\S$-local subset generates a (weak)
quantum vertex algebra and that any quasi-local subset generates a vertex
algebra with $W$ as a (left) quasi module-at-infinity. Using this result we
associate the Lie algebra of pseudo-differential operators on the circle with
vertex algebras in terms of quasi modules-at-infinity.

In this work, we investigate the obfuscation complexity of planar graphs, a problem that has significant implications in cryptography and network security. Specifically, we focus on the question of how to efficiently mask the structure of planar graphs so that an attacker cannot extract any useful information or exploit vulnerabilities.

Our research approach consists of analyzing various obfuscation techniques from the literature and proposing new ones, and evaluating their effectiveness and efficiency in terms of measures such as obfuscation ratio and execution time. We also provide theoretical bounds on the complexity of the problem, showing that it is NP-hard in general and providing polynomial-time algorithms for some restricted cases.

The results of this work suggest that planar graph obfuscation is a challenging problem that is still not fully understood or solved. Our contributions include a better understanding of the feasibility and limitations of various obfuscation methods, as well as some new practical solutions that can be used in real-world applications.

In summary, this work aims to address an important and practical problem by providing a comprehensive analysis of obfuscation complexity in planar graphs, and by proposing novel algorithms and techniques to improve their security and privacy.

The four-body Yakubovsky equations in a Three-Dimensional approach with the
inclusion of the three-body forces is proposed. The four-body bound state with
two- and three-body interactions is formulated in Three-Dimensional approach
for identical particles as function of vector Jacobi momenta, specifically the
magnitudes of the momenta and the angles between them. The modified three
dimensional Yakubovsky integral equations is successfully solved with the
scalar two-meson exchange three-body force where the Malfliet-Tjon-type
two-body force is implemented. The three-body force effects on the energy
eigenvalue and the four-body wave function, as well as accuracy of our
numerical calculations are presented.The four-body Yakubovsky equations in a
Three-Dimensional approach with the inclusion of the three-body forces is
proposed. The four-body bound state with two- and three-body interactions is
formulated in Three-Dimensional approach for identical particles as function of
vector Jacobi momenta, specifically the magnitudes of the momenta and the
angles between them. The modified three dimensional Yakubovsky integral
equations is successfully solved with the scalar two-meson exchange three-body
force where the Malfliet-Tjon-type two-body force is implemented. The
three-body force effects on the energy eigenvalue and the four-body wave
function, as well as accuracy of our numerical calculations are presented.

In this study, we investigate the influence of the cylindrical shape of the nucleosomes and H1 defects on the properties of chromatin. The motivation for this work stems from the fact that chromatin plays a crucial role in DNA packaging and gene expression, and any alteration in its properties can have significant consequences on various cellular processes. 

Previous research has established that the cylindrical shape of nucleosomes is important for the formation of compact chromatin fibers and the efficiency of transcription. Furthermore, H1 linker histones have been shown to regulate chromatin structure and accessibility to transcription factors. However, the molecular mechanisms underlying these phenomena remain poorly understood. 

In our study, we employed coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations to investigate the effects of cylindrical shape and H1 defects on chromatin properties. Our results show that cylindrical nucleosomes favor the formation of regular chromatin fibers and promote compaction, while H1 defects destabilize chromatin structure and decrease fiber density. We also observed that H1 defects induce local chromatin unfolding, leading to enhanced accessibility to transcription factors. 

In conclusion, our study sheds light on the crucial role of cylindrical nucleosomes and H1 linker histones in chromatin structure and accessibility to transcription factors. These findings may have important implications for understanding the regulation of gene expression and its role in various cellular processes.

In our work, we presented a large acceptance scintillator detector with wavelength shifting fibre read-out, which was specifically designed for the search of eta-nucleus bound states. The detection of these bound states can provide valuable information about the interaction between mesons and nuclei, which is of great interest in the field of nuclear physics.

The main idea behind our detector was to provide a high-efficiency and low-background detection system for eta mesons, which have a short lifetime and are often difficult to distinguish from background signals. The detector had a large active area of 1 m², which made it possible to detect multiple particles simultaneously and to better discriminate signals from backgrounds.

The key methodology we used was the combination of scintillation and wavelength shifting fibre technology, which allowed the detection of both charged and neutral particles. The scintillation material provided the initial energy deposition, while the wavelength shifting fibre read-out system allowed for efficient light collection and signal transmission.

Our results showed that the detector had a high detection efficiency, particularly for low-energy eta mesons, and a low background rate. The detector also had a good spatial resolution, which allowed for the identification of the origin of particles in the detector.

Overall, our work provides a valuable contribution to the search for eta-nucleus bound states and highlights the potential of large acceptance scintillator detectors with wavelength shifting fibre read-out for nuclear physics research.

In this work, we investigate the influence of the Dirac sea on pion propagation in asymmetric nuclear matter. Aiming to better understand pion dynamics in dense nuclear environments, we consider a self-consistent chiral model incorporating the Dirac sea, which is known to play a fundamental role in describing the properties of the nucleon in nuclear matter. Our results show that the Dirac sea significantly contributes to the pion dispersion relation in asymmetric nuclear matter, leading to a considerable increase in the pion effective mass, especially in the presence of large isospin asymmetries. We find that the pion velocity decreases in the presence of the Dirac sea for all values of isospin asymmetry. Our study also reveals the crucial role of the isovector part of the pion-nucleon interaction in modulating the effective mass and velocity of the pion in nuclear matter.

The motivation for our research lies in the importance of understanding the behavior of pions in dense nuclear matter, which has important implications for the dynamics of many astrophysical phenomena, such as supernova explosions and neutron star mergers. Our work provides a novel theoretical framework that incorporates the effects of the Dirac sea on pion propagation, which may have previously been neglected in many theoretical models. To achieve our results, we use a self-consistent chiral model and apply various numerical techniques to solve the model equations. Our study offers insights into the role of the Dirac sea in pion-nucleon interactions and contributes to a better understanding of the properties of hadrons in dense nuclear environments.

This is a sequel of our paper hep-th/0606125 in which we have studied the
{\cal N}=1 SU(N) SYM theory obtained as a marginal deformation of the {\cal
N}=4 theory, with a complex deformation parameter \beta and in the planar
limit. There we have addressed the issue of conformal invariance imposing the
theory to be finite and we have found that finiteness requires reality of the
deformation parameter \beta. In this paper we relax the finiteness request and
look for a theory that in the planar limit has vanishing beta functions. We
perform explicit calculations up to five loop order: we find that the
conditions of beta function vanishing can be achieved with a complex
deformation parameter, but the theory is not finite and the result depends on
the arbitrary choice of the subtraction procedure. Therefore, while the
finiteness condition leads to a scheme independent result, so that the
conformal invariant theory with a real deformation is physically well defined,
the condition of vanishing beta function leads to a result which is scheme
dependent and therefore of unclear significance. In order to show that these
findings are not an artefact of dimensional regularization, we confirm our
results within the differential renormalization approach.

Our group recently reproduced the water-assisted growth method, so-called
"super growth", of millimeter-thick single-walled carbon nanotube (SWNT)
forests by using C2H4/ H2/ H2O/ Ar reactant gas and Fe/ Al2O3 catalyst. In this
current work, a parametric study was carried out on both reaction and catalyst
conditions. Results revealed that a thin Fe catalyst layer (about 0.5 nm)
yielded rapid growth of SWNTs only when supported on Al2O3, and that Al2O3
support enhanced the activity of Fe, Co, and Ni catalysts. The growth window
for the rapid SWNT growth was narrow, however. Optimum amount of added H2O
increased the SWNT growth rate but further addition of H2O degraded both the
SWNT growth rate and quality. Addition of H2 was also essential for rapid SWNT
growth, but again, further addition decreased both the SWNT growth rate and
quality. Because Al2O3 catalyzes hydrocarbon reforming, Al2O3 support possibly
enhances the SWNT growth rate by supplying the carbon source to the catalyst
nanoparticles. The origin of the narrow window for rapid SWNT growth will also
be discussed.

In this work, we investigate the chiral properties of baryon interpolating fields. Our motivation stemmed from the interest to study the behavior of these fields under chiral symmetries, which are fundamental to our understanding of the strong interaction. We aim to provide a theoretical framework to better understand the chiral properties of baryon fields and provide a foundation for future research in this field.

Our work attempts to solve the problem of inconsistency in the literature regarding the role of chiral symmetry in baryon interpolating fields. We propose a new formalism that is based on the chiral Ward identities and construct a set of operators that satisfy the constraints of chiral symmetry.

The results obtained highlight the importance of chiral symmetry in the study of baryon fields and help clarify the role it plays in their properties. In particular, we find that the chiral properties of baryon interpolating fields are closely related to their flavor content. We also provide a method to construct operators that have good chiral properties and can be used in lattice QCD simulations.

Overall, our work provides a significant contribution to the study of chiral symmetry in baryon fields and offers a potential pathway for the development of improved theoretical models in this field.

We investigate the {\em nonlinearity-assisted quantum tunneling} and
formation of nonlinear collective excitations in a matter-wave interferometer,
which is realised by the adiabatic transformation of a double-well potential
into a single-well harmonic trap. In contrast to the linear quantum tunneling
induced by the crossing (or avoided crossing) of neighbouring energy levels,
the quantum tunneling between different nonlinear eigenstates is assisted by
the nonlinear mean-field interaction. When the barrier between the wells
decreases, the mean-field interaction aids quantum tunneling between the ground
and excited nonlinear eigenstates. The resulting {\em non-adiabatic evolution}
depends on the input states. The tunneling process leads to the generation of
dark solitons, and the number of the generated dark solitons is highly
sensitive to the matter-wave nonlinearity. The results of the numerical
simulations of the matter-wave dynamics are successfully interpreted with a
coupled-mode theory for multiple nonlinear eigenstates.

Molecular dynamics simulations are used to study fluidization of a vertically
vibrated, three-dimensional shallow granular layer. As the container
acceleration is increased above g, the granular temperature and root mean
square particle displacement increase, gradually fluidizing the layer. For
nearly elastic particles, or low shaking frequencies, or small layer depths,
the end of the fluidization process is marked by an abrupt increase in the
granular temperature and rms particle displacement. The layer is then fully
fluidized since macroscopic, fluid-like phenomena such as convection rolls and
surface waves are observed. Increasing the total dissipation (by either
decreasing the restitution coefficient or increasing the total number of
particles) decreases the increase in granular temperature and rms particle
displacement at fluidization, and shifts the increase to higher accelerations.
Increasing the frequency also decreases the magnitude of the jump, and shifts
the change to lower accelerations.

We present the first part of our work investigating the impact of accretion disk winds on the X-ray spectrum of Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) using the X-ray Spectral Convolution and Response Tool (XSCORT). Our study aimed to understand the characteristics of the outflowing wind, which is an important factor in shaping the X-ray spectrum of AGN. 

Using XSCORT, we simulated the X-ray spectra of AGN with and without the presence of wind. We varied the wind velocity, column density, and ionization state to investigate their respective contributions to the X-ray spectrum. We also considered the effects of absorption and reflection on the X-ray spectrum. 

Our study presents the results of the simulated X-ray spectra, which show that the presence of accretion disk winds has a significant impact on the X-ray spectrum of AGN. We found that the wind velocity and column density play a crucial role in modifying the X-ray spectral shape. Furthermore, our results show that the wind ionization state and the presence of absorption and reflection also contribute to changes in the X-ray spectrum. 

Our study provides a framework for characterizing the impact of accretion disk winds on the X-ray spectral properties of AGN. These results have important implications for the interpretation of observations of AGN and may help to better understand the physical processes underlying these objects.

It has been shown that, for the two-qubit Heisenberg XY model, anisotropy and
magnetic field may together be used to produce entanglement for any finite
temperature by adjusting the external magnetic field beyond some finite
critical strength. This interesting result arises from an analysis employing
the Wootters concurrence, a computable measure of entanglement for two-qubit
states. Recently, Mintert {\em et al.} proposed generalizations of Wootters
concurrence for multipartite states. These MKB concurrences possess a
mathematical property that enables one to understand the origin of this
characteristic behavior. Here, we first study the effect of anisotropy and
magnetic field on the multipartite thermal entanglement of a four-qubit
Heisenberg XY chain using the MKB concurrences. We show that this model
exhibits characteristic behavior similar to that of the two-qubit model. In
addition, we show that this can again be understood using the same mathematical
property. Next, we show that the six-qubit Heisenberg XY chain possesses
properties necessary for it to have the characteristic behavior too. Most
importantly, it is possible to directly measure the multipartite MKB
concurrences of pure states. This may provide an experimental verification of
our conjecture that for a Heisenberg XY chain of any even number of qubits, it
is always possible to obtain non-zero genuine multipartite entanglement at any
finite temperature by applying a sufficiently large magnetic field.

Our work presents a new model reduction algorithm called the Quasi Equilibrium Grid Algorithm. The main goal of this work is to reduce the computational complexity of nonlinear models while preserving their important dynamical features. The proposed methodology is based on a geometric construction that preserves the equilibrium structure of the system, enabling an efficient approximation of its dynamics. 

The Quasi Equilibrium Grid Algorithm is built upon the idea of balancing the need for maintaining a sufficient level of accuracy of the reduced model with the need for reducing its dimensionality. The proposed algorithm is particularly suitable for systems with multiple time scales or for systems that naturally exhibit a nonlinear mechanism of self-regulation. 

To illustrate the potential of the presented approach, we apply it to a complex chemical reaction network demonstrating its efficiency and accuracy. We also demonstrate that the proposed methodology significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of computational efficiency, maintaining accuracy, and preserving the dynamic structure of the original model.

The motivation for this research stems from the practical need for faster and more efficient technologies that can address complex, multidimensional systems in various scientific domains, including biology, chemistry, engineering, and physics. The Quasi Equilibrium Grid Algorithm offers a promising technique for optimizing computational resources without sacrificing accuracy.

We present the report of the US Long Baseline Neutrino Experiment Study, which is aimed at advancing the knowledge of neutrino physics and understanding of the universe. The motivation for this research stems from a long-standing gap in our understanding of neutrinos. Despite their prevalence, these subatomic particles remain mysterious, and their properties are not well understood. The study aimed to address this problem by designing and building a neutrino beamline and two multi-ton detectors to observe neutrinos produced at Fermilab.

The results obtained from this study have been groundbreaking. A total of 1754 neutrino interactions were observed, leading to the measurement of several properties of neutrinos. Most notably, the observation of oscillation between neutrinos of different flavors and precise measurements of neutrino masses.

The research solves several problems, including understanding the hierarchy and nature of neutrino masses, detailed measurements of charge-parity violation in the lepton sector, and searching for new physics beyond the standard model. Several future experiments are proposed to improve the precision of the measurements and provide spectral information on neutrinos produced in the beamline.

In conclusion, the Long Baseline Neutrino Experiment study has made fundamental contributions to the field of neutrino physics and has opened up new opportunities to better understand the universe.

In this paper, we explore the concept of nontrivial geometries and their implications for the curvature of the universe. We investigate the possibility of a universe with a non-zero curvature and its consequences for our understanding of spacetime. 

Our work attempts to solve the problem of determining the upper and lower bounds for the curvature of the universe. We examine the theoretical limits predicted by Einstein's theory of general relativity, as well as observational data gathered from cosmic microwave background radiation measurements and other astrophysical observations. 

The key ideas underlying our methodology involve the use of mathematical tools, including differential geometry and topology, to analyze the possible geometries of the universe. We develop mathematical models to describe the curvature of spacetime and apply them to observational data to determine whether the universe is flat, positively curved, or negatively curved. 

Our results show that the curvature of the universe is likely to be very close to zero, consistent with the flatness prediction of inflationary cosmology. However, our analysis also reveals that a non-zero curvature cannot be ruled out entirely, and we provide upper and lower bounds on the curvature based on our analysis. Our work has important implications for our understanding of the universe's evolution and the development of future cosmological theories.

We present the design and implementation of a circulating hydrogen ultra-high purity system utilized for the MuCap experiment for the measurement of the singlet capture rate in hydrogen. The purpose of this study is to improve the current measurement by reducing the background and systematic uncertainties. The MuCap experiment aims to measure the capture rate of negative muons in hydrogen by trapping the muon in hydrogen atoms, where it eventually forms a bound state with the neutron. The detection of this state relies on the utilization of detectors with high spatial and time resolution. The ultra-high purity system used in this work allows for the removal of residual gases, impurities, and moisture that can contribute to unwanted background signals, consequently improving the experiment's overall sensitivity and reducing systematic uncertainties. 
   
The implementation of the circulating hydrogen system enabled a reliable and stable purification process, which resulted in a significant reduction of interfering signals, providing a cleaner environment and increased data quality for the MuCap experiment. The system achieved a hydrogen purity level of over 99.999%, which meets the MuCap experiment's requirements and has enabled the achievement of the most precise measurement of the singlet capture rate to date. Our results establish the critical role of the ultra-high purity system in facilitating a clean and stable experimental setup, providing a substantial contribution to the improvement of the overall sensitivity and accuracy of the measurement.

The motion of a black hole about the centre of gravity of its host galaxy
induces a strong response from the surrounding stellar population. We treat the
case of a harmonic potential analytically and show that half of the stars on
circular orbits in that potential shift to an orbit of lower energy, while the
other half receive a positive boost and recede to a larger radius. The black
hole itself remains on an orbit of fixed amplitude and merely acts as a
catalyst for the evolution of the stellar energy distribution function f(E). We
show that this effect is operative out to a radius of approx 3 to 4 times the
hole's influence radius, R_bh. We use numerical integration to explore more
fully the response of a stellar distribution to black hole motion. We consider
orbits in a logarithmic potential and compare the response of stars on circular
orbits, to the situation of a `warm' and `hot' (isotropic) stellar velocity
field. While features seen in density maps are now wiped out, the kinematic
signature of black hole motion still imprints the stellar line-of-sight mean
velocity to a magnitude ~18% the local root mean-square velocity dispersion
sigma.

In this work, we focus on exploring the phenomenon of spin-orbit coupling in non-Abelian hydrodynamics. The motivation behind this research is the growing relevance of spintronics in the field of computing and information technology. Spin transport mediated by spin-orbit coupling is a promising area of study for creating next-generation information processing devices. However, the understanding of spin transport in non-Abelian systems, where particles exchange both spin and charge, is still incomplete due to the complex nature of the interactions.

Using a combination of theoretical and computational methods, we introduce a framework to study the spin flow in non-Abelian hydrodynamics. We derive a set of equations that describe the dynamics of spin flow under the influence of various factors such as magnetic fields and boundary conditions. We also study the effect of non-Abelian topological structures on spin flow.

Our results reveal the emergence of novel transport phenomena in non-Abelian systems, such as spin vortices and spin textures. Additionally, we find that the character of the spin transport is significantly altered by the non-Abelian topology, leading to surprising behavior not observed in Abelian systems.

In conclusion, this work sheds light on the interplay of non-Abelian hydrodynamics and spin transport, opening doors for innovative ideas in the field of spintronics.

In this study, we present a power-efficient direct-voting assurance mechanism for data fusion in wireless sensor networks. Our proposed mechanism aims to reduce energy consumption and improve the accuracy and reliability of data fusion in wireless sensor networks. We begin with a comprehensive investigation of existing direct-voting assurance techniques and identify the key challenges in designing a power-efficient mechanism that can provide reliable data fusion with reduced energy consumption. Subsequently, we propose a novel approach that combines energy-efficient direct-voting assurance with dynamic clustering and selective forwarding to efficiently handle data aggregation and fusion requests while conserving energy.

We evaluated the performance of our proposed mechanism using simulations and compared its results to existing direct-voting assurance techniques. Our results demonstrate that our proposed mechanism outperforms existing mechanisms in terms of energy consumption, reliability, and accuracy. We also conducted a comparative analysis of the proposed mechanism with other state-of-the-art data fusion techniques, and our results indicate that our mechanism achieves superior performance in terms of energy efficiency.

Our research motivation stems from the essential need for efficient energy consumption in wireless sensor networks, where the limited battery life of sensor nodes is a critical issue. Our proposed mechanism not only increases the energy efficiency of sensor networks but also improves the overall performance of data fusion systems. Therefore, our work has significant implications for researchers and practitioners working on wireless sensor networks, data fusion, and related technologies.

In this study, we investigate the micro and macrorheological properties of isotropically cross-linked actin networks. Actin is an essential cytoskeletal protein that plays an essential role in cell structure and movement. It is a crucial component of many biological processes, including cell division, migration, and signaling. The aim of this research is to gain a better understanding of the mechanical properties of actin networks and their response to external forces.

We conducted rheological experiments on actin networks with varying degrees of cross-linking using optical trapping, micropipette aspiration, and passive microrheology. We found that the elastic moduli of the networks increased with cross-linking density, indicating that cross-linking enhances the strength of the actin network. Additionally, we observed that the frequency dependence of the complex shear modulus was higher in more highly cross-linked networks, indicating that cross-linking alters the mechanical response of the network.

The motivation for this research stems from the need to understand the mechanical properties of actin networks in various physiological conditions. This knowledge will aid in developing new therapies and treatments for diseases that affect the cytoskeleton, such as cancer and muscular dystrophy. Our research sheds light on the molecular mechanisms behind cellular mechanics, providing a better understanding of the fundamental processes that dictate cellular behavior.

In this work, we investigate the behavior of vicinal silicon surfaces and how they transition from step density wave to faceting. Our motivation stems from the importance of understanding the physics behind silicon growth, which has many applications in electronics and photonics. Moreover, understanding how vicinal surfaces change over time can have practical implications for controlling the growth of silicon surfaces.

To achieve this, we utilize a range of experimental and theoretical methods to explore the dynamics of vicinal silicon surfaces. Our results show that the transition between the step density wave and faceting regimes is driven by the competition between step-edge diffusion and attachment kinetics. Specifically, we find that the step-edge velocity is influenced by surface morphology, and we develop a quantitative model that accurately predicts surface morphology as a function of temperature and deposition rate. We also show that our model is consistent with experimental observations, which indicates its applicability in predicting growth behavior under various conditions.

Overall, our work advances the understanding of vicinal silicon surfaces and provides a valuable tool for predicting the behavior of silicon surfaces during growth. These results have the potential to impact a wide range of technological applications and pave the way for future research in this area.

In this paper, we report on our work to synthesize Taylor phase screens using Karhunen-Loeve (KL) basis functions. The motivation of this research comes from the need to simulate realistic phase screens for applications such as free-space optical communication, imaging through atmospheric turbulence, and adaptive optics. Taylor phase screens are widely used for this purpose, but their synthesis with conventional methods is computationally expensive and difficult to achieve. In contrast, KL basis functions have been shown to be an effective tool for phase screen synthesis with high accuracy and reduced computational complexity. 

We show that the KL basis functions can be used to synthesize Taylor phase screens that closely match the statistical properties of real turbulence scenes. Our methodology involves a two-step approach where we first construct the KL basis functions from a set of training phase screens and then use these basis functions in a projection method to generate synthetic phase screens. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach by comparing the statistical properties of our synthetic phase screens with those of real turbulence data generated by experiments, and we obtain close agreement between the two. 

Our results show that the Karhunen-Loeve basis functions can provide a viable alternative for Taylor phase screen synthesis with reduced computational complexity and more accurate results that can benefit various applications in optical and atmospheric sciences.

This paper includes a reflection on the role of networks in the study of
English language acquisition, as well as a collection of practical criteria to
annotate free-speech corpora from children utterances. At the theoretical
level, the main claim of this paper is that syntactic networks should be
interpreted as the outcome of the use of the syntactic machinery. Thus, the
intrinsic features of such machinery are not accessible directly from (known)
network properties. Rather, what one can see are the global patterns of its use
and, thus, a global view of the power and organization of the underlying
grammar. Taking a look into more practical issues, the paper examines how to
build a net from the projection of syntactic relations. Recall that, as opposed
to adult grammars, early-child language has not a well-defined concept of
structure. To overcome such difficulty, we develop a set of systematic criteria
assuming constituency hierarchy and a grammar based on lexico-thematic
relations. At the end, what we obtain is a well defined corpora annotation that
enables us i) to perform statistics on the size of structures and ii) to build
a network from syntactic relations over which we can perform the standard
measures of complexity. We also provide a detailed example.

We study the late evolution of solar metallicity stars in the transition
region between white dwarf formation and core collapse. This includes the
super-asymptotic giant branch (super-AGB, SAGB) stars, which have massive
enough cores to ignite carbon burning and form an oxygen-neon (ONe) core. The
most massive SAGB stars have cores that may grow to the Chandrasekhar mass
because of continued shell-burning. Their cores collapse, triggering a so
called electron capture supernovae (ECSN). From stellar evolution models we
find that the initial mass range for SAGB evolution is 7.5 ... 9.25\msun. We
perform calculations with three different stellar evolution codes to
investigate the sensitivity of this mass range to some of the uncertainties in
current stellar models. The mass range significantly depends on the treatment
of semiconvective mixing and convective overshooting. To consider the effect of
a large number of thermal pulses, as expected in SAGB stars, we construct
synthetic SAGB models that include a semi-analytical treatment of dredge-up,
hot-bottom burning, and thermal pulse properties. This synthetic model enables
us to compute the evolution of the main properties of SAGB stars from the onset
of thermal pulses until the core reaches the Chandrasekhar mass or is uncovered
by the stellar wind. Thereby, we determine the stellar initial mass ranges that
produce ONe-white dwarfs and electron-capture supernovae. The latter is found
to be 9.0 ... 9.25\msun for our fiducial model, implying that electron-capture
supernovae would constitute about 4% of all supernovae in the local universe.
Our synthetic approach allows us to explore the uncertainty of this number
imposed by uncertainties in the third dredge-up efficiency and ABG mass loss
rate. We find for ECSNe a upper limit of ~20% of all supernovae (abridged).

Time variability of the solar neutrino flux especially in the low and
intermediate energy sector remains an open question and, if it exists, it is
likely to be originated from the magnetic moment transition from active to
light sterile neutrinos at times of intense solar activity and magnetic field.
We examine the prospects for the SNO+ experiment to address this important
issue and to distinguish between the two classes of solar models which are
currently identified as corresponding to a high (SSM I) and a low (SSM II)
heavy element abundance. We also evaluate the predictions from these two models
for the Chlorine experiment event rate in the standard LMA and LMA+Spin Flavour
Precession (SFP) scenarios. It is found that after three years of SNO+ data
taking, the pep flux measurement will be able to discriminate between the
standard LMA and LMA+SFP scenarios, independently of which is the correct solar
model. If the LMA rate is measured, SFP with $B_0 \sim 280kG$ for the resonant
$\Delta m^2_{01}$ can be excluded at more than $4\sigma$. A low rate would
signal new physics, excluding all the 90% allowed range of the standard LMA
solution at 3$\sigma$, and a time variability would be a strong signature of
the SFP model. The CNO fluxes are the ones for which the two SSM predictions
exhibit the largest differences, so their measurement at SNO+ will be important
to favour one or the other. The distinction will be clearer after LMA or SFP
are confirmed with pep, but still, a CNO measurement at the level of SSM I/LMA
will disfavour SSM II at about $3 \sigma$. We conclude that consistency between
future pep and CNO flux measurements at SNO+ and Chlorine would either favour
an LMA+SFP scenario or favour SSM II over SSM I.

Optical/near-infrared (optical/NIR; OIR) light from low-mass neutron star
X-ray binaries (NSXBs) in outburst is traditionally thought to be thermal
emission from the accretion disc. Here we present a comprehensive collection of
quasi-simultaneous OIR and X-ray data from 19 low-magnetic field NSXBs,
including new observations of three sources: 4U 0614+09, LMC X-2 and GX 349+2.
The average radio-OIR spectrum for NSXBs is alpha ~ +0.2 (where L_nu propto
nu^alpha) at least at high luminosities when the radio jet is detected. This is
comparable to, but slightly more inverted than the alpha ~ 0.0 found for black
hole X-ray binaries. The OIR spectra and relations between OIR and X-ray fluxes
are compared to those expected if the OIR emission is dominated by thermal
emission from an X-ray or viscously heated disc, or synchrotron emission from
the inner regions of the jets. We find that thermal emission due to X-ray
reprocessing can explain all the data except at high luminosities for some
NSXBs, namely the atolls and millisecond X-ray pulsars (MSXPs). Optically thin
synchrotron emission from the jets (with an observed OIR spectral index of
alpha_thin < 0) dominate the NIR light above L_x ~ 10^36 erg/s and the optical
above L_x ~ 10^37 erg/s in these systems. For NSXB Z-sources, the OIR
observations can be explained by X-ray reprocessing alone, although synchrotron
emission may make a low level contribution to the NIR, and could dominate the
OIR in one or two cases.

In this study, we present the results of our photometric observations of the SW Sex-type nova-like object BH Lyncis during a high state. The motivation for this research is to gain a better understanding of the nature of this class of objects, which are believed to be precursors to cataclysmic variable stars. We used the 1.3-meter telescope at Skinakas Observatory in Crete, Greece, to obtain high-quality time-series photometry in the B and V bands. Our analysis revealed a complex light curve with a period of 0.156 days and an amplitude of 0.3 magnitudes. We also identified a low-amplitude modulation with a period of 0.069 days, which we interpret as the orbital period of the system. Our findings suggest that BH Lyncis is a semi-detached binary system in which the primary star is a white dwarf and the secondary is a red dwarf. We discuss the implications of our results for the understanding of the accretion process in SW Sex-type objects and provide a comparison with other nova-like systems. Our research contributes to the body of knowledge about cataclysmic variables and sheds light on the physical processes involved in the evolution of these systems.

The electronic density of states of graphene is equivalent to that of
relativistic electrons. In the absence of disorder or external doping the Fermi
energy lies at the Dirac point where the density of states vanishes. Although
transport measurements at high carrier densities indicate rather high
mobilities, many questions pertaining to disorder remain unanswered. In
particular, it has been argued theoretically, that when the average carrier
density is zero, the inescapable presence of disorder will lead to electron and
hole puddles with equal probability. In this work, we use a scanning single
electron transistor to image the carrier density landscape of graphene in the
vicinity of the neutrality point. Our results clearly show the electron-hole
puddles expected theoretically. In addition, our measurement technique enables
to determine locally the density of states in graphene. In contrast to
previously studied massive two dimensional electron systems, the kinetic
contribution to the density of states accounts quantitatively for the measured
signal. Our results suggests that exchange and correlation effects are either
weak or have canceling contributions.

The Bonami-Beckner hypercontractive inequality is a powerful tool in Fourier
analysis of real-valued functions on the Boolean cube. In this paper we present
a version of this inequality for matrix-valued functions on the Boolean cube.
Its proof is based on a powerful inequality by Ball, Carlen, and Lieb. We also
present a number of applications. First, we analyze maps that encode $n$
classical bits into $m$ qubits, in such a way that each set of $k$ bits can be
recovered with some probability by an appropriate measurement on the quantum
encoding; we show that if $m<0.7 n$, then the success probability is
exponentially small in $k$. This result may be viewed as a direct product
version of Nayak's quantum random access code bound. It in turn implies strong
direct product theorems for the one-way quantum communication complexity of
Disjointness and other problems. Second, we prove that error-correcting codes
that are locally decodable with 2 queries require length exponential in the
length of the encoded string. This gives what is arguably the first
``non-quantum'' proof of a result originally derived by Kerenidis and de Wolf
using quantum information theory, and answers a question by Trevisan.

Full details are given for the definition and construction of the wreath
product of two arbitrary Lie algebras, in the hope that it can lead to the
definition of a suitable Lie group to be the wreath product of two given Lie
groups. In the process, quite a few new notions are needed, and introduced.
Such are, for example : Formal series with variables in a vector space and
coefficients in some other vector space. Derivation of a formal series relative
to another formal series. The Lie algebra of a vector space. Formal actions of
Lie algebras over vector spaces. The basic formal action of a Lie algebra over
itself (as a formal version of the analytic aspect of the infinitesimal
operation law of a Lie groupuscule). More generally, the wreath product of two
Lie algebras is defined, relative to a formal action of the second onto an
arbitrary vector space. Main features are : A description of the triangular
actions of wreath products over product vector spaces, and a Kaloujnine-Krasner
type theorem : In essence, it says that all Lie extensions of a given Lie
algebra by another Lie algebra are, indeed, subalgebras of their wreath
product.

We present the results of a Gemini spectroscopic survey of young star clusters in NGC 3256, a nearby merging/interacting galaxy with a highly disturbed morphology and a starburst nucleus. Our goal is to investigate the effects of merging and interaction on the formation and evolution of star clusters, as well as their relation to the host galaxy properties. Our methodology involves using the multi-object spectroscopic mode of the Gemini Multi-Object Spectrograph to obtain spectra of the candidate clusters identified from the HST data, and utilizing the spectral synthesis techniques to derive their physical properties such as age, mass, and metallicity. 

Our analysis reveals a rich population of young star clusters with ages ranging from a few Myr to ~1 Gyr, and masses from a few 10^3 to ~10^6 M_sun. A significant fraction of the clusters appear to be rapidly evolving, showing strong line emission indicative of ionized gas and massive stars. We find evidence for an age spread among the clusters, suggesting that star formation activity has been ongoing in NGC 3256 over an extended period of time. Our results suggest that the merging process can trigger the formation of new star clusters, and the newly formed clusters could play a significant role in shaping the stellar population and the chemical enrichment history of the host galaxy.

In several classes of particle physics models -- ranging from the classical
Majoron models, to the more recent scenarios of late neutrino masses or
Mass-Varying Neutrinos -- one or more of the neutrinos are postulated to couple
to a new light scalar field. As a result of this coupling, neutrinos in the
early universe instead of streaming freely could form a self-coupled fluid,
with potentially observable signatures in the Cosmic Microwave Background and
the large scale structure of the universe. We re-examine the constraints on
this scenario from the presently available cosmological data and investigate
the sensitivity expected from the Planck satellite. In the first case, we find
that the sensitivity strongly depends on which piece of data is used. The SDSS
Main sample data, combined with WMAP and other data, disfavors the scenario of
three coupled neutrinos at about the 3.5$\sigma$ confidence level, but also
favors a high number of freely streaming neutrinos, with the best fit at 5.2.
If the matter power spectrum is instead taken from the SDSS Large Red Galaxy
sample, best fit point has 2.5 freely streaming neutrinos, but the scenario
with three coupled neutrinos becomes allowed at $2\sigma$. In contrast, Planck
alone will exclude even a single self-coupled neutrino at the $4.2\sigma$
confidence level, and will determine the total radiation at CMB epoch to
$\Delta N_\nu^{eff} = ^{+0.5}_{-0.3}$ ($1\sigma$ errors). We investigate the
robustness of this result with respect to the details of Planck's detector.
This sensitivity to neutrino free-streaming implies that Planck will be capable
of probing a large region of the Mass-Varying Neutrino parameter space. Planck
may also be sensitive to a scale of neutrino mass generation as high as 1 TeV.

In this work, we investigate the centrality of transitive powers of Young-Jucys-Murphy (YJM) elements. Motivated by earlier research which showed that the sum of all transitive powers of YJM elements is equal to the dimension of the irreducible representation of the symmetric group, we aim to determine whether these powers are also central elements in the group algebra of the symmetric group. 

To tackle this problem, we make use of the isomorphism between the group algebra of the symmetric group and the ring of symmetric functions. We show that the transitive powers of YJM elements correspond to certain Schur functions, which are known to be central elements.
