Our main result is the formal proof of the centrality of transitive powers of YJM elements in the group algebra of the symmetric group.

Our findings have significant implications, especially in the study of algebraic structures and representations of the symmetric group. The centrality of transitive powers of YJM elements provides a new approach to the study of the algebraic properties of the symmetric group, as well as a unique perspective on representation theory. We hope that our work opens up new avenues for future research on the algebraic structures of permutation groups.

In this work, we explore the focusing of high-energy particles in the electrostatic field of a homogeneously charged sphere using the effective momentum approximation. Our primary motivation for this research is to gain insights into the behavior of charged particles in the presence of highly charged objects, which can have important implications in the fields of material science and biophysics. 

We develop a theoretical model to describe the behavior of particles of different energies in the electrostatic field of a sphere with a uniform surface charge density. Our methodology involves numerically solving the equations of motion of particles in the field using the effective momentum approximation, which approximates the motion of particles as a harmonic oscillator with an effective mass. 

Our results reveal that the effective momentum approximation provides a good description of the motion of particles in the electrostatic field of a charged sphere and that the focusing of particles is strongly influenced by both their initial energy and the magnitude of the surface charge density of the sphere. Additionally, we find that for particles with sufficiently high energies, the focusing is largely independent of the particle's initial position. 

Overall, our work provides valuable insights into the behavior of charged particles in the presence of homogeneously charged objects, which can inform the design of nanoscale devices and improve our understanding of biological systems.

Pinpointing the progenitors of long duration gamma-ray bursts (LGRBs) remains
an extremely important question, although it is now clear that at least a
fraction of LGRBs originate in the core collapse of massive stars in type Ic
supernovae, the pathways to the production of these stars, and their initial
masses, remain uncertain. Rotation is thought to be vital in the creation of
LGRBs, and it is likely that black hole creation is also necessary. We suggest
that these two constraints can be met if the GRB progenitors are very massive
stars (>20 solar masses) and are formed in tight binary systems. Using simple
models we compare the predictions of this scenario with observations and find
that the location of GRBs on their host galaxies are suggestive of
main-sequence masses in excess of 20 solar masses, while 50% of the known
compact binary systems may have been sufficiently close to have had the
necessary rotation rates for GRB creation. Thus, massive stars in compact
binaries are a likely channel for at least some fraction of LGRBs.

In this paper, we present a novel approach to ensemble learning that leverages the power of evolutionary algorithms (EAs) to automatically generate diverse ensembles. Ensemble learning is a popular technique in machine learning, which combines multiple classifiers to improve the overall prediction accuracy of a given task. The traditional approaches to ensemble learning involve manually selecting and combining the member classifiers, which can be time-consuming and requires expert knowledge. Our proposed approach enables the automatic generation of diverse ensembles with minimal human intervention.

Our key idea is to apply EAs to evolve the ensemble composition, aiming to maximize the ensemble's accuracy on a given task. We frame the problem of ensemble composition as a multi-objective optimization problem, with the objectives of maximizing the ensemble's accuracy and minimizing its complexity. We then use a specialized EA, the NSGA-II algorithm, to explore the ensemble's solution space and generate diverse ensembles.

Our approach is evaluated on several benchmark datasets, and the results show that our evolved ensembles outperform or match the performances of traditional ensembles, which require more human intervention. Moreover, our approach generates diverse ensembles that are not available in the literature, which can be useful for improving the understanding of the ensemble's performance and the role of the individual classifiers in the ensemble.

In summary, our work proposes a novel approach to ensemble learning with EAs, which reduces the human intervention required in traditional approaches and generates diverse ensembles with improved accuracy.

We test the isotropy of the Hubble diagram. At small redshifts, this is
possible without assumptions on the cosmic inventory and provides a fundamental
test of the cosmological principle. At higher redshift we check for the
self-consistency of the LambdaCDM model. At small redshifts, we use public
supernovae (SNe) Ia data to determine the deceleration parameter q_0 and the SN
calibration on opposite hemispheres. For the complete data sets we fit Omega_M
and the SN calibration on opposite hemispheres. A statistically significant
anisotropy of the Hubble diagram at redshifts z < 0.2 is discovered (> 95%
C.L.). While data from the North Galactic hemisphere favour the accelerated
expansion of the Universe, data from the South Galactic hemisphere are not
conclusive. The hemispheric asymmetry is maximal toward a direction close to
the equatorial poles. The discrepancy between the equatorial North and South
hemispheres shows up in the SN calibration. For the LambdaCDM model fitted to
all available SNe, we find the same asymmetry. The alignment of discrepancies
between hemispheric Hubble diagrams with the equatorial frame seems to point
toward a systematic error in the SN search, observation, analysis or data
reduction. We also find that our model independent test cannot exclude the case
of the deceleration of the expansion at a statistically significant level.

In this work, we investigate the positronium ground state within the relativistic Schrödinger theory. Positronium is an ideal system to test our understanding of non-perturbative effects in quantum field theory. Moreover, it provides a unique playground to perform precision tests of quantum electrodynamics (QED) in the strong coupling regime. Our goal is to incorporate the relativistic effects into the non-relativistic wave equation for positronium, which will enable us to calculate the ground state energy levels and wave functions. 

We use a novel approach that combines the Hamiltonian and path-integral formulations of relativistic quantum mechanics. This approach allows us to extract the relevant information about the relativistic effects from the non-relativistic wave equation by expanding it in powers of the electron velocity. We find that the relativistic correction to the positronium ground state energy is significant and in good agreement with QED predictions. 

Our work provides a fundamental understanding of the positronium ground state, which is essential for a wide range of applications in material science, astrophysics, and particle physics. The methodology we develop in this work can be extended to other relativistic systems, and it opens up new avenues to explore the non-perturbative regimes of QED and other quantum field theories.

In this study, we explore the spin-flip phenomenon in supermassive black hole binary mergers. The motivation for this research comes from the recent detection of gravitational waves from the merger of two supermassive black holes, which suggests that spin-flip can occur during the binary merger process. This phenomenon can have significant implications for understanding the formation and evolution of supermassive black holes in the universe, as well as the gravitational waves they produce.

Our work attempts to solve the problem of understanding the physical mechanisms responsible for spin-flip in supermassive black hole binary mergers. To achieve this, we perform simulations using the publicly available code LIGO Algorithm Library (LAL) to model the spin evolution of supermassive black hole binaries during a merger. We investigate how the orientation of the black hole spin affects the merger dynamics and the resulting gravitational waves.

Our results show that spin-flip is indeed possible in supermassive black hole binary mergers and it can result in large changes in the black hole spin orientation, leading to a more complex gravitational wave signal. We also find that the final spin orientation of the merged black hole is not always aligned with the orbital angular momentum. These findings have important implications for future gravitational wave detections and our understanding of the physics of supermassive black holes.

For many years various asymmetrical profiles of different spectral lines
emitted from solar flares have been frequently observed. These asymmetries or
line shifts are caused predominantly by vertical mass motions in flaring layers
and they provide a good diagnostics for plasma flows during solar flares. There
are many controversial results of observations and theoretical analysis of
plasma flows in solar chromospheric flares. The main difficulty is the
interpretation of line shifts or asymmetries. For many years, methods based on
bisector techniques were used but they give a reliable results only for some
specific conditions and in most cases cannot be applied. The most promising
approach is to use the non-LTE techniques applied for flaring atmosphere. The
calculation of synthetic line profiles is performed with the radiative transfer
techniques and the assumed physical conditions correspond to flaring
atmosphere. I will present an overview of different observations and
interpretations of line asymmetries in chromospheric flares. I will explain
what we have learnt about the chromospheric evaporation in the frame of
hydrodynamical models as well as reconnection models. A critical review will be
done on the classical methods used to derive Doppler-shifts for optically thick
chomospheric lines. In particular, details on the new approach for interpreting
chromospheric line asymmetries based on the non-LTE techniques will be
presented.

This paper studies two high-pollution episodes over Greater Tokyo: 9 and 10
December 1999, and 31 July and 1 August 2001. Results obtained with the
chemistry-transport model (CTM) Polair3D are compared to measurements of
inorganic PM2.5. To understand to which extent the aerosol processes modeled in
Polair3D impact simulated inorganic PM2.5, Polair3D is run with different
options in the aerosol module, e.g. with/without heterogeneous reactions. To
quantify the impact of processes outside the aerosol module, simulations are
also done with another CTM (CMAQ). In the winter episode, sulfate is mostly
impacted by condensation, coagulation, long-range transport, and deposition to
a lesser extent. In the summer episode, the effect of long-range transport
largely dominates. The impact of condensation/evaporation is dominant for
ammonium, nitrate and chloride in both episodes. However, the impact of the
thermodynamic equilibrium assumption is limited. The impact of heterogeneous
reactions is large for nitrate and ammonium, and taking heterogeneous reactions
into account appears to be crucial in predicting the peaks of nitrate and
ammonium. The impact of deposition is the same for all inorganic PM2.5. It is
small compared to the impact of other processes although it is not negligible.
The impact of nucleation is negligible in the summer episode, and small in the
winter episode. The impact of coagulation is larger in the winter episode than
in the summer episode, because the number of small particles is higher in the
winter episode as a consequence of nucleation.

Far-infrared (IR) images of the nearby Sb galaxy NGC2841 and the Sc galaxy
NGC2976 at wavelengths of 65, 90, 140, and 160 um have been obtained with the
Far-Infrared Surveyor (FIS) onboard AKARI. Both galaxies reveal similar
morphologies of dust rings. They are, however, significantly different in the
dust temperature: a cold (21 K) ring for NGC2841 and a warm (30 K) ring for
NGC2976, which presumably reflects the difference in the origin of the ring
structure for the different Hubble type of the galaxy. In addition to the dust
ring structure, a warm dust component is detected from the central region of
NGC2841, which may be attributed to the heating by its Low-Ionization Nuclear
Emission-line Region nucleus. As for NGC2976, an extended dust component is
observed along the minor axis, which shows a distribution somewhat asymmetrical
to the galactic disk; this might be associated with the HI bridge in the
M81/M82 group that NGC2976 belongs to. By taking advantage of a wealth of the
far-IR bands of the FIS, it is demonstrated that the spectral energy
distribution of NGC2841 is spatially more variable than that of NGC2976.

Active galactic nuclei, x-ray binaries, pulsars, and gamma-ray bursts are all
believed to be powered by compact objects surrounded by relativistic plasma
flows driving phenomena such as accretion, winds, and jets. These flows are
often accurately modelled by the relativistic magnetohydrodynamics (MHD)
approximation. Time-dependent numerical MHD simulations have proven to be
especially insightful, but one regime that remains difficult to simulate is
when the energy scales (kinetic, thermal, magnetic) within the plasma become
disparate. We develop a numerical scheme that significantly improves the
accuracy and robustness of the solution in this regime. We use a modified form
of the WENO method to construct a finite-volume general relativistic
hydrodynamics code called WHAM that converges at fifth order. We avoid (1)
field-by-field decomposition by adaptively reducing down to 2-point stencils
near discontinuities for a more accurate treatment of shocks, and (2) excessive
reduction to low order stencils, as in the standard WENO formalism, by
maintaining high order accuracy in smooth monotonic flows. Our scheme performs
the proper surface integral of the fluxes, converts cell averaged conserved
quantities to point conserved quantities before performing the reconstruction
step, and correctly averages all source terms. We demonstrate that the scheme
is robust in strong shocks, very accurate in smooth flows, and maintains
accuracy even when the energy scales in the flow are highly disparate.

Inversion symmetry breaking allows contrasted circular dichroism in different
k-space regions, which takes the extreme form of optical selection rules for
interband transitions at high symmetry points. In materials where band-edges
occur at noncentral valleys, this enables valley dependent interplay of
electrons with light of different circular polarizations, in analogy to spin
dependent optical activities in semiconductors. This discovery is in perfect
harmony with the previous finding of valley contrasted Bloch band features of
orbital magnetic moment and Berry curvatures from inversion symmetry breaking
[Phys. Rev. Lett. 99, 236809 (2007)]. A universal connection is revealed
between the k-resolved optical oscillator strength of interband transitions,
the orbital magnetic moment and the Berry curvatures, which also provides a
principle for optical measurement of orbital magnetization and intrinsic
anomalous Hall conductivity in ferromagnetic systems. The general physics is
demonstrated in graphene where inversion symmetry breaking leads to valley
contrasted optical selection rule for interband transitions. We discuss
graphene based valley optoelectronics applications where light polarization
information can be interconverted with electronic information.

Many ambitious experiments have been proposed to constrain dark energy and
detect its evolution. At present, observational constraints are consistent with
a cosmological constant and there is no firm evidence for any evolution in the
dark energy equation of state w. In this paper, we pose the following question:
suppose that future dark energy surveys constrain w at low redshift to be
consistent with -1 to a percent level accuracy, what are the implications for
models of dynamical dark energy? We investigate this problem in a
model-independent way by following quintessence field trajectories in `energy'
phase-space. Attractor dynamics in this phase-space leads to two classes of
acceptable models: 1) models with flat potentials, i.e. an effective
cosmological constant, and 2) models with potentials that suddenly flatten with
a characteristic kink. The prospect of further constraining the second class of
models from distance measurements and fluctuation growth rates at low redshift
(z<3) seems poor. However, in some models of this second class, the dark energy
makes a significant contribution to the total energy density at high redshift.
Such models can be further constrained from observation of the cosmic microwave
background anisotropies and from primordial nucleosynthesis. It is possible,
therefore, to construct models in which the dark energy at high redshift causes
observable effects, even if future dark energy surveys constrain w at low
redshift to be consistent with -1 to high precision.

We use high-resolution hydrodynamic re-simulations to investigate the
properties of the thermal Sunyaev-Zel'dovich (SZ) effect from galaxy clusters.
We compare results obtained using different physical models for the
intracluster medium (ICM), and show how they modify the SZ emission in terms of
cluster profiles and scaling relations. We also produce realistic mock
observations to verify whether the results from hydrodynamic simulations can be
confirmed. We find that SZ profiles depend marginally on the modelled physical
processes, while they exhibit a strong dependence on cluster mass. The central
and total SZ emission strongly correlate with the cluster X-ray luminosity and
temperature. The logarithmic slopes of these scaling relations differ from the
self-similar predictions by less than 0.2; the normalization of the relations
is lower for simulations including radiative cooling. The observational test
suggests that SZ cluster profiles are unlikely to be able to probe the ICM
physics. The total SZ decrement appears to be an observable much more robust
than the central intensity, and we suggest using the former to investigate
scaling relations.

In this work, we explore the possibility of large lifetime differences in neutral B meson systems and investigate the sources and implications of such differences. The study of neutral B meson systems is of great interest in the realm of high-energy physics due to their potential to provide precise measurements of CP violation, which is crucial for understanding the origin of the matter-antimatter asymmetry in the universe.

The problem we aim to tackle is the discrepancy between the experimentally measured lifetime difference of neutral B mesons and the theoretical predictions based on the standard model of particle physics. To resolve this issue, we propose a new physics scenario that involves non-standard interactions in the decay of B mesons, and we examine the implications of this scenario on the CP violation measurements.

The key ideas and methodology revolve around constructing a comprehensive numerical model that incorporates the new physics scenario and simulating the expected lifetime differences and CP violation observables. We use theoretical and experimental constraints to constrain the parameters of the model and compare the predictions to the available data.

By exploring the possibility of large lifetime differences in neutral B meson systems and proposing a new physics scenario to explain it, our work aims to contribute to the ongoing efforts to discover new physics beyond the standard model and improve our understanding of the fundamental particles and interactions in the universe.

In our work titled "Spin-String Interaction in QCD Strings", we explore the interaction between spin and strings in Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) by studying the behavior of spinning strings. The main problem that our work attempts to solve is the lack of understanding regarding the dynamics of string-spin in the context of QCD strings. 

We employ a methodology that involves using the Nambu-Goto action to model string motion and the Polyakov action to couple the spin with the string. We analyze the equations of motion to obtain the dynamics of the spinning string and their implications for string-spin coupling. 

Our results show that the string's spin induces a precession in the motion of the string which results in an additional term in the spin-dependent potential that couples the string and spin. This term opens up new possibilities for understanding the strong force and gives rise to new insights into the behavior of QCD strings. 

Overall, our study provides a new way of describing the spin-string interaction in QCD strings and gives researchers a more comprehensive understanding of the dynamics of these systems. We hope that our work will pave the way for future investigations and lead to new discoveries in the field of theoretical physics.

In this work, we present a set of lecture notes on the emerging field of optical quantum computing. As quantum computers provide tremendous computational power, they have the potential to revolutionize many applications in science and industry. Optical-based quantum computing is particularly attractive due to the promise of low error rates, scalability, and the ability to interface with existing optical communication systems. 

Our lecture notes provide a comprehensive overview of the principles underlying optical quantum computing, including the basics of quantum mechanics, quantum gates, and quantum algorithms. We also delve into the experimental methods used to implement these concepts using photonic platforms, such as integrated photonics, and discuss the current state-of-the-art in optical quantum computing. 

The motivation for this work arises from the recent surge of interest in quantum computing and the need for a comprehensive resource for students and researchers entering the field. With these lecture notes, we aim to provide a clear and concise introduction to optical quantum computing and its potential applications. 

Ultimately, our work attempts to address the central challenge of developing a reliable and scalable quantum computing system. By exploring the principles and experimental techniques, we hope to contribute to the advancement of optical quantum computing and enable new applications in fields such as cryptography, chemistry, and materials science.

In our study, we investigate the ionization structure of 3-He in the Milky Way interstellar medium. The motivation for this research lies in the importance of understanding the abundance and distribution of 3-He in interstellar space. 3-He is a key element that plays a crucial role in the evolution of our galaxy, as it is involved in the production of heavier elements such as carbon and oxygen. 

We used a combination of observational data and numerical simulations to model the ionization structure of 3-He in the Milky Way. Our results reveal that the ionization fraction of 3-He is strongly influenced by the local radiation field and the density of the interstellar gas. In addition, we found that the ionization structure is highly complex, with distinct layers of ionized and neutral gas that are dependent on the local conditions. 

Overall, our study highlights the importance of considering the ionization structure of 3-He when modeling the evolution of the interstellar medium in the Milky Way. Our key ideas and methodology provide a framework for future studies on the abundance and distribution of 3-He, and how it contributes to the overall chemical evolution of our galaxy. Our findings contribute to a better understanding of the processes that shape the interstellar medium and the formation of new stars and planetary systems.

In this work, we investigate the evolution of interstellar clouds that interact with a streaming hot plasma, while considering heat conduction as a key factor. Our goal is to understand how such interactions can lead to the destruction or the evolution of these clouds, which play a key role in forming stars and planets. 

We use a numerical simulation approach that models both the hot plasma and the cold interstellar clouds in three dimensions. We adopt a fully compressible magnetohydrodynamics framework that accounts for the effects of conduction, gravity, and radiative cooling. We also consider the impact of various physical parameters, such as the plasma and cloud densities, velocities, and temperatures, on the evolution of the cloud.

Our findings show that the interaction between the cloud and the hot plasma has a significant impact on the cloud's evolution, leading to the formation of complex morphologies and features, such as shocks, filaments, and vortices. Our results also highlight the importance of heat conduction, which plays a crucial role in redistributing the energy and smoothing out temperature gradients. We find that the cloud's mass loss through evaporation depends strongly on the initial conditions and the plasma properties.

Our study sheds light on the fundamental physical processes that drive the evolution of interstellar clouds in a magnetized and turbulent environment, providing insights that are relevant for understanding star formation and the structure of the interstellar medium.

New HI images from the VLA Galactic Plane Survey (VGPS) show clear absorption
features associated with the supernova remnant (SNR) G18.8+0.3. High-resolution
$^{13}$CO images reveal that molecular clouds overlap the radio filaments of
G18.8+0.3. The $^{13}$CO emission spectrum over the full velocity range in the
direction of G18.8+0.3 shows two molecular components with high
brightness-temperatureand three molecular components with low
brightness-temperature, all with respective HI absorption. This implies that
these clouds are in front of G18.8+0.3. In the HI images, the highest velocity
absorption feature seen against the continuum image of G18.8+0.3 is at 129 km
s$^{-1}$, which corresponds to the tangent point in this direction. This yields
a lower distance limit of 6.9 kpc for G18.8+0.3. Absence of absorption at
negative velocities gives an upper distance limit of 15 kpc. The broadened
profile at 20$\pm$5 km s$^{-1}$ in the $^{13}$CO emission spectra is a strong
indicator of a possible SNR/CO cloud interaction. Thus, G18.8+0.3 is likely to
be at the distance of about 12 kpc. The upper mass limit and mean density of
the giant CO cloud at 20$\pm$5 km s$^{-1}$ are \sim 6 \times 10^{5} M_{\odot}
and \sim 2 \times 10^{2} cm^{-3}. We find an atomic hydrogen column density in
front of G18.8+0.3 of $N_{HI}$ \sim 2 \times 10^{22} cm^{-2}. The ROSAT PSPC
observations show a diffuse X-ray enhancement apparently associated with part
of the radio shell of G18.8+0.3. Assuming an association, the unabsorbed flux
is 2.2 \times10^{-12} erg cm^{-2} s^{-1}, suggesting an intrinsic luminosity of
3.6 \times 10^{34} erg s^{-1} for G18.8+0.3.

The contribution of a spin-rich $qqq$ force (in conjunction with pairwise
$qq$ forces) to the analytical structure of the $qqq$ wave function is worked
out in the high momentum regime of QCD where the confining interaction may be
ignored, so that the dominant effect is $Coulombic$. A distinctive feature of
this study is that the spin-rich $qqq$ force is generated by a $ggg$ vertex (a
genuine part of the QCD Lagrangian) wherein the 3 radiating gluon lines end on
as many quark lines, giving rise to a (Mercedes-Benz type) $Y$-shaped diagram.
The dynamics is that of a Salpeter-like equation (3D support for the kernel)
formulated covariantly on the light front, a la Markov-Yukawa Transversality
Principle (MYTP) which warrants a 2-way interconnection between the 3D and 4D
Bethe-Salpeter (BSE) forms for 2 as well as 3 fermion quarks. With these
ingredients, the differential equation for the 3D wave function $\phi$ receives
well-defined contributions from the $qq$ and $qqq$ forces. In particular a
$negative$ eigenvalue of the spin operator $i \sigma_1.\sigma_2\times \sigma_3$
which is an integral part of the $qqq$ force, causes a characteristic
singularity in the differential equation, signalling the dynamical effect of a
spin-rich $qqq$ force not yet considered in the literature. The potentially
crucial role of this interesting effect vis-a-vis the so-called `spin anomaly'
of the proton, is a subject of considerable physical interest.

In our work titled "Elastic theory of low-dimensional continua and its applications in bio- and nano-structures," we explore the fundamental concepts of elastic theory and their applications in the design and behavior of low-dimensional materials. Our research is motivated by the growing demand for advanced materials with tailored properties that can be precisely controlled at the nanoscale. Our work addresses key problems associated with the design and optimization of these materials, including their mechanical behavior, deformation mechanisms, and failure modes.

The key ideas and methodology proposed in our work are based on the development of a rigorous elastic theory that fully accounts for the anisotropic and non-linear behavior of low-dimensional continua. This theory is applied to study the mechanical properties of various types of nanomaterials, including 2D and 1D materials such as graphene, nanotubes, and nanowires, as well as biological membranes, such as cell membranes and lipid bilayers. Our research employs analytical, numerical, and experimental methodologies to validate the theoretical predictions and to establish correlations between the material properties and the dimensional and structural parameters of the systems studied.

Our work contributes to the development of predictive models for the mechanical behavior of low-dimensional materials, which are essential for their design and optimization for various applications, including drug delivery, biosensors, and flexible electronics. We envision that the results of this research will advance the understanding and engineering of low-dimensional materials, providing new opportunities for their applications in a broad range of emerging technologies.

In this work, we investigate the global polarization of quark-gluon plasma (QGP) in non-central heavy ion collisions at high energies. The motivation for this research comes from the fact that QGP, a state of matter where quarks and gluons are deconfined, is produced in ultrarelativistic heavy-ion collisions. Global polarization, a vectorial quantity, is defined relative to the reaction plane of the collision and is expected to be related to the angular momentum of the system. Polarization studies have the potential to provide insights into the properties of QGP, including its viscosity and magnetic properties. 

Using the Monte Carlo Glauber model and the AMPT model, we have simulated non-central collisions of gold ions at the Relativistic Heavy Ion Collider (RHIC). Our results show that the global polarization of QGP is indeed present in non-central heavy-ion collisions at RHIC energies. The magnitude of polarization increases as the impact parameter increases, indicating a stronger correlation between the QGP and the angular momentum of the system.

As a collective effort, our work attempts to solve the problem of understanding the properties of QGP and how they are related to the initial state of the system. This research is crucial for gaining a deeper understanding of the physics of QGP and improving our knowledge of the fundamental forces of nature. Overall, our findings have significant implications for future studies of the quark-gluon plasma in high-energy heavy-ion collisions.

A Kerr black hole with mass parameter m and angular momentum parameter a
acting as a gravitational lens gives rise to two images in the weak field
limit. We study the corresponding magnification relations, namely the signed
and absolute magnification sums and the centroid up to post-Newtonian order. We
show that there are post-Newtonian corrections to the total absolute
magnification and centroid proportional to a/m, which is in contrast to the
spherically symmetric case where such corrections vanish. Hence we also propose
a new set of lensing observables for the two images involving these
corrections, which should allow measuring a/m with gravitational lensing. In
fact, the resolution capabilities needed to observe this for the Galactic black
hole should in principle be accessible to current and near-future
instrumentation. Since a/m >1 indicates a naked singularity, a most interesting
application would be a test of the Cosmic Censorship conjecture. The technique
used to derive the image properties is based on the degeneracy of the Kerr lens
and a suitably displaced Schwarzschild lens at post-Newtonian order. A simple
physical explanation for this degeneracy is also given.

In this publication, we attempt to address a long-standing puzzlement among astrophysicists: which came first, supermassive black holes or their host galaxies? Our motivation for investigating this question is based on the observable correlation between the mass of supermassive black holes and the mass of their host galaxy, resulting in the well-known M-sigma relation. It is known that supermassive black holes exist at the centers of most, if not all, massive galaxies. However, it is not clear whether these black holes grow first and then influence the growth of their host galaxies, or if the galaxy formation process is the driver of black hole growth.

To approach this problem, we employ a variety of methods, including numerical simulations and observational constraints. Our results suggest a "feedback" mechanism, whereby the supermassive black hole's radiation and outflows regulate the growth of its host galaxy. Our simulations show that the growth patterns of black holes and galaxies are intimately intertwined and influence each other in a reciprocal and nonlinear fashion. These findings offer new insights into our understanding of the cosmic evolution of galaxies and black holes. Our work provides a framework for future research to tease out more detailed mechanisms in the growth and co-evolution of galaxies and supermassive black holes, leading to a better understanding of the universe we observe.

Fluctuations in the abundance of molecules in the living cell may affect its
growth and well being. For regulatory molecules (e.g., signaling proteins or
transcription factors), fluctuations in their expression can affect the levels
of downstream targets in a network. Here, we develop an analytic framework to
investigate the phenomenon of noise correlation in molecular networks.
Specifically, we focus on the metabolic network, which is highly inter-linked,
and noise properties may constrain its structure and function. Motivated by the
analogy between the dynamics of a linear metabolic pathway and that of the
exactly soluable linear queueing network or, alternatively, a mass transfer
system, we derive a plethora of results concerning fluctuations in the
abundance of intermediate metabolites in various common motifs of the metabolic
network. For all but one case examined, we find the steady-state fluctuation in
different nodes of the pathways to be effectively uncorrelated. Consequently,
fluctuations in enzyme levels only affect local properties and do not propagate
elsewhere into metabolic networks, and intermediate metabolites can be freely
shared by different reactions. Our approach may be applicable to study
metabolic networks with more complex topologies, or protein signaling networks
which are governed by similar biochemical reactions. Possible implications for
bioinformatic analysis of metabolimic data are discussed.

The continuation of the Liouville conformal field theory to c<=1 is
considered. The viability of an interpretation involving a timelike boson which
is the conformal factor for two-dimensional asymptotically de Sitter geometries
is examined. The conformal bootstrap leads to a three-point function with a
unique analytic factor which is the same as that which appears along with the
fusion coefficients in the minimal models. A corresponding non-analytic factor
produces a well-defined metric on fields only when the central charge is
restricted to those of the topological minimal models, and when the conformal
dimensions satisfy h>(c-1)/24. However, the theories considered here have a
continuous spectrum which excludes the degenerate representations appearing in
the minimal models. The c=1 theory has been investigated previously using
similar techniques, and is identical to a non-rational CFT which arises as a
limit of unitary minimal models. When coupled to unitary matter fields, the
non-unitary theories with c<=-2 produce string amplitudes which are similar to
those of the minimal string.

In this work, we present a prediction derived from string phenomenology that suggests the existence of extra matter at low energy. The motivation behind our research is to study the fundamental properties of string theory, one of the most promising candidates for a theory of everything. The prediction we propose arises from the fact that string theory predicts new symmetries related to the presence of extra dimensions, which could result in the emergence of extra matter fields. 

Our study is based on a mathematical framework that combines string theory with field theory to generate a set of predictions that can be tested through experimental observation. We find that the extra matter fields could have distinct implications for the behavior of particles at low-energy scales, including interactions with the Higgs field and other known particles. Moreover, the presence of this extra matter could help address some longstanding problems with the standard model, such as the hierarchy problem and the nature of dark matter. 

Our methodology consists of applying advanced mathematical techniques to extract predictions from string theory. Specifically, we analyze the properties of the compactified extra dimensions of the theory to evaluate the implications for particle physics. Overall, our study offers a novel perspective on the relationship between string theory and low-energy physics, and could motivate future experiments and theoretical developments in this area.

We consider the problem of sequential decision making on random fields
corrupted by noise. In this scenario, the decision maker observes a noisy
version of the data, yet judged with respect to the clean data. In particular,
we first consider the problem of sequentially scanning and filtering noisy
random fields. In this case, the sequential filter is given the freedom to
choose the path over which it traverses the random field (e.g., noisy image or
video sequence), thus it is natural to ask what is the best achievable
performance and how sensitive this performance is to the choice of the scan. We
formally define the problem of scanning and filtering, derive a bound on the
best achievable performance and quantify the excess loss occurring when
non-optimal scanners are used, compared to optimal scanning and filtering.
  We then discuss the problem of sequential scanning and prediction of noisy
random fields. This setting is a natural model for applications such as
restoration and coding of noisy images. We formally define the problem of
scanning and prediction of a noisy multidimensional array and relate the
optimal performance to the clean scandictability defined by Merhav and
Weissman. Moreover, bounds on the excess loss due to sub-optimal scans are
derived, and a universal prediction algorithm is suggested.
  This paper is the second part of a two-part paper. The first paper dealt with
sequential decision making on noiseless data arrays, namely, when the decision
maker is judged with respect to the same data array it observes.

In this paper, we present the results of the study of molecular gas in the nucleus of NGC4569, a galaxy presenting a strong bar feature. This research was conducted as part of the Molecular gas in NUclei of GAlaxies (NUGA) VII project. The motivation for this work stems from the need to understand the role of gas inflow in the formation and evolution of galactic nuclei. Previous studies have suggested that gas is transported towards the nuclear region through spiral arms and bars and plays a crucial role in triggering starburst activity.

Our aim was to investigate the distribution and kinematics of molecular gas in the nucleus of NGC4569. Through observations with the IRAM-30m telescope, we obtained maps of the CO(1–0) and CO(2–1) emission lines. We observed a strong concentration of molecular gas at the center of the galaxy, coincident with the nucleus. We also observed a clear velocity gradient along the major axis of the galaxy, consistent with gas inflow towards the nucleus.

Our findings provide further evidence for the crucial role of bar-driven gas inflows towards the nucleus of galaxies. Our work adds to the understanding of the mechanisms responsible for the formation and evolution of galactic nuclei, with implications for our understanding of the evolution of galaxies as a whole.

In this study, we investigate the abundances of light neutron-capture elements in planetary nebulae, focusing on s-process enrichments and interpretation. Our methodology involves the use of high-resolution spectroscopic observations obtained from the Very Large Telescope in Chile. We analyze the spectra of eight planetary nebulae and calculate the abundances of several light neutron-capture elements including Y, Zr, Ba, La, and Ce. 

Our observations show that the s-process enrichments in planetary nebulae are consistent with predictions from theoretical models, with enhancements in Y and Zr and strong enhancements in Ba, La, and Ce. We find that the s-process yields can vary greatly between different stars, and therefore the chemical composition of planetary nebulae can reveal important information about the types of stars that produced them. 

Overall, this work attempts to solve the problem of understanding the origin and evolution of planetary nebulae. By investigating the abundances of light neutron-capture elements, we gain insights into the processes that contribute to the chemical enrichment of these objects. These findings have important implications for our understanding of stellar evolution and the synthesis of heavy elements in stars.

Because of the limit in the number of gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) with available
redshifts and spectra, all current investigations on the correlation among GRB
variables use burst samples with redshifts that span a very large range. The
evolution and selection effects have thus been ignored, which might have
important influence on the results. In this Letter, we divide the 48
long-duration GRBs in Amati (2006, 2007) into four groups with redshift from
low to high, each group contains 12 GRBs. Then we fit each group with the Amati
relation $\log E_\iso = a + b \log E_\p$, and check if the parameters $a$ and
$b$ evolve with the GRB redshift. We find that $a$ and $b$ vary with the mean
redshift of the GRBs in each group systematically and significantly.
Monte-Carlo simulations show that there is only $\sim 4$ percent of chance that
the variation is caused by the selection effect arising from the fluence limit.
Hence, our results may indicate that GRBs evolve strongly with the cosmological
redshift.

We study plasma effects in a micromachined high-electron mobility transistor
(HEMT) with the microcantilever serving as the gate using the developed a
model. The model accounts for mechanical motion of the microcantilever and
spatio-temporal variations (plasma effects) of the two-dimensional electron
gas(2DEG) system in the transistor channel. The microcantilever mechanical
motion is described in the point-mass approximation. The hydrodynamic electron
transport model is used to describe distributed electron plasma phenomena in
the 2DEG system. Using the developed model, we calculated the response function
characterizing the amplitude microcantilever oscillations and the output
electric signal as functions of the signal frequency and the bias voltage for
the devices with different parameters. We find the voltage dependences of the
frequency of the mechanical resonance and its damping. In particular, it is
demonstrated that the amplitudes of the mechanical oscillations and output
electric signal exhibit pronounced maxima at the bias voltages close to the
voltage of the 2DEG channel depletion followed by a steep drop with further
increase in the bias voltage.

In our research, we explore the wave mechanics of photons and analyze the position eigenvectors. Our work is motivated by the need to understand the fundamental characteristics of quantum mechanics and improve our understanding of photon behavior. Our research focuses on addressing a key problem in photon wave mechanics, which involves the limitations of existing models in describing photon behavior in complex systems.

Our work attempts to solve this problem through the development of a novel approach that uses position eigenvectors to model the behavior of photons. Our approach is based on the premise that a photon can be described as a wave packet that contains position information. Furthermore, we integrate this position information with quantum mechanics principles to model the behavior of photons in complex systems.

Our key ideas and methodology involve the development of a mathematical framework for describing photon behavior using position eigenvectors. Our approach makes use of Schrödinger's equation to model the behavior of photons in complex systems, and we test our approach using computer simulations. Our findings suggest that our approach is more accurate and provides a better understanding of photon behavior than existing models. Overall, our work has important implications for the development of more advanced technologies that rely on photon behavior, such as quantum computing and communication systems.

In this work, we investigate the emergence of Neel order in square and triangular lattice Heisenberg models. The motivation for this research stems from the importance of understanding the behavior of magnetic materials at low temperatures, as well as the fundamental interest in studying the quantum properties of interacting spin systems in condensed matter physics.

Using Monte Carlo simulations, we find that the square lattice model exhibits Neel order at low temperatures, in agreement with theoretical predictions. However, the triangular lattice model does not exhibit Neel order even at the lowest temperatures studied. Instead, we find evidence of two sub-lattices showing different magnetic behaviors, leading to a non-uniform magnetic pattern.

Our results contribute to a better understanding of the magnetic behavior of two-dimensional Heisenberg models and provide insights into the differences between the square and triangular lattice systems. The methodology employed in this work involves the use of a stochastic simulation method, which enables us to model the behavior of complex physical systems with a large number of interacting variables.

In conclusion, our work sheds light on the rich diversity of magnetic order observed in Heisenberg models and provides a valuable platform for investigating the behavior of other complex quantum systems in condensed matter physics.

In this paper, we present a comprehensive analysis of the luminous pulsar wind nebula (PWN) G328.4+0.2, its neutron star, and the progenitor supernova explosion. The main goal of this research is to investigate the connection between the properties of PWNs, their associated neutron stars, and the supernova explosions that formed them. 

To achieve this, we carried out a multi-wavelength study of G328.4+0.2, combining radio, X-ray, and hydrodynamics data. Using the Australia Telescope Compact Array, we obtained high-resolution radio images that reveal the morphology and spectral index of the PWN. We also used data from the Chandra X-ray Observatory to investigate the properties of the X-ray emission from the nebula and its central neutron star. Finally, we performed numerical simulations of the supernova explosion that gave rise to G328.4+0.2, using the FLASH code.

Our results provide new insights into the physics of PWNs and their associated neutron stars. We find that the morphology and spectral index of G328.4+0.2 are consistent with a PWN that is expanding into a lower-density environment. The X-ray emission from the PWN and its central neutron star is highly variable and exhibits complex spectral features that reflect the non-thermal nature of the emission. Our supernova simulations suggest that the progenitor star was a massive, rapidly rotating star with a high magnetic field.

Overall, our study highlights the importance of multi-wavelength observations and numerical simulations in understanding the complex physics of PWNs, their associated neutron stars, and the supernova explosions that formed them.

In this study, we investigate the phenomenon of Critical Ionization Velocity (CIV) effect in interstellar space, and explore the possibility of detecting related continuum emission. The CIV effect refers to the rapid ionization of neutral particles such as atoms and molecules when they move at a certain velocity. This effect is of great significance for astrophysical phenomena like shock waves, plasma instabilities, and cosmic ray acceleration, but its impact on the interstellar medium remains largely unexplored. 

Our research employs theoretical modeling and simulations to gain insights into the physics of the CIV effect, its dependence on factors like gas density and temperature, and the types of emission that may result from it. We also analyze existing observations of continuum emission from interstellar gas to identify possible signatures of the CIV effect. We use the results of our analysis to propose new observational strategies and experiments that could provide further evidence for the CIV effect in interstellar space. 

The central motivation for our research is to advance our understanding of the fundamental physics that governs the behavior of the interstellar medium, and to shed light on the complex processes that shape the evolution of galaxies and the universe as a whole. Our study has important implications for a range of astrophysical questions, from the origin of cosmic rays to the role of gas dynamics in shaping the structure of galaxies.

In the equilibrium statistical mechanics of classical Coulomb fluids, the
long-range tail of the Coulomb potential gives rise to the Stillinger-Lovett
sum rules for the charge correlation functions. For the jellium model of mobile
particles of charge $q$ immersed in a neutralizing background, the fixing of
one of the $q$-charges induces a screening cloud of the charge density whose
zeroth and second moments are determined just by the Stillinger-Lovett sum
rules. In this paper, we generalize these sum rules to the screening cloud
induced around a pointlike guest charge $Z q$ immersed in the bulk interior of
the 2D jellium with the coupling constant $\Gamma=\beta q^2$ ($\beta$ is the
inverse temperature), in the whole region of the thermodynamic stability of the
guest charge $Z>-2/\Gamma$. The derivation is based on a mapping technique of
the 2D jellium at the coupling $\Gamma$ = (even positive integer) onto a
discrete 1D anticommuting-field theory; we assume that the final results remain
valid for all real values of $\Gamma$ corresponding to the fluid regime. The
generalized sum rules reproduce for arbitrary coupling $\Gamma$ the standard
Z=1 and the trivial Z=0 results. They are also checked in the Debye-H\"uckel
limit $\Gamma\to 0$ and at the free-fermion point $\Gamma=2$. The generalized
second-moment sum rule provides some exact information about possible sign
oscillations of the induced charge density in space.

We report the discovery of an Active Galactic Nucleus (AGN) in the nearby SAd
galaxy NGC 3621 using Spitzer high spectral resolution observations. These
observations reveal the presence of [NeV] 14 um and 24 um emission which is
centrally concentrated and peaks at the position of the near-infrared nucleus.
Using the [NeV] line luminosity, we estimate that the nuclear bolometric
luminosity of the AGN is ~ 5 X 10^41 ergs s^-1, which corresponds based on the
Eddington limit to a lower mass limit of the black hole of ~ 4 X 10^3 Msun.
Using an order of magnitude estimate for the bulge mass based on the Hubble
type of the galaxy, we find that this lower mass limit does not put a strain on
the well-known relationship between the black hole mass and the host galaxy's
stellar velocity dispersion established in predominantly early-type galaxies.
Mutli-wavelength follow-up observations of NGC 3621 are required to obtain more
precise estimates of the bulge mass, black hole mass, accretion rate, and
nuclear bolometric luminosity. The discovery reported here adds to the growing
evidence that a black hole can form and grow in a galaxy with no or minimal
bulge.

We review the possibility that the Supersymmetric Standard Model arises from
orbifold constructions of the E_8 x E_8 Heterotic Superstring, and the
phenomenological properties that such a model should have. In particular,
trying to solve the discrepancy between the unification scale predicted by the
Heterotic Superstring (g_{GUT}x5.27x10^{17} GeV) and the value deduced from LEP
experiments (2x10^{16} GeV), we will predict the presence at low energies of
three families of Higgses and vector-like colour triplets. Our approach relies
on the Fayet-Iliopoulos breaking, and this is also a crucial ingredient,
together with having three Higgs families, to obtain in these models an
interesting pattern of fermion masses and mixing angles at the renormalizable
lebel. Namely, after the gauge breaking some physical particles appear combined
with other states, and the Yukawa couplings are modified in a well controlled
way. On the other hand, dangerous flavour-changing neutral currents may appear
when fermions of a given charge receive their mass through couplings with
several Higgs doublets. We will address this potential problem, finding that
viable scenarios can be obtained for a reasonable light Higgs spectrum.

Only the 1/V_max method has been employed so far for observationally
determining the white dwarf luminosity function, whereas for other kind of
luminosity functions several other methods have been frequently used. Moreover,
the procedures to determine the white dwarf luminosity function are not free of
biases. These biases have two different origins: they can either be of
statistical nature or a consequence of the measurement errors. In a previous
paper we carried out an in-depth study of the first category of biases for
several luminosity function estimators. In this paper we focus on the biases
introduced by the measurement errors and on the effects of the degree of
contamination of the input sample used to build the disc white dwarf luminosity
function by different kinematical populations. To assess the extent of these
biases we use a Monte Carlo simulator to generate a controlled synthetic
population and analyse the behaviour of the disc white dwarf luminosity
function for several assumptions about the magnitude of the measurement errors
and for several degrees of contamination, comparing the performances of the
most robust luminosity function estimators under such conditions.

We study numerically finite-size corrections in scaling relations for
roughness distributions of various interface growth models. The most common
relation, which considers the average roughness $<w_2>$ as scaling factor, is
not obeyed in the steady states of a group of ballistic-like models in 2+1
dimensions, even when very large system sizes are considered. On the other
hand, good collapse of the same data is obtained with a scaling relation that
involves the root mean square fluctuation of the roughness, which can be
explained by finite-size effects on second moments of the scaling functions. We
also obtain data collapse with an alternative scaling relation that accounts
for the effect of the intrinsic width, which is a constant correction term
previously proposed for the scaling of $<w_2>$. This illustrates how
finite-size corrections can be obtained from roughness distributions scaling.
However, we discard the usual interpretation that the intrinsic width is a
consequence of high surface steps by analyzing data of restricted
solid-on-solid models with various maximal height differences between
neighboring columns. We also observe that large finite-size corrections in the
roughness distributions are usually accompanied by huge corrections in height
distributions and average local slopes, as well as in estimates of scaling
exponents. The molecular-beam epitaxy model of Das Sarma and Tamborenea in 1+1
dimensions is a case example in which none of the proposed scaling relations
works properly, while the other measured quantities do not converge to the
expected asymptotic values. Thus, although roughness distributions are clearly
better than other quantities to determine the universality class of a growing
system, it is not the final solution for this task.

The use of the sine-Gordon equation as a model of magnetic flux propagation
in Josephson junctions motivates studying the initial-value problem for this
equation in the semiclassical limit in which the dispersion parameter $\e$
tends to zero. Assuming natural initial data having the profile of a moving
$-2\pi$ kink at time zero, we analytically calculate the scattering data of
this completely integrable Cauchy problem for all $\e>0$ sufficiently small,
and further we invert the scattering transform to calculate the solution for a
sequence of arbitrarily small $\e$. This sequence of exact solutions is
analogous to that of the well-known $N$-soliton (or higher-order soliton)
solutions of the focusing nonlinear Schr\"odinger equation. Plots of exact
solutions for small $\e$ reveal certain features that emerge in the
semiclassical limit. For example, in the limit $\epsilon\to 0$ one observes the
appearance of nonlinear caustics. In the appendices we give a self contained
account of the Cauchy problem from the perspectives of both inverse scattering
and classical analysis (Picard iteration). Specifically, Appendix A contains a
complete formulation of the inverse-scattering method for generic $L^1$-Sobolev
initial data, and Appendix B establishes the well-posedness for $L^p$-Sobolev
initial data (which in particular completely justifies the inverse-scattering
analysis in Appendix A).

We present the design and implementation of a Corbino experimental set-up for the determination of Cooper pair mass spectroscopy and mobility in normal phase. The motivation for this research stems from the need to better understand the behavior and properties of superconductors at the microscopic level. Our experimental set-up employs a unique Corbino geometry that eliminates the need for external magnetic fields or wires, thus reducing electromagnetic interference and minimizing any potential for sample damage.

We conducted experiments on two different superconducting samples, niobium and lead, at various temperatures and applied electric fields. The results obtained demonstrate the effectiveness of our Corbino geometrical setup in measuring the Cooper pair mass spectroscopy and the mobility of the superconducting samples without any external fields. Additionally, we observed useful information regarding the temperature and electric field dependence of the Cooper pair mass spectroscopy and normal-state transport properties.

Our work aims to solve the problem of accurately measuring the Cooper pair mass spectroscopy and mobility of superconducting samples at low temperatures without employing any external fields. The use of the Corbino experimental set-up provides a unique and non-invasive approach to address these challenges and offers a potential new tool for investigating the fundamental properties of superconductors.

We present a mathematical framework (referred to as Context-driven
Actualization of Potential, or CAP) for describing how entities change over
time under the influence of a context. The approach facilitates comparison of
change of state of entities studied in different disciplines. Processes are
seen to differ according to the degree of nondeterminism, and the degree to
which they are sensitive to, internalize, and depend upon a particular context.
Our analysis suggests that the dynamical evolution of a quantum entity
described by the Schrodinger equation is not fundamentally different from
change provoked by a measurement often referred to as collapse, but a limiting
case, with only one way to collapse. The biological transition to coded
replication is seen as a means of preserving structure in the fact of
context-driven change, and sextual replication as a means of increasing
potentiality thus enhancing diversity through interaction with context. The
framework sheds light on concepts like selection and fitness, reveals how
exceptional Darwinian evolution is as a means of 'change of state', and
clarifies in what sense culture, and the creative process underlying it, are
Darwinian.

We present an investigation of the size distribution of a largest planar matching and a largest planar subgraph in random bipartite graphs. We aim to address the gap in the literature that lacks the understanding of the distribution of these sizes. Our work attempts to solve the fundamental question of whether the largest planar matching and the largest planar subgraph in a random bipartite graph become unique with high probability. 

Our results demonstrate that the distribution of the maximum planar matching and the maximum planar subgraph converge weakly to a simple exponential distribution. Furthermore, we have shown that with high probability, the maximum planar matching and the maximum planar subgraph are unique up to isomorphism in a random bipartite graph. Our numerical simulations corroborate our theoretical findings giving evidence to our main results.

The motivation for this research stems from the potential applications of having a better understanding of planar matching and planar subgraphs in random bipartite graphs. The presence of planar graphs is common in many fields, such as computer science and network design. Therefore, our results not only contribute to the understanding of a fundamental question in random bipartite graphs but also have practical implications.

We study the growth of correlations in systems with weak long-range
interactions. Starting from the BBGKY hierarchy, we determine the evolution of
the two-body correlation function by using an expansion of the solutions of the
hierarchy in powers of 1/N in a proper thermodynamic limit $N\to +\infty$.
These correlations are responsible for the ``collisional'' evolution of the
system beyond the Vlasov regime due to finite $N$ effects. We obtain a general
kinetic equation that can be applied to spatially inhomogeneous systems and
that takes into account memory effects. These peculiarities are specific to
systems with unshielded long-range interactions. For spatially homogeneous
systems with short memory time like plasmas, we recover the classical Landau
(or Lenard-Balescu) equations. An interest of our approach is to develop a
formalism that remains in physical space (instead of Fourier space) and that
can deal with spatially inhomogeneous systems. This enlightens the basic
physics and provides novel kinetic equations with a clear physical
interpretation. However, unless we restrict ourselves to spatially homogeneous
systems, closed kinetic equations can be obtained only if we ignore some
collective effects between particles. General exact coupled equations taking
into account collective effects are also given. We use this kinetic theory to
discuss the processes of violent collisionless relaxation and slow collisional
relaxation in systems with weak long-range interactions. In particular, we
investigate the dependence of the relaxation time with the system size and
provide a coherent discussion of all the numerical results obtained for these
systems.

We present a novel approach for selectively controlling the apoptosis signaling network within heterogeneous cell populations. The motivation for this research stems from the need to treat diseases that arise due to dysregulated apoptotic pathways, such as cancer and autoimmune disorders, which can manifest as a result of defects in the apoptotic machinery. While previous attempts to manipulate apoptosis have focused on inducing or inhibiting cell death, our approach targets specific nodes in the apoptosis signaling network to modulate cell survival selectively.

Our method involves the use of small molecule inhibitors that target specific signaling hubs in the apoptotic cascade, effectively rewiring the network to favor either cell survival or death. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in vitro using a mixed population of cancer cells, whereby inhibition of the pro-survival protein Bcl-2 results in selective killing of only cells expressing high levels of Bcl-2.

This work addresses an important problem in the field of cancer therapeutics, as current treatments often result in collateral damage to healthy cells due to the indiscriminate nature of traditional chemotherapy. By selectively targeting specific nodes in the apoptosis signaling network, we can improve outcomes for patients with cancers that exhibit heterogeneous expression of apoptosis pathway proteins. Our approach may also be extended to other disease contexts where targeted modulation of apoptosis could be beneficial.

In our study, we investigate the potential threat posed by the massive star Eta Carinae to nearby superluminous supernovae. This star has been known to exhibit erratic behavior and in the past, has produced some of the brightest light in the galaxy. Given its proximity, it has been the subject of intense scrutiny to determine whether its unprecedented luminosity could threaten neighboring superluminous supernovae. We focused on the possibility of Eta Carinae producing destructive gamma rays in its agitated state, which could affect its neighboring stars. To address this issue, we carried out simulations of both Eta Carinae and superluminous supernovae, taking into account various possible scenarios.

Our research revealed that a putative blast of gamma rays that could emanate from Eta Carinae would not have a significant impact on nearby superluminous supernovae. Our findings show that such a blast of energy would be diluted by cosmic expansion and distance, making it less harmful than initially feared. Hence, we demonstrate that there is no compelling reason for concern over the potential threat posed by Eta Carinae to superluminous supernovae in its vicinity. Our study provides a better understanding of the astrophysical phenomena, which can be crucial in assessing potential threats to astronomical phenomena.

Our work aimed to extract physical laws from joint experimental data using a novel approach based on machine learning techniques. The motivation for our research lies in the fact that physical phenomena are governed by fundamental laws which are difficult to directly measure due to their complexity or inaccessibility. Therefore, we sought to find a way to extract these laws from observed data. 

This problem has been addressed previously by analytical or numerical methods. However, these methods require data to follow specific mathematical forms and are limited to simple models. Our approach, on the other hand, uses machine learning to extract physical laws from experimental data without prior knowledge of the underlying mathematical relationship. To validate our approach, we used different experimental datasets and compared the derived physical laws to those obtained by analytical models. 

Our results demonstrate that our approach is effective in extracting physical laws and can achieve better accuracy than analytical models in some cases. Furthermore, our approach is more flexible and can handle complex and nonlinear relationships among variables. Thus, we believe that our work provides a promising direction for physics research, where data-driven methods are becoming increasingly important for understanding complex physical phenomena.

A large acceptance scintillator detector with wavelength shifting optical
fibre readout has been designed and built to detect the decay particles of
$\eta$-nucleus bound system (the so-called $\eta$-mesic nuclei), namely,
protons and pions. The detector, named as ENSTAR detector, consists of 122
pieces of plastic scintillator of various shapes and sizes, which are arranged
in a cylindrical geometry to provide particle identification, energy loss and
coarse position information for these particles. A solid angle coverage of
$\sim$95% of total 4$\pi$ is obtained in the present design of the detector.
Monte Carlo phase space calculations performed to simulate the formation and
decay of $\eta$-mesic nuclei suggest that its decay particles, the protons and
pions are emitted with an opening angle of 150$^\circ \pm 20^\circ$, and with
energies in the range of 25 to 300 MeV and 225 to 450 MeV respectively. The
detailed GEANT simulations show that $\sim$ 80 % of the decay particles
(protons and pions) can be detected within ENSTAR. Several test measurements
using alpha source, cosmic-ray muons etc. have been carried out to study the
response of ENSTAR scintillator pieces. The in-beam tests of fully assembled
detector with proton beam of momentum 870 MeV/c from the Cooler synchrotron
COSY have been performed. The test results show that the scintillator fiber
design chosen for the detector has performed satisfactorily well. The present
article describes the detector design, simulation studies, construction details
and test results.

In this paper, we present our findings on a very massive runaway star from Cygnus OB2. The motivation for this research stems from the desire to better understand the formation and evolution of massive stars in clusters. We employed a combination of spectroscopic and photometric data to constrain the properties of the star, which has a mass of approximately 70 solar masses and a high velocity of around 200 km/s relative to the cluster.

Our analysis indicates that the runaway star likely formed through a binary supernova ejection process, in which the star's companion exploded first, launching the runaway star out of the cluster. This finding is supported by the star's peculiar chemical abundances, which are consistent with supernova nucleosynthesis, as well as by the lack of any other significant massive stars in the vicinity.

Overall, our work sheds new light on the formation and dynamics of massive stars in clusters, as well as the various mechanisms that can lead to runaway stars. These findings have important implications for understanding the formation of high-mass stars and their contribution to the chemical enrichment of galaxies. Further observations and theoretical modeling will be necessary to fully characterize the properties of this intriguing runaway star and its role within the broader astrophysical context.

Open Access to particle physics literature does not sound particularly new or
exciting, since particle physicists have been reading preprints for decades,
and arXiv.org for 15 years. However new movements in Europe are attempting to
make the peer-reviewed literature of the field fully Open Access. This is not a
new movement, nor is it restricted to this field. However, given the field's
history of preprints and eprints, it is well suited to a change to a fully Open
Access publishing model. Data shows that 90% of HEP published literature is
freely available online, meaning that HEP libraries have little need for
expensive journal subscriptions. As libraries begin to cancel journal
subscriptions, the peer review process will lose its primary source of funding.
Open Access publishing models can potentially address this issue. European
physicists and funding agencies are proposing a consortium, SCOAP3, that might
solve many of the objections to traditional Open Access publishing models in
Particle Physics. These proposed changes should be viewed as a starting point
for a serious look at the field's publication model, and are at least worthy of
attention, if not adoption.

Interrelation between the two-particle and mean-field problems is used to
describe the strong pseudogap and superconducting states in cuprates. We
present strong pseudogap state as off-diagonal short-range order (ODSRO)
originating from quasi-stationary states of the pair of repulsing particles
with large total momentum (K - pair). Phase transition from the ODSRO state
into the off-diagonal long-range ordered (ODLRO) superconducting state is
associated with Bose-Einstein condensation of the K - pairs. A checkerboard
spatial order observable in the superconducting state in the cuprates is
explained by a rise of the K - pair density wave. A competition between the
ODSRO and ODLRO states leads to the phase diagram typical of the cuprates.
Biordered superconducting state of coexisting condensates of Cooper pairs with
zero momentum and K - pairs explains some properties of the cuprates observed
below Tc: Drude optical conductivity, unconventional isotope effect and two-gap
quasiparticle spectrum with essentially different energy scales.

In this work, we present a novel approach to derive effective stochastic interactions in Dissipative Particle Dynamics (DPD) simulations using force covariance. The inherent stochasticity in DPD simulations stems from the random interactions between particles that are modeled using stochastic forces. These forces are usually defined using a weight function or a distribution function that requires empirical tuning. Our work proposes a systematic way to compute these stochastic forces by analyzing the covariance of the forces acting on a pair of particles. By treating the forces as a random variable, we derive a set of equations that relate the covariance of the forces to the parameters defining the weight function. We validate our approach using simulations of simple fluids and showed that our method accurately reproduces the target dynamical properties of the fluid. Furthermore, we demonstrate the applicability of our technique to complex systems such as polymers and gels. Our technique eliminates the need for an empirical tuning of the weight function and paves the way for accurate simulations of complex systems using DPD. In conclusion, our work provides a robust and systematic approach to derive effective stochastic interactions in DPD, which can have a profound impact on the studies of soft matter and complex fluids.

We analyse new integral field spectroscopy of the inner region (central 2.5
kpc) of the spiral galaxy NGC 4321 to study the peculiar kinematics of this
region. Fourier analysis of the velocity residuals obtained by subtracting an
axisymmetric rotation model from the $\rm H\alpha$ velocity field, indicates
that the distortions are {\em global} features generated by an $m=2$
perturbation of the gravitational potential which can be explained by the
nuclear bar. This bar has been previously observed in the near-infrared but not
in the optical continuum dominated by star formation. We detect the optical
counterpart of this bar in the 2D distribution of the old stellar population
(inferred from the equivalent width map of the stellar absorption lines). We
apply the Tremaine--Weinberg method to the stellar velocity field to calculate
the pattern speed of the inner bar, obtaining a value of
$\Omega_b$=160$\pm70\rm km s^{-1} kpc^{-1} $. This value is considerably la
rger than the one obtained when a simple bar model is considered. However the
uncertainties in the pattern speed determination prevent us to give support to
alternative scenarios.

A population of committees of agents that learn by using neural networks is
implemented to simulate the stock market. Each committee of agents, which is
regarded as a player in a game, is optimised by continually adapting the
architecture of the agents using genetic algorithms. The committees of agents
buy and sell stocks by following this procedure: (1) obtain the current price
of stocks; (2) predict the future price of stocks; (3) and for a given price
trade until all the players are mutually satisfied. The trading of stocks is
conducted by following these rules: (1) if a player expects an increase in
price then it tries to buy the stock; (2) else if it expects a drop in the
price, it sells the stock; (3)and the order in which a player participates in
the game is random. The proposed procedure is implemented to simulate trading
of three stocks, namely, the Dow Jones, the Nasdaq and the S&P 500. A linear
relationship between the number of players and agents versus the computational
time to run the complete simulation is observed. It is also found that no
player has a monopolistic advantage.

Recent analyses of the WMAP data seem to indicate the possible presence of
large-angle anisotropy in the Universe. If confirmed, these can have important
consequences for our understanding of the Universe. A number of attempts have
recently been made to establish the reality and nature of such anisotropies in
the CMB data. Among these is a directional indicator recently proposed by the
authors. A distinctive feature of this indicator is that it can be used to
generate a sky map of the large-scale anisotropies of the CMB maps. Applying
this indicator to full-sky temperature maps we found a statistically
significant preferred direction. The full-sky maps used in these analyses are
known to have residual foreground contamination as well as complicated noise
properties. Thus, here we performed the same analysis for a map where regions
with high foreground contamination were removed. We find that the main feature
of the full-sky analysis, namely the presence of a significant axis of
asymmetry, is robust with respect to this masking procedure. Other subtler
anomalies of the full-sky are on the other hand no longer present.

Initiated by Gromov, the study of holomorphic curves in symplectic manifolds
has been a powerfull tool in symplectic topology, however the moduli space of
holomorphic curves is often very difficult to find. A common technique is to
study the limiting behavior of holomorphic curves in a degenerating family of
complex structures which corresponds to a kind of adiabatic limit. The category
of exploded fibrations is an extension of the smooth category in which some of
these degenerations can be described as smooth families.
  The first part of this paper is devoted to defining exploded fibrations and a
slightly more specialized category of exploded torus fibrations. Later sections
contain the transverse interesction theory for exploded fibrations and some
examples of holomorphic curves in exploded torus fibrations, including a brief
