We found that the frequency of planets increases with decreasing mass, and we estimated the occurrence of planets in the Milky Way to be 0.51 planets per star. Our results also suggest that a significant fraction of rocky planets with masses similar to Earth occupy orbits that are too close to their host star to support life. This study provides an important contribution to the field of exoplanets and lays the groundwork for future surveys, which will allow us to delve deeper into the characterization of exoplanets and their environments.

A path-integral for the t-J model in two dimensions is constructed based on
Dirac quantization, with an action found originally by Wiegmann (Phys. Rev.
Lett. {\bf 60}, 821 (1988); Nucl. Phys. B323, 311 (1989)). Concentrating on the
low doping limit, we assume short range antiferromagnetic order of the spin
degrees of freedom. Going over to a local spin quantization axis of the dopant
fermions, that follows the spin degree of freedom, staggered CP$^1$ fields
result and the constraint against double occupancy can be resolved. The
staggered CP$^1$ fields are split into slow and fast modes, such that after a
gradient expansion, and after integrating out the fast modes and the dopant
fermions, a CP$^1$ field-theory with a massive gauge field is obtained that
describes generically incommensurate coplanar magnetic structures, as discussed
previously in the context of frustrated quantum antiferromagnets. Hence, the
possibility of deconfined spinons is opened by doping a colinear
antiferromagnet.

In this research, we present a study of the microwave continuum emission from air shower plasmas, which is a phenomenon that occurs when high-energy cosmic rays interact with the Earth’s atmosphere and produce electron-positron pairs. The microwave emission from these plasmas is of interest as it can provide valuable information about the primary cosmic ray particles that produced the air shower. 

Our methodology involved the use of the Murchison Widefield Array (MWA) radio telescope in Western Australia to observe microwave emission from air shower plasmas. We analyzed data from various air shower events and compared them to simulations of microwave emission from the same events.

Our results show that the observed microwave continuum emission from air shower plasmas is consistent with current theoretical models and simulations. However, we observed a significant variation in the polarization of the emission, which can be used to study the properties of the magnetic field in the air shower region.

This research is motivated by the need to understand the origin and properties of high-energy cosmic rays, which are one of the most energetic phenomena in the Universe. The observation of microwave emission from air shower plasmas can provide us with new insights into the nature and properties of these particles.

In this work we develop a microscopic physical model of early evolution,
where phenotype,organism life expectancy, is directly related to genotype, the
stability of its proteins in their native conformations which can be determined
exactly in the model. Simulating the model on a computer, we consistently
observe the Big Bang scenario whereby exponential population growth ensues as
soon as favorable sequence-structure combinations (precursors of stable
proteins) are discovered. Upon that, random diversity of the structural space
abruptly collapses into a small set of preferred proteins. We observe that
protein folds remain stable and abundant in the population at time scales much
greater than mutation or organism lifetime, and the distribution of the
lifetimes of dominant folds in a population approximately follows a power law.
The separation of evolutionary time scales between discovery of new folds and
generation of new sequences gives rise to emergence of protein families and
superfamilies whose sizes are power-law distributed, closely matching the same
distributions for real proteins. On the population level we observe emergence
of species, subpopulations which carry similar genomes. Further we present a
simple theory that relates stability of evolving proteins to the sizes of
emerging genomes. Together, these results provide a microscopic first
principles picture of how first gene families developed in the course of early
evolution

We present the analysis of X-ray emission from PSR J1809-1917 and its pulsar wind nebula as well as their possible association with the TeV gamma-ray source HESS J1809-193. We utilized data from the XMM-Newton and Chandra X-ray space observatories to study the spectral and morphological properties of the pulsar and its surrounding nebula. Our analysis of the X-ray data provides evidence of non-thermal emission, which is consistent with the synchrotron radiation mechanism, indicating the presence of energetic electrons. We also find that the pulsar wind nebula exhibits a toroidal morphology, which is possibly due to the interaction of the pulsar wind with the surrounding medium. Furthermore, we find evidence of a possible association between the pulsar wind nebula and the TeV gamma-ray emission observed by HESS, supporting the hypothesis that pulsar wind nebulae are potential sources of TeV gamma-ray emission.

This research has important implications for our understanding of high-energy astrophysical phenomena, and provides insights into the physical properties of pulsar wind nebulae. These results can be used to inform future studies of pulsar wind nebulae, which will help to further our understanding of the complex interactions between astrophysical plasmas and magnetic fields. Overall, our analysis of the X-ray emission from PSR J1809-1917 and its pulsar wind nebula provides valuable insights into the high-energy properties of pulsars and their associated pulsar wind nebulae.

In this study, we present a quantum-like representation of extensive form games, specifically the well-known Wine Testing Game. The key idea behind this approach is to use quantum mechanics to model the player's decision-making process, which has been shown to match the behavior of real-world decision makers more closely than classical game theory models. To achieve this, we adapted the Dirac notation and von Neumann measurement to extensive form games. Our results showed that the quantum-like representation of the Wine Testing Game produced different Nash equilibria than the classical representation, including cases where one player abstained from choosing an action, and cases where players chose different actions even though their payoffs were the same. We also analyzed the strategies of the players and found that some strategies that led to suboptimal outcomes in classical game theory, such as always choosing one particular action, were often optimal in the quantum-like representation. Our methodology involved representing the game as a matrix and performing calculations using qubits and quantum gates. The motivation for this research was to provide a more accurate and robust model for decision making in strategic interactions, which has applications in fields such as economics, political science, and psychology.

In this work, we present a hypothesis that post-T Tauri stars, often observed with enhanced metallic content, may have experienced a planetary bombardment during their early formation. Currently, it is unknown how such enhanced metallic content could have been produced in stars that have already fully formed and exhausted their nuclear fuel. Therefore, we propose that these stars were subject to a planetary body bombardment that deposited large amounts of metallic material onto their surfaces.

To explore this hypothesis, we utilized spectroscopic data on a sample of 129 post-T Tauri stars and analyzed their metallic content. Our analysis revealed a statistically significant correlation between the age of these stars and their metallicity, with younger stars showing higher metallic content.

We also calculated the expected amount of metal deposition onto a star through planetary bombardment, and found that it was sufficient to account for the observed metallic enhancement in post-T Tauri stars.

Our findings provide important insight into the formation and evolution of post-T Tauri stars, and suggest that planetary bodies may play a significant role in shaping the properties of these stars even after they have fully formed. Furthermore, our hypothesis may help explain the variability observed in metallicity among post-T Tauri stars, and could lead to new understanding of the evolution of planets and planetary systems.

Pores are one of the various features forming in the photosphere by the
emergence of magnetic field onto the solar surface. They lie at the border
between tiny magnetic elements and larger sunspots. Light bridges, in such
structures, are bright features separating umbral areas in two or more
irregular regions. Commonly, light bridges indicate that a the merging of
magnetic regions or, conversely, the breakup of the area is underway. We
investigate the velocity structure of a solar pore (AR10812) with light bridge,
and of the quiet solar photosphere nearby, analyzing high spatial and spectral
resolution images. The pore area has been observed with the Interferometric
BI-dimensional Spectrometer (IBIS) at the Dunn Solar Telescope, acquiring
monochromatic images in the Ca II 854.2 nm line and in the Fe I 709.0 nm line
as well as G-band and broad-band images. We also computed the Line of Sight
(LoS) velocity field associated to the Fe I and Fe II photospheric lines. The
amplitude of the LoS velocity fluctuations, inside the pore, is smaller than
that observed in the quiet granulation near the active region. We computed the
azimuthal average LoS velocity and derived its radial profile. The whole pore
is characterized by a downward velocity -200 m/s and by an annular downflow
structure with an average velocity of -350 m/s with respect to the nearby quiet
sun. The light bridge inside the pore, when observed in the broad-band channel
of IBIS and in the red wing of Ca II 854.2 nm line, shows an elongated dark
structure running along its axis, that we explain with a semi-analytical model.
In the highest resolution LoS velocity images the light bridge shows a profile
consistent with a convective roll: a weak upflow, 50-100m/s, in correspondence
of the dark lane, flanked by a downflow, -(200-300) m/s.

We introduce and test a new and highly efficient method for treating the
thermal and radiative effects influencing the energy equation in SPH
simulations of star formation. The method uses the density, temperature and
gravitational potential of each particle to estimate a mean optical depth,
which then regulates the particle's heating and cooling. The method captures --
at minimal computational cost -- the effects of (i) the rotational and
vibrational degrees of freedom of H2, H2 dissociation, H0 ionisation, (ii)
opacity changes due to ice mantle melting, sublimation of dust, molecular
lines, H-, bound-free and free-free processes and electron scattering; (iv)
external irradiation; and (v) thermal inertia. The new algorithm reproduces the
results of previous authors and/or known analytic solutions. The computational
cost is comparable to a standard SPH simulation with a simple barotropic
equation of state. The method is easy to implement, can be applied to both
particle- and grid-based codes, and handles optical depths 0<tau<10^{11}.

Mixed action lattice calculations allow for an additive lattice spacing
dependent mass renormalization of mesons composed of one sea and one valence
quark, regardless of the type of fermion discretization methods used in the
valence and sea sectors. The value of the mass renormalization depends upon the
lattice actions used. This mixed meson mass shift is an important lattice
artifact to determine for mixed action calculations; because it modifies the
pion mass, it plays a central role in the low energy dynamics of all hadronic
correlation functions. We determine the leading order, $\mathcal{O}(a^2)$, and
next to leading order, $\mathcal{O}(a^2 m_\pi^2)$, additive mass shift of
\textit{valence-sea} mesons for a mixed lattice action with domain-wall valence
fermions and rooted staggered sea fermions, relevant to the majority of current
large scale mixed action lattice efforts. We find that on the asqtad improved
coarse MILC lattices, this additive mass shift is well parameterized in lattice
units by $\Delta(am)^2 = 0.034(2) -0.06(2) (a m_\pi)^2$, which in physical
units, using $a=0.125$ fm, corresponds to $\Delta(m)^2 = (291\pm 8
\textrm{MeV})^2 -0.06(2) m_\pi^2$. In terms of the mixed action effective field
theory parameters, the corresponding mass shift is given by $a^2
\Delta_\mathrm{Mix} = (316 \pm 4 \textrm{MeV})^2$ at leading order plus
next-to-leading order corrections including the necessary chiral logarithms for
this mixed action calculation, determined in this work. Within the precision of
our calculation, one can not distinguish between the full next-to-leading order
effective field theory analysis of this additive mixed meson mass shift and the
parameterization given above.

In this work, we investigate the possibility of obtaining an almost supersymmetric Standard Model from intersecting D6-branes on the Z_6' orientifold. We focus on the compactification of type IIA string theory on a toroidal background with orientifold planes. We consider a six-dimensional orbifold obtained by dividing a six-dimensional torus by a Z_6' orientation, which leads to orientifold planes and D6-branes at fixed points. We study the low-energy effective theory of the D6-branes and analyze the spectrum of chiral matter under the Standard Model gauge group. We find that the resulting spectrum contains all the particles of the Standard Model, except for the right-handed neutrinos, and that the Yukawa couplings are generated by instanton effects.

Our work aims to address the problem of obtaining a realistic model of particle physics from string theory. The success of the Standard Model of particle physics in explaining the experimental data leaves little room for new physics beyond the Standard Model at low energies. String theory provides a consistent framework for quantum gravity that unifies all fundamental forces, but constructing a realistic model of particle physics from string theory is a challenging task.

Our approach involves the use of intersecting D6-branes and the Z_6' orientifold, which is a particularly interesting model due to its rich phenomenology. Intersecting branes provide a geometric realization of the chiral matter spectrum, while the orientifold projection allows for the cancellation of tadpoles and the breaking of supersymmetry. Our methodology involves a detailed analysis of the low-energy effective theory of the D6-branes and the computation of instanton-generated Yukawa couplings. Our results show that a realistic model of the Standard Model can be obtained from string theory using the methods of intersecting branes and orientifolds.

We present the results of a new spectroscopic and photometric survey of the
hot, binary X-ray cluster A115 at z=0.193, containing a radio relic. We combine
galaxy velocity and position information to select 85 galaxies recognized as
cluster members (from 115 galaxies observed at TNG telescope), determine global
dynamical properties and detect substructures. We find that A115 presents
velocity dispersion of sigma_v=1362_{-108}^{+126} km/s. Our analysis confirms
the presence of two structures (A115N and A115S) of cluster--type well
recognizable in the plane of the sky and shows that they differ of ~2000 km/s
in the LOS velocity. We estimate that A115S is slightly dynamically more
important than A115N having sigma_v=900-1100 km/s vs sigma_v=750-850 km/s.
Moreover, we find evidence for two small groups at low velocities. We estimate
a global cluster virial mass of 2.2--3.5 e15 h_70 Msun. Our results agree with
a pre--merging scenario where A115N and A115S are colliding with a LOS impact
velocity Delta v~1600 km/s.The most likely solution to the two--body problem
suggests that the merging axis lies at $\sim 20$ degrees from the plane of the
sky and that the cores will cross after ~0.1 Gyr. The radio relic with its
largest dimension perpendicular to the merging axis is likely connected to this
merger.

In this paper, we present a study of semiclassical non-characteristic Cauchy problems for the sine-Gordon equation. The aim of this work is to provide exact solutions to this equation under specific conditions while keeping the semiclassical approximation and non-characteristic conditions at play. Our research methodology involves using the inverse scattering transform technique to derive the relevant formulas for each case analyzed. The main contribution of our work lies in the derivation of new exact solutions for the semiclassical non-characteristic Cauchy problems for the sine-Gordon equation. Through our analysis, we demonstrated that the solutions obtained are valid and can be used to describe various physical phenomena in different fields of science, including physics, mathematics, and engineering.

One of the motivations for conducting this research is to advance the current knowledge in the field of semiclassical non-characteristic Cauchy problems. Moreover, the study of the sine-Gordon equation has long been recognized as an important area of research in physics that has direct implications in other fields. Our results have the potential to enhance the ongoing efforts to better understand physical systems and processes that are governed by this equation. The analytical techniques developed in this work may also provide insights into the solution of similar problems in other systems. In conclusion, this work represents a significant step towards a better understanding of the dynamics of nonlinear wave equations.

In this work, we investigate the electrical excitation of shock and soliton-like waves in two-dimensional electron channels. The problem we attempt to solve is the understanding of the dynamic behavior of electrons in highly confined geometries and how they can produce nonlinear waves upon electrical excitation. Our key ideas are to simulate the dynamics of two-dimensional electron channels, study wave propagation, and relate them to the underlying physical mechanisms. 

We use the hydrodynamic model to describe the collective motion of electrons, while considering Coulomb interactions and electron-electron scattering. We analyze the linear and nonlinear regimes of wave propagation by solving the telegraphist's equation and the Korteweg-de Vries equation, respectively. We also provide a physical interpretation of the results by linking wave behavior to the structure of the electron density in the channel and the carrier mobility.

The main motivation for this research is the potential application of these nonlinear waves in electronics for signal processing and energy dissipation. Understanding the dynamics and characterizing the performance of the waves is crucial for designing and optimizing devices that take advantage of these phenomena. Our results provide a comprehensive understanding of shock and soliton-like wave behavior in two-dimensional electron channels, improving the theoretical knowledge and applicability of these waves in electronics.

We discuss decoherence in charge qubits formed by multiple lateral quantum
dots in the framework of the spin-boson model and the Born-Markov
approximation. We consider the intrinsic decoherence caused by the coupling to
bulk phonon modes. Two distinct quantum dot configurations are studied: (i)
Three quantum dots in a ring geometry with one excess electron in total and
(ii) arrays of quantum dots where the computational basis states form multipole
charge configurations. For the three-dot qubit, we demonstrate the possibility
of performing one- and two-qubit operations by solely tuning gate voltages.
Compared to the proposal by DiVincenzo {\it et al.} involving a linear
three-dot spin qubit, the three-dot charge qubit allows for less overhead on
two-qubit operations. For small interdot tunnel amplitudes, the three-dot
qubits have $Q$ factors much higher than those obtained for double dot systems.
The high-multipole dot configurations also show a substantial decrease in
decoherence at low operation frequencies when compared to the double-dot qubit.

We present a study on the strange nucleon form factors in $ep$ and $\nu p$ elastic scattering. The motivation for this research stems from the need to understand the nature and properties of the strange quark in nucleons. We focus on the strange form factors which play an important role in understanding the strange quark's contribution to nucleon properties and structure. The strange nucleon form factors are obtained by measuring parity-violating asymmetry in electron and neutrino scattering experiments.

Our key idea is to use the latest data from the Jefferson Lab and MINERvA experiments to extract the strange form factors by analyzing cross sections and asymmetry data for electroweak interactions between electrons and protons and neutrinos and protons. We use the dipole form factor parametrization to fit the data and extract strange form factors.

We employ a maximum likelihood analysis to obtain the best-fit dipole masses and strange form factors with error estimation methods by using covariance matrix and statistical errors. Our results show significant deviations from previous theoretical predictions and provide a unique insight into the understanding of nucleon structure.

This study contributes to the broader picture of characterizing nucleon properties and provides valuable information towards constraining theoretical models of nucleon structure. We will discuss the implications of our findings and future directions in the search for answers to unresolved questions.

Barchans are dunes of high mobility which have a crescent shape and propagate
under conditions of unidirectional wind. However, sand dunes only appear above
a critical size, which scales with the saturation distance of the sand flux [P.
Hersen, S. Douady, and B. Andreotti, Phys. Rev. Lett. {\bf{89,}} 264301 (2002);
B. Andreotti, P. Claudin, and S. Douady, Eur. Phys. J. B {\bf{28,}} 321 (2002);
G. Sauermann, K. Kroy, and H. J. Herrmann, Phys. Rev. E {\bf{64,}} 31305
(2001)]. It has been suggested by P. Hersen, S. Douady, and B. Andreotti, Phys.
Rev. Lett. {\bf{89,}} 264301 (2002) that this flux fetch distance is itself
constant. Indeed, this could not explain the proto size of barchan dunes, which
often occur in coastal areas of high litoral drift, and the scale of dunes on
Mars. In the present work, we show from three dimensional calculations of sand
transport that the size and the shape of the minimal barchan dune depend on the
wind friction speed and the sand flux on the area between dunes in a field. Our
results explain the common appearance of barchans a few tens of centimeter high
which are observed along coasts. Furthermore, we find that the rate at which
grains enter saltation on Mars is one order of magnitude higher than on Earth,
and is relevant to correctly obtain the minimal dune size on Mars.

We investigate the segregation of the extragalactic population via colour
criteria to produce an efficient and inexpensive methodology to select specific
source populations as a function of far-infrared flux. Combining galaxy
evolution scenarios and a detailed spectral library of galaxies, we produce
simulated catalogues incorporating segregation of the extragalactic population
into component types (Normal, star-forming, AGN) via color cuts. As a practical
application we apply our criteria to the deepest survey to be undertaken in the
far-infrared with the AKARI (formerly ASTRO-F) satellite. Using the
far-infrared wavebands of the Far-Infrared Surveyor (FIS, one of the
focal-plane instruments on AKARI) we successfully segregate the normal,
starburst and ULIRG populations. We also show that with additional MIR imaging
from AKARI's Infrared Camera (IRC), significant contamination and/or degeneracy
can be further decreased and show a particular example of the separation of
cool normal galaxies and cold ULIRG sources. We conclude that our criteria
provide an efficient means of selecting source populations (including rare
luminous objects) and produce colour-segregated source counts without the
requirement of time intensive ground-based follow up to differentiate between
the general galaxy population.

For the past fifteen years, we have conducted extensive high-resolution radio imaging of Supernova 1987A. Our primary goal was to achieve a detailed understanding of the supernova shock interaction with the surrounding circumstellar medium (CSM). This research aimed to explore the complex interplay between the blast wave and the CSM, leading to a comprehensive understanding of the dynamics of supernova explosions. 

Our motivation for this research lies in the desire to obtain concrete information of the long-term evolution and behavior of supernova remnants. Supernovae serve as potent engines of cosmic-ray acceleration, playing a critical role in constraining the origin of galactic cosmic-rays. Detailed investigations of the characteristics of their remnant phenomena can also help uncover the nature of magnetic fields in the interstellar medium and their role in the evolution of the galaxy. 

We have employed a variety of observing techniques, starting from single-dish telescopes to interferometric arrays in different frequency bands, yielding detailed information on the properties of the radio emission in the supernova remnant at different evolutionary stages. Our results have proved fundamental in constraining the physical parameters of the expanding shock wave. Our findings provide vital clues to the distortion of the shock wave caused by the inhomogeneous structure of the CSM, shedding light on the physical processes driving the formation and evolution of supernova remnants.

In this study, we present a new multiphilic descriptor that can be used to predict the reactivity and selectivity of chemical compounds. The motivation for this research comes from the need to develop a reliable and accurate method for predicting the behavior of chemical molecules in various reactions and environments. Current methods for predicting reactivity and selectivity are often limited in their ability to incorporate multiple factors that can influence the behavior of chemical compounds. Our multiphilic descriptor is based on the concept of integration, which allows for the incorporation of multiple chemical properties into a single descriptor.

To test the utility of our new descriptor, we applied it to a diverse set of chemical reactions and found that it was able to accurately predict the reactivity and selectivity of the compounds involved. Our results demonstrate that our multiphilic descriptor is a valuable tool for chemists and researchers working in the field of chemical synthesis and design. We believe that our descriptor has the potential to accelerate the discovery of new chemical compounds with desired properties and can help to reduce the time and cost associated with traditional trial-and-error-based methods.

Overall, our study presents a novel approach to predicting reactivity and selectivity in chemical compounds and has the potential to significantly impact the field of chemical synthesis and design. Our work highlights the importance of incorporating multiple chemical properties into predictive models and demonstrates the potential for multiphilic descriptors to be used as powerful tools for chemical discovery.

In this study, we present annotations to a certain passage of Descartes' work that aims to provide a solution for finding the quadrature of the circle. The key idea behind Descartes' work is to use geometry to find a formula that can calculate the area of a circle. In doing so, he utilized his analytical geometry skills to solve this problem, which was considered a famous challenge at the time. Our research aims to provide annotations to this specific passage that will help interpret the methodology and ideas behind Descartes' work. 

Our motivation for this study is rooted in the fact that Descartes' work was a significant advancement in the field of mathematics. We hope that our annotations will help readers understand the problem as it was presented by Descartes, and contribute to the development of the field by providing valuable insights into the methodology used to solve this particular mathematical problem. 

The problem our work attempts to solve is to provide a comprehensive interpretation of a certain portion of Descartes' work, which is considered one of the most important contributions to the field of mathematics. Our annotations reveal the key concepts, ideas, and techniques used by Descartes to analyze the problem and develop a solution. They also help to contextualize the work and provide a deeper understanding of the significance and contribution of Descartes' work to the history of mathematics.

A Relativistic Transport Model (ART1.0) is applied to simulate the stopping
effects in tip-tip and body-body U+U collisions, at a beam kinetic energy of
520 MeV/nucleon. Our simulation results have demonstrated that both central
collisions of the two extreme orientations can achieve full stopping, and also
form a bulk of hot, dense nuclear matter with a sufficiently large volume and
long duration, due to the largely deformed uranium nuclei. The nucleon sideward
flow in the tip-tip collisions is nearly 3 times larger than that in body-body
ones at normalized impact parameter $b/b_{max}<0.5$, and that the body-body
central collisions have a largest negative nucleon elliptic flow $v_{2}=-12%$
in contrast to zero in tip-tip ones. Thus the extreme circumstance and the
novel experimental observables in tip-tip and body-body collisions can provide
a good condition and sensitive probe to study the nuclear EoS, respectively.
The Cooling Storage Ring (CSR) External Target Facility (ETF) to be built at
Lanzhou, China, delivering the uranium beam up to 520 MeV/nucleon is expected
to make significant contribution to explore the nuclear equation of state
(EoS).

In this paper we analyse the mechanisms responsible for the bonding of
electrons to metal surfaces. We present and validate a method to measure the
energy distribution of dense electron ensembles at ambient conditions. We have
found sharp structures in the energy distribution of charge confined at metal
surfaces. These structures correspond to the onset of space charge i.e.
electron emission. The threshold energy for emission increases linear with the
free electron density. We conclude that the work function is a direct measure
of the Hartree-Fock exchange energy. Remarkably, the emissions that have been
observed do not obey the Einstein-Millikan threshold relation for the photo
electric effect. By discrimination between requirements for energy and momentum
we deduce that electron emissions are trigged by photon momentum. We claim that
the photo electric effect is the result of a phase transition in an electron
gas that connects a surface bound, condensed state to a free continuum state.
The assignment of a phase transition to the effect under consideration is
justified by analyzing the behaviour of the Fermi-Dirac energy distribution
upon raising the Fermi level. We predict an abrupt increment in the number of
electrons in the continuum that exceeds 70 orders of magnitude. Both the
observed threshold energy and the line shape of the transition are in good
agreement with theoretical predictions.

Very high energy gamma-rays from blazars traversing cosmological distances
through the metagalactic radiation field can convert into electron-positron
pairs in photon-photon collisions. The converted gamma-rays initiate
electromagnetic cascades driven by inverse-Compton scattering off the microwave
background photons. Using a model for the time-dependent metagalactic radiation
field consistent with all currently available far-infrared-to-optical data, we
calculate the cascade contribution from faint, unresolved high- and low-peaked
blazars to the extragalactic gamma-ray background as measured by EGRET. For
low-peaked blazars, we adopt a spectral index consistent with the mean spectral
index of EGRET detected blazars, and the luminosity function determined by
Chiang and Mukherjee (1998). For high-peaked blazars, we adopt template spectra
matching prototype sources observed with air-Cherenkov telescopes up to 30 TeV,
and a luminosity function based on X-ray measurements. The low number of about
20 for nearby high-peaked blazars with a flux exceeding 10^-11 cm^-2 s^-1 above
300 GeV inferred from the luminosity function is consistent with the results
from air-Cherenkov telescope observations. Including the cascade emission from
higher redshifts, the total high-peaked blazar contribution to the observed
gamma-ray background at GeV energies can account up to about 30.

In a dynamic nuclear polarization experiment on a 40 mM solution of
4-amino-TEMPO in a 40:60 water/glycerol mixture, we have observed that the bulk
dipolar reservoir is cooled to a spin temperature of 15.5 micro-K, following
microwave irradiation for 800 s. This is significantly cooler than the 35 mK
spin temperature of the Zeeman reservoir. Equilibration of the two reservoirs
results in a 50 % increase in the NMR signal intensity, corresponding to a
Zeeman spin temperature of 23 mK. In order to achieve this polarization
directly, it was necessary to irradiate the sample with microwaves for 1500 s.
Cooling of the dipolar reservoir occurs during polarization transport through
the magnetic field gradient around the paramagnetic impurity, and is rapidly
communicated to the bulk by dipolar spin diffusion. As dipolar spin diffusion
is significantly faster than Zeeman spin diffusion, the bulk dipolar reservoir
cools faster than the Zeeman reservoir. This process can be exploited to
rapidly polarize the nuclear spins, by repeatedly cooling the dipolar system
and transferring the polarization to the Zeeman reservoir.

In this work, we investigate the thermal effects in solid state lasers with a particular focus on ytterbium-doped materials. Our study aims to address the problem of laser heating, which can lead to thermal distortion and ultimately limit laser performance. To gain insights into this phenomenon, we employed a theoretical model that considers not only the usual laser rate equations but also the heat transfer equation. Our simulation results indicate that thermal effects on laser performance depend critically on the absorption coefficient, pump power, and the thermal conductivity of the crystal. Furthermore, we discovered that there is a critical threshold for the absorption coefficient, beyond which the temperature rise in the crystal becomes intense, leading to a reduced output power; this result highlights the importance of careful thermal management in laser design. 

Our research adds to the growing body of literature investigating thermal issues in ytterbium-doped solid-state lasers and provides a quantitative framework for understanding and predicting thermal effects on laser performance. Our results have practical implications for laser engineers and designers looking to maximize the efficiency and performance of solid-state lasers. By taking into account thermal effects early in the laser design stage, we can ensure more robust and reliable laser systems for scientific research, industrial applications, and medical technology.

We have carried out a comparative theoretical study of the electronic
structure of the novel one-dimensional Ca$_3$CoRhO$_6$ and Ca$_3$FeRhO$_6$
systems. The insulating antiferromagnetic state for the Ca$_3$FeRhO$_6$ can be
well explained by band structure calculations with the closed shell high-spin
$d^5$ (Fe$^{3+}$) and low-spin $t_{2g}^{6}$ (Rh$^{3+}$) configurations. We
found for the Ca$_3$CoRhO$_6$ that the Co has a strong tendency to be $d^7$
(Co$^{2+}$) rather than $d^6$ (Co$^{3+}$), and that there is an orbital
degeneracy in the local Co electronic structure. We argue that it is the
spin-orbit coupling which will lift this degeneracy thereby enabling local spin
density approximation + Hubbard U (LSDA+U) band structure calculations to
generate the band gap. We predict that the orbital contribution to the magnetic
moment in Ca$_3$CoRhO$_6$ is substantial, i.e. significantly larger than 1
$\mu_B$ per formula unit. Moreover, we propose a model for the contrasting
intra-chain magnetism in both materials.

In this paper, we present a new model for the Galactic diffuse emission observed by the Large Area Telescope (LAT) on the Gamma-ray Large Area Space Telescope (GLAST). This model is based on the observations of the LAT during the first few years of operation and is intended to provide a more accurate prediction of the Galactic diffuse emission than the previous models. The motivation for this work stems from the need to have a more accurate model of the Galactic diffuse emission in order to improve the sensitivity of the LAT to gamma-ray sources.

Our approach involves fitting the LAT data to a model that includes both the Galactic diffuse emission and the residual cosmic-ray background. The model parameters are then optimized using a maximum likelihood method, which ensures that the model provides the best fit to the data. The results obtained show that our new model provides a significant improvement over the previous models in terms of its accuracy and reliability.

The problem that our work attempts to solve is the need for a more accurate model of the Galactic diffuse emission, which is crucial for the detection and analysis of gamma-ray sources. This work provides an important contribution to the field of gamma-ray astronomy and is likely to have a significant impact on future observations and analyses of the LAT data.

We review recent results obtained in the physics of the thermal Casimir force
acting between two dielectrics, dielectric and metal, and between metal and
semiconductor. The detailed derivation for the low-temperature behavior of the
Casimir free energy, pressure and entropy in the configuration of two real
dielectric plates is presented. For dielectrics with finite static dielectric
permittivity it is shown that the Nernst heat theorem is satisfied. Hence, the
Lifshitz theory of the van der Waals and Casimir forces is demonstrated to be
consistent with thermodynamics. The nonzero dc conductivity of dielectric
plates is proved to lead to a violation of the Nernst heat theorem and, thus,
is not related to the phenomenon of dispersion forces. The low-temperature
asymptotics of the Casimir free energy, pressure and entropy are derived also
in the configuration of one metal and one dielectric plate. The results are
shown to be consistent with thermodynamics if the dielectric plate possesses a
finite static dielectric permittivity. If the dc conductivity of a dielectric
plate is taken into account this results in the violation of the Nernst heat
theorem. We discuss both the experimental and theoretical results related to
the Casimir interaction between metal and semiconductor with different charge
carrier density. Discussions in the literature on the possible influence of
spatial dispersion on the thermal Casimir force are analyzed. In conclusion,
the conventional Lifshitz theory taking into account only the frequency
dispersion remains the reliable foundation for the interpretation of all
present experiments.

We test our capability of deriving stellar physical parameters of giant stars
by analysing a sample of field stars and the well studied open cluster IC 4651
with different spectroscopic methods. The use of a technique based on
line-depth ratios (LDRs) allows us to determine with high precision the
effective temperature of the stars and to compare the results with those
obtained with a classical LTE abundance analysis. (i) For the field stars we
find that the temperatures derived by means of the LDR method are in excellent
agreement with those found by the spectral synthesis. This result is extremely
encouraging because it shows that spectra can be used to firmly derive
population characteristics (e.g., mass and age) of the observed stars. (ii) For
the IC 4651 stars we use the determined effective temperature to derive the
following results. a) The reddening E(B-V) of the cluster is 0.12+/-0.02,
largely independent of the color-temperature calibration used. b) The age of
the cluster is 1.2+/-0.2 Gyr. c) The typical mass of the analysed giant stars
is 2.0+/-0.2M_sun. Moreover, we find a systematic difference of about 0.2 dex
in log g between spectroscopic and evolutionary values. We conclude that, in
spite of known limitations, a classical spectroscopic analysis of giant stars
may indeed result in very reliable stellar parameters. We caution that the
quality of the agreement, on the other hand, depends on the details of the
adopted spectroscopic analysis.

In this work, we present new methods for determining the mass loss rates of asymptotic giant branch (AGB) stars based on radio data. AGB stars are crucial in the chemical enrichment of galaxies, and determining their mass loss rates is essential for understanding their evolution. However, accurately determining these rates has proven to be challenging due to the complex nature of AGB winds. 

We propose two new methods based on radio observations: the first uses spectral indices to estimate mass loss rates, while the second utilizes radio light curves to directly measure the mass loss rates. We validate our methods using observational data of AGB stars. Our results demonstrate the feasibility and accuracy of these methods, providing a valuable tool for future studies of AGB stars and their winds. 

Our work seeks to address the challenge of accurately determining mass loss rates of AGB stars. Our proposed methods are innovative and add to the current techniques used in the field. We believe that our methods can be used in conjunction with existing methods to further constrain the mass loss rates of AGB stars. This work showcases the potential of radio observations in improving our understanding of AGB stars, and we hope it will inspire future work in this area.

We compute the high-frequency emission and absorption noise in a fractional
quantum Hall effect (FQHE) sample at arbitrary temperature. We model the edges
of the FQHE as chiral Luttinger liquids (LL) and we use the non-equilibrium
perturbative Keldysh formalism. We find that the non-symmetrized high frequency
noise contains important signatures of the electron-electron interactions that
can be used to test the Luttinger liquid physics, not only in FQHE edge states,
but possibly also in other one-dimensional systems such as carbon nanotubes. In
particular we find that the emission and absorption components of the excess
noise (defined as the difference between the noise at finite voltage and at
zero voltage) are different in an interacting system, as opposed to the
non-interacting case when they are identical. We study the resonance features
which appear in the noise at the Josephson frequency (proportional to the
applied voltage), and we also analyze the effect of the distance between the
measurement point and the backscattering site. Most of our analysis is
performed in the weak backscattering limit, but we also compute and discuss
briefly the high-frequency noise in the tunneling regime.

We use recently introduced three-point dynamic susceptibilities to obtain an
experimental determination of the temperature evolution of the number of
molecules, N_corr, that are dynamically correlated during the structural
relaxation of supercooled liquids. We first discuss in detail the physical
content of three-point functions that relate the sensitivity of the averaged
two-time dynamics to external control parameters (such as temperature or
density), as well as their connection to the more standard four-point dynamic
susceptibility associated with dynamical heterogeneities. We then demonstrate
that these functions can be experimentally determined with a good precision. We
gather available data to obtain the temperature dependence of N_corr for a
large number of supercooled liquids over a wide range of relaxation timescales
from the glass transition up to the onset of slow dynamics. We find that N_corr
systematically grows when approaching the glass transition. It does so in a
modest manner close to the glass transition, which is consistent with an
activation-based picture of the dynamics in glassforming materials. For higher
temperatures, there appears to be a regime where N_corr behaves as a power-law
of the relaxation time. Finally, we find that the dynamic response to density,
while being smaller than the dynamic response to temperature, behaves
similarly, in agreement with theoretical expectations.

In this study, we present the observational characteristics of the first protostellar cores. Our motivation was to understand the physical properties and evolutionary stages of these cores, which are the precursors to the formation of stars. The protostellar cores were identified from the molecular line data of three star-forming regions in the Chamaeleon, Lupus 3, and Perseus molecular clouds. Our analysis showed that the protostellar cores have typical sizes and masses of 0.05 pc and 0.5 M⊙, respectively, and exhibit signs of inward motions. We also found that the protostellar cores in the Chamaeleon and Lupus 3 regions have higher temperatures and higher column densities compared to those in Perseus. Furthermore, our results suggest that the protostellar cores are embedded in a turbulent medium and have a filamentary structure.

This study aimed to contribute to the understanding of the early stages of star formation and the physical properties of the first protostellar cores. The identified protostellar cores can be considered as the building blocks of future cluster-forming sites and provide insight into the early conditions and processes that lead to the formation of stars. Our study sheds light on the problem of the formation of stars and their early stages, contributing to the broader knowledge on the evolution of the universe.

In this study, we present our findings on the alignment of the radio polarizations in flat-spectrum radio sources from the Jodrell Bank-VLA Astrometric Survey (JVAS) and Cosmic Lens All-Sky Survey (CLASS). We extracted data from polarization maps of 180 flat-spectrum sources using deep imaging with the Very Large Array. Our objective was to investigate whether the polarizations of the radio sources were aligned, with a view to uncovering the underlying properties of the magnetic fields.

Our results reveal that approximately 60% of the sources displayed significant polarization alignment, indicating a preferred direction for the magnetic fields. We found that the degree of polarization alignment was independent of the source redshift or intrinsic luminosity. We also observed a clear trend with the morphology of the radio sources, where objects with greater fuzziness displayed stronger polarization alignments than sources with sharper edges.

Our study provides a glimpse into the nature of the magnetic fields in flat-spectrum radio sources. The findings offer novel insights into the processes that govern the polarization and emission of the sources. The significance of these results extends to the larger field of astrophysics, where understanding the interplay between magnetic fields and radiation is central to our understanding of celestial objects and their evolution.

In this work, we investigate nonsupersymmetric brane/antibrane configurations in Type IIA and M theory. Our motivation for this research comes from the fact that despite being difficult to study, these configurations carry physical significance in the context of string theory. Moreover, their study forms an interesting theoretical challenge as the configurations break the underlying supersymmetric symmetry of the theory. 

Our approach involves exploring the properties of these configurations that allow for their stability and analysing their dynamics using a combination of analytical and numerical tools. Specifically, we consider configurations of D-branes and anti-D-branes, and study their interactions, energy, and stability. We also examine the role of fluxes and higher curvature corrections to understand the features of these higher-dimensional objects.

The key idea in our work is to gain a deeper understanding of the fundamental nature of the brane/antibrane system in the non-supersymmetric scenarios. We expect our analysis to produce insights into the mechanisms that stabilize these systems, and how they compare to supersymmetric brane systems in the present theories. 

In summary, our work aims to solve the problem of understanding the properties and dynamics of nonsupersymmetric brane/antibrane configurations in Type IIA and M theory. Our study can possible shed light into how non-supersymmetric scenarios arise in string theory and help develop new frameworks for studying and analysing the properties of string theoretic objects.

Our work involves studying the behavior of the two-dimensional N=(2,2) super Yang-Mills (SYM) theory on a computer. This theory is an important tool for understanding the behavior of quantum field theories in lower dimensions. The motivation for our research stems from the fact that traditional analytic methods are limited in their ability to fully understand these theories due to their complexity. Therefore, numerical simulations are necessary to gain deeper insights into their behavior.

To accomplish this task, we employ lattice gauge theory simulations to investigate properties of the super Yang-Mills theory. The methodology involves discretizing the continuum theory onto a four-dimensional lattice and utilizing Monte Carlo techniques to perform numerical simulations. We utilize a specific algorithm, the Hybrid Monte Carlo (HMC) method, to efficiently generate gauge field configurations that are used to compute correlation functions in the super Yang-Mills theory.

Our results show that HMC is a reliable method for simulating the super Yang-Mills theory. We also find that the symmetry properties of the theory hold at short distances, while they break down at long distances. Our work highlights the importance of numerical simulations in understanding complex quantum field theories and opens the possibility for further exploration of the N=(2,2) super Yang-Mills theory in the future.

There exist methods for determining effective conservative interactions in
coarse grained particle based mesoscopic simulations. The resulting models can
be used to capture thermal equilibrium behavior, but in the model system we
study do not correctly represent transport properties. In this article we
suggest the use of force covariance to determine the full functional form of
dissipative and stochastic interactions. We show that a combination of the
radial distribution function and a force covariance function can be used to
determine all interactions in dissipative particle dynamics. Furthermore we use
the method to test if the effective interactions in dissipative particle
dynamics (DPD) can be adjusted to produce a force covariance consistent with a
projection of a microscopic Lennard-Jones simulation. The results indicate that
the DPD ansatz may not be consistent with the underlying microscopic dynamics.
We discuss how this result relates to theoretical studies reported in the
literature.

We present the preliminary results from two new surveys of blazars that have
direct implications on the GLAST detection of extragalactic sources from two
different perspectives: microwave selection and a combined deep X-ray/radio
selection. The first one is a 41 GHz flux-limited sample extracted from the
WMAP 3-yr catalog of microwave point sources. This is a statistically well
defined sample of about 200 blazars and radio galaxies, most of which are
expected to be detected by GLAST. The second one is a new deep survey of
Blazars selected among the radio sources that are spatially coincident with
serendipitous sources detected in deep X-ray images (0.3-10 keV) centered on
the Gamma Ray Bursts (GRB) discovered by the Swift satellite. This sample is
particularly interesting from a statistical viewpoint since a) it is unbiased
as GRBs explode at random positions in the sky, b) it is very deep in the X-ray
band (\fx \simgt $10^{-15}$ \ergs) with a position accuracy of a few
arc-seconds, c) it will cover a fairly large (20-30 square deg.) area of sky,
d) it includes all blazars with radio flux (1.4 GHz) larger than 10 mJy, making
it approximately two orders of magnitude deeper than the WMAP sample and about
one order of magnitude deeper than the deepest existing complete samples of
radio selected blazars, and e) it can be used to estimate the amount of
unresolved GLAST high latitude gamma-ray background and its anisotropy
spectrum.

The presence of dust at high redshift requires efficient condensation of
grains in SN ejecta, in accordance with current theoretical models. Yet,
observations of the few well studied SNe and SN remnants imply condensation
efficiencies which are about two orders of magnitude smaller. Motivated by this
tension, we have (i) revisited the model of Todini & Ferrara (2001) for dust
formation in the ejecta of core collapse SNe and (ii) followed, for the first
time, the evolution of newly condensed grains from the time of formation to
their survival - through the passage of the reverse shock - in the SN remnant.
We find that 0.1 - 0.6 M_sun of dust form in the ejecta of 12 - 40 M_sun
stellar progenitors. Depending on the density of the surrounding ISM, between
2-20% of the initial dust mass survives the passage of the reverse shock, on
time-scales of about 4-8 x 10^4 yr from the stellar explosion. Sputtering by
the hot gas induces a shift of the dust size distribution towards smaller
grains. The resulting dust extinction curve shows a good agreement with that
derived by observations of a reddened QSO at z =6.2. Stochastic heating of
small grains leads to a wide distribution of dust temperatures. This supports
the idea that large amounts (~ 0.1 M_sun) of cold dust (T ~ 40K) can be present
in SN remnants, without being in conflict with the observed IR emission.

In this work, we investigate the phase structure of an Abelian two-Higgs model of strongly correlated electrons in the context of Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) at finite density. The main problem we seek to address is the phenomenon of confinement-deconfinement phase transition in strongly correlated electron systems. Through intensive numerical simulations, we demonstrate that the inclusion of an additional Higgs field in the standard model of particle physics leads to a significant strengthening of the confinement-deconfinement phase transition, resulting in a rich and complex phase diagram. Furthermore, we establish a direct link between QCD at finite density and the topological properties of the Higgs fields, demonstrating the pivotal role played by bosonic excitations in the system. Our key contribution lies in the development of a novel theoretical and numerical framework that allows us to investigate the intricate interplay between the electromagnetic and strong interactions in a unified formalism, shedding new light on the fundamental nature of confinement in strongly correlated electron systems. These results have implications for the design of new materials with tunable topological properties, and provide a novel perspective for the study of complex quantum systems beyond the standard model of particle physics.

(Abridged) Using cosmological hydrodynamic simulations in combination with
analytic modeling, we show that the galaxy stellar mass-metallicity relation
(MZR) provides strong constraints on galactic outflows across cosmic time. We
compare three outflow models: No outflows, a "constant wind" (cw) model that
emulates the popular Dekel & Silk (1986) scenario, and a ``momentum-driven
wind" (vzw) model. We find that only the vzw scaling reproduces the observed
z~2 MZR's slope, amplitude, and scatter. Comparing our fully three-dimensional
simulations with a simple one-zone chemical evolution model, we find that the
MZR can be understood in terms of three parameters: (1) The equilibrium
metallicity Z_eq=y*SFR/ACC (where y=true yield), reflecting the enrichment
balance between star formation rate SFR and gas accretion rate ACC; (2) the
dilution time t_d=M_g/ACC, representing the timescale for a galaxy to return to
Z_eq after a metallicity-perturbing interaction; and (3) the blowout mass
M_blowout, which is the galaxy stellar mass above which winds can escape its
halo. When outflows with mass loading factor MLF are present, galaxies below
M_blowout obey Z_eq = y/(1+MLF), while above M_blowout, Z_eq->y. Our cw model
has M_blowout ~ 10^10 M_sun, which yields a sharp upturn in the MZR above this
scale and a flat MZR with large scatter below it, in strong disagreement with
observations. Our vzw model reproduces the observed Z_g \propto M_*^0.3 because
Z_eq \propto MLF^-1 \propto M_*^1/3 when MLF >> 1 (i.e. at low masses). The
flattening of the MZR at M_* > 10^10.5 M_sun observed by Tremonti et al. (2004)
reflects the mass scale where MLF~1, rather than a characteristic wind speed.
The tight observed MZR scatter is ensured when t_d<1 dynamical time, which is
only satisified at all masses and epochs in our momentum-driven wind model.

We report an Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) study on thick multi lamellar
stacks of approx. 10 mum thickness (about 1500 stacked membranes) of DMPC
(1,2-dimyristoyl-sn-glycero-3-phoshatidylcholine) deposited on silicon wafers.
These thick stacks could be stabilized for measurements under excess water or
solution. From force curves we determine the compressional modulus B and the
rupture force F_r of the bilayers in the gel (ripple), the fluid phase and in
the range of critical swelling close to the main transition. AFM allows to
measure the compressional modulus of stacked membrane systems and values for B
compare well to values reported in the literature. We observe pronounced
ripples on the top layer in the Pbeta' (ripple) phase and find an increasing
ripple period Lambda_r when approaching the temperature of the main phase
transition into the fluid Lalpha phase at about 24 C. Metastable ripples with
2Lambda_r are observed. Lambda_r also increases with increasing osmotic
pressure, i.e., for different concentrations of polyethylene glycol (PEG).

In this work, we investigate the saturation effects that arise in sub-Doppler spectroscopy of Cesium vapor confined in an Extremely Thin Cell (ETC). Our research is motivated by the potential use of sub-Doppler spectroscopy in precision measurements of fundamental constants and the need to understand the limitations of this technique.

We first discuss the theoretical background of sub-Doppler spectroscopy and describe the key ideas and methodology we use in our experiments. We then present our experimental results, which show that the spectroscopic lineshape undergoes a significant change as the vapor density is increased beyond a certain threshold value. This saturation effect, which is related to the interaction between the vapor atoms, is further investigated by varying the temperature and the laser power.

The saturation effects we observe can have implications for precision measurements that rely on sub-Doppler spectroscopy, such as atomic clocks and tests of fundamental physics. Our results can also provide insights into the behavior of dense atomic gases and aid in the development of atomic sensors and devices.

In this work, we investigate the dynamic properties of molecular motors in burnt-bridge models. Our research is motivated by the desire to better understand how molecular motors can generate force and motion in biological cells. Specifically, we focus on the burnt-bridge model, a theoretical framework that describes motor motion along a filament. The key idea behind our research is to simulate the behavior of molecular motors under different conditions and observe how their dynamic properties change.

To accomplish this, we use a combination of analytical and computational methods, including Monte Carlo simulations and mean-field calculations. Our simulations take into account the stochastic nature of motor motion and the effects of external forces and various binding conditions on motor dynamics. Our results show that the dynamic properties of motors in burnt-bridge models depend on parameters such as load force, motor concentration, and ATP concentration. In particular, we find that the motors exhibit a novel type of cooperative behavior when subject to high loads and that their velocity can be tuned by adjusting the ATP concentration.

Overall, our work contributes to the ongoing efforts to understand the complex dynamics of molecular motors in biological cells and could have important implications for the design of new drugs and therapies that target these motors.

We present broadband NIR spectra of A0620-00 obtained with SpeX on the IRTF.
The spectrum is characterized by a blue continuum on which are superimposed
broad emission lines of HI and HeII and a host of narrower absorption lines of
neutral metals and molecules. Spectral type standard star spectra scaled to the
dereddened spectrum of A0620-00 in K exceed the A0620-00 spectrum in J and H
for all stars of spectral type K7V or earlier, demonstrating that the donor
star, unless later than K7V, cannot be the sole NIR flux source in A0620-00. In
addition, the atomic absorption lines in the K3V spectrum are too weak with
respect to those of A0620-00 even at 100% donor star contribution, restricting
the spectral type of the donor star in A0620-00 to later than K3V. Comparison
of the A0620-00 spectrum to scaled K star spectra indicates that the CO
absorption features are significantly weaker in A0620-00 than in field dwarf
stars. Fits of scaled model spectra of a Roche lobe-filling donor star to the
spectrum of A0620-00 show that the best match to the CO absorption lines is
obtained when the C abundance is reduced to [C/H] = -1.5. The donor star
contribution in the H waveband is determined to be 82+-2%. Combined with
previous published results from Froning & Robinson (2001) and Marsh et al.
(1994), this gives a precise mass for the black hole in A0620-00 of M_BH =
9.7+-0.6 M_solar.

In this study, we investigated the ferroelectric properties of potassium nitrate using the Density Functional Theory (DFT) method. Our research aimed to understand the electronic and structural properties of ferroelectric potassium nitrate and to determine the possible mechanisms of its ferroelectric behavior. 

Our theoretical analysis revealed that potassium nitrate has a prominent ferroelectric behavior attributed to its structural phase transition from the high-temperature paraelectric phase to the low-temperature ferroelectric phase. The ferroelectric polarization of potassium nitrate was found to be mainly contributed by the displacement of nitrogen and potassium ions in the opposite direction, which leads to a spontaneous polarization in the ferroelectric phase. 

Furthermore, we discovered that the ferroelectric properties of potassium nitrate can be enhanced by doping with certain transition metal ions such as copper or zinc, which can optimize the polarization and increase the Curie temperature. 

Our work attempts to solve the problem of a lack of understanding of the fundamental mechanisms governing the ferroelectric properties of potassium nitrate and to provide insights into the potential application of potassium nitrate for electronic and optical devices. In summary, this theoretical study sheds light on the unique and intricate nature of ferroelectric potassium nitrate and opens up new avenues for further experimental and theoretical investigations.

In this note we study two related questions. (1) For a compact group G, what
are the ranges of the convolution maps on A(GXG) given for u,v in A(G) by $u X
v |-> u*v' ($v'(s)=v(s^{-1})$) and $u X v |-> u*v$? (2) For a locally compact
group G and a compact subgroup K, what are the amenability properties of the
Fourier algebra of the coset space A(G/K)? The algebra A(G/K) was defined and
studied by the first named author.
  In answering the first question, we obtain for compact groups which do not
admit an abelian subgroup of finite index, some new subalgebras of A(G). Using
those algebras we can find many instances in which A(G/K) fails the most
rudimentary amenability property: operator weak amenability. However, using
different techniques, we show that if the connected component of the identity
of G is abelian, then A(G/K) always satisfies the stronger property that it is
hyper-Tauberian, which is a concept developed by the second named author. We
also establish a criterion which characterises operator amenability of A(G/K)
for a class of groups which includes the maximally almost periodic groups.

In this work, we investigate the finite-size effects in the scaling of roughness distribution in various systems. Scaling has been a primary tool for understanding the behavior of rough surfaces, where roughness distribution is one of the essential characteristics. Theoretical and experimental studies demonstrated that roughness distribution follows a universal scaling behavior over a wide range of length scales. However, finite-size effects can significantly affect the scaling of roughness distribution, leading to deviations from the expected behavior. 

In this study, we employed simulations and theoretical analysis to investigate the impact of finite-size effects in roughness distribution scaling. Specifically, we explored how the scaling behavior of roughness distribution changes with system size and how to correct for finite-size effects. Our results indicated that finite-size effects can influence the scaling behavior of roughness distribution, and it is essential to account for these effects in any analysis. Moreover, we proposed new correction procedures that can accurately quantify the finite-size effects, leading to improved analyses and predictions. 

Overall, this work aims to address one of the fundamental problems in the analysis of rough surfaces and has significant implications for a wide range of applications, including material science, engineering, and physics. By providing new insights into the scaling of roughness distribution, this work offers a valuable contribution to the field, enhancing our understanding of the underlying mechanisms governing surface roughness.

In the present paper we study two qubit entanglement in the most general
$XYZ$ Heisenberg magnetic chain with (non)homogeneous magnetic fields and the
DM anisotropic antisymmetric exchange interaction, arising from the spin-orbit
coupling . The model includes all known results as particular cases, for both
antiferromagnetic and ferromagnetic $XX, XY, XXX, XXZ, XYZ$ chains. The
concurrence of two qubit thermal entanglement and its dependence on anisotropic
parameters, external magnetic field and temperature are studied in details. We
found that in all cases, inclusion of the DM interaction, which is responsible
for weak ferromagnetism in mainly antiferromagnetic crystals and spin
arrangement in low symmetry magnets, creates (when it does not exist) or
strengthens (when it exists) entanglement in $XYZ$ spin chain. This implies
existence of a relation between arrangement of spins and entanglement, in which
the DM coupling plays an essential role. It suggests also that anisotropic
antisymmetric exchange interaction could be an efficient control parameter of
entanglement in the general $XYZ$ case.

The ever-increasing knowledge of the structure of various real-world networks
has uncovered their complex multi-mechanism-governed evolution processes.
Therefore, a better understanding of the structure and evolution of these
networked complex systems requires us to describe such processes in a more
detailed and realistic manner. In this paper, we introduce a new type of
network growth rule which comprises addition and deletion of nodes, and propose
an evolving network model to investigate the effect of node deleting on network
structure. It is found that, with the introduction of node deleting, network
structure is significantly transformed. In particular, degree distribution of
the network undergoes a transition from scale-free to exponential forms as the
intensity of node deleting increases. At the same time, nontrivial
disassortative degree correlation develops spontaneously as a natural result of
network evolution in the model. We also demonstrate that node deleting
introduced in the model does not destroy the connectedness of a growing network
so long as the increasing rate of edges is not excessively small. In addition,
it is found that node deleting will weaken but not eliminate the small-world
effect of a growing network, and generally it will decrease the clustering
coefficient in a network.

In this study, we investigated the formation and evolution of protostellar clusters in intermediate-mass (IM) star forming regions. The problem that our work attempts to solve is the lack of understanding regarding the physical mechanisms that govern the formation of protostellar clusters and their evolution in these regions. This lack of knowledge has hindered our ability to map the formation of star clusters and the subsequent development of their member stars.

To address this problem, we developed a methodology that allowed us to analyze the star formation process in IM star forming regions. We used data obtained from the Spitzer Space Telescope and the Herschel Space Observatory to map the star formation in two protostellar clusters. Our results suggest that protostellar clusters in IM star forming regions form through gravitational fragmentation of dense molecular clouds. We observed several stages of evolution, including the formation of protostars, the growth of clusters, and the emergence of OB stars and their impact on the surrounding interstellar medium.

In conclusion, our study sheds light on the formation and evolution of protostellar clusters in IM star forming regions. It highlights the key ideas and methodology that can be used to map the evolution of star clusters and study the physical processes that determine their characteristics. Our work has important implications for our understanding of the formation and evolution of stars and galaxies.

Our study investigates the structure of high galactic latitude interstellar neutral hydrogen (HI) and its association with high frequency continuum emission data collected by the Wilkinson Microwave Anisotropy Probe (WMAP). The motivation behind this research lies in the understanding of the physical and chemical processes that govern the formation and evolution of the interstellar medium (ISM) in the Milky Way galaxy.

Previous studies have observed a peculiar pattern of structures in the high-latitude HI distribution called "supershells." These structures are believed to have originated from massive supernova explosions and star forming regions. Our work aims to explore how these supershells influence the distribution and properties of the high-frequency continuum emission detected by the WMAP.

We used a combination of observational data and numerical simulations to investigate the properties and distribution of the HI supershells. The simulations were based on the observational properties of the Milky Way's ISM and the energy output from massive supernova explosions. The results indicate that the observed structure in the HI distribution is consistent with the formation and evolution of supershells. Additionally, we found a strong correlation between the continuum emission data and the distribution of HI supershells.

Our research provides new insights into the nature of the ISM and its complex physical processes. The results of this study have important implications for future research in astrophysics, particularly in the understanding of the interplay between supernova explosions, star formation, and galactic evolution.

Context: Ly-alpha-emitters have proven to be excellent probes of faint,
star-forming galaxies in the high redshift universe. However, although the
sample of known emitters is increasingly growing, their nature (e.g. stellar
masses, ages, metallicities, star-formation rates) is still poorly constrained.
  Aims: We aim to study the nature of Ly-alpha-emitters, to find the properties
of a typical Ly-alpha-emitting galaxy and to compare these properties with the
properties of other galaxies at similar redshift, in particular Lyman-break
galaxies.
  Methods: We have performed narrow-band imaging at the VLT, focused on
Ly-alpha at redshift z ~ 3.15, in the GOODS-S field. We have identified a
sample of Ly-alpha-emitting candidates, and we have studied their Spectral
Energy Distributions (SEDs).
  Results: We find that the emitters are best fit by an SED with low
metallicity (Z/Z_sun = 0.005), low dust extinction (A_V ~ 0.32) and medium
stellar masses of approximately 10^9 M_sun. The age is not very well
constrained. One object out of 24 appears to be a high redshift
Ly-alpha-emitting dusty starburst galaxy. We find filamentary structure as
traced by the Ly-alpha-emitters at the 4 sigma level. The rest-frame UV SED of
these galaxies is very similar to that of Lyman Break Galaxies (LBGs) and
comply with the selection criteria for U-band drop-outs, except they are
intrinsically fainter than the current limit for LBGs.
  Conclusion: Ly-alpha-emitters are excellent probes of galaxies in the distant
universe, and represent a class of star-forming, dust and AGN free, medium mass
objects.

There are two main methods of nulcear fusion: inertial confinement fusion
(ICF) and magnetic confinement fusion (MCF). Existing thermonuclear reactors
are very complex, expensive, large, and heavy. They cannot achieve the Lawson
creterion.
  The author offers an innovation. ICF has on the inside surface of the
shell-shaped combustion chamber a covering of small Prism Reflectors (PR) and
plasma reflector. These prism reflectors have a noteworthy advantage, in
comparison with conventional mirror and especially with conventional shell:
they multi-reflect the heat and laser radiation exactly back into collision
with the fuel target capsule (pellet). The plasma reflector reflects the
Bremsstrahlung radiation.
  The offered innovation decreases radiation losses, creates significant
radiation pressure and increases the reaction time. The Lawson criterion
increases by hundreds of times. The size, cost, and weight of a typical
installation will decrease by tens of times.
  The author is researching the efficiency of these innovations. Keywords:
Thermonuclear reactor, Multi-reflex AB-thermonuclear reactor, aerospace
thermonuclear engine.
  This work is presented as paper AIAA-2006-7225 to Space-2006 Conference,
19-21 September, 2006, San Jose, CA, USA.

In this study, we investigate the correlation between the X-ray-derived and weak-lensing-derived masses for shear-selected galaxy clusters, with A781 being the primary cluster of interest. The motivation for this research stems from the need to establish reliable methods for measuring mass, a crucial parameter for studying the formation and evolution of galaxy clusters. The problem addressed by this work is the discrepancy often found between X-ray and weak-lensing mass estimates, which presents a challenge for accurate modeling of cluster properties.

We begin with an analysis of archival X-ray data from XMM-Newton and weak-lensing data from Subaru/Suprime-Cam for A781. We then compare the two mass measurements and find a significant discrepancy between X-ray and weak-lensing mass estimates. We attribute this discrepancy to the presence of substructures and non-thermal pressure support in the intra-cluster medium, which can bias X-ray mass estimates. In contrast, weak-lensing mass measurements are unaffected by these factors and provide more reliable estimates of total cluster mass.

Our findings demonstrate the importance of utilizing complementary methods for measuring cluster mass, with weak-lensing mass estimates providing more accurate and robust results. The analysis presented in this work serves as a template for future investigations of other galaxy clusters, and may ultimately improve our understanding of the physical processes that govern their formation and evolution.

The ``trans-Planckian'' challenge in cosmology appears when we trace the
present physical wavelengths of fluctuations backwards in time. They become
smaller and smaller until crossing the Planck scale where conventional QFT is
challenged, so that unknown ultraviolet physics may be traced in the observable
cosmological fluctuations. Usually this issue is addressed in the inflationary
context, but trans-Planckian reasoning is much broader. We examine this logic
in a simple example of scalar quantum field theory in the expanding and
contracting Milne universes, where wavelengths of the eigenmodes are red- or
blue-shifted. Trans-Planckian modifications of QFT should result in a
UV-dependent VeV of the energy momentum tensor of a scalar field in the Milne
universe. On the other hand, the Milne universe is another coordinate systems
of flat Minkowski space-time, and the covariant energy momentum tensor should
be the same (but vacuum-dependent) in different coordinates of flat space time.
We explicitly demonstrate that in conventional QFT the energy momentum tensor,
choosing the adiabatic vacuum, is identical to zero in Minkowski coordinates,
and remains zero in the contracting Milne universe (due to non-trivial
cancellations of contributions from particles which appear in the accelerating
frame and from vacuum polarization there). In contrast to this, the
trans-Planckian modification of the energy momentum tensor is not motivated. We
provide a similar argument for the expanding Milne universe, where the energy
momentum tensor in the conformal vacuum is non-zero. Similar arguments are
applicable for other cosmological models where the curvature is much lower than
Planckian which leads to conflicts with trans-Planckian considerations.

Cooling is the main process leading to the condensation of gas in the dark
matter potential wells and consequently to star and structure formation. In a
metal-free environment, the main available coolants are H, He, H$_2$ and HD;
once the gas is enriched with metals, these also become important in defining
the cooling properties of the gas. We discuss the implementation in Gadget-2 of
molecular and metal cooling at temperatures lower that $\rm10^4 K$, following
the time dependent properties of the gas and pollution from stellar evolution.
We have checked the validity of our scheme comparing the results of some test
runs with previous calculations of cosmic abundance evolution and structure
formation, finding excellent agreement. We have also investigated the relevance
of molecule and metal cooling in some specific cases, finding that inclusion of
HD cooling results in a higher clumping factor of the gas at high redshifts,
while metal cooling at low temperatures can have a significant impact on the
formation and evolution of cold objects.
