In this work, we present an analysis of halo-model signatures using 380,000 luminous red galaxies (LRGs) from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) with photometric redshifts. The halo model is a powerful theoretical framework that describes the distribution of matter in the Universe and the clustering of galaxies within it. Our analysis focuses on the two-point correlation function and the power spectrum, which are important statistics for understanding the large-scale structure of the Universe and its evolution over time. 

The main goal of this work is to investigate the relationship between the halo model and the observed clustering properties of LRGs. We seek to quantify the impact of photometric redshift uncertainties on the measurements of statistics derived from the halo model, and to explore the implications of these uncertainties for cosmological inference. Our analysis uses a novel Bayesian hierarchical modeling approach that allows us to account for the uncertainties in photometric redshifts and other observational effects. 

One key result of our study is the measurement of the halo occupation distribution (HOD) of LRGs, which provides insights into the relationship between galaxies and dark matter halos. We find that the HOD of LRGs is consistent with previous measurements, and that the scatter in the HOD is sensitive to the photometric redshift errors. We also find that the observed clustering of LRGs is well described by the halo model, and that these measurements provide valuable constraints on cosmological parameters. Overall, our results demonstrate the importance of understanding the impact of observational uncertainties on cosmological inference, and highlight the power of the halo model for describing the large-scale structure of the Universe.

The fourth science run of the LIGO and GEO 600 gravitational-wave detectors,
carried out in early 2005, collected data with significantly lower noise than
previous science runs. We report on a search for short-duration
gravitational-wave bursts with arbitrary waveform in the 64-1600 Hz frequency
range appearing in all three LIGO interferometers. Signal consistency tests,
data quality cuts, and auxiliary-channel vetoes are applied to reduce the rate
of spurious triggers. No gravitational-wave signals are detected in 15.5 days
of live observation time; we set a frequentist upper limit of 0.15 per day (at
90% confidence level) on the rate of bursts with large enough amplitudes to be
detected reliably. The amplitude sensitivity of the search, characterized using
Monte Carlo simulations, is several times better than that of previous
searches. We also provide rough estimates of the distances at which
representative supernova and binary black hole merger signals could be detected
with 50% efficiency by this analysis.

In this work, we present two results related to homogeneous Hessian nilpotent polynomials. Our main motivation is to explore the theory of Hessian polynomials in the context of nilpotent polynomials. The key idea of our research is to study the relationship between the Hessian matrix and the nilpotency of the polynomial.   

In the first result, we show that if a homogeneous polynomial of degree d is Hessian nilpotent, then so is the polynomial obtained by removing any monomial of degree less than d from the initial polynomial. The proof of this result relies on the algebraic manipulation of the Hessian matrix and the application of some basic linear algebra tools. 

In the second result, we give an explicit construction of a class of Hessian nilpotent polynomials. Specifically, we show that any homogeneous polynomial of degree 2d-2 whose Hessian matrix has rank less than or equal to d-1 is Hessian nilpotent. The construction involves a systematic iteration process that generalizes a classical result in algebraic geometry known as the "determinantal representation." 

Our results contribute to the theory of nilpotent polynomials and provide new insights into the interplay between the Hessian matrix and the nilpotency of polynomials. The techniques presented here could also be used to study other families of polynomials and matrices.

In this study, we present a generalized version of the Stillinger-Lovett (SL) sum rules for the two-dimensional jellium. The SL sum rules provide a relationship between the total potential energy of a system and its charge density, thus serving as a powerful tool for understanding the electronic structure of materials. However, the original SL sum rules were developed for three-dimensional systems, and their applicability to two-dimensional materials remains uncertain. 

To address this issue, we derived a set of two-dimensional SL sum rules using the real-space Green’s function approach. Our results indicate that these sum rules hold true for a wide range of jellium parameters, from the low-density regime to the strongly correlated electron regime. We also compared our results with those obtained from the Kohn-Sham density functional theory and found excellent agreement between the two methods. 

The development of these new SL sum rules was motivated by the increasing interest in two-dimensional materials, especially their mechanical and electronic properties. As such, our study provides a valuable tool for understanding and predicting the behavior of these materials in a wide range of applications, from electronics to energy storage. Overall, our study demonstrates the utility of SL sum rules for understanding the electronic structure of two-dimensional jellium, and offers a promising avenue for future studies in this area of research.

We study models in which supersymmetry breaking appears at an intermediate
scale, M_{in}, below the GUT scale. We assume that the soft
supersymmetry-breaking parameters of the MSSM are universal at M_{in}, and
analyze the morphology of the constraints from cosmology and collider
experiments on the allowed regions of parameter space as M_{in} is reduced from
the GUT scale. We present separate analyses of the (m_{1/2},m_0) planes for
tan(beta)=10 and tan(beta)=50, as well as a discussion of non-zero trilinear
couplings, A_0. Specific scenarios where the gaugino and scalar masses appear
to be universal below the GUT scale have been found in mirage-mediation models,
which we also address here. We demand that the lightest neutralino be the LSP,
and that the relic neutralino density not conflict with measurements by WMAP
and other observations. At moderate values of M_{in}, we find that the allowed
regions of the (m_{1/2},m_0) plane are squeezed by the requirements of
electroweak symmetry breaking and that the lightest neutralino be the LSP,
whereas the constraint on the relic density is less severe. At very low M_{in},
the electroweak vacuum conditions become the dominant constraint, and a
secondary source of astrophysical cold dark matter would be necessary to
explain the measured relic density for nearly all values of the soft
SUSY-breaking parameters and tan(beta). We calculate the neutralino-nucleon
cross sections for viable scenarios and compare them with the present and
projected limits from direct dark matter searches.

In our work titled "Oscillation Effects and Time Variation of the Supernova Neutrino Signal", we investigate the time and oscillation variations of the neutrino signal arising from a core collapse supernova. Neutrinos are instrumental in providing information about the dynamics of supernovae and the possible formation of neutron stars and black holes. We employ a detailed numerical model of the core-collapse and neutrino emission processes to simulate an expected neutrino signal.

Our analysis incorporates the complex interplay between the neutrino production rate and the propagation of the neutrinos through the dense and varying environment of the supernova core. We demonstrate that the observed neutrino signal can vary by as much as 40% over a period of tens of milliseconds due to these effects. Additionally, we show that neutrino oscillations can lead to a substantial deformation of the neutrino spectra and, at certain epochs, even a total suppression of certain neutrino flavors.

The results show that the time variation of the neutrino signal is linked to the dynamics of the supernova and can be used to probe the structure of the supernova core which is not accessible to other means. Our work provides valuable insights for future neutrino detections from core-collapse supernovae, which will aid in deciphering the astrophysical and particle physics properties of these fascinating events.

[Abridged] The WIde-field Nearby Galaxy-cluster Survey (WINGS) is a project
aiming at the study of the galaxy populations in clusters in the local universe
(0.04<z<0.07) and the influence of environment on the physical properties of
galaxies. This survey provides a high quality set of spectroscopic data for
~6000 galaxies in 48 clusters. A salient feature of this model is the
possibility of treating dust extinction as a function of age, allowing younger
stars to be more obscured than older ones. Our technique, for the first time,
takes into account this feature in a spectral fitting code. A set of template
spectra spanning a wide range of star formation histories is built, with
features closely resembling those of typical spectra in our sample in terms of
spectral resolution, noise and wavelength coverage. Our method of analyzing
these spectra allows us to test the reliability and the uncertainties related
to each physical parameter we are inferring. The well-known degeneracy problem,
i.e. the non-uniqueness of the best fit solution (mass and extinction in
different age bins), can be addressed by assigning adequate error bars to the
recovered parameters. The values found in this way, together with their error
bars, identify the region of parameter space which contains all the possible
solutions for a given spectrum. A comparison test was also performed on a WINGS
subsample, containing objects in common with the Sloan Digital Sky Survey,
yielding excellent agreement. We find that the stellar content as a function of
age is reliably recovered in four main age bins and that the uncertainties only
mildly depend on the S/N ratio. The metallicity of the dominant stellar
population is not always recoverable unambiguosly, depending on the Star
Formation History pattern.

In this work, we present an effective theory for trapped few-fermion systems. The problem we aim to solve is the accurate description of ultracold atomic gases containing a small number of fermions in a trap, especially when the inter-particle interaction is strong. The key idea behind our approach is to construct an effective field theory that captures the low-energy behavior of the system by integrating out the high-energy degrees of freedom. Our method takes into account the effects of the external trapping potential and the inter-particle interactions, as well as the finite size and statistics of the particles. We also include the effect of three-body interactions, which play an important role in some experimental settings.

The motivation for this research is twofold. On the one hand, understanding few-fermion systems is important for the development of quantum technologies, such as quantum computing and quantum simulation. On the other hand, these systems provide a unique testing ground for the study of many-body physics, as the small number of particles allows for precise control and measurement of the system's properties. Our approach provides a systematic way of studying these systems, allowing for the calculation of observables such as energy levels and pair correlations, which can be compared to experiments.

In conclusion, our effective theory for trapped few-fermion systems provides a powerful tool for the study of ultracold atomic gases and the many-body physics they exhibit. The key ideas and methodology presented in this work can be extended to other systems, such as trapped bosons and molecules, as well as to more complex scenarios where the trap is time-dependent or the particles are in different internal states.

Power control is a fundamental task accomplished in any wireless cellular
network; its aim is to set the transmit power of any mobile terminal, so that
each user is able to achieve its own target SINR. While conventional power
control algorithms require knowledge of a number of parameters of the signal of
interest and of the multiaccess interference, in this paper it is shown that in
a large CDMA system much of this information can be dispensed with, and
effective distributed power control algorithms may be implemented with very
little information on the user of interest. An uplink CDMA system subject to
flat fading is considered with a focus on the cases in which a linear MMSE
receiver and a non-linear MMSE serial interference cancellation receiver are
adopted; for the latter case new formulas are also given for the system SINR in
the large system asymptote. Experimental results show an excellent agreement
between the performance and the power profile of the proposed distributed
algorithms and that of conventional ones that require much greater prior
knowledge.

Water molecules play a fundamental role in the physics of slow crack
propagation in glasses. It is commonly understood that, during
stress-corrosion, water molecules that move in the crack cavity effectively
reduce the bond strength at the strained crack tip and, thus, support crack
propagation. Yet the details of the environmental condition at the crack tip in
moist air are not well determined. In a previous work, we reported direct
evidence of the presence of a 100 nm long liquid condensate at the crack tip in
fused silica glass during very slow crack propagation (10^-9 to 10^-10 m/s).
These observations are based on in-situ AFM phase imaging techniques applied on
DCDC glass specimens in controlled atmosphere. Here, we discuss the physical
origin of the AFM phase contrast between the liquid condensate and the glass
surface in relation to tip-sample adhesion induced by capillary bridges. We
then report new experimental data on the water condensation length increase
with relative humidity in the atmosphere. The measured condensation lengths
were much larger than what predicted using the Kelvin equation and expected
geometry of the crack tip.

The frequency distribution of DNA bases A, C, G, T exhibit fractal
fluctuations ubiquitous to dynamical systems in nature. The power spectra of
fractal fluctuations exhibit inverse power law form signifying long-range
correlations between local (small-scale) and global (large-scale)
perturbations. The author has developed a general systems theory based on
classical statistical physics for fractal fluctuations which predicts that the
probability distribution of eddy amplitudes and the variance (square of eddy
amplitude)spectrum of fractal fluctuations follow the universal Boltzmann
inverse power law expressed as a function of the golden mean. The model
predicted distribution is very close to statistical normal distribution for
fluctuations within two standard deviations from the mean and exhibits a fat
long tail. In this paper it is shown that DNA base CG frequency distribution in
Takifugu rubripes (Puffer fish) Genome Release 4 exhibit universal inverse
power law form consistent with model prediction. The observed long-range
correlations in the DNA bases implies that the non-coding 'junk' or 'selfish'
DNA which appear to be redundant, may also contribute to the efficient
functioning of the protein coding DNA, a result supported by recent studies.

We study the effects of dissipation on electron transport in a semiconductor
superlattice with an applied bias voltage and a magnetic field that is tilted
relative to the superlattice axis.In previous work, we showed that although the
applied fields are stationary,they act like a THz plane wave, which strongly
couples the Bloch and cyclotron motion of electrons within the lowest miniband.
As a consequence,the electrons exhibit a unique type of Hamiltonian chaos,
which creates an intricate mesh of conduction channels (a stochastic web) in
phase space, leading to a large resonant increase in the current flow at
critical values of the applied voltage. This phase-space patterning provides a
sensitive mechanism for controlling electrical resistance. In this paper, we
investigate the effects of dissipation on the electron dynamics by modifying
the semiclassical equations of motion to include a linear damping term. We
demonstrate that even in the presence of dissipation,deterministic chaos plays
an important role in the electron transport process. We identify mechanisms for
the onset of chaos and explore the associated sequence of bifurcations in the
electron trajectories. When the Bloch and cyclotron frequencies are
commensurate, complex multistability phenomena occur in the system. In
particular, for fixed values of the control parameters several distinct stable
regimes can coexist, each corresponding to different initial conditions. We
show that this multistability has clear, experimentally-observable, signatures
in the electron transport characteristics.

In this work, we investigate the thermal symmetry of the Markovian master equation. Our motivation for this research is rooted in the fact that the Markovian master equation is widely used in various fields, such as quantum mechanics and statistical physics. The thermal symmetry of such an equation plays a crucial role in understanding the dynamics of the system under consideration.

The key idea of our research is to investigate the thermal symmetry of the Markovian master equation by considering the non-equilibrium steady states of the system. We employ a variety of mathematical techniques, including the use of heat baths and the derivation of a detailed balance condition, to understand the thermal symmetry of the equation.

Our results demonstrate that the Markovian master equation exhibits thermal symmetry under certain conditions, which is crucial in understanding the thermodynamic properties of the system. Specifically, we show that the thermal symmetry is maintained if the underlying system is time-reversal symmetric, or if the Hamiltonian of the system is symmetric under the exchange of particles.

In conclusion, our work provides an important contribution to the field of statistical physics and quantum mechanics by elucidating the thermal symmetry of the Markovian master equation. Our findings offer a deeper understanding of the thermodynamic properties of systems governed by this equation, which has important implications in the design of devices such as quantum computers, where precise thermal control is essential.

In this work, we present a dynamical analysis of the 14 Her planetary system, which consists of a hot Jupiter and a distant giant planet. The motivation for this research, is to understand the origin and stability of this system, as well as to improve our understanding of the dynamics of hot Jupiter systems. Our study is based on a numerical simulation of the system's dynamical evolution over a period of 10 million years, which takes into account the planets' gravitational interactions, as well as the effects of General Relativity and tidal dissipation. 

Our results show that the 14 Her system is in a dynamically stable configuration, with the two planets maintaining their orbits for the entire simulation period. The hot Jupiter undergoes significant eccentricity oscillations on a time scale of a few thousand years, but its orbit remains within a safe distance from the star. The distant planet, on the other hand, is shown to be in a long-term stable orbit with a moderate eccentricity. 

Our study concludes that the 14 Her system is a rare example of a dynamically stable hot Jupiter system, and may provide important insights into the formation and evolution of such systems. The methods and techniques used in this work can also be applied to other exoplanetary systems, providing key insights into the dynamical processes that shape planetary systems.

Using examples of the square- and triangular-lattice Heisenberg models we
demonstrate that the density matrix renormalization group method (DMRG) can be
effectively used to study magnetic ordering in two-dimensional lattice spin
models. We show that local quantities in DMRG calculations, such as the on-site
magnetization M, should be extrapolated with the truncation error, not with its
square root, as previously assumed. We also introduce convenient sequences of
clusters, using cylindrical boundary conditions and pinning magnetic fields,
which provide for rapidly converging finite-size scaling. This scaling behavior
on our clusters is clarified using finite-size analysis of the effective
sigma-model and finite-size spin-wave theory. The resulting greatly improved
extrapolations allow us to determine the thermodynamic limit of M for the
square lattice with an error comparable to quantum Monte Carlo. For the
triangular lattice, we verify the existence of three-sublattice magnetic order,
and estimate the order parameter to be M = 0.205(15).

Traversable wormholes are objects that present a lot of interest in the last
years because of their geometric features and their relation with exotic
matter. In this paper we presnt a review of the principal characteristics of
traversable Morris-Thorne wormholes, their construction proccess and some
aspects about the exotic matter that is needed in order to mantain them. Then,
we use a junction proccess to obatin two specific wormhole solutions in the
(2+1) gravity formalism with negative cosmological constant. The obtained
solutions represent wormholes with an external spacetime correspondient to the
BTZ black hole solution. We also show that exotic matter is needed to mantain
these wormholes.
  -----
  Los agujeros de gusano atravesables son objetos que presentan un gran interes
en la actualidad debido a sus caracteristicas geometricas y a su relacion con
la materia exotica. En el presente trabajo se muestra una revision de las
caracteristicas de los agujeros de gusano atravesables al estilo de Morris y
Thorne, al igual que el proceso de construccion y aspectos de la materia
exotica necesaria para mantenerlos. Luego, se utiliza un proceso de juntura
para construir dos soluciones especificas tipo agujero de gusano en el
formalismo de la gravedad (2+1) con constante cosmologica negativa. Con esta
construccion, se obtienen agujeros atravesables que se encuentran unidos a un
espacio-tiempo externo correspondiente al agujero negro BTZ sin momento angular
y sin carga electrica. Ademas de esto, se muestra que para mantener este tipo
de solucion es necesaria la existencia de materia exotica, es decir, materia
que viole las condiciones de energia.

We search for and characterize substructures in the projected distribution of
galaxies observed in the wide field CCD images of the 77 nearby clusters of the
WIde-field Nearby Galaxy-cluster Survey (WINGS). This sample is complete in
X-ray flux in the redshift range 0.04<z<0.07. We search for substructures in
WINGS clusters with DEDICA, an adaptive-kernel procedure. We test the procedure
on Monte-Carlo simulations of the observed frames and determine the reliability
for the detected structures. DEDICA identifies at least one reliable structure
in the field of 55 clusters. 40 of these clusters have a total of 69
substructures at the same redshift of the cluster (redshift estimates of
substructures are from color-magnitude diagrams). The fraction of clusters with
subclusters (73%) is higher than in most studies. The presence of subclusters
affects the relative luminosities of the brightest cluster galaxies (BCGs).
Down to L ~ 10^11.2 L_Sun, our observed differential distribution of subcluster
luminosities is consistent with the theoretical prediction of the differential
mass function of substructures in cosmological simulations.

In this paper, we investigate the depletion effects in a mixture of hard rod--hard sphere and its implications on the properties of smectic phases. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that the study of smectic phases has significant implications not only for basic science but also for a wide range of practical applications such as in materials science, liquid crystal displays, and drug delivery systems. 

The depletion effects in smectic phases of hard rod--hard sphere mixtures result from the interplay of the competition between the orientational ordering of the rods and the ordering of the spheres. This competition leads to a decrease in the effective excluded volume of the spheres which, in turn, affects the properties of the smectic phases.

The research problem that this work attempts to solve is to understand the depletion effects on the smectic phases of hard rod--hard sphere mixtures and to elucidate their effects on the structure and properties of the smectic phases. We employ a combination of theoretical and simulation approaches to investigate these effects and to gain a deeper understanding of the underlying physics.

Our key findings suggest that the depletion effects in hard rod--hard sphere mixtures alter the orientational order of the smectic phases and affect their free energy and elastic properties. These insights provide critical information for the design and optimization of the smectic phases in various applications.

In this study, we report on our search for gravitational-wave bursts in data collected by LIGO during its fourth science run. The main problem we aim to address is the detection of short-lived, high-frequency gravitational-wave signals that are challenging to detect using traditional techniques. These signals may originate from a range of astrophysical events, such as black hole mergers or supernova explosions, and may provide important information about the dynamics of the universe.

To address this problem, we employed a novel search algorithm that is based on a suite of time-frequency methods designed to identify coherent signals above the detector noise. The methodology involves the detection and characterization of short-lived transient events in the data using wavelet transforms and matched filtering. We also implemented a hierarchical follow-up procedure to reduce the number of false positives detected.

The motivation for this research stems from the desire to deepen our understanding of the universe and the physical processes that drive it. The detection of gravitational waves has opened up new avenues of exploration and has the potential to transform our understanding of fundamental physics. Our search for gravitational-wave bursts in LIGO data from the fourth science run represents a significant step forward in the ongoing quest to detect and characterize these elusive signals.

In this study, we present an improved version of the quantum hard-sphere ground-state equation of state (EOS) that accurately describes both the low-density and high-density regimes. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that the current hard-sphere EOS is known to be inadequate in describing certain aspects of materials such as dense uranium and other simple metals. Previous attempts have focused on improving subsets of the EOS rather than the whole EOS, which led to inconsistencies and limitations in predicting the thermodynamic properties of these materials.

The key idea behind our improved EOS is to incorporate a more accurate description of quantum mechanical effects. Specifically, we utilize a new analytical expression for the quantum correction factor, which accounts for the wave nature of the particles. To test the validity and accuracy of our improved EOS, we carry out extensive Monte Carlo simulations of hard-sphere systems with varying densities. Our results show that our improved EOS is in excellent agreement with the simulation data over a wide range of densities.

Overall, our study has provided an effective theoretical tool that can accurately predict the structural and thermodynamic behavior of complex systems such as dense metals. Our analytical expression for the quantum correction factor has enabled us to create a more accurate and predictable EOS, which is a significant step towards improving the quantitative description of many materials that have hard-sphere-like interactions.

In our study, "The substellar mass function in sigma Orionis. II. Optical, near-infrared and IRAC/Spitzer photometry of young cluster brown dwarfs and planetary-mass objects," we sought to understand the substellar mass distribution within the young Sigma Orionis cluster. This work builds upon prior observations within the cluster that detected a relative excess of high masses, indicating that the Sigma Orionis cluster is a unique site for studying the formation of brown dwarfs and planetary-mass objects. Our key motivation was to develop a more complete understanding of the mass function of brown dwarfs and planetary-mass objects within the cluster to test theoretical models of substellar formation.

We performed optical, near-infrared and IRAC/Spitzer photometry of observed objects in the Sigma Orionis cluster, alongside estimating their masses and ages using previously discovered fundamental relations. We then compared our mass distribution to theoretical predictions, revealing both similarities and discrepancies between data and models. Overall, our work represents a significant step forward in understanding the formation and evolution of substellar objects within the Sigma Orionis cluster. Our results will provide valuable insights into the substellar mass function in regions of intense star formation and help to refine our understanding of the mechanisms that lead to the development of brown dwarfs and planetary-mass objects.

In this work, we investigate the maximum number of solutions of normalized Ricci flows on 4-manifolds. The problem we attempt to solve is to determine the maximum number of solutions of normalized Ricci flows on 4-manifolds, subject to the initial conditions of the flow being a Kähler metric. Our results show that there can be at most a finite number of solutions, and in some cases, the maximum number of solutions is exactly two.

Our key ideas and methodology involve using tools from geometric analysis, particularly the study of Ricci flows on complex manifolds, and the use of the Bochner-Kodaira-Nakano identity. We analyze the conditions under which the maximum number of solutions can be obtained, and we provide a proof of our result for the case of two solutions. Additionally, our analysis of the properties of the solutions of normalized Ricci flows on 4-manifolds allows us to draw connections to the geometry of complex algebraic surfaces, as well as to the classification of compact complex surfaces.

Overall, our work presents important contributions to the study of normalized Ricci flows on 4-manifolds, offering new insights into their behavior, and opening new avenues toward further investigation of the geometry of complex surfaces.

We introduce a method to constrain general cosmological models using Baryon
Acoustic Oscillation (BAO) distance measurements from galaxy samples covering
different redshift ranges, and apply this method to analyse samples drawn from
the SDSS and 2dFGRS. BAO are detected in the clustering of the combined 2dFGRS
and SDSS main galaxy samples, and measure the distance--redshift relation at
z=0.2. BAO in the clustering of the SDSS luminous red galaxies measure the
distance--redshift relation at z=0.35. The observed scale of the BAO calculated
from these samples and from the combined sample are jointly analysed using
estimates of the correlated errors, to constrain the form of the distance
measure D_V(z)=[(1+z)^2D_A^2cz/H(z)]^(1/3). Here D_A is the angular diameter
distance, and H(z) is the Hubble parameter. This gives
r_s/D_V(0.2)=0.1980+/-0.0058 and r_s/D_V(0.35)=0.1094+/-0.0033 (1sigma errors),
with correlation coefficient of 0.39, where r_s is the comoving sound horizon
scale at recombination. Matching the BAO to have the same measured scale at all
redshifts then gives D_V(0.35)/D_V(0.2)=1.812+/-0.060. The recovered ratio is
roughly consistent with that predicted by the higher redshift SNLS supernovae
data for Lambda cosmologies, but does require slightly stronger cosmological
acceleration at low redshift. If we force the cosmological model to be flat
with constant w, then we find Om_m=0.249+/-0.018 and w=-1.004+/-0.089 after
combining with the SNLS data, and including the WMAP measurement of the
apparent acoustic horizon angle in the CMB.

In this work, we explore the higher order Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) corrections in exclusive charmless B decays. Charmless B decays play a significant role in probing the Standard Model and testing new physics. However, their theoretical prediction poses a significant challenge due to the presence of nonperturbative QCD effects. The purpose of our research is to investigate and understand the role of higher-order QCD corrections in charmless B decays, and their impact on the theoretical prediction of branching ratios and other observables.

Our methodology involves the use of effective field theories and perturbation theory to calculate the higher-order QCD corrections. Specifically, we employ the soft-collinear effective theory (SCET) framework to carry out the calculations. We use the operator product expansion (OPE) to separate the contributions of the different scales and carry out the perturbative expansion of the hard, soft, and collinear modes. 

The motivation for our research stems from the need to improve the accuracy of theoretical predictions of charmless B decays, which are essential for the interpretation of experimental measurements. Understanding the higher-order corrections is crucial in this regard since they significantly impact the theoretical uncertainties.

Overall, our work contributes to the ongoing effort to improve the theoretical prediction of exclusive charmless B decays, which is essential for testing the Standard Model and discovering new physics.

In this study, we aim to address the issue of accurate calibration of the Gamma-ray Large Area Space Telescope (GLAST) Burst Monitor (GBM) detectors. This calibration process is crucial in obtaining reliable and precise measurements of gamma-ray bursts, which are important in the study of high-energy astrophysics. The key challenge in calibrating GBM detectors is the non-linear response of the detectors to gamma-rays due to the various physical properties of the detectors.

To overcome these challenges, our study proposes a detailed methodology for calibrating GBM detectors. This involves a series of calibration steps, including the characterization of detector response as a function of energy and time, the determination of the energy gain and the detector baseline, and the development of the detector response matrix. We implement this methodology on a set of data collected from the detectors during a calibration campaign and evaluate the results using statistical methods.

The motivation for this research is to improve the accuracy and reliability of gamma-ray measurements and to enhance our understanding of high-energy astrophysics. Our calibration methodology provides an essential tool for achieving this goal and is an important contribution to the field of astrophysics.

In this research, we investigate the timing and lensing effects of the colliding Bullet Clusters and the acceleration process of the bullet. The goal of the paper is to explore how gravity and time constraints affect the behavior of the colliding Bullet Clusters. 

Our methodology involves using multi-wavelength observations from the Hubble Space Telescope and Chandra X-ray observatory to study the physical properties and evolution of the Cluster. We also employ a statistical model to study the properties and evolution of the Bullet Cluster after the collision. 

Our results show that the collision between the two clusters was a recent occurrence with the Bullet Cluster traveling at a velocity of nearly 4700 km/sec. Furthermore, the analysis indicated that the Bullet Cluster was decelerated only slightly by gravity, leading to the conclusion that the gravity of the Bullet Cluster was not enough to slow it down. 

Overall, our study provides additional insight into the behavior of Bullet Clusters and the collision process and highlights that timing and gravity play an essential role in determining the outcome. Our research showcases the importance of multi-wavelength observations and advanced statistical modelling techniques for the study of Cluster dynamics and evolution.

In our current study, "Nonequilibrium Steady States of Matrix Product Form: A Solver's Guide," we address the problem of finding nonequilibrium steady states for one-dimensional spin systems with local interactions. These systems exhibit complex and often intractable non-equilibrium behavior, generating wealth of phenomena ranging from transport properties to thermodynamic anomalies. For that reason, the need to solve the dynamics of nonequilibrium systems has become a central issue in condensed matter physics and statistical mechanics. In this work, we focus on the exact computation of the stationary states of the dynamics, expressed in matrix product form, using a real-space renormalization group algorithm. Our solver guarantees an efficient and numerically stable diagonalization of the density matrix, with intrinsic error control, making it usable as a benchmark for other numerical methods. Furthermore, we analyze the key properties of the steady states, such as the fluctuation-dissipation relation, and the nonequilibrium generalizations of the maximum entropy principle. Our approach paves the way for treating more complex systems, including models with long-range interactions, higher dimensions, or non-abelian symmetries. We hope that our results will prove essential for the theoretical and computational study of nonequilibrium dynamics, which are expected to play a crucial role in the development of future quantum technologies and energy distributions.

A transmitter without channel state information (CSI) wishes to send a
delay-limited Gaussian source over a slowly fading channel. The source is coded
in superimposed layers, with each layer successively refining the description
in the previous one. The receiver decodes the layers that are supported by the
channel realization and reconstructs the source up to a distortion. The
expected distortion is minimized by optimally allocating the transmit power
among the source layers. For two source layers, the allocation is optimal when
power is first assigned to the higher layer up to a power ceiling that depends
only on the channel fading distribution; all remaining power, if any, is
allocated to the lower layer. For convex distortion cost functions with convex
constraints, the minimization is formulated as a convex optimization problem.
In the limit of a continuum of infinite layers, the minimum expected distortion
is given by the solution to a set of linear differential equations in terms of
the density of the fading distribution. As the bandwidth ratio b (channel uses
per source symbol) tends to zero, the power distribution that minimizes
expected distortion converges to the one that maximizes expected capacity.
While expected distortion can be improved by acquiring CSI at the transmitter
(CSIT) or by increasing diversity from the realization of independent fading
paths, at high SNR the performance benefit from diversity exceeds that from
CSIT, especially when b is large.

In this paper, we present our assessment of the Accretion vs Colliding Wind (AVCW) models for the gamma-ray binary LS I +61 303, which has been a topic of controversy among the astrophysical community. The motivation behind our research was the need to clarify the origin of the gamma-ray emission in this binary. Our methodology involved the use of non-LTE radiative transfer simulation to construct synthetic spectra, which were then compared with the observed data. We also made use of Chandra and HST observations to study the X-ray and optical emission, respectively.

Our results suggest that the colliding wind scenario is favored over the accretion scenario since it better reproduces the observed gamma-ray spectrum of LS I +61 303. Furthermore, the colliding wind model is able to explain the observed behavior of the binary on larger timescales. This result is consistent with previous studies that have suggested that colliding wind interactions between high mass stars can produce non-thermal gamma-ray emission.

In conclusion, our study provides new insights into the nature of gamma-ray emission in LS I +61 303, and adds to the growing body of evidence that suggests colliding wind models are a viable mechanism for producing non-thermal radiation in high-mass binary systems.

In this work, we aim to reconcile the observations of two branches of neutron stars: the first, a high-mass pulsar with a mass of around 2 M_sun, and the second, a neutron star formed by the explosion of the supernova SN1987A, which has a lower mass. This discrepancy has puzzled physicists for years, and we want to provide a solution that helps us understand the underlying physics of neutron stars.

The motivation for this research is clear: neutron stars are one of the most enigmatic objects in the universe, with extreme conditions that challenge our understanding of fundamental physics. Their properties, such as their mass, radius, and spin, can reveal the nature of the densest matter in the universe. However, current models and simulations struggle to explain the existence of these two different branches.

To tackle this problem, we propose a new theoretical framework that explains the two branches as the result of different physical mechanisms. In particular, we suggest that the high-mass pulsar was formed by a mechanism that enhances the mass of a newborn neutron star through a strong magnetic field, while SN1987A is the result of a failed core-collapse supernova that left a smaller neutron star.

Our key idea is to use a combination of numerical simulations and analytical calculations to support this hypothesis. We also compare our results with the latest observations of neutron stars to validate the consistency of our model. Overall, this work provides a new perspective on the properties and formation mechanisms of neutron stars that can shed light on some of the most pressing questions in fundamental physics.

We present a comprehensive analysis of the OZI-forbidden nonleptonic decays $J/\psi(\Upsilon)\to \pi\pi,\rho\pi$ in Orthoquarkonia states within the framework of QCD sum rules. Motivated by the fact that these particular decays involve a large number of competing mechanisms, which makes the calculation of decay widths complicated, we aim to explore the impact of the four-quark condensates within the current formalism. Our main results show that the $\rho\pi$ decay mode is considerably suppressed compared to the $\pi\pi$ channel, in agreement with experimental measurements. We also find that the contributions of the four-quark condensates are significant in both channels and cannot be neglected.

Our methodology involves an effective Lagrangian that describes the interaction between the quarks and the mesons, which is used to derive the relevant correlation functions. These correlation functions are then calculated in the framework of QCD sum rules and matched with the phenomenological side to obtain the decay width. In addition, the factorization method is used to estimate the contributions of the four-quark condensates. 

In conclusion, our study sheds light on the importance of the four-quark condensates in the nonleptonic decays of Orthoquarkonia states. Our findings provide a deeper understanding of these decays and contribute to the ongoing research of QCD sum rules in nonperturbative physics.

In this work, we explore the perspectives for the detection and measurement of supersymmetry (SUSY) in the focus point region of mSUGRA models with the ATLAS detector at LHC. The main motivation for our research lies in the fact that SUSY, a theoretical extension of the Standard Model of particle physics, provides a solution for several limitations of the latter, including the hierarchy and dark matter problems. Despite the promising nature of SUSY, no experimental evidence has been found so far to support its existence.

Our work attempts to solve this problem by analyzing the focus point region of mSUGRA models, which predicts a high SUSY mass that makes detection challenging. By using the ATLAS detector at LHC, we investigate the potential sensitivity of the detector to the SUSY particles produced in this region. We apply a series of cuts to the data to reduce the background noise and increase the signal-to-noise ratio. Using Monte Carlo simulations, we determine the potential for statistical significance of our results.

Our results show that the focus point region of mSUGRA models may still be detectable by the ATLAS detector at LHC, depending on the specific values of the model parameters. Our research opens the possibility of exploring these models further and of shedding further light on the existence of SUSY.

In this work, we investigate the distribution of the very first PopIII stars and their correlation with bright z~6 quasars. The motivation for this research arises from the fact that the first stars are crucial in shaping the evolution of the early universe and that the quasars at z~6 represent the most luminous objects in the universe, which can help us understand the formation of galaxies and black holes. We employ numerical simulations and analyze their outputs to trace the formation and evolution of the first stars and their spatial distribution with respect to bright quasars. Our main result is that PopIII stars appear to have formed in the highest density regions of the universe, where the first bright quasars are observed. Furthermore, we find that the feedback from these stars and quasars leads to the heating and ionization of gas in their vicinity, which sets the stage for the formation of the first galaxies. This work provides insights into the early universe, shedding light on the mechanisms that governed the formation of the first sources of light and their relation to the most luminous objects we observe today.

In this work, we investigate electronic spin transport and spin precession in single graphene layers at room temperature. The key idea is to study the behavior of the electron spins in graphene, which are expected to have long spin lifetimes due to its weak spin-orbit coupling and low hyperfine interaction. We employ a non-local spin valve device to measure the spin transport and precession properties in the single layer graphene. The spin current is injected from a ferromagnetic electrode and detected at another distant ferromagnetic electrode, while the graphene layer serves as a spin channel.

The problem this work attempts to solve is to provide experimental evidence for the long spin lifetimes of electrons in graphene, which is crucial for the development of spintronics applications based on graphene. Our results show a clear signature of spin precession in single graphene layers, which indicates that the electron spins can transport over long distances before losing phase coherence. The spin lifetimes are found to be on the order of nanoseconds, which is consistent with theoretical predictions.

In conclusion, this work provides important insights into the electronic spin transport and precession in single graphene layers, which has potential implications for the development of spin-based electronics. The methodology employed here can also be extended to spin transport studies in other 2D materials and heterostructures.

We jointly model 22 early-type gravitational lens galaxies with stellar
dynamical measurements using standard CDM halo models. The sample is
inhomogeneous in both its mass distributions and the evolution of its stellar
populations unless the true uncertainties are significantly larger than the
reported measurement errors. In general, the individual systems cannot
constrain halo models, in the sense that the data poorly constrains the stellar
mass fraction of the halo. The ensemble of systems, however, strongly
constrains the average stellar mass represented by the visible galaxies to
$0.026\pm0.006$ of the halo mass if we neglect adiabatic compression, rising to
$0.056\pm0.011$ of the halo mass if we include adiabatic compression. Both
estimates are significantly smaller than the global baryon fraction,
corresponding to a star formation efficiency for early-type galaxies of
$10%-30%$. In the adiabatically compressed models, we find an average local
B-band stellar mass-to-light ratio of $(M/L)_0 =
(7.2\pm0.5)(M_{\sun}/L_{\sun})$ that evolves by $d\log(M/L)/dz = -0.72\pm0.08$
per unit redshift. Adjusting the isotropy of the stellar orbits has little
effect on the results. The adiabatically compressed models are strongly favored
if we impose either local estimates of the mass-to-light ratios of early-type
galaxies or the weak lensing measurements for the lens galaxies on 100 kpc
scales as model constraints.

Phenomenologically viable and interesting regions of parameter space in the
minimal super-gravity (mSUGRA) model with small $m_0$ and small $m_{1/2}$
consistent with the WMAP data on dark matter relic density and the bound on the
mass of the lightest Higgs scalar $ m_h>$ 114 GeV from LEP2 open up if the
rather adhoc assumption $A_0$=0, where $A_0$ is the common trilinear soft
breaking parameter, employed in most of the existing analyses is relaxed. Since
this region corresponds to relatively light squarks and gluinos which are
likely to be probed extensively in the very early stages of the LHC
experiments, the consequences of moderate or large negative values of $A_0$ are
examined in detail. We find that in this region several processes including
lightest supersymmetric particle (LSP) pair annihilation, LSP - lighter tau
slepton (${\tilde \tau}_1$) coannihilation and LSP - lighter top squark
(${\tilde t}_1$) coannihilation contribute to the observed dark matter relic
density. %\sout{The possibility that a relic density producing ${\tilde t}_1$
can be %observed at the current experiments at the Tevatron is wide open.} The
possibility that a ${\tilde t}_1$ that can participate in coannihilation with
the lightest neutralino to satisfy the WMAP bound on relic density and at the
same time be observed at the current experiments at the Tevatron is wide open.
At the LHC a large number of squark - gluino events lead to a very distinctive
semi-inclusive signature $\tau^\pm$+X$_\tau$ (anything without a tau lepton)
with a characteristic size much larger than $e^\pm$+X$_e$ or $\mu^\pm$+X$_\mu$
events.

For Wireless Personal Area Network (WPAN) systems, resource allocation
between several users within a piconet and the coexistence of several piconets
are very important points to take into consideration for the optimization of
high data rate Ultra Wide Band (UWB) systems. To improve the performance of the
Multi-Band OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplex) solution proposed by
the Multi-Band OFDM Alliance (MBOA), the addition of a spreading component in
the frequency domain is a good solution since it makes resource allocation
easier and also offers better robustness against channel frequency selectivity
and narrowband interference. The Spread Spectrum - Multi-Carrier - Multiple
Access (SS-MC-MA) system proposed in this article offers not only the
advantages of Multi-Carrier - Coded Division Multiple Access (MC-CDMA) brought
by frequency spreading, but also a more effective dynamic resource allocation
in a multi-user and multi-piconet context. These improvements are obtained
without increasing the complexity of the radio-frequency part compared to the
classical MBOA solution.

We investigate the behavior of ultracold bosons in optical lattices with a
disorder potential generated via a secondary species frozen in random
configurations. The statistics of disorder is associated with the physical
state in which the secondary species is prepared. The resulting random
potential, albeit displaying algebraic correlations, is found to lead to
localization of all single-particle states. We then investigate the real-time
dynamics of localization for a hardcore gas of mobile bosons which are brought
into sudden interaction with the random potential. Regardless of their initial
state and for any disorder strength, the mobile particles are found to reach a
steady state characterized by exponentially decaying off-diagonal correlations
and by the absence of quasi-condensation; when the mobile particles are
initially confined in a tight trap and then released in the disorder potential,
their expansion is stopped and the steady state is exponentially localized in
real space, clearly revealing Anderson localization.

In this study, we investigate the gravitational wave signals emitted from a chaotic system comprising a point mass orbited by a disk of matter. Our goal is to explore the dynamics of this system and understand the effects of its chaotic behavior on the gravitational waves it generates. We utilize numerical simulations and analyze the resulting waveforms to study the effects of the disk's mass and size on the emitted signals.

Our motivation for this research stems from the importance of understanding chaotic systems in astrophysics, as they are ubiquitous in our universe. By studying the gravitational wave signals generated by chaotic systems, we can gain insights into the nature of these systems and the dynamics they exhibit. In particular, our study sheds light on the differences between the gravitational wave signals generated by chaotic and non-chaotic systems.

Our results indicate that the gravitational wave signals emitted from the chaotic system studied here are distinct from those generated by non-chaotic systems. We observe the presence of high-frequency oscillations in the signal due to the disk's chaotic motion, which is absent in the non-chaotic system. Furthermore, we find that both the mass and size of the disk are crucial in determining the structure and properties of the emitted signals.

In conclusion, our study demonstrates the unique characteristics of gravitational wave signals generated by chaotic systems and underscores the importance of studying such systems in astrophysics.

Traditionally, it is understood that fluctuations in the equilibrium
distribution are not evident in thermodynamic systems of large $N$ (the number
of particles in the system) \cite{Huang1}. In this paper we examine the
validity of this perception by investigating whether such fluctuations can in
reality depend on temperature.
  Firstly, we describe fluctuations in the occupation numbers of the energy
levels for an isolated system, using previously unknown identities that we have
derived for the purpose, which allow us to calculate the moments of the
occupation numbers. Then we compute analytically the probability distribution
of these fluctuations. We show that, for every system of fixed and finite $N$,
fluctuations about the equilibrium distribution do, in fact, depend on the
temperature. Indeed, at higher temperatures the fluctuations can be so large
that the system does not fully converge on the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
but actually fluctuates around it. We term this state, where not one macrostate
but a region of macrostates closely fit the underlying distribution, a ``{\it
fluctuating equilibrium}''. Finally, we speculate on how this finding is
applicable to networks, financial markets, and other thermodynamic-like
systems.

Mass loss rate formulae are derived from observations or from suites of
models. For theoretical models, the following have all been identified as
factors greatly influencing the atmospheric structure and mass loss rates:
Pulsation with piston amplitude scaling appropriately with stellar L; dust
nucleation and growth, with radiation pressure and grain-gas interactions and
appropriate dependence on temperature and density; non-grey opacity with at
least 51 frequency samples; non-LTE and departures from radiative equilibrium
in the compressed and expanding flows; and non-equilibrium processes affecting
the composition (grain formation; molecular chemistry). No one set of models
yet includes all the factors known to be important. In fact, it is very
difficult to construct a model that can simultaneously include these factors
and be useful for computing spectra. Therefore, although theoretical model
grids are needed to separate the effects of M,L,R and/or $T_{\mathrm{eff}}$ or
Z on the mass loss rates, these models must be carefully checked against
observations. Getting the right order of magnitude for the mass loss rate is
only the first step in such a comparison, and is not sufficient to determine
whether the mass loss formula is correct. However, there are observables that
do test the validity of mass loss formulae as they depend directly on $d\log
\dot M/d\log L$, $d\log \dot M/d\log R$, or $d\log \dot M/d\log P$.

This paper details the use of the IT Infrastructure Library Framework (ITIL)
for optimising process workflows in the IT Service Centre of Harz University in
Wernigerode, Germany, exemplified by the Release Management Process. It is
described, how, during the course of a special ITIL project, the As-Is-Status
of the various original processes was documented as part of the process life
cycle and then transformed in the To-Be-Status, according to the ITIL Best
Practice Framework. It is also shown, how the ITIL framework fits into the
four-layered-process model, that could be derived from interviews with the
universities IT support staff, and how the various modified processes
interconnect with each other to form a value chain. The paper highlights the
final results of the project and gives an outlook on the future use of ITIL as
a business modelling tool in the IT Service Centre of Harz University. It is
currently being considered, whether the process model developed during the
project could be used as a reference model for other university IT centres.

We study Gaussian valence bond states of continuous variable systems,
obtained as the outputs of projection operations from an ancillary space of M
infinitely entangled bonds connecting neighboring sites, applied at each of $N$
sites of an harmonic chain. The entanglement distribution in Gaussian valence
bond states can be controlled by varying the input amount of entanglement
engineered in a (2M+1)-mode Gaussian state known as the building block, which
is isomorphic to the projector applied at a given site. We show how this
mechanism can be interpreted in terms of multiple entanglement swapping from
the chain of ancillary bonds, through the building blocks. We provide optical
schemes to produce bisymmetric three-mode Gaussian building blocks (which
correspond to a single bond, M=1), and study the entanglement structure in the
output Gaussian valence bond states. The usefulness of such states for quantum
communication protocols with continuous variables, like telecloning and
teleportation networks, is finally discussed.

Hall drift, i. e., transport of magnetic flux by the moving electrons giving
rise to the electrical current, may be the dominant effect causing the
evolution of the magnetic field in the solid crust of neutron stars. It is a
nonlinear process that, despite a number of efforts, is still not fully
understood. We use the Hall induction equation in axial symmetry to obtain some
general properties of nonevolving fields, as well as analyzing the evolution of
purely toroidal fields, their poloidal perturbations, and current-free, purely
poloidal fields. We also analyze energy conservation in Hall instabilities and
write down a variational principle for Hall equilibria. We show that the
evolution of any toroidal magnetic field can be described by Burgers' equation,
as previously found in plane-parallel geometry. It leads to sharp current
sheets that dissipate on the Hall time scale, yielding a stationary field
configuration that depends on a single, suitably defined coordinate. This
field, however, is unstable to poloidal perturbations, which grow as their
field lines are stretched by the background electron flow, as in instabilities
earlier found numerically. On the other hand, current-free poloidal
configurations are stable and could represent a long-lived crustal field
supported by currents in the fluid stellar core.

Elaborating on previous work (hep-th/0605211, hep-th/0611247), we show how
the linear and nonlinear chiral multiplets of N=4 supersymmetric mechanics with
the off-shell content (2,4,2) can be obtained by gauging three distinct
two-parameter isometries of the ``root'' (4,4,0) multiplet actions. In
particular, two different gauge groups, one abelian and one non-abelian, lead,
albeit in a disguised form in the second case, to the same (unique) nonlinear
chiral multiplet. This provides an evidence that no other nonlinear chiral N=4
multiplets exist. General sigma model type actions are discussed, together with
the restricted potential terms coming from the Fayet-Iliopoulos terms
associated with abelian gauge superfields. As in our previous work, we use the
manifestly supersymmetric language of N=4, d=1 harmonic superspace. A novel
point is the necessity to use in parallel the \lambda and \tau gauge frames,
with the ``bridges'' between these two frames playing a crucial role. It is the
N=4 harmonic analyticity which, though being non-manifest in the \tau frame,
gives rise to both linear and nonlinear chirality constraints.

We study gravitational waves from a particle moving around a system of a
point mass with a disk in Newtonian gravitational theory. A particle motion in
this system can be chaotic when the gravitational contribution from a surface
density of a disk is comparable with that from a point mass. In such an orbit,
we sometimes find that there appears a phase of the orbit in which particle
motion becomes to be nearly regular (the so-called ``stagnant motion'') for a
finite time interval between more strongly chaotic phases. To study how these
different chaotic behaviours affect on observation of gravitational waves, we
investigate a correlation of the particle motion and the waves. We find that
such a difference in chaotic motions reflects on the wave forms and energy
spectra. The character of the waves in the stagnant motion is quite different
from that either in a regular motion or in a more strongly chaotic motion. This
suggests that we may make a distinction between different chaotic behaviours of
the orbit via the gravitational waves.

In this study, we present the results of a panchromatic analysis of the globular cluster NGC 1904. This cluster is located in the halo of our Milky Way galaxy and is known for its rich population of blue straggler stars (BSSs). The motivation for this research is to shed light on the formation and evolution mechanisms of the BSSs population, which has long been a topic of debate in the field.

Our analysis takes advantage of photometric and spectroscopic data obtained from various telescopes, including Hubble Space Telescope and Magellan telescopes. By combining these multi-wavelength observations, we derived the BSSs' properties, including their ages and masses, and compared them to those of the cluster's ordinary main-sequence stars. We found that the BSSs in NGC 1904 are significantly younger and more massive than the typical stars in the cluster.

Furthermore, our results suggest that the BSSs in NGC 1904 may have formed through a different mechanism than those in other clusters, such as mass transfer or collision-induced mergers. We propose that the BSSs in NGC 1904 may have formed through dynamical interactions with binary stars.

Overall, our study presents new insights into the formation and evolution mechanisms of the BSSs in NGC 1904, which can contribute to a better understanding of the globular cluster formation and evolution processes.

We evaluate the doping dependence of the quasiparticle current and low
temperature superfluid density in two slave-particle theories of the tt't''J
model -- the slave-boson theory and doped-carrier theory. In the slave-boson
theory, the nodal quasiparticle current renormalization factor $\alpha$
vanishes proportionally to the zero temperature superfluid density $\rho_S(0)$;
however, we find that away from the $\rho_S(0) \to 0$ limit $\alpha$ displays a
much weaker doping dependence than $\rho_S(0)$. A similar conclusion applies to
the doped-carrier theory, which differentiates the nodal and antinodal regions
of momentum space. Due to its momentum space anisotropy, the doped-carrier
theory enhances the value of $\alpha$ in the hole doped regime, bringing it to
quantitative agreement with experiments, and reproduces the asymmetry between
hole and electron doped cuprate superconductors. Finally, we use the
doped-carrier theory to predict a specific experimental signature of local
staggered spin correlations in doped Mott insulator superconductors which, we
propose, should be observed in STM measurements of underdoped high-Tc
compounds. This experimental signature distinguishes the doped-carrier theory
from other candidate mean-field theories of high-Tc superconductors, like the
slave-boson theory and the conventional BCS theory.

The behavior of the angular velocity of a test particle moving in a stable
circular orbit in the vacuum on the brane is considered. In the brane world
scenario, the four dimensional effective Einstein equation acquire extra terms,
called dark radiation and dark pressure, respectively, which arise from the
embedding of the 3-brane in the bulk. A large number of independent
observations have shown that the rotational velocities of test particles
gravitating around galaxies tend, as a function of the distance from the
galactic center, toward constant values. By assuming a constant tangential
velocity, the general solution of the vacuum gravitational field equations on
the brane can be obtained in an exact analytic form. This allows us to obtain
the explicit form of the projections of the bulk Weyl tensor on the brane, and
the equation of state of the dark pressure as a function of the dark radiation.
The physical and geometrical quantities are expressed in terms of
observable/measurable parameters, like the tangential velocity, the baryonic
mass and the radius of the galaxy. We also analyze the dynamics of test
particles by using methods from the qualitative analysis of dynamical systems,
by assuming a simple linear equation of state for the dark pressure. The
obtained results provide a theoretical framework for the observational testing
at the extra-galactic scale of the predictions of the brane world models.

In this paper, we present WHAM, a high-order, General Relativistic (GR) numerical scheme for solving hydrodynamical problems. Our scheme utilizes the Weighted Essentially Non-Oscillatory (WENO) technique, which allows for sharp interfaces and high accuracy. We also use a modified version of the moving puncture method in order to evolve the GR field equations. 

The main objective of our work is to solve hydrodynamic problems in a GR setting, which is crucial for a number of important astrophysical scenarios such as compact binary mergers and supernovae explosions. These calculations require a high level of accuracy, since small errors can lead to significant changes in the outcome of the system. 

To test the accuracy and usefulness of WHAM, we perform simulations of a number of standard hydrodynamic test problems. Our results show that our scheme maintains both high accuracy and stability, even under conditions of strong shocks and gravitational waves. We also compare our results to other numerical techniques in the same settings, and show that our method outperforms them in both accuracy and stability. 

Overall, we believe that WHAM is a valuable new tool in the field of computational astrophysics. Future work includes upgrading the scheme to include magnetohydrodynamics and other more complex systems.

In this work, we present a study of neutral hydrogen absorption toward XTE J1810-197, a radio-emitting magnetar located in the Galactic plane. The aim of this research is to determine the distance to the source using the absorption features seen in the spectra. We have used the Green Bank Telescope to obtain high-resolution and high-sensitivity absorption spectra at 21-cm wavelength.

Our analysis shows that the absorption features are consistent with cold gas in the foreground of XTE J1810-197. Based on the properties of the absorbing gas, and by comparing our data with previous studies, we constrain the distance to the source to be 3.5 ± 0.2 kpc.

The distance measurement is crucial for studying the physical properties of XTE J1810-197, such as its magnetic field and age. One of our key findings is that the absorption features suggest that the magnetar is located beyond the Carina-Sagittarius spiral arm, which is in agreement with previous studies.

Our methodology involves a careful analysis of the spectral data, including modeling of the continuum emission and fitting of the absorption features. This study highlights the importance of neutral hydrogen absorption as a tool for Galactic distance measurements and provides a valuable constraint for understanding the properties of radio-emitting magnetars.

In our work entitled "Space-time percolation," we aim to investigate the interplay between space and time in percolation models, where the connectivity of a network is essential. The key idea is to incorporate time into the traditional percolation framework and explore whether the addition of time enhances or reduces the connectivity of the network. Such investigations have significant implications for the spread of information, disease, and other phenomena that depend on network connectivity. 

We start by setting up the space-time percolation model, where sites in space-time form nodes, and edges connect nodes that are adjacent in either space or time. We then define and study essential quantities, such as the critical probability, percolation probability, and the size of the giant component. Our primary methodology is a combination of analytical and numerical tools, such as the renormalization group analysis and Monte Carlo simulations. 

The motivation for our research stems from the theoretical and practical significance of understanding space-time percolation. It provides insights into the behavior of complex systems and helps us design efficient algorithms for network-based applications. Indeed, our work can be applied to various disciplines, ranging from physics and mathematics to computer science and epidemiology.

In this work, we present an analytic approach to the thermal Casimir force between metal and dielectric surfaces. The Casimir force is a quantum mechanical effect that arises due to fluctuations of the electromagnetic field. The force is of particular interest due to its potential for applications in nanotechnology.

The motivation for our research stems from the fact that existing models for calculating the Casimir force require sophisticated numerical techniques, which can hinder their applicability in certain situations. Hence, we aim to develop an analytic approach that can be easily extended to different geometries and materials.

Our methodology involves the use of the mean-field approximation to calculate the correlation function of the electromagnetic field. We then use this correlation function to calculate the Casimir force between the metal and dielectric surfaces.

Our work attempts to solve the problem of accurately calculating the thermal Casimir force between metal and dielectric surfaces, while also providing an analytic approach that can be extended to different geometries and materials. Our approach provides an alternative to existing numerical techniques and can help improve the accuracy and applicability of Casimir force calculations in nanotechnology.

Our study explores the surface structure in an accretion disk annulus with comparable radiation and gas pressure. The motivation behind this research is to better understand the properties of accretion disks around compact objects, such as black holes and neutron stars, where the dynamics of the gas and radiation are critically important.

The key concept of our work is to explain how gas pressure and radiation act together to create the surface structure in accretion disks. We used a numerical method to model accretion disks with different levels of radiation and gas pressure. The results showed that the ratio between gas pressure and radiation energy density has a significant impact on the disk's surface structure. In particular, we found that the disk height is higher when radiation and gas pressure are comparable, compared to when radiation dominates.

Our findings suggest that the morphology of accretion disks is highly sensitive to the relative contributions of radiation and gas pressure. Our study provides a framework for understanding the critical factors that govern the disk’s structure and should help facilitate better interpretations of observational data. Further work is needed to analyze the complex dynamical structures in these types of systems, including the effects of magnetic fields and turbulence.

We introduce a density tensor hierarchy for open system dynamics, that
recovers information about fluctuations lost in passing to the reduced density
matrix. For the case of fluctuations arising from a classical probability
distribution, the hierarchy is formed from expectations of products of pure
state density matrix elements, and can be compactly summarized by a simple
generating function. For the case of quantum fluctuations arising when a
quantum system interacts with a quantum environment in an overall pure state,
the corresponding hierarchy is defined as the environmental trace of products
of system matrix elements of the full density matrix. Only the lowest member of
the quantum noise hierarchy is directly experimentally measurable. The unit
trace and idempotence properties of the pure state density matrix imply descent
relations for the tensor hierarchies, that relate the order $n$ tensor, under
contraction of appropriate pairs of tensor indices, to the order $n-1$ tensor.
As examples to illustrate the classical probability distribution formalism, we
consider a quantum system evolving by It\^o stochastic and by jump process
Schr\"odinger equations. As examples to illustrate the corresponding trace
formalism in the quantum fluctuation case, we consider collisional Brownian
motion of an infinite mass Brownian particle, and the weak coupling Born-Markov
master equation. In different specializations, the latter gives the hierarchies
generalizing the quantum optical master equation and the Caldeira--Leggett
master equation. As a further application of the density tensor, we contrast
stochastic Schr\"odinger equations that reduce and that do not reduce the state
vector, and discuss why a quantum system coupled to a quantum environment
behaves like the latter.

Ad-hoc networks, a promising trend in wireless technology, fail to work
properly in a global setting. In most cases, self-organization and cost-free
local communication cannot compensate the need for being connected, gathering
urgent information just-in-time. Equipping mobile devices additionally with GSM
or UMTS adapters in order to communicate with arbitrary remote devices or even
a fixed network infrastructure provides an opportunity. Devices that operate as
intermediate nodes between the ad-hoc network and a reliable backbone network
are potential injection points. They allow disseminating received information
within the local neighborhood. The effectiveness of different devices to serve
as injection point differs substantially. For practical reasons the
determination of injection points should be done locally, within the ad-hoc
network partitions. We analyze different localized algorithms using at most
2-hop neighboring information. Results show that devices selected this way
spread information more efficiently through the ad-hoc network. Our results can
also be applied in order to support the election process for clusterheads in
the field of clustering mechanisms.

In this work, we present a detailed study of the radio afterglow from GRB 030329, a gamma-ray burst event occurring in 2003. The main aim of our research is to investigate the non-relativistic phase of the afterglow emission, which occurs after the initial relativistic blast wave. Previous studies have mainly focused on the relativistic phase, neglecting the non-relativistic phase, which is crucial in understanding the physics of these events.

Our motivation for this research is to shed light on the mechanism of the afterglow emission, which is still not fully understood. By studying the non-relativistic phase, we hope to contribute to the understanding of the interaction between the ejecta and the circumburst medium. We performed radio observations using the Very Large Array (VLA) at multiple frequencies, which enabled us to study the temporal and spectral evolution of the afterglow emission.

Our results reveal important insights into the physics of the afterglow emission, such as evidence of inverse Compton scattering, which indicates the presence of relativistic electrons. Furthermore, we observe the evolution of the radio spectral index, which is consistent with the standard afterglow model. Our findings suggest that the non-relativistic phase plays a crucial role in shaping the afterglow emission, and thereby aids in understanding the physical processes behind gamma-ray bursts.

In this work, we investigate the valley-dependent optoelectronics originating from inversion symmetry breaking in two-dimensional materials. The problem we aim to address is the lack of understanding of charge and spin dynamics in such materials. The motivation for this research stems from the potential application of valleytronics in quantum computing and communication technologies.

Our approach involves designing models for two-dimensional materials with broken inversion symmetry, calculating the electronic band structures, and analyzing the valley-dependent optical and electronic properties of the materials. We explore the impact of various perturbations, such as strain, electric field, and magnetic field, on the valley polarization and spin lifetime of the materials.

Our findings indicate that valley polarization and spin lifetime can be manipulated in the presence of these perturbations. We observe that the valley polarization is highly dependent on the polarization direction of the incident light and can be efficiently tuned by external factors. We also note that the spin lifetime is sensitive to the scattering mechanisms in the materials and can be greatly extended under proper conditions.

Our work enhances our understanding of the fundamental properties of two-dimensional materials and opens up exciting opportunities for future applications in valleytronics. We anticipate that our findings will inspire further exploration in this field and contribute to the advancement of technological innovation.
