The main motivation for this research is to improve our understanding of the fundamental interactions between particles and to provide more accurate predictions for experiments involving muon decay. Previous parameterizations have often failed to fully capture the complexity of the decay process, leading to uncertainties and inaccuracies in experimental data.

To address this problem, we developed a new parameterization that takes into account the effects of polarization and angular distribution on the type of neutrino emitted during muon decay. We also integrated the latest experimental data into our calculations to ensure that our parameterization is as accurate as possible.

Our new parameterization has the potential to improve the precision of experimental measurements of muon decay, which in turn can lead to new insights into the fundamental interactions of particles. Furthermore, it provides a new tool for theoretical physicists to study the properties of neutrinos and the weak interaction, contributing to the ongoing efforts to understand the fundamental nature of the universe.

In the classical one-dimensional solution of fluid dynamics equations all
unknown functions depend only on time t and Cartesian coordinate x. Although
fluid spreads in all directions (velocity vector has three components) the
whole picture of motion is relatively simple: trajectory of one fluid particle
from plane x=const completely determines motion of the whole plane. Basing on
the symmetry analysis of differential equations we propose generalization of
this solution allowing movements in different directions of fluid particles
belonging to plane x=const. At that, all functions but an angle determining the
direction of particle's motion depend on t and x only, whereas the angle
depends on all coordinates. In this solution the whole picture of motion
superposes from identical trajectories placed under different angles in 3D
space. Orientations of the trajectories are restricted by a finite relation
possessing functional arbitrariness. The solution describes three-dimensional
nonlinear processes and singularities in infinitely conducting plasma, gas or
incompressible liquid.

In this paper, we provide a brief introduction to open access publishing in particle physics for those who are unfamiliar with the subject. This is important because particle physics is a rapidly evolving discipline that is producing increasingly complex research results, and it is vital that these results are accessible to the broader scientific community and the public. However, traditional journals have put up barriers to access through high subscription fees and author publishing charges, which can limit the dissemination of information.

We argue that open access publishing is the solution to this problem, and we discuss the benefits and challenges of this approach. We highlight the key concepts of open access publishing, such as Creative Commons licenses and preprint servers, and we provide examples of successful open access journals in particle physics.

Our results show that open access publishing is gaining popularity in the field of particle physics, with more journals adopting this model. We conclude that open access publishing has the potential to revolutionize the way scientific research is disseminated and that it is crucial for the advancement of particle physics and other scientific disciplines. We call upon all scientists and stakeholders to embrace open access publishing and work towards a more equitable and accessible scientific community.

There exist many observational evidences implying the expansion of our
universe is undergoing a late-time acceleration, the mechanism of this
acceleration is yet unknown. In the so-called thick brane model this phenomena
is attributed to the thickness of the brane along the extra dimension. In this
study we mainly rely to the consistency of this model with most recent
observational data related to the background evolution. The new Supernova Type
Ia (SNIa) Gold sample and Supernova Legacy Survey (SNLS) data, the position of
the acoustic peak at the last scattering surface from the WMAP observations and
the baryon acoustic oscillation peak found in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey
(SDSS) are used to constrain the free parameter of the thick codimension 1
brane model. To infer its consistency with age of our universe, we compare the
age of old cosmological objects with what computed using the best fit values
for the model parameters. When the universe is matter dominated, $w=0$, at 68%
level of confidence, the combination of Gold sample SNIa, CMB shift parameter
and SDSS databases provides $\Omega_m=0.31_{-0.02}^{+0.02}$,
$\Omega_{\cal{C}}=0.05_{-0.01}^{+0.01}$, $w_r=-1.40_{-0.20}^{+0.20}$, hence a
spatially open universe with $\Omega_k=0.21_{-0.08}^{+0.08}$. The same
combination with SNLS supernova observation gives
$\Omega_m=0.28_{-0.02}^{+0.03}$, $\Omega_{\cal{C}}=0.037_{-0.004}^{+0.003}$,
$w_r=-2.05_{-0.15}^{+0.15}$ consequently provides a spatially open universe
$\Omega_k=0.11_{-0.07}^{+0.10}$. These results obviously seem to be in
contradiction with the most recent WMAP results indicating a flat universe.

In our work titled "Feedback Heating by Cosmic Rays in Clusters of Galaxies", we explore the role of cosmic rays in heating the intracluster medium (ICM) in galaxy clusters. Galaxy clusters are the largest gravitationally-bound structures in the universe and they contain ICM, which is a hot and diffuse gas. The prevailing view is that feedback from active galactic nuclei (AGN) is the primary source of heating for the ICM. However, recent observations have revealed the presence of cosmic rays in galaxy clusters, and it is possible that they also contribute to heating the ICM.

The key idea behind our work is to investigate how cosmic rays can heat the ICM and how it compares to heating from AGN feedback. We construct a series of models that include different sources and efficiencies of cosmic ray heating, and compare them to models with AGN feedback alone. Our methodology involves using numerical simulations of the evolution of galaxy clusters, including the production and propagation of cosmic rays, and the effects of their interaction with the ICM.

The motivation for our research is to provide a better understanding of the heating processes in galaxy clusters, which can impact their observable properties such as their X-ray emission and gas content. Furthermore, this can also shed light on the origin and evolution of cosmic ray populations in the universe.

Overall, our work attempts to solve the problem of understanding the complex interplay between different heating sources in galaxy clusters, and the relative contribution of cosmic rays to the energy budget of the ICM. Our findings have implications for a wide range of astrophysical phenomena, from galaxy evolution to the large-scale structure of the universe.

We have carried out a survey toward the central regions of 85 starless cores
in HCN J = 1-0 to study inward motions in the cores. Sixty-four cores were
detected with HCN lines. The infall asymmetry in the HCN spectra is found to be
more prevalent, and more prominent than in any other previously used infall
tracers such as CS J = 2-1, DCO+ J = 2-1, and N2H+ J = 1-0. We found close
relation between the intensities of the HCN and N2H+ lines. This implies that
the HCN is not much depleted in the central regions of the cores. In some
cores, the HCN spectra show different sign of asymmetry from other molecular
lines. A few cores show various signs of asymmetry in individual HCN hyperfine
lines. The distribution of the velocity shift dV of the HCN profiles with
respect to the systemic velocity of the optically thin tracer is found to be
more shifted toward bluer side than those of other infall tracers, indicating
that the HCN traces inward motions more frequently. The dV distribution of each
HCN hyperfine line for all sources is similar. Moreover the dV values obtained
from different HCN hyperfine lines for each source are nearly similar. These
may mean that most of starless cores are in similar kinematic states across the
layers of the cores. We identify 17 infall candidates using all available
indicators such as the velocity shift dV and the blue to red peak intensity
ratio of double peaked profiles for HCN J = 1-0, CS J = 2-1, J = 3-2, DCO+ J =
2-1, and N2H+ J = 1-0. Four of them, L63, L492, L694-2, and L1197 are found to
show higher blue to red ratio in the HCN hyperfine line along the lower
opacity, suggesting that infall speed becomes higher toward the center.

In this paper, we investigate the phenomenon of higher order antibunching in intermediate states. This research aims to address the need for a deeper understanding of non-classical correlations in quantum systems. In particular, we explore the behavior of high-order coherence functions in intermediate states, which are states that are created by manipulating the initial and final states of a quantum system.

Our key idea is to use second-order correlation measurements to explore the higher-order correlations in intermediate states. We develop a theoretical framework to investigate the properties of these states and propose an experimental setup to measure them. To demonstrate our approach, we apply it to a three-level system, a model of an atom with two excited states.

Our results show that intermediate states can exhibit higher-order antibunching, which is a more intricate form of non-classicality than standard antibunching. We find that, in intermediate states, photons are less likely to be emitted in clusters of three or more, demonstrating that the intermediate states exhibit a genuinely higher degree of non-classicality compared to the initial and final states.

Overall, this work contributes to a better understanding of the non-classical behavior of quantum systems and opens up new avenues for exploring intermediate states in a variety of other systems.

In this work, we explore the properties of algebraically closed real geodesics on n-dimensional ellipsoids and their relation to hyperelliptic tangential coverings. Our main objective is to establish the density of these geodesics in the parameter space of n-dimensional ellipsoids. To achieve this, we use an algebraic-geometric approach based on the Riemann-Hurwitz formula and Weierstrass representation theory. Our methodology involves analyzing the structure of the moduli space of algebraically closed real geodesics and constructing an explicit map between this space and the moduli space of hyperelliptic tangential coverings. We prove that this map is bijective, which allows us to transfer our results from one space to the other.

Our results show that the algebraically closed real geodesics on n-dimensional ellipsoids are dense in the parameter space, which has important implications for the study of geometric structures and dynamics in these spaces. Moreover, we establish a connection between these geodesics and hyperelliptic tangential coverings, which provides new tools for analyzing the geometry and topology of n-dimensional ellipsoids. In summary, our work sheds light on the intricate interplay between algebraic and geometric structures in higher-dimensional spaces and opens up new avenues for further research in this area.

A small quantum scattering system (the microsystem) is studied in interaction
with a large system (the macrosystem) described by unknown stochastic
variables. The interaction between the two systems is diagonal for the
microsystem in a certain orthonormal basis, and the interaction gives an
imprint on the macrosystem. Moreover, the interaction is assumed to involve
only small transfers of energy and momentum between the two systems (as
compared to typical energies/momenta within the microsystem). The analysis is
carried out within scattering theory. Calculated in the conventional way, the
transition amplitude for the whole system factorizes. The interaction taking
place within the macrosystem is assumed to depend on the stochastic variables
in such a way that, on the average, no particular basis vector state of the
microsystem is favoured. The density matrix is studied in a formalism which
includes generation of the ingoing state and absorption of the final state.
Then the dependence of the final state on the conventional scattering amplitude
for the microsystem is highly non-linear.
  In the thermodynamic limit of the macrosystem, the density matrix of the
ensemble (of microsystem plus macrosystem) develops into a final state which
involves a set of macroscopically distinguishable states, each with the
microsystem in one of the basis vector states and the macrosystem in an
entangled state.
  For an element of the ensemble, i.e., for a single measurement, the result is
instead a random walk, where the microsystem ends up in one of the basis vector
states (reduction of the wave packet).

In this work, we explore the evolution of dust in the early universe and its implications for the galaxy SDSS J1148+5251. The presence of dust in the early universe presents a significant challenge to our understanding of the origin and evolution of galaxies. Furthermore, the high redshift of the galaxy SDSS J1148+5251 makes it an ideal testbed for studying the early universe. 

Our work aims to address the problem of understanding the evolution of dust in the early universe and its impact on the formation of galaxies. To this end, we utilize a combination of theoretical models and observational data to investigate the role of dust in the formation and evolution of SDSS J1148+5251. 

We develop a theoretical model that incorporates the effects of grain growth, grain destruction, and gas-phase metallicity on the evolution of dust in the early universe. We then compare our model predictions to observations of SDSS J1148+5251, including its spectral energy distribution and metallicity measurements. 

Overall, our results suggest that the evolution of dust in the early universe played a critical role in the formation and evolution of galaxies like SDSS J1148+5251. Our work highlights the need for more comprehensive theoretical models that incorporate the complex interplay between dust and gas-phase metallicity in the early universe.

In this work, we investigate the relationship between ageing memory and glassiness in a driven vortex system. Using numerical simulations, we demonstrate that the vortex system exhibits a glassy phase in the presence of quenched disorder. Furthermore, we show that the system displays a non-equilibrium ageing memory effect characteristic of glassy systems.

Our investigation aims to address the fundamental question of how glassiness arises in driven systems. We focus on the vortex system as it offers a highly tunable experimental platform for probing the interplay between disorder, driving, and ageing memory. Understanding this interplay has significant implications for the prediction and control of physical systems ranging from colloidal suspensions to granular materials and glasses.

Our methodology involves a series of numerical simulations, where we model the driven vortex system as a set of interacting particles subject to external driving and quenched disorder. We analyze the dynamics of the resulting glassy phase and ageing memory effect using tools from statistical physics, such as relaxation and correlation function analysis.

Overall, our results shed light on the mechanisms underlying the formation of glassy phases in driven systems and provide insights into the nature of non-equilibrium ageing memory effects. Our findings contribute to the wider research effort aimed at understanding the fundamental properties of complex systems.

In this study, we investigated the $p d \to p d \eta$ reaction by analyzing data from the WASA detector at COSY, Forschungszentrum Jülich. The aim of this research was to contribute to a better understanding of the properties of the $\eta$ particle and its interactions with nucleons, as well as to provide information for testing theoretical models.

We found that the differential cross-sections of the reaction were consistent with previous measurements and theoretical predictions, although with some differences in the angular distributions. We also observed evidence of partial-wave interference effects in the data, which would require further analysis and study.

The $p d \to p d \eta$ reaction is of interest because it provides a way to study the $\eta$ meson in a nuclear environment, which can affect its properties due to modifications of the hadronic medium. Additionally, the reaction can be used to study the isospin dependence of nucleon-eta interactions and the role of baryon resonances in the $\eta$ production mechanism.

In conclusion, our study contributed to the understanding of the $p d \to p d \eta$ reaction and the properties of the $\eta$ meson. Future work could investigate different aspects of the reaction, such as the analysis of other observables or the study of related reactions with heavier nuclei.

It has been shown based on radio variability arguments that some BALQSOs
(broad absorption line quasars) are viewed along the polar axis (orthogonal to
accretion disk) in the recent article of Zhou et al. These arguments are based
on the brightness temperature, $T_{b}$ exceeding $10^{12} ^{\circ}$ K which
leads to the well-known inverse Compton catastrophe unless the radio jet is
relativistic and is viewed along its axis. In this letter, we expand the Zhou
et al sample of polar BALQSOs to the entire SDSS DR5. In the process, we
clarify a mistake in their calculation of brightness temperature. The expanded
sample of high $T_{b}$ BALQSOS, has an inordinately large fraction of LoBALQSOs
(low ionization BALQSOs). We consider this an important clue to understanding
the nature of the polar BALQSOs. This is expected in the polar BALQSO
analytical/numerical models of Punsly in which LoBALQSOs occur when the line of
sight is very close to the polar axis, where the outflow density is the
highest.

We present Gemini optical spectroscopy of 23 young star clusters in NGC3256.
We find that the cluster ages range are from few Myr to ~150 Myr. All these
clusters are relatively massive (2--40)x 10^{5} \msun$ and appear to be of
roughly 1.5 \zo metallicity. The majority of the clusters in our sample follow
the same rotation curve as the gas and hence were presumably formed in the
molecular-gas disk. However, a western subsample of five clusters has
velocities that deviate significantly from the gas rotation curve. These
clusters may either belong to the second spiral galaxy of the merger or may
have formed in tidal-tail gas falling back into the system. We discuss our
observations in light of other known cluster populations in merging galaxies,
and suggest that NGC 3256 is similar to Arp 220, and hence may become an
Ultra-luminous Infrared Galaxy as the merger progresses and the star-formation
rate increases.
  Some of the clusters which appeared as isolated in our ground-based images
are clearly resolved into multiple sub-components in the HST-ACS images. The
same effect has been observed in the Antennae galaxies, showing that clusters
are often not formed in isolation, but instead tend to form in larger groups or
cluster complexes.

We present optical and near-infrared light curves of SN 2001el from 310 to
445 days past maximum light, obtained with the Very Large Telescope. The
late-time optical (UBVRI) light curves decay in a nearly linear fashion with
decay time scales of 1.43\pm0.14, 1.43\pm0.06, 1.48\pm0.06, 1.45\pm0.07, and
1.03\pm0.07 magnitudes (per hundred days) in the U, B, V, R and I bands,
respectively. In contrast, in the near-infrared (JHKs) bands the time evolution
of the flux appears to be nearly constant at these epochs. We measure decline
rates (per hundred days) of 0.19\pm0.10 and 0.17\pm0.11 magnitudes in the J and
H bands, respectively. We construct a UVOIR light curve, and find that the
late-time luminosity has a decay time scale nearly consistent with complete
depostion of positron kinetic energy. The late-time light curves of the normal
type Ia SN 2001el demonstrate the increased importance of the near-infrared
contribution. This was previously observed in the peculiar SN 2000cx, and the
results for SN 2001el thus ensure that the conclusions previously based on a
single peculiar event are applicable to normal type Ia supernovae. The measured
late-time UVOIR decline rate suggests that a majority of the positrons are
trapped within the ejecta. This results does not favor the predictions of a
weak and/or radially combed magnetic field configuration.

We study 7 Gamma Ray Bursts (GRBs), detected both by the BATSE instrument,
on-board the Compton Gamma Ray Observatory, and by the Wide Field Camera (WFC),
on-board BeppoSAX. These bursts have measured spectroscopic redshifts and are a
sizeable fraction of the bursts defining the correlation between the peak
energy E_peak (i.e. the peak of the vFv spectrum) and the total prompt
isotropic energy E_iso (the so called "Amati" relation). Recent theoretical
interpretations of this correlation assume that black-body emission dominates
the time resolved spectra of GRBs, even if, in the time integrated spectrum,
its presence may be hidden by the change of its temperature and by the dilution
of a possible non-thermal power law component. We perform a time resolved
spectral analysis, and show that the sum of a power-law and a black-body gives
acceptable fits to the time dependent spectra within the BATSE energy range,
but overpredicts the flux in the WFC X-ray range. Moreover, a fit with a cutoff
power-law plus a black-body is consistent with the WFC data, but the black-body
component contributes a negligible fraction of the total flux. On the contrary,
we find that fitting the spectra with a Band model or a simple cutoff power-law
model yields an X-ray flux and spectral slope which well matches the WFC
spectra.

Using the analytic assembly map that appears in the Baum-Connes conjecture in
noncommutative geometry, we generalise the $\Spin^c$-version of the
Guillemin-Sternberg conjecture that `quantisation commutes with reduction' to
(discrete series representations of) semisimple groups $G$ with maximal compact
subgroups $K$ acting cocompactly on symplectic manifolds. We prove this
statement in cases where the image of the momentum map in question lies in the
set of strongly elliptic elements, the set of elements of $\g^*$ with compact
stabilisers. This assumption on the image of the momentum map is equivalent to
the assumption that $M = G \times_K N$, for a compact Hamiltonian $K$-manifold
$N$. The proof comes down to a reduction to the compact case. This reduction is
based on a `quantisation commutes with induction'-principle, and involves a
notion of induction of Hamiltonian group actions. This principle, in turn, is
based on a version of the naturality of the assembly map for the inclusion of
$K$ into $G$.

In this paper, we investigate the decoherence of highly mixed macroscopic quantum superpositions, which is a crucial step towards understanding the boundary between the classical and the quantum worlds. The main idea behind our work is to analyze the behavior of entanglement and purity in a system consisting of two macroscopic objects that are initially prepared in a highly mixed quantum superposition state. We use a novel approach based on quantum trajectory theory to investigate the evolution of the system under the influence of environmental noise. Our results demonstrate that, despite the highly mixed nature of the initial state, the system exhibits rapid decoherence and loses its quantum coherence in a very short time. In particular, we find that the entanglement decays faster than the purity, indicating that entanglement is more sensitive to decoherence than purity in this system. Our work aims to shed light on the fundamental problem of how macroscopic objects can lose their quantum coherence in a noisy environment, which is one of the central challenges in the field of quantum mechanics. Our findings might have important implications for the development of quantum technologies, such as quantum computing and communication, which rely on the control of highly mixed quantum states.

We investigate non-perturbative features of a three-dimensional Abelian Higgs
model with singly- and doubly-charged scalar fields coupled to a single compact
Abelian gauge field. The model is pretending to describe various planar systems
of strongly correlated electrons such as high-Tc superconductivity in the
overdoped regime and exotic materials possessing excitations with
fractionalized quantum numbers. The complicated phase structure of the model is
studied thoroughly using numerical tools and analytical arguments. In the
three-dimensional space of coupling parameters we identify the Fermi liquid,
the spin gap, the superconductor and the strange metallic phases. The behavior
of three kinds of topological defects -- holon and spinon vortices and
monopoles - is explored in various phases. We also observe a new effect, the
strong enhancement of the phase transition strength reflected in a lower order
of the transition: at sufficiently strong gauge coupling the two second order
phase transitions -- corresponding to spinon-pair and holon condensation lines
- join partially in the phase diagram and become a first order phase transition
in that region. The last observation may have an analogue in Quantum
Chromodynamics at non-zero temperature and finite baryon density. We argue that
at sufficiently large baryon density the finite-temperature transition between
the (3-flavor paired) color superconducting phase and the quark-gluon plasma
phases should be much stronger compared with the transition between 2-flavor
paired and 3-flavor paired superconducting phases.

In our study, we attempt to solve the problem of understanding the nature of dark energy through Chandra X-ray observations of the largest relaxed galaxy clusters. The motivation for this research lies in the fact that dark energy, which is responsible for the accelerating expansion of the universe, is one of the greatest mysteries in science today. Clusters of galaxies provide the ideal laboratory to investigate the properties of dark energy as they are the largest structures in the universe.

We use Chandra X-ray observations of 42 galaxy clusters with well-understood properties and well-modeled mass distributions to constrain the properties of dark energy. Our key idea is to use X-ray measurements of cluster gas, which serves as a proxy for the total cluster mass, to constrain the cosmological parameters. We use the Bayesian approach to maximize the joint likelihood of our dataset, including information from measurements of the cosmic microwave background radiation, supernovae, and baryon acoustic oscillations.

We find that our results are consistent with the standard cosmological model and provide improved constraints on the nature of dark energy. Our methodology and results are of significant scientific importance and will help to improve our understanding of the most fundamental aspects of the universe.

In this paper, we present an almost product evaluation of Hankel determinants by employing the concepts of quasi-analytic functions. The main objective of this work is to provide a new methodology to evaluate Hankel determinants that arise in various fields such as number theory, analysis, and statistics. In particular, we aim to compute the limits of such determinants as the number of terms approaches infinity, while considering the cases where the limit exists and where it does not. 

The key idea of our method is to approximate the Hankel determinant by a sum of integrals, where each integral corresponds to a subsequence of the original sequence. We use asymptotic estimates of the integrals involving quasi-analytic functions to obtain upper and lower bounds for the determinant. Our approach requires only the knowledge of the analyticity properties of the subsequence, which can be easily derived from the generating function or by using classical results from the theory of orthogonal polynomials.

The motivation for this research stems from the fact that Hankel determinants arise frequently in different areas of mathematics and are associated with fundamental problems, such as the determination of the zeros of special functions, the study of random matrices, and the regularization of divergent series. Our method provides a powerful tool to compute Hankel determinants in a wide range of situations and can be generalized to more general sequences. We illustrate the effectiveness of our approach with several examples, including the evaluation of the Wiener-Ikehara theorem and the computation of the moment generating function of the Tracy-Widom distribution.

Our study focuses on the anticenter open cluster NGC 1883 and aims to investigate its radial velocity and metallicity. We utilized multi-object spectroscopy for 45 targets in the cluster region using the 6.5-m telescope at Xinglong Station, NAOC. Radial velocities of cluster members were obtained by cross-correlating with template stars, highlighting the high membership probability of stars with consistent radial velocities. In addition, calcium triplet line strengths were measured to determine cluster metallicity. Using a high-resolution spectroscopic template, we found that the cluster has a metallicity of [Fe/H]=-0.41 ± 0.06 dex, indicating a relatively metal-poor system.

Our results provide important contributions to the current database of open clusters in the Galactic anticenter direction. NGC 1883 is a significant target for study due to its crucial role in understanding the Galactic disk dynamics and chemical evolution. We aim to develop a more comprehensive view of the cluster's properties and identify its origin, evolutionary stage, and possible connection with nearby structures. Our research provides insights into the interplay between the Galactic evolution and the formation of open clusters. This study also sets the stage for future research on this and other Galactic open clusters.

Any homogeneous polynomial $P(x, y, z)$ of degree $d$, being restricted to a
unit sphere $S^2$, admits essentially a unique representation of the form
$\lambda + \sum_{k = 1}^d [\prod_{j = 1}^k L_{kj}]$, where $L_{kj}$'s are
linear forms in $x, y$ and $z$ and $\lambda$ is a real number. The coefficients
of these linear forms, viewed as 3D vectors, are called \emph{multipole}
vectors of $P$. In this paper we consider similar multipole representations of
polynomial and analytic functions on other quadratic surfaces $Q(x, y, z) = c$,
real and complex. Over the complex numbers, the above representation is not
unique, although the ambiguity is essentially finite. We investigate the
combinatorics that depicts this ambiguity. We link these results with some
classical theorems of harmonic analysis, theorems that describe decompositions
of functions into sums of spherical harmonics. We extend these classical
theorems (which rely on our understanding of the Laplace operator
$\Delta_{S^2}$) to more general differential operators $\Delta_Q$ that are
constructed with the help of the quadratic form $Q(x, y, z)$. Then we introduce
modular spaces of multipoles. We study their intricate geometry and topology
using methods of algebraic geometry and singularity theory. The multipole
spaces are ramified over vector or projective spaces, and the compliments to
the ramification sets give rise to a rich family of $K(\pi, 1)$-spaces, where
$\pi$ runs over a variety of modified braid groups.

We re-analyze the direct $\jp$ production processes at the Fermilab Tevatron
in view of the recent observation at the B-factories, where both $\jp$
inclusive and exclusive production rates are found to be about an order of
magnitude larger than the leading order estimates of non-relativistic QCD. The
charm quark fragmentation to $\jp$, which is the dominant color-singlet process
at high $p_T$, is normalized by the B-factory measurements. The process
receives further enhancement due to the charm sea contribution which has so far
been ignored in most analyses. After summing up all sub-process contributions,
we find that the color-singlet mechanism alone can account for 20% to 90% of
the observed direct $\jp$ high $p_T$ production. The polarization rate of the
directly produced $\jp$ is sensitive to the fraction of the color-octet
contribution, which is employed to fill the gap between the color-singlet
prediction and experimental data. With a bigger $K$-factor for the charm quark
fragmentation probability, we envisage a smaller matrix element for the
color-octet $^3S_1^{(8)}$ state, and this can be examined at the LHC in near
future.

In this work, we explore the thermodynamics and virial expansion for trapped fluids in arbitrary external potentials. Our aim is to understand the behavior of fluids confined in complex spatial geometries, such as those encountered in microfluidic devices and biological systems. To achieve this, we employ a combination of theoretical and computational methods rooted in statistical mechanics and thermodynamics.

Using a virial expansion, we derive expressions for the equation of state and thermodynamic properties of the confined fluid as a function of temperature and confinement geometry. We show that these properties depend crucially on the spatial distribution of the external potential and the degree of confinement.

Our results demonstrate that the thermodynamic behavior of confined fluids can be significantly altered in the presence of complex spatial geometries. We find that the virial coefficients exhibit non-monotonic dependence on temperature, leading to the emergence of non-trivial thermodynamic states, such as multiple phase transitions and criticality.

The motivation for this research is to provide a theoretical foundation for understanding the thermodynamic properties of fluids in complex environments, with implications for the design and optimization of microfluidic devices and biological systems. Our findings have implications for a broad range of scientific fields, including soft matter physics, biophysics, and chemical engineering.

Our work focuses on the development of a graphene spin transistor, which shows promise in revolutionizing spintronics technology. Our motivation for this research stems from the fact that current spintronic devices, which utilize the spin of electrons in addition to their charge, face limitations in their efficiency and speed. 

In our methodology, we first fabricate a graphene device with a ferromagnetic insulator tunnel barrier, allowing for spin injection and detection. By applying a gate voltage to the device, we can control the spin transport properties of the graphene. Through extensive characterization and analysis, we demonstrate the ability of our graphene spin transistor to achieve high spin injection efficiency, high spin signal amplitude, and low leakage current. 

The problem that our work attempts to solve is the need for efficient and high-speed spintronic devices that can overcome the limitations of current technology. Our approach through the use of graphene and a ferromagnetic insulator tunnel barrier shows great potential in achieving this goal. This work provides a significant step forward in the development of spintronics technology, which has the potential to transform the landscape of electronics and computing.

In this work, we aim to solve the problem of efficiently updating finite element models (FEMs) using a Response Surface Method (RSM). FEMs are widely used in engineering to simulate and analyze complex systems, but their accuracy heavily relies on the input parameters and assumptions. Thus, updating the models to fit with experimental data is essential for obtaining accurate predictions. However, traditional methods for updating FEMs require time-consuming iterations between simulations and experiments, making them impractical for large-scale problems.

The motivation for this research stems from the need for efficient and accurate model updating methods for real-world engineering problems. RSM is a statistical optimization technique that enables surrogate modeling between variables, reducing the number of simulations required for model updating. In this study, we propose an efficient FEM updating method by integrating RSM with FEM. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method through a numerical example and compare the accuracy and efficiency with traditional methods. Our results show that the RSM-based approach can achieve higher accuracy with fewer simulations, reducing the computational time and cost of model updating.

In conclusion, this work offers an effective and efficient method for updating FEMs using RSM, which can benefit a wide range of engineering applications.

We compute the Coleman Weinberg effective potential for the Higgs field in RS
Gauge-Higgs unification scenarios based on a bulk SO(5) x U(1)_X gauge
symmetry, with gauge and fermion fields propagating in the bulk and a custodial
symmetry protecting the generation of large corrections to the T parameter and
the coupling of the Z to the bottom quark. We demonstrate that electroweak
symmetry breaking may be realized, with proper generation of the top and bottom
quark masses for the same region of bulk mass parameters that lead to good
agreement with precision electroweak data in the presence of a light Higgs. We
compute the Higgs mass and demonstrate that for the range of parameters for
which the Higgs boson has Standard Model-like properties, the Higgs mass is
naturally in a range that varies between values close to the LEP experimental
limit and about 160 GeV. This mass range may be probed at the Tevatron and at
the LHC. We analyze the KK spectrum and briefly discuss the phenomenology of
the light resonances arising in our model.

In this study, our team aimed to search for Earth analogues around the nearest stars by characterizing the disk age-metallicity relation and the age distribution in the Solar Neighbourhood. The motivation for this research stems from the importance of Earth analogues in the search for habitable planets outside of our Solar System. By investigating the age-metallicity relation and age distribution of stars in our neighbourhood, we can identify the stars that are most likely to host Earth-like planets.

To accomplish our goal, we obtained data on a sample of stars within 100 parsecs of the Sun. We measured the elemental abundances of these stars and applied a Bayesian statistical framework to determine their ages. Our results show a clear age-metallicity relation, with older stars generally having higher metallicities. Additionally, we found that the age distribution of stars in the Solar Neighbourhood is complex, with a significant number of young stars.

Overall, this study provides valuable insights into the age-metallicity relation and age distribution of stars in our neighbourhood. Our findings can inform future searches for Earth analogues and contribute to our understanding of the formation and evolution of planetary systems.

In this work, we provide a rigorous formulation and detailed proof for general graphs regarding the effective mapping of Ising spin glass models versus Ising models at high temperature. Our motivation for this research stems from the fact that Ising models are extensively used in various scientific fields to understand and predict phenomena such as magnetization, phase transitions, and critical behavior. However, the behavior of spin glass models is much less understood, and insights into their behavior can have implications in areas such as neural networks and optimization problems. 

Our work attempts to offer a rigorous understanding of the mapping between these two models, which could provide a more complete understanding of the behavior of spin glass models. We employ a rigorous mathematical framework using random matrix theory and find that the effective potentials of both models are generally equivalent at high temperatures. Moreover, we demonstrate that this mapping holds for general graphs, which can include complex and disordered systems. 

Overall, our work aims to bridge the gap between Ising models and spin glass models to provide deeper insights into the behavior of more complex systems. By providing a rigorous formulation, our findings have implications for the development of more accurate computational algorithms in areas such as optimization and artificial intelligence.

SDSS J080531.84+481233.0 is a peculiar L-type dwarf that exhibits unusually
blue near-infrared and mid-infrared colors and divergent optical (L4) and
near-infrared (L9.5) spectral classifications. These peculiar spectral traits
have been variously attributed to condensate cloud effects or subsolar
metallicity. Here I present an improved near-infrared spectrum of this source
which further demonstrates the presence of weak CH4 absorption at 1.6 micron
but no corresponding band at 2.2 micron. It is shown that these features can be
collectively reproduced by the combined light spectrum of a binary with L4.5
and T5 components, as deduced by spectral template matching. Thus, SDSS
J080531.84+481233.0 appears to be a new low-mass binary straddling the L
dwarf/T dwarf transition, an evolutionary phase for brown dwarfs that remains
poorly understood by current theoretical models. The case of SDSS
J080531.84+481233.0 further illustrates how a select range of L dwarf/T dwarf
binaries could be identified and characterized without the need for high
angular resolution imaging or radial velocity monitoring, potentially
alleviating some of the detection biases and limitations inherent to such
techniques.

To investigate the universality of the structure of interactions in different
markets, we analyze the cross-correlation matrix C of stock price fluctuations
in the National Stock Exchange (NSE) of India. We find that this emerging
market exhibits strong correlations in the movement of stock prices compared to
developed markets, such as the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE). This is shown to
be due to the dominant influence of a common market mode on the stock prices.
By comparison, interactions between related stocks, e.g., those belonging to
the same business sector, are much weaker. This lack of distinct sector
identity in emerging markets is explicitly shown by reconstructing the network
of mutually interacting stocks. Spectral analysis of C for NSE reveals that,
the few largest eigenvalues deviate from the bulk of the spectrum predicted by
random matrix theory, but they are far fewer in number compared to, e.g., NYSE.
We show this to be due to the relative weakness of intra-sector interactions
between stocks, compared to the market mode, by modeling stock price dynamics
with a two-factor model. Our results suggest that the emergence of an internal
structure comprising multiple groups of strongly coupled components is a
signature of market development.

IGR J11215-5952 is a hard X-ray transient source discovered in April 2005
with INTEGRAL and a confirmed member of the new class of High Mass X-ray
Binaries, the Supergiant Fast X-ray Transients (SFXTs). Archival INTEGRAL data
and RXTE observations showed that the outbursts occur with a periodicity of
~330 days. Thus, IGR J11215-5952 is the first SFXT displaying periodic
outbursts, possibly related to the orbital period. We performed a Target of
Opportunity observation with Swift with the main aim of monitoring the source
behaviour around the time of the fifth outburst, expected on 2007 Feb 9. The
source field was observed with Swift twice a day (2ks/day) starting from 4th
February, 2007, until the fifth outburst, and then for ~5 ks a day afterwards,
during a monitoring campaign that lasted 23 days for a total on-source exposure
of ~73 ks. This is the most complete monitoring campaign of an outburst from a
SFXT. The spectrum during the brightest flares is well described by an absorbed
power law with a photon index of 1 and N_H~1 10^22 cm^-2. A 1-10 keV peak
luminosity of ~10^36 erg s^-1 was derived (assuming 6.2 kpc, the distance of
the optical counterpart). These Swift observations are a unique data-set for an
outburst of a SFXT, thanks to the combination of sensitivity and time coverage,
and they allowed a study of IGR J11215-5952 from outburst onset to almost
quiescence. We find that the accretion phase lasts longer than previously
thought on the basis of lower sensitivity instruments observing only the
brightest flares. The observed phenomenology is consistent with a smoothly
increasing flux triggered at the periastron passage in a wide eccentric orbit
with many flares superimposed, possibly due to episodic or inhomogeneous
accretion.

In our study, we present a quantum mechanical approach to understanding decoherence and relaxation in systems exposed to a fluctuating environment. Our motivation for this research arises from the crucial need to understand and control the effects of environmental perturbations, which lead to the degradation of quantum states, ultimately limiting the practical applications of quantum technologies.

We derived a master equation that describes the time evolution of the density matrix of the system, coupled to the environment characterized by a continuous spectrum of oscillators. To obtain explicit expressions of decoherence and relaxation rates, we utilized various analytical and numerical techniques, such as Laplace transform and Monte Carlo simulations.

Our key results indicate that the relaxation process is strongly influenced by the spectral density of the environment, with low-frequency modes playing a crucial role. We found that, in contrast, decoherence is mostly influenced by the coupling strength between the system and the environment. Furthermore, we observed that the dynamics of the system's coherence are bounded by two timescales, corresponding to the slow and fast modes of the environment.

Overall, our study provides important insights into the complex interplay between quantum systems and fluctuating environments. Our findings can help understand and optimize the performance of quantum technologies and pave the way for the development of new methods for quantum control and information processing.

In our study, we investigate the acceleration and substructure constraints in a quasar outflow. Quasars are incredibly luminous, active galactic nuclei that eject high-velocity winds and play a vital role in regulating the growth of galaxies. These outflows, composed of gas and dust, are critical to understand quasar-host galaxy feedback and have been linked to the chemical enrichment of galaxies. Our results show that the outflow exhibits acceleration over a wide range of velocities, and the substructure along the outflow axis correlates with velocity and acceleration. 

Our methodology involved obtaining high-resolution spectra of an intervening galaxy that was illuminated by the quasar outflow. We analyzed the absorption lines in the spectra to determine the kinematic properties of the outflow. Using statistical models, we inferred the acceleration and substructure properties of the outflow.

Our study aims to solve the problem of understanding the acceleration and substructure of quasar outflows and their impact on the host galaxy. These results provide insights into the mechanisms driving the formation and evolution of galaxies and could inform models of galaxy formation and evolution. Our findings contribute to the scientific understanding of the physical processes that shape the universe.

The modeling of the early universe is done through the quantization of a
Friedmann-Robertson-Walker model with positive curvature. The material content
consists of two fluids: radiation and Chaplygin gas. The quantization of these
models is made by following the Wheeler and DeWitt's prescriptions. Using the
Schutz formalism, the time notion is recovered and the Wheeler-DeWitt equation
transforms into a time dependent Schr\"{o}dinger equation, which rules the
dynamics of the early universe, under the action of an effective potential
$V_{ef}$. Using a finite differences method and the Crank-Nicholson scheme, in
a code implemented in the program OCTAVE, we solve the corresponding time
dependent Schr\"{o}dinger equation and obtain the time evolution of a initial
wave packet. This wave packet satisfies appropriate boundary conditions. The
calculation of the tunneling probabilities shows that the universe may emerge
from the Planck era to an inflationary phase. It also shows that, the tunneling
probability is a function of the mean energy of the initial wave packet and of
two parameters related to the Chaplygin gas. We also show a comparison between
these results and those obtained by the WKB approximation.

In this paper, we investigate the Stokes phenomenon that arises in the confluence of the hypergeometric differential equation using the Riccati equation. Our research seeks to provide a deeper understanding of the Stokes phenomenon and explore a new method for studying it. 

The motivation for this research stems from the fact that the Stokes phenomenon is an important concept in the study of differential equations and has numerous applications in physics and engineering. However, its occurrence in the confluence of the hypergeometric equation is particularly challenging to analyze. 

To address this issue, we employ the Riccati equation as a tool for studying the Stokes phenomenon. Our methodology involves transforming the hypergeometric equation into a Riccati equation, which provides us with a more accessible equation to work with. From there, we apply the theory of G-functions to obtain asymptotic solutions and analyze the Stokes phenomenon. 

Our results demonstrate that the Riccati equation provides a useful framework for studying the Stokes phenomenon in the confluence of the hypergeometric equation. We obtain explicit formulas for the Stokes matrices and show that they exhibit the expected behavior. Additionally, we investigate the case where the confluence parameter approaches infinity and provide asymptotic expansions for the coefficients of the Stokes matrices. Overall, this research contributes to the understanding of the Stokes phenomenon and provides a new method for analyzing it.

In this work, we investigate the magnification relations for gravitational lensing around rotating black holes, specifically Kerr lensing, and explore its potential role in testing the Cosmic Censorship conjecture. The motivation for this research stems from the need to better understand the properties of black holes and their behavior in different gravitational lensing scenarios, as well as to answer some of the fundamental questions about the nature of the spacetime fabric itself.

We derive the magnification relations for Kerr lensing using the Newman-Penrose formalism, and explore their behavior for different black hole spin values and lens-source geometries. We find that the magnification patterns around rotating black holes are significantly different from non-rotating ones, and can be used to test the Cosmic Censorship conjecture by analyzing the image distortion and the null geodesics around the black hole.

Our methodology involves numerical simulations of the Kerr metric using the ray-tracing algorithm, with a range of lens-source configurations and spin values. We compute the deflection angles, magnification ratios, and time delays of the observed images, and analyze their behavior in different gravitational lensing regimes.

In conclusion, our work sheds new light on the properties of Kerr lensing and its potential role in testing the Cosmic Censorship hypothesis, thus advancing our understanding of the fundamental properties of black holes and the spacetime fabric.

In this work, we study the Pressure induced Insulator-Metal transition in LaMnO$_3$ using density functional theory calculations. Our primary aim is to resolve the long-standing problem of the origin of the insulating ground state in LaMnO$_3$. This material belongs to the family of perovskite manganites and exhibits unique electronic and magnetic properties such as colossal magnetoresistance and charge/orbital ordering. The insulating state of LaMnO$_3$ is attributed to the Jahn-Teller distortion that splits the d orbitals and opens a band gap. However, experimental observations suggest that pressure can reverse this trend and induce a metal-to-insulator transition.

Our results show that the pressure-induced Insulator-Metal transition in LaMnO$_3$ is primarily due to a decrease in the Mn-O bond length, which increases the electron hopping between the Mn ions. This electron delocalization leads to a partial filling of the d-band resulting in a metallic ground state. Our calculations match well with available experimental data and provide a significant insight into the nature of the pressure-induced transition. This work is essential not only for understanding the origin of the insulating ground state in LaMnO$_3$ but also for exploring the potential of this material for various technological applications.

In this paper, we present a novel approach to solving the problem of optimal routing for decode-and-forward based cooperation in wireless networks. The problem we attempt to solve is how to effectively determine the best routes for forwarding information from a source node to a destination node with the aid of relay nodes. Our methodology involves formulating a mathematical model for the problem, which is optimized using linear programming techniques. Our key idea is to incorporate a novel objective function that takes into account both the energy consumption and delay constraints of the network, while also minimizing the overall transmission delay and maximizing the network throughput.

We evaluate our proposed routing method in a simulated wireless network, and our results demonstrate that it outperforms several state-of-the-art routing algorithms in terms of network throughput and energy efficiency. Specifically, we show that our approach significantly reduces the transmission delay of information packets, leading to better overall network performance. Additionally, we demonstrate that our method allows for the effective use of relay nodes in the network, leading to greater energy efficiency.

In conclusion, our work presents a new and effective approach for optimal routing in decode-and-forward based wireless networks. Our methodology is designed to address the key challenges associated with routing in these networks, and our results show that our approach offers significant improvements over existing methods. This research has the potential to contribute to the development of more efficient and effective wireless networks, with potential applications in areas such as mobile communication and the Internet of Things.

How do T Tauri disks accrete? The magneto-rotational instability (MRI)
supplies one means, but protoplanetary disk gas is typically too poorly ionized
to be magnetically active. Here we show that the MRI can, in fact, explain
observed accretion rates for the sub-class of T Tauri disks known as
transitional systems. Transitional disks are swept clean of dust inside rim
radii of ~10 AU. Stellar coronal X-rays ionize material in the disk rim,
activating the MRI there. Gas flows from the rim to the star, at a rate limited
by the depth to which X-rays ionize the rim wall. The wider the rim, the larger
the surface area that the rim wall exposes to X-rays, and the greater the
accretion rate. Interior to the rim, the MRI continues to transport gas; the
MRI is sustained even at the disk midplane by super-keV X-rays that Compton
scatter down from the disk surface. Accretion is therefore steady inside the
rim. Blown out by radiation pressure, dust largely fails to accrete with gas.
Contrary to what is usually assumed, ambipolar diffusion, not Ohmic
dissipation, limits how much gas is MRI-active. We infer values for the
transport parameter alpha on the order of 0.01 for GM Aur, TW Hyd, and DM Tau.
Because the MRI can only afflict a finite radial column of gas at the rim, disk
properties inside the rim are insensitive to those outside. Thus our picture
provides one robust setting for planet-disk interaction: a protoplanet interior
to the rim will interact with gas whose density, temperature, and transport
properties are definite and decoupled from uncertain initial conditions. Our
study also supplies half the answer to how disks dissipate: the inner disk
drains from the inside out by the MRI, while the outer disk photoevaporates by
stellar ultraviolet radiation.

Probing the geometry of the universe is one of the most important endevours
in cosmology. Current observational data from the Cosmic Microwave Background
anisotropy (CMB), galaxy surveys and type Ia supernovae (SNe Ia) strongly
constrain the curvature of the universe to be close to zero for a universe
dominated by a cosmological constant or dark energy with a constant equation of
state. Here we investigate the role of cosmic priors on deriving these tight
bounds on geometry, by considering a landscape motivated scenario with an
oscillating curvature term. We perform a likelihood analysis of current data
under such a model of non-trivial geometry and find that the uncertainties on
curvature, and correspondingly on parameters of the matter and dark energy
sectors, are larger. Future dark energy experiments together with CMB data from
experiments like Planck could dramatically improve our ability to constrain
cosmic curvature under such models enabling us to probe possible imprints of
quantum gravity.

In this work, we present a study on the evaporation of buffer gas-thermalized anions out of a multipole rf ion trap. Our motivation for this research is to improve the understanding of buffer gas cooling methods in ion trapping experiments, which can enhance their sensitivity and precision. The problem we aim to solve is to elucidate the mechanisms that govern the release of trapped ions from the buffer gas cloud, which is a crucial aspect of such cooling methods.

We employ a combination of experimental measurements and numerical simulations to investigate the process of anion evaporation. Our results show that the evaporation rate of anions is strongly dependent on their mass and temperature, as well as the properties of the buffer gas and trap. Moreover, we find that the evaporation process is primarily determined by the interplay between radial and axial confinement of the ion cloud, as well as the diffusion and collisions of the buffer gas atoms.

Overall, our study provides new insights into the buffer gas cooling mechanisms in ion trapping experiments and highlights the potential of evaporation as a tool for manipulating trapped ions. By shedding light on the underlying physics of buffer gas cooling, our work contributes to the development of more precise and sensitive ion trapping techniques for a wide range of applications in physics, chemistry, and metrology.

In this work, we present simulations and theoretical predictions of one- and two-component bottle-brush polymers. We explore the effects of both polymer segment length and volume fraction on the structure and self-assembly behavior of these complex macromolecules. Our approach is based on molecular dynamics simulations combined with theoretical methods such as self-consistent field theory. We find that the self-assembly behavior of one-component bottle-brush polymers is strongly influenced by the segment length, and we observe both disordered and ordered phases. In contrast, for two-component bottle-brush polymers, we observe the formation of alternating phases with well-defined interfaces. Our simulations are in good agreement with theoretical predictions, and we discuss the underlying physical mechanisms that govern the self-assembly behavior of these systems.

Our research is motivated by the potential applications of bottle-brush polymers in various fields such as nanotechnology and materials science. These polymers have unique properties due to their high molecular weight and complex architecture. However, their behavior is not fully understood, and our simulations and theoretical predictions provide new insights into the structure and self-assembly behavior of these fascinating macromolecules. Overall, this work contributes to the understanding of bottle-brush polymers and lays the foundation for future design and synthesis of functional materials based on these polymers.

We investigate convolutions on compact groups and Fourier algebras of coset spaces in this work. Our primary goal is to determine which coset spaces can be realized as Fourier algebras of compact groups. We attempt to solve this problem by studying the essential ideals of Fourier algebras and the properties of convolution operators. 

Our methodology involves examining the properties of harmonic functions and the Fourier transform on compact groups. We also use the theory of Banach algebras to study commutative function algebras and their representations. By combining these techniques, we establish conditions under which a coset space is isomorphic to a Fourier algebra of a compact group.

Our investigation is motivated by the desire to better understand the structure of Fourier algebras on compact groups and to classify the coset spaces that can be realized as such algebras. This research has applications in several areas of mathematics, including representation theory, harmonic analysis, and operator algebras. 

In conclusion, through our work on convolutions on compact groups and Fourier algebras of coset spaces, we have developed a deeper understanding of the structure and properties of Fourier algebras, essential ideals, and convolution operators. Our findings have important implications in the fields of representation theory and harmonic analysis, and we expect them to spark future research in these areas.

Recent galaxy redshift surveys have brought in a large amount of accurate
cosmological data out to redshift 0.3, and future surveys are expected to
achieve a high degree of completeness out to a redshift exceeding 1.
Consequently, a numerical programme for determining the metric of the universe
from observational data will soon become practical; and thereby realise the
ultimate application of Einstein's equations. Apart from detailing the cosmic
geometry, this would allow us to verify and quantify homogeneity, rather than
assuming it, as has been necessary up to now, and to do that on a metric level,
and not merely at the mass distribution level. This paper is the beginning of a
project aimed at such a numerical implementation. The primary observational
data from our past light cone consists of galaxy redshifts, apparent
luminosities, angular diameters and number densities, together with source
evolution functions, absolute luminosities, true diameters and masses of
sources. Here we start with the simplest case, that of spherical symmetry and a
dust equation of state, and execute an algorithm that determines the unknown
metric functions from this data. We discuss the challenges of turning the
theoretical algorithm into a workable numerical procedure, particularly
addressing the origin and the maximum in the area distance. Our numerical
method is tested with several artificial data sets for homogeneous and
inhomogeneous models, successfully reproducing the original models. This
demonstrates the basic viability of such a scheme. Although current surveys
don't have sufficient completeness or accuracy, we expect this situation to
change in the near future, and in the meantime there are many refinements and
generalisations to be added.

A confluence of numerical and theoretical results leads us to conjecture that
the Hilbert-Schmidt separability probabilities of the 15- and 9-dimensional
convex sets of complex and real two-qubit states (representable by 4 x 4
density matrices rho) are 8/33 and 8/17, respectively. Central to our reasoning
are the modifications of two ansatze, recently advanced (quant-ph/0609006),
involving incomplete beta functions B_{nu}(a,b), where nu= (rho_{11}
rho_{44})/(rho_{22} rho_{33}). We, now, set the separability function
S_{real}(nu) propto B_{nu}(nu,1/2},2) =(2/3) (3-nu) sqrt{nu}. Then, in the
complex case -- conforming to a pattern we find, manifesting the Dyson indices
(1, 2, 4) of random matrix theory-- we take S_{complex}(nu) propto S_{real}^{2}
(nu). We also investigate the real and complex qubit-qutrit cases. Now, there
are two Bloore ratio variables, nu_{1}= (rho_{11} rho_{55})(rho_{22} rho_{44}),
nu_{2}= (rho_{22} rho_{66})(rho_{33} rho_{55}), but they appear to remarkably
coalesce into the product, eta = nu_1 nu_2 = rho_{11} \rho_{66}}{\rho_{33}
\rho_{44}}, so that the real and complex separability functions are again
univariate in nature.

In this study, we investigate the stellar content and recent star formation history of the Local Group Dwarf Irregular Galaxy IC1613. Our motivation is to better understand the formation and evolution of small, gas-rich galaxies in the Local Group, which provide a unique window into the early universe. Using deep imaging data from the Hubble Space Telescope, we analyze the spatial distribution and age distribution of the stellar populations in IC1613. Our key results show that IC1613 has a complex and extended stellar structure, with a prominent central bar and an outer disk component. We also find evidence for recent star formation activity, with a significant population of young (less than 100 million years old) stars located in the outer regions of the galaxy. Our analyses confirm that IC1613 has experienced a prolonged period of star formation activity, with multiple episodes of bursty star formation over the past several hundred million years. Our methodology includes the use of photometric analysis and color-magnitude diagrams to identify and characterize different populations of stars, as well as the application of spatial statistics and galaxy modeling to map the distribution of stars in IC1613. Overall, our findings provide new insights into the formation and evolution of small galaxies in the Local Group and highlight the important role of dwarf galaxies in shaping our understanding of the universe.

We review and extend the progress made over the past few years in
understanding the structure of toric quiver gauge theories; those which are
induced on the world-volume of a stack of D3-branes placed at the tip of a
toric Calabi-Yau cone, at an ``orbifold point'' in Kaehler moduli space. These
provide an infinite class of four-dimensional N=1 superconformal field theories
which may be studied in the context of the AdS/CFT correspondence. It is now
understood that these gauge theories are completely specified by certain
two-dimensional torus graphs, called brane tilings, and the combinatorics of
the dimer models on these graphs. In particular, knowledge of the dual
Sasaki-Einstein metric is not required to determine the gauge theory, only
topological and symplectic properties of the toric Calabi-Yau cone. By
analyzing the symmetries of the toric quiver theories we derive the dimer
models and use them to construct the moduli space of the theory both
classically and semiclassically. Using mirror symmetry the brane tilings are
shown to arise in string theory on the world-volumes of the fractional
D6-branes that are mirror to the stack of D3-branes at the tip of the cone.

This paper is dedicated to triangulated categories endowed with weight
structures (a new notion; D. Pauksztello has independently introduced them as
co-t-structures). This axiomatizes the properties of stupid truncations of
complexes in $K(B)$. We also construct weight structures for Voevodsky's
categories of motives and for various categories of spectra. A weight structure
$w$ defines Postnikov towers of objects; these towers are canonical and
functorial 'up to morphisms that are zero on cohomology'. For $Hw$ being the
heart of $w$ (in $DM_{gm}$ we have $Hw=Chow$) we define a canonical
conservative 'weakly exact' functor $t$ from our $C$ to a certain weak category
of complexes $K_w(Hw)$. For any (co)homological functor $H:C\to A$ for an
abelian $A$ we construct a weight spectral sequence $T:H(X^i[j])\implies
H(X[i+j])$ where $(X^i)=t(X)$; it is canonical and functorial starting from
$E_2$. This spectral sequences specializes to the 'usual' (Deligne's) weight
spectral sequences for 'classical' realizations of motives and to
Atiyah-Hirzebruch spectral sequences for spectra. Under certain restrictions,
we prove that $K_0(C)\cong K_0(Hw)$ and $K_0(End C)\cong K_0(End Hw)$.
  The definition of a weight structure is almost dual to those of a
t-structure; yet several properties differ. One can often construct a certain
$t$-structure which is 'adjacent' to $w$ and vice versa. This is the case for
the Voevodsky's $DM^{eff}_-$ (one obtains certain new Chow weight and
t-structures for it; the heart of the latter is 'dual' to $Chow^{eff}$) and for
the stable homotopy category. The Chow t-structure is closely related to
unramified cohomology.

In this work, we investigate the rates and characteristics of intermediate mass ratio inspirals (IMRIs) that can be detected by Advanced Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory (LIGO). IMRIs are binary systems composed of a stellar mass compact object, such as a neutron star or black hole, and a more massive black hole with a mass ratio between 1:10 to 1:100. They are expected to be a significant source of gravitational waves for LIGO, particularly for the lower frequency band. We use a population synthesis model to simulate IMRI formation and merger rates in different galaxy environments, taking into account stellar evolution, binary interactions, and dynamical processes. We then calculate the expected signal-to-noise ratio and detection rates for Advanced LIGO based on the generated population. Our results suggest that IMRIs can be detected by Advanced LIGO with a significant detection rate, ranging from 5 to 600 events per year, depending on the galaxy environment. We also find that most of the detectable IMRIs have relatively short merger timescales, typically less than 100 million years. Our research provides important insights into the potential of Advanced LIGO to explore the astrophysical properties of IMRIs and the underlying galaxy formation and evolution.

We present a generalized analysis on the $B\to K^*\rho$ decay within and beyond the Standard Model (SM) and explore its implications on the long-standing $B \to K \pi$ puzzle. Understanding the origin of this puzzle is of utmost importance as it could potentially shed light on the structure of physics beyond the SM. The $B\to K^*\rho$ decay encompasses the $B \to K\pi$ mode as a limiting case, where the $\rho$ meson in the final state decays into a pion. By analyzing this decay, we extract valuable information about the unitarity triangle, which describes the geometric relationship between the weak phases in the CKM matrix. 

We perform a model-independent analysis of the decay amplitude, including both tree and penguin contributions, as well as new physics (NP) effects that could arise from possible beyond-SM interactions. Our results reveal that NP contributions to the decay amplitude can act constructively or destructively, leading to potentially significant deviations from the SM prediction. Moreover, by comparing the branching ratio and CP-asymmetries between $B \to K^* \rho$ and $B \to K \pi$, we can shed light on fundamental aspects of the $B \to K\pi$ puzzle. 

Our findings highlight the importance of a generalized analysis of different $B$ decays in deciphering the origin of the $B \to K\pi$ puzzle. They also point to possible experimental avenues to clarify the nature of NP in the $B\to K^*\rho$ sector and its relevance to the $B \to K\pi$ puzzle.

We present the properties of 11 $\mu$m selected sources detected in the early
data of the North Ecliptic Pole Deep (NEP-Deep) Survey of AKARI. The data set
covers 6 wavelength bands from 2.5 to 11 $\mu$m, with the exposure time of 10 ~
20 minutes. This field lies within the CFHT survey with four filter bands ($g',
r', i',z'), enabling us to establish nearly continuous spectral energy
distributions (SEDs) for wavelengths ranging from 0.4 to 11 $\mu$m. The main
sample studied here consists of 71 sources whose 11 $\mu$m AB magnitudes are
equal to or brighter than 18.5 (251 $\mu$Jy), which is complete to more than
90%. The 11 $\mu$m band has an advantage of sampling star forming galaxies with
low to medium redshifts since the prominent PAH feature shifts into this band.
As expected, we find that the majority (~68%) of 11 $\mu$m bright sources are
star forming galaxies at 0.2 < z < 0.7 with $L_{IR} ~ 10^{10}$ -- $10^{12}
L_{\odot}$ based on the detailed modelling of SEDs. We also find four AGNs
lying at various redshifts in the main sample. In addition, we discuss a few
sources which have non-typical SEDs of the main sample, including a brown dwarf
candidate, a steep power-law source, flat spectrum object, and an early-type
galaxy at moderate redshift.

The embedder and the detector (or decoder) are the two most important
components of the digital watermarking systems. Thus in this work, we discuss
how to design a better embedder and detector (or decoder). I first give a
summary of the prospective applications of watermarking technology and major
watermarking schemes in the literature. My review on the literature closely
centers upon how the side information is exploited at both embedders and
detectors. In Chapter 3, I explore the optimum detector or decoder according to
a particular probability distribution of the host signals. We found that the
performance of both multiplicative and additive spread spectrum schemes depends
on the shape parameter of the host signals. For spread spectrum schemes, the
performance of the detector or the decoder is reduced by the host interference.
Thus I present a new host-interference rejection technique for the
multiplicative spread spectrum schemes. Its embedding rule is tailored to the
optimum detection or decoding rule. Though the host interference rejection
schemes enjoy a big performance gain over the traditional spread spectrum
schemes, their drawbacks that it is difficult for them to be implemented with
the perceptual analysis to achieve the maximum allowable embedding level
discourage their use in real scenarios. Thus, in the last chapters of this
work, I introduce a double-sided technique to tackle this drawback. It differs
from the host interference rejection schemes in that it utilizes but does not
reject the host interference at its embedder. The perceptual analysis can be
easily implemented in our scheme to achieve the maximum allowable level of
embedding strength.

Phase screens above a telescope pupil represent the variation of the phase of
the electromagnetic field induced by atmospheric turbulence. Instances drawn
from such statistics are represented by a vector of random phase amplitudes
which are coefficients of a linear superposition of two-dimensional basis
functions across the pupil. This work shortly reviews Fried's analysis of this
modal decomposition for the case of Kolmogorov statistics of the phase
covariance as a function of separation in the pupil plane.
  We focus on the numerical synthesis of phase screens. The statistically
independent modes are transformed into the eigen-modes of a gradient matrix as
time-dependence is introduced such that on short time scales the instances of
the phase screens are rigidly shifted into a direction imposed by some wind
velocity - known as the Taylor frozen screen approximation. This simple
technique factorizes spatial and temporal variables and aims at binding the
time dependence of the phase screens to the few expansion coefficients of the
basis functions that obey a stochastic time-dependent differential equation.

In this study, we present simulations of the boundary layer between a white dwarf and its accretion disk, aiming to shed light on the physical mechanisms governing this region and its importance in the evolution of binary star systems. To accomplish this, we performed 3D numerical simulations using the Magnetohydrodynamics code Athena++, incorporating the effects of magnetic fields and radiative cooling in the accretion disk. Our simulations show that the boundary layer is highly dynamic and subject to strong magnetic stresses and instabilities, which lead to the formation of turbulence and the generation of heat and radiation. The temperature and density profiles of the boundary layer are found to be sensitive to the details of the accretion flow, including the accretion rate and the strength of the magnetic field. Our results support the idea that the boundary layer plays a crucial role in regulating the accretion rate onto the white dwarf and in shaping the observational properties of these systems, such as their X-ray and optical emission. This work provides a framework for future studies of accretion processes in compact binary systems and highlights the importance of simulations for understanding complex astrophysical phenomena.

In this work, we aim to investigate the emergence of quantum Hall ferromagnetism in graphene by employing a SU(4) bosonization approach. Specifically, we seek to address the question of whether graphene can host quantum Hall ferromagnetism, which has only been observed in a few select materials. Our methodology involves applying a field theory description, which allows us to capture the interactions between electrons in a more comprehensive manner. We first use an analysis of the spin and valley degrees of freedom in graphene to construct the SU(4) symmetry. We then develop a field theory description that captures the complex interplay of electron correlations and topology that leads to quantum Hall ferromagnetism. Our motivation for this research stems from the belief that graphene could be a promising candidate for developing spin-based electronics, due to its unique electronic and topological properties. Therefore, understanding quantum Hall ferromagnetism in graphene is a critical step towards exploring its potential applications in spintronics. By developing a comprehensive understanding of quantum Hall ferromagnetism in graphene, this research could potentially pave the way for its use in the development of novel devices and technologies.

In this work, we investigate the possibility of distinguishing between quintessence and a cosmological constant in the framework of the concordance model of cosmology. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that basic observations of the universe have shown that the universe is undergoing an accelerated expansion. This fact has led to the development of two possible explanations: the existence of a mysterious energy component known as dark energy that is driving the acceleration or a theoretical vacuum energy known as a cosmological constant. 

To address the problem of distinguishing between these two possibilities, we simulated the cosmic microwave background radiation and used observational data to fit the parameters of the model. Our results show that the current observational data is consistent with either a cosmological constant or a quintessence field with a exponential potential. However, the data is not precise enough to distinguish between them conclusively.
