This research aims to solve the problem of determining the nature of dark energy that is driving the accelerated expansion of the universe. By providing a more precise method for distinguishing between quintessence and a cosmological constant, our work can help to reduce uncertainties in measurements of cosmological parameters and improve our understanding of the evolution of the universe.

In this paper, we introduce VESPA, a code designed to infer the star formation and metallicity histories of galaxies from their spectra. Our work responds to the need to understand the formation and evolution of galaxies, which remain active areas of research in modern astrophysics. The challenge lies in teasing out the details of star formation and chemical enrichment histories, which are encoded in the spectra of the galaxies we observe.

To accomplish this, VESPA uses a Bayesian framework that incorporates a library of composite stellar populations to fit the observed spectra. This approach enables us to simultaneously extract the overall star formation and metallicity histories, as well as the contributions of individual stellar populations at different times.

We demonstrate the power of VESPA by applying it to a sample of galaxies from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey. Our results indicate that the star formation histories of these galaxies vary widely, ranging from smooth and gradual to more rapid and episodic. Additionally, we find that metallicity evolution shows a mild dependence on star formation rate, as expected from theoretical models.

Overall, our work highlights the strength of VESPA in recovering detailed and accurate galaxy formation histories from observational data. By enabling a deeper understanding of the physical processes at work in galaxy evolution, VESPA will play an important role in shaping our understanding of the universe.

We present a study on the gas-grain chemistry occurring in cold interstellar cloud cores, employing a microscopic Monte Carlo approach to surface chemistry. The main objective of our work is to understand the chemical evolution of such cloud cores by modeling the physical-chemical interactions between gas-phase species and the interstellar dust grains. Our motivation stems from the fact that these molecular clouds, containing a large amount of molecular hydrogen, are believed to be the birthplace of new stars and planets; therefore, studying the chemical evolution of these regions can help us understand the origins of life in the universe. 

We employ an improved version of the KMC (kinetic Monte Carlo) method, which allows for a more realistic modelling of the surface chemistry on interstellar dust grains, taking into account the physical and chemical properties of the surface sites. Our results show that the gas-phase species can readily adsorb onto the dust grain surfaces, with formation of molecular hydrogen being the dominant process. Furthermore, we find that the density of the gas-phase species and the surface temperature have a significant influence on the chemical evolution of these regions. 

Overall, our study provides deeper insight into the chemical processes occurring in cold interstellar cloud cores, and can assist in the comprehension of the chemical and physical conditions necessary for the formation of organic molecules and their relevance to the origin of life.

In nuclear chiral perturbation theory (ChPT), an operator is defined in a
space with a cutoff which may be varied within a certain range. The operator
runs as a result of the variation of the cutoff [renormalization group (RG)
running]. In order for ChPT to be useful, the operator should run in a way
consistent with the counting rule; that is, the running of chiral counter terms
have to be of natural size. We vary the cutoff using the Wilsonian
renormalization group (WRG) equation, and examine this consistency. As an
example, we study the s-wave pion production operator for NN\to d pi, derived
in ChPT. We demonstrate that the WRG running does not generate any
chiral-symmetry-violating (CSV) interaction, provided that we start with an
operator which does not contain a CSV term. We analytically show how the
counter terms are generated in the WRG running in case of the infinitesimal
cutoff reduction. Based on the analytic result, we argue a range of the cutoff
variation for which the running of the counter terms is of natural size. Then,
we numerically confirm this.

Let P(z) and Q(y) be polynomials of the same degree k>=1 in the complex
variables z and y, respectively. In this extended abstract we study the
non-linear functional equation P(z)=Q(y(z)), where y(z) is restricted to be
analytic in a neighborhood of z=0. We provide sufficient conditions to ensure
that all the roots of Q(y) are contained within the range of y(z) as well as to
have y(z)=z as the unique analytic solution of the non-linear equation. Our
results are motivated from uniqueness considerations of polynomial canonical
representations of the phase or amplitude terms of oscillatory integrals
encountered in the asymptotic analysis of the coefficients of mixed powers and
multivariable generating functions via saddle-point methods. Uniqueness shall
prove important for developing algorithms to determine the Taylor coefficients
of the terms appearing in these representations. The uniqueness of Levinson's
polynomial canonical representations of analytic functions in several variables
follows as a corollary of our one-complex variables results.

We have upgraded the 60-cm radio survey telescope located in Nobeyama, Japan.
We developed a new waveguide-type sideband-separating SIS mixer for the
telescope, which enables the simultaneous detection of distinct molecular
emission lines both in the upper and lower sidebands. Over the RF frequency
range of 205-240 GHz, the single-sideband receiver noise temperatures of the
new mixer are 40-100 K for the 4.0-8.0 GHz IF frequency band. The image
rejection ratios are greater than 10 dB over the same range. For the dual IF
signals obtained by the receiver, we have developed two sets of acousto-optical
spectrometers and a telescope control system. Using the new telescope system,
we successfully detected the 12CO (J=2-1) and 13CO (J=2-1) emission lines
simultaneously toward Orion KL in 2005 March. Using the waveguide-type
sideband-separating SIS mixer for the 200 GHz band, we have initiated the first
simultaneous 12CO (J=2-1) and 13CO (J=2-1) survey of the galactic plane as well
as large-scale mapping observations of nearby molecular clouds.

This review presents the elastic theory of low-dimensional (one- and
two-dimensional) continua and its applications in bio- and nano-structures.
First, the curve and surface theory, as the geometric representation of the
low-dimensional continua, is briefly described through Cartan moving frame
method. The elastic theory of Kirchhoff rod, Helfrich rod, bending-soften rod,
fluid membrane, and solid shell is revisited. Secondly, the application and
availability of the elastic theory of low-dimensional continua in
bio-structures, including short DNA rings, lipid membranes, and cell membranes,
are discussed. The kink stability of short DNA rings is addressed by using the
theory of Kirchhoff rod, Helfrich rod, and bending-soften rod. The lipid
membranes obey the theory of fluid membrane. A cell membrane is simplified as a
composite shell of lipid bilayer and membrane skeleton, which is a little
similar to the solid shell. It is found that the membrane skeleton enhances
highly the mechanical stability of cell membranes. Thirdly, the application and
availability of the elastic theory of low-dimensional continua in
nano-structures, including graphene and carbon nanotubes, are discussed. A
revised Lenosky lattice model is proposed based on the local density
approximation. Its continuum form up to the second order terms of curvatures
and strains is the same as the free energy of 2D solid shells. Several typical
mechanical properties of carbon nanotubes are revisited and investigated based
on this continuum form. It is possible to avoid introducing the controversial
concepts, the Young's modulus and thickness of graphene and single-walled
carbon nanotubes, with this continuum form.

In this work, we present an extensive study on the modelling effects and observational biases that can significantly affect Sunyaev-Zel'dovich (SZ) profiles and scaling relations within galaxy clusters. Our motivation for conducting this research lies in the need to better understand the SZ effect; a phenomenon that has become an essential tool in the measurement of cluster properties. However, the SZ effect is prone to various biases, including contamination by radio sources, calibration uncertainties, and astrophysical uncertainties affecting the modelling of the intracluster medium. Therefore, this work attempts to address these potential biases and modelling effects in SZ profiles to improve astrophysical interpretations and measurements and investigate the uncertainties in scaling relations.

We use a sophisticated set of simulations to examine various bias and modelling effects that are not always taken into account in observational studies. Additionally, we calculate scaling relations and use them to create mock SZ catalogues that can be compared to real observational data to examine the impact of these effects on surveys. Our results show that the observed SZ profiles have uncertainties of up to 8% due to radio contamination effects, while calibration uncertainties have a more modest impact. We also find that uncertainties in the cluster gas properties lead to uncertainties in SZ scaling relations, with slope variations of up to 20%. The proposed correction techniques and observations presented in this study could help in measuring fundamental cosmological parameters and probing the astrophysics of galaxy clusters more accurately.

We address the problem of estimating the spherical-harmonic power spectrum of
a statistically isotropic scalar signal from noise-contaminated data on a
region of the unit sphere. Three different methods of spectral estimation are
considered: (i) the spherical analogue of the one-dimensional (1-D)
periodogram, (ii) the maximum likelihood method, and (iii) a spherical analogue
of the 1-D multitaper method. The periodogram exhibits strong spectral leakage,
especially for small regions of area $A\ll 4\pi$, and is generally unsuitable
for spherical spectral analysis applications, just as it is in 1-D. The maximum
likelihood method is particularly useful in the case of nearly-whole-sphere
coverage, $A\approx 4\pi$, and has been widely used in cosmology to estimate
the spectrum of the cosmic microwave background radiation from spacecraft
observations. The spherical multitaper method affords easy control over the
fundamental trade-off between spectral resolution and variance, and is easily
implemented regardless of the region size, requiring neither non-linear
iteration nor large-scale matrix inversion. As a result, the method is ideally
suited for most applications in geophysics, geodesy or planetary science, where
the objective is to obtain a spatially localized estimate of the spectrum of a
signal from noisy data within a pre-selected and typically small region.

In this work, we present a study on the Single Transverse-Spin Asymmetry (STSA) in Hadronic Dijet Production. The aim of this study is to investigate the asymmetry in the production of dijets and how it changes under single transverse spin. The results obtained highlight that the STSA is measurable and significant in dijet production. This implies that there is an apparent effect of transverse spin on dijet production.

The methodology employed involves Monte Carlo simulations, where we simulate the scattering of polarized protons by unpolarized protons in the center-of-mass frame. We then use the generated data to calculate the spin-dependent cross-section asymmetry. The key idea behind this study is to reveal the extent to which a single transverse spin affects dijet production. 

The problem that this work aims to solve is to provide insight into how single transverse spin asymmetry affects hadronic dijet production. This is an essential step in advancing our understanding of the spin structure of hadrons. This work contributes to the improvement of our knowledge of the spin-dependent mechanisms and dynamics in hadronic processes. We hope that this study serves as a stepping stone for further research into spin-dependent asymmetries in other hadronic processes.

We present a novel approach to the study of brain prostheses as a dynamic system, exploring the potential for immortality of the human brain through integration with technology. Our research is motivated by the limitations of current brain-computer interfaces that suffer from signal degradation and loss over time, leading to unreliable performance. In contrast, our approach seeks to understand brain prostheses as a constantly evolving, adaptive system that can learn and improve with time.

Using a combination of physiological modeling and machine learning techniques, we demonstrate that brain prostheses can be effectively integrated with human neural networks to create a dynamic, self-improving system. Our simulations suggest that such a system has the potential to achieve near-immortality, as its ability to learn and adapt would enable it to compensate for neural degeneration and loss over time.

Our work addresses the problem of limited lifespan of existing brain-computer interfaces and explores the potential of brain prostheses as a dynamic, self-adaptive system. Our findings suggest that such a system could not only provide a solution to the problem of degeneration over time but also open up new opportunities for enhancing human capabilities and achieving immortality of the human mind.

We point out some pitfalls related to the concept of an oracle property as
used in Fan and Li (2001, 2002, 2004) which are reminiscent of the well-known
pitfalls related to Hodges' estimator. The oracle property is often a
consequence of sparsity of an estimator. We show that any estimator satisfying
a sparsity property has maximal risk that converges to the supremum of the loss
function; in particular, the maximal risk diverges to infinity whenever the
loss function is unbounded. For ease of presentation the result is set in the
framework of a linear regression model, but generalizes far beyond that
setting. In a Monte Carlo study we also assess the extent of the problem in
finite samples for the smoothly clipped absolute deviation (SCAD) estimator
introduced in Fan and Li (2001). We find that this estimator can perform rather
poorly in finite samples and that its worst-case performance relative to
maximum likelihood deteriorates with increasing sample size when the estimator
is tuned to sparsity.

In this study, we present the results of polarization-sensitive spectroscopy of charged quantum dots. The key idea behind this research is to understand the charge carrier dynamics in these materials, which is crucial for their potential applications in optoelectronics and quantum computing. To achieve this, we carried out optical spectroscopy measurements with different polarization angles on a sample of quantum dots embedded in a polymer matrix. The experimental setup allowed us to study the excitonic transitions and Coulomb interactions that occur in these structures.

Our results showed that the polarization dependence of the exciton peaks provides valuable information about the electronic structure and charge distribution in the quantum dots. Specifically, we observed a strong correlation between the polarization angle of the incident light and the emission intensity of the charged quantum dot. This allowed us to deduce the orientation of the electron and hole wave functions relative to the quantum dot surface.

The methodology employed in this study involved synthesizing quantum dots of different sizes, incorporating them into the polymer matrix, and measuring their absorption and emission spectra under different polarization angles. Our findings demonstrate the potential of polarization-sensitive spectroscopy as a powerful tool for investigating the electronic properties of quantum dots and other nanostructures. This research is motivated by the need to gain a deeper understanding of how charged quantum dots behave, which will help drive the development of new technologies in the field of photonics and beyond.

In this work, we investigate the magnetic ground state of an experimental S=1/2 Kagome antiferromagnet. The Kagome lattice is a perfect playground to study frustrated magnetism, which arises from the competition between conflicting interactions between magnetic moments. The goal is to understand how magnetic frustration affects the ground state of S=1/2 spins, which can help us understand the behavior of a range of materials from spin liquids, topological magnets to high-temperature superconductors.

We use neutron scattering to systematically study the $S=1/2$ Kagome antiferromagnet with a Heisenberg-like superexchange interaction on single crystals of $\rm ZnCu_3(OH)_6Cl_2$. We use three samples with different interlayer gap spacing to experimentally vary the degree of frustration in the system. The attempt is to map out the phase diagram, magnetic correlations and establish the role of magnetic frustration in driving exotic quantum phases of matter.

Our experimental data reveals a highly frustrated ground state with no ordering down to the lowest temperature. We observe a broad continuum of magnetic excitations, characteristic of a magnetic quantum spin liquid state. We map out the spin correlations in momentum space and establish the presence of two characteristic length scales, corresponding to the separated triangular plaquettes and the entire Kagome unit cell.

The results of our study shed light on the ways in which magnetic frustration affects the ground state of S=1/2 spins. The experimental data we’ve gathered can be used as a benchmark for numerical and analytical methods to understand the nature of a quantum spin liquid and thereby help us understand a range of materials from spin liquids to high-temperature superconductors.

A characteristic of D-brane inflation is that fluctuations in the inflaton
field can propagate at a speed significantly less than the speed of light. This
yields observable effects that are distinct from those of single-field slow
roll inflation, such as a modification of the inflationary consistency relation
and a potentially large level of non-Gaussianities. We present a numerical
algorithm that extends the inflationary flow formalism to models with general
speed of sound. For an ensemble of D-brane inflation models parameterized by
the Hubble parameter and the speed of sound as polynomial functions of the
inflaton field, we give qualitative predictions for the key inflationary
observables. We discuss various consistency relations for D-brane inflation,
and compare the qualitative shapes of the warp factors we derive from the
numerical models with analytical warp factors considered in the literature.
Finally, we derive and apply a generalized microphysical bound on the inflaton
field variation during brane inflation. While a large number of models are
consistent with current cosmological constraints, almost all of these models
violate the compactification constraint on the field range in four-dimensional
Planck units. If the field range bound is to hold, then models with a
detectable level of non-Gaussianity predict a blue scalar spectral index, and a
tensor component that is far below the detection limit of any future
experiment.

In this work, we present the analysis of the fifth outburst of IGR J11215-5952, a Supergiant Fast X-ray Transient. This periodic transient was discovered in 2004 and shows recurrent outbursts with a period of approximately 165 days. The outburst was detected and monitored by the Swift/XRT telescope in December 2020, and it lasted for about 7 days. 

The goal of this study is to analyze the timing and spectral properties of the source during the outburst and to compare them with the previous ones. We applied spectral and timing techniques to a set of Swift/XRT observations and created a light curve of the outburst.

The analysis demonstrates that the X-ray flux and spectral shape of the outburst were similar to the previous outbursts. We also found that the source shows a hard X-ray tail in the spectrum, which is consistent with previous findings.

Our results suggest that the underlying mechanism that produces the outbursts remains the same. However, the physical processes responsible for the emission and the geometry of the system are still unclear. The observations of the periodic outbursts of IGR J11215-5952 provide a unique opportunity to study the physics of Supergiant Fast X-ray Transients, and additional monitoring campaigns are needed to shed light on the nature of these sources.

In this work, we explore the properties of smooth foliations with Morse singularities, aiming to deepen our understanding of their behavior. Our motivation for this research lies in the fact that such singularities are ubiquitous in many physical and mathematical systems, and their emergence often leads to topological and dynamical transitions.

To approach this problem, we rely on the theory of singular foliations and Morse theory. We analyze the local and global structures of the singularities, identifying their types and their geometric properties. Additionally, we investigate the relationships between the singularities and the topology of the foliation, in particular its cohomology and homotopy groups.

Our main result is the classification of smooth foliations with Morse singularities up to isotopy. This classification is based on the study of the bifurcation diagrams of the singularities and their intersections, and it provides a systematic way to verify the existence and uniqueness of foliations with given properties.

Overall, this work contributes to the development of a comprehensive theory of foliations with singularities, which can be applied to various fields such as physics, engineering, and applied mathematics. It also sheds light on the intricate interplay between topology and geometry in smooth dynamical systems, and paves the way for further investigations of the dynamical behavior of singular foliations.

In this work, we tackle the problem of multiple-antenna beamforming with imperfect knowledge of the channel state information at the transmitter (CSIT), where the goal is to improve the error performance of the system while maintaining or increasing spectral efficiency. We propose a new scheme, which we call bit-interleaved coded multiple beamforming (BICMBF), that combines channel coding, bit interleaving, and beamforming to achieve this objective. 

The motivation for this research stems from the fact that in practical systems, the channel information available to the transmitter is often inaccurate or outdated due to channel estimation errors, feedback delays, or fast channel variations. Such imperfections can severely degrade the performance of beamforming systems, especially at high data rates, and therefore need to be addressed. 

The key ideas and methodology behind BICMBF involve jointly optimizing the coding rate, beamforming weights, and bit interleaving pattern to minimize the bit error rate (BER) under imperfect CSIT. We leverage tools from information theory, optimization theory, and communication theory to derive analytical expressions for the BER and spectral efficiency of the BICMBF scheme, as well as to develop efficient algorithms for practical implementation. 

Our simulation results show that BICMBF outperforms existing coded beamforming schemes for various channel conditions and SNR regimes, and can achieve substantial gains in BER while maintaining high spectral efficiency. Thus, our work contributes to the development of practical and robust multiple-antenna systems for future wireless networks.

The Grassmann manifold G_{n,p}(L) is the set of all p-dimensional planes
(through the origin) in the n-dimensional Euclidean space L^{n}, where L is
either R or C. This paper considers an unequal dimensional quantization in
which a source in G_{n,p}(L) is quantized through a code in G_{n,q}(L), where p
and q are not necessarily the same. It is different from most works in
literature where p\equiv q. The analysis for unequal dimensional quantization
is based on the volume of a metric ball in G_{n,p}(L) whose center is in
G_{n,q}(L). Our chief result is a closed-form formula for the volume of a
metric ball when the radius is sufficiently small. This volume formula holds
for Grassmann manifolds with arbitrary n, p, q and L, while previous results
pertained only to some special cases. Based on this volume formula, several
bounds are derived for the rate distortion tradeoff assuming the quantization
rate is sufficiently high. The lower and upper bounds on the distortion rate
function are asymptotically identical, and so precisely quantify the asymptotic
rate distortion tradeoff. We also show that random codes are asymptotically
optimal in the sense that they achieve the minimum achievable distortion with
probability one as n and the code rate approach infinity linearly. Finally, we
discuss some applications of the derived results to communication theory. A
geometric interpretation in the Grassmann manifold is developed for capacity
calculation of additive white Gaussian noise channel. Further, the derived
distortion rate function is beneficial to characterizing the effect of
beamforming matrix selection in multi-antenna communications.

We discuss the link between dark matter halos hosting the first PopIII stars
and the rare, massive, halos that are generally considered to host bright
quasars at high redshift z~6. The main question that we intend to answer is
whether the super-massive black holes powering these QSOs grew out from the
seeds planted by the first intermediate massive black holes created in the
universe. This question involves a dynamical range of 10^13 in mass and we
address it by combining N-body simulations of structure formation to identify
the most massive halos at z~6 with a Monte Carlo method based on linear theory
to obtain the location and formation times of the first light halos within the
whole simulation box. We show that the descendants of the first ~10^6 Msun
virialized halos do not, on average, end up in the most massive halos at z~6,
but rather live in a large variety of environments. The oldest PopIII
progenitors of the most massive halos at z~6, form instead from density peaks
that are on average one and a half standard deviations more common than the
first PopIII star formed in the volume occupied by one bright high-z QSO. The
intermediate mass black hole seeds planted by the very first PopIII stars at
z>40 can easily grow to masses m_BH>10^9.5 Msun by z=6 assuming Eddington
accretion with radiative efficiency \epsilon~0.1. Quenching of the black hole
accretion is therefore crucial to avoid an overabundance of supermassive black
holes at lower redshift. This can be obtained if the mass accretion is limited
to a fraction \eta~6*10^{-3} of the total baryon mass of the halo hosting the
black hole. The resulting high end slope of the black hole mass function at z=6
is \alpha ~ -3.7, a value within the 1\sigma error bar for the bright end slope
of the observed quasar luminosity function at z=6.

In this paper, we present optimal bounds for the Green function of half-spaces for the relativistic $\alpha$-stable process. The main goal of this work is to develop an understanding of the behavior of the Green function of the half-space for this particular type of process. 

The motivation behind this research stems from the fact that the Green function is a fundamental quantity in the study of stochastic processes, and understanding its properties is central in harmonizing various areas of mathematical analysis. In addition, the $\alpha$-stable process is a well-known stochastic process in signal processing, finance, and physics, and developing optimal bounds for its Green function has the potential to shed new light on its statistical and spatial properties. 

The key idea in our work is to construct two-sided bounds for the Green function using refined estimates for potentials of signed measures. These bounds provide a more precise estimate of the behavior of the Green function close to the boundary and reveal important new asymptotic regimes. Our methodology involves delicate analysis of positive and negative potentials of suitable measures and careful control of the constants arising in the estimates. 

Overall, our work is a significant contribution to the study of $\alpha$-stable processes and has the potential to impact a wide range of fields where these processes arise.

In this study, we investigate the effects of turbulent diffusion on the chemistry of diffuse clouds. Diffuse clouds are interstellar regions characterized by low density and low temperature, which makes their chemistry highly sensitive to environmental factors. Turbulent diffusion is known to play a key role in mixing different populations of molecules and atoms in the interstellar medium, but its impact on the chemical evolution of diffuse clouds is not well understood yet. With this goal in mind, we use a combination of numerical simulations and analytical modeling to study the diffusion of hydrogen, helium, and other species in a diffuse cloud with different turbulence levels.

Our results show that turbulent diffusion has a significant influence on the abundances of molecular and atomic species in diffuse clouds. In particular, we find that small-scale turbulence enhances the mixing of different atomic species, leading to higher levels of molecular formation and fragmentation. On the other hand, large-scale turbulence promotes the diffusion of heavy elements, such as carbon and oxygen, which can trigger complex chemical pathways involving ion-molecule reactions. Our findings suggest that turbulent diffusion is a key factor in shaping the chemical diversity of the interstellar medium and should be taken into account in models of Galactic chemical evolution.

In this work, we present the measurement of the cross sections for the processes $e^+e^-\rightarrow K^+K^-\pi^+\pi^-, K^+K^-\pi^0\pi^0,$ and $K^+K^-K^+K^-$ using initial-state radiation. These measurements were performed by analyzing the data collected by the BABAR detector at the PEP-II B-Factory accelerator at SLAC. The key idea of this work was to study the hadronic final states produced by initial-state radiation and to use this information to extract the cross sections for the aforementioned processes. The methodology involved selecting the events with two oppositely charged tracks and up to two neutral pions in the final state. These events were further categorized based on the virtual photon energy and the invariant mass of the hadronic system. 

The obtained results were in good agreement with the theoretical predictions and the previous experimental measurements. Our work represents an improvement in the precision of the cross section measurements for these processes. Moreover, it provides valuable data for the study of the strong interaction in the energy range accessible at the B-Factory. The problem our work attempts to solve is to improve our understanding of the hadronic final states produced in $e^+e^-$ collisions and to provide experimental data for the testing of theoretical models beyond perturbation theory.

We have observed the ultra-compact low-mass X-ray binary (LMXB) 1A 1246-588
with the Rossi X-ray Timing Explorer (RXTE). In this manuscript we report the
discovery of a kilohertz quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) in 1A 1246-588. The
kilohertz QPO was only detected when the source was in a soft high-flux state
reminiscent of the lower banana branch in atoll sources. Only one kilohertz QPO
peak is detected at a relatively high frequency of 1258+-2 Hz and at a single
trial significance of more than 7 sigma. Kilohertz QPOs with a higher frequency
have only been found on two occasions in 4U 0614+09. Furthermore, the frequency
is higher than that found for the lower kilohertz QPO in any source, strongly
suggesting that the QPO is the upper of the kilohertz QPO pair often found in
LMXBs. The full-width at half maximum is 25+-4 Hz, making the coherence the
highest found for an upper kilohertz QPO. From a distance estimate of ~6 kpc
from a radius expansion burst we derive that 1A 1246-588 is at a persistent
flux of ~0.2-0.3 per cent of the Eddington flux, hence 1A 1246-588 is one of
the weakest LMXBs for which a kilohertz QPO has been detected. The
root-mean-square (rms) amplitude in the 5-60 keV band is 27+-3 per cent, this
is the highest for any kilohertz QPO source so far, in line with the general
anti-correlation between source luminosity and rms amplitude of the kilohertz
QPO peak identified before. Using the X-ray spectral information we produce a
colour-colour diagram. The source behaviour in this diagram provides further
evidence for the atoll nature of the source.

In this work, we investigate the formation and properties of bubbles in self-accelerating universes. Our motivation for this research comes from the need to better understand the nature of the universe and the mechanisms by which it expands. We specifically focus on models of the universe that rely on a cosmological constant, which can drive a rapid acceleration of the universe and lead to the formation of bubbles of different sizes and shapes.

Our key idea is to use numerical simulations to study the formation and evolution of these bubbles in different cosmological scenarios. We adopt a hybrid approach that combines analytic solutions for the background cosmology with numerical simulations of the bubble formation process. This allows us to study the bubble properties, such as their sizes, shapes, and distributions, and to compare them with observations.

The main problem that our work attempts to solve is to understand the observational implications of self-accelerating universes with bubbles. We aim to identify the key features that distinguish these models from other cosmological scenarios and to test them against observations of large-scale structure, cosmic microwave background, and other cosmological observables.

In summary, our work presents a novel study of bubbles in self-accelerating universes and their observational consequences. Our approach combines analytical and numerical methods to explore the properties of bubbles and their implications for cosmology. We hope that our findings will contribute to a better understanding of the universe and its evolution.

In this work, we present Spitzer spectral line mapping observations of four supernova remnants (SNRs): 3C391, W28, IC443, and W44. The primary objective of this study is to explore the infrared emission of these remnants and to provide a more detailed understanding of the physical conditions of the gas and dust within them. The observed emission lines cover a range of excitation potentials, from [Ne II] at 12.8um to [O IV] at 25.9um.

To analyze the spectral data, we employ principal component analysis (PCA) to identify the underlying physical processes responsible for the observed line emission. PCA reveals distinct components for each SNR, with each component corresponding to specific emission lines that are likely produced by a particular physical process. Our PCA analysis indicates that the dominant physical mechanisms in the SNRs include shocks, radiative excitation, photoionization, and photon heating.

This work presents an important step forward in our understanding of the physical conditions of SNRs and highlights the power of PCA as a method to interpret complex spectral line data. The detailed information provided by this technique enables us to better quantify the energy input and output in these objects, and to constrain models of SNR evolution. The results of this study will inform future observational and theoretical efforts in the field of supernova remnants and their impact on the interstellar medium.

In this paper, we present the discovery of multiperiodicity in the newly discovered mid-late Be star V2104 Cygni. The motivation for this work stems from the fact that Be stars have long been known to exhibit complex variability, both in terms of their spectral lines and their photometric light curves. However, until recently, few tools were available to systematically detect and characterize such variability, particularly in the case of mid-late Be stars. Our work addresses this issue by utilizing Fourier analysis techniques to identify and quantify periodic variations in the spectral lines and photometry of V2104 Cygni.

Our results reveal the presence of multiple periodicities in both the photometry and spectral lines of V2104 Cygni. Specifically, we find two dominant frequencies in the photometry, along with two additional frequencies that are weaker. Similarly, spectral line variations show three dominant frequencies. Our analysis also reveals a possible connection between the spectral line and photometric variability, which is consistent with the idea of a pulsation-driven disk instability as the mechanism behind Be star variability.

In conclusion, the work presented here provides evidence for multiperiodicity in a newly discovered mid-late Be star, which has implications for our understanding of the physical mechanisms that drive variability in these objects. We hope that our results will help pave the way for future studies of Be stars and their complex variability.

A discrete tensegrity framework can be thought of as a graph in Euclidean
n-space where each edge is of one of three types: an edge with a fixed length
(bar) or an edge with an upper (cable) or lower (strut) bound on its length.
Roth and Whiteley, in their 1981 paper "Tensegrity Frameworks", showed that in
certain cases, the struts and cables can be replaced with bars when analyzing
the framework for infinitesimal rigidity. In that case we call the tensegrity
"bar equivalent". In specific, they showed that if there exists a set of
positive weights, called a positive "stress", on the edges such that the
weighted sum of the edge vectors is zero at every vertex, then the tensegrity
is bar equivalent.
  In this paper we consider an extended version of the tensegrity framework in
which the vertex set is a (possibly infinite) set of points in Euclidean
n-space and the edgeset is a compact set of unordered pairs of vertices. These
are called "continuous tensegrities". We show that if a continuous tensegrity
has a strictly positive stress, it is bar equivalent and that it has a
semipositive stress if and only if it is partially bar equivalent. We also show
that if a tensegrity is minimally bar equivalent (it is bar equivalent but
removing any open set of edges makes it no longer so), then it has a strictly
positive stress.
  In particular, we examine the case where the vertices form a rectifiable
curve and the possible motions of the curve are limited to local isometries of
it. Our methods provide an attractive proof of the following result: There is
no locally arclength preserving motion of a circle that increases any antipodal
distance without decreasing some other one.

With increasing applied current we show that the moving vortex lattice
changes its structure from a triangular one to a set of parallel vortex rows in
a pinning free superconductor. This effect originates from the change of the
shape of the vortex core due to non-equilibrium effects (similar to the
mechanism of vortex motion instability in the Larkin-Ovchinnikov theory). The
moving vortex creates a deficit of quasiparticles in front of its motion and an
excess of quasiparticles behind the core of the moving vortex. This results in
the appearance of a wake (region with suppressed order parameter) behind the
vortex which attracts other vortices resulting in an effective
direction-dependent interaction between vortices. When the vortex velocity $v$
reaches the critical value $v_c$ quasi-phase slip lines (lines with fast vortex
motion) appear which may coexist with slowly moving vortices between such
lines. Our results are found within the framework of the time-dependent
Ginzburg-Landau equations and are strictly valid when the coherence length
$\xi(T)$ is larger or comparable with the decay length $L_{in}$ of the
non-equilibrium quasiparticle distribution function. We qualitatively explain
experiments on the instability of vortex flow at low magnetic fields when the
distance between vortices $a \gg L_{in} \gg \xi (T)$. We speculate that a
similar instability of the vortex lattice should exist for $v>v_c$ even when
$a<L_{in}$.

The observed correlation of oxygen vacancies and room temperature
ferromagnetic ordering in Co doped ZnO1-o nanoparticles reported earlier (Naeem
et al Nanotechnology 17, 2675-2680) has been further explored by transport and
optical measurements. In these particles room temperature ferromagnetic
ordering had been observed to occur only after annealing in forming gas. In the
current work the optical properties have been studied by diffuse reflection
spectroscopy in the UV-Vis region and the band gap of the Co doped compositions
has been found to decrease with Co addition. Reflections minima are observed at
the energies characteristic of Co+2 d-d (tethrahedral symmetry) crystal field
transitions, further establishing the presence of Co in substitutional sites.
Electrical transport measurements on palletized samples of the nanoparticles
show that the effect of a forming gas is to strongly decrease the resistivity
with increasing Co concentration. For the air annealed and non-ferromagnetic
samples the variation in the resistivity as a function of Co content are
opposite to those observed in the particles prepared in forming gas. The
ferromagnetic samples exhibit an apparent change from insulator to metal with
increasing temperatures for T>380K and this change becomes more pronounced with
increasing Co content. The magnetic and resistive behaviors are correlated by
considering the model by Calderon et al [M. J. Calderon and S. D. Sarma, Annals
of Physics 2007 (Accepted doi: 10.1016/j.aop.2007.01.010] where the
ferromagnetism changes from being mediated by polarons in the low temperature
insulating region to being mediated by the carriers released from the weakly
bound states in the higher temperature metallic region.

Extending our earlier work on PSL(2|2), we explain how to reduce the solution
of WZNW models on general type I supergroups to those defined on the bosonic
subgroup. The new analysis covers in particular the supergroups GL(M|N) along
with several close relatives such as PSL(N|N), certain Poincare supergroups and
the series OSP(2|2N). This remarkable progress relies on the use of a special
Feigin-Fuchs type representation. In preparation for the field theory analysis,
we shall exploit a minisuperspace analogue of a free fermion construction to
deduce the spectrum of the Laplacian on type I supergroups. The latter is shown
to be non-diagonalizable. After lifting these results to the full WZNW model,
we address various issues of the field theory, including its modular invariance
and the computation of correlation functions. In agreement with previous
findings, supergroup WZNW models allow to study chiral and non-chiral aspects
of logarithmic conformal field theory within a geometric framework. We shall
briefly indicate how insights from WZNW models carry over to non-geometric
examples, such as e.g. the W(p) triplet models.

We study the formation of cosmic strings by confining a stochastic magnetic
field into flux tubes in a numerical simulation. We use overdamped evolution in
a potential that is minimized when the flux through each face in the simulation
lattice is a multiple of the fundamental flux quantum. When the typical number
of flux quanta through a correlation-length-sized region is initially about 1,
we find a string network similar to that generated by the Kibble-Zurek
mechanism. With larger initial flux, the loop distribution and the Brownian
shape of the infinite strings remain unchanged, but the fraction of length in
infinite strings is increased. A 2D slice of the network exhibits bundles of
strings pointing in the same direction, as in earlier 2D simulations. We find,
however, that strings belonging to the same bundle do not stay together in 3D
for much longer than the correlation length. As the initial flux per
correlation length is decreased, there is a point at which infinite strings
disappear, as in the Hagedorn transition.

In this work, we present evolutionary optimisation methods for template-based image registration. Image registration is a fundamental task in medical imaging, where it is crucial to align two or more images to facilitate their comparative analysis. Template-based registration is a subtype of image registration, where a reference or template image is used to match the objects or regions of interest in the target image. The use of evolutionary optimisation methods guarantees the selection of the best matching transformation from a set of transformations to achieve accurate registration. 

The proposed method combines evolutionary algorithms with image intensity-based similarity measures, such as mutual information and correlation coefficient, to achieve robust and accurate registration of medical images. The algorithm optimises a set of transformation parameters such as scaling, rotation, and translation, while considering the spatial information of the image. 

The method was tested on a dataset of Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) brain data and compared to other existing registration methods using the Dice coefficient and the Hausdorff distance as performance measures. The results demonstrated the superiority of our method in terms of both accuracy and robustness.

The motivation for this research is to provide a reliable method for accurate and robust medical image registration, which has numerous applications in medical diagnosis, treatment planning, and surgical guidance. The proposed method is a major step towards enhanced accuracy in template-based image registration and a stepping-stone for future research in this field.

In this work, we present a catalog of star cluster candidates in the nearby galaxy M33. Studying star clusters provides important insights into the formation and evolution of galaxies. However, despite the abundance of information available, many gaps still remain in our understanding of this phenomenon. Our main goal with this catalog is to improve our understanding of the clustering properties of M33's stars and to identify new potential sources of data for further research.

To achieve this, we used a photometric technique to identify star clusters in M33 based on their color and brightness. We applied this technique to archival data from the Local Group Galaxy Survey, an extensive survey of the Local Group of galaxies, which includes M33. The resulting catalog contains information on more than 2,000 star cluster candidates.

We then analyzed the clustering properties of the star clusters and found that they exhibit a power-law behavior consistent with that of other galaxy types. Our results also suggest that M33 has a relatively low number of massive star clusters compared to other galaxies of similar mass.

In conclusion, our catalog provides a valuable resource for future studies of M33 and contributes to the growing body of knowledge on the formation and evolution of star clusters and galaxies.

We compute solutions of the Lagrangian-Averaged Navier-Stokes alpha-model
(LANS) for significantly higher Reynolds numbers (up to Re 8300) than have
previously been accomplished. This allows sufficient separation of scales to
observe a Navier-Stokes (NS) inertial range followed by a 2nd LANS inertial
range. The analysis of the third-order structure function scaling supports the
predicted l^3 scaling; it corresponds to a k^(-1) scaling of the energy
spectrum. The energy spectrum itself shows a different scaling which goes as
k^1. This latter spectrum is consistent with the absence of stretching in the
sub-filter scales due to the Taylor frozen-in hypothesis employed as a closure
in the derivation of LANS. These two scalings are conjectured to coexist in
different spatial portions of the flow. The l^3 (E(k) k^(-1)) scaling is
subdominant to k^1 in the energy spectrum, but the l^3 scaling is responsible
for the direct energy cascade, as no cascade can result from motions with no
internal degrees of freedom. We verify the prediction for the size of the LANS
attractor resulting from this scaling. From this, we give a methodology either
for arriving at grid-independent solutions for LANS, or for obtaining a
formulation of a LES optimal in the context of the alpha models. The fully
converged grid-independent LANS may not be the best approximation to a direct
numerical simulation of the NS equations since the minimum error is a balance
between truncation errors and the approximation error due to using LANS instead
of the primitive equations. Furthermore, the small-scale behavior of LANS
contributes to a reduction of flux at constant energy, leading to a shallower
energy spectrum for large alpha. These small-scale features, do not preclude
LANS to reproduce correctly the intermittency properties of high Re flow.

Using a semi-analytical model for galaxy formation, combined with a large
N-body simulation, we investigate the origin of the dichotomy among early-type
galaxies. We find that boxy galaxies originate from mergers with a progenitor
mass ratio $n < 2$ and with a combined cold gas mass fraction $F_{\rm cold} <
0.1$. Our model accurately reproduces the observed fraction of boxy systems as
a function of luminosity and halo mass, for both central galaxies and
satellites. After correcting for the stellar mass dependence, the properties of
the last major merger of early-type galaxies are independent of their halo
mass. This provides theoretical support for the conjecture of Pasquali et al
(2007) that the stellar mass of an early-type galaxy is the main parameter that
governs its isophotal shape. We argue that the observed dichotomy of early-type
galaxies has a natural explanation within hierarchical structure formation, and
does not require AGN feedback. Rather, we argue that it owes to the fact that
more massive systems (i) have more massive progenitors, (ii) assemble later,
and (iii) have a larger fraction of early-type progenitors. Each of these three
trends causes the cold gas mass fraction of the progenitors of more massive
early-types to be lower, so that their last major merger was dryer. Finally,
our model predicts that (i) less than 10 percent of all early-type galaxies
form in major mergers that involve two early-type progenitors, (ii) more than
95 percent of all boxy early-type galaxies with $M_* < 2 \times 10^{10} h^{-1}
\Msun$ are satellite galaxies, and (iii) about 70 percent of all low mass
early-types do not form a supermassive black hole binary at their last major
merger. The latter may help to explain why low mass early-types have central
cusps, while their massive counterparts have cores.

In this paper, we present the results of radiative transfer modeling of the dust distribution in a sample of edge-on galaxies, performed using V and K'-band images obtained with the Hubble Space Telescope. The main goal of this study was to investigate the spatial distribution and properties of the dust in these galaxies, and to test the accuracy of radiative transfer modeling in reproducing the observed data. Our methodology involves fitting a radiative transfer model to the observed images, with the input parameters based on physical assumptions about the distribution and properties of the dust, including its optical properties, size distribution, and location within the galaxy. The results show that the model provides a good fit to the data, and that the dust distribution is primarily determined by the location and orientation of the galactic disk relative to the observer. We also find evidence for clumpiness in the dust distribution, and suggest that this may be related to the formation and evolution of the galaxies. Overall, this work provides new insights into the dust content and distribution of edge-on galaxies, and offers a valuable tool for future studies of these intriguing objects.

Mechanistic home range models are important tools in modeling animal dynamics
in spatially-complex environments. We introduce a class of stochastic models
for animal movement in a habitat of varying preference. Such models interpolate
between spatially-implicit resource selection analysis (RSA) and
advection-diffusion models, possessing these two models as limiting cases. We
find a closed-form solution for the steady-state (equilibrium) probability
distribution u* using a factorization of the redistribution operator into
symmetric and diagonal parts. How space use is controlled by the preference
function w then depends on the characteristic width of the redistribution
kernel: when w changes rapidly compared to this width, u* ~ w, whereas on
global scales large compared to this width, u* ~ w^2. We analyse the behavior
at discontinuities in w which occur at habitat type boundaries. We simulate the
dynamics of space use given two-dimensional prey-availability data and explore
the effect of the redistribution kernel width. Our factorization allows such
numerical simulations to be done extremely fast; we expect this to aid the
computationally-intensive task of model parameter fitting and inverse modeling.

In this work, we explore the theory of unequal dimensional small balls and quantization on Grassmann Manifolds. The motivation for this research is rooted in identifying a novel approach to improve our understanding of the quantization process of low-dimensional manifolds, which are critically important in many areas of modern physics and mathematics. The problem we attempt to solve is to develop a mathematical framework that enables the quantization of these manifolds in a way that guarantees the preservation of the essential geometric properties of the manifold and produces a well-behaved quantized version of the manifold.

Our approach relies on the algebraic and geometric aspects of Grassmann Manifolds, which provide a fertile ground for the development of a quantization framework that is better adapted to low-dimensional manifolds. Specifically, we introduce a novel concept of unequal dimensional small balls on Grassmann Manifolds, which allows us to develop an explicit formula for quantization that respects the essential properties of the manifold. Our key idea is to use the decomposition of the manifold into different subspaces to construct a coherent and well-behaved quantization procedure.

The methodology behind our work involves extensive mathematical analysis, relying on advanced tools from algebraic and differential geometry. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach by applying it to specific examples of low-dimensional manifolds, including the real projective plane and the Stiefel manifold. Our findings have important implications for understanding the quantization process more generally and provide a promising avenue for future research.

In this study, we investigate the crystalline silicates and dust processing in protoplanetary disks of the Taurus young cluster in order to better understand the formation of our own solar system. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that silicates are key building blocks in the formation of planets, and the crystalline nature of these silicates plays a crucial role in determining the chemical and physical properties of the dust. Using observations from the Infrared Spectrograph on the Spitzer Space Telescope, we analyze the spectra of 53 young stars and determine the mineralogical composition of the disks. Our results show a clear correlation between the spectral features of crystalline silicates and the stellar age, with younger disks having a higher percentage of crystalline silicates than older disks. We also observe that processed dust grains are present in the inner regions of the disks but are absent in the outer regions. This suggests that the dust processing occurs early on in the disk evolution and highlights the importance of understanding the role of dust processing in planet formation. Overall, our study provides valuable insight into the formation of protoplanetary disks and their evolution.

In this work, we present a statistical analysis of supersonic isothermal turbulence, motivated by the need to understand the properties of astrophysical fluids such as interstellar medium and molecular clouds. Turbulence is a ubiquitous feature of astrophysical environments and plays an important role in various dynamical processes, including star formation and galactic dynamics. Our main focus is on the statistical properties of isothermal turbulence, where the energy injection is balanced by cooling due to radiative losses. 

We employ numerical simulations of supersonic isothermal turbulence and statistical analysis tools such as structure functions and probability distribution functions (PDFs) to study the properties of the fluid. Our primary objective is to understand how the statistics of turbulence depend on the Mach number, the cooling rate, and the driving scale. We find that the PDFs of the density and velocity fluctuations show non-Gaussian behavior, with strong deviations from the log-normal distribution expected for subsonic turbulence. 

This work addresses a significant problem in astrophysics, namely how to relate the statistical properties of turbulence to the underlying physical processes that drive it. Our results provide insights into the role of cooling in shaping the statistical properties of supersonic turbulence, which have implications for our understanding of how star formation and other astrophysical processes occur in the interstellar medium.

Our work focuses on investigating the process of star formation at very low metallicity in order to better understand the formation of the early Universe. The main problem we aim to solve is the lack of knowledge regarding the formation of stars in extremely metal-poor conditions, which affected the early Universe. 

In order to approach this problem, we conducted simulations of gas clouds with different metallicities and densities, and analyzed the chemistry and cooling mechanisms in play during star formation. Our results indicate that at low densities, gas cooling occurs primarily through molecular hydrogen rather than metal lines, and the ionization rates of carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen play a crucial role in determining the nature of the gas. Additionally, we found that the rate of cooling by molecular hydrogen is significantly diminished in metal-poor conditions, which suggests that the formation of low-mass stars may be inhibited in the early Universe. 

We are motivated by the desire to better understand the origins of star formation in the Universe, and by the potential applications of our findings to the fields of astrophysics and cosmology. Our work serves as a foundation for future studies of star formation in the early Universe, and may help shed light on the formation of the first galaxies.

We address the following question: When a randomly chosen regular bipartite
multi--graph is drawn in the plane in the ``standard way'', what is the
distribution of its maximum size planar matching (set of non--crossing disjoint
edges) and maximum size planar subgraph (set of non--crossing edges which may
share endpoints)? The problem is a generalization of the Longest Increasing
Sequence (LIS) problem (also called Ulam's problem). We present combinatorial
identities which relate the number of r-regular bipartite multi--graphs with
maximum planar matching (maximum planar subgraph) of at most d edges to a
signed sum of restricted lattice walks in Z^d, and to the number of pairs of
standard Young tableaux of the same shape and with a ``descend--type''
property. Our results are obtained via generalizations of two combinatorial
proofs through which Gessel's identity can be obtained (an identity that is
crucial in the derivation of a bivariate generating function associated to the
distribution of LISs, and key to the analytic attack on Ulam's problem).
  We also initiate the study of pattern avoidance in bipartite multigraphs and
derive a generalized Gessel identity for the number of bipartite 2-regular
multigraphs avoiding a specific (monotone) pattern.

In this work, we investigate the holographic confinement/deconfinement phase transitions of AdS/QCD in curved spaces. Our study is motivated by the possibility that the interaction between gravity and QCD, the theory of strong nuclear interactions, will play a crucial role in the understanding of the behavior of strong interactions at low energies. To achieve this, we focus on a holographic description of QCD in which the confinement/deconfinement phase transitions take place in the context of an AdS geometry.

Our study reveals that the confinement/deconfinement phase transition is sensitive to the geometry of the AdS space. We use the gauge/gravity duality to map QCD to the classical gravitational theory and investigate the behavior of the AdS/QCD system in a black hole geometry background. Our findings suggest that the confinement phase can be suppressed in certain regions of the parameter space. In particular, a high temperature black hole geometry can cause a transition from the confined phase to a deconfined phase.

The key idea behind this work is to investigate the effect of curved spaces on the confinement/deconfinement phase transitions of AdS/QCD. Our research employs a combination of theoretical and numerical techniques, such as the holographic renormalization procedure and the computation of the free energy. Our study addresses the problem of understanding the behavior of strong interactions at low energies in the context of gravitational theories and provides a new framework for investigating the AdS/QCD system in curved backgrounds.

We examine the relationship between little Higgs and 5d composite models with
identical symmetry structures. By performing an "extreme" deconstruction, one
can reduce any warped composite model to a little Higgs theory on a handful of
sites. This allows us to use 4d intuition and the powerful constraints of
nonlinear sigma models to elucidate obscure points in the original setup. We
find that the finiteness of the Higgs potential in 5d is due to the same
collective symmetry breaking as in the little Higgs. We compare a 4d and a 5d
model with the same symmetry to the data. Reviewing the constraints on models
related to the Minimal Composite Higgs (hep-ph/0412089), we see that it has
difficulty in producing acceptable values for S, T, and m_{top} simultaneously.
By contrast, in a global analysis, the Minimal Moose with custodial symmetry is
viable in a large region of its parameter space and suffers from no numeric
tunings. We conjecture that this result is generic for 4d and 5d models with
identical symmetries. The data will less strongly constrain the little theory.

In this work, we present a new method for measuring electric fields produced by surface contaminants using neutral atoms. The motivation for this research stems from the need to develop techniques for detecting and characterizing surface contaminants, which can have significant impacts on the performance of electronic devices. The key idea behind our method is to use a beam of neutral atoms, such as helium, to probe the electric field near the surface of a sample. We measure the deflection of the beam caused by the surface electric field and use this information to calculate the strength and direction of the electric field. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method by measuring the electric field produced by various surface contaminants, including water and hydrocarbons, on different types of surfaces, such as silicon and gold. Our results show that our method can detect electric fields as low as tens of millivolts per centimeter and has a spatial resolution of a few micrometers. This work has the potential to contribute to the development of more effective techniques for surface contamination detection and characterization, which could have important implications for a broad range of applications, including microelectronics, biotechnology, and environmental monitoring.

In this study, we report the direct imaging of the lateral movements of AMPA receptors within synapses. The steady-state availability of AMPA receptors within the synaptic cleft is a critical determinant of synaptic strength and, ultimately, synaptic plasticity. However, the mechanisms underlying the movement of AMPA receptors within the synaptic membrane remain poorly understood, which limits our comprehensive understanding of synapse function.

To investigate this phenomenon, we employed high-resolution imaging techniques, including total internal reflection fluorescence microscopy and single-particle tracking. By imaging fluorescently tagged AMPA receptors in live hippocampal neurons, we achieved a direct visualization of their movement at high temporal and spatial resolution. Our data reveal that a significant fraction of AMPA receptors diffuse laterally within the synaptic membrane and that this lateral diffusion is regulated by the synaptic actin cytoskeleton.

Our findings provide novel insights into the mechanisms governing AMPA receptor movement at synapses and shed light on how this movement modulates synaptic function. By understanding the dynamic behavior of AMPA receptors, we can elucidate the fundamental processes regulating synaptic transmission and long-term synaptic plasticity, thereby informing the development of novel therapeutic interventions for neurological disorders that disrupt synaptic transmission.

We present the results of our investigation of the intermediate-age star
cluster BS 90, located in the vicinity of the HII region N 66 in the SMC,
observed with HST/ACS. The high-resolution data provide a unique opportunity
for a very detailed photometric study performed on one of the rare
intermediate-age rich SMC clusters. The complete set of observations is
centered on the association NGC 346 and contains almost 100,000 stars down to V
~28 mag. In this study we focus on the northern part of the region, which
covers almost the whole stellar content of BS 90. We construct its stellar
surface density profile and derive structural parameters. Isochrone fits on the
CMD of the cluster results in an age of about 4.5 Gyr. The luminosity function
is constructed and the present-day mass function of BS 90 has been obtained
using the mass-luminosity relation, derived from the isochrone models. We found
a slope between -1.30 and -0.95, comparable or somewhat shallower than a
typical Salpeter IMF. Examination of the radial dependence of the mass function
shows a steeper slope at larger radial distances, indicating mass segregation
in the cluster. The derived half-mass relaxation time of 0.95 Gyr suggests that
the cluster is mass segregated due to its dynamical evolution. From the
isochrone model fits we derive a metallicity for BS 90 of [Fe/H]=-0.72, which
adds an important point to the age-metallicity relation of the SMC. We discuss
our findings on this relation in comparison to other SMC clusters.

G156.2+5.7 is a large supernova remnant (SNR) first discovered in the ROSAT
X-ray survey but radio observations are rare because of its low surface
brightness and large size. Radio continuum and polarization images of the SNR
G156.2+5.7 at lambda 6 cm are presented for the first time to be discussed in
the context with available data to investigate its physical properties. We
obtained an integrated flux density for G156.2+5.7 of S6cm= 2.5+-0.5Jy. The
spectral index of the integrated emission is alpha=-0.48+-0.08 between lambda
74 cm and 6 cm excluding compact radio sources. We also obtained a spectral
index map, which shows little variation between the shells and the central area
of the SNR. This is consistent with the constant integrated flux density
spectrum. Highly polarized radio emission has been detected from the SNR shell,
but also from a central patch, which probably originates in the front part of
the spherical SNR shell. We derived the distribution of rotation measure from
polarization data at 11 cm and 6 cm and found RM gradients of opposite
direction in the SNR shell. The SNR G156.2+5.7 is unusual by its exceptionally
high X-ray brightness and very low surface-brightness in the radio range. The
magnetic field is very well ordered along the shell periphery as expected for a
compressed ambient magnetic field. A toroidal magnetic field component is
indicated by the RM distribution. (abridged)

We present the discovery of 11 new T dwarfs, found during the course of a
photometric survey for mid-to-late T dwarfs in the 2MASS Point Source Catalog
and from a proper motion selected sample of ultracool dwarfs in the 2MASS
Working Database. Using the NASA Infrared Telescope Facility SpeX spectrograph,
we obtained low-resolution (R~150) spectroscopy, allowing us to derive
near-infrared spectral types of T2-T8. One of these new T dwarfs, 2MASS
J13243559+6358284, was also discovered independently by Metchev et al., in
prep. This object is spectroscopically peculiar and possibly a binary and/or
very young (<300 Myr). We specifically attempted to model the spectrum of this
source as a composite binary to reproduce its peculiar spectral
characteristics. The latest-type object in our sample is a T8 dwarf, 2MASS
J07290002-3954043, now one of the four latest-type T dwarfs known. All 11 T
dwarfs are nearby given their spectrophotometric distance estimates, with 1 T
dwarf within 10 pc and 8 additional T dwarfs within 25 pc, if single. These new
additions increase the 25 pc census of T dwarfs by ~14%. Their proximity offers
an excellent opportunity to probe for companions at closer separations than are
possible for more distant T dwarfs.

We report on transcritical bifurcations of periodic orbits in non-integrable
two-dimensional Hamiltonian systems. We discuss their existence criteria and
some of their properties using a recent mathematical description of
transcritical bifurcations in families of symplectic maps. We then present
numerical examples of transcritical bifurcations in a class of generalized
H\'enon-Heiles Hamiltonians and illustrate their stabilities and unfoldings
under various perturbations of the Hamiltonians. We demonstrate that for
Hamiltonians containing straight-line librating orbits, the transcritical
bifurcation of these orbits is the typical case which occurs also in the
absence of any discrete symmetries, while their isochronous pitchfork
bifurcation is an exception. We determine the normal forms of both types of
bifurcations and derive the uniform approximation required to include
transcritically bifurcating orbits in the semiclassical trace formula for the
density of states of the quantum Hamiltonian. We compute the coarse-grained
density of states in a specific example both semiclassically and quantum
mechanically and find excellent agreement of the results.

It has been shown that the luminosity of AGNs and the size of their broad
line region obey a simple relation of the type R=a L^g, from faint Seyfert
nuclei to bright quasars, allowing single-epoch determination of the central
black hole mass M=b L^g D^2 from their luminosity L and width of H_beta
emission line. Adopting this mass determination for cosmological studies
requires the extrapolation to high z and L of a relation whose calibration
relies so far on reverberation mapping measurements performed for L<10^46 erg/s
and z<0.4. We initiated a campaign for the monitoring of a few luminous,
intermediate z quasars whose apparent magnitude V<15.7 allows observations with
a 1.8m telescope, aimed at proving that emission lines vary and respond to
continuum variations even for luminosities >10^47 erg/s, and determining
eventually their M_BH from reverberation mapping. We have repeatedly performed
simultaneous observations of quasars and reference stars to determine relative
variability of continuum and emission lines. We describe the observations and
methods of analysis. For the quasars PG1634+706 and PG1247+268 we obtain
light-curves respectively for CIII], MgII and for CIV, CIII] emission lines
with the relevant continua. During 3.2 years of observation, in the former case
no continuum variability has been detected and the evidence for line
variability is marginal, while in the latter case both continuum and line
variability are detected with high significance and the line variations appear
correlated with continuum variations. The detection of the emission line
variability in a quasar with L~10^47 erg/s encourages the prosecution of the
campaign which should provide a black hole mass estimate in other 5-6 years,
constraining the M_BH-L relation in a poorly explored range of luminosity.

The usefulness of the Relativistic Schr\"odinger Theory (RST) is studied in
the field of atomic physics. As a concrete demonstration, the positronium
groundstate is considered in great detail; especially the groundstate energy
$E_0$ is worked out in the non-relativistic approximation and under neglection
of the magnetic interactions between the positron and the electron. The
corresponding RST prediction $(E_0\simeq -6,48 [eV])$ misses the analogous
conventional Schr\"odinger result $(E_0\simeq -6,80 [eV])$ but is closer to the
latter than the corresponding Hartree approximation $(-2,65 [eV])$. The missing
binding energy of $6,80-6,48=0,32 [eV]$ can be attributed to the approximative
use of an SO(3) symmetric interaction potential which in RST, however, is
actually only SO(2) invariant against rotations around the z-axis. It is
expected that, with the correct use of an anisotropic interaction potential due
to the SO(2) symmetry, the RST predictions will come even closer to the
conventional Schr\"odinger result, where however the mathematical structure of
RST relies on exotic (i.e. double-valued) wave functions and on the
corresponding unconventional interaction potentials (e.g. Struve-Neumann
potential).

In this work, we explore the astrophysical and cosmological implications of large volume string compactifications. The key idea underlying this research is that string theory, a theoretical framework that aims to unify all known physical laws, predicts the existence of extra dimensions beyond the three spatial and one temporal dimension we observe in our everyday lives. These extra dimensions are typically compactified or curled up in a tiny space, making them invisible to our senses.

We employ a modern mathematical tool called the Large Volume Scenario (LVS) to construct compactifications of string theory that give rise to a large volume of space in the extra dimensions. This scenario has far-reaching implications for cosmology and astrophysics, as it opens up the possibility of extra dimensions playing a role in phenomena such as dark matter and inflation.

Our analysis focuses on the impact of large volume string compactifications on the evolution of the Universe, the formation of galaxies, and the properties of dark matter. Specifically, we investigate the effects of the compactification on the spectrum of primordial gravitational waves, the abundance of dark matter particles, and the formation of baryonic structures.

We find that the LVS generates a rich phenomenology that can be tested with current and future observations, including the cosmic microwave background radiation, large scale structure surveys, and particle accelerator experiments. Our research provides a new avenue for exploring the interplay between fundamental physics and cosmology, and may ultimately lead to the discovery of new physical laws and phenomena.

In this third and final paper of a series, elastic properties of numerically
simulated isotropic packings of spherical beads assembled by different
procedures and subjected to a varying confining pressure P are investigated. In
addition P, which determines the stiffness of contacts by Hertz's law, elastic
moduli are chiefly sensitive to the coordination number, the possible values of
which are not necessarily correlated with the density. Comparisons of numerical
and experimental results for glass beads in the 10kPa-10MPa range reveal
similar differences between dry samples compacted by vibrations and lubricated
packings. The greater stiffness of the latter, in spite of their lower density,
can hence be attributed to a larger coordination number. Voigt and Reuss bounds
bracket bulk modulus B accurately, but simple estimation schemes fail for shear
modulus G, especially in poorly coordinated configurations under low P.
Tenuous, fragile networks respond differently to changes in load direction, as
compared to load intensity. The shear modulus, in poorly coordinated packings,
tends to vary proportionally to the degree of force indeterminacy per unit
volume. The elastic range extends to small strain intervals, in agreement with
experimental observations. The origins of nonelastic response are discussed. We
conclude that elastic moduli provide access to mechanically important
information about coordination numbers, which escape direct measurement
techniques, and indicate further perspectives.

A wireless network in which packets are broadcast to a group of receivers
through use of a random access protocol is considered in this work. The
relation to previous work on networks of interacting queues is discussed and
subsequently, the stability and throughput regions of the system are analyzed
and presented. A simple network of two source nodes and two destination nodes
is considered first. The broadcast service process is analyzed assuming a
channel that allows for packet capture and multipacket reception. In this small
network, the stability and throughput regions are observed to coincide. The
same problem for a network with N sources and M destinations is considered
next. The channel model is simplified in that multipacket reception is no
longer permitted. Bounds on the stability region are developed using the
concept of stability rank and the throughput region of the system is compared
to the bounds. Our results show that as the number of destination nodes
increases, the stability and throughput regions diminish. Additionally, a
previous conjecture that the stability and throughput regions coincide for a
network of arbitrarily many sources is supported for a broadcast scenario by
the results presented in this work.

In our study, we present some new experimental photonic flame effect features that could contribute to advances in fire research. We aimed to explore the potential of using photonic flames for visualizing and analyzing the behavior of fire. We developed a novel experimental setup that uses a custom-built burner device to produce photonic flames with various optical characteristics. Our methodology involved using high-speed cameras and image processing software to capture and analyze the flames' properties, such as shape, color, and brightness.
