We also conducted experiments to investigate the effects of various parameters on the flames' characteristics, such as fuel composition, flow rate, and burner geometry. 

Our results indicate that photonic flames can provide valuable insights into the fundamental properties and dynamics of fire. We observed that photonic flames have unique visual properties that could potentially enhance the understanding of complex fire phenomena. Moreover, our experiments revealed that the optical characteristics of photonic flames are affected by various factors that influence their shape and color, such as fuel type, flow rate, and burner geometry. Overall, we believe that our study provides a new direction for fire research and could lead to the development of more advanced flame visualization techniques.

We study $B \to K^* \rho$ modes that are analogues of the much studied $B\to
K \pi$ modes with B decaying to two vector mesons instead of pseudoscalar
mesons, using topological amplitudes in the quark diagram approach. We show how
$B \to K^*\rho$ modes can be used to obtain many more observables than those
for $B \to K \pi$ modes, even though the quark level subprocesses of both modes
are exactly the same. All the theoretical parameters (except for the weak phase
$\gamma$), such as the magnitudes of the topological amplitudes and their
strong phases, can be determined in terms of the observables without any
model-dependent assumption. We demonstrate how $B\to K^*\rho$ can also be used
to verify if there exist any relations between theoretical parameters, such as
the hierarchy relations between the topological amplitudes and possible
relations between the strong phases. Conversely, if there exist reliable
theoretical estimates of amplitudes and strong phases, the presence of New
physics could be probed. We show that if the tree and color-supressed tree are
related to the electroweak penguins and color-supressed electroweak penguins,
it is not only possible to verify the validity of such relations but also to
have a clean measurement of New Physics parameters. We also present a numerical
study to examine which of the observables are more sensitive to New Physics.

In this study, we examined the criteria for selecting target events for Planetary Microlensing Follow-Up Observation. Planetary Microlensing is a technique used to detect exoplanets by measuring the small gravitational deflection of background stars' light caused by the presence of planets. Follow-up observations are carried out to confirm the presence of exoplanets and gather information on their properties. Our main objective was to identify effective selection criteria to maximize the chances of detecting exoplanets and gathering relevant scientific data.

We analyzed a sample of 152 planetary microlensing events and established various selection criteria based on the duration, mass, and distance of the lensing event. We used statistical analysis to identify events that are more likely to contain planets based on past observations and theoretical predictions. We also considered the feasibility of the follow-up observations based on factors such as the location of the event, weather conditions, and observational constraints.

Our results indicate that the most effective criteria to select target events for follow-up observation is based on their duration and distance from Earth. By applying these criteria, we achieved a success rate of over 60% in detecting exoplanets. Our findings contribute to improving the efficiency and effectiveness of planetary microlensing follow-up observations and provide valuable insights for astronomers seeking to discover and characterize exoplanets.

A mesh is a graph that divides physical space into regularly-shaped regions.
Meshes computations form the basis of many applications, e.g. finite-element
methods, image rendering, and collision detection. In one important mesh
primitive, called a mesh update, each mesh vertex stores a value and repeatedly
updates this value based on the values stored in all neighboring vertices. The
performance of a mesh update depends on the layout of the mesh in memory.
  This paper shows how to find a memory layout that guarantees that the mesh
update has asymptotically optimal memory performance for any set of memory
parameters. Such a memory layout is called cache-oblivious. Formally, for a
$d$-dimensional mesh $G$, block size $B$, and cache size $M$ (where
$M=\Omega(B^d)$), the mesh update of $G$ uses $O(1+|G|/B)$ memory transfers.
The paper also shows how the mesh-update performance degrades for smaller
caches, where $M=o(B^d)$.
  The paper then gives two algorithms for finding cache-oblivious mesh layouts.
The first layout algorithm runs in time $O(|G|\log^2|G|)$ both in expectation
and with high probability on a RAM. It uses $O(1+|G|\log^2(|G|/M)/B)$ memory
transfers in expectation and $O(1+(|G|/B)(\log^2(|G|/M) + \log|G|))$ memory
transfers with high probability in the cache-oblivious and disk-access machine
(DAM) models. The layout is obtained by finding a fully balanced decomposition
tree of $G$ and then performing an in-order traversal of the leaves of the
tree. The second algorithm runs faster by almost a $\log|G|/\log\log|G|$ factor
in all three memory models, both in expectation and with high probability. The
layout obtained by finding a relax-balanced decomposition tree of $G$ and then
performing an in-order traversal of the leaves of the tree.

In this research, we aim to test different models of line-number evolution through the study of stochastic absorption of background light by intergalactic neutral hydrogen. The cosmic flux decrement is an important tool for detecting the absorbed light from distant sources due to the presence of intervening neutral hydrogen absorbers along the line of sight. The key idea behind this work is to examine how the evolution of the neutral hydrogen population has impacted the observed cosmic flux decrement over time. We employ a statistical analysis to investigate the behavior of different models, including the constant line-density model and the evolving line-density model, within the context of a cosmological simulation framework. Our results demonstrate that the evolving line-density model provides a more accurate description of the observed cosmic flux decrement, indicating a non-uniform evolution of the neutral hydrogen population over cosmic time. Our methodology involves generating artificial spectra of light from background sources and simulating their stochastic absorption by neutral hydrogen, followed by comparisons with observed spectra. The motivation for this research stems from its potential to shed light on the processes that influenced the evolution of neutral hydrogen in the early universe and their impact on the observable properties of distant sources.

We discuss how, in a Universe restricted to the causal region connected to
the observer, General Relativity implies the quantum nature of physical
phenomena and directly leads to a string theory scenario, whose dynamics is
ruled by a functional that weights all configurations according to their
entropy. The most favoured configurations are those of minimal entropy. Along
this class of vacua a four-dimensional space-time is automatically selected;
when, at large volume, a description of space-time in terms of classical
geometry can be recovered, the entropy-weighted sum reduces to the ordinary
Feynman's path integral. What arises is a highly predictive scenario,
phenomenologically compatible with the experimental observations and
measurements, in which everything is determined in terms of the fundamental
constants and the age of the Universe, with no room for freely-adjustable
parameters. We discuss how this leads to the known spectrum of particles and
interactions. Besides the computation of masses and couplings, CKM matrix
elements, cosmological constant, expansion parameters of the Universe etc...,
all resulting, within the degree of the approximation we used, in agreement
with the experimental observations, we also discuss how this scenario passes
the tests provided by cosmology and the constraints imposed by the physics of
the primordial Universe.

In this study, we address the issue of quantile and probability curves crossing each other, which can lead to erroneous statistical conclusions. The main objective of our work is to propose a novel approach that ensures the monotonicity of both curves, without crossing. 

The motivation behind this work is to provide a solution to the inconsistencies that arise when quantile and probability curves cross each other. These curves are used in various fields, such as finance, healthcare, and environmental studies, to evaluate the distribution of data and estimate probabilities. However, the crossing of these curves can lead to biased estimates and incorrect inferences.

To achieve our goal, we introduce a constrained optimization problem that takes into account the statistical properties of both curves. We show that the proposed method preserves the order of the data and results in smoother and more accurate curves compared to existing methods.

Our results demonstrate that the proposed approach is effective in solving the problem of crossing quantile and probability curves. The smoothed curves obtained using our technique have better goodness-of-fit statistics compared to existing methods. Furthermore, our approach is computationally efficient and can be applied to large datasets.

Overall, our study provides a robust method for generating quantile and probability curves that do not cross, providing more accurate and reliable estimates for statistical analysis.

We investigate whether the floating phase (where the correlation length is
infinite and the spin-spin correlation decays algebraically with distance)
exists in the temperature($T$) - frustration parameter ($\kappa$) phase diagram
of 2D ANNNI model. To identify this phase, we look for the region where (i)
finite size effect is prominent and (ii) some relevant physical quantity
changes somewhat sharply and this change becomes sharper as the system size
increases. For $\kappa < 0.5 $, the low temperature phase is ferromagnetic and
we study energy and magnetization. For $\kappa > 0.5 $, the low temperature
phase is antiphase and we study energy, layer magnetization, length of domain
walls running along the direction of frustration, number of domain-intercepts
that are of length 2 along the direction of frustration, and the number of
domain walls that do not touch the upper and/or lower boundary. In agreement
with some previous studies, our final conclusion is that, the floating phase
exists, if at all, only along a line.

In this paper, we investigate the behavior of ultra-relativistic shocks in the presence of vorticity. We use the geometrical shock dynamics framework to describe the evolution of the shock wave and the coupling of vorticity with the shock structure. Our methodology is based on a numerical simulation of the leptonic flows within the shocked plasma and their interaction with vorticity. The problem we attempt to solve is to understand the interplay between the shock and the vorticity and its impact on the acceleration of cosmic rays. We also aim to derive the conditions under which vorticity can affect shock dynamics at ultra-relativistic speeds.

Our motivation for this research is to improve our understanding of the high-energy astrophysical phenomena that occur in our universe. Ultra-relativistic shocks are present in many cosmic objects such as supernovae, gamma-ray bursts and active galactic nuclei. The interaction between the shock wave and the magnetic fields, as well as the presence of vorticity, can significantly affect particle acceleration and energy dissipation. Therefore, our work aims to provide a better theoretical framework for interpreting observational data and providing insights into the underlying physical mechanisms that govern these astrophysical phenomena.

In this work, we present a Weighted Essentially Non-Oscillatory (WENO) scheme for studying the growth of ionized regions during the reionization epoch. The motivation for this research comes from the need to accurately predict the formation and evolution of cosmic structures at a crucial epoch in the history of the universe. Our key idea is to develop a numerical algorithm that accurately and efficiently models the complex ionization processes that occur during reionization.

Our methodology involves three main steps. First, we derive a set of governing equations that describe the ionization and recombination processes. Second, we implement a WENO scheme to solve these equations on a numerical grid. Third, we validate our method by comparing results with existing simulation data and analytic solutions. 

Our results show that our WENO algorithm accurately captures the complex ionization processes during reionization, including the formation of ionized bubbles, the growth of ionized regions, and the effects of clumping and scattering. Additionally, our method is computationally efficient, making it a valuable tool for studying large-scale structures in the early universe. 

In conclusion, our work presents a novel WENO algorithm for simulating the growth of ionized regions during the reionization epoch. Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach for studying the important astrophysical processes that occur during reionization, and we believe that our method will be a valuable tool for future cosmological studies.

Nowadays, to achieve competitive advantage, the industrial companies are
considering that success is sustained to great product development. That is to
manage the product throughout its entire lifecycle. Achieving this goal
requires a tight collaboration between actors from a wide variety of domains,
using different software tools producing various product data types and
formats. The actors' collaboration is mainly based on the exchange /share
product information. The representation of the actors' viewpoints is the
underlying requirement of the collaborative product development. The multiple
viewpoints approach was designed to provide an organizational framework
following the actors' perspectives in the collaboration, and their
relationships. The approach acknowledges the inevitability of multiple
integration of product information as different views, promotes gathering of
actors' interest, and encourages retrieved adequate information while providing
support for integration through PLM and/or SCM collaboration. In this paper, a
multiple viewpoints representation is proposed. The product, process,
organization information models are discussed. A series of issues referring to
the viewpoints representation are discussed in detail. Based on XML standard,
taking electrical connector as an example, an application case of part of
product information modeling is stated.

The notion of {\em $p$-adic multiresolution analysis (MRA)} is introduced. We
discuss a ``natural'' refinement equation whose solution (a refinable function)
is the characteristic function of the unit disc. This equation reflects the
fact that the characteristic function of the unit disc is a sum of $p$
characteristic functions of mutually disjoint discs of radius $p^{-1}$. This
refinement equation generates a MRA. The case $p=2$ is studied in detail. Our
MRA is a 2-adic analog of the real Haar MRA. But in contrast to the real
setting, the refinable function generating our Haar MRA is 1-periodic, which
never holds for real refinable functions. This fact implies that there exist
infinity many different 2-adic orthonormal wavelet bases in ${\cL}^2(\bQ_2)$
generated by the same Haar MRA. All of these bases are described. We also
constructed multidimensional 2-adic Haar orthonormal bases for
${\cL}^2(\bQ_2^n)$ by means of the tensor product of one-dimensional MRAs. A
criterion for a multidimensional $p$-adic wavelet to be an eigenfunction for a
pseudo-differential operator is derived. We proved also that these wavelets are
eigenfunctions of the Taibleson multidimensional fractional operator. These
facts create the necessary prerequisites for intensive using our bases in
applications.

In this research work, we investigated the dislocation dynamics in a crystal lattice using the Peierls-Nabarro model. The purpose of this work is to better understand how dislocations interact with the lattice structure and how this interaction contributes to the mechanical properties of materials. We used molecular dynamics simulations to study the behavior of dislocations in the lattice and analyzed the energy barriers that prevent their motion by taking into account the Peierls stress and the collective movement of atoms along the slip plane.

Our results confirmed that Peierls-Nabarro model-based simulations can correctly predict dislocation behaviors in a crystal lattice and provide new insights into how defects evolve over time. We observed that dislocations tended to concentrate in regions where lattice strain was highest, which significantly affects materials' mechanical properties. Additionally, we studied the effect of temperature and strain-rate on the propagation of dislocations, and our findings suggest that both factors have significant impacts on the deformation of materials.

This work is important because it provides a fundamental understanding of the role that dislocation plays in a material's mechanical behavior. Such knowledge is crucial in materials science and engineering, as it can aid in the development of new materials and improve the operational efficiency of existing technologies. The present study highlights the relevance of the Peierls-Nabarro model in describing the behavior of dislocations in materials.

We model a sheared disordered solid using the theory of Shear Transformation
Zones (STZs). In this mean-field continuum model the density of zones is
governed by an effective temperature that approaches a steady state value as
energy is dissipated. We compare the STZ model to simulations by Shi, et
al.(Phys. Rev. Lett. 98 185505 2007), finding that the model generates
solutions that fit the data,exhibit strain localization, and capture important
features of the localization process. We show that perturbations to the
effective temperature grow due to an instability in the transient dynamics, but
unstable systems do not always develop shear bands. Nonlinear energy
dissipation processes interact with perturbation growth to determine whether a
material exhibits strain localization. By estimating the effects of these
interactions, we derive a criterion that determines which materials exhibit
shear bands based on the initial conditions alone. We also show that the shear
band width is not set by an inherent diffusion length scale but instead by a
dynamical scale that depends on the imposed strain rate.

Many systems in nature, glasses, interfaces and fractures being some
examples, cannot equilibrate with their environment, which gives rise to novel
and surprising behaviour such as memory effects, ageing and nonlinear dynamics.
Unlike their equilibrated counterparts, the dynamics of out-of- equilibrium
systems is generally too complex to be captured by simple macroscopic laws.
Here we investigate a system that straddles the boundary between glass and
crystal: a Bragg glass formed by vortices in a superconductor. We find that the
response to an applied force evolves according to a stretched exponential, with
the exponent reflecting the deviation from equilibrium. After the force is
removed, the system ages with time and its subsequent response time scales
linearly with its age (simple ageing), meaning that older systems are slower
than younger ones. We show that simple ageing can occur naturally in the
presence of sufficient quenched disorder. Moreover, the hierarchical
distribution of timescales, arising when chunks of loose vortices cannot move
before trapped ones become dislodged, leads to a stretched-exponential
response.

We analyze fading interference relay networks where M single-antenna
source-destination terminal pairs communicate concurrently and in the same
frequency band through a set of K single-antenna relays using half-duplex
two-hop relaying. Assuming that the relays have channel state information
(CSI), it is shown that in the large-M limit, provided K grows fast enough as a
function of M, the network "decouples" in the sense that the individual
source-destination terminal pair capacities are strictly positive. The
corresponding required rate of growth of K as a function of M is found to be
sufficient to also make the individual source-destination fading links converge
to nonfading links. We say that the network "crystallizes" as it breaks up into
a set of effectively isolated "wires in the air". A large-deviations analysis
is performed to characterize the "crystallization" rate, i.e., the rate (as a
function of M,K) at which the decoupled links converge to nonfading links. In
the course of this analysis, we develop a new technique for characterizing the
large-deviations behavior of certain sums of dependent random variables. For
the case of no CSI at the relay level, assuming amplify-and-forward relaying,
we compute the per source-destination terminal pair capacity for M,K converging
to infinity, with K/M staying fixed, using tools from large random matrix
theory.

In this paper, we present a novel technique to deconstruct functions on quadratic surfaces into their respective multipole components. The problem that our work attempts to solve is the inability to obtain a clear understanding of the underlying physical or mathematical reasons that govern the behavior of the functions on the quadratic surfaces. We demonstrate that by understanding the individual contributions of each multipole component, one can more easily comprehend the behavior of functions on curved surfaces.

The motivation for our research stems from the lack of efficient methods to analyze the complex behavior of functions on curved surfaces. The inability to discern the contribution and impact of individual components on the overall behavior of the function hinders both scientific and engineering endeavors. Our approach resolves this issue by breaking down the functions into their component parts, providing a clear understanding of the function's behavior.

Our results show that the technique presented in this paper allows us to gain deeper insights into the behavior of functions on quadratic surfaces. With the multipole decomposition approach, we show that a clear understanding of the behavior of the individual components of the function allows one to better predict the impact of perturbations in the function. Overall, the approach presented in this paper could have significant implications for areas such as materials science, engineering, and physics.

In this study, we investigate the dynamics of vortices in superconductors with fractal cluster structures at the initial stage of resistive transition. The problem we attempt to solve is the understanding of how complex structures, such as fractals, affect the onset of resistance in superconductors. Fractal structures have been found in various systems, including superconducting materials, and have been shown to affect their physical properties. Our research aims to uncover the dynamics of vortex matter in such systems to gain a more comprehensive understanding of the behavior of superconductors with fractal structures. 

We employ a numerical simulation approach using the time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau equation to study the vortex dynamics. We consider the behavior of vortex matter under the influence of magnetic fields at various temperatures, exploring the transition from the superconducting state to the resistive state. Our findings indicate that the fractal cluster structure affects the dynamics of vortices, leading to a more complex transition from the superconducting to the resistive state. 

Overall, our study provides insight into the role of fractal cluster structures in the physical properties of superconductors, which may have implications for the design and development of high-performance superconducting materials.

In this article, we investigate the gravitational collapse of spherically symmetric plasmas in an Einstein-Maxwell spacetime with the aid of numerical simulations. This problem is of fundamental interest in gravitational physics and astrophysics because it provides a laboratory to study the non-linear dynamics of spacetime curvature and matter in extreme regimes. Our motivation for studying this problem stems from its potential applications to astrophysical objects, such as neutron stars and black holes.

We explore a wide range of parameters, including the plasma density and the strength of the electromagnetic field, to investigate their effects on the final outcome of the collapse. Our results show that, for sufficiently large values of the plasma density, the collapse leads to the formation of a black hole surrounded by an accretion disk. Interestingly, we also find that the electromagnetic field can delay the formation of the black hole, leading to the emergence of an extended, highly non-linear, and dynamical electromagnetic structure.

We provide a detailed analysis of the formation and evolution of the gravitational singularity, the apparent horizons, and the electromagnetic radiation emitted during the collapse. Our findings shed new light on the interplay between gravity and electromagnetism in extreme astrophysical environments, and they could have important implications for the interpretation of observational data from astrophysical sources.

Motivated by the work of Salvetti and Settepanella we introduce certain total
orderings of the faces of any shellable regular CW-complex (called
`shelling-type orderings') that can be used to explicitly construct maximum
acyclic matchings of the poset of cells of the given complex. Building on an
application of this method to the classical zonotope shellings we describe a
class of maximum acyclic matchings for the Salvetti complex of a linear
complexified arrangement. To do this, we introduce and study a new
combinatorial stratification of the Salvetti complex. For the obtained acyclic
matchings we give an explicit description of the critical cells that depends
only on the chosen linear extension of the poset of regions. It is always
possible to choose the linear extension so that the critical cells can be
explicitly constructed from the chambers of the arrangement via the bijection
to no-broken-circuit sets defined by Jewell and Orlik. Our method can be
generalized to arbitraty oriented matroids.

In this work, we examine Catalan's intervals, which are essential tools in the study of triangulations. The main goal of this paper is to provide a new characterization of realizers of certain types of triangulations in terms of Catalan's intervals. 

Our methodology involves analyzing the relationship between the combinatorial properties of triangulations and Catalan's intervals. We specifically focus on triangulations where all vertices have degree three and all faces are triangles, also known as 3-regular triangulations. We show that realizers of these triangulations can be characterized by certain properties of the Catalan intervals associated with the triangulations.

To achieve our goal, we use a combination of advanced mathematical techniques, including the use of graph theory and combinatorial structures. We carefully establish our results through detailed proofs and rigorous verification.

In summary, our research provides a deeper understanding of the connection between Catalan's intervals and the structure of triangulations, and offers a new approach to characterizing realizers of certain types of triangulations in terms of these intervals. Our results have important implications for the design and analysis of algorithms related to triangulation, and provide a framework for future work in the field.

In this paper, we explore the concept of DNA hash pooling, and its potential use in solving various biological problems. DNA hash pooling, as a technique, essentially involves taking a large number of DNA samples, partitioning them into smaller subsets, and then pooling the subsets together to generate a smaller number of mixed samples for further analysis.

The key motivation behind this approach is to reduce the cost and time associated with analyzing large numbers of individual samples, while still maintaining a high level of accuracy and specificity in the resulting data. To evaluate the efficacy of this approach, we performed a series of experiments using both simulated and real-world DNA datasets, and compared the performance of hash pooling to traditional individual sample analysis.

Our results demonstrate that hash pooling can provide significant time and cost savings without compromising the accuracy or sensitivity of the analysis, particularly when applied to large-scale studies involving thousands or even millions of samples. Furthermore, we discuss the potential applications of DNA hash pooling in various fields of biology, including genomics, microbiology, and epidemiology.

Overall, the concept of DNA hash pooling presents a promising avenue for advancing various areas of biological research, and our work provides a solid foundation for future exploration and development of this approach.

Double white dwarfs are expected to be a source of confusion-limited noise
for the future gravitational wave observatory LISA. In a specific frequency
range, this 'foreground noise' is predicted to rise above the instrumental
noise and hinder the detection of other types of signals, e.g., gravitational
waves arising from stellar mass objects inspiraling into massive black holes.
In many previous studies only detached populations of compact object binaries
have been considered in estimating the LISA gravitational wave foreground
signal. Here, we investigate the influence of compact object detached and
Roche-Lobe Overflow Galactic binaries on the shape and strength of the LISA
signal. Since >99% of remnant binaries which have orbital periods within the
LISA sensitivity range are white dwarf binaries, we consider only these
binaries when calculating the LISA signal. We find that the contribution of
RLOF binaries to the foreground noise is negligible at low frequencies, but
becomes significant at higher frequencies, pushing the frequency at which the
foreground noise drops below the instrumental noise to >6 mHz. We find that it
is important to consider the population of mass transferring binaries in order
to obtain an accurate assessment of the foreground noise on the LISA data
stream. However, we estimate that there still exists a sizeable number (~11300)
of Galactic double white dwarf binaries which will have a signal-to-noise ratio
>5, and thus will be potentially resolvable with LISA. We present the LISA
gravitational wave signal from the Galactic population of white dwarf binaries,
show the most important formation channels contributing to the LISA disc and
bulge populations and discuss the implications of these new findings.

We report on measurements of the adiabatic temperature change in the inverse
magnetocaloric Ni$_{50}$Mn$_{34}$In$_{16}$ alloy. It is shown that this alloy
heats up with the application of a magnetic field around the Curie point due to
the conventional magnetocaloric effect. In contrast, the inverse magnetocaloric
effect associated with the martensitic transition results in the unusual
decrease of temperature by adiabatic magnetization. We also provide
magnetization and specific heat data which enable to compare the measured
temperature changes to the values indirectly computed from thermodynamic
relationships. Good agreement is obtained for the conventional effect at the
second-order paramagnetic-ferromagnetic phase transition. However, at the first
order structural transition the measured values at high fields are lower than
the computed ones. Irreversible thermodynamics arguments are given to show that
such a discrepancy is due to the irreversibility of the first-order martensitic
transition.

We present a study of the magneto-optical resonances observed in Cs vapour confined in an extremely thin cell. Our work aimed to investigate the ground-state properties of the Cs atoms in the presence of a static magnetic field and resonant light. The experimental setup comprised a Cs cell with two ultra-thin glass plates separated by a distance of 350 nm. The resonant light was obtained from a laser that scanned across the Cs D2 line, while a magnetic field of 51.5 G was applied perpendicular to the cell plates.

Our results show that the confinement of Cs atoms in an extremely thin cell leads to the narrowing of the magneto-optical resonances, which is attributed to the strong interaction between the Cs atoms and the cell walls. In addition, we observed the presence of multiple resonances corresponding to the Zeeman sub-levels of the ground state of Cs.

The key idea behind our work is to investigate the effects of confinement on the properties of the ground-state atoms. Our methodology involved the use of a narrow-bandwidth laser and a magnetic field to probe the Cs atoms in the thin cell. Overall, our results provide insights into the fundamental properties of confined atoms, which may find applications in novel devices such as atomic clocks and sensors.

This study investigates the isospin asymmetry in the continuum of the A=14 mirror nuclei, namely, 14C and 14O. The primary objective of the research is to address the disparity between mirror nuclei and understand the fundamental properties of nuclear reaction dynamics. The theoretical framework is based on the mathematical formulation of the shell-model approach incorporating the isospin degree of freedom. The experiments were performed using a magnetic spectrograph and the Beverly-Wheeling data analysis method.

Our project findings reveal that 14C has a smaller isospin asymmetry than 14O in the continuum due to the energy needed to break the nuclear bond. Moreover, the work sheds light on the subtle correlation between nuclear forces and the isospin degree of freedom. The study emphasizes the importance of understanding the isotopic properties of atomic nuclei to develop a comprehensive theoretical understanding of nuclear structure and properties.

The approach implemented in this research is fundamental in nature and opens up new avenues for future research projects, which could significantly impact the field of nuclear physics. The methodology employed in the research focuses on studying the nuclear force in different systems, including those not accessible by other experimental techniques. The findings of this work are relevant to other scientific fields, including astrophysics and particle physics.

We present the results from an optical spectroscopic analysis of the massive
stars HD 37366 and HD 54662. We find that HD 37366 is a double-lined
spectroscopic binary with a period of 31.8187 +/- 0.0004 days, and HD 54662 is
also a double lined binary with a much longer period of 557.8 +/- 0.3 days. The
primary of HD 37366 is classified as O9.5 V, and it contributes approximately
two-thirds of the optical flux. The less luminous secondary is a broad-lined,
early B-type main-sequence star. Tomographic reconstruction of the individual
spectra of HD 37366 reveals absorption lines present in each component,
enabling us to constrain the nature of the secondary and physical
characteristics of both stars. Tomographic reconstruction was not possible for
HD 54662; however, we do present mean spectra from our observations that show
that the secondary component is approximately half as bright as the primary.
The observed spectral energy distributions (SEDs) were fit with model SEDs and
galactic reddening curves to determine the angular sizes of the stars. By
assuming radii appropriate for their classifications, we determine distance
ranges of 1.4 - 1.9 and 1.2 - 1.5 kpc for HD 37366 and HD 54662, respectively.

We capture the off-shell as well as the on-shell nilpotent
Becchi-Rouet-Stora-Tyutin (BRST) and anti-BRST symmetry invariance of the
Lagrangian densities of the four (3 + 1)-dimensional (4D) (non-)Abelian 1-form
gauge theories within the framework of the superfield formalism. In particular,
we provide the geometrical interpretations for (i) the above nilpotent symmetry
invariance, and (ii) the above Lagrangian densities, in the language of the
specific quantities defined in the domain of the above superfield formalism.
Some of the subtle points, connected with the 4D (non-)Abelian 1-form gauge
theories, are clarified within the framework of the above superfield formalism
where the 4D ordinary gauge theories are considered on the (4, 2)-dimensional
supermanifold parametrized by the four spacetime coordinates x^\mu (with \mu =
0, 1, 2, 3) and a pair of Grassmannian variables \theta and \bar\theta. One of
the key results of our present investigation is a great deal of simplification
in the geometrical understanding of the nilpotent (anti-)BRST symmetry
invariance.

In this work, we investigate the complex Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) matrix in the context of spontaneous CP violation and generalized $\mu$-$\tau$ symmetry. The CKM matrix describes the mixing of the three generations of quarks in the Standard Model of particle physics and plays a crucial role in the description of CP violation phenomena. While the CKM matrix is usually taken to be a unitary matrix, it can be shown that the presence of spontaneous CP violation allows for the CKM matrix to have complex elements. 

We explore the consequences of such a complex CKM matrix in the case of generalized $\mu$-$\tau$ symmetry, which relates the second- and third-generation leptons. This symmetry has recently gained attention due to its potential to explain certain neutrino oscillation data. We find that the presence of a complex CKM matrix allows for additional sources of CP violation beyond the usual phases, and that these sources can have important implications for neutrino physics.

Our results provide a new perspective on CP violation in the context of the CKM matrix and broaden the potential implications of generalized $\mu$-$\tau$ symmetry. By considering a complex CKM matrix and its effects on neutrino physics, we contribute to the ongoing effort to understand the fundamental mechanisms underlying the properties of matter and the universe.

Ni\'eb\'e is a food leguminous plant cultivated in tropical Africa for its
seeds rich in proteins. The main problem setted by its production is the
conservation of harvests. In the fields as in the stocks, the seeds are
destroyed by pests (bruchids). These bruchids are always associated with
several entomophagous species of hymenoptera. Four entomophagous species were
listed : an egg parasitoid (U lariophaga Stephan), and three solitary larval
and pupal ectoparasitoids (D. Basalis Rondoni, Pteromalidae; E. vuilleti
Crawford and E. orientalis Crawford, Eupelmidae). The survey of the populations
shows that at the beginning of storage, E orientalis is the most abundant
specie (72 %) whereas E. vuilleti and D. Basalis respectively represent 12 %
and 16 % of the hymenoptera. During storage, the E orientalis population
decreases gradually and it disappears completely in less than two months after
the beginning of storage. E. Vuilleti population becomes gradually more
important than D. basalis population which regress until less than 10 % of the
emerging parasitoids. E vuilleti adopts ovicide and larvicide behaviour against
D. Basalis. This behaviour explains its population regression inside granaries.
If the aggressive behaviour of this Eupelmidae is a constant, that could also
explain the disappearance of E orientalis. However if this species is
maintained in stocks, it would be an effective control agent of bruchids
according to their parasitic capacities. This study shows that ovicide and
larvicide behaviour of E vuilleti is not expressed against E orientalis. When
the females have exclusively the hosts already parasitized by E orientalis,
they do not lay eggs. The disappearance of E orientalis could not thus be
explained by the presence of E. vuilleti.

The stationary hydrodynamic equations for the transonic accretion disks and
flows around rotating black holes are presented by using the Kerr-Schild
coordinate where there is no coordinate singularity at the event horizon. We
use two types of the causal viscosity prescription, and the boundary conditions
for the transonic accretion flows are given at the sonic point. For one type of
the causal viscosity prescription we also add the boundary conditions at the
viscous point where the accreting radial velocity is nearly equal to the
viscous diffusion velocity. Based on the formalism for the transonic accretion
disks, after we present the calculation method of the transonic solutions, the
horizon-penetrating transonic solutions which smoothly pass the event horizon
are calculated for several types of the accretion flow models: the ideal
isothermal flows, the ideal and the viscous polytropic flows, the advection
dominated accretion flows (ADAFs) with the relativistic equation of state, the
adiabatic accretion disks, the standard accretion disks, the supercritical
accretion disks. These solutions are obtained for both non-rotating and
rotating black holes. The calculated accretion flows plunge into black hole
with finite three velocity smaller than the speed of light even at the event
horizon or inside the horizon, and the angular velocities of the accretion flow
at the horizon are generally different from the angular velocity of the
frame-dragging due to the black hole's rotation. These features contrast to the
results obtained by using the Boyer-Lindquist coordinate with the coordinate
singularity at the horizon.

The dynamics of information dissemination in social networks is of paramount
importance in processes such as rumors or fads propagation, spread of product
innovations or "word-of-mouth" communications. Due to the difficulty in
tracking a specific information when it is transmitted by people, most
understanding of information spreading in social networks comes from models or
indirect measurements. Here we present an integrated experimental and
theoretical framework to understand and quantitatively predict how and when
information spreads over social networks. Using data collected in Viral
Marketing campaigns that reached over 31,000 individuals in eleven European
markets, we show the large degree of variability of the participants' actions,
despite them being confronted with the common task of receiving and forwarding
the same piece of information. This have a profound effect on information
diffusion: Firstly, most of the transmission takes place due to super-spreading
events which would be considered extraordinary in population-average models.
Secondly, due to the different way individuals schedule information
transmission we observe a slowing down of the spreading of information in
social networks that happens in logarithmic time. Quantitative description of
the experiments is possible through an stochastic branching process which
corroborates the importance of heterogeneity. Since high variability of both
the intensity and frequency of human responses are found in many other
activities, our findings are pertinent to many other human driven diffusion
processes like rumors, fads, innovations or news which has important
consequences for organizations management, communications, marketing or
electronic social communities.

We explore the implications of a possible cosmic ray (CR) background
generated during the first supernova explosions that end the brief lives of
massive Population III stars. We show that such a CR background could have
significantly influenced the cooling and collapse of primordial gas clouds in
minihaloes around redshifts of z ~ 15 - 20, provided the CR flux was sufficient
to yield an ionization rate greater than about 10^-19 s^-1 near the center of
the minihalo. The presence of CRs with energies less than approximately 10^7 eV
would indirectly enhance the molecular cooling in these regions, and we
estimate that the resulting lower temperatures in these minihaloes would yield
a characteristic stellar mass as low as ~ 10 M_sun. CRs have a less pronounced
effect on the cooling and collapse of primordial gas clouds inside more massive
dark matter haloes with virial masses greater than approximately 10^8 M_sun at
the later stages of cosmological structure formation around z ~ 10 - 15. In
these clouds, even without CR flux the molecular abundance is already
sufficient to allow cooling to the floor set by the temperature of the cosmic
microwave background.

In this study, we report the results of our work on infrared (IR) observations of the galaxy cluster MS 1054-03. Our research has focused on exploring the star formation and its evolution in rich galaxy clusters, which is a crucial area of study in astrophysics. Our primary motivation for this research was to understand the physical processes that govern star formation in such environments.

Our observations of MS 1054-03 revealed three distinct subpopulations of galaxies, separated by their IR luminosities. The first subpopulation exhibited only a mild enhancement in IR luminosities, whereas the second subpopulation showed a significantly higher increase in IR luminosities than the first. The third subpopulation, which consisted of the brightest and most massive galaxies in the cluster, showed the highest increase in IR luminosities.

By comparing the observed distributions of these subpopulations with theoretical models of galaxy evolution, we found that the observed IR luminosity enhancement in each subpopulation can be explained by different physical mechanisms. Our work thus provides new insights into the interplay between star formation and the overall evolution of galaxies in rich galaxy clusters.

In summary, this study represents a significant step towards understanding the complex processes governing star formation and galaxy evolution in rich galaxy clusters. Our findings provide crucial constraints for theoretical models of galaxy evolution in such environments.

In this study, we present the results of our survey conducted in the southern hemisphere, where we observed 13CO emissions from candidate massive young stellar objects (YSOs). The motivation for conducting this research was to understand the early stages of massive star formation and investigate the properties of the molecular cloud associated with these objects. YSOs play a critical role in shaping and enriching the interstellar medium, and understanding their formation process is crucial for studying the evolution of galaxies.

We used the Atacama Pathfinder EXperiment (APEX) telescope to observe 150 YSO candidates selected from the Red MSX Source (RMS) survey. Our observations detected 13CO molecular cloud emission associated with 76 YSO candidates (50% of the sample). We found that the detected YSOs were located in high-density gas environments with temperatures of 20-40 K. Additionally, we detected a significant amount of turbulence in these regions, with velocity dispersions ranging from 0.5 to 2.0 km/s.

Our study provides insights into the properties of the molecular cloud that hosts massive YSOs and reveals crucial information about their early stages of formation. These results will enhance our understanding of massive star formation and contribute to the development of theoretical models of massive YSOs. The information gained from this research will improve our understanding of the processes that drive the evolution of galaxies.

New high-resolution observations reveal that small-scale magnetic flux
concentrations have a delicate substructure on a spatial scale of 0.1''. Its
basic structure can be interpreted in terms of a magnetic flux sheet or tube
that vertically extends through the ambient weak-field or field-free atmosphere
with which it is in mechanical equilibrium. A more refined interpretation comes
from new three-dimensional magnetohydrodynamic simulations that are capable of
reproducing the corrugated shape of magnetic flux concentrations and their
signature in the visible continuum. Furthermore it is shown that the
characteristic asymmetric shape of the contrast profile of facular granules is
an effect of radiative transfer across the rarefied atmosphere of the magnetic
flux concentration. I also discuss three-dimensional radiation
magnetohydrodynamic simulations of the integral layers from the top of the
convection zone to the mid-chromosphere. They show a highly dynamic
chromospheric magnetic field, marked by rapidly moving filaments of stronger
than average magnetic field that form in the compression zone downstream and
along propagating shock fronts. The simulations confirm the picture of flux
concentrations that strongly expand through the photosphere into a more
homogeneous, space filling chromospheric field. Future directions in the
simulation of small-scale magnetic fields are indicated by a few examples of
very recent work.

Our research explores the neglected electromagnetic consequences of decoupling in the early Universe. The decoupling period is a crucial epoch that spanned approximately 400,000 years after the Big Bang and marks the time when the primordial plasma cooled down to the point where neutral hydrogen atoms could form. During this period, the Universe saw a dramatic change from a plasma-dominated state to a neutral gas-dominated state. However, the process of decoupling resulted in a series of electromagnetic fluctuations that have largely been overlooked in cosmological studies.

Using numerical simulations, we investigated the behavior of the electric and magnetic fields that were generated during decoupling. We found that these fields had substantial magnitudes, with electric fields reaching up to 10^-12 V/m and magnetic fields up to 10^-16 T. Additionally, we observed that these electromagnetic fields acted as a source of radiation that could be detected by future experiments.

Our study provides a new perspective on the Universe's evolution during the decoupling epoch and indicates that the electromagnetic consequences of this period have significant implications for observational cosmology. Our exploration into this aspect of the Universe's evolution may lead to a better understanding of the early Universe and may open new avenues for future research.

In this extended comment, we analyze and critique the work presented in "One-Range Addition Theorems for Coulomb Interaction Potential and Its Derivatives" by I. I. Guseinov in the journal of Chemical Physics. 
The article discusses the one-range addition theorems, which aim to provide an analytical expression for the integral of the Coulomb interaction potential and its derivatives. The author uses a recursive method to derive these formulas, allowing for the computation of molecular properties in an efficient and straightforward manner. 
However, we identify several shortcomings in the article, including the lack of clarity in the methodology and the inconsistency in the notation. Additionally, we point out the limited scope of the formulas and the need for further research to extend them to other types of molecular interactions. 
Despite these limitations, we acknowledge the valuable contribution of the author in providing a mathematical framework for calculating molecular properties based on Coulomb interaction potentials. We encourage future work in this field to address the issues we raised and build upon this foundational research.

The nonequilibrium tunnelling center model of a localized electronic level
coupled to a fluctuating two-state system and to two electronic reservoirs, is
solved via an Anderson-Yuval-Hamann mapping onto a plasma of alternating
positive and negative charges time-ordered along the two "Keldysh" contours
needed to describe nonequilibrium physics. The interaction between charges
depends both on whether their time separation is small or large compared to a
dephasing scale defined in terms of the chemical potential difference between
the electronic reservoirs and on whether their time separation is larger or
smaller than a decoherence scale defined in terms of the current flowing from
one reservoir to another. A renormalization group transformation appropriate to
the nonequilibrium problem is defined. An important feature is the presence in
the model of a new coupling, essentially the decoherence rate, which acquires
an additive renormalization similar to that of the energy in equilibrium
problems. The method is used to study interplay between the dephasing-induced
formation of independent resonances tied to the two chemical potentials and the
decoherence which cuts off the scaling and leads to effectively classical
long-time behavior. We determine the effect of departures from equilibrium on
the localization-delocalization phase transition.

At least two arguments suggest that the orbits of a large fraction of binary
stars and extrasolar planets shrank by 1-2 orders of magnitude after formation:
(i) the physical radius of a star shrinks by a large factor from birth to the
main sequence, yet many main-sequence stars have companions orbiting only a few
stellar radii away, and (ii) in current theories of planet formation, the
region within ~0.1 AU of a protostar is too hot and rarefied for a Jupiter-mass
planet to form, yet many "hot Jupiters" are observed at such distances. We
investigate orbital shrinkage by the combined effects of secular perturbations
from a distant companion star (Kozai oscillations) and tidal friction. We
integrate the relevant equations of motion to predict the distribution of
orbital elements produced by this process. Binary stars with orbital periods of
0.1 to 10 days, with a median of ~2 d, are produced from binaries with much
longer periods (10 d to 10^5 d), consistent with observations indicating that
most or all short-period binaries have distant companions (tertiaries). We also
make two new testable predictions: (1) For periods between 3 and 10 d, the
distribution of the mutual inclination between the inner binary and the
tertiary orbit should peak strongly near 40 deg and 140 deg. (2) Extrasolar
planets whose host stars have a distant binary companion may also undergo this
process, in which case the orbit of the resulting hot Jupiter will typically be
misaligned with the equator of its host star.

We present a detailed dynamical study of the eclipsing cataclysmic variable WZ Sge using archival optical spectra obtained with the Hobby-Eberly Telescope. Using the Doppler tomography technique, we derive the radial velocities of the prominent emission lines in the accretion disk and white dwarf (WD) companion. In conjunction with the photometric light curve fitting, we estimate the component masses and other physical parameters of the system. Our analysis reveals a small disk size, a high mass ratio of the components, and a massive WD companion of ∼1.2 M_sun. The system appears to be consistent with the dynamical evolution scenario of a period bouncer, where it has gone through a common-envelope evolution phase that led to the shrinking of its orbit and the production of a massive WD. The detailed model fitting indicates a low inclination of ∼58° and a distance of ∼1075 pc consistent with previous estimates. Our results contribute to the understanding of the formation and evolution of exceptionally short-period CVs and provide important constraints on the parameter space of binary evolution models.

In this work, we present a documentary of high-mass star formation in the Orion Source I region. We aim to probe the dynamical evolution of this region on 10-100 AU scales, using SiO masers as probes. High-mass stars are crucial components of galaxies, playing a crucial role in the evolution of the Universe. However, their formation and evolution are still not fully understood due to their complex nature. The Orion Source I region is one of the most studied high-mass star-forming regions, and the presence of masers makes it an ideal target to understand the dynamics of high-mass star formation. 

Our observations with the Very Large Telescope Interferometer revealed that the SiO masers in Orion Source I are spatially extended, indicative of an outflow emanating from the star formation region. We also found that the masers exhibit a nonthermal velocity structure, suggesting that the emission arises from shock fronts. These results provide new insights into the formation and evolution of high-mass stars and the role of outflows and shocks in this process.

Our methodology involved using a combination of spectroscopy and data analysis techniques to extract and process the data, followed by modeling and interpretation of the results. Our findings suggest that the use of SiO masers can be an effective tool for probing the dynamics of high-mass star formation.

The impact of the strongly attractive electromagnetic field of heavy nuclei
on electrons in quasi-elastic (e,e') scattering is often accounted for by the
effective momentum approximation. This method is a plane wave Born
approximation which takes the twofold effect of the attractive nucleus on
initial and final state electrons into account, namely the modification of the
electron momentum in the vicinity of the nucleus, and the focusing of electrons
towards the nuclear region leading to an enhancement of the corresponding wave
function amplitudes. The focusing effect due to the attractive Coulomb field of
a homogeneously charged sphere on a classical ensemble of charged particles
incident on the field is calculated in the highly relativistic limit and
compared to results obtained from exact solutions of the Dirac equation. The
result is relevant for the theoretical foundation of the effective momentum
approximation and describes the high energy behavior of the amplitude of
continuum Dirac waves in the potential of a homogeneously charged sphere. Our
findings indicate that the effective momentum approximation is a useful
approximation for the calculation of Coulomb corrections in (e,e') scattering
off heavy nuclei for sufficiently high electron energies and momentum transfer.

In the second part of our study on the richest superclusters, we investigate the galaxy populations within these massive structures. Our motivation for this research is to understand how these clusters form and evolve over time. We use data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey, which allows us to study the properties of galaxies within the Clusters of Galaxies Catalog (CGC) and the Nearby Superclusters Catalog (NSC). 

We found that the richest superclusters contain a high concentration of massive galaxies, with up to 40% of their member galaxies classified as "elliptical" or "lenticular". This is in contrast to less massive superclusters, which have a higher percentage of "spiral" galaxies. We also found evidence of "galaxy harassment" in these structures, which is the process by which smaller galaxies are stripped of their gas and their star formation is quenched as they orbit within the gravitational potential of the larger galaxies. 

Our study has important implications for the formation and evolution of superclusters and the galaxies they contain. We conclude that the richest superclusters are likely the result of complex merging events and that these structures continue to grow through the accretion of smaller galaxies over time. Overall, our key ideas and methodology showcase the importance of observations and statistical analysis in shaping our understanding of galaxy populations within superclusters.

The curvaton scenario for the generation of the cosmological curvature
perturbation on large scales represents an alternative to the standard
slow-roll scenario of inflation in which the observed density perturbations are
due to fluctuations of the inflaton field itself. Its basic assumption is that
the initial curvature perturbation due to the inflaton field is negligible.
This is attained by lowering the energy scale of inflation, thereby highly
suppressing the amount of gravitational waves produced during inflation. We
compute the power-spectrum of the gravitational waves generated at second order
in perturbation theory by the curvaton (isocurvature) perturbations between the
end of inflation and the curvaton decay. An interesting property of this
contribution to the tensor perturbations is that it is directly proportional to
the amount of non-Gaussianity predicted within the curvaton scenario. We show
that the spectrum of gravitational waves may be in the range of future
gravitational wave detectors.

In this paper, we build upon our previous work on scanning and sequential decision-making for multi-dimensional data in the presence of noise. We present a further extension of the algorithm that we proposed earlier, which is able to handle noisy data by incorporating a thresholding step. The threshold value is set according to the characteristics of the noise present in the data. We demonstrate the effectiveness of this algorithm through experiments on a range of datasets, and show that it can successfully accomplish the task of scanning and decision making even in the presence of high levels of noise.

The key idea behind our approach is to take a scanning-based approach to the multi-dimensional data analysis problem, in which we sequentially probe the data in order to identify regions that are of particular interest. We then apply a decision-making algorithm to determine what action to take based on the information gathered thus far. Our approach is highly flexible, allowing us to tailor the algorithm to suit the specific needs of different datasets. We believe that our work has important applications in a wide range of fields, including neuroscience, finance, and climate science, among others. Overall, our results provide strong evidence that this approach is highly effective, and we believe that this work will be of great interest to researchers in many different areas of study who are seeking to extract useful insights from complex, multi-dimensional data.

We present Gemini integral field spectroscopy and Keck II longslit
spectroscopy of the extended emission-line region (EELR) around the quasar 4C
37.43. The velocity structure of the ionized gas is complex and cannot be
explained globally by a simple dynamical model. The spectra from the clouds are
inconsistent with shock or ``shock + precursor'' ionization models, but they
are consistent with photoionization by the quasar nucleus. The best-fit
photoionization model requires a low-metallicity (12+log(O/H) < 8.7) two-phase
medium, consisting of a matter-bounded diffuse component with a unity
filling-factor (N ~ 1 \cc, T ~ 15000 K), in which are embedded small, dense
clouds (N ~ 400 \cc, T ~ 10^4 K). The high-density clouds are transient and can
be re-generated through compressing the diffuse medium by low-speed shocks (V_S
\lesssim 100 \kms). Our photoionization model gives a total mass for the
ionized gas of about 3x10^{10} M_sun, and the total kinetic energy implied by
this mass and the observed velocity field is ~2x10^{58} ergs. The fact that
luminous EELRs are confined to steep-spectrum radio-loud quasars, yet show no
morphological correspondence to the radio jets, suggests that the driving force
producing the 4C 37.43 EELR was a roughly spherical blast wave initiated by the
production of the jet. That such a mechanism seems capable of ejecting a mass
comparable to that of the total interstellar medium of the Milky Way suggests
that ``quasar-mode'' feedback may indeed be an efficient means of regulating
star formation in the early universe.

A cascade model is described based on multiplier distributions determined
from 3D direct numerical simulations (DNS) of turbulent particle laden flows,
which include two-way coupling between the phases at global mass loadings equal
to unity. The governing Eulerian equations are solved using pseudo-spectral
methods on up to 512**3 computional grid points. DNS results for particle
concentration and enstrophy at Taylor microscale Reynolds numbers in the range
34 - 170 were used to directly determine multiplier distributions (PDFs) on
spatial scales 3 times the Kolmogorov length scale. The width of the PDFs,
which is a measure of intermittency, decreases with increasing mass loading
within the local region where the multipliers are measured. The functional form
of this dependence is not sensitive to Reynolds numbers in the range
considered. A partition correlation probability is included in the cascade
model to account for the observed spatial anticorrelation between particle
concentration and enstrophy. Joint probability distribution functions of
concentration and enstrophy generated using the cascade model are shown to be
in excellent agreement with those derived directly from our 3D simulations.
Probabilities predicted by the cascade model are presented at Reynolds numbers
well beyond what is achievable by direct simulation. These results clearly
indicate that particle mass loading significantly reduces the probabilities of
high particle concentration and enstrophy relative to those resulting from
unloaded runs. Particle mass density appears to reach a limit at around 100
times the gas density. This approach has promise for significant computational
savings in certain applications.

This paper studies the problem of maximizing the expected utility of terminal
wealth for a financial agent with an unbounded random endowment, and with a
utility function which supports both positive and negative wealth. We prove the
existence of an optimal trading strategy within a class of permissible
strategies -- those strategies whose wealth process is a supermartingale under
all pricing measures with finite relative entropy. We give necessary and
sufficient conditions for the absence of utility-based arbitrage, and for the
existence of a solution to the primal problem.
  We consider two utility-based methods which can be used to price contingent
claims. Firstly we investigate marginal utility-based price processes
(MUBPP's). We show that such processes can be characterized as local
martingales under the normalized optimal dual measure for the utility
maximizing investor. Finally, we present some new results on utility
indifference prices, including continuity properties and volume asymptotics for
the case of a general utility function, unbounded endowment and unbounded
contingent claims.

In this article, we present a computer simulation study on model cohesive powders to investigate the influence of assembling procedure and contact laws on low consolidation states. The motivation behind our work is to understand how the assembly process of powder particles affects the properties of the final bulk material. These properties are fundamental for a wide range of applications, including pharmaceutical manufacturing, food processing, and the development of new materials. Our simulation model uses the Discrete Element Method to track the motion and interactions between particles. We vary the assembly procedure by changing the initial distribution of particle velocities and orientations, and we vary the contact laws between particles. Our results reveal that the assembly procedure has a crucial effect on the mechanical properties of the powder bed. In contrast, the choice of the contact law has a less pronounced effect. We also find that the mechanical properties of the powder bed depend on the size distribution of the particles. Our study highlights the importance of considering the assembling procedure in modeling powder materials and provides insight into how to optimize the properties of the final bulk material.

In our work, we present a first-principles model of early evolution that aims to unravel the origins of gene families, species, and preferred protein folds. The problem we attempt to solve is the lack of a holistic and comprehensive understanding of the mechanisms that enabled life to emerge and persist in the early stages of evolution. While previous studies have shed light on individual aspects of early evolution, such as the emergence of simple replicators or the evolution of functional proteins, they often fail to capture the complex interplay between various evolutionary processes and the constraints imposed by the physical and chemical environment of the early Earth.

Our motivation for this research is to develop a more unified and predictive framework that can explain the emergence of key biological features such as gene duplication, speciation, and protein folding. Leveraging the power of computational modeling and statistical physics, we construct a self-consistent model that simulates the replication, mutation, and selection of genetic sequences and their corresponding protein products. We explore the role of different evolutionary forces such as drift, selection, and recombination in shaping the diversity of gene families and the emergence of novel protein folds. In particular, we find that gene duplication followed by subfunctionalization and neofunctionalization can lead to the rapid diversification of gene families, and that the selective pressure for efficient folding can drive the emergence of stable and robust protein structures.

Our results provide new insights into the early stages of biological evolution, and our model can serve as a valuable tool for testing hypotheses and predicting the behavior of evolving systems.

LS V +4621 is the DAO-type central star of the planetary nebula Sh 2-216. We
perform a comprehensive spectral analysis of high-resolution, high-S/N
ultraviolet observations obtained with FUSE and STIS aboard the HST as well as
the optical spectrum of LS V +4621 by means of state-of-the-art NLTE
model-atmosphere techniques in order to compare its photospheric properties to
theoretical predictions from stellar evolution theory as well as from diffusion
calculations.
  From the N IV - NV, O IV - O VI, Si IV - Si V, and Fe V - Fe VII ionization
equilibria, we determined an effective temperature of 95 +/- 2 kK with high
precision. The surface gravity is log g = 6.9 +/- 0.2. An unexplained
discrepancy appears between the spectroscopic distance d = 224 +46/-58 pc and
the parallax distance d = 129 +6/-5 pc of LS V +4621. For the first time, we
have identified Mg IV and Ar VI absorption lines in the spectrum of a
hydrogen-rich central star and determined the Mg and Ar abundances as well as
the individual abundances of iron-group elements (Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, and Ni). With
the realistic treatment of metal opacities up to the iron group in the
model-atmosphere calculations, the so-called Balmer-line problem (found in
models that neglect metal-line blanketing) vanishes.
  Spectral analysis by means of NLTE model atmospheres has presently arrived at
a high level of sophistication, which is now hampered largely by the lack of
reliable atomic data and accurate line-broadening tables. Strong efforts should
be made to improve upon this situation.

Results from electric charge correlations studied with the Balance Function
method in A+A collisions from 20\emph{A} to 158\emph{A} GeV are presented in
two different rapidity intervals: In the mid-rapidity region we observe a
decrease of the width of the Balance Function distribution with increasing
centrality of the collision, whereas this effect vanishes in the forward
rapidity region.
  Results from the energy dependence study in central Pb+Pb collisions show
that the narrowing of the Balance Function expressed by the normalised width
parameter \textit{W} increases with energy towards the highest SPS and RHIC
energies.
  Finally we compare our experimental data points with predictions of several
models. The hadronic string models UrQMD and HIJING do not reproduce the
observed narrowing of the Balance Function. However, AMPT which contains a
quark-parton transport phase before hadronization can reproduce the narrowing
of the BF's width with centrality. This confirms the proposed sensitivity of
the Balance Function analysis to the time of hadronization.

In our research, we aim to study the multiplicity of planet host stars and uncover new low-mass companions to these stars. The study employs an observational approach, and we have used the HARPS-North spectrograph to collect precise radial velocity measurements for our sample. We studied 28 stars hosting exoplanets and searched for any potential long-period planets or brown dwarf companions. We also employed a Bayesian analysis to address the effects of activity and noise in our data.

The motivation for our research is to better understand the processes of planetary formation and evolution, and how they are affected by the presence of stellar companions. Our study seeks to investigate whether the observed exoplanets are part of multiple systems or if there are undiscovered low-mass companions that play a role in shaping the planetary system architecture. The problem our work attempts to solve is to explore the multiplicity of exoplanet host stars comprehensively, and to identify new planetary companions to these systems.

Our observations revealed five new candidates for low-mass companions, which require further observation and validation. The detection of multiple systems can deepen our understanding of exoplanetary systems, and provide an opportunity for future research into exoplanetary formation and evolution. Our work highlights the importance of comprehensive multiplicity studies for exoplanetary systems, and the potential to detect low-mass companions to these systems.

In this study, we investigated the X-ray emission from the planet pulsar B1257+12 using the XMM-Newton satellite telescope. This pulsar is known to have three planets orbiting around it, and it presents a unique case for studying X-ray emissions from planets. We analyzed the X-ray spectra and light curves of the pulsar and its surrounding environment, focusing on the energy range of 0.5-10 keV. Our methodology involved using X-ray spectral modelling to determine the spectral parameters of the emission, and fitting the light curves to understand the variability and timing of the emission.

Our results showed the detection of X-ray emission from the pulsar and its surrounding environment, with a strong signal detected from planet b. The X-ray spectra were found to be consistent with thermal emission, suggesting that the X-ray emission is likely due to the planet's atmosphere. The light curves revealed pulsations from the pulsar, as well as variations in the X-ray emission from the planet and the pulsar's surrounding environment. 

The motivation for this research is to gain a better understanding of the physical properties of exoplanets and their surrounding environments. The detection of X-ray emissions from exoplanets presents a unique opportunity to study their atmospheres and compositions. The study of the planet pulsar B1257+12 provides valuable insight into planetary dynamics and astrophysical processes such as accretion, magnetic interaction, and atmospheric escape.

Using a simple method to interpret the luminosity function of Ly-alpha
emitters, we explore properties of Ly-alpha emitters from 5.7 < z < 8.8 with
various assumptions about metallicity and stellar mass spectra. We constrain a
mass-to-'observed' light ratio, M_h/L_band. For narrow-band surveys, L_band is
simply related to the intrinsic Ly-alpha luminosity with a survival fraction of
Ly-alpha photons, alpha_esc. The mass-to-'bolometric light', M_h/L_bol, can
also be deduced, once the metallicity and stellar mass spectrum are given. The
inferred M_h/L_bol is more sensitive to metallicity than to the mass spectrum.
We find the following constraints on a mass-to-light ratio of Ly-alpha emitters
from 5.7 < z < 7: (M_h/L_bol)(alpha_{esc}epsilon^{1/gamma})^{-1}=21-38, 14-26,
and 9-17 for Z=0, 1/50, and 1 Z_sun, respectively, where epsilon is the 'duty
cycle' of Ly-alpha emitters, and gamma ~ 2 is a local slope of the cumulative
luminosity function. Only weak lower limits are obtained for z=8.8. Therefore,
Ly-alpha emitters are consistent with either starburst galaxies M_h/L_bol ~
0.1-1 with a smaller Ly-alpha survival fraction, alpha_{esc}epsilon^{1/gamma}
~0.01-0.05, or normal populations (M_h/L_bol ~ 10) if a good fraction of
Ly-alpha photons survived, alpha_{esc}epsilon^{1/gamma} ~ 0.5-1. We find no
evidence for the end of reionization in the luminosity functions of Ly-alpha
emitters discovered in the current Ly-alpha surveys, including recent discovery
of one Ly-alpha emitter at z=7. The data are consistent with no evolution of
intrinsic properties of Ly-alpha emitters or neutral fraction in the
intergalactic medium up to z=7. No detection of sources at z=8.8 does not yield
a significant constraint yet. We also show that the lack of detection at z=8.8
does not rule out the high-z galaxies being the origin of the excess NIRB.

Revised Hipparcos parallaxes for classical Cepheids are analysed together
with 10 HST-based parallaxes (Benedict et al.). In a reddening-free V,I
relation we find that the coefficient of logP is the same within the
uncertainties in our Galaxy as in the LMC, contrary to some previous
suggestions. Cepheids in the inner region of NGC4258 with near solar
metallicities (Macri et al.) confirm this result. We obtain a zero-point for
the reddening-free relation and apply it to Cepheids in galaxies used by
Sandage et al. to calibrate the absolute magnitudes of SNIa and to derive the
Hubble constant. We revise their result from 62 to 70+/-5 km/s/Mpc. The
Freedman et al. 2001 value is revised from 72 to 76+/-8 km/s/Mpc. These results
are insensitive to Cepheid metallicity corrections. The Cepheids in the inner
region of NGC4258 yield a modulus of 29.22+/-0.03(int) compared with a
maser-based modulus of 29.29+/-0.15. Distance moduli for the LMC, uncorrected
for any metallicity effects, are; 18.52+/-0.03 from a reddening-free relation
in V,I; 18.47+/-0.03 from a period-luminosity relation at K; 18.45+/-0.04 from
a period-luminosity-colour relation in J,K. Adopting a metallicity correction
in V,I from Marci et al. leads to a true LMC modulus of 18.39+/-0.05.

In this paper, we present the Hamiltonian theory of adiabatic motion of relativistic charged particles. Our study is motivated by the need to understand the behavior of charged particles in electromagnetic fields at high speeds. The problem we attempt to solve is to find a mathematical description of the adiabatic motion of these particles in a way that represents the dynamics of the system accurately and efficiently.

We begin by introducing the Hamiltonian formalism, which is a powerful tool for analyzing the dynamics of physical systems. We then derive the Hamiltonian that describes the motion of a relativistic charged particle in an electromagnetic field. Our method is based on the canonical transformation that relates the usual Cartesian coordinates with the general coordinates. This transformation is performed in order to make the Hamiltonian easier to handle.

We then use the Hamiltonian theory to analyze the adiabatic motion of charged particles. We show that the adiabatic invariants of the system can be expressed in terms of the Wilson loop and the Aharonov-Bohm phase.
