These invariants are important because they provide a measure of the stability of the adiabatic motion of the particles.

Our results provide a rigorous mathematical framework for describing the adiabatic motion of relativistic charged particles in electromagnetic fields. Our method is applicable to a wide range of physical systems, from plasma physics to particle accelerators. This work can potentially pave the way for the development of more accurate and efficient numerical simulations of these systems.

The recent observation of a insulator to metal transition (IMT) [I. Loa et
al, PRL 87, 125501 (2001)] in pure LaMnO$_3$ at 32 GPa and room temperature,
well above the Neel temperature (145 K) and below the Jahn-Teller transition
temperature (780 K), opens the way to a study of the role of the orbital
degrees of freedom on the electronic structure in a stoichiometric material. In
this paper we focus our attention in the orbital aspects of the insulator to
metal transition. We use a model Hamiltonian for the $e_g$ orbitals of Mn that
includes the on site Coulomb repulsion $U$, the hopping $t$, and its dependence
with pressure. In order to include in an appropriate way the strong
correlations induced by the dominant electron-electron interactions we
introduce auxiliary fields (Slave Bosons,SB) to the description of the low
energy states. We use a O-Mn distance ($d$) dependence of $t$ and the
pressure-$d$ relation from the experimental data to describe the evolution of
the electronic structure with pressure. Our results confirm and make
transparent the conclusion reached in previous ab-initio calculations: the
inclusion of the Coulomb energy is necessary and constitutes an important
factor enhancing the orbital polarization in these compounds.

We present our study on IRAS 18317-0757, a massive cluster of embedded stars and protostars observed using the Submillimeter Array and the Very Large Array. Our analysis reveals the presence of ten massive young stellar objects and eight protostellar sources, distributed within a radius of 0.3 pc. The cluster is associated to a dense clump, with a total mass of approximately 3000 solar masses.

Our results suggest that the cluster formation is characterized by sequential events of star formation, rather than a single starburst. The protostellar population is concentrated in the central region of the cluster, while the massive young stellar objects are distributed in a more extended pattern. The kinematics of the clump and the young stellar objects suggest the presence of a dynamical equilibrium, with a velocity dispersion of 1.1 km s-1 and a virial parameter of 0.6.

The methodology employed in our work involves the analysis of molecular line emission, continuum emission, and multi-wavelength imaging. We used radiative transfer models to derive physical parameters such as mass, radius, and luminosity for the protostellar and young stellar objects.

Our work aims to understand the process of cluster formation in high-mass star-forming regions, as well as to investigate the connection between the protostellar and massive young stellar populations. These results are relevant to our understanding of the formation and evolution of massive stars, and they provide insight into the properties of young massive clusters.

We report here on an exceptional Very High Energy (VHE) gamma-ray flare of the blazar PKS 2155-304, detected by the H.E.S.S. telescopes in July 2006. During this flare, the source reached an unprecedented VHE flux level of nearly 100 times its average flux, indicating an extraordinarily efficient particle acceleration mechanism at work in the jet of the blazar. The H.E.S.S. observations allowed us to study the temporal evolution of the source during the flare, and to constrain the spectra of the source during different flux states. We show that the source spectrum during the flare significantly differed from that during the quiescent states. Our modeling indicates that a possible scenario for the flare involves the acceleration of electrons during a shock acceleration process inside the jet, capable of producing secondary gamma rays through Inverse Compton scattering with the synchrotron photons produced in the same region. The exceptional nature of this flare and its analysis provide a unique opportunity to improve our understanding of the fundamental physics behind the emission of VHE gamma-rays from blazars.

Our work focuses on investigating the free energy of activation for the Comorosan Effect, a phenomenon where a low-mass object exhibits a sudden increase in its weight when brought in close proximity to another object. This effect has been attributed to the modification of gravitational forces between the objects, but the exact mechanism and the magnitude of the energy required for this transformation are not well understood.

Motivated by the lack of quantitative data on the Comorosan Effect, we have used a combination of experimental and theoretical methods to determine the free energy of activation for this phenomenon. Our study utilizes a setup where two small metal spheres are suspended from a torsion balance and brought into close proximity to one another. The balance detects even small changes in their gravitational interaction as their separation distance is varied. Our results show a sudden increase in weight as the spheres approach each other, which we attribute to the Comorosan Effect. We have also calculated the free energy of activation associated with this effect by using statistical mechanics tools. Our values suggest that for small masses, the energy required is much smaller than the one predicted by common gravitational theories.

The findings of this work contribute to our understanding of the Comorosan Effect and open up new avenues for further research, including the development of new models consistent with our experimental observations.

We show how gauge coupling unification near the Planck scale $M_P\sim
10^{19}$ GeV can be achieved in the framework of supersymmetry, facilitating a
full unification of all forces with gravity. Below the conventional GUT scale
$M_{GUT}\sim 10^{16}$ GeV physics is described by a Supersymmetric Standard
Model whose particle content is that of three complete $\mathbf{27}$
representations of the gauge group $E_6$. Above the conventional GUT scale the
gauge group corresponds to a left-right symmetric Supersymmetric Pati-Salam
model, which may be regarded as a ``surrogate SUSY GUT'' with all the nice
features of SO(10) but without proton decay or doublet-triplet splitting
problems. At the TeV scale the extra exotic states may be discovered at the
LHC, providing an observable footprint of an underlying $E_6$ gauge group
broken at the Planck scale. Assuming an additional low energy $U(1)_X$ gauge
group, identified as a non-trivial combination of diagonal $E_6$ generators,
the $\mu$ problem of the MSSM can be resolved.

In this work, we investigate the kinematics and dynamics of the globular clusters and the planetary nebulae (PNe) of NGC 5128, also known as Centaurus A. Our motivation is to gain a better understanding of the structure and evolution of this nearby galaxy. We obtained spectroscopic data using the Two-degree Field (2dF) instrument on the Anglo-Australian Telescope, which allows us to study the distribution and velocity of the globular clusters and PNe across the galaxy.

Our results reveal a clear spatial correlation between the most luminous PNe and the globular clusters, indicating that these objects may have formed during the same epoch. We also observe a significant difference in the kinematics of the PNe and globular clusters, with the former being more rotationally-supported and the latter being more pressure-supported. This suggests that the PNe may be tracing the disk-like structure of NGC 5128, while the globular clusters are associated with the galaxy's halo.

To further understand the dynamics of NGC 5128, we constructed models using the Monte Carlo method and compared our results with observations. Our models suggest that the globular cluster system is consistent with being in dynamical equilibrium, while the PNe may be tracing an out-of-equilibrium system. By combining our observations and modeling, we are able to gain insights into the formation and evolution of NGC 5128, as well as the underlying physics of globular clusters and PNe.

In this research, we investigate the demographics of transition objects, which have been identified as a new class of astronomical objects. Our study aims to provide a better understanding of the properties of these objects, especially their age and how they are formed. We used data from the SDSS and UKIDSS surveys to analyze the spectral energy distributions of transition objects and fitted them with evolutionary models. Our results indicate that these objects are likely to be intermediate-age stars with ages ranging from 5 to 10 billion years, although we cannot rule out the possibility of them being very young stars. Additionally, our analysis showed a correlation between the ages of transition objects and their metallicity, suggesting that their formation may be influenced by the metal content of the local environment.

The motivation for our research is driven by the importance of understanding the evolution of galaxies and the role that transition objects play in it. These objects are thought to represent a transitional phase between young and old stars, and therefore provide crucial information about the formation and evolution of galaxies. Furthermore, transition objects have been proposed as potential progenitors of some types of supernovae, making them important objects to study for understanding the production of heavy elements in the universe. Our findings will help to refine our understanding of the demographics of transition objects, improving our ability to interpret their properties and extract meaningful information about the history of galaxies.

In this work, we investigate the properties of a two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) in the presence of a perpendicular magnetic field, focusing on the spin Hall conductance (SHC). The SHC is a fundamental quantity characterizing the ability of a system to generate a spin current perpendicular to an applied electric field. Our study reveals that the SHC of a 2DEG in a perpendicular magnetic field is conserved.

To demonstrate this result, we employ a theoretical framework based on the Kubo formula and matrix Green's function formalism. Our approach allows us to calculate the SHC in the presence of disorder, and we use it to investigate the impact of various physical parameters, such as temperature, disorder strength, and magnetic field strength, on the SHC. We find that the SHC remains conserved, regardless of these parameters, confirming its robustness.

Our work addresses a fundamental question in the field of spintronics, which aims to understand and manipulate the spin properties of electrons in solid-state devices. Our results suggest that the SHC can be used as a reliable and robust indicator of spin transport in 2DEGs, even in the presence of disorder and external fields. This finding may have implications for the design and optimization of spin-based electronics and quantum devices.

In this work, we investigate the zeta-function of a p-adic manifold using Dwork theory, with a specific emphasis on its relevance for physicists. Our motivation for this research lies in the need for a more rigorous and comprehensive understanding of the mathematical foundations that underlie contemporary theoretical physics. In particular, we focus on the connection between p-adic geometry and quantum field theory, which has been an increasingly active area of research in recent years.

Our main aim is to provide a comprehensive introduction to the fundamentals of p-adic geometry, and to demonstrate its applicability to the study of the zeta-function of a p-adic manifold. We use Dwork theory as a key tool, which allows us to establish an explicit expression for the p-adic zeta-function, and to explore its properties in depth. Moreover, we show how this theory can be applied to a wide range of problems in theoretical physics, including the study of p-adic strings and the construction of p-adic quantum field theories.

Our results provide a valuable contribution to the growing body of literature on p-adic geometry and its applications in physics. We demonstrate the power and versatility of this approach, and highlight the potential for further exploration in this field. Overall, this work represents an important step towards a deeper understanding of the fundamental nature of the physical world, and its connection to the abstract realm of mathematics.

In this paper, we present a Non-Gaussianity analysis on local morphological measures of WMAP data. The study aims to assess the statistical properties of the primordial density perturbations that were created during the inflationary phase of the early universe, using local morphological measures in a Non-Gaussianity framework. The problem that this work attempts to address is the detection of deviation from the Gaussian nature of the initial conditions of the universe. While the inflationary theory is well supported by observations, the assumption of the Gaussianity of the primordial perturbations may be challenged by possible mechanisms operating during or after inflation. If detected, these non-Gaussian signatures may reveal important information about the physics of the early universe. In this study, we perform a Non-Gaussianity analysis on local geometric quantities derived from the WMAP data of the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) anisotropies. Our methodology involves the calculation of the local skewness and kurtosis of the geometric measures on different scales, and testing their consistency with a Gaussian distribution. Our results reveal that the local geometric measures display significant deviations from the Gaussian distribution, indicating the presence of non-Gaussian signatures in the primordial perturbations that may provide insights into early universe physics.

General relativity does not allow one to specify the topology of space,
leaving the possibility that space is multiply rather than simply connected. We
review the main mathematical properties of multiply connected spaces, and the
different tools to classify them and to analyse their properties. Following
their mathematical classification, we describe the different possible
muticonnected spaces which may be used to construct Friedmann-Lemaitre universe
models. Observational tests concern the distribution of images of discrete
cosmic objects or more global effects, mainly those concerning the Cosmic
Microwave Background. According to the 2003-2006 WMAP data releases, various
deviations from the flat infinite universe model predictions hint at a possible
non-trivial topology for the shape of space. In particular, a finite universe
with the topology of the Poincar\'e dodecahedral spherical space fits
remarkably well the data and is a good candidate for explaining both the local
curvature of space and the large angle anomalies in the temperature power
spectrum. Such a model of a small universe, whose volume would represent only
about 80% the volume of the observable universe, offers an observational
signature in the form of a predictable topological lens effect on one hand, and
rises new issues on the physics of the early universe on the other hand.

In this paper, we present some notes on Liouville theory with central charge c ≤ 1. The problem that we attempt to solve is to understand the behavior of the theory near the boundary of the moduli space, where the conformal factor becomes infinite. Specifically, we focus on the study of the so-called degenerate field configurations, which correspond to certain singular points in the moduli space.

We start by reviewing some basic concepts and results on Liouville theory, including the definition of the theory, its classical and quantum properties, and some known results on the moduli space and the degenerate fields. Then, we introduce a novel approach to the theory based on the use of non-local observables, which allows us to obtain new insights into the structure of the theory and its relation to other conformal field theories.

Our main results include a detailed analysis of the degenerate fields and their properties, including their conformal weights, fusion rules, and correlation functions. We also find some unexpected connections with other conformal field theories and mathematical structures, which shed new light on the nature of Liouville theory and its applications to various fields of physics and mathematics.

The motivation for our research comes from the fact that Liouville theory is a fundamental concept in modern theoretical physics, with applications to fields such as string theory, condensed matter physics, and mathematical physics. Our work contributes to a deeper understanding of the theory and its connections to other areas of physics and mathematics, and may have implications for the development of new theoretical tools and techniques.

In our work, we present an Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) study of thick lamellar stacks of phospholipid bilayers. The purpose of this research was to characterize the morphology and mechanical properties of thick lipid layers and to evaluate the suitability of AFM for analyzing them. In addition, we aimed to gain insights into the structural features of lipid bilayers, such as their thickness, roughness, and interlayer spacing.

Using AFM imaging, we were able to observe the shape and topography of the multi-layered phospholipid stacks with high resolution, and to accurately determine the thickness and layer-to-layer distance of the bilayers. Our results showed that the thickness of the bilayers were consistent within the same stack, with an average thickness of ~4.7 nm. Furthermore, we found that the roughness and spacing between the layers increase with an increase in the number of layers.

The motivation for this study arises from the need for a better understanding of the structural properties of lipid bilayers, which play important roles in various biological processes. Our results provide important insights into the structure of thick lipid bilayer stacks, thereby contributing to the development of new models of lipid bilayer membranes with potential applications in drug delivery and biomimetic materials. In conclusion, our AFM study represents a valuable tool for investigating complex lipid systems, with promising prospects for future research on the nanoscale characterization of biological and soft matter samples.

In this work, we present the unusual morphologic structure of a young star that resembles that of a diamond ring. Our research aims to understand the cause of this uncommon morphologic structure and explore the possibility of multiple young objects in the system.

The motivation for our research stems from the quest to unravel the mysteries of the cosmos and the desire to expand knowledge on the formation and morphology of young stars. We employed high-spatial resolution, multi-wavelength observations and sophisticated image processing techniques to examine the object, and found that it is indeed a young star with an accretion disk, but with a bright, circular, and apparently empty inner region that forms a ring shape surrounding the star.

The results obtained from our study suggest that the empty region inside the bright ring may be a consequence of dust sublimation, or the existence of a binary system, while also suggesting that the system may consist of multiple young objects rather than just one. These findings are of great significance in the field of astrophysics, as they provide a new perspective that can be used to improve the understanding of star formation and evolution processes.

In this research work, we analyzed the baryon fractions and mass-to-light ratios of early-type galaxies, aiming to understand the discrepancy between the predicted and observed values. The problem we attempted to solve is rooted in the assumption that dark matter exists, and its presence affects the distribution of baryonic matter, leading to differences in observed mass-to-light ratios and baryon fractions in galaxies. Therefore, our main object was to investigate the extent and mechanism of dark matter's influence on these factors.

We examined a sample of thirty-two early-type galaxies using the spatially resolved spectroscopy of their stars and ionized gas to estimate their mass distributions. Our results showed that the dark matter fraction varies significantly from plane to plane and that the mass-to-light ratios decrease with increasing stellar luminosity. Furthermore, some galaxies displayed remarkably high baryon fractions within their central regions, indicating the presence of a highly concentrated baryonic component.

The motivation for this research stems from the lingering questions concerning the nature and distribution of dark matter and its relationship with baryonic matter. Our findings suggest that more massive galaxies tend to have a higher baryonic fraction, and the presence of dark matter plays a vital role in determining the structure of galaxies. These results provide a novel insight into the physical mechanisms behind the evolution and formation of galaxies, which may be useful in future studies.

The Leo Elliptical NGC 3379: A Metal-Poor Halo Emerges is a study that aims to investigate the metallicity of a previously discovered, metal-poor halo within the Leo elliptical NGC 3379. The key idea behind this research is to use the Multiple Mirror Telescope (MMT) and Hubble Space Telescope (HST) to analyze the metallicities of stars located within the halo of NGC 3379.

The methodology employed in this study involves the analysis of spectroscopic data obtained using the HST and imaging data obtained using the MMT. The results obtained indicate the presence of a metal-poor halo surrounding NGC 3379, with a metallicity that is significantly lower than that of the main body of the galaxy. Furthermore, the study reveals the presence of a metallicity gradient within the halo, with stars located closer to the center of the galaxy having higher metallicities.

The motivation for this research lies in understanding how and when galaxies form their halos and in gaining insight into the chemical evolution of galaxies. The results presented in this study contribute significantly to our understanding of the metallicity distribution within galaxies and provide valuable insights into the formation and evolution of galaxy halos.

In conclusion, the study of the metal-poor halo surrounding NGC 3379 using the MMT and HST has led to significant findings that provide insight into the chemical evolution of galaxies. The results obtained from this study are a significant contribution to the field of astronomy and will inform future research on galaxy halos and their formation.

I discuss outstanding questions about the formation of the ring nebula around
SN1987A and some implications of similar ring nebulae around Galactic B
supergiants. There are notable obstacles for the formation of SN1987A's bipolar
nebula through interacting winds in a transition from a red supergiant to a
blue supergiant. Instead, several clues hint that the nebula may have been
ejected in an LBV-like event. In addition to the previously known example of
Sher25, there are two newly-discovered Galactic analogs of SN1987A's ringed
nebula. Of these three Galactic analogs around blue supergiants, two (Sher25
and SBW1) have chemical abundances indicating that they have not been through a
red supergiant phase, and the remaining ringed bipolar nebula surrounds a
luminous blue variable (HD168625). Although SK-69 202's initial mass of 20 Msun
is lower than those atributed to most LBVs, it is not far off, and the
low-luminosity end of the LBV phenomenon is not well defined. Furthermore,
HD168625's luminosity indicates an initial mass of only 25 Msun, that of SBW1
is consistent with 20 Msun, and there is a B[e] star in the SMC with an initial
mass of 20 Msun that experienced an LBV outburst in the 1990s. These
similarities may be giving us important clues about Sk-69 202's pre-SN
evolution and the formation mechanism of its nebula.

We present a study of the radio continuum emission from the Seyfert 1.5 galaxy NGC 5033 based on high-resolution observations with the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array (VLA) at 8.4 GHz, complemented with archival VLA data at lower frequencies. The motivation for this research lies in understanding the nature of the radio emission in Seyfert galaxies, particularly in those that are classified as type 1.5. Such objects exhibit both broad and narrow emission lines, which suggests that they have intermediate properties between pure type 1 and type 2 Seyferts. Our study aims to shed light on the relationship between the radio continuum and the ionized gas in these galaxies, which is important for understanding the underlying physical processes that drive their activity. 

Our key ideas involve analyzing the morphology, spectral index, and polarimetric properties of the radio emission at different spatial scales and comparing them with those of the optical and X-ray emission. We employ several image analysis techniques, including CLEAN deconvolution, self-calibration, and polarization synthesis, to obtain high-quality radio maps that reveal the complex structure of the extended radio emission in NGC 5033. We also measure the total flux density and integrated spectral index of the radio emission from the core and lobes of the galaxy, as well as from individual HII regions and star-forming regions in its disk. 

Our results show that the radio emission in NGC 5033 is dominated by synchrotron radiation from a compact, flat-spectrum core and a pair of extended, steep-spectrum lobes that are aligned with the major axis of the galaxy. The polarimetric analysis reveals a significant degree of linear polarization in the lobes and some regions of the disk, indicating the presence of ordered magnetic fields. The comparison with the optical and X-ray data suggests that the radio emission is associated with both the nuclear activity and the star formation in the disk of NGC 5033, which is consistent with the scenario of a hybrid Seyfert galaxy.

We present a numerical study and analytical model of the optical near-field
diffracted in the vicinity of subwavelength grooves milled in silver surfaces.
The Green's tensor approach permits computation of the phase and amplitude
dependence of the diffracted wave as a function of the groove geometry. It is
shown that the field diffracted along the interface by the groove is equivalent
to replacing the groove by an oscillating dipolar line source. An analytic
expression is derived from the Green's function formalism, that reproduces well
the asymptotic surface plasmon polariton (SPP) wave as well as the transient
surface wave in the near-zone close to the groove. The agreement between this
model and the full simulation is very good, showing that the transient
"near-zone" regime does not depend on the precise shape of the groove. Finally,
it is shown that a composite diffractive evanescent wave model that includes
the asymptotic SPP can describe the wavelength evolution in this transient
near-zone. Such a semi-analytical model may be useful for the design and
optimization of more elaborate photonic circuits whose behavior in large part
will be controlled by surface waves.

We present the results of a search for faint HI 21-cm emission line wings at
velocities forbidden by Galactic rotation in the Galactic plane using the
Leiden/Dwingeloo HI Survey data and the HI Southern Galactic Plane Survey data.
These ``forbidden-velocity wings (FVWs)'' appear as protruding excessive
emission in comparison with their surroundings in limited (< 2 deg) spatial
regions over velocity extent more than ~20 km/s in large-scale (l-v) diagrams.
Their high-velocities imply that there should be some dynamical phenomena
associated. We have identified 87 FVWs. We present their catalog, and discuss
their distribution and statistical properties. We found that 85% of FVWs are
not coincident with known supernova remnants (SNRs), galaxies, or high-velocity
clouds. Their natures are currently unknown. We suspect that many of them are
fast-moving HI shells and filaments associated with the oldest SNRs that are
essentially invisible except via their HI line emission. We discuss other
possible origins.

In this work, we explore the geometry of PT-symmetric quantum mechanics. PT-symmetry is a concept that extends the symmetry of a physical system beyond the conventional notions of parity and time-reversal. In particular, we consider a class of Hamiltonians that are Hermitian with respect to a combination of parity (P) and time-reversal (T) operations. Previous works have mainly focused on the spectral properties and mathematical foundations of PT-symmetric quantum mechanics. 

Our work takes a different approach, using tools from differential geometry to study the geometric properties of PT-symmetric Hamiltonians. Specifically, we introduce the notion of a PT-symmetric metric, which is a geometric object that encodes the PT-symmetric structure of the Hamiltonian. We show that this metric is intimately related to the spectral properties of the PT-symmetric Hamiltonian, and in particular to the existence and location of exceptional points in the spectrum. 

Our methodology includes a combination of analytical and numerical techniques, including the use of differential forms and the calculation of the Berry curvature. We apply our results to various examples of PT-symmetric systems, including non-Hermitian extensions of the harmonic oscillator and the damped harmonic oscillator. Our work contributes to a deeper understanding of the geometric structure of PT-symmetric quantum mechanics and paves the way for future investigations of the interplay between geometry, topology, and PT-symmetry in quantum mechanics.

In this study, we present the findings of our three-year long investigation into the Mira variable stars using CCD photometry. Our work aims to comprehensively analyze the long-term variability of Miras and reveal the underlying physical mechanisms that govern their periodic behavior.

To this end, we examined the temporal changes in brightness and color of 23 Mira variables with light curves spanning three years. Our methodology involves a combination of visual inspection and signal processing techniques, such as Fourier transform and wavelet analysis. Our observations were carried out mainly in the Johnson V and I filters, and we performed time-series photometry of these variables on a weekly basis.

Our results reveal significant differences in the period and amplitude of the observed Mira variables. We observe that the fundamental period of Mira variables changes over time, which is consistent with the presence of pulsation-driven shock waves in their atmospheres. Furthermore, we detect the presence of secondary periods (with low amplitude) that could indicate the existence of non-radial pulsations, magnetic activity, or binarity in some of these stars.

Our research contributes to the understanding of the evolution of Mira stars and their intrinsic properties. It also provides a basis for further studies in the field of variable star astrophysics.

In this work, we investigate the mass spectrum of diquarks and mesons in the color-flavor locked phase of dense quark matter. The color-flavor locked phase is a predicted state of matter in which quarks bind together in pairs with different colors and flavors, resulting in a unique set of physical properties. We use a model of effective field theory to calculate the masses of the particles in this phase, taking into account the interactions between the quarks. 

Our results show that the meson masses are significantly higher than those in the vacuum due to the strong interaction between the quarks. The diquark masses, on the other hand, are found to be relatively lower than meson masses, indicating that diquarks are more likely to form in dense quark matter. Additionally, we find that the spectrum of mesons and diquarks exhibits a chiral symmetry breaking pattern, which is characteristic of QCD at low energies.

The motivation for our research is to better understand the properties of dense quark matter, which is relevant in the study of neutron stars and heavy-ion collisions. Our study provides insight into the behavior of quarks in extreme conditions, which can aid in the development of new theories and technologies in the field of nuclear physics. Overall, our work contributes to the ongoing effort to uncover the mysteries of the strong force and the fundamental building blocks of matter.

In this work, we present a numerical study of the scalar-field perturbations generated by a massive particle orbiting a black hole in 2+1 dimensions. This research attempts to shed light on the dynamics of such systems and the properties of the resulting gravitational waves, with potential implications for astrophysical observations and the study of black hole mergers.

Our approach combines numerical relativity techniques with a novel numerical evolution scheme for scalar fields, which allows us to accurately simulate the behaviour of the system over a large range of orbital parameters and black hole masses. The resulting simulations reveal complex patterns of scalar radiation, including the emission of scalar jets and the formation of scalar clouds around the black hole.

The motivation for this research lies in the need to better understand the astrophysical processes that lead to the formation and evolution of black hole systems. By investigating the scalar-field perturbations generated by a particle orbiting a black hole, we aim to provide insights into the behaviour of these systems and the gravitational waves they produce. Moreover, our numerical methods and results may be applied in the study of other astrophysical systems, such as neutron-star binaries or accretion disks around black holes. Overall, our work contributes to the ongoing effort to develop accurate and reliable numerical techniques for the simulation of gravitational-wave sources in astrophysics.

The concept of individuality in quantum mechanics shows radical differences
from the concept of individuality in classical physics, as E. Schroedinger
pointed out in the early steps of the theory. Regarding this fact, some authors
suggested that quantum mechanics does not possess its own language, and
therefore, quantum indistinguishability is not incorporated in the theory from
the beginning. Nevertheless, it is possible to represent the idea of quantum
indistinguishability with a first order language using quasiset theory (Q). In
this work, we show that Q cannot capture one of the most important features of
quantum non individuality, which is the fact that there are quantum systems for
which particle number is not well defined. An axiomatic variant of Q, in which
quasicardinal is not a primitive concept (for a kind of quasisets called finite
quasisets), is also given. This result encourages the searching of theories in
which the quasicardinal, being a secondary concept, stands undefined for some
quasisets, besides showing explicitly that in a set theory about collections of
truly indistinguishable entities, the quasicardinal needs not necessarily be a
primitive concept.

In this work, we present evidence suggesting that Luminous Blue Variables (LBVs) may be supernova progenitors. We analyze spectroscopic data obtained with the Very Large Telescope from two stars, Sk-69 202 and Sk-69 203, which are located within the nebula surrounding SN 1987A. Our analysis shows that these stars have spectra and luminosity consistent with other known LBVs, suggesting that they are also LBVs. Moreover, we find that their position, velocity, and chemical abundances are similar to those of the ring around SN 1987A, indicating that they are the likely progenitors of the supernova.

Our research is motivated by the need to understand the origins of supernovae and to provide new insights into the role of LBVs in the evolution of massive stars. Although LBVs are known to undergo large optical and spectroscopic variations, their connection to supernova explosions has remained unclear. By analyzing the properties of these two LBVs and their surroundings, we aim to shed light on the connection between LBVs and supernovae and to better understand the physical processes that lead to massive star explosions.

Our work attempts to solve the problem of identifying the progenitors of type II supernovae, which are the most common type of supernovae and are associated with the explosion of massive stars. By identifying the likely progenitors of SN 1987A, we aim to contribute to a better understanding of the origins of this type of supernova and to provide new insights into the evolution of massive stars. Our methodology involves using spectroscopic data analysis and comparing it with existing models and observations.

We study the stabilizing effect of rotational forcing in the nonlinear
setting of two-dimensional shallow-water and more general models of
compressible Euler equations. In [H. Liu and E. Tadmor, Phys. D 188 (2004), no.
3-4, 262-276] we have shown that the pressureless version of these equations
admit global smooth solution for a large set of sub-critical initial
configurations. In the present work we prove that when rotational force
dominates the pressure, it \emph{prolongs} the life-span of smooth solutions
for t < ln(1/d); here d << 1 is the ratio of the pressure gradient measured by
the inverse squared Froude number, relative to the dominant rotational forces
measured by the inverse Rossby number. Our study reveals a ``nearby''
periodic-in-time approximate solution in the small d-regime, upon which hinges
the long time existence of the exact smooth solution. These results are in
agreement with the close-to periodic dynamics observed in the ``near inertial
oscillation'' (NIO) regime which follows oceanic storms. Indeed, our results
indicate the existence of smooth, ``approximate periodic'' solution for a time
period of \emph{days}, which is the relevant time period found in NIO
obesrvations.

We study the isospin asymmetry in the isoscalar (IS) excitations in the
mirror nuclei $^{14}$O and $^{14}$C by using the Hartree-Fock(HF)+random phase
approximation (RPA) linear response function theory with a Skyrme interaction
to take into account the continuum effect properly. The asymmetry in the IS
monopole, dipole responses is pointed out in the continuum near the particle
threshold with respect to the excitation energy and the sum rule strength. On
the other hand, no clear sign of the asymmetry is found in the giant resonance
(GR) region. In the quadrupole case, the calculated strengths of the mirror
nuclei show almost the same energy dependence from the threshold to the GR
region. It is found that the transition densities of the monopole response show
an extended halo structure near the threshold, while those of GR region show a
typical radial dependence of the compressional collective mode without any halo
effect. Contrary to the transition densities of the monopole response, those of
quadrupole response do not show any sign of the extended feature of wave
functions neither near the threshold nor the GR energy region. Calculated
strength distributions of the IS multipole states are compared with recent
experimental data obtained by the multipole decomposition analysis of $\alpha $
inelastic scattering on $^{14}$O.

We present a comprehensive study of the electrical and optical properties of ferromagnetic Cobalt doped Zinc Oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles, exhibiting a metal-insulator transition (MIT). We investigate the effect of Cobalt doping on the electronic structure and magnetic properties of ZnO nanoparticles. We synthesized nanoparticles using a chemical co-precipitation technique and characterized their structural, magnetic, electrical, and optical properties using various techniques. Our electrical transport measurements show a transition from metallic to insulating behavior at a critical temperature. Furthermore, optical measurements indicate that the material's optical properties change as we approach the MIT temperature, revealing a critical behavior that is consistent with a metal-insulator phase transition. Importantly, our results support the observation that the magnetic behavior of the nanoparticles becomes ferromagnetic and persists at high temperatures near the MIT. Finally, our work provides a fundamental understanding of the origin of the MIT in Cobalt doped ZnO nanoparticles, which has significant implications for the development of novel materials with promising spintronics and optoelectronics applications.

In this study, we investigate the cycle of interstellar dust in galaxies of different morphological types. We carried out a comprehensive analysis of different dust properties, including its temperature, luminosity, and mass, in a sample of 23 nearby galaxies with a range of morphologies. Our results show that the properties of interstellar dust in a galaxy are closely related to its morphological type. We also observed significant variations in the dust-to-gas mass ratio and the dust-to-stellar mass ratio for galaxies with different morphologies. Our study provides new insights into the connection between the physical properties of interstellar dust and the evolution of galaxies.

The primary problem we aim to solve is to understand the complex interplay between dust and gas in galaxies of different morphologies and how this affects galaxy evolution. Our research has several implications for the field of astrophysics, including the role of dust in driving galaxy evolution and the connection between dust and star formation. Our investigation of the cycle of interstellar dust in galaxies of different morphological types is motivated by the need for a comprehensive understanding of the physical processes regulating galaxy evolution. This study provides the basis for further investigations into how the properties of interstellar dust influence the evolution of galaxies, which is crucial for understanding the formation and evolution of the universe.

In this paper, we investigate the diameter in preferential attachment (PA-)
models, thus quantifying the statement that these models are small worlds. The
models studied here are such that edges are attached to older vertices
proportional to the degree plus a constant, i.e., we consider affine PA-models.
There is a substantial amount of literature proving that, quite generally,
PA-graphs possess power-law degree sequences with a power-law exponent \tau>2.
  We prove that the diameter of the PA-model is bounded above by a constant
times \log{t}, where t is the size of the graph. When the power-law exponent
\tau exceeds 3, then we prove that \log{t} is the right order, by proving a
lower bound of this order, both for the diameter as well as for the typical
distance. This shows that, for \tau>3, distances are of the order \log{t}. For
\tau\in (2,3), we improve the upper bound to a constant times \log\log{t}, and
prove a lower bound of the same order for the diameter. Unfortunately, this
proof does not extend to typical distances. These results do show that the
diameter is of order \log\log{t}.
  These bounds partially prove predictions by physicists that the typical
distance in PA-graphs are similar to the ones in other scale-free random
graphs, such as the configuration model and various inhomogeneous random graph
models, where typical distances have been shown to be of order \log\log{t} when
\tau\in (2,3), and of order \log{t} when \tau>3.

In this article, we investigate the geometry of curves with exceptional secant planes by studying linear series along the general curve. The motivation behind this research lies in understanding the structures of algebraic varieties and their associated linear systems. Our main objective is to determine the dimensions of the spaces of global sections of certain line bundles on curves.

The key idea is to use the theory of minimal models and the nef cone of curves to describe the geometry of curves with exceptional secant planes. We employ a combination of techniques from algebraic geometry and commutative algebra, including intersection theory and the theory of Wronski maps, to obtain our main results.

Our methodology involves studying curves with exceptional secant planes and their deformations. We also use the notion of Brill-Noether theory to investigate birational maps between curves and the corresponding spaces of linear series.

Our results reveal that the geometry of curves with exceptional secant planes has interesting properties, which can be described in terms of linear systems and their associated Brill-Noether theory. We obtain precise numerical bounds for the dimensions of the spaces of global sections of line bundles on these curves, which are related to the geometry of their secant planes. Our findings have significant implications for the study of the geometry of algebraic varieties.

In this work, we investigate the relationship between G\"odel incompleteness and the black hole information paradox. We present a mathematical framework to illustrate how the incompleteness theorems can be used to shed light on this longstanding paradox. Our research is motivated by the fact that despite decades of research, the black hole information paradox remains an unresolved problem in physics. We therefore seek to gain new insights into this issue by leveraging one of the most profound results in mathematical logic.

To achieve our objective, we start by reviewing the key concepts of G\"odel incompleteness and the black hole information paradox. We then develop a formal model that combines these two areas of inquiry and use it to highlight the fundamental limitations of our ability to comprehend the nature of black holes. Our methodology involves the use of advanced mathematical techniques to derive novel insights from the interaction between these two powerful frameworks.

Our primary result is a new perspective on the nature of black hole information loss. We argue that this phenomenon cannot be resolved within the standard framework of quantum mechanics and general relativity, and that new physical principles may be necessary to fully understand black hole physics. Our work therefore opens up new avenues for research in this field.

During a crossover via a switching mechanism from one 2-body potential to
another as might be applied in modeling (chemical) reactions in the vicinity of
bond formation, energy violations would occur due to finite step size which
determines the trajectory of the particles relative to the potential
interactions of the unbonded state by numerical (e.g. Verlet) integration. This
problem is overcome by an algorithm which preserves the coordinates of the
system for each move, but corrects for energy discrepancies by ensuring both
energy and momentum conservation in the dynamics. The algorithm is tested for a
hysteresis loop reaction model with an without the implementation of the
algorithm. The tests involve checking the rate of energy flow out of the MD
simulation box; in the equilibrium state, no net rate of flows within
experimental error should be observed. The temperature and pressure of the box
should also be invariant within the range of fluctuation of these quantities.
It is demonstrated that the algorithm satisfies these criteria.

In this work, we present the results of our study of the far-infrared (FIR) properties in two nearby spiral galaxies, NGC2841 and NGC2976, using the AKARI/FIS (Astro-Far Infrared Surveyor/Far Infrared Surveyor) data. The FIR is an important component in tracing dust and star-forming activities in galaxies. However, a majority of the previous studies on FIR emission have focused on nearby galaxies that have undergone significant interactions, which may not be representative of the general properties of normal, isolated spiral galaxies. 

Therefore, our main goal in this work is to understand the FIR distribution and its relation to the star-forming activity in these two nearby spiral galaxies that resemble the Milky Way in its normal state. We found that both NGC2841 and NGC2976 have relatively weak FIR emission and exhibit centrally-peaked FIR surface brightness profiles as well as a clear correlation between the FIR and Hα emission. Our findings suggest that the FIR emission may be a reliable tracer of the star-forming activity in galaxies, even in normal and isolated spiral galaxies.

Our methodology involved analyzing the AKARI/FIS data by using the multi-component fitting technique and fitting the observed FIR spectral energy distributions with a combination of dust components to obtain the spatially-resolved FIR properties in each galaxy. Our study provides valuable information on the FIR properties in normal spiral galaxies, which is crucial for our understanding of the nature of star formation and the origins and evolution of dust in galaxies.

This paper presents a study of quasi-steady spherical accretion in the early
Universe, before the formation of the first stars and galaxies. The main
motivation is to derive the basic formulas that will be used in a companion
paper to calculate the accretion luminosity of primordial black holes and their
effect on the cosmic ionization history.
  The following cosmological effects are investigated: the coupling of the gas
to the CMB photon fluid (i.e., Compton drag), Hubble expansion, and the growth
of the dark matter halo seeded by the gravitational potential of the central
point mass. The gas equations of motion are solved assuming either a polytropic
or an isothermal equation of state. We consider the cases in which the
accreting object is a point mass or a spherical dark matter halo with power-law
density profile, as predicted by the theory of "secondary infall''. Analytical
solutions for the sonic radius and fitting formulas for the accretion rate are
provided.
  Different accretion regimes exist depending on the mass of the accreting
object. If the black hole mass is smaller than 50-100 Msun, gas accretion is
unaffected by Compton drag. A point mass and an extended dark halo of equal
mass accrete at the same rate if M>5000 Msun, while smaller mass dark halos
accrete less efficiently than the equivalent point mass. For masses M>3 x 10^4
Msun, the viscous term due to the Hubble expansion becomes important and the
assumption of quasi-steady flow fails. Hence, the steady Bondi solutions
transition to the time-dependent self-similar solutions for "cold cosmological
infall".

In this work, we present a novel approach for probing the high redshift neutral hydrogen (HI) fog, which has remained elusive so far. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that the HI fog is thought to be responsible for the damping of Lyman-alpha photons from distant galaxies, thus hiding crucial information about the early universe. Furthermore, the HI fog can also reveal insights into the formation and evolution of galaxies, so any attempts to unveil it hold significant value.

We demonstrate our approach by combining deep learning techniques with hydrodynamical simulations to predict the HI distribution in the early universe. The results obtained through our method show strong correlations with the true HI distribution and offer several advantages over traditional observational methods. Our methodology offers a direct and efficient method of studying the HI fog, which can avoid uncertainties and biases introduced by other techniques. Furthermore, our work paves the way for new avenues of research, such as exploring the connection between the HI fog and the cosmic web.

Overall, this work presents a novel and promising approach to unearth the elusive HI fog, which holds immense potential for understanding the early universe and the formation and evolution of galaxies.

An epidemic multi-strain model with temporary cross-immunity shows chaos,
even in a previously unexpected parameter region. Especially dengue fever
models with strong enhanced infectivity on secondary infection have previously
shown deterministic chaos motivated by experimental findings of
antibody-dependent-enhancement (ADE). Including temporary cross-immunity in
such models, which is common knowledge among field researchers in dengue, we
find a deterministically chaotic attractor in the more realistic parameter
region of reduced infectivity on secondary infection (''inverse ADE'' parameter
region). This is realistic for dengue fever since on second infection people
are more likely to be hospitalized, hence do not contribute to the force of
infection as much as people with first infection.
  Our finding has wider implications beyond dengue in any multi-strain
epidemiological systems with altered infectivity upon secondary infection,
since we can relax the condition of rather high infectivity on secondary
infection previously required for deterministic chaos. For dengue the finding
of wide ranges of chaotic attractors open new ways to analysis of existing data
sets.

We investigate the Fractional Quantum Hall Effect (FQHE) in optical lattices, using the Hofstadter model with Zeeman coupling. Our work aims to address the following fundamental problems in solid state physics: the nature of strongly correlated electron systems, and the measurement of topological quantum states. We report the following results. Firstly, we show the emergence of the FQHE in the optical lattice, which is a hallmark of strong correlations. Secondly, we demonstrate the topological nature of the FQHE by the computation of the Chern number. We find that it is possible to engineer an optical lattice which supports the FQHE, and that the properties of these systems can be analyzed with well-established methods from the solid state physics literature. Our methodology relies on a combination of exact diagonalization, mean-field theory, and a Chern number computation. By investigating a range of different parameters, we discuss how the FQHE can be experimentally realized and how to characterize its topological properties. Our results provide insights into the potential applications of optical lattices to study strongly correlated systems, as well as the theoretical understanding of topological quantum states.

We present results of a 20 ksec X-ray observation of the Wolf-Rayet (WR)
binary system WR 147 obtained with XMM-Newton. Previous studies have shown that
this system consists of a nitrogen-type WN8 star plus an OB companion whose
winds are interacting to produce a colliding wind shock. X-ray spectra from the
pn and MOS detectors confirm the high extinction reported from IR studies and
reveal hot plasma including the first detection of the Fe K-alpha line complex
at 6.67 keV. Spectral fits with a constant-temperature plane-parallel shock
model give a shock temperature kT(shock) = 2.7 keV [T(shock) ~ 31 MK], close to
but slightly hotter than the maximum temperature predicted for a colliding wind
shock. Optically thin plasma models suggest even higher temperatures, which are
not yet ruled out. The X-ray spectra are harder than can be accounted for using
2D numerical colliding wind shock models based on nominal mass-loss parameters.
Possible explanations include: (i) underestimates of the terminal wind speeds
or wind abundances, (ii) overly simplistic colliding wind models, or (iii) the
presence of other X-ray emission mechanisms besides colliding wind shocks.
Further improvement of the numerical models to include potentially important
physics such as non-equilibrium ionization will be needed to rigorously test
the colliding wind interpretation.

In this work, we investigate the space velocities of 10 southern globular clusters in the low galactic latitude region. Previous studies of globular clusters focused on the high galactic latitude region, but understanding the dynamics of clusters in the low latitude region is essential to accurately model the overall motion of the Milky Way. Our aim is to determine the three-dimensional velocities of the clusters using radial velocities and proper motions taken from the literature. Additionally, we measure distances to the clusters using the infrared magnitude of the horizontal branch method. Our results show that the clusters in the low galactic latitude region have a higher velocity dispersion in the vertical direction compared to the clusters in the high galactic latitude region. This suggests that the low latitude clusters may be more susceptible to tidal disruption by the Galactic disk. Furthermore, we find evidence for significant rotation in the clusters, with a possible trend towards prograde rotation for clusters with smaller Galactocentric distances. Our study highlights the importance of studying clusters in the low galactic latitude region to further our understanding of the Milky Way’s formation and evolution.

Numerous ongoing experiments aim at detecting WIMP dark matter particles from
the galactic halo directly through WIMP-nucleon interactions. Once such a
detection is established a confirmation of the galactic origin of the signal is
needed. This requires a direction-sensitive detector. We show that such a
detector can measure the velocity anisotropy beta of the galactic halo.
Cosmological N-body simulations predict the dark matter anisotropy to be
nonzero, beta~0.2. Baryonic matter has beta=0 and therefore a detection of a
nonzero beta would be strong proof of the fundamental difference between dark
and baryonic matter. We estimate the sensitivity for various detector
configurations using Monte Carlo methods and we show that the strongest signal
is found in the relatively few high recoil energy events. Measuring beta to the
precision of ~0.03 will require detecting more than 10^4 WIMP events with
nuclear recoil energies greater than 100 keV for a WIMP mass of 100 GeV and a
32S target. This number corresponds to ~10^6 events at all energies. We discuss
variations with respect to input parameters and we show that our method is
robust to the presence of backgrounds and discuss the possible improved
sensitivity for an energy-sensitive detector.

We investigate the Bondi accretion mechanism in the early universe to understand the formation of massive black holes (MBHs) with masses of $\sim 10^9 M_\odot$ observed in quasars at redshifts $z\gtrsim 6$. The rapid accretion rates necessary for their formation cannot be explained by traditional accretion models, which assume negligible gas velocities and density fluctuations. Bondi accretion describes the flow of gas onto a central point-like object. In this study, we explore Bondi accretion under the conditions of the early universe and attempt to reconcile its predictions with the formation of MBHs. Using numerical simulations with an adaptive-mesh refinement code, we find that the Bondi radius is highly sensitive to the cosmic density field and gas velocity dispersion. Additionally, we find that the gas density inside the radius is weakly dependent on the halo mass, gas temperature, and the redshift. Our results indicate that Bondi accretion could be responsible for the rapid growth of MBHs at high redshifts, although additional factors, such as mergers and feedback, may also play a role. Our research provides a promising avenue for future studies of MBH formation and evolution in the early universe.

The dust-to-gas ratios in three different samples of luminous, ultraluminous,
and hyperluminous infrared galaxies are calculated by modelling their radio to
soft X-ray spectral energy distributions using composite models which account
for the photoionizing radiation from HII regions, starbursts, or AGNs, and for
shocks. The models are limited to a set which broadly reproduces the mid-IR
fine structure line ratios of local, IR bright, starburst galaxies. The results
show that two types of clouds contribute to the IR emission. Those
characterized by low shock velocities and low preshock densities explain the
far-IR dust emission, while those with higher velocities and densities
contribute to mid-IR dust emission. An AGN is found in nearly all of the
ultraluminous IR galaxies and in half of the luminous IR galaxies of the
sample. High IR luminosities depend on dust-to-gas ratios of about 0.1 by mass,
however, most hyperluminous IR galaxies show dust-to-gas ratios much lower than
those calculated for the luminous and ultraluminous IR galaxies.

Historically, variable and transient sources have both surprised astronomers
and provided new views of the heavens. Here we report the discovery of an
optical transient in the outskirts of the lenticular galaxy Messier 85 in the
Virgo Cluster. With a peak absolute R magnitude of -12 this event is distinctly
brighter than novae, but fainter than type Ia supernovae (expected from a
population of old stars in lenticular galaxies). Archival images of the field
do not show a luminous star at that position with an upper limit of ~-4.1, so
it is unlikely to be a giant eruption from a luminous blue variable star. Over
a two month period the transient emitted radiation energy of almost 10E47 erg
and subsequently faded in the opical sky. It is similar to, but more luminous
at peak by a factor of 6 than, an enigmatic transient in the galaxy M31. A
possible origin of M85 OT2006-1 is a stellar merger. If so, searches for
similar events in nearby galaxies will not only allow to study the physics of
hyper-Eddington sources, but also probe an important phase in the evolution of
stellar binary systems.

Given a multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) channel, feedback from the
receiver can be used to specify a transmit precoding matrix, which selectively
activates the strongest channel modes. Here we analyze the performance of
Random Vector Quantization (RVQ), in which the precoding matrix is selected
from a random codebook containing independent, isotropically distributed
entries. We assume that channel elements are i.i.d. and known to the receiver,
which relays the optimal (rate-maximizing) precoder codebook index to the
transmitter using B bits. We first derive the large system capacity of
beamforming (rank-one precoding matrix) as a function of B, where large system
refers to the limit as B and the number of transmit and receive antennas all go
to infinity with fixed ratios. With beamforming RVQ is asymptotically optimal,
i.e., no other quantization scheme can achieve a larger asymptotic rate. The
performance of RVQ is also compared with that of a simpler reduced-rank scalar
quantization scheme in which the beamformer is constrained to lie in a random
subspace. We subsequently consider a precoding matrix with arbitrary rank, and
approximate the asymptotic RVQ performance with optimal and linear receivers
(matched filter and Minimum Mean Squared Error (MMSE)). Numerical examples show
that these approximations accurately predict the performance of finite-size
systems of interest. Given a target spectral efficiency, numerical examples
show that the amount of feedback required by the linear MMSE receiver is only
slightly more than that required by the optimal receiver, whereas the matched
filter can require significantly more feedback.

Using archival RXTE data we derive the 2-16 keV Fourier-resolved spectra of
the Atoll source 4U 1728-34 in a sequence of its timing states as its low QPO
frequency spans the range between 6 and 94 Hz. The increase in the QPO
frequency accompanies a spectral transition of the source from its island to
its banana states. The banana-states' Fourier-resolved spectra are well fitted
by a single blackbody component with $kT \sim 2-3$ keV depending on the source
position in the color -- color diagram and the Fourier frequency, thus
indicating that this spectral component is responsible for the source
variability on these timescales. This result is in approximate agreement with
similar behavior exhibited by the Z sources, suggesting that, as in that case,
the boundary layer -- the likely source of the thermal component -- is
supported by radiation pressure. Furthermore, it is found that the iron line at
$\sim$6.6 keV, clearly present in the averaged spectra, not apparent within the
limitations of our measurements in the frequency-resolved spectra irrespective
of the frequency range. This would indicate that this spectral component
exhibits little variability on time scales comprising the interval
$10^{-2}-10^2$ seconds. In the island state the single blackbody model proved
inadequate, particularly notable in our lowest frequency band ($0.008-0.8$ Hz).
An absorbed powerlaw or an additive blackbody plus hard powerlaw model was
required to obtain a satisfactory fit. Statistics do not allow unambiguous
discrimination between these possible scenarios.

In this study, we investigate a double main sequence turn-off (DMSTO) in the star cluster NGC 1846 situated in the Large Magellanic Cloud. Using deep imaging observations obtained from the Hubble Space Telescope, we identify the presence of a DMSTO in the color-magnitude diagram of the cluster. Our analysis reveals that the cluster's DMSTO is comprised of two distinct stellar populations with different ages, masses, and metallicities. We find that the population contributing to the blue main sequence is older and metal-poor, while the population comprising the red main sequence is younger and metal-rich.

The presence of a DMSTO is a puzzle for the standard models of stellar evolution, but our work sheds new light on this phenomenon by demonstrating that it can be explained by the presence of multiple stellar populations within a single cluster. Our findings provide valuable insights into the formation and evolution of star clusters, indicating that multiple generations of stars can coexist and evolve in the same system.

This research is motivated by the need to better understand the properties of DMSTOs and how they relate to the characteristics of star clusters. Our results have important implications for the study of the Milky Way's halo, which is expected to contain many ancient globular clusters hosting multiple stellar populations. Ultimately, our work contributes to the broader goal of unveiling the complex interplay between star formation, galaxy assembly, and cosmic evolution.

In this study, we investigate the X-ray emission in gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) using extensive observations of Swift and Fermi satellites. The motivation for this research lies in the need to understand the afterglow emission mechanisms of GRBs which have been regarded as the most luminous explosions in the Universe. X-ray emission is crucial for understanding the physical processes that occur during the afterglow phase of GRBs. Our research aims to solve the problem of identifying the origin of the observed X-ray radiation in GRBs.

Our results show that the X-ray emission in the afterglow phase comes from two distinct synchrotron components – forward and reverse shock. We observe a strong correlation in the X-ray emission between these two components, and the temporal behaviors of the two components are found to be linked. Our analysis also reveals that the observed X-ray emission is consistent with the predictions of the external shock model and, thus, supports the idea that the afterglow emission in GRBs is dominated by synchrotron radiation from shock-accelerated electrons.

Our findings provide compelling evidence for the importance of the synchrotron process in the afterglow of GRBs, and our research fills in a critical gap in our understanding of these enigmatic and fascinating astrophysical phenomena.

In this paper, we present our research on the relationship between the directed star arboricity and Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM) networks. The motivation behind our research stems from the need to improve the efficiency of WDM networks with respect to their implementation and performance, as well as the objective of uncovering new theoretical insights that can aid in our understanding of the underlying structure of these networks. The problem we attempt to solve is the characterization of the directed star arboricity of WDM networks, which helps us determine the minimum number of colors required to edge-color graphs, and, in turn, the minimum number of wavelengths required in WDM networks. 

Our results show that the directed star arboricity of WDM networks, i.e., the minimum number of colors required to edge-color such graphs, is equal to their fractional chromatic number. We further prove that the fractional chromatic number of WDM networks is a lower bound for the maximum throughput of these networks. These findings have significant implications for WDM network design and optimization, as they provide an improved understanding of the fundamental properties of these networks. This work contributes to the theoretical framework underlying the analysis and design of WDM networks and can help improve their performance in practice.

In this research, we address the optimization of a novel prismatic drive. The problem we attempt to solve is the lack of efficient and precise linear motion components capable of handling loads in various industrial applications. Our prismatic drive design utilizes high precision, ball screw-driven prismatic pairs that enable accurate linear motion and reduced friction. However, this innovative mechanism is not fully optimized and faces several challenges, such as oscillation, vibration, and noise. 

To tackle these constraints, we propose a comprehensive approach that combines optimization algorithms, simulation tools, and experimental studies. By employing a multi-objective optimization technique, we effectively explore the design space and identify the best tradeoffs in terms of performance criteria, namely displacement accuracy, stiffness, and damping ratio. We then utilize finite element analysis and time-domain simulation to verify the optimized design and investigate the dynamic behavior of the prismatic drive.

Our motivation for this research stems from the growing demand for high precision and low-friction linear drives in various fields like robotics, aerospace, and automotive. The proposed prismatic drive system has significant potential in these areas, particularly in applications requiring high precision and dynamic performance. This optimization study paves the way for future developments and enhancements of this novel mechanism and opens new horizons for efficient and accurate linear motion components.

In this work, we investigate the relationship between the weighted complexity and the determinant functions of graphs. Our motivation stems from the fact that the study of spectral graph theory has become increasingly relevant in fields such as computer science, physics, and chemistry. Furthermore, the weighted complexity of a graph measures the amount of information contained in its adjacency matrix, which is a foundational object in spectral graph theory. Our objective is to exemplify the importance of the weighted complexity by establishing a connection between this parameter and the determinant function of the graph. 

To achieve our goal, we first introduce the definition of the weighted complexity and study its properties. Next, we provide a thorough discussion of the determinant function, including its definition, properties, and various known bounds and estimations. We then present our main results, which include formulas that relate the weighted complexity of a graph to the values and moments of the determinant function. Our findings not only exhibit the utility of the weighted complexity, but also provide new insights into the determinant function, which is a central object in graph theory and beyond.

Advanced electromagnetic potentials are indigenous to the classical Maxwell
theory. Generally however they are deemed undesirable and are forcibly
excluded, destroying the theory's inherent time-symmetry. We investigate the
reason for this, pointing out that it is not necessary and in some cases is
counter-productive. We then focus on the direct-action theory in which the
advanced and retarded contributions are present symmetrically, with no
opportunity to supplement the particular integral solution of the wave equation
with an arbitrary complementary function. One then requires a plausible
explanation for the observed broken symmetry that, commonly, is understood
cannot be met by the Wheeler-Feynman mechanism because the necessary boundary
condition cannot be satisfied in acceptable cosmologies. We take this
opportunity to argue that the boundary condition is already met by all
expanding cosmologies simply as a result of cosmological red-shift. A
consequence is that the cosmological and thermodynamic arrows of time can be
equated, the direct action version of EM is preferred, and that advanced
potentials are ubiquitous.

In this study, we investigate the interaction of a charge with a thin plasma sheet. The problem we attempt to solve is to understand how a charge and a plasma sheet interact with each other in response to an external field. The motivation for our research is to advance our understanding of the behavior of plasmas in space, specifically in the Earth's magnetosphere and its interaction with the solar wind.

Our key idea is to use the Vlasov-Maxwell equations to model the charge-plasma interaction. We examine the different modes of the plasma sheet, such as the Kelvin-Helmholtz instability and the tearing instability, and the effect they have on the charge. We also consider the different properties of the plasma, such as temperature and density.

To carry out our research, we use numerical simulations to understand the dynamics of the interaction between the charge and the plasma sheet. Our methodology involves constructing a model of the plasma sheet, with the charge inserted at different locations and velocities. We then simulate the dynamics of the plasma sheet and the charge for different time periods to understand the behavior of the interaction.

Our findings show that the behavior of the plasma sheet and the charge are strongly influenced by the modes of the plasma sheet. The results of this study have implications for understanding the behavior of the magnetosphere and its interaction with the external environment, such as the solar wind.

In this study, we investigate the influence of oxygen partial pressure on the structural, transport, and magnetic properties of Co-doped TiO2 films. The motivation for this research stems from the increasing demand for materials with multifunctional properties, particularly those with the potential to exhibit both magnetic and semiconducting behaviors. Understanding the underlying factors that affect the magnetic and transport properties of Co-doped TiO2 films is critical for engineering new materials for use in spintronic and magnetic sensing applications.

To achieve this, we grew Co-doped TiO2 films under varying oxygen partial pressures using pulsed laser deposition. We then conducted a thorough characterization of the samples’ structural, transport, and magnetic properties. Our results indicate that the oxygen partial pressure strongly influences the structural properties of the films, with a transition from an anatase to rutile structure observed at lower oxygen partial pressures. Moreover, we find that the oxygen partial pressure also influences the magnetic and transport properties of the Co-doped TiO2 films, with a significant improvement in both properties observed at higher oxygen partial pressures.

In summary, our work demonstrates the potential for manipulating the oxygen partial pressure during the growth of Co-doped TiO2 films to engineer materials with tailored magnetic and transport properties for use in spintronic and magnetic sensing applications.

We present our study on the baryon density at redshifts between z=0.9-1.9, based on the observation of the broad Lyman-alpha absorption. Our research aims to trace the warm-hot intergalactic medium (WHIM), which consists of gas with temperatures between 10^5-10^7 K. The WHIM has been missing from previous studies of the cosmic baryon budget, and we aim to determine its contribution to the total baryon density.

To achieve this goal, we used the European Southern Observatory’s Very Large Telescope to observe a sample of 33 quasars, which were selected based on their redshifts and other properties. We analyzed the spectra of our sample to measure the broad Lyman-alpha absorption, which is known to be a signal of the WHIM.

Our results show that the WHIM contributes significantly to the total baryon density, accounting for up to 30% of the cosmic baryon budget. This finding has implications for our understanding of the distribution of matter in the universe and the formation of structures such as galaxies and clusters of galaxies.

In conclusion, our study highlights the importance of tracing the WHIM in order to have a complete picture of the baryonic matter in the universe. By utilizing the broad Lyman-alpha absorption, we were able to detect this elusive component and provide new insights into the cosmic baryon budget.

In this paper, we explore the nature of complexity in systems and consider the factors that contribute to self-organization and emergence in such systems. Our work attempts to solve the problem of defining complexity as a fundamental concept in scientific and academic research. We argue that complexity is not just a matter of size or quantity, but rather a function of the relationships between individual components within a system and the emergent behaviors that arise from these interactions.

To illustrate our approach, we consider a wide range of examples, including ecological systems, social networks, and biological organisms. Through our analysis, we identify several key factors that contribute to complex behavior in these systems, including non-linearity, feedback mechanisms, and the role of information flow.

Ultimately, our work is motivated by a desire to develop a more nuanced and accurate understanding of complex systems, one which can inform our thinking across a range of domains, from economics and business to engineering and computer science. We hope that our approach will prove useful not only in theoretical and academic research, but also in practical applications, as we seek to develop more effective strategies for managing and controlling complex systems.

This letter extends our previous fluctuation analysis of the near infrared
background at 1.6 microns to the 1.1 micron (F110W) image of the Hubble Ultra
Deep field. When all detectable sources are removed the ratio of fluctuation
power in the two images is consistent with the ratio expected for faint, z<8,
sources, and is inconsistent with the expected ratio for galaxies with z>8. We
also use numerically redshifted model galaxy spectral energy distributions for
50 and 10 million year old galaxies to predict the expected fluctuation power
at 3.6 microns and 4.5 microns to compare with recent Spitzer observations. The
predicted fluctuation power for galaxies at z = 0-12 matches the observed
Spitzer fluctuation power while the predicted power for z>13 galaxies is much
higher than the observed values. As was found in the 1.6 micron (F160W)
