analysis the fluctuation power in the source subtracted F110W image is two
orders of magnitude below the power in the image with all sources present. This
leads to the conclusion that the 0.8--1.8 micron near infrared background is
due to resolved galaxies in the redshift range z<8, with the majority of power
in the redshift range of 0.5--1.5.

Author offers and researched the new and revolutionary inflatable
electrostatic AB space towers (mast, new space elevator) up to one hundred
twenty thousands kilometers (or more) in height. The main innovation is filling
the tower by electron gas, which can create pressure up one atmosphere, has
negligible small weight and surprising properties.
  The suggested mast has following advantages in comparison with conventional
space elevator: 1. Electrostatic AB tower may be built from Earth surface
without the employment of any rockets. That decreases the cost of electrostatic
mast by thousands of times. 2. One can have any height and has a big control
load capacity. 3. Electrostatic tower can have the height of a geosynchronous
orbit (36,000 km) WITHOUT the additional top cable as the space elevator (up
120,000 - 160,000 km) and counterweight (equalizer) of hundreds of tons. 4. The
offered mast has less total mass than conventional space elevator. 5. The
offered tower can have the high-speed electrostatic climbers moved by
high-voltage electricity from Earth's surface. 6. The electrostatic mast can
bend in any needed direction when we give the necessary electric voltage in the
required parts of the extended mast. 7. Control mast has stability for any
altitude. Three projects 100 km, 36,000km (GEO), 120,000 km are computed and
presented.

In this paper, we present a novel approach to studying regular CW-complexes using shelling-type orderings and acyclic matchings of their corresponding Salvetti complexes. The motivation for this research lies in the desire to develop new tools for analyzing the combinatorial structure of these complex spaces and understanding their topological properties. Our main result is the construction of a shelling-type ordering and acyclic matching of the Salvetti complex of any regular CW-complex. This is achieved by introducing a new combinatorial tool called the "Lefschetz property," which allows us to establish the existence of such orderings and matchings. 

To give some context, shelling-type orderings are a fundamental tool in the study of cellular complexes, while acyclic matchings have been used extensively in the analysis of simplicial complexes. However, their usage in the study of CW-complexes has not been explored to date. Our key idea is to use the combinatorial structure of the Salvetti complex of the given CW-complex to define a shelling-type ordering and an acyclic matching. This enables us to prove a number of important structural results for regular CW-complexes, which could not be established using other standard techniques for simplicial or cellular complexes. 

Our methodology is based on a combination of topological and combinatorial arguments. We use the theory of posets, the Lefschetz fixed point theorem, and the theory of acyclic matchings to establish our main results. Overall, our work provides a new and powerful framework for studying the combinatorial and topological properties of regular CW-complexes, which has potential applications in a wide range of areas, such as algebraic topology, computational geometry, and combinatorial optimization.

Using a combination of Chandra and XMM observations, we confirmed the
presence of a significant velocity gradient along the NE/E-W/SW direction in
the intracluster gas of the cluster Abell 576. The results are consistent with
a previous ASCA SIS analysis of this cluster. The error weighted average over
ACIS-S3, EPIC MOS 1 & 2 spectrometers for the maximum velocity difference is
>3.3E03 km/s at the 90% confidence level, similar to the velocity limits
estimated indirectly for the "bullet" cluster (1E0657-56). The probability that
the velocity gradient is generated by standard random gain fluctuations with
Chandra and XMM is <0.1%. The regions of maximum velocity gradient are in CCD
zones that have the lowest temporal gain variations. It is unlikely that the
velocity gradient is due to Hubble distance differences between projected
clusters (probability<~0.01%). We mapped the distribution of elemental
abundance ratios across the cluster and detected a strong chemical
discontinuity using the abundance ratio of silicon to iron, equivalent to a
variation from 100% SN Ia iron mass fraction in the West-Northwest regions to
32% in the Eastern region. The "center" of the cluster is located at the
chemical discontinuity boundary, which is inconsistent with the radially
symmetric chemical gradient found in some regular clusters, but consistent with
a cluster merging scenario. We predict that the velocity gradient as measured
will produce a variation of the CMB temperature towards the East of the core of
the cluster that will be detectable by current and near-future bolometers. The
measured velocity gradient opens for the possibility that this cluster is
passing through a near line-of-sight merger stage where the cores have recently
crossed.

In this work, we present a detailed discussion on particle number and quantum indistinguishability in the context of many-body quantum systems. The problem we attempt to solve is to shed light on the effects of particle exchange and quantum statistics on the properties of such systems. In particular, we explore the implications of quantum indistinguishability which leads to a fundamental limitation on our ability to count the number of identical particles within a given volume. 

Our analysis reveals that the particle number in a quantum system is an emergent property that depends on the statistics of the particles and the symmetry constraints imposed by the system. This result challenges the classical notion of counting discrete objects and highlights the importance of considering the quantum nature of the system.

We employ a theoretical approach based on the use of the second quantization formalism and Green's function techniques. We show that these methods provide a powerful framework for addressing the problem of particle number and quantum indistinguishability.

Our findings have implications for a wide range of many-body systems, including quantum gases, solids, and plasmas. Furthermore, our work provides a deeper understanding of the fundamental properties of quantum systems, which has implications for the development of new quantum technologies.

In this study, we investigate the periodic variability in the infrared excess of the young binary system UZ Tau E and propose a possible mechanism that could account for this variability. Circumbinary disks are known to exist around some binary systems, and we explore the possibility that accretion from such a disk could produce the observed periodic signal. We use data from the Spitzer Space Telescope and model the infrared excess as the sum of an accretion component and a dust component. We fit the parameters of these components to the observed data using Bayesian inference, and find that an accretion component that varies periodically with a period of approximately 39 days provides a good fit to the data. We discuss possible physical mechanisms that could produce this periodicity, including gravitational interactions with the companion star and instabilities in the circumbinary disk. Our results suggest that periodic accretion from a circumbinary disk is a viable explanation for the observed variability in UZ Tau E, and demonstrates the importance of circumbinary disks in shaping the early evolution of binary systems.

We conducted a study to investigate if Eupelmus vuilleti Crawford, a parasitoid wasp, can eliminate Eupelmus orientalis Crawford, another parasitoid wasp, from storage systems of cowpea (Vigna unguiculata Walp). The motivation for this research was the need to find an efficient biological control strategy for the cowpea seed beetle, Callosobruchus maculatus, the major pest of stored cowpea seeds. The methodology involved collecting infested cowpea seeds from storage systems in different locations in Nigeria, identifying the parasites present and conducting laboratory experiments to determine the competitive ability and host specificity of the two wasps. Our results confirmed that E. vuilleti was highly competitive and specific in attacking the larvae of C. maculatus, while E. orientalis was less effective. Interestingly, when introduced into infested storage systems, E. vuilleti reduced the population of C. maculatus and displaced E. orientalis. Hence, we conclude that E. vuilleti can effectively eliminate E. orientalis from storage systems and can be employed in biological control programs for stored cowpea seeds. Further studies are needed to determine the effectiveness of this strategy under field conditions.

In this paper we will first introduce the notion of affine structures on a
ringed space and then obtain several properties. Affine structures on a ringed
space, arising mainly from complex analytical spaces of algebraic schemes over
number fields, behave like differential structures on a smooth manifold.
  As one does for differential manifolds, we will use pseudogroups of affine
transformations to define affine atlases on a ringed space. An atlas on a space
is said to be an affine structure if it is maximal. An affine structure is
admissible if there is a sheaf on the underlying space such that they are
coincide on all affine charts, which are in deed affine open sets of a scheme.
In a rigour manner, a scheme is defined to be a ringed space with a specified
affine structure if the affine structures are in action in some special cases
such as analytical spaces of algebraic schemes. Particularly, by the whole of
affine structures on a space, we will obtain respectively necessary and
sufficient conditions that two spaces are homeomorphic and that two schemes are
isomorphic, which are the two main theorems of the paper. It follows that the
whole of affine structures on a space and a scheme, as local data, encode and
reflect the global properties of the space and the scheme, respectively.

In this work, we delve into the relationship between Dyson indices and Hilbert-Schmidt separability functions and probabilities. The main problem we attempt to solve is to determine the extent to which the Dyson indices and Hilbert-Schmidt separability functions are correlated, and how this correlation affects the probabilities of the separability of density matrices. 

Our findings indicate that there exists a fascinating connection between the two concepts. Specifically, we demonstrate that the Dyson indices and Hilbert-Schmidt separability functions have an intricate correlation characterized by a series of unique properties. We apply these properties to study the conditions necessary for separability, and solve the problem of identifying the extent to which separability probabilities are influenced by changes in the Dyson indices and Hilbert-Schmidt separability functions. Our analysis of these properties leads to the development of an efficient solution algorithm that provides accurate predictions of separability probabilities. 

The key ideas and methodology in this work draw upon the fundamental principles of quantum mechanics and tools from algebraic topology. The use of formal mathematical language and rigorous proofs form the crux of our scientific writing voice, communicating our findings systematically and persuasively. Overall, the results of our study have important implications for the development of novel quantum computing techniques and quantum error correction schemes.

In this study, we present HST photometry for the halo stars in the Leo elliptical NGC 3377. Our analysis shows a clear detection of the stellar halo, extending to a distance of ~20 kpc from the galaxy center. We obtained a total of 22,214 objects, with photometric uncertainty better than 0.2 magnitudes. The color-magnitude diagram reveals a prominent red giant branch, with a significant population of blue and horizontal branch stars. The density profile of the halo shows a steeper slope than the outer disk region, suggesting a different formation history for the halo.

This research aims to investigate the structural properties of the stellar halo in NGC 3377, which has been poorly studied compared to other nearby galaxies. The detection of a significant population of blue and horizontal branch stars indicates that NGC 3377 possesses a significant metal-poor component, which can provide important insight into the early star formation history of this galaxy. The study also contributes to our understanding of the formation and evolution of elliptical galaxies, which are crucial in the hierarchical structure formation scenario. By obtaining detailed photometry of the halo stars in NGC 3377, we hope to uncover more clues to the galaxy's formation history and the underlying physical processes that drive the formation of the most massive galaxies in the universe.

This paper considers the quantization problem on the Grassmann manifold with
dimension n and p. The unique contribution is the derivation of a closed-form
formula for the volume of a metric ball in the Grassmann manifold when the
radius is sufficiently small. This volume formula holds for Grassmann manifolds
with arbitrary dimension n and p, while previous results are only valid for
either p=1 or a fixed p with asymptotically large n. Based on the volume
formula, the Gilbert-Varshamov and Hamming bounds for sphere packings are
obtained. Assuming a uniformly distributed source and a distortion metric based
on the squared chordal distance, tight lower and upper bounds are established
for the distortion rate tradeoff. Simulation results match the derived results.
As an application of the derived quantization bounds, the information rate of a
Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) system with finite-rate channel-state
feedback is accurately quantified for arbitrary finite number of antennas,
while previous results are only valid for either Multiple-Input Single-Output
(MISO) systems or those with asymptotically large number of transmit antennas
but fixed number of receive antennas.

We study Gauge-Higgs unification model on the orbifold $S^{1}/Z_{2}$ based on
the extended SM gauge group $G_{SM}^{ex}=SU(2)_{L} \times U(1)_{Y} \times
SO(3)_{F}$. The group $SO(3)_{F}$ is treated as a chiral gauged flavour
symmetry. Electroweak-, flavour- and Higgs interactions are unified in one
single gauge group SU(7) which is broken again down to $G_{SM}^{ex}$ by
orbifolding and imposing additional boundary conditions. The compactification
scale is O(1) TeV. The orbifold $S^{1}/Z_{2}$ is put on a lattice. This setting
gives a staring point for RG-transformations. As a result the bulk is
integrated out and the extra dimension consist of only two points: the orbifold
fixed points. Parallel transporters (PT) in the extra dimension become
nonunitary as a result of the blockspin transformations. In addition, a Higgs
potential emerges naturally. The PTs can be written as a product $e^{A_{y}}
e^{\eta} e^{A_{y}}$ of unitary factors $e^{A_{y}}$ and a selfadjoint factor
$e^{\eta}$. The reduction $48 \to 35 + 6 + \bar{6} + 1$ of the adjoint reps of
SU(7) with respect to $SU(6) \supset G_{SM}^{ex}$ leads to three $SU(2)_{L}$
Higgs doublets: one for each flavour. Their zero modes serve as a substitute
for the SM Higgs. When $G_{SM}^{ex}$ is spontaneously broken down to
$U(1)_{em}$, an exponential gauge boson mass splitting occurs naturally. This
breaking leads to $SO(3)_{F}$ flavour gauge boson masses much above the
compactification scale. Thus tree-level FCNC are naturally suppressed. Making
some simplifying assumptions we also calculate fermion masses and CKM mixing
angles. As for the gauge bosons an exponential fermion mass splitting occurs
naturally. The model predicts a large Higgs sector consisting of 30 Higgs
particles and in its simplest form the weak mixing angle $\theta=0.125$.

In this work, we investigate the electromagnetic response of high-Tc superconductors using two theoretical models: the slave-boson and doped-carrier theories. Our motivation stems from the need to understand the behavior of high-Tc superconductors, which exhibit unusually high critical temperatures compared to classical superconductors. The slave-boson theory proposes a new way of describing the quantum-mechanical many-particle system of high-Tc superconductors, whereas the doped-carrier theory treats the superconducting system as a type of doped semiconductor.

Our methodology involves a theoretical study of the two models by solving the relevant equations and analyzing the resulting electromagnetic properties such as the dielectric function, ac conductivity, and reflectivity. We apply our methods to YBCO and La-based cuprates and make predictions about the corresponding electromagnetic response.

Our results show that both slave-boson and doped-carrier theories offer insights into the unique electromagnetic properties of high-Tc superconductors. We find that the dielectric function, ac conductivity, and reflectivity predictions of the two models are consistent with experimental measurements. Additionally, we investigate the thermodynamic properties of high-Tc superconductors, and find that both theoretical models explain the temperature dependence of some thermodynamic quantities. Overall, our work contributes to the fundamental understanding of the behavior of high-Tc superconductors, which will enable the design of better superconducting materials with technological applications.

We present a characterization of the soluble nanoparticles formed through the Coulombic interaction of bovine serum albumin (BSA) with anionic graft copolymers at low pH. The stability of these colloidal systems is crucial for various biomedical and industrial applications. However, the formation of stable and uniform nanoparticles has been a challenging task because their characteristics depend on various factors such as pH, ionic strength, and polymer type. 

In this study, we used dynamic light scattering and transmission electron microscopy to investigate the effects of pH, NaCl concentration, and anionic graft copolymer composition on the formation of BSA-copolymer nanoparticles. Our results revealed that the size and stability of the nanoparticles varied significantly as a function of these parameters. Interestingly, we observed that the polymers with a high degree of branching and a long length provided the most stable nanoparticles at pH 4.0 with an optimal NaCl concentration. Moreover, we demonstrated that the interaction between the BSA and the polymer is driven by electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions.

Overall, our findings contribute to a fundamental understanding of the formation of BSA-copolymer nanoparticles and can inform the design of more robust and effective drug delivery vehicles and diagnostic agents.

In this paper we have presented the calculation of pair correlation functions
in a nematic phase for a model of spherical particles with the long-range
anisotropic interaction from the mean spherical approximation(MSA) and the
Percus-Yevick (PY) integral equation theories. The results found from the MSA
theory have been compared with those found analytically by Holovko and
Sokolovska (J. Mol. Liq. $\bf 82$, 161(1999)). A free energy functional which
involves both the symmetry conserving and symmetry broken parts of the direct
pair correlation function has been used to study the properties of the nematic
phase. We have also examined the possibility of constructing a free energy
functional with the direct pair correlation function which includes only the
principal order parameter of the ordered phase and found that the resulting
functional gives results that are in good agreement with the original
functional. The isotropic-nematic transition has been located using the grand
thermodynamic potential. The PY theory has been found to give nematic phase
with pair correlation function harmonic coefficients having all the desired
features. In a nematic phase the harmonic coefficient of the total pair
correlation function $h({\bf x_1},{\bf x_2})$ connected with the correlations
of the director transverse fluctuations should develop a long-range tail. This
feature has been found in both the MSA and PY theories.

We study the exponential scale length of total radio emission, the spectral
index distribution, and the linear radio polarization in the Scd galaxy M33.
Observations were carried out using the 3.6 cm dual channel and the 6.2 cm four
channel receivers of the 100--m Effelsberg telescope along with the L-band VLA
D--array at 20 cm. High spatial resolution and sensitivity in both total and
linearly polarized radio continuum emission from M33 were achieved. We found
considerable extended emission, not only from the main arms I S and I N, but
also from the weaker arms. The large--scale magnetic field exhibits
well--ordered spiral structure with almost the same orientation as that of the
optical spiral arms, however, it does not show a clear structural correlation
or anti--correlation with the optical arms. There is a north-south asymmetry in
polarization that is frequency-dependent. We found that the ring mean spectral
index versus radius increases faster beyond $R$ = 4 kpc. At each wavelength,
the exponential scale length is larger inside than outside $R$ = 4 kpc. From
the larger scales lengths at $R$ $<$ 4 kpc, we conclude that star forming
regions are mainly spread over the region $R$ $<$ 4 kpc without a dominant
nuclear concentration. Furthermore, at $R$ $<$ 4 kpc, a spatial correlation
between cosmic rays and star forming regions may exist. From the behaviour of
the mean spectral indices obtained from different pairs of the radio continuum
data at 3.6, 6.2, and 20 cm, we confirm that a decrease in the thermal fraction
causes an increase in the spectral index. The frequency-dependent asymmetry in
the polarization hints to an asymmetry in Faraday depolarization.

We systematically explore the evolution of the merger of two carbon-oxygen
(CO) white dwarfs. The dynamical evolution of a 0.9 Msun + 0.6 Msun CO white
dwarf merger is followed by a three-dimensional SPH simulation. We use an
elaborate prescription in which artificial viscosity is essentially absent,
unless a shock is detected, and a much larger number of SPH particles than
earlier calculations. Based on this simulation, we suggest that the central
region of the merger remnant can, once it has reached quasi-static equilibrium,
be approximated as a differentially rotating CO star, which consists of a
slowly rotating cold core and a rapidly rotating hot envelope surrounded by a
centrifugally supported disc. We construct a model of the CO remnant that
mimics the results of the SPH simulation using a one-dimensional hydrodynamic
stellar evolution code and then follow its secular evolution. The stellar
evolution models indicate that the growth of the cold core is controlled by
neutrino cooling at the interface between the core and the hot envelope, and
that carbon ignition in the envelope can be avoided despite high effective
accretion rates. This result suggests that the assumption of forced accretion
of cold matter that was adopted in previous studies of the evolution of double
CO white dwarf merger remnants may not be appropriate. Our results imply that
at least some products of double CO white dwarfs merger may be considered good
candidates for the progenitors of Type Ia supernovae. In this case, the
characteristic time delay between the initial dynamical merger and the eventual
explosion would be ~10^5 yr. (Abridged).

In this paper, we propose a variational geomagnetic data assimilation approach to understand the dynamics of magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) systems. We investigate a one-dimensional, nonlinear MHD system that is sparsely observed. Our methodology involves defining a cost function that quantifies the difference between available observational data and model predictions. Using this cost function, we then optimize the model parameters using a variational technique. The optimization problem is formulated as a minimization of the cost function subject to the constraints imposed by the MHD system. By using the variational assimilation approach, we can use all available data while being consistent with the physical laws governing the MHD system.

We apply our approach to a simulation of solar wind using a sparse set of observations, and we demonstrate the effectiveness of the variational assimilation approach in obtaining more accurate predictions compared to traditional data assimilation methods. Our results show that the proposed approach is capable of estimating the model parameters accurately and can provide realistic predictions of the magnetic field, plasma velocity, and pressure of the MHD system.

This work aims to solve the problem of estimating model parameters in MHD simulations using sparse and noisy observational data. Our approach provides a useful tool for integrating observational data into MHD simulations, thus improving the accuracy and reliability of predictions. The proposed methodology can be extended to multi-dimensional, time-varying MHD systems, making it suitable for applications in space weather forecasting, climate adaptation, and fluid dynamics.

We present here our study of the adiabatic quantum dynamics of a random Ising
chain across its quantum critical point. The model investigated is an Ising
chain in a transverse field with disorder present both in the exchange coupling
and in the transverse field. The transverse field term is proportional to a
function $\Gamma(t)$ which, as in the Kibble-Zurek mechanism, is linearly
reduced to zero in time with a rate $\tau^{-1}$, $\Gamma(t)=-t/\tau$, starting
at $t=-\infty$ from the quantum disordered phase ($\Gamma=\infty$) and ending
at $t=0$ in the classical ferromagnetic phase ($\Gamma=0$). We first analyze
the distribution of the gaps -- occurring at the critical point $\Gamma_c=1$ --
which are relevant for breaking the adiabaticity of the dynamics. We then
present extensive numerical simulations for the residual energy $E_{\rm res}$
and density of defects $\rho_k$ at the end of the annealing, as a function of
the annealing inverse rate $\tau$. %for different lenghts of the chain. Both
the average $E_{\rm res}(\tau)$ and $\rho_k(\tau)$ are found to behave
logarithmically for large $\tau$, but with different exponents, $[E_{\rm
res}(\tau)/L]_{\rm av}\sim 1/\ln^{\zeta}(\tau)$ with $\zeta\approx 3.4$, and
$[\rho_k(\tau)]_{\rm av}\sim 1/\ln^{2}(\tau)$. We propose a mechanism for
$1/\ln^2{\tau}$-behavior of $[\rho_k]_{\rm av}$ based on the Landau-Zener
tunneling theory and on a Fisher's type real-space renormalization group
analysis of the relevant gaps. The model proposed shows therefore a
paradigmatic example of how an adiabatic quantum computation can become very
slow when disorder is at play, even in absence of any source of frustration.

We present the results of our investigation on the growth behavior of millimeter-thick single-walled carbon nanotube (SWCNT) forests and possible growth mechanisms that give rise to the unique characteristics of these structures. Our motivation for this study stems from the potential of these forests to have large surface areas and high conductivity, which make them ideal for various applications including electrochemical energy storage, sensing, and electronic devices.

Our work involves optimizing the growth parameters to obtain uniform and dense SWCNT forests that are several millimeters thick. We investigate the growth window, which refers to the range of growth conditions that enable the formation of the desired structure, as well as the role of the catalyst and gas flow rates in achieving the desired growth behavior. Furthermore, we propose a possible growth mechanism based on the saturation of reactive sites and kinetic limitations that explain the dependence of forest thickness on the growth time.

The problem that our work attempts to solve is the lack of understanding of the growth behavior of millimeter-thick SWCNT forests, which hinders their widespread adoption in practical applications. Our study provides insights into the factors that affect the growth of these structures and a possible mechanism that can be used to rationally design and tailor their properties for specific applications.

This paper addresses the performance of bit-interleaved coded multiple
beamforming (BICMB) [1], [2] with imperfect knowledge of beamforming vectors.
Most studies for limited-rate channel state information at the transmitter
(CSIT) assume that the precoding matrix has an invariance property under an
arbitrary unitary transform. In BICMB, this property does not hold. On the
other hand, the optimum precoder and detector for BICMB are invariant under a
diagonal unitary transform. In order to design a limited-rate CSIT system for
BICMB, we propose a new distortion measure optimum under this invariance. Based
on this new distortion measure, we introduce a new set of centroids and employ
the generalized Lloyd algorithm for codebook design. We provide simulation
results demonstrating the performance improvement achieved with the proposed
distortion measure and the codebook design for various receivers with linear
detectors. We show that although these receivers have the same performance for
perfect CSIT, their performance varies under imperfect CSIT.

We present observations from the Microvariability and Oscillations of STars (MOST) satellite of the cool dwarf star kappa^1 Ceti. With a rotation period of about 10 days, the star exhibits significant differential rotation, with the equator rotating about 3% faster than the poles. We measure the differential rotation using the photometric modulation of the star due to spots rotating in and out of view. The observed differential rotation is higher than predicted by the solar-like convective dynamo models, which typically predict a differential rotation of less than 1%. We also observe a long-lived spot at high latitudes, which is unusual for stars with a fast rotation period. This spot appears to be migrating poleward, possibly indicating a change in the global magnetic field topology. We have used these observations to test and constrain differential rotation models for cool dwarf stars and to investigate the complex interplay between differential rotation, convection, and magnetic fields. These observations provide valuable constraints for models of stellar activity and the potential habitability of exoplanets orbiting such stars.

In this work, we investigate the possibility of obtaining a Massive CP$^1$ theory starting from a microscopic model for doped antiferromagnets. Our main goal is to find a Lagrangian that describes the low-energy behavior of spin excitations in a doped antiferromagnet. The starting point is a Z$_2$ lattice gauge theory with a spinon Fermi surface, which is believed to describe the low-energy behavior of the CuO$_2$ planes in cuprate superconductors. We show that by integrating out the gauge fields, one obtains an effective action for the spinons that has the form of a Massive CP$^1$ theory. Our approach is based on the large-$N$ limit of the spinon theory, where $N$ is the number of spin components. We argue that the resulting effective action is valid for small doping, where the Fermi surface is still well-defined. We then derive the equations of motion for the Massive CP$^1$ theory and study their properties. We find that the theory supports topological solitons, which can be interpreted as vortices in the spin texture. Our results provide a new perspective on the physics of doped antiferromagnets and may shed light on the origins of high-temperature superconductivity in cuprates.

In this research, we investigate the predictability of the solar cycle using mean-field models, a popular tool in the field of solar physics. Our motivation for this investigation is to improve our understanding of the solar cycle and to make accurate predictions of its activity. To achieve this goal, we first propose a new approach that combines mean-field models with the Bayesian framework. This method allows the incorporation of new observations over time and provides us with a constantly updated estimate of the solar activity levels. We then apply this approach to the observed solar cycle data and compare our predictions with the actual observations. Our analysis shows that our new method outperforms the traditional mean-field models and provides more accurate predictions of the solar cycle. Furthermore, we use our approach to make predictions for the next solar cycle, which is expected to peak around 2025. Our results suggest that the activity levels during the next solar cycle will be similar to the current cycle, with a peak in activity around 2025. Our findings have important implications for predicting the effects of solar activity on Earth's climate and for forecasting space weather events.

In this work, we investigate the nature of phase transitions in magnetic thin films with the ultimate objective of understanding the magnetic behavior of materials confined to two dimensions. The motivation behind this research stems from the increased interest in magnetic thin films and their potential applications in the field of spintronics. These materials have novel physical properties that make them suitable for use in high-density information storage devices and magnetic sensors. However, the thin film nature of these materials can affect the magnetic ordering and its transition temperature.

Through the use of X-ray diffraction and magnetometry techniques, we have successfully synthesized and characterized FePt thin films of various thicknesses. We have explored the structural and magnetic properties of these films and observed significant changes in their magnetic phase transitions as their thickness reduces. Our results suggest that the magnetic phase transition in thin FePt films is heavily influenced by the interaction between the magnetic moments and the non-magnetic atoms in the neighboring layers.

The problem that this research aims to solve is to understand the effects of thin film confinement on magnetic phase transitions. Our findings can advance the development of novel magnetic materials and lead to the next generation of spintronic devices.

We present a numerical study of the $\Delta H(M,\Delta M)$ method and its
ability to accurately determine intrinsic switching field distributions in
interacting granular magnetic materials such as perpendicular recording media.
In particular, we study how this methodology fails for large ferromagnetic
inter-granular interactions, at which point the associated strongly correlated
magnetization reversal cannot be properly represented by the mean-field
approximation, upon which the $\Delta H(M,\Delta M)$ method is based. In this
study, we use a 2-dimensional array of symmetric hysterons that have an
intrinsic switching field distribution of standard deviation $\sigma$ and
ferromagnetic nearest-neighbor interactions $J$. We find the $\Delta H(M,\Delta
M)$ method to be very accurate for small $J/\sigma$ values, while substantial
errors develop once the effective exchange field becomes comparable with
$\sigma$, corroborating earlier results from micromagnetic simulations. We
furthermore demonstrate that this failure is correlated with deviations from
data set redundancy, which is a key property of the mean-field approximation.
Thus, the $\Delta H(M,\Delta M)$ method fails in a well defined and
quantifiable manner that can be easily assessed from the data sets alone.

The morphological and spectroscopic properties of the giant (>60 kpc)
Ly-alpha nebulae associated with three radio galaxies at z~2.5 (MRC 1558-003,
MRC 2025-218 and MRC 0140-257) have been investigated using integral field
spectroscopic data obtained with VIMOS on VLT.
  The morphologies are varied. The nebula of one source has a centrally peaked,
rounded appearance. In the other two objects, it consists of two spatial
components. The three nebulae are aligned with the radio axis within <30 deg.
The total Ly-alpha luminosities are in the range (0.3-3.4) x 1e44 erg s-1. The
Ly-alpha spectral profile shows strong variation through the nebulae, with FWHM
values in the range ~400-1500 km s-1 and velocity shifts V~120-600 km s-1.
  We present an infall model which can explain successfully most Ly-alpha
morphological and spectroscopic properties of the nebula associated with MRC
1558-003. This adds further support to our previous conclusion that the
_quiescent_ giant nebulae associated with this and other high redshift powerful
radio galaxies are in infall. A problem for this model is the difficulty to
reproduce the large Ly-alpha FWHM values.
  We have discovered a giant (~85 kpc) Ly-alpha nebula associated with the
radio galaxy MRC 0140-257 at z=2.64. It shows strikingly relaxed kinematics
(FWHM< 300 km s-1 and V<120 km s-1), unique among high z (>2) radio galaxies.

We have constructed an approximate microscopic model for the neutron dynamic
structure factor of solid methane in phase II. We expect our model to apply for
neutron energies below 1\textit{eV} at pressures near 1 bar and temperatures
below 20K, where solid methane exists in a phase, called phase II, in which the
tetrahedral hydrogens exist in a mixed phase of free rotors and hindered
rotors. Certain matrix elements needed for the dynamic structure factor $S(Q,
\omega)$ are adapted from a calculation by Ozaki \textit{et
al.}\cite{ozaki:3442}\cite{ozaki2}. The model treats the effects of molecular
translations, intramolecular vibrations and the free and hindered rotational
degrees of freedom as uncoupled ones. Total scattering cross sections
calculated from the model agree with the cross section measurements of
Grieger\cite{grieger:3161} and Whittemore\cite{Wittemore} for the incident
neutron energies of 0.5 \textit{meV} $\sim$ 1\textit{eV}. The effective density
of states extracted from the model was compared with the Harker $&$ Brugger
frequency spectrum extracted from neutron scattering measurements conducted at
22K\cite{HARKER1967}.

In this work, we introduce a new family of templates for the detection of gravitational waves from comparable mass black hole binaries. Our motivation for this research stems from the recent detections of gravitational waves, which have opened a new window into the study of the universe. However, the current template families used for black hole binary detections are tailored to unequal mass binaries and may not be optimal for comparable mass systems. Our new templates take into account the spin and precession of the black holes, providing a more accurate model for the expected gravitational wave signals.

To develop our new template family, we used numerical simulations to compute the expected gravitational waveforms for a range of black hole masses and spins. We then used these waveforms to construct a new family of template waveforms, which we tested on simulated data sets. Our analysis shows that our new templates improve the detectability of gravitational waves from comparable mass black hole binaries. Specifically, our templates achieve a detection volume improvement factor of up to 2.5 relative to current template families.

In conclusion, we have introduced a new template family for the detection of gravitational waves from comparable mass black hole binaries, which outperforms current templates. Our methodology is based on numerical simulations and enables us to accurately model the expected gravitational wave signals, which can ultimately lead to a better understanding of the universe.

In this research, we focused on the merging activity of galaxy clusters and its impact on the kinetic polarization of the Sunyaev-Zel'dovich (SZ) signal. The motivation behind this research was to better understand the underlying physical mechanisms that govern the galaxy cluster formation and evolution, which is crucial for studying the large-scale structure of the universe.

Using high-fidelity hydrodynamical simulations, we investigated the effects of merging on the SZ signal polarization. Our results showed that the merging of galaxy clusters can induce significant changes in the polarization profile of the SZ signal, increasing its amplitude and altering its shape and orientation. We found that this effect is most prominent in the post-merger phase, where multiple clusters are coalescing and the gas dynamics is highly complex.

Our work attempts to solve a fundamental problem in modern cosmology, which is to accurately model and predict the observable properties of galaxy clusters, including the SZ signal polarization. By providing a more accurate and comprehensive picture of the merging activity and its influence on the SZ signal, our research can help improve the theoretical models and observational strategies for studying galaxy clusters, as well as shed light on the mysteries of the universe's evolution.

In this work, we investigate the synchrotron radio emission from a fast halo coronal mass ejection (CME) that occurred on 10 September 2017. The motivation for our research stems from the need to better understand the mechanisms that drive CMEs and the associated radio emissions. CMEs are large-scale eruptions of plasma and magnetic fields from the Sun, which can impact Earth's space environment and cause geomagnetic storms. Radio emission from CMEs is an important diagnostic tool for studying the magnetic field and plasma properties of the CMEs.

We used radio observations from the Low Frequency Array (LOFAR) and the Wind/WAVES spacecraft to study the radio emission from the fast halo CME. Our analysis shows that the radio emission was consistent with synchrotron emission from relativistic electrons accelerated in the CME's magnetic field. We also found evidence for a shock wave ahead of the CME, which may have played a role in accelerating the relativistic electrons.

These results provide insights into the physical processes that govern CMEs and their associated radio emissions. In particular, our study highlights the importance of synchrotron emission in understanding the acceleration and propagation of relativistic particles in CMEs. This work has implications for space weather forecasting and the development of models for CME-driven radio emission.

In our work, we investigate the origin of magnetoelectric behavior in BiFeO$_3$, a material that exhibits remarkable properties, such as a large magnetoelectric effect coupled with multiferroic properties. The motivation behind this research is the immense potential for technological applications, such as data storage, spintronics, and sensor devices. Despite several attempts, the specific mechanism governing the magnetoelectric behavior in BiFeO$_3$ is still unresolved. Thus, our work aims to solve this crucial problem.

We employ a combination of experimental and theoretical methods to identify the fundamental principles resulting in the magnetoelectric coupling. Our methodology includes first-principles calculations utilizing density-functional theory, X-ray resonant magnetic scattering, and magneto-transport measurements. Through these techniques, we find that the BiFeO$_3$ crystal structure is a contributing factor to its magnetic behavior, with Fe$^{3+}$ ions experiencing both electric and magnetic fields simultaneously, resulting in the magnetocristalline anisotropy. Furthermore, our research highlights the importance of spin-orbit interactions in understanding the multiferroic properties of BiFeO$_3$.

In conclusion, our work provides crucial insights into the fundamental mechanisms underlying magnetoelectric behavior in BiFeO$_3$. Our results will inform the design of novel magnetoelectric materials for a range of technological applications.

We discuss the application of wavelet transforms to a critical interface
model, which is known to provide a good description of Barkhausen noise in soft
ferromagnets. The two-dimensional version of the model (one-dimensional
interface) is considered, mainly in the adiabatic limit of very slow driving.
On length scales shorter than a crossover length (which grows with the strength
of surface tension), the effective interface roughness exponent $\zeta$ is
$\simeq 1.20$, close to the expected value for the universality class of the
quenched Edwards-Wilkinson model. We find that the waiting times between
avalanches are fully uncorrelated, as the wavelet transform of their
autocorrelations scales as white noise. Similarly, detrended size-size
correlations give a white-noise wavelet transform. Consideration of finite
driving rates, still deep within the intermittent regime, shows the wavelet
transform of correlations scaling as $1/f^{1.5}$ for intermediate frequencies.
This behavior is ascribed to intra-avalanche correlations.

Splitting trees are those random trees where individuals give birth at
constant rate during a lifetime with general distribution, to i.i.d. copies of
themselves. The width process of a splitting tree is then a binary, homogeneous
Crump--Mode--Jagers (CMJ) process, and is not Markovian unless the lifetime
distribution is exponential. Here, we allow the birth rate to be infinite, that
is, pairs of birth times and lifespans of newborns form a Poisson point process
along the lifetime of their mother, with possibly infinite intensity measure. A
splitting tree is a random (so-called) chronological tree. Each element of a
chronological tree is a (so-called) existence point $(v,\tau)$ of some
individual $v$ (vertex) in a discrete tree, where $\tau$ is a nonnegative real
number called chronological level (time). We introduce a total order on
existence points, called linear order, and a mapping $\varphi$ from the tree
into the real line which preserves this order. The inverse of $\varphi$ is
called the exploration process, and the projection of this inverse on
chronological levels the contour process. For splitting trees truncated up to
level $\tau$, we prove that thus defined contour process is a L\'evy process
reflected below $\tau$ and killed upon hitting 0. This allows to derive
properties of (i) splitting trees: conceptual proof of Le Gall--Le Jan's
theorem in the finite variation case, exceptional points, coalescent point
process, age distribution; (ii) CMJ processes: one-dimensional marginals,
conditionings, limit theorems, asymptotic numbers of individuals with infinite
vs finite descendances.

We present a simplified chemical and thermal model designed to allow
computationally efficient study of the thermal evolution of metal-poor gas
within large numerical simulations. Our main simplification is the neglect of
the molecular chemistry of the heavy elements. The only molecular chemistry
retained within the model is the formation and destruction of molecular
hydrogen. Despite this major simplification, the model allows for accurate
treatment of the thermal evolution of the gas within a large volume of
parameter space. It is valid for temperatures 50 < T < 10000 K and
metallicities 0 < Z < 0.1 Z_solar. In gas with a metallicity Z = 0.1 Z_solar,
and in the absence of an incident ultraviolet radiation field, it is valid for
hydrogen number densities n_H < 500 / t_char cm^-3, where t_char is the size in
Myr of the characteristic physical timescale of interest in the problem. If Z
<< 0.1 Z_solar, or if a strong ultraviolet radiation field is present, then the
model remains accurate up to significantly higher densities. We also discuss
some possible applications of this model.

The closedness condition for real geodesics on n-dimensional ellipsoids is in
general transcendental in the parameters (semiaxes of the ellipsoid and
constants of motion). We show that it is algebraic in the parameters if and
only if both the real and the imaginary geodesics are closed and we
characterize such double--periodicity condition via real hyperelliptic
tangential coverings. We prove the density of algebraically closed geodesics on
n-dimensional ellipsoids with respect to the natural topology in the
(2n)-dimensional real parameter space. In particular, the approximating
sequence of algebraic closed geodesics on the approximated ellipsoids may be
chosen so to share the same values of the length and of the real period vector
as the limiting closed geodesic on the limiting ellipsoid.
  Finally, for real doubly-periodic geodesics on triaxial ellipsoids, we show
how to evaluate algebraically the period mapping and we present some explicit
examples of families of algebraically closed geodesics.

In this work, we investigate the problem of searching a database using binary quantum search. The traditional binary search algorithm has linear time complexity and requires sorted data, whereas quantum search algorithms provide a quadratic speedup and work for unsorted data. Our work aims to investigate binary quantum search and its potential advantages over classical binary search. 

Our methodology involves designing an implementable circuit for binary quantum search and analyzing its efficiency in terms of the time complexity and accuracy of the search algorithm. We utilize the Grover search algorithm, which has been proven to provide a quadratic speedup for unstructured search problems, and apply it to binary search.

Our results demonstrate that binary quantum search can provide significant improvements over classical binary search in terms of time complexity. We show that quantum search algorithms can perform a search operation on an unsorted database of N items in O(√N) time complexity, a substantial improvement over the O(N) time complexity of classical algorithms. Additionally, we demonstrate that our binary quantum search algorithm is highly accurate and can reliably find the target item in an unsorted database with high probability.

In conclusion, our work presents a novel approach to binary search using quantum computing and demonstrates its potential advantages over classical algorithms. Our research can pave the way for future developments in quantum search algorithms and their applications in database searching and other related fields.

Utilizing 21 new Chandra observations as well as archival Chandra, ROSAT, and
XMM-Newton data, we study the X-ray properties of a representative sample of 59
of the most optically luminous quasars in the Universe (M_i~~-29.3 to -30.2)
spanning a redshift range of z~~1.5-4.5. Our full sample consists of 32 quasars
from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) Data Release 3 (DR3) quasar catalog,
two additional objects in the DR3 area that were missed by the SDSS selection
criteria, and 25 comparably luminous quasars at z>~4. This is the largest X-ray
study of such luminous quasars to date. By jointly fitting the X-ray spectra of
our sample quasars, excluding radio-loud and broad absorption line (BAL)
objects, we find a mean X-ray power-law photon index of
Gamma=1.92^{+0.09}_{-0.08} and constrain any neutral intrinsic absorbing
material to have a mean column density of N_H<~2x10^{21} cm^{-2}. We find,
consistent with other studies, that Gamma does not change with redshift, and we
constrain the amount of allowed Gamma evolution for the most-luminous quasars.
Our sample, excluding radio-loud and BAL quasars, has a mean X-ray-to-optical
spectral slope of a_ox=-1.80+/-0.02, as well as no significant evolution of
a_ox with redshift. We also comment upon the X-ray properties of a number of
notable quasars, including an X-ray weak quasar with several strong narrow
absorption-line systems, a mildly radio-loud BAL quasar, and a well-studied
gravitationally lensed quasar.

In this work, we investigate the magnetic properties and structure of the rare earth intermetallic compound Sm2IrIn8. The compound belongs to the class of heavy fermion materials that are characterized by their high mass-enhancement due to strong electron correlations. Our study is motivated by the interest in the magnetic behavior of heavy fermion systems, which can give insight into the underlying physics of the materials. Additionally, the compound Sm2IrIn8 exhibits an antiferromagnetic ground state, which is intriguing since most heavy fermion systems are known to have either a paramagnetic or a ferromagnetic ground state.

Magnetic susceptibility measurements performed on powdered samples of Sm2IrIn8 reveal that this compound exhibits an antiferromagnetic transition at around 4 K. Further investigations using neutron diffraction reveal that the magnetic order in this material is of the antiferromagnetic type, with magnetic moments on the Sm ions pointing along the c-axis. The moments ordered in an antiparallel arrangement alternating between the different Sm sites observed in the crystal structure.

The results of this study provide insight into the magnetic ground state of heavy fermion compounds such as Sm2IrIn8 and show that the magnetic structure in this compound is of the antiferromagnetic type. These results broaden our understanding of the magnetic behavior of heavy fermion materials and may aid the development of new materials with desirable magnetic properties.

We report on the observation of the rare decay D^0 --> K^_pi^+pi^-e^+nu_e based on the analysis of a data sample corresponding to an integrated luminosity of 2.93 fb^-1. The study is motivated by the interest in understanding the weak interaction in heavy meson decays. The decay mode under investigation is characterized by a Cabibbo-suppressed transition, where a strange quark is replaced by an up or down quark in the final state. This process is forbidden in the Standard Model at tree level and proceeds via a loop diagram involving virtual W and Z bosons, as well as charged leptons. 

The key idea behind the study is to reconstruct the decay topology using a semileptonic tagging technique, where one of the D mesons involved in the event is reconstructed in a semileptonic decay mode, such as D^0 --> Kpi e nu. The remaining meson is identified as a D^0 candidate through its decay products, which include at least two charged pions and a kaon. A fit to the kinematic distribution of the selected events is performed to extract the signal yield.

We observe a signal of D^0 --> K^_pi^+pi^-e^+nu_e with a significance of 5.3 standard deviations, which corresponds to a branching fraction of (3.51 ± 0.72(stat) ± 0.35(syst)) x 10^-5. This measurement provides a test of the Standard Model predictions and represents a valuable input for future studies of the weak interaction in heavy meson decays.

We give formulae for the Ozsvath-Szabo invariants of 4-manifolds X obtained
by fiber sum of two manifolds M_1, M_2 along surfaces S_1, S_2 having trivial
normal bundle and genus g>0. The formulae follow from a general theorem on the
Ozsvath-Szabo invariants of the result of gluing two 4-manifolds along a common
boundary, which is phrased in terms of relative invariants of the pieces. These
relative invariants take values in a version of Heegaard Floer homology with
coefficients in modules over certain Novikov rings; the fiber sum formula
follows from the theorem that this "perturbed" version of Heegaard Floer theory
recovers the usual Ozsvath-Szabo invariants, when the 4-manifold in question
has b^+>1. The construction allows an extension of the definition of the
Ozsvath-Szabo invariants to 4-manifolds having b^+ = 1 depending on certain
choices, in close analogy with Seiberg-Witten theory. The product formulae lead
quickly to calculations of the Ozsvath-Szabo invariants of various 4-manifolds;
in all cases the results are in accord with the conjectured equivalence between
the Ozsvath-Szabo and Seiberg-Witten invariants.

We apply a recently developed perturbative formalism which describes the
evolution under their self-gravity of particles displaced from a perfect
lattice to quantify precisely, up to shell crossing, the effects of
discreteness in dissipationless cosmological N-body simulations. We give simple
expressions, explicitly dependent on the particle density, for the evolution of
power in each mode as a function of red-shift. For typical starting red-shifts
the effect of finite particle number is to {\it slow down} slightly the growth
of power compared to that in the fluid limit (e.g. by about ten percent at half
the Nyquist frequency), and to induce also dispersion in the growth as a
function of direction at a comparable level. In the limit that the initial
red-shift tends to infinity, at fixed particle density, the evolution in fact
diverges from that in the fluid limit (described by the Zeldovich
approximation). Contrary to widely held belief, this means that a simulation
started at a red-shift much higher than the red-shift of shell crossing
actually gives a worse, rather than a better, result. We also study how these
effects are modified when there is a small-scale regularization of the
gravitational force. We show that such a smoothing may reduce the anisotropy of
the discreteness effects, but it then {\it increases} their average effect.
This behaviour illustrates the fact that the discreteness effects described
here are distinct from those usually considered in this context, due to
two-body collisions. Indeed the characteristic time for divergence from the
collisionless limit is proportional to $N^{2/3}$, rather than $N/ \log N$ in
the latter case.

The Variable Star One-shot Project (VSOP) is an ongoing initiative aimed at generating comprehensive photometric and spectroscopic data for a wide range of variable stars. In this paper, we present a summary of the project and announce the release of our first batch of data. Our major motivation is to improve our understanding of the nature of variable stars and their underlying causes, which are fundamental to a range of astrophysical studies. Our methodology involves the use of robotic telescopes that carry out multiple automated observations of a given star in a single night, allowing us to capture changes in brightness and color on short time-scales. Our first data release includes photometry and spectroscopy for 62 targets, encompassing a range of variable star types, including eclipsing binaries, pulsating stars and cataclysmic variables. We report on our findings, such as the detection of a likely outburst of an eclipsing binary, and the notable phase shifts observed in certain pulsating stars. Overall, the initial results from the VSOP project demonstrate the benefits of comprehensive monitoring of variable stars and the potential for uncovering new phenomena in the field.

We investigate cooperative wireless relay networks in which the nodes can
help each other in data transmission. We study different coding strategies in
the single-source single-destination network with many relay nodes. Given the
myriad of ways in which nodes can cooperate, there is a natural routing
problem, i.e., determining an ordered set of nodes to relay the data from the
source to the destination. We find that for a given route, the
decode-and-forward strategy, which is an information theoretic cooperative
coding strategy, achieves rates significantly higher than that achievable by
the usual multi-hop coding strategy, which is a point-to-point non-cooperative
coding strategy. We construct an algorithm to find an optimal route (in terms
of rate maximizing) for the decode-and-forward strategy. Since the algorithm
runs in factorial time in the worst case, we propose a heuristic algorithm that
runs in polynomial time. The heuristic algorithm outputs an optimal route when
the nodes transmit independent codewords. We implement these coding strategies
using practical low density parity check codes to compare the performance of
the strategies on different routes.

We have used the ACS camera on HST to obtain (V,I) photometry for 57,000
red-giant stars in the halo of the Leo elliptical NGC 3377. We use this sample
of stars to derive the metallicity distribution function (MDF) for its halo
field stars, and comment on its chemical evolution history compared with both
larger and smaller E galaxies. Our ACS/WFC field spans a radial range extending
from 4 to 18 kpc projected distance from the center of NGC 3377 and thus covers
a significant portion of this galaxy's halo. We find that the MDF is broad,
reaching a peak at [m/H] ~ -0.6$, but containing virtually no stars more
metal-poor than log [m/H] = -1.5$. It may, in addition, have relatively few
stars more metal-rich than [m/H] = -0.3$, although interpretation of the
high-metallicity end of the MDF is limited by photometric completeness that
affects the detection of the reddest, most metal-rich stars. NGC 3377 appears
to have an enrichment history intermediate between those of normal dwarf
ellipticals and the much larger giants. As yet, we find no clear evidence that
the halo of NGC 3377 contains a significant population of ``young'' (< 3 Gy)
stars.

Context. Searching for planets in open clusters allows us to study the
effects of dynamical environment on planet formation and evolution.
  Aims. Considering the strong dependence of planet frequency on stellar
metallicity, we studied the metal rich old open cluster NGC 6791 and searched
for close-in planets using the transit technique.
  Methods. A ten-night observational campaign was performed using the
Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope (3.6m), the San Pedro M\'artir telescope (2.1m),
and the Loiano telescope (1.5m). To increase the transit detection probability
we also made use of the Bruntt et al. (2003) eight-nights observational
campaign. Adequate photometric precision for the detection of planetary
transits was achieved.
  Results. Should the frequency and properties of close-in planets in NGC 6791
be similar to those orbiting field stars of similar metallicity, then detailed
simulations foresee the presence of 2-3 transiting planets. Instead, we do not
confirm the transit candidates proposed by Bruntt et al. (2003). The
probability that the null detection is simply due to chance coincidence is
estimated to be 3%-10%, depending on the metallicity assumed for the cluster.
  Conclusions. Possible explanations of the null-detection of transits include:
(i) a lower frequency of close-in planets in star clusters; (ii) a smaller
planetary radius for planets orbiting super metal rich stars; or (iii)
limitations in the basic assumptions. More extensive photometry with 3-4m class
telescopes is required to allow conclusive inferences about the frequency of
planets in NGC 6791.

The Marcus-Lushnikov process is a finite stochastic particle system in which
each particle is entirely characterized by its mass. Each pair of particles
with masses $x$ and $y$ merges into a single particle at a given rate $K(x,y)$.
We consider a {\it strongly gelling} kernel behaving as $K(x,y)=x^\alpha y + x
y^\alpha$ for some $\alpha\in (0,1]$. In such a case, it is well-known that
{\it gelation} occurs, that is, giant particles emerge. Then two possible
models for hydrodynamic limits of the Marcus-Lushnikov process arise: the
Smoluchowski equation, in which the giant particles are inert, and the Flory
equation, in which the giant particles interact with finite ones. We show that,
when using a suitable cut-off coagulation kernel in the Marcus-Lushnikov
process and letting the number of particles increase to infinity, the possible
limits solve either the Smoluchowski equation or the Flory equation. We also
study the asymptotic behaviour of the largest particle in the Marcus-Lushnikov
process without cut-off and show that there is only one giant particle. This
single giant particle represents, asymptotically, the lost mass of the solution
to the Flory equation.

Yukawa potentials are often used as effective potentials for systems as
colloids, plasmas, etc. When the Debye screening length is large, the Yukawa
potential tends to the non-screened Coulomb potential ; in this small screening
limit, or Coulomb limit, the potential is long ranged. As it is well known in
computer simulation, a simple truncation of the long ranged potential and the
minimum image convention are insufficient to obtain accurate numerical data on
systems. The Ewald method for bulk systems, i.e. with periodic boundary
conditions in all three directions of the space, has already been derived for
Yukawa potential [cf. Y., Rosenfeld, {\it Mol. Phys.}, \bm{88}, 1357, (1996)
and G., Salin and J.-M., Caillol, {\it J. Chem. Phys.}, \bm{113}, 10459,
(2000)], but for systems with partial periodic boundary conditions, the Ewald
sums have only recently been obtained [M., Mazars, {\it J. Chem. Phys.}, {\bf
126}, 056101 (2007)]. In this paper, we provide a closed derivation of the
Ewald sums for Yukawa potentials in systems with periodic boundary conditions
in only two directions and for any value of the Debye length. A special
attention is paid to the Coulomb limit and its relation with the
electroneutrality of systems.

In this work, we investigate the long-time existence of smooth solutions for the rapidly rotating shallow-water and Euler equations. The motivation for this research arises from the fact that these equations model the low-frequency dynamics of geophysical fluids, such as oceans and atmospheres, and understanding the properties of their solutions is crucial for many scientific and engineering applications.

Our approach is based on the use of a refined energy method, combined with a careful analysis of the asymptotic behavior of the solutions as the rotation parameter tends to infinity. We show that for both the shallow-water and Euler equations, there exist smooth solutions that exist globally in time, and converge to a steady state that depends on the initial data.

Our results are significant because they provide a rigorous mathematical framework to study the behavior of geophysical fluids under rapid rotation, which is an essential aspect of many climate and weather phenomena. In addition, our methodology may have potential applications in the study of other models in fluid dynamics, such as magnetohydrodynamics or quantum fluids. Overall, this work contributes to the ongoing effort to develop mathematical tools to tackle the complex and fascinating challenges of fluid dynamics.

In this study, we investigate the orbital periods of the AM CVn stars HP Librae and V803 Centauri. These stars are interesting because they are binary systems in which both stars are white dwarfs, one accreting material from the other. By analyzing the orbital periods of these systems, we can gain insights into the processes of accretion and gravitational radiation that govern their evolution.

To determine the orbital periods of these stars, we analyze light curves obtained from the Catalina Real-time Transient Survey and the All-Sky Automated Survey for Supernovae. Using the Lomb-Scargle periodogram and other statistical tools, we identify the dominant periodicities in the light curves, which correspond to the orbital periods of the systems.

Our research is motivated by the need to better understand the evolution of AM CVn stars, which are some of the most extreme and exotic objects in the universe. These stars have the potential to yield important insights into the formation and evolution of binary systems, the production of gravitational waves, and the behavior of matter under extreme conditions.

Overall, our work seeks to contribute to a deeper understanding of the complex processes that govern the behavior of AM CVn stars, and to open up new avenues for research in this fascinating and rapidly-evolving field.

The Cornell Caltech Atacama Telescope (CCAT) is a state-of-the-art millimeter-wave telescope that aims to explore the universe's mysteries by capturing images of galaxies, stars, and interstellar gas. The instrument is located at one of the world's highest and driest observatory sites, in the Atacama Desert of Chile, presenting extraordinary possibilities for deep sky surveys. The CCAT is a collaboration between Cornell University and Caltech, which has proven effective for the development of exemplary instrumentation for astronomy. The primary goal of this research is to present the design of the telescope and its features, operational capabilities, and performance measurement techniques. 

The CCAT is a comprehensive observatory that uses the latest innovations and technologies to detect and study cosmic phenomena. This study aims to address the significant challenges associated with simulating telescopes. The team used advanced software to model the telescope and then used validated simulations to verify how multiple operational configurations of the CCAT would perform. In addition, the team performed sensitivity studies to investigate the optimal observing configurations. 

The research has resulted in a detailed analysis of the CCAT telescope's functionality and potential use of simulating telescopes. The team has shown that the CCAT will have outstanding performance in achieving the science goals outlined in this research. The CCAT is anticipated to transform the study of astronomy significantly and lead to a better understanding of the current cosmological model. The collaborative work done on the CCAT telescope by Cornell University and Caltech marks an exciting phase in astronomical research.

In our work, we aim to explore a remarkable moduli space of rank 6 vector bundles associated with cubic surfaces. The motivation for our research stems from the observation that cubic surfaces play a fundamental role in many areas of mathematics such as algebraic geometry, topology, and representation theory. Specifically, vector bundles over cubic surfaces have been the subject of intense investigation in recent years due to their rich algebraic and geometric structures. 

Our approach to this problem involves utilizing the theory of derived categories and the relative Fourier-Mukai transform to construct families of vector bundles over the moduli space. The key idea is to study the deformation theory of these bundles and establish a connection with the geometry of the cubic surface. Our methodology also involves using explicit computations with the Grothendieck-Riemann-Roch theorem and Serre duality to study the cohomology of vector bundles over the moduli space. 

The main problem that our work attempts to solve is to understand the geometry of the moduli space and its relation to the geometry of the underlying cubic surface. Specifically, we seek to investigate the deformation theory of vector bundles and study the spectral data associated with the moduli space. Our work contributes to the ongoing research in algebraic geometry and is relevant in the study of the moduli space of sheaves on higher-dimensional algebraic varieties.

In this work, we investigate the inside-out evacuation of transitional protoplanetary disks by the Magneto-Rotational Instability (MRI). Transitional disks, characterized by a large inner cavity and narrow dust rings, are believed to be in the process of forming planets. The MRI is a crucial process for disk evolution, as it generates turbulence that allows for accretion onto the central star. In this study, we simulate the MRI in transitional disks with a focus on the effects of varying magnetic fields and ionization levels. Our results show that higher magnetic fields and ionization levels lead to more efficient evacuation of the inner disk. Interestingly, we find that the MRI can lead to significant outflows of gas and dust, which could significantly impact the formation of planets. Our simulations also suggest that disk irradiation may play an important role in the MRI process, as it affects the ionization level of the disk. Overall, our work underscores the importance of the MRI for the evolution of transitional disks, highlighting the need for more detailed studies of this fundamental process.

Unsustained matter distributions unescapely collapse unless fragmentation and
centrifugal or pressure support take place. Starting from the above evidence,
supermassive compact objects at the centre of large-mass galaxies are conceived
as the end-product of the gravitational collapse of local density maxima around
which overdensities are located. At the beginning of evolution, local density
maxima are idealized as homogeneous peaks, while the surrounding envelopes are
described by a power-law density profile. The dependence of the density profile
on a second parameter, chosen to be the ratio between peak and total mass, is
analysed. Overdensity evolution is discussed in the context of quintessence
cosmological models and further investigation is devoted to a special case with
the aim to describe the central collapse. An empirical relation between hole
and dark halo mass is translated into a dependence of the fractional hole mass
on the overdensity mass. Computations are performed up to the end of central
collapse, and density profiles of related configurations are determined
together with additional parameters. The central collapse is completed in early
times, no longer than a few hundredths of Gyr, which implies hole formation
when proto-haloes, proto-bulges, and proto-disks are still expanding or
contracting. No appreciable change in evolution is found with regard to
different mean peak heights related to equal masses. On the other hand, it is
recognized that homogeneous peaks collapse ``faster'' with respect to
surroundings envelopes, in low-mass than in large-mass overdensities. In
conclusion, it is inferred that gravitational collapse of homogeneous peaks
within overdensities may be a viable mechanism for hole generation.

Background: Duplication of genes is important for evolution of molecular
networks. Many authors have therefore considered gene duplication as a driving
force in shaping the topology of molecular networks. In particular it has been
noted that growth via duplication would act as an implicit way of preferential
attachment, and thereby provide the observed broad degree distributions of
molecular networks.
  Results: We extend current models of gene duplication and rewiring by
including directions and the fact that molecular networks are not a result of
unidirectional growth. We introduce upstream sites and downstream shapes to
quantify potential links during duplication and rewiring. We find that this in
itself generates the observed scaling of transcription factors for genome sites
in procaryotes. The dynamical model can generate a scale-free degree
distribution, p(k)∝ 1/k^γ, with exponent γ=1 in the
non-growing case, and with γ>1 when the network is growing.
  Conclusions: We find that duplication of genes followed by substantial
recombination of upstream regions could generate main features of genetic
regulatory networks. Our steady state degree distribution is however to broad
to be consistent with data, thereby suggesting that selective pruning acts as a
main additional constraint on duplicated genes. Our analysis shows that gene
duplication can only be a main cause for the observed broad degree
distributions, if there is also substantial recombinations between upstream
regions of genes.

In this work, we propose a metric for the gradient renormalization group (RG) flow of the worldsheet sigma model beyond first order. The worldsheet sigma model is a crucial tool for studying fundamental objects in string theory, such as D-branes. However, its perturbative expansion breaks down at high orders due to infrared divergences. The gradient RG flow is a non-perturbative method that resums these divergences. 

The metric we propose is based on the optimal transport theory, which provides a geometric framework for studying flows on manifolds. Our metric satisfies several desirable properties, such as positivity, invariance under diffeomorphisms, and compatibility with the gradient flow equation. To illustrate the power of our metric, we compute its value for a variety of examples, including the Gross-Neveu model, the nonlinear O(2) sigma model, and the O(N) model.

Our work is motivated by the need for a better understanding of the non-perturbative dynamics of the worldsheet sigma model. The gradient RG flow has proven to be a powerful tool for this purpose, but until now, there hasn't been a well-defined metric for this flow beyond first order. Our result fills this gap and is likely to have many applications in string theory, quantum field theory, and statistical mechanics. 

Overall, our work represents a significant step forward in our understanding of the non-perturbative dynamics of the worldsheet sigma model and provides a useful tool for future research in this area.

A novel approach to protein multiple sequence alignment is discussed:
substantially this method counterparts with substitution matrix based methods
(like Blosum or PAM based methods), and implies a more deterministic approach
to chemical/physical sub-grouping of amino acids . Amino acids (aa) are divided
into sub-groups with successive derivations, that result in a clustering based
on the considered property. The properties can be user defined or chosen
between default schemes, like those used in the analysis described here.
Starting from an initial set of the 20 naturally occurring amino acids, they
are successively divided on the basis of their polarity/hydrophobic index, with
increasing resolution up to four level of subdivision. Other schemes of
subdivision are possible: in this thesis work it was employed also a scheme
based on physical/structural properties (solvent exposure, lateral chain
mobility and secondary structure tendency), that have been compared to the
chemical scheme with testing purposes. In the method described in this chapter,
the total score for each position in the alignment accounts for different
degree of similarity between amino acids. The scoring value result form the
contribution of each level of selectivity for every individual property
considered. Simply the method (called M_Al) analyse the n sequence alignment
position per position and assigns a score which have contributes by aa identity
plus a composed valuation of the chemical or of the structural affinity between
the n aligned amino acids. This method has been implemented in a series of
programs written in python language; these programs have been tested in some
biological cases, with benchmark purposes.

We report on multi-frequency linear polarization monitoring of 15 active
galactic nuclei containing highly relativistic jets with apparent speeds from
$\sim$4 $c$ to $>40c$. The measurements were obtained at optical, 1 mm, and 3
mm wavelengths, and at 7 mm with the Very Long Baseline Array. The data show a
wide range in degree of linear polarization among the sources, from $<$1% to
$>$30%, and interday polarization variability in individual sources. The
polarization properties suggest separation of the sample into three groups with
low, intermediate, and high variability of polarization in the core at 7 mm :
LVP, IVP, and HVP, respectively. The groups are partially associated with the
common classification of active galactic nuclei as radio galaxies and quasars
with low optical polarization (LVP), BL Lacertae objects (IVP), and highly
optically polarized quasars (HVP). Our study investigates correlations between
total flux, fractional polarization, and polarization position angle at the
different wavelengths. We interpret the polarization properties of the sources
