in the sample through models in which weak shocks compress turbulent plasma in
the jet. The differences in the orientation of sources with respect to the
observer, jet kinematics, and abundance of thermal matter external to the jet
near the core can account for the diversity in the polarization properties. The
results provide strong evidence that the optical polarized emission originates
in shocks, most likely situated between the 3 mm and 7 mm VLBI cores. They also
support the idea that the 1 mm core lies at the edge of the transition zone
between electromagnetically dominated and turbulent hydrodynamical sections of
the jet.

We investigate isophotal properties and dust morphology in the nuclear
regions of 84 radio galaxies, imaged in the optical and near-infrared as part
of Hubble Space Telescope snapshot surveys. We present a sample-wide trend
between host galaxy isophotal structure and the inclination of dusty
circumnuclear disks at the centers of 13 of these objects. We find that
galaxies containing edge-on disks are invariably seen to possess boxy
isophotes, while round, face-on disks are seen exclusively in objects with
round or elliptical isophotes. Dust-rich sources with disky isophotes are
observed only to possess dust in the form of extended filamentary lanes, and
not in settled distributions like disks. As we do not expect that edge-on and
face-on disks reside in different populations of galaxies, we conclude that
perceived isophotal boxiness is dependent upon the angle at which the observer
views the host galaxy's axis of symmetry. We discuss our results in the context
of dissipative merger scenarios, and infer that dusty disks primarily reside in
old, boxy remnants of gas-poor galaxy mergers, whereas filamentary dust lanes
reside in younger disky remnants of gas-rich mergers.

The favoured progenitors of long-duration gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) are rapidly
rotating Wolf-Rayet (WR) stars. However, most Galactic WR stars are slow
rotators, as stellar winds are thought to remove angular momentum. This poses a
challenge to the collapsar model. Recent observations indicate that GRBs occur
predominately in low metallicity (Z) environments, which may resolve the
problem: lower Z leads to less mass loss, which may inhibit angular momentum
removal, allowing WR stars to remain rotating rapidly until collapse. We wish
to determine whether low Z WR stars rotate on average more rapidly than
Galactic WR stars, and perform a Very Large Telescope (VLT) linear
spectropolarimetry survey of WR stars in the low Z environment of the Large
Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and compare our results with the Galactic sample. We
find that only 2 out of 13 (i.e. 15%) of LMC WR stars show line polarization
effects, compared to a similarly low fraction of ~15-20% for Galactic WR stars.
The low incidence of line polarization effects in LMC WR stars suggests that
the threshold metallicity where significant differences in WR rotational
properties occur is below that of the LMC (Z ~ 0.5 Zsun), possibly constraining
GRB progenitor channels to this upper metallicity.

We consider the problem of coloring the vertices of a large sparse random
graph with a given number of colors so that no adjacent vertices have the same
color. Using the cavity method, we present a detailed and systematic analytical
study of the space of proper colorings (solutions).
  We show that for a fixed number of colors and as the average vertex degree
(number of constraints) increases, the set of solutions undergoes several phase
transitions similar to those observed in the mean field theory of glasses.
First, at the clustering transition, the entropically dominant part of the
phase space decomposes into an exponential number of pure states so that beyond
this transition a uniform sampling of solutions becomes hard. Afterward, the
space of solutions condenses over a finite number of the largest states and
consequently the total entropy of solutions becomes smaller than the annealed
one. Another transition takes place when in all the entropically dominant
states a finite fraction of nodes freezes so that each of these nodes is
allowed a single color in all the solutions inside the state. Eventually, above
the coloring threshold, no more solutions are available. We compute all the
critical connectivities for Erdos-Renyi and regular random graphs and determine
their asymptotic values for large number of colors.
  Finally, we discuss the algorithmic consequences of our findings. We argue
that the onset of computational hardness is not associated with the clustering
transition and we suggest instead that the freezing transition might be the
relevant phenomenon. We also discuss the performance of a simple local Walk-COL
algorithm and of the belief propagation algorithm in the light of our results.

In this work, we present a study on scale-free avalanches in the multifractal random walk (MFRW) model. The MFRW model is a stochastic process that exhibits multifractal features and has been used to model a wide range of natural phenomena, such as turbulence and financial markets. Our main focus is on the analysis of the avalanche properties in the MFRW model, such as the size and duration of the avalanches.

To study the avalanche properties, we use numerical simulations and statistical analysis techniques. We find that the avalanches in the MFRW model exhibit power-law distributed sizes and durations, which is a characteristic feature of scale-free systems. We also investigate the multifractal exponents of the MFRW model and their relation to the avalanche properties.

The main problem that this work attempts to solve is to provide a better understanding of the scale-free dynamics in the MFRW model. By analyzing the avalanche properties, we gain insights into the underlying dynamics of the system and how they contribute to the multifractal properties. Furthermore, our study can help to explain the occurrence of extreme events in natural systems that exhibit multifractal features.

The motivation for this research is rooted in the need to understand the complex dynamics of natural systems and their underlying mechanisms. By studying the MFRW model, we hope to contribute to the development of a unified framework to describe these complex systems. Our work can also have applications in various fields, such as risk management and resource allocation.

In this study, we present a spectrophotometric model that has been applied to the Wide-Field Infrared Survey Explorer (WINGS) dataset of cluster galaxies. The motivation for this research was to investigate the physical properties that govern the evolution of galaxies in clusters, including their star formation histories, chemical enrichment, and mass assembly. By analyzing the spectroscopic data from the WINGS survey, we aimed to identify key correlations between these properties and determine their underlying physical drivers.

Our model is based on a Bayesian approach, which allows us to quantify the uncertainties associated with our results and provides a robust statistical framework for comparing different models. Using this method, we found that the star formation histories of galaxies in clusters are complex and exhibit a wide range of timescales and amplitudes of star formation activity. We also observed that the chemical enrichment histories of these galaxies are strongly influenced by their stellar masses, with more massive galaxies showing higher metallicity.

Overall, our results support the hypothesis that the evolution of galaxies in clusters is driven by a combination of internal processes, such as star formation and chemical enrichment, as well as external processes, such as tidal interactions and gas stripping. Our spectrophotometric model provides a powerful tool for understanding these complex mechanisms and sheds new light on the fundamental processes that shape the evolution of galaxies in the universe.

In this work, we investigate the uniqueness of polynomial canonical representations in algebraic geometry. Our study departs from the long-standing problem of finding a constructive and universally accepted method of representing a polynomial, which is capable of reflecting both the properties of the root and the invariance of the polynomial's formulation under particular transformations.

We adopt a systematic approach that enables us to establish the existence and uniqueness of canonical representations of polynomials that are determined by specific modules. Using this methodology, we prove that two distinct polynomials have different canonical representations if and only if they are non-isomorphic in the associated module. Additionally, we provide algorithmic procedures that can be used to compute the canonical forms of polynomials, which gives us a concrete solution to the problem.

Our motivation for this research is primarily driven by the need to explore more effective and efficient ways of representing polynomials in algebraic geometry. The use of canonical representations has significant implications for decision-making algorithms and can simplify calculations of invariants such as Hilbert polynomials. Our study provides a formal and scientific perspective on the uniqueness of polynomial canonical representations. Our work significantly contributes to the field of algebraic geometry by providing an innovative approach to the problem of polynomial representation that has both theoretical and practical benefits.

In this study, we investigate the galaxies hosting strong CaII QSO absorption systems at redshifts lower than 0.5. These systems are characterized by a strong absorption feature in their spectra, which allows us to infer the presence of a dense, cool gas surrounding the absorber. While previous studies suggest that these gas clouds are often associated with galaxies, it is still unclear what properties of these galaxies may be responsible for the observed absorption features. 

We use data from images and spectra obtained with the Keck telescope and the Sloan Digital Sky Survey to identify the host galaxies of 17 strong CaII absorbers. We measure the properties of these galaxies, such as their stellar masses, star formation rates, and position relative to the absorber line-of-sight. In addition, we investigate the morphology of the galaxies, using an expert-based classification system. 

Our results suggest that the majority of the strong CaII absorbers are associated with galaxies that are massive and actively star-forming. We also find evidence for a spatial correlation between the absorbers and the galaxies, suggesting the importance of proximity in triggering the absorption phenomena. 

In conclusion, our study provides new insights into the nature of strong CaII QSO absorption systems, shedding light on the key role played by the host galaxies in shaping the properties of these gas clouds.

In this research, we introduce a collaborative framework that aims to facilitate the exchange and sharing of product information in a supply chain context. The motivation for this research comes from the challenges that many companies face when it comes to managing product information across their supply chain. We aimed to address this issue by developing a framework that would allow for easier exchange of product information between suppliers, manufacturers, and customers. Our framework is built on the concept of collaboration, recognizing that effective communication and cooperation are essential for efficient supply chain management.

To develop our framework, we conducted a thorough review of the literature on supply chain management and collaboration, identifying key factors that would need to be incorporated into our design. We then conducted a series of interviews with stakeholders from different sectors of the supply chain, gathering their input on their needs and challenges. From this research, we developed a collaborative framework that allows for secure and efficient sharing of product information between supply chain partners.

The results show that our framework can significantly improve supply chain efficiency, reduce costs, and enhance customer satisfaction. Our approach represents an innovative way of addressing the problem of limited product information exchange between supply chain partners and presents new opportunities for collaboration that can lead to more efficient and effective supply chain management.

This paper presents hyperbolic rank rigidity results for rank 1,
nonpositively curved spaces. Let $M$ be a compact, rank 1 manifold with
nonpositive sectional curvature and suppose that along every geodesic in $M$
there is a parallel vector field making curvature $-a^2$ with the geodesic
direction. We prove that $M$ has constant curvature equal to $-a^2$ if $M$ is
odd dimensional, or if $M$ is even dimensional and has sectional curvature
pinched as follows: $-\Lambda^2 < K < -\lambda^2$ where $\lambda/\Lambda >
>.93$. When $-a^2$ is the upper curvature bound this gives a shorter proof of
the hyperbolic rank rigidity theorem of Hamenst\"{a}dt, subject to the pinching
condition in even dimension; in all other cases it is a new result. We also
present a rigidity result using only an assumption on maximal Lyapunov
exponents in direct analogy with work done by Connell. The proof of the main
theorem is simplified considerably by assuming strict negative curvature; in
fact, in all dimensions but 7 and 8 it is then an immediate consequence of
ergodicity of the $(dim(M)-1)$-frame flow. In these exceptional dimensions,
recourse to the dynamics of the 2-frame flow must be made and the scheme of
proof developed there can be generalized to deal with rank 1, nonpositively
curved spaces.

A basic question of protein structural studies is to which extent mutations
affect the stability. This question may be addressed starting from sequence
and/or from structure. In proteomics and genomics studies prediction of protein
stability free energy change (DDG) upon single point mutation may also help the
annotation process. The experimental SSG values are affected by uncertainty as
measured by standard deviations. Most of the DDG values are nearly zero (about
32% of the DDG data set ranges from -0.5 to 0.5 Kcal/mol) and both the value
and sign of DDG may be either positive or negative for the same mutation
blurring the relationship among mutations and expected DDG value. In order to
overcome this problem we describe a new predictor that discriminates between 3
mutation classes: destabilizing mutations (DDG<-0.5 Kcal/mol), stabilizing
mutations (DDG>0.5 Kcal/mol) and neutral mutations (-0.5<=DDG<=0.5 Kcal/mol).
In this paper a support vector machine starting from the protein sequence or
structure discriminates between stabilizing, destabilizing and neutral
mutations. We rank all the possible substitutions according to a three state
classification system and show that the overall accuracy of our predictor is as
high as 52% when performed starting from sequence information and 58% when the
protein structure is available, with a mean value correlation coefficient of
0.30 and 0.39, respectively. These values are about 20 points per cent higher
than those of a random predictor.

In this study, we investigated the nature of the metal-insulator transition in the low-dimensional organic conductor (TMTSF)2FSO3. Our work aimed to elucidate the mechanisms behind this intriguing phase transition that has been subject to intense research in the last few decades. To achieve this, we used infrared microspectroscopy, an extremely sensitive tool that allowed us to analyze the electronic properties of the sample with high spatial resolution. Our results showed that the transition from the metallic to the insulating state is driven by a loss of long-range coherence in the electronic states of the material, which leads to an increase in the resistance and a decrease in the electronic mobility. We found that this phenomenon is related to the formation of a charge density wave, which induces a modulation of the lattice periodicity, and can be controlled by varying the temperature of the sample.

Our work contributes to the understanding of the fundamental physics behind metal-insulator transitions, which have important implications in the development of electronic devices and materials with novel functionalities. The key ideas behind our study were to identify the electronic mechanisms that drive the transition and to provide a quantitative description of the electronic properties of the sample in both the metallic and insulating phases. Our methodology involved the fabrication of high-quality samples and the use of state-of-the-art infrared microspectroscopy, which allowed us to probe the electronic properties of the material with high spatial resolution. Overall, our findings provide valuable insights into the complex interplay between electronic, optical, and structural properties in low-dimensional materials, and pave the way for the development of new technologies based on their unique properties.

The generation interval is the time between the infection time of an infected
person and the infection time of his or her infector. Probability density
functions for generation intervals have been an important input for epidemic
models and epidemic data analysis. In this paper, we specify a general
stochastic SIR epidemic model and prove that the mean generation interval
decreases when susceptible persons are at risk of infectious contact from
multiple sources. The intuition behind this is that when a susceptible person
has multiple potential infectors, there is a ``race'' to infect him or her in
which only the first infectious contact leads to infection. In an epidemic, the
mean generation interval contracts as the prevalence of infection increases. We
call this global competition among potential infectors. When there is rapid
transmission within clusters of contacts, generation interval contraction can
be caused by a high local prevalence of infection even when the global
prevalence is low. We call this local competition among potential infectors.
Using simulations, we illustrate both types of competition.
  Finally, we show that hazards of infectious contact can be used instead of
generation intervals to estimate the time course of the effective reproductive
number in an epidemic. This approach leads naturally to partial likelihoods for
epidemic data that are very similar to those that arise in survival analysis,
opening a promising avenue of methodological research in infectious disease
epidemiology.

We analyse high-time-resolution spectroscopy of the AM CVn stars HP Librae
and V803 Centauri, taken with the New Technology Telescope (NTT) and the Very
Large Telescope (VLT) of the European Southern Observatory, Chile.
  We present evidence that the literature value for V803 Cen's orbital period
is incorrect, based on an observed `S-wave' in the binary's spectrogram. We
measure a spectroscopic period P=1596.4+/-1.2s of the S-wave feature, which is
significantly shorter than the 1611-second periods found in previous
photometric studies. We conclude that the latter period likely represents a
`superhump'. If one assumes that our S-wave period is the orbital period, V803
Cen's mass ratio can be expected to be much less extreme than previously
thought, at q~0.07 rather than q~0.016. This relaxes the constraints on the
masses of the components considerably: the donor star does then not need to be
fully degenerate, and the mass of the accreting white dwarf no longer has to be
very close to the Chandrasekhar limit.
  For HP Lib, we similarly measure a spectroscopic period P=1102.8+/-0.2s. This
supports the identification of HP Lib's photometric periods found in the
literature, and the constraints upon the masses derived from them.

In this paper, we present an analysis of infrared sources in a 11-micron selected sample from early data of the AKARI North Ecliptic Pole Deep Survey. Our motivation for this research was to characterize the nature of the sources and determine their properties, in order to gain insight into their origin and evolution. 

We utilized a multi-wavelength approach in our analysis, incorporating data from various surveys including AKARI, WISE and Spitzer. We identified a total of 94 sources within the selected sample, with 19 classified as galaxies and 75 as stars. Of the galaxies, 11 were classified as Seyfert galaxies and five as starburst galaxies. Several of the sources exhibited mid-infrared excess emission, indicating the presence of circumstellar disks or envelopes. Follow-up observations with higher sensitivity and resolution are required to confirm these results and provide more detailed information on the properties of these sources. 

Our work attempts to solve the problem of understanding the nature of infrared sources in this particular survey sample. Our findings provide valuable information for future studies of these sources and contribute to our understanding of the mid-infrared properties of galaxies and stars.

In this paper we address the problem of finding the sensing capacity of
sensor networks for a class of linear observation models and a fixed SNR
regime. Sensing capacity is defined as the maximum number of signal dimensions
reliably identified per sensor observation. In this context sparsity of the
phenomena is a key feature that determines sensing capacity. Precluding the SNR
of the environment the effect of sparsity on the number of measurements
required for accurate reconstruction of a sparse phenomena has been widely
dealt with under compressed sensing. Nevertheless the development there was
motivated from an algorithmic perspective. In this paper our aim is to derive
these bounds in an information theoretic set-up and thus provide algorithm
independent conditions for reliable reconstruction of sparse signals. In this
direction we first generalize the Fano's inequality and provide lower bounds to
the probability of error in reconstruction subject to an arbitrary distortion
criteria. Using these lower bounds to the probability of error, we derive upper
bounds to sensing capacity and show that for fixed SNR regime sensing capacity
goes down to zero as sparsity goes down to zero. This means that
disproportionately more sensors are required to monitor very sparse events. Our
next main contribution is that we show the effect of sensing diversity on
sensing capacity, an effect that has not been considered before. Sensing
diversity is related to the effective \emph{coverage} of a sensor with respect
to the field. In this direction we show the following results (a) Sensing
capacity goes down as sensing diversity per sensor goes down; (b) Random
sampling (coverage) of the field by sensors is better than contiguous location
sampling (coverage).

We present here a study that focuses on extending the redshift-distance relation in Cosmological General Relativity to higher redshifts, thus enabling the estimation of distances of distant objects in the universe. Our study aims to address the problem of finding solutions for current theoretical problems in cosmology by refining and adding new insights to the existing model of Cosmological General Relativity. The key ideas of our study revolve around the cosmological principle, which states that the universe is homogeneous and isotropic on large scales, and the validity of the General Theory of Relativity in cosmology. 

Our methodology involves using distance measurements from supernovae and quasars, which we use to determine the redshift-distance relation to higher redshifts. We model the observed luminosity distances as a function of redshift and derive an accurate estimate of the Hubble parameter. We also factor in the effects of gravitational lensing, which distorts the light coming from distant galaxies. 

Our results demonstrate that extending the redshift-distance relation to higher redshifts is valid, and it allows us to derive precise estimates of the cosmological parameters. We are confident that our study provides a solid framework for further investigations into the nature of dark energy, and it will contribute significantly to our understanding of the universe's evolution and structure. This study opens new avenues for researchers to explore by refining the Cosmological General Relativity model to explain the observed large-scale behavior of the universe.

Starting from the Liouville equation, we derive the exact hierarchy of
equations satisfied by the reduced distribution functions of the single species
point vortex gas in two dimensions. Considering an expansion of the solution in
powers of 1/N in a proper thermodynamic limit $N\to +\infty$, and neglecting
some collective effects, we derive a kinetic equation satisfied by the smooth
vorticity field which is valid at order $O(1/N)$. This equation was obtained
previously [P.H. Chavanis, Phys. Rev. E, 64, 026309 (2001)] from a more
abstract projection operator formalism. If we consider axisymmetric flows and
make a markovian approximation, we obtain a simpler kinetic equation which can
be studied in great detail. We discuss the properties of these kinetic
equations in regard to the $H$-theorem and the convergence (or not) towards the
statistical equilibrium state. We also study the growth of correlations by
explicitly calculating the time evolution of the two-body correlation function
in the linear regime. In a second part of the paper, we consider the relaxation
of a test vortex in a bath of field vortices and obtain the Fokker-Planck
equation by directly calculating the second (diffusion) and first (drift)
moments of the increment of position of the test vortex. A specificity of our
approach is to obtain general equations, with a clear physical meaning, that
are valid for flows that are not necessarily axisymmetric and that take into
account non-Markovian effects. A limitations of our approach, however, is that
it ignores collective effects.

In this study, we investigate the Hall drift of axisymmetric magnetic fields in solid neutron-star matter. Neutron-star matter is a complex system with a highly dense and magnetized core, which is the result of the collapse of a massive star. Magnetic fields play a crucial role in neutron-star physics, affecting everything from the dynamics of the star's crust to the generation of high-energy electromagnetic radiation. 

Our work attempts to contribute to the understanding of the Hall drift of magnetic fields in neutron-star matter, which is a phenomenon where the charged particles move perpendicular to the magnetic field lines due to the Hall effect. Understanding this phenomenon is crucial for many astrophysical applications, such as modeling of magnetar outbursts and the generation of pulsar radio emission.

We develop a theoretical model based on the magnetohydrodynamic equations, and we derive a set of equations that describe the motion of a magnetic field in a solid neutron-star crust. We then use numerical simulations to analyze the behavior of the magnetic field lines under different conditions, such as different magnetic field strengths and shear rates.

Our results show that the Hall drift can significantly affect the dynamics of the magnetic field in neutron-star matter, leading to complex and interesting phenomena such as helical twisting and kinking of magnetic field lines. Our study provides a valuable contribution to the understanding of the dynamics of axisymmetric magnetic fields in neutron-star matter and has important implications for the modeling of astrophysical systems.

In this work, we investigate the residual correlations between decay products of $\pi^0\pi^0$ and $p\Sigma^0$ systems. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that understanding the dynamics of these systems can provide us with insights on the strong interaction, which is responsible for binding the quarks that make up the protons and neutrons in atomic nuclei. Our analysis is based on experimental data collected with the CLAS detector at Jefferson Lab, where $\pi^0\pi^0$ and $p\Sigma^0$ systems were produced through the use of a photon beam scattered off a liquid hydrogen target. We employ a two-step analysis procedure, where we first reconstruct the $\pi^0\pi^0$ and $p\Sigma^0$ invariant masses and then study the correlations between the decay products of these systems. By comparing our results with theoretical models, we find that the data can be well-described by calculations that take into account both short- and long-range contributions to the hadronic potential.

Our work aims to shed light on the nature of the final-state interactions that occur between the decay products of these systems, which can help us understand how quarks bind together to form hadrons, and how these hadrons interact with each other. Ultimately, this research can advance our understanding of the strong nuclear force that governs the structure and behavior of atomic nuclei, and may have practical implications for nuclear applications in energy, medicine, and defense.

In this work, we investigate the dipole formation at interfaces of alkanethiolate self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) and Ag(111) to understand the mechanism of surface-adsorbate interactions at the atomic level. The key idea is to shed light on the origin of dipole moments in SAMs and their impact on surface reactivity, catalysis, and electronic properties. To achieve this goal, we utilized a combination of scanning tunneling microscopy (STM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

We found that the dipole moment at the interface between alkanethiolate SAMs and Ag(111) arises from the charge transfer from the substrate to the thiolate headgroups. Furthermore, we observed that the dipole orientation changes with the length and functionalization of the alkanethiolate chain. Our results reveal that the adsorption geometry plays a crucial role in the dipole formation and orientation. The obtained data agrees well with theoretical predictions from DFT calculations, demonstrating its reliability in predicting the properties of SAMs.

This research solves the problem of understanding the nature and function of dipoles in SAMs, which is essential for the development of functionalized surfaces with tailored properties. The motivation for this work is to advance our knowledge of SAMs and their potential applications in catalysis, sensing, and nanoelectronics. Overall, this research contributes to the fundamental understanding of interfacial phenomena and surface chemistry.

We verify that the dilaton together with one exactly marginal field, form a
moduli space of marginal deformations of closed bosonic string field theory to
polynomial order five. We use the results of this successful check in order to
find the best functional form of a fit of quintic amplitudes. We then use this
fit in order to accurately compute the tachyon and dilaton effective potential
in the limit of infinite level. We observe that to order four, the effective
potential gives unexpectedly accurate results for the vacuum. We are thus led
to conjecture that the effective potential, to a given order, is a good
approximation to the whole potential including all interactions from the
vertices up to this order from the untruncated string field. We then go on and
compute the effective potential to order five. We analyze its vacuum structure
and find that it has several saddle points, including the Yang-Zwiebach vacuum,
but also a local minimum. We discuss the possible physical meanings of these
vacua.

Based on previous studies deriving the chiral Lagrangian for pseudo scalar
mesons from the first principle of QCD, we derive the electroweak chiral
Lagrangian and build up a formulation for computing its coefficients from
one-doublet technicolor model and a schematic topcolor-assisted technicolor
model. We find that the coefficients of the electroweak chiral Lagrangian for
the topcolor-assisted technicolor model are divided into three parts: direct
TC2 interaction part, TC1 and TC2 induced effective Z' particle contribution
part, and ordinary quarks contribution part. The first two parts are computed
in this paper and we show that the direct TC2 interaction part is the same as
that in the one-doublet technicolor model, while effective Z' contributions are
at least proportional to the p^2 order parameter \beta_1 in the electroweak
chiral Lagrangian and typical features of topcolor-assisted technicolor model
are that it only allows positive T and U parameters and the T parameter varies
in the range 0\sim 1/(25\alpha), the upper bound of T parameter will decrease
as long as Z' mass become large. The S parameter can be either positive or
negative depending on whether the Z' mass is large or small. The Z' mass is
also bounded above and the upper bound depend on value of T parameter. We
obtain the values for all the coefficients of the electroweak chiral Lagrangian
up to order of p^4.

To examine the evolution of giant molecular clouds in the stream of a hot
plasma we performed two-dimensional hydrodynamical simulations that take full
account of self-gravity, heating and cooling effects and heat conduction by
electrons. We use the thermal conductivity of a fully ionized hydrogen plasma
proposed by Spitzer and a saturated heat flux according to Cowie & McKee in
regions where the mean free path of the electrons is large compared to the
temperature scaleheight. Significant structural and evolutionary differences
occur between simulations with and without heat conduction. Dense clouds in
pure dynamical models experience dynamical destruction by Kelvin-Helmholtz (KH)
instability. In static models heat conduction leads to evaporation of such
clouds. Heat conduction acting on clouds in a gas stream smooths out steep
temperature and density gradients at the edge of the cloud because the
conduction timescale is shorter than the cooling timescale. This diminishes the
velocity gradient between the streaming plasma and the cloud, so that the
timescale for the onset of KH instabilities increases, and the surface of the
cloud becomes less susceptible to KH instabilities. The stabilisation effect of
heat conduction against KH instability is more pronounced for smaller and less
massive clouds. As in the static case more realistic cloud conditions allow
heat conduction to transfer hot material onto the cloud's surface and to mix
the accreted gas deeper into the cloud.

We study the possibility that the mutual interactions between Jupiter and
Saturn prevented Type II migration from driving these planets much closer to
the Sun. Our work extends previous results by Masset and Snellgrove (2001), by
exploring a wider set of initial conditions and disk parameters, and by using a
new hydrodynamical code that properly describes for the global viscous
evolution of the disk. Initially both planets migrate towards the Sun, and
Saturn's migration tends to be faster. As a consequence, they eventually end up
locked in a mean motion resonance. If this happens in the 2:3 resonance, the
resonant motion is particularly stable, and the gaps opened by the planets in
the disk may overlap. This causes a drastic change in the torque balance for
the two planets, which substantially slows down the planets' inward migration.
If the gap overlap is substantial, planet migration may even be stopped or
reversed. As the widths of the gaps depend on disk viscosity and scale height,
this mechanism is particularly efficient in low viscosity, cool disks. We
discuss the compatibility of our results with the initial conditions adopted in
Tsiganis et al. (2005) and Gomes et al. (2005) to explain the current orbital
architecture of the giant planets and the origin of the Late Heavy Bombardment
of the Moon.

In this study, we investigate whether the Bardeen-Petterson effect can be attributed to the warping and precession of the disk in NGC 4258. We use hydrodynamic simulations to analyze the behavior of the disk in the presence of a central supermassive black hole and determine its dynamical evolution. Our results indicate that the Bardeen-Petterson effect could play a significant role in inducing the observed warping and precession in the disk. We find that the inner disk aligns with the black hole's equatorial plane due to its strong gravitational influence while the outer disk retains its initial orientation leading to a warped disk. 

The problem we attempt to solve is to understand the nature of the warp and precession in NGC 4258 since it is essential for understanding the evolution of similar systems. The Bardeen-Petterson effect was first proposed as a mechanism to explain the alignment of accretion disks with black hole equatorial planes. However, its role in warping and precession has been unclear. We aim to clarify its contribution in the warp and precession observed in NGC 4258. 

The motivation for this research comes from the need to understand the process of disk alignment and evolution in systems with black holes. NGC 4258 is an ideal object for studying this process due to its well-observed disk and black hole. Our findings could provide fundamental insights into the dynamics of similar systems and help to constrain models of disk-black hole interaction.

In this study, our team conducted experimental investigations to explore the phenomenon of vacancy-induced ferromagnetism in undoped TiO2. This research is motivated by the need to clarify and understand the underlying mechanisms that drive ferromagnetic behavior in non-magnetic materials. The problem that our work attempts to tackle is the scarce knowledge of the origin and characteristics of ferromagnetic ordering in TiO2, which could have potential applications in various fields like spintronics and catalysis. 

We employed various experimental techniques, namely thermoluminescence measurements, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM), and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy, to investigate the properties of TiO2 samples under varying conditions. Our study confirmed that the presence of oxygen vacancies in TiO2 induces ferromagnetism in the system, which is attributed to the exchange interaction between the localized electrons at the vacancy sites. Furthermore, we observed that the magnetization of TiO2 samples improved with increasing irradiation dose, temperature, and annealing time.

In conclusion, our experimental findings provide essential insights into the origin and nature of vacancy-induced ferromagnetism in TiO2. Such knowledge could pave the way for the development of new approaches for engineering the magnetic properties of non-magnetic materials, bringing significant advancements in various scientific and industrial applications.

A comprehensive picture of high-mass star formation has remained elusive, in
part because examples of high-mass YSOs tend to be relatively distant, deeply
embedded, and confused with other emission sources. These factors have impeded
dynamical investigations within tens of AU of high-mass YSOs--scales that are
critical for probing the interfaces where outflows from accretion disks are
launched and collimated. Using observations of SiO masers obtained with the VLA
and the VLBA, the KaLYPSO project is overcoming these limitations by mapping
the structure and dynamical/temporal evolution of the material 10-1000 AU from
the nearest high-mass YSO: Radio Source I in the Orion BN/KL region. Our data
include ~40 epochs of VLBA observations over a several-year period, allowing us
to track the proper motions of individual SiO maser spots and to monitor
changes in the physical conditions of the emitting material with time.
Ultimately these data will provide 3-D maps of the outflow structure over
approximately 30% of the outflow crossing time. Here we summarize recent
results from the KaLYPSO project, including evidence that high-mass star
formation is occurring via disk-mediated accretion.

Our research investigates the disk oscillation modes that occur in cataclysmic variables (CVs) and other Newtonian accretors. This work aims to address the long-standing problem of understanding the physical processes that contribute to the complex variability observed in these systems. We are motivated by the fact that CVs, in particular, exhibit a range of phenomena such as superhumps, quasi-periodic oscillations, and dwarf nova outbursts that have been challenging to explain with existing models. 

In our study, we use a combination of theoretical and observational techniques to explore the properties of disk oscillations in these systems. Key ideas include the use of non-linear hydrodynamic simulations to model the behavior of accretion disks, and the identification of mode signatures in the observational data through the application of time-series analysis techniques. 

Our methodology involves analysis of large datasets from ground-based and space-based observatories, including the Kepler mission. We apply our theoretical models to these data sets, searching for correlations and patterns that can help us refine our understanding of disk oscillation modes. Ultimately, our research aims to provide new insights into the mechanisms that drive accretion and disk instability in CVs and other accretion-powered systems.

Equation for evolution of the four-component Stokes vector in weakly
anisotropic and smoothly inhomogeneous media is derived on the basis of
quasi-isotropic approximation of the geometrical optics method, which provides
consequent asymptotic solution of Maxwell equations. Our equation generalizes
previous results, obtained for the normal propagation of electromagnetic waves
in stratified media. It is valid for curvilinear rays with torsion and is
capable to describe normal modes conversion in the inhomogeneous media.
Remarkably, evolution of the Stokes vector is described by the
Bargmann-Michel-Telegdi equation for relativistic spin precession, whereas the
equation for the three-component Stokes vector resembles the Landau-Lifshitz
equation for spin precession in ferromegnetic systems. General theory is
applied for analysis of polarization evolution in a magnetized plasma. We also
emphasize fundamental features of the non-Abelian polarization evolution in
anisotropic inhomogeneous media and illustrate them by simple examples.

In this paper, we present our analysis of the Norma Cluster (ACO 3627), the largest and most massive cluster in the Great Attractor. The motivation behind our research was the need to understand the dynamics of this massive cluster, which is essential for understanding the large-scale structure of the universe. Our study is based on data acquired through spectroscopic observations of 311 cluster members using the AAOmega instrument on the Anglo-Australian Telescope. 

Our key idea is to use the radial velocity distribution of cluster members to determine the dynamical state of the cluster, including its virial mass, velocity dispersion, and substructure. We employ various analysis techniques, including the biweight scale estimator, the Dressler-Shectman test, and the maximum-likelihood method, to determine the cluster's properties. Our results show that the cluster has a virial mass of M_vir=1.87×10^15 h^-1 M_☉ and a velocity dispersion of σ_v=1219±49 km s^-1. We also found significant substructure within the cluster, indicating that it is still in the process of formation and is not yet virialized. 

In conclusion, our study provides a detailed analysis of the dynamical properties of the Norma Cluster, emphasizing its significance as the most massive cluster in the Great Attractor and its potential impact on the large-scale structure of the universe. Our results and methodology can be extended to other massive clusters and have significant implications for our overall understanding of the universe's dynamics.

We present bright-dark vector solitons in quasi-one-dimensional spinor (F=1)
Bose-Einstein condensates. Using a multiscale expansion technique, we reduce
the corresponding nonintegrable system of three coupled Gross-Pitaevskii
equations (GPEs) to a completely integrable Yajima-Oikawa system. In this way,
we obtain approximate solutions for small-amplitude vector solitons of
dark-dark-bright and bright-bright-dark types, in terms of the $m_{F}=+1,-1,0$
spinor components, respectively. By means of numerical simulations of the full
GPE system, we demonstrate that these states indeed feature soliton properties,
i.e., they propagate undistorted and undergo quasi-elastic collisions. It is
also shown that, in the presence of a parabolic trap of strength $\omega $, the
bright component(s) is (are) guided by the dark one(s), and, as a result, the
small-amplitude vector soliton as a whole performs harmonic oscillations of
frequency $\omega/ \sqrt{2}$ in the shallow soliton limit. We investigate
numerically deviations from this prediction, as the depth of the solitons is
increased, as well as when the strength of the spin-dependent interaction is
modified.

In our work on "Representations of Tame Quivers and Affine Canonical Bases," our primary motivation was to explore the fundamental relationship between quiver representations and the affine Hecke algebra, which has emerged as a critical tool for the classification and study of representation theory. Specifically, we sought to establish a new connection between these two representations by examining the role of affine canonical bases in the context of representations of tame quivers.

Using a combination of algebraic and combinatorial techniques, we were able to prove several key results linking canonical bases to families of quiver representations, including the construction of new bases for representations of tame quivers that bear a close resemblance to the affine canonical bases. Additionally, we were able to extend these results beyond the tame case, providing new insight into the interplay between finite-dimensional representations of quivers and the more general theory of affine Hecke algebras.

Overall, our work represents a significant advance in the study of algebraic representation theory, providing a deeper understanding of the complex relationship between quivers and affine Hecke algebras. By building on the theoretical foundations established in this work, future researchers may be able to continue pushing the boundaries of our understanding of representation theory and its applications in a wide range of fields.

The cycling operation is a special kind of conjugation that can be applied to
elements in Artin's braid groups, in order to reduce their length. It is a key
ingredient of the usual solutions to the conjugacy problem in braid groups. In
their seminal paper on braid-cryptography, Ko, Lee et al. proposed the {\it
cycling problem} as a hard problem in braid groups that could be interesting
for cryptography. In this paper we give a polynomial solution to that problem,
mainly by showing that cycling is surjective, and using a result by Maffre
which shows that pre-images under cycling can be computed fast. This result
also holds in every Artin-Tits group of spherical type.
  On the other hand, the conjugacy search problem in braid groups is usually
solved by computing some finite sets called (left) ultra summit sets
(left-USS), using left normal forms of braids. But one can equally use right
normal forms and compute right-USS's. Hard instances of the conjugacy search
problem correspond to elements having big (left and right) USS's. One may think
that even if some element has a big left-USS, it could possibly have a small
right-USS. We show that this is not the case in the important particular case
of rigid braids. More precisely, we show that the left-USS and the right-USS of
a given rigid braid determine isomorphic graphs, with the arrows reversed, the
isomorphism being defined using iterated cycling. We conjecture that the same
is true for every element, not necessarily rigid, in braid groups and
Artin-Tits groups of spherical type.

In this paper, we investigate the (an)isotropy of the Hubble diagram by comparing different hemispheres. The Hubble diagram is a plot of the apparent magnitude of an object on the y-axis against its redshift on the x-axis. It is a fundamental tool in modern cosmology, providing valuable insight into the expansion of the universe. However, recent research suggests that the Hubble diagram may exhibit anisotropy, meaning that it looks different depending on the direction in which we observe the universe. This could have significant implications for our understanding of the structure and dynamics of the cosmos.

To investigate this, we compare the Hubble diagrams of two hemisphere maps using different statistical tools and tests. We use the Union2.1 compilation of supernovae type Ia observations for this purpose. Our results suggest that the Hubble diagram exhibits slight anisotropy when comparing hemispheres in terms of the mean of absolute magnitudes or the residuals of the fits. However, we also found some agreement with the null hypothesis of no difference between hemispheres, and this implies that more work needs to be done to confirm the anisotropy findings.

Overall, this work contributes to the ongoing efforts to understand the fundamental properties of the universe, and it suggests that further research is necessary to clarify the (an)isotropy of the Hubble diagram.

In this work, we introduce a novel methodology for decoding the top flavor violation (TFV) at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) with B factories. TFV is a rare phenomenon in the standard model of particle physics and can only occur through the exchange of a Higgs boson. Its detection will provide insights into the Higgs sector and potential extensions to the standard model. However, its rarity makes its detection challenging, which is why the LHC and B factories are used together to increase the chances of detection.

Our methodology is based on combining the differential decay rates of top quarks with heavy quarks at the LHC and B factories. Using a kinematic analysis, we show that this approach increases sensitivity to TFV processes and reduces uncertainties. Our methodology provides a powerful tool for discriminating between different models of new physics that predict TFV, such as two-Higgs-doublet models and models with extra dimensions.

The motivation behind this research is to identify new physics beyond the standard model and to shed light on the Higgs sector. The successful detection of TFV will provide clear evidence of new physics, and our methodology will enable researchers to make more precise predictions and distinguish between different models. This work represents a significant step forward in our understanding of the standard model, and we believe it will have important implications for future high-energy physics experiments.

In this paper, we present a new implementation of feedback due to active
galactic nuclei (AGN) in cosmological simulations of galaxy formation. We
assume that a fraction of jet energy, which is generated by an AGN, is
transferred to the surrounding gas as thermal energy. Combining a theoretical
model of mass accretion onto black holes with a multiphase description of
star-forming gas, we self-consistently follow evolution of both galaxies and
their central black holes. The novelty in our model is that we consider two
distinct accretion modes: standard radiatively efficient thin accretion disks
and radiatively inefficient accretion flows which we will generically refer to
as RIAFs; motivated by theoretical models for jet production in accretion
disks, we assume that only the RIAF is responsible for the AGN feedback. We
find that, after an initial episode of bursting star formation, the accretion
rate onto the central black hole drops so that the accretion disk switches to a
RIAF structure. At this point, the feedback from the AGN becomes efficient and
slightly suppresses star formation in the galactic disk and almost completely
halts star formation in the bulge. As a result, the nucleus becomes a
stochastically fuelled low-luminosity AGN (Seyfert galaxy) with recurrent
short-lived episodes of activity after the star bursts. Our model predicts
several properties of the low-luminosity AGN including the bolometric
luminosity, jet powers, the effect on kpc-scale of the radio jet and the AGN
lifetime, which are in broad agreement with observations of Seyfert galaxies
and their radio activity. We also find that the mass ratios between the central
black hole and the the host spheroid at z = 0 are ~10^{-3} regardless of the
strength of either supernova feedback or AGN feedback. (abridged)

It is well known that multigrid methods are optimally efficient for solution
of elliptic equations (O(N)), which means that effort is proportional to the
number of points at which the solution is evaluated). Thus this is an ideal
method to solve the initial data/constraint equations in General Relativity for
(for instance) black hole interactions, or for other strong-field gravitational
configurations. Recent efforts have produced finite difference multigrid
solvers for domains with holes (excised regions). We present here the extension
of these concepts to higher order (fourth-, sixth- and eigth-order). The high
order convergence allows rapid solution on relatively small computational
grids. Also, general relativity evolution codes are moving to typically
fourth-order; data have to be computed at least as accurately as this same
order for straightfoward demonstration of the proper order of convergence in
the evolution.
  Our vertex-centered multigrid code demonstrates globally high-order-accurate
solutions of elliptic equations over domains containing holes, in two spatial
dimensions with fixed (Dirichlet) outer boundary conditions, and in three
spatial dimensions with {\it Robin} outer boundary conditions. We demonstrate a
``real world'' 3-dimensional problem which is the solution of the conformally
flat Hamiltonian constraint of General Relativity. The success of this method
depends on: a) the choice of the discretization near the holes; b) the
definition of the location of the inner boundary, which allows resolution of
the hole even on the coarsest grids; and on maintaining the same order of
convergence at the boundaries as in the interior of the computational domain.

We have combined multi-wavelength observations of a selected sample of
starforming galaxies with galaxy evolution models in order to compare the
results obtained for different SFR tracers and to study the effect that the
evolution of the starforming regions has on them. We also aimed at obtaining a
better understanding of the corrections due to extinction and nuclear activity
on the derivation of the SFR. We selected the sample from Chandra data for the
well studied region Chandra Deep Field South (CDFS) and chose the objects that
also have UV and IR data from GALEX and GOODS-Spitzer respectively.
  Our main finding is that there is good agreement between the extinction
corrected SFR(UV) and the SFR(X), and we confirm the use of X-ray luminosities
as a trustful tracer of recent star formation activity. Nevertheless, at
SFR(UV) larger than about 5Msol/year there are several galaxies with an excess
of SFR(X) suggesting the presence of an obscured AGN not detected in the
optical spectra. We conclude that the IR luminosity is driven by recent star
formation even in those galaxies where the SFR(X) is an order of magnitude
higher than the SFR(UV) and therefore may harbour an AGN. One object shows
SFR(X) much lower than expected based on the SFR(UV); this SFR(X) `deficit' may
be due to an early transient phase before most of the massive X-ray binaries
were formed. An X-ray deficit could be used to select extremely young bursts in
an early phase just after the explosion of the first supernovae associated with
massive stars and before the onset of massive X-ray binaries.

In this work, we present a microscopic model for the neutron dynamic structure factor of solid methane in phase II. Our study seeks to understand the behavior of the collective excitations of methane molecules in a high-pressure environment. The neutron dynamic structure factor is a tool that provides important information about the atomic and molecular motions within a material. Despite extensive experimental studies of the neutron dynamic structure factor of solid methane, there is still a lack of understanding regarding the underlying mechanisms that drive the excitation dynamics in this material. 

To address this problem, we developed a microscopic model based on first-principles calculations of the intermolecular potentials and the molecular dynamics simulations. Our calculations show that the excitation frequency of the collective excitations in solid methane is highly sensitive to the intermolecular potential and the density of the material. Furthermore, we found that the neutron dynamic structure factor of solid methane in phase II can be described by a superposition of oscillatory modes and a low-frequency background, which arises due to the strong interactions between the methane molecules. 

Overall, this study provides a deeper understanding of the nature of the collective excitations in solid methane and lays the groundwork for future studies of neutron scattering in high-pressure materials. Our results demonstrate the key role of intermolecular interactions and density-induced effects in the neutron scattering properties of materials.

In this work, we explore the dynamics of socioeconomic networks with long-range interactions. To achieve this, we develop a theoretical framework for modeling the interaction between agents in a network by considering the social influence of individuals at distant nodes. We apply our model to empirical data from the United States, analyzing patterns of wealth and income distribution in different social networks. 

Our results demonstrate that long-range interactions significantly affect the dynamics of socioeconomic networks, influencing the distribution of wealth and income across different demographics. Specifically, we find that the influence of individuals in distant nodes can spread rapidly throughout a network, leading to significant inequality in wealth and income. Our research highlights the importance of considering the role of long-range interactions in modeling socioeconomic networks and the potential policy implications of such models.

Our motivation for this work is grounded in the need to better understand the dynamics of socioeconomic networks and their impact on social inequality. By developing an innovative framework that incorporates long-range interactions, we contribute to a growing body of research on the subject and provide valuable insights for policymakers and social scientists. Overall, our findings suggest that addressing the issue of social inequality will require a holistic approach that accounts for the complex dynamics of socioeconomic networks.

In our work titled "Measuring the dark matter velocity anisotropy in galaxy clusters," we explore the dark matter distribution and its velocity anisotropy in galaxy clusters. Galaxy clusters are ideal laboratories for studying dark matter since they are the most massive gravitationally bound systems in the universe. Using numerical simulations and high-resolution spectroscopy data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey, we find that there is a strong velocity anisotropy of dark matter in galaxy clusters. The dark matter has a higher velocity dispersion in the radial direction than in the tangential direction, indicating that it is elongated along the line of sight. Furthermore, we find that there is a correlation between dark matter velocity anisotropy and cluster mass, with more massive clusters having a stronger anisotropy.

Our research is motivated by the need to better understand dark matter, a form of matter that constitutes around 85% of the total matter in the universe but cannot be directly observed. Measuring the dark matter velocity anisotropy in galaxy clusters can provide important insights into the three-dimensional distribution of dark matter, its nature, and its interactions with other matter. Our work attempts to solve the problem of accurately quantifying the dark matter velocity anisotropy in galaxy clusters, which is essential for constraining the properties of dark matter and testing theories of its formation and evolution.

In this study, we investigate multidimensional fluid motions with planar waves and explore the mathematical framework and computational techniques to understand and predict these complex phenomena.  Our aim is to unravel the intricate dynamics of fluids and waves by combining analytical and numerical methods. The study focuses on the evolution of fluids under the influence of planar waves, which are periodic disturbances that move along a single direction. 

We start by developing a mathematical model that incorporates the key physical and geometric factors that are relevant to the problem. This model describes the fluid motion as a system of partial differential equations that capture the conservation of mass, momentum, and energy of the fluid. Using the model, we derive the equations that govern the evolution of fluid properties, such as pressure, velocity, and density, under the influence of planar waves.

To solve the equations, we use numerical methods based on finite difference and finite element methods, which offer accurate and efficient solutions for complex problems. We validate our results using numerical simulations and compare them with analytical solutions where possible.

Our study provides significant insights into the dynamics of fluids with planar waves, which are relevant to many real-world applications, such as ocean engineering, coastal structures, and fluid mechanics. The results of this study could aid design and planning of systems affected by these phenomena.

In this study, we present evidence for nonlinear diffusive shock acceleration of cosmic rays in the 2006 outburst of RS Ophiuchi, a recurrent nova system. The motivation for our research stems from the fact that recurrent novae are believed to be potential sources of Galactic cosmic rays. However, the exact mechanism of how cosmic rays are produced in these systems remains unknown. Our aim was to investigate the acceleration and propagation of cosmic rays in RS Ophiuchi during its outburst, in order to shed more light on the mechanism of cosmic ray production in recurrent novae.

Using observations from multiple telescopes, we found that the acceleration of cosmic rays in RS Ophiuchi is consistent with the expected behavior of nonlinear diffusive shock acceleration. This process involves the transfer of energy from the shock wave to charged particles, which are subsequently accelerated and produce cosmic rays. Our results provide evidence for the role of shocks in the acceleration of cosmic rays in recurrent novae.

This study contributes to our understanding of how cosmic rays are produced in recurrent novae, which is important for understanding the origin and propagation of cosmic rays in the Galaxy. Our work attempts to solve the problem of how cosmic rays are produced in these systems, and our results have important implications for the study of cosmic ray origins and acceleration.

In this work, we examine the problem of retaining black holes with very large recoil velocities. Astrophysical scenarios, such as the merger of two black holes with unequal masses, often result in the ejection of the resulting black hole from the galaxy where it formed. These ejected black holes can reach velocities of thousands of kilometers per second, making it difficult to retain them in the galactic nucleus. Retaining these ejected black holes is crucial for understanding galaxy evolution and testing the predictions of general relativity.

We utilize numerical simulations to investigate the possibility of retaining a black hole with a recoil velocity of up to 5000 km/s via the dynamical friction effect. Our results show that the presence of stars can significantly enhance the dynamical friction, allowing for the retention of a significant fraction of the ejected black hole population. This result is particularly significant considering the potential for the upcoming LISA and Pulsar Timing Array experiments to detect gravitational waves from merged black holes.

The motivation for this research was to better understand the dynamics of black holes, their role in galaxy evolution and the implications for gravitational wave astronomy. Our findings suggest that the presence of stars can play a crucial role in the retention of ejected black holes and motivate further studies to explore the impact of different stellar populations.

In this work, we present a reappraisal of disclinations, dislocations and continuous defects. The aim of this research is to contribute to the understanding of the fundamental nature of crystal defects and to present a novel approach to their characterization. Our motivation lies in the fact that these defects are the foundation of material properties, and as such, the ability to accurately describe and predict their behavior is of paramount importance for material engineering and design.

To achieve this goal, we employed a combination of experimental and theoretical methods. We first investigated the behavior of disclinations, dislocations and continuous defects in different materials, using advanced microscopy techniques such as transmission electron microscopy and scanning transmission electron microscopy. We then utilized theoretical models based on continuum mechanics to gain insight into the underlying mechanisms of these defects.

Our results show that the traditional approach of considering disclinations, dislocations and continuous defects as independent entities is limited in its ability to describe the complex behavior of real materials. Instead, we propose a new perspective in which these defects are seen as interdependent and continuously interacting, forming a complex defect network that shapes the physical properties of materials. This approach, we believe, has the potential to open up new avenues of research in the study of materials, and to pave the way for the development of novel materials with tailored physical properties.

For the 100 largest European universities we studied the statistical
properties of bibliometric indicators related to research performance, field
citation density and journal impact. We find a size-dependent cumulative
advantage for the impact of universities in terms of total number of citations.
In previous work a similar scaling rule was found at the level of research
groups. Therefore we conjecture that this scaling rule is a prevalent property
of the science system. We observe that lower performance universities have a
larger size-dependent cumulative advantage for receiving citations than
top-performance universities. We also find that for the lower-performance
universities the fraction of not-cited publications decreases considerably with
size. Generally, the higher the average journal impact of the publications of a
university, the lower the number of not-cited publications. We find that the
average research performance does not dilute with size. Large top-performance
universities succeed in keeping a high performance over a broad range of
activities. This most probably is an indication of their scientific attractive
power. Next we find that particularly for the lower-performance universities
the field citation density provides a strong cumulative advantage in citations
per publication. The relation between number of citations and field citation
density found in this study can be considered as a second basic scaling rule of
the science system. Top-performance universities publish in journals with
significantly higher journal impact as compared to the lower performance
universities. We find a significant decrease of the fraction of self-citations
with increasing research performance, average field citation density, and
average journal impact.

Having already reported on the first photometric study of the
intermediate-age open cluster NGC 1883 (Carraro et al. 2003), we present in
this paper the first spectroscopic multi-epoch investigation of a sample of
evolved stars in the same cluster. The aim is to derive the cluster membership,
velocity and metallicity, and discuss recent claims in the literature (Tadross
2005) that NGC 1883 is as metal poor as globular clusters in the Halo. Besides,
being one of the few outer Galactic disk intermediate-age open clusters known
so far, it is an ideal target to improve our knowledge of the Galactic disk
radial abundance gradient, that is a basic ingredient for any chemical
evolution model of the Milky Way. The new data we obtained allow us to put NGC
1883's basic parameters more reliable. We find that the cluster has a mean
metallicity of [Fe/H] = -0.20$\pm$0.22, from which we infer an age
(650$^{+70}_{-70}$ Myr) close to the Hyades one and a Galactocentric distance
of 12.3$^{+0.4}_{-0.2}$ kpc. The metal abundance, kinematics, and position make
NGC 1883 a genuine outer disk intermediate-age open cluster. We confirm that in
the outer Galactic disk the abundance gradient is shallower than in the solar
vicinity.

In most theories of gravity involving torsion, the source for torsion is the
intrinsic spin of matter. Since the spins of fermions are normally randomly
oriented in macroscopic bodies, the torsion generated is normally negligible.
However, in a recent paper, Mao et al. point out that there is a class of
theories in which the angular momentum of macroscopic spinning bodies generates
a significant amount of torsion. They argue that by the principle of action
equals reaction, one would expect the angular momentum of test bodies to couple
to a background torsion field, and therefore the precession of the GPB
gyroscopes should be affected in these theories by the torsion generated by the
Earth. We show that in fact the principle of action equals reaction does not
apply to these theories. We examine in detail a generalization of the
Hayashi-Shirafuji theory suggested by Mao et al. called
Einstein-Hayashi-Shirafuji theory. There are a variety of different versions of
this theory, depending on the precise form of the coupling to matter chosen for
the torsion. We show that for any coupling to matter that is compatible with
the spin transport equation postulated by Mao et al., the theory has either
ghosts or an ill-posed initial value formulation. These theoretical problems
can be avoided by specializing the parameters of the theory and in addition
choosing the standard minimal coupling to matter of the torsion tensor. This
yields a consistent theory, but one in which the action equals reaction
principle is violated, and in which the angular momentum of the gyroscopes does
not couple to the Earth's torsion field. Thus, the Einstein-Hayashi-Shirafuji
theory does not predict a detectable torsion signal for Gravity Probe B. There
may be other torsion theories which do.

We have determined a dynamical mass for the companion to HD 33636 which
indicates it is a low-mass star instead of an exoplanet. Our result is based on
an analysis of Hubble Space Telescope (HST) astrometry and ground-based radial
velocity data. We have obtained high-cadence radial velocity measurements
spanning 1.3 years of HD 33636 with the Hobby-Eberly Telescope at McDonald
Observatory. We combined these data with previously published velocities to
create a data set that spans nine years. We used this data set to search for,
and place mass limits on, the existence of additional companions in the HD
33636 system. Our high-precision astrometric observations of the system with
the HST Fine Guidance Sensor 1r span 1.2 years. We simultaneously modeled the
radial velocity and astrometry data to determine the parallax, proper motion,
and perturbation orbit parameters of HD 33636. Our derived parallax, pi = 35.6
+/- 0.2 mas, agrees within the uncertainties with the Hipparcos value. We find
a perturbation period P = 2117.3 +/- 0.8 days, semimajor axis a_A = 14.2 +/-
0.2 mas, and system inclination i = 4.1 +/- 0.1 deg. Assuming the mass of the
primary star M_A = 1.02 +/- 0.03 M_sun, we obtain a companion mass M_B = 142
+/- 11 M_jup = 0.14 +/- 0.01 M_sun. The much larger true mass of the companion
relative to its minimum mass estimated from the spectroscopic orbit parameters
(M sin i = 9.3 M_jup) is due to the near face-on orbit orientation. This result
demonstrates the value of follow-up astrometric observations to determine the
true masses of exoplanet candidates detected with the radial velocity method.

In this work, we present the constraints on the very high-energy emission from short duration gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) provided by Milagro, a large-area air shower detector. GRBs are highly energetic and brief bursts of gamma-ray radiation that are believed to be produced during the collapse of a massive star or the merger of two compact objects, such as neutron stars or black holes. The observation of very high-energy emission from GRBs could help us to understand the physical mechanisms that produce these events, as well as the nature of the sources themselves.

Our research aims to limit the range of possible very high-energy emission from short-duration GRBs by analyzing data from the Milagro detector. We use a statistical technique called Bayesian analysis to estimate the probability distribution of the flux of gamma rays emitted by short duration GRBs. Our modeling includes a description of the background, the exposure, and the angular resolution of Milagro, allowing us to achieve a high level of accuracy in our results. 

The motivation for this research is to test the predictions of current theoretical models of short-duration GRBs, which suggest that they emit very high energy gamma-rays. Our work provides important constraints on these models and offers a more accurate representation of the properties of short-duration GRBs. By exploring the range of possible very high energy emission from GRBs, we advance the understanding of the underlying physics of these enigmatic astrophysical phenomena.

In this study, we investigate the scaling laws for soliton pulse compression through cascaded quadratic nonlinearities. The main objective of this work is to identify the optimum conditions for achieving maximum pulse compression with minimal perturbations. We are motivated by the potential applications of ultra-short pulses in various areas of science and technology, and the need to efficiently generate shorter pulses with higher peak powers. 

To achieve our objectives, we use analytical and numerical methods to study the dynamics of the soliton pulse in a cascaded second-order nonlinear medium. Our analysis considers the effects of dispersion, self-phase modulation, and nonlinear phase modulation, and we identify the optimum pulse duration and peak power that result in the most efficient compression. The key idea of our work is to develop a comprehensive understanding of the dynamics of soliton compression in cascaded nonlinear media. 

Our results show that under appropriate conditions of input pulse duration and power, the soliton pulse can be compressed by several orders of magnitude while maintaining its spectral and temporal characteristics. We also identify certain parameter regimes where higher- or lower-order nonlinearities become dominant, leading to non-ideal compression. Our findings contribute to the development of efficient and robust techniques for generating ultra-short pulses with high peak powers, with potential applications in optical communication, material processing, and medical imaging.

In this work, we investigate the impact of unbalanced seesaws on massive and massless neutrinos. Despite the discovery of neutrino oscillations, the nature of neutrinos remains one of the great mysteries in physics. The seesaw mechanism is a widely accepted theory that allows for the introduction of massive neutrinos. However, the presence of the massless state complicates the theory and necessitates the use of unbalanced seesaws.

Our methodology involves solving a set of coupled differential equations derived from the seesaw mechanism. These equations describe the behavior of the neutrinos and allow us to investigate the effects of different seesaw parameters on the neutrino masses and mixing angles. We also explore how the presence of massive and massless neutrinos affects the cosmological implications of the unbalanced seesaw.

Our results show that the unbalanced seesaw can successfully explain the observed neutrino masses and mixing angles. We find that the mass hierarchy of the neutrinos plays a crucial role in determining the impact of the massless neutrino on the seesaw mechanism. Additionally, including both massive and massless neutrinos in the theory leads to new possibilities for explaining the origin of dark matter.

Overall, our work provides a deeper understanding of the seesaw mechanism and opens up new avenues for exploring the nature of neutrinos and their role in the universe.

The Hubble Space Telescope (HST) has been instrumental in the discovery of
large numbers of extragalactic young massive star clusters (YMCs), often
assumed to be proto-globular clusters (GCs). As a consequence, the field of YMC
formation and evolution is thriving, generating major breakthroughs as well as
controversies on annual (or shorter) time-scales. Here, I review the long-term
survival chances of YMCs, hallmarks of intense starburst episodes often
associated with violent galaxy interactions. In the absence of significant
external perturbations, the key factor determining a cluster's long-term
survival chances is the shape of its stellar initial mass function (IMF). It
is, however, not straightforward to assess the IMF shape in unresolved
extragalactic YMCs. I also discuss the latest progress in worldwide efforts to
better understand the evolution of entire cluster populations, predominantly
based on HST observations, and conclude that there is an increasing body of
evidence that GC formation appears to be continuing until today; their
long-term evolution crucially depends on their environmental conditions,
however.

(abridged) Debris disks around main sequence stars are produced by the
erosion and evaporation of unseen parent bodies. AU Microscopii (GJ 803) is a
compelling object to study in the context of disk evolution across different
spectral types, as it is an M dwarf whose near edge-on disk may be directly
compared to that of its A5V sibling beta Pic. We resolve the disk from 8-60 AU
in the near-IR JHK' bands at high resolution with the Keck II telescope and
adaptive optics, and develop a novel data reduction technique for the removal
of the stellar point spread function. The point source detection sensitivity in
the disk midplane is more than a magnitude less sensitive than regions away
from the disk for some radii. We measure a blue color across the near-IR bands,
and confirm the presence of substructure in the inner disk. Some of the
structural features exhibit wavelength-dependent positions. The disk
architecture and characteristics of grain composition are inferred through
modeling. We approach the modeling of the dust distribution in a manner that
complements previous work. Using a Monte Carlo radiative transfer code, we
compare a relatively simple model of the distribution of porous grains to a
