consecutive factorials and we provide many examples outlining the applications
of the ensuing theory; examples dealing with prime numbers, Fibonacci numbers,
and highly composite numbers among other sets of integers. One of our results
states that given any abstract factorial the series of reciprocals of its
factorials always converges to an irrational number. Thus, for example, for any
positive integer k the series of the reciprocals of the k-th powers of the
cumulative product of the divisors of the numbers from 1 to n is irrational.

We revisit the issue of the stability in the Dvali-Gabadadze-Porrati model,
by considering the nucleation of bubbles of the conventional branch within the
self-accelerating branch. We construct an instanton describing this process in
the thin wall approximation. On one side of the bubble wall, the bulk consists
of the exterior of the brane while on the other side it is the interior. The
solution requires the presence of a 2-brane (the bubble wall) which induces the
transition. However, we show that this instanton cannot be realized as the thin
wall limit of any smooth solution. Once the bubble thickness is resolved, the
equations of motion do not allow O(4) symmetric solutions joining the two
branches. We conclude that the thin wall instanton is unphysical, and that one
cannot have processes connecting the two branches, unless negative tension
bubble walls are introduced. This also suggests that the self-accelerating
branch does not decay into the conventional branch nucleating bubbles. We
comment on other kinds of bubbles that could interpolate between the two
branches.

This is the first paper of a series of three, reporting on numerical
simulation studies of geometric and mechanical properties of static assemblies
of spherical beads under an isotropic pressure. Frictionless systems assemble
in the unique random close packing (RCP) state in the low pressure limit if the
compression process is fast enough, slower processes inducing traces of
crystallization, and exhibit specific properties directly related to
isostaticity of the force-carrying structure. The different structures of
frictional packings assembled by various methods cannot be classified by the
sole density. While lubricated systems approach RCP densities and coordination
number z^*~=6 on the backbone in the rigid limit, an idealized "vibration"
procedure results in equally dense configurations with z^*~=4.5. Near neighbor
correlations on various scales are computed and compared to available
laboratory data, although z^* values remain experimentally inaccessible. Low
coordination packings have many rattlers (more than 10% of the grains carry no
force), which should be accounted for on studying position correlations, and a
small proportion of harmless "floppy modes" associated with divalent grains.
Frictional packings, however slowly assembled under low pressure, retain a
finite level of force indeterminacy, except in the limit of infinite friction.

Our team has developed a novel method for evaluating uncertainty in dose volume histogram (DVH) computation. DVHs provide valuable information on the distribution of radiation doses within target volumes and are important for predicting treatment outcomes. The accuracy of DVHs depends on the uncertainty in the input data, such as contouring, dose calculation, and image registration. However, current methods for assessing uncertainty in DVHs are often subjective and rely on expert opinions. 

Our method focuses on the use of a statistical approach called Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) simulation, which can estimate the uncertainty in DVHs based on probability distributions and statistical models. We applied this method to a dataset of 20 prostate cancer patients and found that the uncertainty in DVHs was greater than previously estimated using traditional methods. Our results highlight the importance of accounting for uncertainty in DVHs and suggest that MCMC simulation can be a valuable tool for improving the accuracy of radiation treatment planning.

The key ideas and methodology behind our approach involve developing a statistical model of the uncertainties in the input data, using MCMC simulation to generate a large number of possible DVHs based on the model, and evaluating the resulting DVHs to estimate uncertainty. Our method represents a significant advance in the field of radiation treatment planning and has the potential to improve patient outcomes by reducing the risk of underdosing or overdosing.

Low-dimensional organic conductors could establish themselves as model
systems for the investigation of the physics in reduced dimensions. In the
metallic state of a one-dimensional solid, Fermi-liquid theory breaks down and
spin and charge degrees of freedom become separated. But the metallic phase is
not stable in one dimension: as the temperature is reduced, the electronic
charge and spin tend to arrange themselves in an ordered fashion due to strong
correlations. The competition of the different interactions is responsible for
which broken-symmetry ground state is eventually realized in a specific
compound and which drives the system towards an insulating state.
  Here we review the various ordering phenomena and how they can be identified
by optic and magnetic measurements. While the final results might look very
similar in the case of a charge density wave and a charge-ordered metal, for
instance, the physical cause is completely different. When density waves form,
a gap opens in the density of states at the Fermi energy due to nesting of the
one-dimension Fermi surface sheets. When a one-dimensional metal becomes a
charge-ordered Mott insulator, on the other hand, the short-range Coulomb
repulsion localizes the charge on the lattice sites and even causes certain
charge patterns.
  We try to point out the similarities and conceptional differences of these
phenomena and give an example for each of them. Particular emphasis will be put
on collective phenomena which are inherently present as soon as ordering breaks
the symmetry of the system.

We investigate the behavior of semiconductor superlattices in the presence of an external magnetic field that is tilted off the vertical. Our research is motivated by the desire to understand the complex dynamical behavior of superlattices as they approach breakdown under strong electric fields. Specifically, we are interested in the emergence of chaotic behavior and bifurcations in these systems. Our methodology involves numerical simulations using a semiclassical model that accounts for the effects of the applied magnetic field. We investigate the dependence of the system's behavior on the strength and tilt angle of the magnetic field, as well as the geometry of the superlattice itself. We observe the emergence of a variety of bifurcation scenarios, including period doubling, tangent bifurcations, and saddle-node bifurcations, which lead to the onset of chaotic behavior. Our results provide insight into the underlying mechanisms that drive the complex dynamics of semiconductor superlattices, and may have important implications for the design and optimization of these materials for practical applications.

We analyze the effect of gravitational radiation reaction on generic orbits
around a body with an axisymmetric mass quadrupole moment Q to linear order in
Q, to the leading post-Newtonian order, and to linear order in the mass ratio.
This system admits three constants of the motion in absence of radiation
reaction: energy, angular momentum, and a third constant analogous to the
Carter constant. We compute instantaneous and time-averaged rates of change of
these three constants. For a point particle orbiting a black hole, Ryan has
computed the leading order evolution of the orbit's Carter constant, which is
linear in the spin. Our result, when combined with an interaction quadratic in
the spin (the coupling of the black hole's spin to its own radiation reaction
field), gives the next to leading order evolution. The effect of the
quadrupole, like that of the linear spin term, is to circularize eccentric
orbits and to drive the orbital plane towards antialignment with the symmetry
axis. In addition we consider a system of two point masses where one body has a
single mass multipole or current multipole. To linear order in the mass ratio,
to linear order in the multipole, and to the leading post-Newtonian order, we
show that there does not exist an analog of the Carter constant for such a
system (except for the cases of spin and mass quadrupole). With mild additional
assumptions, this result falsifies the conjecture that all vacuum, axisymmetric
spacetimes posess a third constant of geodesic motion.

In our research, we aim to investigate the asymptotic behavior of duration-driven long-range dependent (DD-LRD) processes. These processes have been shown to generate a variety of stochastic behaviors, and are of particular interest in various applications such as finance, engineering, and telecommunications. However, the existing literature is limited in its ability to provide a comprehensive understanding of the asymptotic behavior of such processes.

To address this challenge, we develop a new theoretical framework for the analysis of DD-LRD processes. Specifically, we derive a series of deterministic and stochastic scaling limits that capture the limiting behavior of these processes. In addition, we derive a number of key statistical properties of DD-LRD processes, such as their autocorrelation functions, power spectral densities, and their scaling properties.

Our results provide a deeper understanding of the behavior of DD-LRD processes, which can be applied to a range of applications in finance and engineering. The extension of our analysis to include the effect of the duration on the behavior of the DD-LRD processes is an important contribution to the literature in stochastic analysis. The insights we provide are theoretically sound and offer important practical applications.

Thin films of Co-TiO2 are deposited on silicon and quartz substrates using
Pulse Laser Deposition (PLD) process at various oxygen partial pressures
ranging from 6.6 x 10-3 Pascals (Pa) to 53 Pa. Crystal structure, transport and
magnetic properties of reduced CoxTi(1-x)O2 (0 <x< 0.03) thin films are
investigated and are found to have a strong dependence on the oxygen partial
pressure. X-ray diffraction (XRD) data reveals the presence of mixed phase
material containing both anatase and rutile. However, these phases
intertransform with the change in the oxygen partial pressure in the chamber
during the growth of the films. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) shows no
Co or CoO related peaks for samples with Co concentration up to x=0.03.
However, the oxygen 1s peaks are asymmetric suggesting the presence of oxygen
vacancies. The transport and magnetic measurements show a clear dependence on
the concentration of oxygen vacancies. There is an enhancement in the
electrical conductivity and the magnetization as more vacancies are created in
the material. The resistivity as a function of temperature rho(T) follows the
polaronic behavior and the activation energies obtained, ~100 to 150meV, are
within the range that is typical for semiconducting materials.

In this study, we explore the use of analytic models and rapid computations to better understand steady-state space use patterns in mechanistic home range analysis. The motivation for this research is to improve our understanding of animal behavior and habitat use, which has important implications for conservation efforts and management decisions.

Our key idea is to use an analytic approach to model an animal's movement and behavior within its home range, allowing us to gain insights into the factors that influence space use patterns. By incorporating rapid computations, we are able to efficiently analyze a large quantity of data and generate results in a timely manner. 

Our methodology involves applying this approach to real-world data collected from GPS-tagged white-tailed deer in Wisconsin, USA. We analyze the data in light of several different models, including those focused on resource selection and those that incorporate environmental and behavioral factors.

Our results show that our approach is effective in identifying key drivers of space use patterns, such as vegetation and topography. We find that our method is also able to accurately predict the locations of deer and their spatial movements over time. We conclude that the combination of analytic models and rapid computations offers a promising avenue for improving our understanding of animal behavior and habitat use.

In the collinear factorization of the form factor for the transition
$\gamma^* \pi^0 \to \gamma$ the hard part contains double log terms as $\ln^2
x$ with $x$ as the momentum fraction of partons from 0 to 1. A simple
exponentiation for resummation leads to divergent results. We study the
resummation of these $\ln^2 x$ terms. We show that the $\ln^2 x$ terms come
partly from the light-cone wave function(LCWF) and partly from the form factor.
We introduce a jet factor to factorize the $\ln^2 x$ term in the form factor.
To handel the $\ln^2 x$ terms from the LCWF we introduce a nonstandard
light-cone wave function(NLCWF) with the gauge links off the light-cone
direction. An interesting relation between two wave function is found. With the
introduced NLCWF and the jet factor we can re-factorize the form factor and
obtain a new hard part which does not contain terms with $\ln^2 x$. Beside the
renormalization scale $\mu$ the introduce NLCWF and jet factor have extra
scales to characterize their $x$-behaviors. Using the evolutions of the extra
scales and the relation we can do the resummation perturbatively in sense that
the LCWF is the only nonpertubative object in the resumed formula. Our results
with some models of LCWF show that there is a significant difference between
numerical predictions with the resummation and that without the resummation,
and the resummed predictions can describe the experimental data.

In our work on network statistics on early English syntax, we sought to shed light on syntactic structures in Old English by identifying key structural criteria that could be used to construct networks of syntactic relationships. Our motivation for this research was the recognition that previous work on Old English syntax had been largely based on manual analysis of individual texts, which made it difficult to identify broader patterns and trends in the language. Furthermore, the use of network statistics allowed us to examine how different syntactic structures were interconnected and how these networks evolved over time.

To achieve these goals, we used a corpus of Old English texts spanning from the 7th to the 11th century and applied network-based clustering techniques to identify nodes and edges representing syntactic relationships. We also examined various metrics, including degree centrality and betweenness, to identify important nodes in the network. Our results showed that certain syntactic categories, such as prepositions, tended to be highly central in the network, while other categories, such as adjectives, were less central. We also found evidence of evolution in the syntactic network over time, with earlier texts being characterized by more complex, hierarchical structures than later texts.

Overall, our work contributes to a deeper understanding of the syntactic structures of Old English and provides a methodological framework that could be applied to other historical languages and linguistic phenomena.

In this study, we explore predictions from standard solar models and spin flavour precession in the context of the SNO+ experiment. The primary aim of the work is to investigate the impact of spin flavour precession on the overall neutrino flux in the SNO+ experiment. This experiment is designed to observe neutrinos from various sources, including the Sun and the Earth's atmosphere. 

The motivation for this research is to develop a comprehensive understanding of neutrino properties and behaviour in different environments, as well as to test the predictions of the Standard Model. In particular, the study seeks to explore the role of spin flavour precession in the context of solar models and its impact on the overall neutrino flux observed by SNO+. 

Our methodology involves exploring the various spin flavour precession effects that arise in standard solar models, considering the impact of these effects on the total flux of neutrinos detected by SNO+, and comparing the predictions of our models with data obtained from the experiment. 

Overall, our study provides important insights into the behaviour of neutrinos in different environments and highlights the need for further research in this area. Ultimately, the understanding gained from this work could have important implications for broader fields such as particle physics and cosmology.

In this study, we investigate the critical behavior of an evolutionary Ultimatum Game, which is a model that embodies social interactions between individuals under strategic decision-making. Our main objective is to understand and characterize the transition behavior at critical points that separate the equilibrium regimes in the game. To accomplish this goal, we employ numerical simulations and mean-field theory to analyze the system's dynamics and uncover critical exponents and scaling relations.

We find that as the relative cost of cooperation increases between the players, the system exhibits a phase transition from a regime where both players benefit from cooperation to a regime where only the proposer benefits. Interestingly, we observe critical behavior in the form of power laws and scaling relations near the critical point, which suggests that the game belongs to the universality class of the Ising model in statistical physics. Our results provide insight into the evolutionary stability of cooperative behavior in social dilemmas and highlight the importance of critical phenomena in understanding complex adaptive systems.

The motivation for our research comes from the need to understand social dynamics and decision-making processes in human societies and their implications for cooperation and conflict resolution. Our findings can have implications in diverse areas, such as economics, psychology, political science, and sociology.

In this work, we investigate the relationship between two-dimensional twisted sigma models and the mirror chiral de Rham complex, with a focus on twisted generalized mirror symmetry. Our motivation for this research stems from the desire to better understand the correspondence between two-dimensional sigma models and algebraic geometry, as well as to uncover new tools for studying mirror symmetry phenomena. 

To achieve our goals, we perform a detailed study of twisted sigma models and their associated chiral de Rham complexes, including their cohomology and relationships to other mathematical structures. In particular, we show how twisted sigma models with a certain type of symmetry correspond to specific objects in the mirror chiral de Rham complex. We then use these results to obtain a twisted version of the classical generalized mirror symmetry theorem, which relates the topology of two mirror manifolds through their de Rham cohomology structures.

Our research not only contributes to our understanding of the relationship between sigma models and algebraic geometry, but also provides a new avenue for studying mirror symmetry phenomena. In addition, our work highlights the importance of considering twisted symmetries and incorporating them into our mathematical models. Overall, we hope our findings will inspire further research in this area and shed light on new connections between seemingly disparate mathematical concepts.

We present the basic equations and sample solutions for the steady-state
global transonic solutions of the advection-dominated accretion flows (ADAFs)
with a causal viscosity prescription. The procedures for the stable numerical
calculations and all explicit formula for obtaining the solutions of ADAFs are
also presented. We solve the transonic solutions of ADAFs by using the explicit
numerical integrations, as in the Runge-Kutta method. In this calculation
method, we first solve the physical values at the sonic radius where
L'Hopital's rule is used. Then, we numerically solve the coupled differential
equations of the radial velocity, the angular momentum, and the sound speed
from the sonic radius in the inward and outward directions. By the calculation
procedures presented in this paper, we can cover all parameter spaces of the
transonic solutions of ADAFs. Sample transonic solutions for ADAF-thick disk
and ADAF-thin disk are presented. The explicit formula for the analytical
expansion around the singular points, the sonic and the viscous points, are
presented. If we set the diffusion timescale to null, the formalism in this
study becomes the formalism of the acausal viscosity that is usually used in
past studies for calculating of the ADAF structure.

Magnetism in nanographenes (also know as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, or
PAHs) are studied with first principles density functional calculations. We
find that an antiferromagnetic (AFM) phase appears as the PAH reaches a certain
size. This AFM phase in PAHs has the same origin as the one in infinitely long
zigzag-edged graphene nanoribbons, namely, from the localized electronic state
at the zigzag edge. The smallest PAH still having an AFM ground state is
identified. With increased length of the zigzag edge, PAHs approach an
infinitely long ribbon in terms of (1) the energetic ordering and difference
among the AFM, ferromagnetic (FM), and nonmagnetic (NM) phases and (2) the
average local magnetic moment at the zigzag edges. These PAHs serve as ideal
targets for chemical synthesis of nanographenes that possess magnetic
properties. Moreover, our calculations support the interpretation that
experimentally observed magnetism in activated carbon fibers originates from
the zigzag edges of the nanographenes.

In this work, we investigate the phenomenon of superradiance and stimulated scattering in SNR 1987A. Our main motivation for this study is to understand the fundamental principles behind the interaction of photons with matter in astrophysical systems. Superradiance is a collective behavior of a large ensemble of quantum emitters, resulting in the cooperative emission of radiation, while stimulated scattering is the process of photon amplification through the interaction with an ensemble of particles. We explore the manifestation of these two phenomena in the context of the supernova remnant of SN 1987A. 

By analyzing the observational data from the Atacama Large Millimeter Array (ALMA), we find that the emission of the remnant exhibits clear signatures of superradiance and stimulated scattering. The key idea behind this observation is that the dense and compact cloud of particles in the remnant provides an ideal environment for these phenomena to occur. 

Our methodology involves performing a detailed analysis of the ALMA data and simulating the emission using numerical models. We fit the observations to the simulations to extract the relevant parameters and understand the physical processes at play. Our results provide valuable insights into the fundamental mechanisms that govern the interaction of photons with matter in astrophysical systems, highlighting the importance of collective behaviors and ensemble effects.

We present a method to construct semi-numerical ``simulations'', which can
efficiently generate realizations of halo distributions and ionization maps at
high redshifts. Our procedure combines an excursion-set approach with
first-order Lagrangian perturbation theory and operates directly on the linear
density and velocity fields. As such, the achievable dynamic range with our
algorithm surpasses the current practical limit of N-body codes by orders of
magnitude. This is particularly significant in studies of reionization, where
the dynamic range is the principal limiting factor. We test our halo-finding
and HII bubble-finding algorithms independently against N-body simulations with
radiative transfer and obtain excellent agreement. We compute the size
distributions of ionized and neutral regions in our maps. We find even larger
ionized bubbles than do purely analytic models at the same volume-weighted mean
hydrogen neutral fraction. We also generate maps and power spectra of 21-cm
brightness temperature fluctuations, which for the first time include
corrections due to gas bulk velocities. We find that velocities widen the tails
of the temperature distributions and increase small-scale power, though these
effects quickly diminish as reionization progresses. We also include some
preliminary results from a simulation run with the largest dynamic range to
date: a 250 Mpc box that resolves halos with masses M >~ 2.2 x10^8 M_sun. We
show that accurately modeling the late stages of reionization requires such
large scales. The speed and dynamic range provided by our semi-numerical
approach will be extremely useful in the modeling of early structure formation
and reionization.

Linear polarization in X- and gamma-rays is an important diagnostic of many
astrophysical sources, foremost giving information about their geometry,
magnetic fields, and radiation mechanisms. However, very few X-ray polarization
measurements have been made, and then only mono-energetic detections, whilst
several objects are assumed to have energy dependent polarization signatures.
In this paper we investigate whether detection of energy dependent polarization
from cosmic sources is possible using the Compton technique, in particular with
the proposed PoGOLite balloon-experiment, in the 25-100 keV range. We use
Geant4 simulations of a PoGOLite model and input photon spectra based on Cygnus
X-1 and accreting magnetic pulsars (100 mCrab). Effective observing times of 6
and 35 hours were simulated, corresponding to a standard and a long duration
flight respectively. Both smooth and sharp energy variations of the
polarization are investigated and compared to constant polarization signals
using chi-square statistics. We can reject constant polarization, with energy,
for the Cygnus X-1 spectrum (in the hard state), if the reflected component is
assumed to be completely polarized, whereas the distinction cannot be made for
weaker polarization. For the accreting pulsar, constant polarization can be
rejected in the case of polarization in a narrow energy band with at least 50%
polarization, and similarly for a negative step distribution from 30% to 0%
polarization.

The IT Service Centre at Harz University was experiencing shortcomings in the efficiency and effectiveness of its Release Management Process. These issues necessitated the use of Information Technology Infrastructure Library (ITIL) to optimise the process. Our research investigated the use of ITIL in optimising the Release Management Process in this IT Service Centre. 

The study adopted a qualitative research approach, and data were collected through semi-structured interviews with 5 staff members and observational data. Our key findings reveal that ITIL aided the IT Service Centre in defining the structure and framework of the Release Management Process. The methodology involved collaborative efforts among the stakeholders with different roles in the process, including the IT Service Centre, users and IT support personnel. Collaboration and communication were facilitated by the implementation of ITIL practices, which ensured that all stakeholders understood their roles and responsibility clearly, and documented them accordingly. As a result, the Release Management Process achieved optimisation, leading to a reduction in errors and a decrease in its implementation time.

Our research contributes to the existing body of knowledge by highlighting the potentials of ITIL in improving the efficiency and effectiveness of the Release Management Process in IT Service Centres. We recommend that IT Service Centres adopt ITIL to optimise their operations and achieve better performance outcomes.

In this research, we present the discovery of extreme asymmetry in the debris disk surrounding HD 15115. The observation of debris disks is crucial in understanding the evolution of planetary systems, and studies have been conducted on the debris disks to investigate their properties. The debris disk around HD 15115 is interesting because its central star has a mass of about twice that of our Sun.

Our study was conducted using the Atacama Large Millimeter Array (ALMA) in Chile. We obtained high-resolution images of the debris disk, which allowed us to determine the morphology and demonstrate that it is inclined relative to the plane of the sky. Our analysis revealed that the disk is highly asymmetric, with one side being much brighter than the other. The asymmetry in the disk's brightness is most likely due to a clump of material blocking the light from one side of the disk.

This work is a contribution to the knowledge of protoplanetary disks, which are believed to be the birthplaces of planetary systems. These findings suggest that planet formation and disk asymmetries may be closely related. More observations are needed to understand the implications of this discovery fully, but this study highlights the importance of continuing observation of debris disks surrounding stars.

In this work, we present a framework to use gamma-ray observations to probe cosmic-ray pressure in galaxy clusters, shedding light on the energetics of cluster plasma. Our motivation is to understand the role of cosmic rays, energetic particles continuously accelerated by shocks and turbulence, in shaping the intracluster medium, the gas that occupies most of the volume in clusters of galaxies.

We first model the emission of gamma-rays produced by the interaction of cosmic rays with the intracluster gas, showing that it is a sensitive tracer of the cosmic-ray pressure. We then apply our modeling to state-of-the-art gamma-ray data from the Fermi Large Area Telescope, and show that the observed gamma-ray emission from several nearby clusters is consistent with the expected level from cosmic rays. This implies that cosmic rays provide a significant fraction of the pressure support in these clusters, indicating that they are an important ingredient of the cluster plasma.

Our work has important implications for cosmology, as it helps constrain the energy budget of galaxy clusters, one of the largest gravitationally bound structures in the universe and a key element for testing cosmological models. Additionally, our methodology can be applied to future gamma-ray surveys of galaxy clusters, such as those performed by the upcoming Cherenkov Telescope Array, providing a powerful tool to probe cosmic rays and their impact on the evolution of galaxy clusters.

In this research, we examine the microscopic origin of non-Gaussian distributions of financial returns. The key idea is that the aggregated financial data of daily prices conceal important microscopic details that can be uncovered by analyzing shorter intervals. We use high-frequency data to study the distribution of returns and study the role of microstructures in generating non-Gaussian behavior. 

The methodology we employ is to analyze high-frequency financial data of the S&P 500 index from January 2000 to December 2019. We look at a variety of measures, such as volatility, skewness, and kurtosis, to characterize the distribution of returns. We employ detrending techniques and statistical characterization with the aim of identifying the origins of the observed distribution. We also use simulations to validate our findings.

The problem we attempt to solve is to understand the microscopic origin of the non-Gaussian distribution of returns in financial markets. The motivation for this research is that financial models often rely on the assumption of Gaussian behavior, which fails to capture the complex dynamics of the financial markets. This research can contribute to our understanding of the market dynamics and help build better financial models.

In summary, our research provides a new perspective on the origins of the non-Gaussian distribution of financial returns. By examining high-frequency data, we identify microstructures that generate the observed patterns. This has implications for financial models that can be better informed by our results and can improve their predictive power.

We investigate the properties and evolution of accretion tori formed after
the coalescence of two compact objects. At these extreme densities and
temperatures, the accreting torus is cooled mainly by neutrino emission
produced primarily by electron and positron capture on nucleons (beta
reactions). We solve for the disc structure and its time evolution by
introducing a detailed treatment of the equation of state which includes
photodisintegration of helium, the condition of beta-equilibrium, and neutrino
opacities. We self-consistently calculate the chemical equilibrium in the gas
consisting of helium, free protons, neutrons and electron-positron pairs and
compute the chemical potentials of the species, as well as the electron
fraction throughout the disc. We find that, for sufficiently large accretion
rates (> 10 solar masses per second), the inner regions of the disk become
opaque and develop a viscous and thermal instability. The identification of
this instability might be relevant for GRB observations.

In this research paper, we explore the bibliometric statistical properties of the 100 largest European universities in order to identify prevalent scaling rules within the science system. By analyzing a large data set of academic publications and citations, we have generated novel insights into the correlation between university size and research productivity, examining factors such as disciplinary focus, geographic location, and institutional funding. Our methodology involves the use of advanced bibliometric techniques including network analysis, citation statistics, and clustering algorithms. The problem this work attempts to solve is the lack of detailed understanding of the complex relationships between university size and scientific research performance. This topic has become increasingly important due to the ever-growing pressure on research funding agencies to identify the most effective recipients of limited resources. Our results show that larger universities tend to have higher research output, however, this relationship does depend on the specific disciplinary focus of the institution. Furthermore, our analysis reveals the existence of a set of general scaling rules for research productivity across a diverse range of academic fields. This work offers valuable scientific insights for understanding the relative performance of large universities across Europe, and may have broader implications for science policy and research funding decisions.

We have carried out neutron powder-diffraction measurements on zinc
paratacamite Zn$_x$Cu$_{4-x}$(OH)$_6$Cl$_2$ with $x=1$, and studied the heat
capacity in fields of up to 9 T for $0.5 \leq x \leq 1$. The $x=1$ phase has
recently been shown to be an outstanding realisation of the $S=1/2$ kagom\'{e}
antiferromagnet. A weak mixing of Cu$^{2+}$/Zn$^{2+}$ between the Cu and the Zn
sites, corresponding to $\sim 9$% of all Cu$^{2+}$ for $x=1$, is observed using
neutron diffraction. This ``antisite disorder'' provides a consistent
explanation of the field dependence of the heat capacity for $0.8 \leq x \leq
1$. From comparison of the derived Cu$^{2+}$ occupancy of the Zn sites for $x =
0.8... 1$ with the magnetic susceptibility, we argue that for $x = 0.8... 1$
zinc paratacamite is a spin liquid without a spin gap. The presence of unpaired
but nevertheless strongly interacting spins gives rise to a macroscopically
degenerate ground state manifold, with increasingly glassy dynamics as $x$ is
lowered.

In this work, we investigate the effect of motor driven microtubule shape fluctuations on the internal forces within the lattice. Our study is motivated by the fact that microtubules play a crucial role in cellular processes such as cell division, intracellular transport, and cell motility. Previous studies have focused on the external forces acting on microtubules, such as those generated by motor proteins, but far less is known about the forces generated internally within the microtubule itself. 

We use computer simulations to investigate how fluctuations in microtubule shape, induced by the action of motor proteins, affect the internal forces within the microtubule lattice. Our results show that these fluctuations can lead to the generation of significant internal forces, which in turn can affect the dynamic behavior of microtubules. Specifically, we find that these internal forces can influence the stability of the microtubule structure, and can also lead to structural rearrangements within the lattice.

Our study sheds new light on the interplay between motor-driven shape fluctuations and internal forces within microtubules, and highlights the importance of considering these internal forces in future studies of microtubule dynamics. Ultimately, a better understanding of the forces acting within microtubules may help to elucidate the mechanisms underlying their vital biological functions, and may inform the development of new therapeutic approaches for diseases associated with microtubule dysfunction.

The existence of a diffuse near-infrared background (NIRB) has been known since the 1990s but its origin remains uncertain. This prompted us to investigate the contribution of normal galaxies and high-redshift sources to the source-subtracted NIRB. We used the ultra-deep K-band observations from the UKIDSS Ultra-Deep Survey to estimate the intensity of the NIRB up to 10 times fainter than previous studies. We utilized random forests, a machine learning algorithm to separate galaxies from spurious detections. After subtracting the sources detected, we found that the contribution to the residual NIRB is still significant. We performed simulations of the diffuse Halo occupation distribution (HOD) model to investigate the origin of the unresolved sources. We found that the residual NIRB can be attributed to two populations of galaxies: moderate-redshift galaxies with a median redshift of z ~ 0.5 and low luminosities, and to a lesser extent, galaxies with a higher median redshift of z ~ 2-3 but with lower number densities. These results suggest that a significant fraction of the NIRB arises from sources with redshifts of less than 8. This study provides a valuable insight into the nature of the source-subtracted NIRB and will aid in constraining the physical processes driving the formation and evolution of galaxies.

The effect of fractal normal-phase clusters on vortex dynamics in a
percolative superconductor is considered. The superconductor contains
percolative superconducting cluster carrying a transport current and clusters
of a normal phase, acting as pinning centers. A prototype of such a structure
is YBCO film, containing clusters of columnar defects, as well as the BSCCO/Ag
sheathed tape, which is of practical interest for wire fabrication. Transition
of the superconductor into a resistive state corresponds to the percolation
transition from a pinned vortex state to a resistive state when the vortices
are free to move. The dependencies of the free vortex density on the fractal
dimension of the cluster boundary as well as the resistance on the transport
current are obtained. It is revealed that a mixed state of the vortex glass
type is realized in the superconducting system involved. The current-voltage
characteristics of superconductors containing fractal clusters are obtained and
their features are studied.

In our study, we aimed to investigate the role of calcium in the synaptic dynamics underlying reverberatory activity in neuronal networks. The reverberatory activity is defined as the persistence of neuronal activity in the absence of ongoing inputs and has been proposed to underlie short-term memory and pattern recognition. The problem that we attempt to solve is to understand the mechanisms underlying this phenomenon and how it can be modulated to enhance cognitive function. 
Using electrophysiological recordings and pharmacological manipulations, we found that calcium influx through NMDA receptors is necessary for the maintenance of reverberatory activity in the hippocampal network. Additionally, we identified a specific calcium-sensitive potassium channel, BK, which plays a crucial role in regulating the duration and strength of the reverberatory activity. By modulating the activity of this channel, we were able to selectively enhance or decrease the reverberatory activity in the network. 
Our results provide insight into the mechanisms underlying the regulation of reverberatory activity and suggest potential targets for therapeutic interventions in cognitive disorders. By understanding the role of calcium and potassium channels in controlling synaptic dynamics in neuronal networks, we may develop novel approaches for enhancing cognitive function in aging and neurodegenerative disorders.

The effect of Ge substitution for Si in SmMMn2Si2-xGex compounds has been
studied. The Sm ordering temperature is found to be much larger in the compound
with x=2, as compared to the compounds with x=0 and 1. The increase in the
intra layer Mn-Mn distance is found to be responsible for this increase. Among
these three compounds, SmMn2Ge2 is found to show re-entrant ferromagnetism at
low temperatures. The magnetic contribution to the heat capacity has been found
in all the three compounds. The splitting of the ground state multiplet has
been estimated by fitting the magnetic part of the heat capacity data using the
Schottky formula. The isothermal magnetic entropy change is found to remain the
same for x=0 and 1, but decrease in the compound with x=2, though the nature of
magnetic transition changes from second order to first order, as x is increased
from 0 to 2. The electrical resistivity increases with Ge concentration. The
excess resistivity in the antiferromagnetic region has been calculated.

In this study, we investigate the concept of complementarity in the Bohr-Einstein photon box. Our work aims to explore the fundamental limits of our ability to predict the behavior of quantum systems. In particular, we focus on the complementary nature of wave-like and particle-like behavior in light. This work is motivated by the fact that understanding complementarity is key to developing a better understanding of the fundamental nature of quantum mechanics. 

The results of our study show that the Bohr-Einstein photon box can provide important insights into complementarity. We find that there are certain conditions under which the wave and particle-like behavior of photons are mutually exclusive. However, when certain constraints are relaxed, both wave and particle-like behaviors can be observed simultaneously. 

In terms of methodology, we utilize a theoretical framework that describes the behavior of light in the presence of boundary conditions. Our approach involves using mathematical models to describe the behavior of photons and the constraints that act on them in the Bohr-Einstein photon box. 

Overall, our findings contribute to a deeper understanding of the nature of complementarity in quantum mechanics, and provide important insights into the behavior of photons in constrained environments. The findings of this study have implications for the development of quantum technologies, which rely on a nuanced understanding of the behavior of quantum systems.

In a previous paper, new sets of parameters to replace the Michel parameters
were proposed to analyze data for the muon decay $\mu^{+} \to
e^{+}\nu_{e}\bar{\nu_{\mu}}$. Both $(V-A)$ and $(V+A)$ charged currents with
finite neutrino mass have been used to study this decay. In the present paper,
this parameterization is extended to a more general form, and a method for data
analysis (least squares) is discussed for the propose of determining the rate
of contribution from the $(V+A)$ current. We find that there is a simple form
in which the set of parameters is related primitively to the physical
quantities. It is shown that the Michel parameters are one of the other sets
that are obtained from this simple form by rearranging one term. We derive the
condition to obtain the same information regarding unknown physical quantities
in the case that the data are analyzed using these simple and rearranged forms
separately. We find that there is some possibility to get different results
from these analyses, because the equivalent condition is very delicate and the
QED radiative corrections should be treated carefully. We propose a consistent
formula for data analysis. It is useful to compare the value obtained in the
least squares fit using the simple form with that obtained using the prediction
of the standard model, because a large difference is not expected, especially
in the case of the Majorana neutrino. Finally, we point out that the method we
proposed to determine the type of neutrino in the previous paper is incorrect.

Graphitic nanostructures, e.g. carbon nanotubes (CNT) and graphene, have been
proposed as ideal materials for spin conduction[1-7]; they have long electronic
mean free paths[8] and small spin-orbit coupling[9], hence are expected to have
very long spin-scattering times. In addition, spin injection and detection in
graphene opens new opportunities to study exotic electronic states such as the
quantum Hall[10,11] and quantum spin Hall[9] states, and spin-polarized edge
states[12] in graphene ribbons. Here we perform the first non-local four-probe
experiments[13] on graphene contacted by ferromagnetic Permalloy electrodes. We
observe sharp switching and often sign-reversal of the non-local resistance at
the coercive field of the electrodes, indicating definitively the presence of a
spin current between injector and detector. The non-local resistance changes
magnitude and sign quasi-periodically with back-gate voltage, and
Fabry-Perot-like oscillations[6,14,15] are observed, consistent with
quantum-coherent transport. The non-local resistance signal can be observed up
to at least T = 300 K.

In this work, we aim to develop a comprehensive library of synthetic galaxy spectra for unresolved galaxies to facilitate the classification and parametrization of these objects in the context of the Gaia mission. To achieve this, we employ a hybrid approach that combines semi-analytical models with cosmological hydrodynamic simulations. Our methodology involves generating a set of mock spectra using the Flexible Stellar Population Synthesis code, which incorporates a range of physical processes such as star formation, metallicity, and dust attenuation. We then use the mock spectra to train a supervised machine learning algorithm, specifically a Gaussian Naïve Bayes classifier, to classify real unresolved galaxies observed by Gaia. Our preliminary results show promising accuracy levels, with a classification accuracy of up to 91% for certain galaxy types. Furthermore, we use the classified galaxies to estimate their physical properties, such as age and metallicity, using a Bayesian parametrization method. Our synthetic galaxy spectra library will provide an invaluable resource for the analysis of unresolved galaxies using data from Gaia and other astronomical surveys, allowing for a better understanding of the evolution and formation of galaxies.

We present a study on the quasi-periodic oscillations (QPOs) observed in compact objects such as black holes and neutron stars. Our motivation stems from the fact that QPOs often have a characteristic frequency that is related to the mass and spin of the compact object. Additionally, QPOs are observed to be modulated by the charge of the compact object, indicating a potential connection between QPOs and the electromagnetic properties of the object. 

To investigate this relationship further, we utilize the gravitomagnetic theory, which describes the influence of rotation on the curvature of spacetime. We show that the mass and spin of the compact object, as well as its charge, induce a gravitomagnetic field that can interact with the QPOs. Our work attempts to explain the origin of the QPOs, as well as provide new insights into the effects of charge on their behavior.

Our results indicate that the presence of charge can significantly affect QPOs in a variety of settings. We show that the impact of charge on QPOs is dependent on the frequency of the oscillations and the strength of the gravitomagnetic field, which in turn is influenced by the mass and spin of the compact object. Overall, our work presents a new framework for studying the behavior of QPOs and highlights the importance of considering the electromagnetic properties of compact objects in their interpretation.

We present a multi-wavelength study of z = 3.15 Lyman-alpha emitters (LAEs) in the GOODS South Field. The main objective of our work is to understand the physical properties of these galaxies and the mechanism driving their strong Lyman-alpha emission. In particular, we aim to investigate the role of star formation activity, dust attenuation, and feedback processes in shaping the Lyman-alpha line profile.

Our motivation stems from the fact that LAEs are considered to be excellent tracers of the early Universe due to their strong Lyman-alpha emission. However, a detailed understanding of their properties and the physical conditions that give rise to their emission is still lacking. With the help of multi-wavelength data from HST, Spitzer, and ground-based telescopes, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of the spectral energy distributions, rest-frame UV and optical properties, and Lyman-alpha line shapes for a sample of 44 LAEs.

Our results indicate that the majority of LAEs show evidence for ongoing star formation activity and moderate levels of dust attenuation. However, we find a significant fraction of LAEs with a relatively weak Lyman-alpha emission, which is likely the result of resonant scattering, and/or outflows.

In summary, our multi-wavelength study provides new insights into the physical properties of z = 3.15 LAEs and highlights the importance of considering the interplay between different physical processes in shaping their observed properties.

We report precision Doppler measurements of three intermediate-mass subgiants
from Lick and Keck Observatories. All three stars show variability in their
radial velocities consistent with planet-mass companions in Keplerian orbits.
We find a planet with a minimum mass of 2.5 Mjup in a 351.5 day orbit around HD
192699, a planet with a minimum mass of 2.0 Mjup in a 341.1 day orbit around HD
210702, and a planet with a minimum mass of 0.61 Mjup in a 297.3 day orbit
around HD 175541. Stellar mass estimates from evolutionary models indicate that
all of these stars were formerly A-type dwarfs with masses ranging from 1.65 to
1.85 Msun. These three long-period planets would not have been detectable
during their stars' main-sequence phases due to the large rotational velocities
and stellar jitter exhibited by early-type dwarfs. There are now 9 "retired"
(evolved) A-type stars (Mstar > 1.6 Msun) with known planets. All 9 planets
orbit at distances a \geq 0.78 AU, which is significantly different than the
semimajor axis distribution of planets around lower-mass stars. We examine the
possibility that the observed lack of close-in planets is due to engulfment by
their expanding host stars, but we find that this explanation is inadequate
given the relatively small stellar radii of K giants (Rstar < 32 Rsun = 0.15
AU) and subgiants (Rstar < 7 Rsun = 0.03 AU). Instead, we conclude that planets
around intermediate-mass stars reside preferentially beyond ~0.8 AU, which may
be a reflection of different formation and migration histories of planets
around A-type stars.

In this work, we investigated the dynamical status of the galaxy cluster Abell 115. Our study aims to provide insights into the mass distribution and gravitational interactions among the galaxies in the cluster. We used optical spectroscopy data obtained from the Arizona Cluster Redshift Survey and applied the virial theorem to estimate the cluster's mass and velocity dispersion. We also analyzed the spatial distribution of the cluster members and their relative radial velocities. Our findings suggest that Abell 115 is a dynamically old cluster in a post-collapse phase, with a high concentration of dark matter at its core. The excess in the central velocity dispersion is consistent with the presence of substructures, possibly merging groups, within the cluster. The cluster's velocity dispersion profile appears to be nearly isothermal, indicating that the enclosed mass distribution is close to spherically symmetric. Our results provide evidence of the complex dynamics and rich internal structure of Abell 115. This research advances our knowledge of galaxy clusters' physical properties and their evolutionary history, which can contribute to understanding the large-scale structure of the Universe.

We develop a general theory of microtubule (MT) deformations by molecular
motors generating internal force doublets within the MT lattice. We describe
two basic internal excitations, the S and V shape, and compare them with
experimental observations from literature. We explain the special role of
tubulin vacancies and the dramatic deformation amplifying effect observed for
katanin acting at positions of defects. Experimentally observed shapes are used
to determine the ratio of MT shear and stretch moduli ($\approx
6\times10^{-5}$) and to estimate the forces induced in the MT lattice by
katanin (10's of pN). For many motors acting on a single MT we derive
expressions for the end-to-end distance reduction and provide criteria for
dominance of this new effect over thermal fluctuations. We conclude that
molecular motors if acting cooperatively can ''animate'' MTs from within the
lattice and induce slack even without cross-bridging to other structures, a
scenario very much reminiscent of the motor driven axoneme.

We address the presence of non-distillable (bound) entanglement in natural
many-body systems. In particular, we consider standard harmonic and spin-1/2
chains, at thermal equilibrium and characterized by few interaction parameters.
The existence of bound entanglement is addressed by calculating explicitly the
negativity of entanglement for different partitions. This allows to individuate
a range of temperatures for which no entanglement can be distilled by means of
local operations, despite the system being globally entangled. We discuss how
the appearance of bound entanglement can be linked to entanglement-area laws,
typical of these systems. Various types of interactions and topologies are
explored, showing that the presence of bound entanglement is an intrinsic
feature of these systems. In the harmonic case, we analytically prove that
thermal bound entanglement persists for systems composed by an arbitrary number
of particles. Our results strongly suggest the existence of bound entangled
states in the macroscopic limit also for spin-1/2 systems.

In this study, we introduce a novel, very massive modular Liquid Argon Imaging Chamber (LArTPC) designed to detect low energy off-axis neutrinos from the CNGS beam, known as Project MODULAr. The motivation for this research is to investigate the properties of neutrinos and their oscillations, which can provide insights into the fundamental nature of matter and the Universe. Additionally, detecting low energy neutrinos from off-axis provides an opportunity to measure the neutrino-nucleus cross-section and further understand the neutrino interaction mechanisms. 

The key idea of Project MODULAr is to break down the LArTPC into smaller modular units for easy assembly and maintenance. The modules have a size of 3$\times$6$\times$6 m$^3$, and they are constructed with a steel frame and filled with liquid argon. Each module is equipped with an array of photomultiplier tubes (PMTs) to detect scintillation light from passing neutrinos. The data obtained from the PMTs is then analyzed to identify neutrino signals. 

Preliminary results from the first 25-tonne module of Project MODULAr demonstrate the capability of the detector to distinguish signal from background events. The efficiency in selecting true neutrino interactions is high, and the background events are significantly lower than the expected rate. The modular design is also favorable for easy maintenance and scalability, with the potential to construct a LArTPC that can obtain precise measurements of neutrino properties with higher statistical significance. 

In conclusion, Project MODULAr presents a promising prospect for future neutrino hunting experiments with the potential to make breakthroughs in understanding the fundamental nature of the Universe.

In this study, we investigate the formation of galactic disc warps due to intergalactic accretion flows onto the disc. Our methodology involves running high-resolution simulations of isolated discs and comparing them to simulations including an accretion flow. We use a gas-dynamical code and consider different parameters, such as the strength of the gravity of the accreted material and the angle of incidence on the galactic disc.

Our main objective is to understand the origin and evolution of warps in galactic discs. These warps have been observed in several nearby galaxies and are believed to be caused by interactions with nearby galaxies or intergalactic gas streams. However, the role of intergalactic accretion flows in causing disc warps has not been fully explored.

Our results show that intergalactic accretion flows can indeed induce disc warps, with the angle of incidence and the strength of the gravity of the accreted material being the key factors in determining the severity and longevity of the warp. Our findings suggest that accretion flows should be taken into account when studying the evolution of galactic discs and may explain some of the observed warps that cannot be explained by interactions with nearby galaxies. Overall, our research sheds light on the complex interplay between galaxies and their surroundings and provides a new perspective on the formation of galactic disc warps.

In this work, we investigate the wetting transition phenomenon on a one-dimensional disorder. The wetting transition has been a subject of interest for physicists for several decades, as it plays a role in various fields such as fluid mechanics and materials science. In the present study, we simulate a one-dimensional disordered substrate using Monte Carlo simulations and observe the wetting transition of a fluid droplet on it. Our methodology involves studying the adsorption-desorption behavior of a fluid droplet on the substrate as a function of temperature and chemical potential. 

Our results show that the wetting transition is strongly influenced by the one-dimensional disorder, and that the transition temperature shifts towards higher values as the disorder strength increases. Additionally, we observe that the wetting and dewetting behavior of the droplet is strongly dependent on the substrate disorder. Our findings shed light on how the wetting transition is affected in disordered systems and provide insight into how disordered substrates can be used to manipulate the behavior of fluids at interfaces. 

This study aims to contribute to the ongoing effort to understand the wetting transition phenomenon and its role in various fields. Through our simulations, we attempt to solve the problem of lack of understanding regarding the effect of one-dimensional disorder on the wetting transition. Our work will be useful for researchers working on fluid dynamics, materials science, and related fields to formulate new theories and design novel applications based on the behavior of fluids at solid-liquid interfaces.

We present an integral field spectroscopic study of the extended emission-line region (EELR) of the high redshift quasar 4C 37.43. Motivated by the need to gain a deeper understanding of the environment surrounding quasars at earlier cosmic times, we use observations obtained with the Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) instrument mounted on the Very Large Telescope (VLT). Our key idea is to study the EELR, a region of ionized gas located far beyond the host galaxy, through a spatially resolved analysis of its kinematic properties, line ratios and ionization mechanisms. Our methodology consists of a combination of techniques such as spectral extraction, flux calibration, continuum subtraction, and emission-line fitting, which together allow us to obtain a detailed spatially resolved picture of the EELR. Our results indicate the presence of complex kinematic patterns, including the detection of outflowing gas in close proximity to the quasar, as well as strong evidence for an ionization gradient within the EELR. Our findings suggest a possible connection between the quasar and the EELR, indicating that the EELR may be ionized by the quasar itself or by a collimated outflowing wind from the central engine.

First principles calculations were performed to study the ground state
electronic properties of BaFeO3 (BFO) within the density functional theory
(DFT). Adopting generalized gradient approximation (GGA) exchange and
correlation functional and Vosko-Wilk-Nusair correlation energy functional
interpolation, we have systematically conducted the band structure, density of
states and electronic distribution along different crystalline planes.
Calculating results show that band gap in the majority spin band structure and
band gap in the minority spin band structure were found to be 2.7012 eV and
0.6867 eV respectively. Up-spin Fe t2g were fully occupied and down-spin Fe eg
were empty. Moreover, the up-spin Fe eg and down-spin Fe t2g were partially
occupied near the Fermi energy, leading to a finite density of states. The
Fe4+-O-Fe4+ plane superexchange coupling should rearrange the magnetic order to
make the ferromagnetic characteristic being possible, moreover the tetragonal
displacement along the c axis could induce the perovskites materials to acquire
ferroelectric property. These reasons could lead to the fact that the
tetragonal phase BFO could be a potential multiferroics while it was produced
under the very experimental conditions. The charge density along different
crystalline planes were illustrated to show that strong covalent bonding
between O and Fe can be used to investigate the exchange coupling, and this
strong hybridization may further increase the superexchange coupling to enhance
the magnetic ordering.

We present a new technique for time-domain numerical evolution of the scalar
field generated by a pointlike scalar charge orbiting a black hole. Time-domain
evolution offers an efficient way for calculating black hole perturbations,
especially as input for computations of the local self force acting on orbiting
particles. In Kerr geometry, the field equations are not fully separable in the
time domain, and one has to tackle them in 2+1 dimensions (two spatial
dimensions and time; the azimuthal dependence is still separable). A technical
difficulty arises when the source of the field is a pointlike particle, as the
2+1-dimensional perturbation is then singular: Each of the azimuthal modes
diverges logarithmically at the particle. To deal with this problem we split
the numerical domain into two regions: Inside a thin worldtube surrounding the
particle's worldline we solve for a regularized variable, obtained from the
full field by subtracting out a suitable ``puncture'' function, given
analytically. Outside this worldtube we solve for the full, original field. The
value of the evolution variable is adjusted across the boundary of the
worldtube. In this work we demonstrate the applicability of this method in the
example of circular orbits around a Schwarzschild black hole (refraining from
exploiting the spherical symmetry of the background, and working in 2+1
dimensions).

In this study, we investigate the damage propagation at the interface localization-delocalization transition of the confined Ising model. The Ising model is a well-known statistical model to describe the behavior of magnetic materials. The localization-delocalization transition is an interesting phenomenon that occurs when the system goes from being in a disordered state to a ordered one. 

Our methodology involves using Monte Carlo simulations to simulate the system and analyze it. We introduce a new method to measure the damage propagation using the Green's function. Our results indicate that the damage propagates differently depending on the temperature range where the transition occurs. Furthermore, we observe that the damage does not propagate when the transition occurs at a temperature range where the system is in a ordered state.

The problem that our work attempts to solve is to understand the dynamics of damage propagation at the transition between different phases of the Ising model. This can help provide insights into the mechanisms governing the localization-delocalization transition and guide the design of future materials with potential applications in spintronics and other fields.

The motivation for this research is to gain a deeper understanding of the behavior of magnetic materials and explore new ways to control their properties. This study has the potential to contribute to the development of new technologies that rely on spin dynamics.

In this work, we present an advanced method for determining the equation of state of atomic systems beyond s-wave in the limit of large scattering lengths. The motivation for this research stems from the fundamental question of understanding the nature of strongly interacting atomic gases. The low energy scattering properties of such systems are determined by universal physics that is independent of the details of the atomic interactions. This universality is described by a set of integral equations called the renormalization group (RG) equations. However, solving the RG equations is computationally demanding and often requires sophisticated numerical techniques. 

We introduce a lowest order constrained variational (LOCV) method that provides an efficient and accurate alternative to solving the RG equations. Our method involves solving a simplified version of the RG equations subject to a set of constraints. By varying the constraints, we can obtain the equation of state as well as other properties of the system. We demonstrate the effectiveness of this method by computing the equation of state for several model systems and comparing our results with those obtained using other techniques. 

Overall, our work addresses a significant problem in the field of atomic physics and provides a powerful tool for understanding the behavior of strongly interacting atomic gases. The LOC-V method presented here is a rigorous and practical approach for the study of atomic systems beyond s-wave and will enable new insights into the universal physics of these fascinating systems.

For the first time we apply the methodologies of nonlinear analysis to
investigate atomic matter. We use these methods in the analysis of Atomic
Weights and of Mass Number of atomic nuclei. Using the AutoCorrelation Function
and Mutual Information we establish the presence of nonlinear effects in the
mechanism of increasing mass of atomic nuclei considered as a function of the
atomic number. We find that increasing mass is divergent, possibly chaotic. We
also investigate the possible existence of a Power Law for atomic nuclei and,
using also the technique of the variogram, we conclude that a fractal regime
could superintend to the mechanism of increasing mass for nuclei. Finally,
using the Hurst exponent, evidence is obtained that the mechanism of increasing
mass in atomic nuclei is in the fractional Brownian regime. The most
interesting results are obtained by using Recurrence Quantification Analysis
(RQA). New recurrences, psudoperiodicities, self-resemblance and class of
self-similarities are identified with values of determinism showing oscillating
values indicating the presence of more or less stability during the process of
increasing mass of atomic nuclei. In brief, new regimes of regularities are
identified for atomic nuclei that deserve to be studied by future researches.
In particular an accurate analysis of binding energy values by nonlinear
methods is further required.

We presented the result of a high resolution (FWHM~0.5'') 12 ks Chandra HRC-I
observation of the starburst galaxy IC342 taken on 2 April 2006. We identified
23 X-ray sources within the central 30' x 30' region of IC342. Our HRC-I
observation resolved the historical Ultraluminous X-ray sources (ULX), X3, near
the nucleus into 2 sources, namely C12 and C13, for the first time. The
brighter source C12, with L(0.08-10keV)=(6.66\pm0.45)\times10^{38}ergs^-1, was
spatially extended (~82 pc x 127 pc). From the astrometric registration of the
X-ray image, C12 was at R.A.=03h:46m:48.43s, decl.=+68d05m47.45s, and was
closer to the nucleus than C13. Thus we concluded that source was not an ULX
and must instead be associated with the nucleus. The fainter source C13, with
L(0.08-10keV)=(5.1\pm1.4) x 10^{37}ergs^-1 was consistent with a point source
and located $6.51'' at P.A. 240 degree of C12.
  We also analyzed astrometrically corrected optical Hubble Space Telescope and
radio Very Large Array images, a comparison with the X-ray image showed
similarities in their morphologies. Regions of star formation within the
central region of IC342 were clearly visible in HST H alpha image and this was
the region where 3 optical star clusters and correspondingly our detected X-ray
source C12 were observed. We found that a predicted X-ray emission from
starburst was very close to the observed X-ray luminosity of C12, suggesting
that nuclear X-ray emission in IC342 was dominated by starburst. Furthermore,
we discussed the possibility of AGN in the nucleus of IC342. Although our data
was not enough to give a firm existence of an AGN, it could not be discarded.

In this study, we investigate the dynamics of horizon-penetrating transonic accretion disks around rotating black holes. We aim to understand the physical mechanisms controlling the structure and evolution of these disks and their role in the production of high-energy radiation. Our work is motivated by the fact that accretion disks around black holes are an essential component of a wide range of astronomical objects, including active galactic nuclei and X-ray binaries. These systems produce some of the most energetic phenomena in the universe and provide unique insights into the physics of strong gravity and plasma astrophysics.

We employ numerical simulations to study the hydrodynamic flow and radiative properties of accretion disks around rotating black holes. Our findings reveal that the inner parts of the disk exhibit strong shocks, resulting in significant dissipation and energy release. We also demonstrate that the presence of strong magnetic fields can modify the dynamics of the disk and enhance its ability to produce high-energy radiation. Moreover, we show that the observed spectra of accreting black holes can provide valuable information about the properties of the disk and the black hole.

Our work provides critical insights into the physics of accretion disks and their role in the production of high-energy radiation in astrophysical systems. The results of our study can inform future observations and theoretical models of black hole accretion disks in a number of astrophysical contexts.

In this work, we present the results of our multicolor observations of the afterglow of the short/hard GRB 050724. Our motivation for this research is to better understand the physical mechanisms responsible for the prompt emission and afterglow of short/hard gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) and their associated host environments. Short/hard GRBs are brief, intense bursts of gamma-ray radiation with durations of less than two seconds. Unlike long-duration GRBs, they are not associated with supernova explosions and are believed to be produced by mergers of compact objects such as neutron stars or black holes. 

Our observations were carried out using the 2.2-m telescope at Calar Alto Observatory in Spain and the 8.2-m Subaru Telescope in Hawaii. We obtained multicolor photometric data over several epochs spanning a few hours to several days following the GRB. We used these data to construct the spectral energy distribution (SED) of the afterglow, which allowed us to derive its physical properties such as the electron energy distribution and the density of the surrounding medium. 

Our analysis showed that the afterglow of GRB 050724 had a steep spectral slope, indicating that the electron energy distribution was particularly steep. This is in contrast to what is typically observed for long-duration GRBs. Our observations also revealed a possible break in the afterglow light curve, which could be due to either a jet break or the injection of fresh energy into the blast wave. Our study provides valuable insights into the physics of short/hard GRBs and their associated host environments.

We explore an accretion model for low luminosity AGN (LLAGN) that attributes
the low radiative output to a low mass accretion rate rather than a low
radiative efficiency. In this model, electrons are assumed to drain energy from
the ions as a result of collisionless plasma microinstabilities. Consequently,
the accreting gas collapses to form a geometrically thin disk at small radii
and is able to cool before reaching the black hole. The accretion disk is not a
standard disk, however, because the radial disk structure is modified by a
magnetic torque which drives a jet and which is primarily responsible for
angular momentum transport. We also include relativistic effects. We apply this
model to the well known LLAGN M87 and calculate the combined disk-jet
steady-state broadband spectrum. A comparison between predicted and observed
spectra indicates that M87 may be a maximally spinning black hole accreting at
a rate of 10^{-3} solar masses per year. This is about 6 orders of magnitude
below the Eddington rate for the same radiative efficiency. Furthermore, the
total jet power inferred by our model is in remarkably good agreement with the
value independently deduced from observations of the M87 jet on kiloparsec
scales.

We report the detection of two planetary mass companions to the solar-type
star HD 155358. The two planets have orbital periods of 195.0 and 530.3 days,
with eccentricities of 0.11 and 0.18. The minimum masses for these planets are
0.89 and 0.50 Jupiter masses respectively. The orbits are close enough to each
other, and the planets are sufficiently massive, that the planets are
gravitationally interacting with each other, with their eccentricities and
arguments of periastron varying with periods of 2300--2700 years. While large
uncertainties remain in the orbital eccentricities, our orbital integration
calculations indicate that our derived orbits would be dynamically stable for
at least 10^8 years. With a metallicity [Fe/H] of -0.68, HD 155358 is tied with
the K1III giant planet host star HD 47536 for the lowest metallicity of any
planet host star yet found. Thus, a star with only 21% of the heavy-element
content of our Sun was still able to form a system of at least two Jovian-mass
planets and have their orbits evolve to semi-major axes of 0.6-1.2 AU.

In this study, we investigated the Critical Casimir Effect (CCE) in superfluid wetting films. We aimed to understand how the confinement of superfluids influences the CCE, which is an important phenomenon that arises near critical points in fluids. To achieve this, we used Monte Carlo simulations to analyse the behaviour of superfluid films near a wetting transition point. Our results show that the critical behaviour of the CCE strongly depends on the thickness of the film and the properties of the wall confining the fluid. We found that thicker films exhibit a stronger influence of the wall confinement on the CCE, and that this confinement effect dominates over the effects of long-range forces in the vicinity of the critical point. Our findings highlight the complexity of the interplay between confinement and critical phenomena in superfluid films, and provide insight into the fundamental mechanisms underlying the CCE. This research contributes to a broader understanding of critical phenomena in confined systems, and could have important applications in the design of novel materials and devices.

In this study, we investigate evidence for a new planetary companion around the young star AU Mic. The problem that we attempt to solve is the detection of more planetary systems around nearby stars, which will help us understand the formation and evolution of planetary systems. AU Mic is a well-studied young star that is thought to be a prime candidate for harboring a planetary system, and a new planetary companion would confirm this hypothesis. 

Using high-precision radial velocity measurements with the HARPS spectrograph and radial velocity subtraction techniques, we have detected a signal that is consistent with a planetary companion with a minimum mass of 0.27 Jupiter masses and an orbital period of 8.47 days. We have also analyzed the stellar activity of AU Mic, and we find that the newly discovered signal is not caused by stellar spots or other activity. 

The motivation for this research is to contribute to the ongoing search for exoplanets, as well as to increase our understanding of the formation and dynamics of planetary systems. Our results provide evidence for the existence of a planetary companion around AU Mic and add to the growing body of knowledge about exoplanetary systems.

Our research aimed to enhance the lifetime of wireless sensor networks (WSNs) with the collaborative beamforming and cooperative transmission (CBCT) approach. WSNs have a limited lifespan due to their battery constraints, and in many cases, replacing them is not practical. Therefore, extending the lifetime of WSN is of great importance. 

We proposed a novel CBCT approach that combines two techniques, collaboration among sensor nodes and beamforming. The proposed approach optimizes the energy efficiency of the network by reducing the energy consumption of each node. The CBCT approach also makes the transmission more reliable and resilient against interferences and obstacles. 

Our experimental simulations showed that the proposed CBCT method could improve the network's lifetime by nearly 20% compared to traditional techniques. Additionally, our results showed that our approach outperformed existing solutions in terms of reliability, energy consumption, and network lifetime. 

In conclusion, this work demonstrates the effectiveness of CBCT in improving the lifetime of WSNs. The innovative approach combines the benefits of beamforming and cooperative transmission to optimize the energy consumption and enhance the lifetime of the WSN.
