To achieve this aim, we first provide a general overview of the theoretical foundations of Hartree-Fock theory and the HF-SCF approach, focusing on the fundamental principles, mathematical equations, and computational algorithms involved. We then delve into the mathematical and computational aspects of HF-SCF, addressing crucial issues such as convergence, basis sets, electron correlation, and multi-reference problems. 

The results obtained from this work provide a deeper understanding of the underlying theory of HF-SCF methods, as well as the limitations and challenges associated with their numerical implementation. Our analysis highlights the importance of using appropriate numerical techniques to overcome the computational bottlenecks encountered in solving the complex equations involved in HF-SCF calculations. Overall, this study emphasizes the necessity of continuously developing novel and improved computational methods for solving molecular quantum chemistry problems.

We study the morphology of galaxy populations of the richest superclusters
from the catalogue of superclusters of galaxies in the 2dF Galaxy Redshift
Survey using the luminosity density distribution and Minkowski functional V3.
We compare the properties of grouped and isolated galaxies in regions of
different density in superclusters. We find that in high-density cores of rich
superclusters there is an excess of early type, passive galaxies, among
galaxies in groups and clusters, as well as among those which do not belong to
groups, while in lower density outer regions there are more blue, star-forming
galaxies both in groups and among those galaxies which do not belong to groups.
This also shows that the galaxy content of groups depends on the environment
where the groups reside in. The density distributions and the behaviour of the
Minkowski functional V3 for different superclusters show that substructures in
superclusters as traced by different populations of galaxies are very
different. Our results show that both local (group/cluster) and global
(supercluster) environments are important in forming galaxy morphologies and
star formation activity. The presence of a high density core with X-ray
clusters and a relatively small fraction of star-forming galaxies in the
supercluster SCL126 may be an indication that this supercluster has started its
evolution earlier than the supercluster SCL9.

In this work, we study the behavior of Hill's equation with random forcing terms. The idea is to understand how small random fluctuations can affect the dynamics of a classical physical system. Hill's equation is a simple model that captures the behavior of many physical systems, including pendulums and electronic circuits. It describes the motion of a particle in a periodic potential under the influence of external forcing. We introduce randomness in the forcing term to investigate how it affects the stability and the long-term behavior of the system.

Our methodology involves the use of stochastic calculus techniques to derive an approximate solution for the equation. We show that the randomness in the forcing term can induce chaotic behavior in the system, leading to the breakdown of periodicity. This result is significant because it shows that random fluctuations can lead to a drastic change in the dynamics of a classical physical system. 

The problem that our work attempts to solve is to understand the effect of small random perturbations on the behavior of physical systems. Our results offer insight into how random fluctuations can lead to unpredictable behavior in systems that were previously thought to be stable. This work has important implications for many areas of science and engineering, from the study of chemical reactions to the design of control systems.

In this paper we study various fractal geometric aspects of the Minkowski
question mark function $Q.$ We show that the unit interval can be written as
the union of the three sets $\Lambda_{0}:=\{x:Q'(x)=0\}$,
$\Lambda_{\infty}:=\{x:Q'(x)=\infty\}$, and $\Lambda_{\sim}:=\{x:Q'(x)$ does
not exist and $Q'(x)\not=\infty\}.$ The main result is that the Hausdorff
dimensions of these sets are related in the following way.
$\dim_{H}(\nu_{F})<\dim_{H}(\Lambda_{\sim})= \dim_{H} (\Lambda_{\infty}) =
\dim_{H} (\mathcal{L}(h_{\mathrm{top}}))<\dim_{H}(\Lambda_{0})=1.$
  Here, $\mathcal{L}(h_{\mathrm{top}})$ refers to the level set of the
  Stern-Brocot multifractal decomposition at the topological entropy
  $h_{\mathrm{top}}=\log2$ of the Farey map $F,$ and $\dim_{H}(\nu_{F})$
denotes the Hausdorff dimension of the measure of maximal entropy of the
dynamical system associated with $F.$ The proofs rely partially on the
multifractal formalism for Stern-Brocot intervals and give non-trivial
applications of this formalism.

We present a survey of mid-infrared gas-phase lines toward a sample of 76
circumstellar disks around low mass pre-main sequence stars from the Spitzer
"Cores to Disks" legacy program. We report the first detections of [Ne II] and
[Fe I] toward classical T Tauri stars in ~20% respectively ~9% of our sources.
The observed [Ne II] line fluxes and upper limits are consistent with [Ne II]
excitation in an X-ray irradiated disk around stars with X-ray luminosities
L_X=10^{29}-10^{31} erg s^{-1}. [Fe I] is detected at ~10^-5-10^-4 L_Sun, but
no [S I] or [Fe II] is detected down to ~10^{-6} L_Sun. The [Fe I] detections
indicate the presence of gas-rich disks with masses of >~0.1 M_J. No H_2 0-0
S(0) and S(1) disk emission is detected, except for S(1) toward one source.
These data give upper limits on the warm (T~100-200K) gas mass of a few Jovian
masses, consistent with recent T Tauri disk models which include gas heating by
stellar radiation. Compact disk emission of hot (T>~500K) gas is observed
through the H_2 0-0 S(2) and/or S(3) lines toward ~8% of our sources. The line
fluxes are, however, higher by more than an order of magnitude than those
predicted by recent disk models, even when X-ray and excess UV radiation are
included. Similarly the [Ne II]/H_2 0-0 S(2) ratios for these sources are lower
than predicted, consistent with the presence of an additional hot molecular gas
component not included in current disk models. Oblique shocks of stellar winds
interacting with the disk can explain many aspects of the hot gas emission, but
are inconsistent with the non-detection of [S I] and [Fe II] lines.

Diskoseismology, the theoretical study of small adiabatic hydrodynamical
global perturbations of geometrically thin, optically thick accretion disks
around black holes (and other compact objects), is a potentially powerful probe
of the gravitational field. For instance, the frequencies of the normal mode
oscillations can be used to determine the elusive angular momentum parameter of
the black hole. The general formalism developed by diskoseismologists for
relativistic systems can be readily applied to the Newtonian case of
cataclysmic variables (CVs). Some of these systems (e.g., the dwarf nova SS
Cygni) show rapid oscillations in the UV with periods of tens of seconds and
high coherence. In this paper, we assess the possibility that these dwarf nova
oscillations (DNOs) are diskoseismic modes. Besides its importance in
investigating the physical origin of DNOs, the present work could help us to
answer the following question. To what extent are the similarities in the
oscillation phenomenology of CVs and X-ray binaries (XRBs) indicative of a
common physical mechanism?

We have used the ACS camera on HST to obtain (V,I) photometry for 5300
red-giant stars in the halo of the dominant Leo-group member NGC 3379, a galaxy
usually regarded as a classic normal giant elliptical. We use this sample of
stars to derive the metallicity distribution function (MDF) for its outer-halo
field stars 33 kpc from the galaxy center. The MDF is distinctly unlike all the
other E galaxies for which we have similar data (including the Local Group
dwarf ellipticals, the intermediate-luminosity NGC 3377, and the giant NGC
5128). First, the MDF for the NGC 3379 outer halo is broad and flat, with many
stars at every interval in [m/H]. Second, we see a metallicity gradient across
our ACS field such that in its outermost region the blue, low-metallicity stars
([m/H] < -0.7) are beginning to dominate and the higher-metallicity stars are
rapidly diminishing. Our target field is centered at a projected distance about
equal to 12 R_e, twice as far out in units of effective radius as in any of the
other galaxies that we have surveyed. If NGC 3379 is indeed representative of
large E/S0 galaxies, we predict that such galaxies in general will reveal
diffuse low-metallicity subpopulations, but that photometry at radii r ~ 10 -
15 R_e will be necessary to see the faint low-metallicity component clearly.

AIM: We have recently developed a microscopic Monte Carlo approach to study
surface chemistry on interstellar grains and the morphology of ice mantles. The
method is designed to eliminate the problems inherent in the rate-equation
formalism to surface chemistry. Here we report the first use of this method in
a chemical model of cold interstellar cloud cores that includes both gas-phase
and surface chemistry. The surface chemical network consists of a small number
of diffusive reactions that can produce molecular oxygen, water, carbon
dioxide, formaldehyde, methanol and assorted radicals. METHOD: The simulation
is started by running a gas-phase model including accretion onto grains but no
surface chemistry or evaporation. The starting surface consists of either flat
or rough olivine. We introduce the surface chemistry of the three species H, O
and CO in an iterative manner using our stochastic technique. Under the
conditions of the simulation, only atomic hydrogen can evaporate to a
significant extent. Although it has little effect on other gas-phase species,
the evaporation of atomic hydrogen changes its gas-phase abundance, which in
turn changes the flux of atomic hydrogen onto grains. The effect on the surface
chemistry is treated until convergence occurs. We neglect all non-thermal
desorptive processes. RESULTS: We determine the mantle abundances of assorted
molecules as a function of time through 2x10^5 yr. Our method also allows
determination of the abundance of each molecule in specific monolayers. The
mantle results can be compared with observations of water, carbon dioxide,
carbon monoxide, and methanol ices in the sources W33A and Elias 16. Other than
a slight underproduction of mantle CO, our results are in very good agreement
with observations.

In this work, we present molecular dynamics (MD) simulations to study the spatial scale of low energy excitations in a simple polymer system. Our study is motivated by the interest in understanding the fundamental mechanisms underlying energy transfer in molecular systems. The potential applications of this knowledge range from energy harvesting to the design of future materials with desired functionalities. To achieve our goal, we employ the MD technique to analyze the motions of chains under thermal excitations at low temperatures. Key ideas of the study involve quantification of the mobility of polymer chains in response to thermal perturbations, identification of characteristic length scales for energy transfer in the system, and the analysis of the correlation between chain dynamics and the local environment of molecules.

Our results show a clear dependence of the mobility of chains on the local molecular configuration, indicating that the spatial scale of the energy transfer is a function of the local packing density. We observe that chain motions are hindered in densely packed regions and are rather free in regions where molecules are less packed. Our study provides new insights into the correlation between local structure and energy transfer in polymer systems. Our results could be useful in designing materials that are capable of controlled energy transfer, for example, in thermoelectric materials.

In this study, we present a new model for describing repulsive gravity using the concept of creation. We were motivated by the fact that standard gravity theory cannot fully explain the properties of dark energy, which leads to the expansion of the universe. We propose that the universe is created through the release of energy from a pre-existing vacuum state. This energy then generates a repulsive gravitational force that counteracts attractive gravity and causes the observed acceleration of the universe's expansion.

Our methodology is based on a modification of the usual Einstein-Hilbert action and involves introducing a scalar field that couples to gravity. We show that our model leads to a repulsive force proportional to the scalar field gradient, which accounts for the dark energy effects observed in the universe.

The results obtained indicate that the proposed model is consistent with current cosmological data, such as cosmic microwave background radiation and supernova observations. In particular, the model can explain both the accelerating expansion of the universe and the flatness of the universe observed in these data. Our study highlights the possibility of explaining the properties of dark energy through the concept of creation, which opens up new avenues for future research.

It is known that when hard spheres are added to a pure system of hard rods
the stability of the smectic phase may be greatly enhanced, and that this
effect can be rationalised in terms of depletion forces. In the present paper
we first study the effect of orientational order on depletion forces in this
particular binary system, comparing our results with those obtained adopting
the usual approximation of considering the rods parallel and their orientations
frozen. We consider mixtures with rods of different aspect ratios and spheres
of different diameters, and we treat them within Onsager theory. Our results
indicate that depletion effects, and consequently smectic stability, decrease
significantly as a result of orientational disorder in the smectic phase when
compared with corresponding data based on the frozen--orientation
approximation. These results are discussed in terms of the $\tau$ parameter,
which has been proposed as a convenient measure of depletion strength. We
present closed expressions for $\tau$, and show that it is intimately connected
with the depletion potential. We then analyse the effect of particle geometry
by comparing results pertaining to systems of parallel rods of different shapes
(spherocylinders, cylinders and parallelepipeds). We finally provide results
based on the Zwanzig approximation of a Fundamental--Measure
density--functional theory applied to mixtures of parallelepipeds and cubes of
different sizes. In this case, we show that the $\tau$ parameter exhibits a
linear asymptotic behaviour in the limit of large values of the hard--rod
aspect ratio, in conformity with Onsager theory, as well as in the limit of
large values of the ratio of rod breadth to cube side length, $d$, in contrast
to Onsager approximation, which predicts $\tau\sim d^3$.

We present a new catalog of star cluster candidates in the nearby spiral
galaxy M33. It is based on eight existing catalogs wherein we have
cross-referenced identifications and endeavored to resolve inconsistencies
between them. Our catalog contains 451 candidates of which 255 are confirmed
clusters based on HST and high resolution ground-based imaging. The catalog
contains precise cluster positions (RA and Dec), magnitudes and colors in the
UBVRIJHKs filters, metallicities, radial velocities, masses and ages, where
available, and galactocentric distances for each cluster. The color
distribution of the M33 clusters appears to be similar to those in the Large
Magellanic Cloud with major peaks at (B-V)o~0.15, and (B-V)o~0.65. The
intrinsic colors are correlated with cluster ages, which range from 10^{7.5} to
10^{10.3} years. The age distribution of the star clusters supports the notion
of rapid cluster disruption with a slope of alpha=-1.09 +/- 0.07 in the
dN_{cluster}/dt ~ t^{alpha} relation. In addition, comparison to theoretical
single stellar population models suggests the presence of an age-metallicity
relation among these clusters with younger clusters being more metal-rich.
Analysis of the radial distribution of the clusters yields some evidence that
younger clusters (age <~ 1 Gyr) may be more concentrated toward the center of
M33 than older ones. A similar comparison with the radial profile of the M33
field stars shows the clusters to be more centrally concentrated at the greater
than 99.9% confidence level. Possible reasons for this are presented and
discussed; however, the overwhelming conclusion seems to be that a more
complete and thorough cluster search is needed covering at least 4 square
degrees centered on M33.

In this paper, we present our work on the development of velocity-dependent models for non-Abelian/entangled string networks. The motivation of our research stems from the growing interest in exploring the dynamics of string networks in the context of cosmic string theory and their potential observational signatures. However, existing models for string networks have so far been based on Abelian strings, which are not sufficiently realistic for studying non-Abelian strings.

The problem that our work attempts to solve is the lack of a suitable model for simulating the dynamics of non-Abelian/entangled string networks in a computationally efficient manner. We introduce a new approach that takes into account the velocity and inter-string interactions of non-Abelian strings. This allows us to produce accurate predictions for the formation and evolution of non-Abelian string networks, which can then be used to infer their observational consequences.

Our results demonstrate the efficacy of the velocity-dependent model in accurately capturing the dynamics of non-Abelian/entangled string networks. We show that our model is able to reproduce key features such as the scaling behavior and kinks in the string profiles that are indicative of non-Abelian strings. Moreover, we find that our predictions for the relevant observational quantities such as the cosmic string tension can be consistent with current observational bounds.

In conclusion, the velocity-dependent models presented in this paper provide a valuable tool for studying the properties of non-Abelian string networks. They offer a promising avenue for exploring the rich physics of cosmic strings and their potential implications in various cosmological scenarios.

In this paper, we delve into the topic of decomposition numbers for finite Coxeter groups via the use of generalised non-crossing partitions. The motivation for this research stems from the desire to understand the representation theory of Coxeter groups, a fundamental topic in algebraic combinatorics with many applications in physics and other fields.

Our key idea is to use generalised non-crossing partitions to compute the decomposition numbers of finite Coxeter groups. Generalised non-crossing partitions are combinatorial objects that generalize the classical theory of non-crossing partitions, and they have shown to be a valuable tool in the study of Coxeter groups. Using the theory of generalised non-crossing partitions, we obtain explicit formulae for the decomposition numbers in terms of certain generating functions associated to these partitions.

Our methodology combines techniques from representation theory, algebraic geometry, and combinatorics. We use the theory of Coxeter groups and their Hecke algebras to establish a connection between the decomposition numbers and the geometry of certain algebraic varieties called Springer fibers. To compute these generating functions, we utilize the theory of quiver representations and their associated cluster algebras.

Our main result is a formula for the decomposition numbers of all finite Coxeter groups that extends previous results in the literature. These formulae have been successfully applied to obtain new insights into the representation theory of Coxeter groups and to solve open problems in this area.

In this work, we present a study of thermally unstable accretion disks around black holes using the adaptive pseudo-spectral domain decomposition method. Our main focus is on the limit-cycle behavior of disks with moderate viscosity. We were motivated by the importance of understanding the dynamics of accretion disks, which play a crucial role in astrophysical phenomena such as X-ray binary systems and active galactic nuclei.

Our approach involves solving the governing equations of the disk evolution using a high-order adaptive pseudo-spectral method, coupled with a domain decomposition technique. Our method allows us to accurately capture the dynamics of the disk at a high resolution, while efficiently handling the nonlinear interactions between different spatial scales.

Our results demonstrate that disks with moderate viscosity can exhibit limit-cycle behavior, characterized by intermittent outbursts of matter and energy from the inner disk. The duration of these outbursts and the amplitude of the accretion rate depend on the disk's initial conditions. We also observe a correlation between the outburst frequency and the disk's viscosity parameter.

The insights gained from this study can help us better understand the observational properties of accretion disks and the processes that govern their evolution. Moreover, our approach can be extended to study a wider range of physical settings, providing a valuable tool for future research in astrophysics.

In this study, we investigate the Planck scale unification within the framework of a supersymmetric standard model. Our primary motivation was to explore the possibility of unifying the fundamental forces of nature at the Planck scale, which is the energy scale at which quantum gravity becomes dominant. Such unification would provide us with a deeper understanding of the nature of the universe and contribute significantly to the development of a grand unified theory. 

We formulated a supersymmetric standard model in which we introduced a heavy gauge boson at the Planck scale. We then calculated the running of the standard model gauge couplings and identified the values at which they unify. Our results reveal a consistent unification scenario at the Planck scale with a precision of 10% for the gauge coupling unification. Our findings also suggest that this heavy gauge boson might play a role in generating the observed matter-antimatter asymmetry in the universe.

Overall, this study contributes to the ongoing research in particle physics and cosmology by exploring the unification of the fundamental forces of nature at the Planck scale. Our work attempts to solve the problem of unification by introducing a heavy gauge boson, and we hope that our findings will inspire further investigations towards a grand unified theory of everything.

Aims: We present a photometric study of the deeply eclipsing SW Sex-type
nova-like cataclysmic variable star BH Lyn
  Methods: Time-resolved V-band CCD photometry was obtained for seven nights
between 1999 and 2004.
  Results: We determined 11 new eclipse timings of BH Lyn and derived a refined
orbital ephemeris with an orbital period of 0.155875577(14) day. During the
observations, BH Lyn was in high-state with V~15.5 mag. The star presents ~1.5
mag deep eclipses with mean full-width at half-flux of 0.0683(+/-0.0054)P_orb.
  The eclipse shape is highly variable, even changing form cycle to cycle. This
is most likely due to accretion disc surface brightness distribution
variations, most probably caused by strong flickering. Time-dependent accretion
disc self-occultation or variations of the hot spot(s) intensity are also
possible explanations. Negative superhumps with period of ~0.145 day are
detected in two long runs in 2000. A possible connection between SW Sex and
negative superhump phenomena through the presence of tilted accretion disc is
discussed, and a way to observationally test this is suggested.

We employ the Monte Carlo particle collision code DPMJET3.04 to determine the
multiplicity spectra of various secondary particles (in addition to $\pi^0$'s)
with $\gamma$'s as the final decay state, that are produced in cosmic-ray
($p$'s and $\alpha$'s) interactions with the interstellar medium. We derive an
easy-to-use $\gamma$-ray production matrix for cosmic rays with energies up to
about 10 PeV. This $\gamma$-ray production matrix is applied to the GeV excess
in diffuse Galactic $\gamma$-rays observed by EGRET, and we conclude the
non-$\pi^0$ decay components are insufficient to explain the GeV excess,
although they have contributed a different spectrum from the $\pi^0$-decay
component. We also test the hypothesis that the TeV-band $\gamma$-ray emission
of the shell-type SNR RX J1713.7-3946 observed with HESS is caused by hadronic
cosmic rays which are accelerated by a cosmic-ray modified shock. By the
$\chi^2$ statistics, we find a continuously softening spectrum is strongly
preferred, in contrast to expectations. A hardening spectrum has about 1%
probability to explain the HESS data, but then only if a hard cutoff at 50-100
TeV is imposed on the particle spectrum.

The formation of interface dipoles in self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) of
--CH$_3$ and --CF$_3$ terminated short-chain alkanethiolates on Ag(111) is
studied by means of density functional theory calculations. The interface
dipoles are characterized by monitoring the change in the surface work function
upon adsorption of the SAM. We compare results obtained for SAMs in structures
with a different packing density of molecules, i.e. {$(\sqrt{7}\times\sqrt{7})
R19.1^{\circ}$}, {$(\sqrt{3}\times\sqrt{3}) R30^{\circ}$}, and {p(2$\times$2)}.
The work function of alkanethiolate SAMs on silver depends weakly on the
packing density; that of fluorinatedalkanethiolates shows a stronger
dependance. The results are analyzed in terms of two nearly independent
contributions to the interface dipole. These originate respectively from the
molecular dipoles and from a charge transfer between the metal surface and the
molecules. The charge transfer is determined by the silver--sulfur bond and it
is independent of the electronegativity of the molecules.

In this study, we present a comprehensive analysis of the velocity profiles in the solar corona using multispectral observations from the Hinode and the Atmospheric Imaging Assembly (AIA) instruments. Our goal is to investigate the physical processes that drive the dynamics of the solar atmosphere using the spectral signatures of the ions that are present in it. By analyzing the Doppler shifts of the spectral lines, we derived the velocity profiles of the plasma in different regions of the corona.

Our results show that the velocity profiles in the corona are highly complex and they exhibit a wide range of velocities that vary both spatially and temporally. We found that the velocity profiles are strongly correlated with the active regions and the coronal holes, indicating that the magnetic field plays a critical role in driving the plasma dynamics. We also identified a number of high-velocity jets and flows that are associated with the reconnection events in the corona.

Our approach used an advanced methodology that combines the imaging and spectroscopic observations from multiple instruments, allowing us to capture the different physical processes that occur in the corona. Our study aims to improve our understanding of the processes that govern the dynamics of the solar atmosphere, and to provide new insights into the drivers of space weather phenomena.

In this work, we investigate the super star cluster NGC1705-1 as a potential local analogue to the birthsite of long-duration gamma-ray bursts (LGRBs). Our motivation stems from the elusive nature of LGRBs and the need for a better understanding of their formation mechanisms. We utilize high-resolution Hubble Space Telescope imaging to analyze the stellar populations and physical properties of NGC1705-1.

Our results demonstrate that NGC1705-1 displays a strikingly similar morphology to known LGRB host galaxies, including a compact and irregular morphology, prominent starburst activity, and a high surface brightness. We also detect a significant population of massive and young stars in NGC1705-1, indicating that it is undergoing a recent episode of intense star formation, akin to the conditions of LGRB host galaxies.

We propose that NGC1705-1 is a promising candidate for further studies on LGRB formation and that its similarity to LGRB host galaxies indicates a potential connection between the two. The methodology used in this work focuses on stellar population analysis and comparison with known LGRB host galaxies. Our findings offer new insights into the complex nature of LGRBs and provide a foundation for future research in the field.

In this work, we investigate the linkage between accretion disks and blazars. The accretion disk is a primary source of radiation in an active galactic nucleus, and blazars are the most powerful astrophysical sources of high-energy gamma rays. Therefore, understanding the connection between these two phenomena is crucial to comprehend the key physical processes in the high-energy cosmos. The motivation for this research stems from the difficulty in explaining the observed properties of blazars with existing models used to describe accretion disk behavior.

Our work attempts to solve this problem by exploring the mechanism that initiates and sustains the blazar phenomenon. We approach this by developing models to describe the physical interaction between the accretion disk and the jet in blazars. We consider the jet to be a higher-energy form of the accretion disk and examine the energy transfer between these two sources, through detailed modeling and simulation. We argue that the critical factor controlling the link between accretion disks and blazars is the formation and acceleration of the jet. Our methodology is based on combining analytical and numerical techniques, including magnetohydrodynamic simulations and statistical analysis of large data sets from gamma-ray telescopes.

In conclusion, our work sheds light on the fundamental question of how astrophysical jets, blazars, and accretion disks are interlinked and can effectively transfer energy. Our research aims to deepen our understanding of the physics behind the high-energy universe, and our approach could have implications for a range of astrophysical phenomena.

Dilute Fermi systems with large s-wave scattering length a_s exhibit
universal properties if the interparticle spacing r_o greatly exceeds the range
of the underlying two-body interaction potential. In this regime, r_o is the
only relevant length scale and observables such as the energy per particle
depend only on r_o (or, equivalently, the energy E_{FG} of the free Fermi gas).
This paper investigates Bose and Fermi systems with non-vanishing angular
momentum l using the lowest order constrained variational method. We focus on
the regime where the generalized scattering length becomes large and determine
the relevant length scales. For Bose gases with large generalized scattering
lengths, we obtain simple expressions for the energy per particle in terms of a
l-dependent length scale \xi_l, which depends on the range of the underlying
two-body potential and the average interparticle spacing. We discuss possible
implications for dilute two-component Fermi systems with finite l. Furthermore,
we determine the equation of state of liquid and gaseous bosonic helium.

This paper examines the effect of temperature on the structural stability and
mechanical properties of 20 layered (10,10) single walled carbon nanotubes
(SWCNTs) under tensile loading using an O(N) tight binding molecular dynamics
(TBMD) simulation method. We observed that (10,10) tube can sustain its
structural stability for the strain values of 0.23 in elongation and 0.06 in
compression at 300K. Bond breaking strain value decreases with increasing
temperature under streching but not under compression. The elastic limit,
Young's modulus, tensile strength and Poisson ratio are calculated as 0.10,
0.395 TPa, 83.23 GPa, 0.285, respectively, at 300K. In the temperature range
from 300K to 900K; Young's modulus and the tensile strengths are decreasing
with increasing temperature while the Poisson ratio is increasing. At higher
temperatures, Young's modulus starts to increase while the Poisson ratio and
tensile strength decrease. In the temperature range from 1200K to 1800K, the
SWCNT is already deformed and softened. Applying strain on these deformed and
softened SWCNTs do not follow the same pattern as in the temperature range of
300K to 900K.

A synfire chain is a simple neural network model which can propagate stable
synchronous spikes called a pulse packet and widely researched. However how
synfire chains coexist in one network remains to be elucidated. We have studied
the activity of a layered associative network of Leaky Integrate-and-Fire
neurons in which connection we embed memory patterns by the Hebbian Learning.
We analyzed their activity by the Fokker-Planck method. In our previous report,
when a half of neurons belongs to each memory pattern (memory pattern rate
$F=0.5$), the temporal profiles of the network activity is split into
temporally clustered groups called sublattices under certain input conditions.
In this study, we show that when the network is sparsely connected ($F<0.5$),
synchronous firings of the memory pattern are promoted. On the contrary, the
densely connected network ($F>0.5$) inhibit synchronous firings. The sparseness
and denseness also effect the basin of attraction and the storage capacity of
the embedded memory patterns. We show that the sparsely(densely) connected
networks enlarge(shrink) the basion of attraction and increase(decrease) the
storage capacity.

We investigate the astrometric effects of stellar surface structures as a
practical limitation to ultra-high-precision astrometry, e.g. in the context of
exoplanet searches, and to quantify the expected effects in different regions
of the HR-diagram. Stellar surface structures are likely to produce
fluctuations in the integrated flux and radial velocity of the star, as well as
a variation of the observed photocentre, i.e. astrometric jitter, and closure
phase. We use theoretical considerations supported by Monte Carlo simulations
to derive statistical relations between the corresponding astrometric,
photometric, and radial-velocity effects. For most stellar types the
astrometric jitter due to stellar surface structures is expected to be of order
10 micro-AU or greater. This is more than the astrometric displacement
typically caused by an Earth-size exoplanet in the habitable zone, which is
about 1-4 micro-AU for long-lived main-sequence stars. Only for stars with
extremely low photometric variability (<0.5 mmag) and low magnetic activity,
comparable to that of the Sun, will the astrometric jitter be of order 1
micro-AU, suffcient to allow the astrometric detection of an Earth-sized planet
in the habitable zone. While stellar surface structure may thus seriously
impair the astrometric detection of small exoplanets, it has in general
negligible impact on the detection of large (Jupiter-size) planets and on the
determination of stellar parallax and proper motion. From the starspot model we
also conclude that the commonly used spot filling factor is not the most
relevant parameter for quantifying the spottiness in terms of the resulting
astrometric, photometric and radial-velocity variations.

Unconstrained CO2 emission from fossil fuel burning has been the dominant
cause of observed anthropogenic global warming. The amounts of "proven" and
potential fossil fuel reserves are uncertain and debated. Regardless of the
true values, society has flexibility in the degree to which it chooses to
exploit these reserves, especially unconventional fossil fuels and those
located in extreme or pristine environments. If conventional oil production
peaks within the next few decades, it may have a large effect on future
atmospheric CO2 and climate change, depending upon subsequent energy choices.
Assuming that proven oil and gas reserves do not greatly exceed estimates of
the Energy Information Administration, and recent trends are toward lower
estimates, we show that it is feasible to keep atmospheric CO2 from exceeding
about 450 ppm by 2100, provided that emissions from coal, unconventional fossil
fuels, and land use are constrained. Coal-fired power plants without
sequestration must be phased out before mid-century to achieve this CO2 limit.
It is also important to "stretch" conventional oil reserves via energy
conservation and efficiency, thus averting strong pressures to extract liquid
fuels from coal or unconventional fossil fuels while clean technologies are
being developed for the era "beyond fossil fuels". We argue that a rising price
on carbon emissions is needed to discourage conversion of the vast fossil
resources into usable reserves, and to keep CO2 beneath the 450 ppm ceiling.

In this study, we investigate the entanglement dynamics and the effects of DM antisymmetric anisotropic exchange interaction on a two-qubit $XYZ$ magnetic chain. Our work aims to provide a deeper understanding of the interaction between DM and exchange interactions and how these interactions impact entanglement properties. To achieve this aim, we adopted a numerical approach based on time-dependent density matrix renormalization group (tDMRG) simulations. 

Our results show that the antisymmetric exchange interaction in the $XYZ$ magnetic chain with DM can significantly enhance the entanglement between two qubits and change the entanglement dynamics compared to the conventional exchange interaction. In particular, we find that the DM interaction can induce the emergence of a multipartite entanglement structure in the two-qubit system, leading to a critical entanglement behavior like sudden death and revival of entanglement. Furthermore, we show that the entanglement dynamics can be controlled by manipulating the external magnetic field strength or tuning the DM interaction. 

Our findings have significant implications in the development of quantum computation, communication, and information processing. Our methodology and analytical framework provide a basis for future theoretical and experimental studies on quantum information processing and quantum technologies more broadly.

Normal-incidence transmission and dispersion properties of optical
multilayers and one-dimensional stepwise potential barriers in the
non-tunneling regime are analytically investigated. The optical paths of every
constituent layer in a multilayer structure, as well as the parameters of every
step of the stepwise potential barrier, are constrained by a generalized
quarter-wave condition. No other restrictions on the structure geometry is
imposed, i.e., the layers are arranged arbitrarily. We show that the density of
states (DOS) spectra of the multilayer or barrier in question are subject to
integral conservation rules similar to the Barnett-Loudon sum rule but ocurring
within a finite frequency or energy interval. In the optical case, these
frequency intervals are regular. For the potential barriers, only non-periodic
energy intervals can be present in the spectrum of any given structure, and
only if the parameters of constituent potential steps are properly chosen.
  Abstract The integral conservation relations derived analytically have also
been verified numerically. The relations can be used in dispersion-engineered
multilayer-based devices, e.g., ultrashort pulse compressors or ultracompact
optical delay lines, as well as to design multiple-quantum-well electronic
heterostructures with engineered DOS.

Hundreds of substellar companions to solar-type stars will be discovered with
the Kepler satellite. Kepler's extreme photometric precision gives access to
low-amplitude stellar variability contributed by a variety of physical
processes. We discuss in detail the periodic flux modulations arising from the
tidal force on the star due to a substellar companion. An analytic expression
for the variability is derived in the equilibrium-tide approximation. We
demonstrate analytically and through numerical solutions of the linear,
nonadiabatic stellar oscillation equations that the equilibrium-tide formula
works extremely well for stars of mass <1.4 Msun with thick surface convection
zones. More massive stars with largely radiative envelopes do not conform to
the equilibrium-tide approximation and can exhibit flux variations $\ga$10
times larger than naive estimates. Over the full range of stellar masses
considered, we treat the oscillatory response of the convection zone by
adapting a prescription that A. J. Brickhill developed for pulsating white
dwarfs. Compared to other sources of periodic variability, the ellipsoidal
lightcurve has a distinct dependence on time and system parameters. We suggest
that ellipsoidal oscillations induced by giant planets may be detectable from
as many as ~100 of the 10^5 Kepler target stars. (Abridged)

Selective control in a population is the ability to control a member of the
population while leaving the other members relatively unaffected. The concept
of selective control is developed using cell death or apoptosis in
heterogeneous cell populations as an example. Apoptosis signaling in
heterogeneous cells is described by an ensemble of gene networks with identical
topology but different link strengths. Selective control depends on the
statistics of signaling in the ensemble of networks and we analyse the effects
of superposition, non-linearity and feedback on these statistics. Parallel
pathways promote normal statistics while series pathways promote skew
distributions which in the most extreme cases become log-normal. We also show
that feedback and non-linearity can produce bimodal signaling statistics, as
can discreteness and non-linearity. Two methods for optimizing selective
control are presented. The first is an exhaustive search method and the second
is a linear programming based approach. Though control of a single gene in the
signaling network yields little selectivity, control of a few genes typically
yields higher levels of selectivity. The statistics of gene combinations
susceptible to selective control is studied and is used to identify general
control strategies. We found that selectivity is promoted by acting on the
least sensitive nodes in the case of weak populations, while selective control
of robust populations is optimized through perturbations of more sensitive
nodes. High throughput experiments with heterogeneous cell lines could be
designed in an analogous manner, with the further possibility of incorporating
the selectivity optimization process into a closed-loop control system.

In this study, we present a new AB-Thermonuclear reactor that has been designed for aerospace applications. The main objective of the research is to develop a new kind of energy source that can provide a high level of energy density, high power output, and long operational life in space missions. The methodology used in this work is based on the principles of nuclear physics and engineering, which involve the design of a fusion reactor that can produce a high-energy plasma. 

The results obtained show the potential of the AB-Thermonuclear reactor as a viable energy source for aerospace applications. The structure of the reactor is based on a toroidal geometry, which allows for a high plasma stability. The key features of the reactor include a high energy conversion efficiency, compact design, and autonomous operation. 

The motivation for this research stems from the need for advanced energy systems that can support long-term space missions. Conventional power systems, such as solar panels and batteries, are limited in their energy density and lifespan, which makes them unsuitable for long-range missions. The AB-Thermonuclear reactor is a promising alternative that can provide a continuous and reliable power source for space exploration. 

In conclusion, this study presents a new AB-Thermonuclear reactor for aerospace applications, which offers significant advantages over conventional power systems. The results obtained demonstrate the feasibility of this technology and its potential for future space missions. This research contributes to the development of advanced energy technologies that are essential for the success of future space exploration programs.

In this study, we present a model that explores the polarimetric variability of hot star winds, which is caused by the clumping of ionized gas in the wind. This phenomenon leads to strong polarization signals that can be observed with polarimeters, providing valuable insights into the dynamics and structure of hot star winds. We developed a Monte Carlo radiative transfer code that takes into account the non-spherical shapes of clumps, as well as their internal properties such as density, temperature, and ionization fraction. Our model predicts the polarization spectrum of a clumped wind, from the UV to the near-IR spectral range, and accounts for the effects of geometric projection, selective absorption, and electron and dust scattering.

The results obtained from our model show that clumping-induced polarization signals are highly sensitive to clump properties such as size, filling factor, and velocity dispersion. The polarization angle also varies significantly with wavelength and depends on the clump geometry. We compare our model predictions with observational data of hot star winds, such as the O-star HD 163758, and find good agreement with the measured polarization angles and degree of polarization.

The motivation for this research lies in the need to understand the complex and often poorly understood physics of hot star winds. By modeling the polarization properties of clumped winds, we can gain insight into the clump formation process, wind variability, and the interaction between the wind and its environment. Our results provide a useful framework for interpreting future polarimetric observations of hot star winds and improving our understanding of these intriguing astrophysical environments.

In this work, we investigate the plasma effects in a newly-designed micromachined floating-gate high-electron-mobility transistor (FET). We aim to understand the behavior of plasma in the proposed device under different operation conditions. The main problem we are trying to solve is to develop a FET with improved performance and reliability compared to traditional FETs under plasma exposure, which is a common occurrence in many applications such as spacecraft electronics and plasma processing. The combination of micromachining and floating-gate technology in the proposed device allows for higher voltage operation and better radiation tolerance, which are important factors for space applications.

To achieve this goal, we conduct simulations and experiments to investigate the plasma effects in the micromachined FET by varying parameters such as gate voltage and frequency. Our results show that the proposed device exhibits improved plasma resistance with enhanced stability and performance under plasma exposure. Moreover, this work also sheds light on the importance of considering plasma effects in the design and optimization of FETs for space applications.

Overall, this research is motivated by the need for reliable and durable electronic components in space exploration and plasma processing, and our findings contribute to the development of more robust FETs for these applications.

In this study, we investigate the likelihood of supernova enrichment of protoplanetary disks. The problem that our work attempts to solve is that the origins of elements heavier than iron, such as gold, platinum, and uranium, remain a mystery. It is hypothesized that supernovae are responsible for these heavy elements, and we want to determine the conditions under which these elements can be incorporated into protoplanetary disks.

The key ideas behind this work are that supernovae can enrich the interstellar medium with heavy elements, which can then be incorporated into protoplanetary disks during their formation. We use a combination of analytical and numerical methods to model the flow of gas and metals in protoplanetary disks. We also consider the impact of stellar winds on the mixing of the material within the disk.

Our main result is the determination of the likelihood of supernova enrichment of protoplanetary disks. Our simulations suggest that this process is possible in some scenarios, but depends on a number of factors, including the mass and metallicity of the parent star, as well as the timing and location of the supernova event. This work sheds new light on the origins of heavy elements in our solar system and beyond.

We present a study of the decay properties of the weak interaction between the 4He nucleus and the Lambda baryon. The weak decays of 4He-Lambda provide a unique opportunity to probe the structure and interaction properties of these two hadrons, which are fundamental building blocks of nuclear matter. However, the decay mechanism for this particular system has not been fully understood, and experimental data on the decay rates and angular distributions are scarce. Therefore, we seek to fill this gap by using a theoretical model that takes into account the strong and weak interactions between the two particles.

Our results show that the dominant decay mechanism for 4He-Lambda is through the weak interaction, in which the Lambda baryon undergoes a beta decay to a proton while emitting an electron and an antineutrino. We also find that the angular distribution of the emitted particles is highly sensitive to the structure of the 4He nucleus, and that the decay rates depend on the energy and momentum transfer involved in the process. Our analysis confirms the importance of studying weak decays of composite hadrons, and provides new insights into the mechanism of nuclear interactions.

Our methodology involves the use of a theoretical model based on the effective Lagrangian approach, which incorporates the strong interaction between the hadrons through an effective potential, and the weak interaction through the exchange of W and Z bosons. We solve the coupled-channel scattering equations numerically, and extract the decay amplitudes and rates from the resultant scattering wave functions. The accuracy and consistency of our results are verified by comparing with available experimental data and by varying the model parameters within their physical range. Overall, our work sheds light on the underlying physics of weak decays in nuclear systems, and provides a basis for further experimental and theoretical investigations.

Spectroscopic observations of the 2006 outburst of the recurrent nova RS
Ophiuchi at both infrared (IR) and X-ray wavelengths have shown that the blast
wave has decelerated at a higher rate than predicted by the standard
test-particle adiabatic shock-wave model. Here we show that the observed
evolution of the nova remnant can be explained by the diffusive shock
acceleration of particles at the blast wave and the subsequent escape of the
highest energy ions from the shock region. Nonlinear particle acceleration can
also account for the difference of shock velocities deduced from the IR and
X-ray data. The maximum energy that accelerated electrons and protons can have
achieved in few days after outburst is found to be as high as a few TeV. Using
the semi-analytic model of nonlinear diffusive shock acceleration developed by
Berezhko & Ellison, we show that the postshock temperature of the shocked gas
measured with RXTE/PCA and Swift/XRT imply a relatively moderate acceleration
efficiency.

In this work, we present the results of our scanning-probe spectroscopy investigation of semiconductor donor molecules. The motivation behind this research is to better understand the electronic properties of donor molecules used in the fabrication of semiconductor devices, a crucial area of study in modern electronics. Specifically, we aim to determine the energies associated with the electronic transitions of these molecules, which have not been measured previously using scanning-probe techniques.

Using a combination of scanning tunneling microscopy and spectroscopy, we successfully measured the electronic transitions of single, isolated donor molecules embedded in a semiconductor matrix. Our results show a clear correlation between the donor energy level and the observed electronic transitions. Additionally, we found that the size and shape of the electronic wave function of the donor molecule strongly affect the measured electronic transitions. These findings shed light on the fundamental electronic properties of donor molecules in semiconductor devices, which are essential for understanding and improving device performance.

Overall, our work highlights the power of scanning-probe techniques for studying the electronic properties of semiconductor donor molecules at the single molecule level. By providing a deeper understanding of these properties, our research aims to contribute to the next generation of high-performance semiconductor devices.

The intrinsic fraction of broad absorption line quasars (BALQSOs) is
important in constraining geometric and evolutionary models of quasars. We
present the fraction of BALQSOs in 2MASS detected quasars within the SDSS DR3
sample in the redshift range of 1.7 < z < 4.38. The fraction of BALQSOs is
40.4^{+3.4}_{-3.3}% in the 2MASS 99% database K_s band completeness sample, and
38.5^{+1.7}_{-1.7}% in the larger 2MASS sample extending below the completeness
limit. These fractions are significantly higher than the 26% reported in the
optical bands for the same parent sample. We also present the fraction of
BALQSOs as functions of apparent magnitudes, absolute magnitudes, and redshift
in the 2MASS and SDSS bands. The 2MASS fractions are consistently higher than
the SDSS fractions in every comparison, and the BALQSO fractions steadily
increase with wavelength from the SDSS u to the 2MASS K_s bands. Furthermore,
the i - K_s color distributions of BALQSOs and non-BALQSOs indicate that
BALQSOs are redder than non-BALQSOs, with a K-S test probability of 2e-12.
These results are consistent with the spectral difference between BALQSOs and
non-BALQSOs including both the absorption troughs and dust extinction in
BALQSOs, which leads to significant selection biases against BALQSOs in the
optical bands. Using a simple simulation incorporating the luminosity function
of quasars and the amount of obscuration for BALQSOs, we simultaneously fit the
BALQSO fractions in the SDSS and 2MASS bands. We obtain a true BALQSO fraction
of 43\pm2% for luminous quasars (M_{K_s} \lesssim -30.1 mag).

In this work, we investigate the non-perturbative corrections and modularity in N=1 Type IIB compactifications. Our research aims to address the problem of understanding the non-perturbative corrections to the effective superpotential in four-dimensional N=1 supergravity models with Type IIB string compactifications. We seek to explore how modular invariance and non-perturbative effects are related in these models and how this relationship can be used to gain insights into the underlying physics of compactifications.

The key idea of our research is to use the framework of mirror symmetry to study the non-perturbative corrections in Type IIB compactifications. We show how these non-perturbative corrections can be understood in terms of modular forms, which are functions that transform in a specific way under modular transformations. Our methodology involves a combination of mirror symmetry, modular forms, and supergravity computations.

Our research is motivated by the desire to deepen our understanding of the physics of string compactifications and to explore the connections between different areas of mathematics and physics. We aim to shed light on the role of modularity in string theory and to uncover the symmetries underlying the non-perturbative effects that appear in string compactifications. Our research has important implications for the study of supersymmetric field theories and their applications in high-energy physics.

In our work titled "Generation interval contraction and epidemic data analysis," we propose a novel approach to the problem of estimating the generation interval in an infectious disease outbreak and its subsequent impact on epidemic data analysis. The generation interval represents the time between the onset of symptoms in a primary case and its secondary transmission to a new case. We demonstrate that this interval can be affected by the presence of asymptomatic and presymptomatic cases, which have been previously overlooked in classical epidemiological models. 

Our methodology relies on a Susceptible-Infected-Recovered (SIR) modeling framework that incorporates the presence of asymptomatic and presymptomatic cases in the generation interval estimation. We apply our approach to the COVID-19 pandemic in China and Italy and show how the estimation of the generation interval can vary depending on the inclusion of asymptomatic and presymptomatic cases.

The problem our work attempts to solve is the lack of accurate estimation of the generation interval and its impact on parameter estimation, outbreak control strategies, and epidemic forecasting. Our results indicate that the contraction of the generation interval due to the presence of asymptomatic and presymptomatic cases can result in faster epidemic growth rates and wider epidemic spread.

In conclusion, we present a new perspective on the generation interval estimation in infectious disease outbreaks and demonstrate how its contraction can affect epidemic data analysis. Our methodology provides valuable insights into the dynamics of the COVID-19 pandemic and can inform public health interventions aimed at outbreak control.

In this work, we present a numerical study of the $\Delta H(M, \Delta M)$ method for determining the intrinsic switching field distribution (ISFD) in perpendicular recording media. The ISFD reflects the statistical distribution of magnetic anisotropy energies in the medium and is a critical parameter for the design of high-density magnetic recording media. The $\Delta H(M, \Delta M)$ method is based on the measurement of the differential field $\Delta H$ required to switch the magnetization of a small magnetic element with a given magnitude of the reversal field $M$ and a given angle $\Delta M$ between the precessional and reversal fields. 

Our study reveals that the $\Delta H(M, \Delta M)$ method can determine the ISFD with accuracy comparable to the more established first-order reversal curve (FORC) method. Moreover, by exploring a range of $\Delta M$ values, the $\Delta H(M, \Delta M)$ method can provide additional information on the magnetic relaxation processes in the medium. We demonstrate the utility of the $\Delta H(M, \Delta M)$ method for a model medium with a log-normal ISFD and a distribution of magnetic interactions. By comparing the results of the $\Delta H(M, \Delta M)$ and FORC methods, we show that the former can provide complementary insights into the magnetic behavior of recording media. Overall, our study highlights the potential of the $\Delta H(M, \Delta M)$ method for characterizing the magnetic properties of advanced recording media.

Recent rapid localizations of short, hard gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) by the
Swift and HETE satellites have led to the observation of the first afterglows
and the measurement of the first redshifts from this type of burst. Detection
of >100 GeV counterparts would place powerful constraints on GRB mechanisms.
Seventeen short duration (< 5 s) GRBs detected by satellites occurred within
the field of view of the Milagro gamma-ray observatory between 2000 January and
2006 December. We have searched the Milagro data for >100 GeV counterparts to
these GRBs and find no significant emission correlated with these bursts. Due
to the absorption of high-energy gamma rays by the extragalactic background
light (EBL), detections are only expected for redshifts less than ~0.5. While
most long duration GRBs occur at redshifts higher than 0.5, the opposite is
thought to be true of short GRBs. Lack of a detected VHE signal thus allows
setting meaningful fluence limits. One GRB in the sample (050509b) has a likely
association with a galaxy at a redshift of 0.225, while another (051103) has
been tentatively linked to the nearby galaxy M81. Fluence limits are corrected
for EBL absorption, either using the known measured redshift, or computing the
corresponding absorption for a redshift of 0.1 and 0.5, as well as for the case
of z=0.

A digraph is $m$-labelled if every arc is labelled by an integer in $\{1,
\dots,m\}$. Motivated by wavelength assignment for multicasts in optical
networks, we introduce and study $n$-fibre colourings of labelled digraphs.
These are colourings of the arcs of $D$ such that at each vertex $v$, and for
each colour $\alpha$, $in(v,\alpha)+out(v,\alpha)\leq n$ with $in(v,\alpha)$
the number of arcs coloured $\alpha$ entering $v$ and $out(v,\alpha)$ the
number of labels $l$ such that there is at least one arc of label $l$ leaving
$v$ and coloured with $\alpha$. The problem is to find the minimum number of
colours $\lambda_n(D)$ such that the $m$-labelled digraph $D$ has an $n$-fibre
colouring. In the particular case when $D$ is $1$-labelled, $\lambda_1(D)$ is
called the directed star arboricity of $D$, and is denoted by $dst(D)$. We
first show that $dst(D)\leq 2\Delta^-(D)+1$, and conjecture that if
$\Delta^-(D)\geq 2$, then $dst(D)\leq 2\Delta^-(D)$. We also prove that for a
subcubic digraph $D$, then $dst(D)\leq 3$, and that if $\Delta^+(D),
\Delta^-(D)\leq 2$, then $dst(D)\leq 4$. Finally, we study
$\lambda_n(m,k)=\max\{\lambda_n(D) \tq D \mbox{is $m$-labelled} \et
\Delta^-(D)\leq k\}$. We show that if $m\geq n$, then $\ds
\left\lceil\frac{m}{n}\left\lceil \frac{k}{n}\right\rceil + \frac{k}{n}
\right\rceil\leq \lambda_n(m,k) \leq\left\lceil\frac{m}{n}\left\lceil
\frac{k}{n}\right\rceil + \frac{k}{n} \right\rceil + C \frac{m^2\log k}{n}$ for
some constant $C$. We conjecture that the lower bound should be the right value
of $\lambda_n(m,k)$.

In this study, we investigated global and regional brain metabolic scaling and its functional consequences. We aimed to explore how brain metabolism scales with different brain regions and how it affects brain functions. Our methodology included a comprehensive analysis of published glucose metabolic data of various brain regions from different mammalian species. We used a scaling model and functional characterization techniques to analyze the data.

Our analysis revealed that brain metabolism follows an allometric scaling pattern, where small changes in brain size significantly alter metabolic rates. We observed that brain metabolic scaling is not uniform across brain regions, with some regions showing higher metabolic rates than others. Furthermore, we discovered that these differential metabolic rates have important functional consequences, such as affecting the processing speed and energy consumption of different brain regions.

Our findings offer a new perspective on how the brain functions as a complex network of interacting regions. Our study provides a framework to better understand the relationship between brain size, metabolic rates, and functional outcomes. The results of this work may have implications for understanding brain evolution and inform therapeutic interventions for brain disorders. Overall, this study highlights the importance of considering both global and regional brain metabolism in understanding brain function.

We present high-resolution, multiwavelength continuum and molecular-line
images of the massive star forming region IRAS 18317-0757. The IR through mm
spectral energy distribution can be approximated by a two-temperature model (25
and 63 K) with a total luminosity of approximately log(L/Lsun)=5.2. Previous
submm imaging resolved this region into a cluster of 5 dust cores, one of which
is associated with the UCHII region G23.96+0.15, and another with an H2O maser.
In our new 2.7 mm continuum image, only the UCHII region is detected, with
total flux and morphology in good agreement with the free-free emission in VLA
cm-wave maps. For the other four objects, the nondetections at 2.7 mm and in
the MSX mid-IR bands are consistent with cool dust emission with a temperature
of 13-40K and luminosity of 1000-40000 Lsun. By combining single-dish and
interferometric data, we have identified over two dozen virialized C18O cores
in this region that contain ~40% of the total molecular gas mass present. While
the overall extent of the C18O and dust emission is similar, the emission peaks
do not correlate well in detail. At least 11 of the 123 stars identified by
2MASS in this region are likely to be within the star-forming cluster. Two
stars (both associated with the UCHII region) were previously identified as O
stars via IR spectroscopy. Most of the rest of the reddened stars have no
obvious correlation with the C18O cores or the dust cores. In summary, our
observations indicate that considerable fragmentation of the molecular cloud
has taken place during the time required for the UCHII region to form and the O
stars to become detectable at IR wavelengths. Additional star formation appears
to be ongoing on the periphery of the central region, where up to four B-type
(proto)stars have formed among a substantial number of C18O molecular cores.

In this work, we present GEMS, a publicly available catalog of galaxy fitting parameters obtained by testing a suite of parametric fitting codes on simulated data. The aim of this study is to provide a benchmark for galaxy fitting codes that will enable astronomers to select the code best suited to their scientific aims.

To achieve this goal, we generated simulated galaxies with varying morphological characteristics and then fit them using five widely-used parametric fitting codes: GALFIT, GIM2D, SExtractor, ACS GALFIT, and the Fitting and Assessment of Synthetic Templates code (FAST; a new code developed for this study). We tested the codes on realism, completeness, and repeatability and found that all codes perform well on bright galaxies with simple morphologies. However, faint and complex galaxies pose significant challenges for all codes, with GALFIT and ACS GALFIT performing best overall.

The GEMS catalog contains fitting parameters for 173 object simulations generated with 9 morphological templates, covering a range of magnitude, flux, and galaxy sizes and inclination angles. These simulated galaxies represent diverse galaxy populations from local to high-redshift universe and different signal-to-noise ratios. GEMS also provides data on the corresponding input parameters for each simulated galaxy, including total magnitudes and half-light radii, enabling validation of the accuracy of the outputs of galaxy fitting codes.

Overall, GEMS provides a unique resource for those interested in comparing and improving existing parametric fitting codes, as well as for those developing new algorithms suitable for galaxy fitting. This work contributes to enhancing the accuracy of photometric measurements and demonstrating their reliability.

The electronic correlations on a C$_{20}$ molecule, as described by an
extended Hubbard Hamiltonian with a nearest neighbor Coulomb interaction of
strength $V$, are studied using quantum Monte Carlo and exact diagonalization
methods. For electron doped C$_{20}$, it is known that pair-binding arising
from a purely electronic mechanism is absent within the standard Hubbard model
(V=0). Here we show that this is also the case for hole doping for $0<U/t\leq
3$ and that, for both electron and hole doping, the effect of a non-zero $V$ is
to work against pair-binding. We also study the magnetic properties of the
neutral molecule, and find transitions between spin singlet and triplet ground
states for either fixed $U$ or $V$ values. In addition, spin, charge and
pairing correlation functions on C$_{20}$ are computed. The spin-spin and
charge-charge correlations are very short-range, although a weak enhancement in
the pairing correlation is observed for a distance equal to the molecular
diameter.

We present new imaging and spectroscopic observations of the fields of five
QSOs with very strong intervening CaII absorption systems at redshifts z<0.5
selected from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey. Recent studies of these very rare
absorbers indicate that they may be related to damped Lyman alpha systems
(DLAs). In all five cases we identify a galaxy at the redshift of the CaII
system with impact parameters up to ~24 kpc. In four out of five cases the
galaxies are luminous (L ~L*), metal-rich (Z ~Zsun), massive (velocity
dispersion, sigma ~100 km/s) spirals. Their star formation rates, deduced from
Halpha emission, are high, in the range SFR = 0.3 - 30 Msun/yr. In our
analysis, we paid particular attention to correcting the observed emission line
fluxes for stellar absorption and dust extinction. We show that these effects
are important for a correct SFR estimate; their neglect in previous low-z
studies of DLA-selected galaxies has probably led to an underestimate of the
star formation activity in at least some DLA hosts. We discuss possible links
between CaII-selected galaxies and DLAs and outline future observations which
will help clarify the relationship between these different classes of QSO
absorbers.

In this revised version of our work titled, "On monotonicity, stability, and construction of central schemes for hyperbolic conservation laws with source terms", we explore the problem of constructing numerical schemes to solve hyperbolic conservation laws with source terms, a class of partial differential equations that arise in the modeling of various physical phenomena, such as fluid dynamics, gas dynamics, and traffic flow. The main challenge in constructing such schemes is to ensure the accuracy, stability, and monotonicity of the solution while preserving important physical properties of the system, such as conservation and positivity. To address these issues, we propose a new family of central schemes which are monotonicity-preserving, stable, and accurate under a wide range of conditions. In particular, we show that our schemes satisfy a discrete maximum principle, which guarantees the non-negativity of the solution. We also provide a detailed analysis of the stability properties of the schemes and compare them to other existing methods. Our key ideas and methodology are based on combining the benefits of central schemes with new numerical fluxes and limiting techniques. Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed approach in solving a variety of challenging problems in hyperbolic conservation laws with source terms.

It remains an open question how magnetic energy is rapidly released in the
solar corona so as to create solar explosions such as solar flares and coronal
mass ejections (CMEs). Recent studies have confirmed that a system consisting
of a flux rope embedded in a background field exhibits a catastrophic behavior,
and the energy threshold at the catastrophic point may exceed the associated
open field energy. The accumulated free energy in the corona is abruptly
released when the catastrophe takes place, and it probably serves as the main
means of energy release for CMEs at least in the initial phase. Such a release
proceeds via an ideal MHD process in contrast with nonideal ones such as
magnetic reconnection. The catastrophe results in a sudden formation of
electric current sheets, which naturally provide proper sites for fast magnetic
reconnection. The reconnection may be identified with a solar flare associated
with the CME on one hand, and produces a further acceleration of the CME on the
other. On this basis, several preliminary suggestions are made for future
observational investigations, especially with the proposed KuaFu satellites, on
the roles of the MHD catastrophe and magnetic reconnection in the magnetic
energy release associated with CMEs and flares.

We present an original method of digitizing and astrometrically reducing
"Carte du Ciel" plate material using an inexpensive flatbed scanner, to
demonstrate that for this material there is an alternative to more specialized
measuring machines that are very few in number and thus not readily available.
The sample of plates chosen to develop this method are original "Carte du Ciel"
plates of the San Fernando zone, photographic material with a mean epoch
1903.6, and a limiting photographic magnitude ~14.5, covering the declination
range of -10 < dec < -2. Digitization has been made using a commercial flatbed
scanner, demonstrating the internal precision that can be attained with such a
device. A variety of post-scan corrections are shown to be necessary. In
particular, the large distortion introduced by the non-uniform action of the
scanner is modelled using multiple scans of each plate. We also tackle the
specific problems associated with the triple-exposure images on some plates and
the grid lines present on all. The final measures are reduced to celestial
coordinates using the Tycho-2 Catalogue. The internal precision obtained over a
single plate, 3microns ~ 0.18" in each axis, is comparable to what is realized
with similar plate material using slower, less affordable, and less widely
available conventional measuring machines, such as a PDS microdensitometer. The
accuracy attained over large multi-plate areas, employing an overlapping plate
technique, is estimated at 0.2".

The focus of our work is on Brane Tilings, a mathematical concept that has been extensively studied in theoretical physics. Our study aims to explore the mathematical implications of Brane Tilings, with the objective of providing a better understanding of the structure and properties of these objects.

The research problem we addressed is the lack of a comprehensive mathematical framework for Brane Tilings. Despite their importance in string theory, Brane Tilings have not been fully understood from a mathematical perspective. Our work attempts to address this gap by developing a rigorous mathematical theory that encapsulates the structure and properties of Brane Tilings.

Our research draws on tools and techniques from algebraic geometry, combinatorics, and representation theory. We develop a mathematical theory that describes the geometric and algebraic properties of Brane Tilings, and demonstrate its effectiveness through various examples. In particular, we show that our theory can be used to compute various physical quantities, such as the number of supersymmetric vacua in a gauge theory.

The motivation for our research is to contribute to the ongoing effort to bridge the gap between physics and mathematics. Our work provides a platform for future research in this area, and opens up avenues for exploring the mathematical structures that underpin the physical world.

We have developed an efficient photometric technique for identifying young
early-type galaxy candidates using a combination of photometric redshifts,
spectral-type classification, and optical/near-infrared colors. Applying our
technique to the GOODS HST/ACS and VLT/ISAAC data we have selected a complete
and homogeneous sample of young elliptical candidates among early-type field
galaxies. The distribution of structural parameters for these candidates shows
that their selection, which is based on early spectral types, is fully
consistent with early morphological types. We investigate the evolution of
their luminosities and colors as a function of redshift and galaxy mass and
find evidence for an increasing starburst mass fraction in these young
early-type galaxy candidates at higher redshifts, which we interpret in terms
of massive field galaxies experiencing more massive/intense starbursts at
higher redshifts. Moreover, we find indications for a systematically larger
young elliptical fraction among sub-L*/2 early-type galaxies compared to their
brighter counterparts. The total fraction among the field early-type galaxies
increases with redshift, irrespective of galaxy luminosity. Our results are
most consistent with galaxy formation scenarios in which stars in massive
early-type field galaxies are assembled earlier than in their low-mass
counterparts.

In this work, we present a new method of integrating stochastic differential equations on Lie groups using stochastic Lie group integrators. Our method is motivated by the difficulty of numerically solving stochastic differential equations on non-Euclidean manifolds such as Lie groups, which arise frequently in applications such as robotics and control theory. Current approaches using the Euler-Maruyama scheme suffer from the fact that they do not preserve the geometric structure of the Lie group, leading to numerical errors that accumulate over time.

Our method overcomes this issue by constructing stochastic integrators that are exactly geometric, allowing us to simulate stochastic processes on Lie groups with high accuracy and long-term stability. We provide a theoretical analysis of our method and show that it possesses desirable properties such as strong convergence. Furthermore, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach by applying it to practical problems such as simulating the motion of a rigid body in a viscous fluid.

Our results show that the stochastic Lie group integrators offer a practical and efficient method for simulating stochastic differential equations on Lie groups. The numerical experiments conducted revealed that our approach provides more accurate approximations than existing methods, with lower computational costs. This work provides a valuable contribution to the field of stochastic analysis on manifolds and opens up new avenues for applications in areas such as control theory, robotics, and machine learning.

In this personal historical perspective, we reflect on the mass loss and evolution of stars and star clusters, influenced by our own research experiences and collaborations with experts in the field. The exploration covers the key ideas and methodologies that have guided the study of stars’ evolution, beginning with the seminal work by Edwin Salpeter. We describe how, during the 1970s and 1980s, a shift occurred towards the inclusion of mass loss as one of the key drivers of stellar evolution, leading ultimately to the newer perspective of clusters as the natural crucibles for star formation. 

Our work has resulted in several key findings that help contribute to the ongoing discourse in this field. We document the evolution of stars up to more massive endpoints and explore how highly massive AC-stellar systems could become the source of gamma-ray bursts, among other things. Additionally, we examine the role of binary stars governed by mass-transfer in the context of star cluster evolution. Finally, we reflect on some of the remaining enigmas in the field, perhaps most notably the stunted formation of massive stars and its intersections with observed star clusters. This work aims to delineate a complete picture of the interwoven mechanics of star evolution.
