To achieve our goals, we used astrometric data from the Gaia mission, which provides trigonometric parallax measurements of unprecedented accuracy for a large sample of stars in the Milky Way. We selected a sample of white dwarf candidates based on their photometry and proper motions, and obtained parallax measurements for 14 objects, thus confirming their white dwarf nature. We analyzed the properties of the sample, including their space motions, Galactic orbits, and Galactic distributions. Our results indicate that these high velocity halo white dwarf candidates are indeed members of the Galactic halo population, with most of them originating from the inner halo and having relatively old ages. Our findings contribute to the ongoing efforts to understand the formation and evolution of the Milky Way and its stellar populations.

In this work, we aim to gauge the hidden symmetries in two-dimensional systems. The problem we attempt to solve is to reveal previously unobserved symmetries in physical systems by introducing a new method of analysis. Our approach utilizes the concept of topological invariants, which are measures of symmetry that are insensitive to local changes in the system. These invariants allow us to identify the presence of hidden symmetries that may not be readily apparent in the system's physical properties.

Through our research, we have developed a new algorithm that can calculate topological invariants without requiring detailed knowledge of the system's properties. Using this algorithm, we have been able to demonstrate the presence of additional symmetries in a wide range of systems, including electronic wave functions, geometric patterns, and biological systems.

Our research is motivated by the desire to gain a deeper understanding of the fundamental principles that underlie the behavior of complex systems. By uncovering hidden symmetries, we aim to shed light on the underlying structure of these systems and open up new avenues for scientific inquiry. In addition, our work has practical applications in fields such as materials science, where the discovery of new symmetries can lead to the development of novel materials with unique properties.

In this study, we investigate the branching ratios and CP asymmetries of B meson decays into a1(1260)π and a1(1260)K final states. Our motivation for this research stems from the need to understand the fundamental symmetry properties of the strong interaction. We aim to explore the differences between the decays into the two final states in order to shed light on the dynamics of the strong interaction. 

To carry out this research, we use the heavy quark expansion (HQE) method to calculate the branching ratios and CP asymmetries. Our approach is based on the factorization approximation, which allows us to separate the long and short-distance contributions to the decay amplitudes. The framework also enables us to incorporate various hadronic parameters and Wilson coefficients that contribute to the decay processes. 

The problem that our work attempts to solve is to provide a more precise understanding of the dynamics of B meson decays. The study of these decays is crucial for understanding CP violation phenomena and the search for new physics beyond the standard model. Our results show that there are significant differences between the branching ratios and CP asymmetries of the B -> a1(1260)π and B -> a1(1260)K decays, which could provide further insight into the underlying physics of these processes.

In this study, we investigate the formation of bipolar lobes and disks in a rotating surface explosion, and explore the underlying mechanisms that govern the formation of these structures. By conducting numerical simulations, we track the motion of a fluid layer that is set into rotation and then subjected to a sudden explosion from below. Our results reveal that the explosion causes a vertical outflow of material, which generates vortices that stretch the fluid layer into a disk-shaped structure. Furthermore, the rotation of the fluid layer drives a secondary flow that produces two symmetric outflows, giving rise to a bipolar lobe structure. The formation of these structures is driven by a complex interplay of various physical processes, including fluid-dynamic instabilities, rotation, and buoyancy.

The motivation behind this research lies in understanding the physical processes that shape the structures of explosions in astrophysical environments. These structures play a crucial role in regulating the energy and momentum transport in such environments, and thus impact the evolution of galaxies, stars, and planets. Our work attempts to solve the problem of understanding the fundamental physical mechanisms that underlie the formation of these structures, with potential implications for a range of astrophysical phenomena.

This paper and its prequel ("Part I") prove a generalization of the usual
gluing theorem for two index 1 pseudoholomorphic curves U_+ and U_- in the
symplectization of a contact 3-manifold. We assume that for each embedded Reeb
orbit gamma, the total multiplicity of the negative ends of U_+ at covers of
gamma agrees with the total multiplicity of the positive ends of U_- at covers
of gamma. However, unlike in the usual gluing story, here the individual
multiplicities are allowed to differ. In this situation, one can often glue U_+
and U_- to an index 2 curve by inserting genus zero branched covers of
R-invariant cylinders between them. This paper shows that the signed count of
such gluings equals a signed count of zeroes of a certain section of an
obstruction bundle over the moduli space of branched covers of the cylinder.
Part I obtained a combinatorial formula for the latter count and, assuming the
result of the present paper, deduced that the differential d in embedded
contact homology satisfies d^2=0. The present paper completes all of the
analysis that was needed in Part I. The gluing technique explained here is in
principle applicable to more gluing problems. We also prove some lemmas
concerning the generic behavior of pseudoholomorphic curves in
symplectizations, which may be of independent interest.

This paper deals with a family of spatial rational curves that were
introduced by Andradas, Recio and Sendra, under the name of hypercircles, as an
algorithmic cornerstone tool in the context of improving the rational
parametrization (simplifying the coefficients of the rational functions, when
possible) of algebraic varieties. A real circle can be defined as the image of
the real axis under a Moebius transformation in the complex field. Likewise,
and roughly speaking, a hypercircle can be defined as the image of a line ("the
${\mathbb{K}}$-axis") in a $n$-degree finite algebraic extension
$\mathbb{K}(\alpha)\thickapprox\mathbb{K}^n$ under the transformation
$\frac{at+b}{ct+d}:\mathbb{K}(\alpha)\to\mathbb{K}(\alpha)$.
  The aim of this article is to extend, to the case of hypercircles, some of
the specific properties of circles. We show that hypercircles are precisely,
via $\mathbb{K}$-projective transformations, the rational normal curve of a
suitable degree. We also obtain a complete description of the points at
infinity of these curves (generalizing the cyclic structure at infinity of
circles). We characterize hypercircles as those curves of degree equal to the
dimension of the ambient affine space and with infinitely many
${\mathbb{K}}$-rational points, passing through these points at infinity.
Moreover, we give explicit formulae for the parametrization and implicitation
of hypercircles. Besides the intrinsic interest of this very special family of
curves, the understanding of its properties has a direct application to the
simplification of parametrizations problem, as shown in the last section.

In this work, we investigate the thermal effects on nuclear symmetry energy using a momentum-dependent effective interaction. The nuclear symmetry energy is an essential parameter describing the behavior of matter in extreme conditions, such as those present in neutron stars and heavy-ion collisions. However, the effects of temperature on the nuclear symmetry energy are not yet fully understood. 

To address this issue, we employ a reaction-diffusion model that incorporates the momentum-dependent effective interaction to investigate the impact of temperature on the nuclear symmetry energy. Our model incorporates the role of temperature on the kinetic and potential energies of nucleons, which we find to be important for descriptions of nuclear systems at high temperatures. 

Our results indicate a strong dependence of the nuclear symmetry energy on temperature, with a decrease in the symmetry energy at high temperatures. We also find that the momentum-dependent interaction enhances the temperature dependence of the symmetry energy. Our work contributes to a better understanding of the behavior of nuclear matter at high temperatures, which is essential for modeling astrophysical phenomena such as supernova explosions and neutron star mergers.

In this study, we present the results of our investigation into obscured accretion in the Chandra Deep Field South. Our primary goal was to identify and study the properties of active galactic nuclei (AGN) that were previously undetected due to obscuration by dust and gas. 

To achieve this, we used data gathered by the Chandra X-ray Observatory, which allowed us to detect the X-ray emission from AGN that would otherwise be concealed by dust. We also employed a variety of statistical techniques, including Bayesian analysis, to distinguish the X-ray emission from AGN from that of other sources.

Our findings provide evidence for obscured AGN activity at redshifts up to z ∼ 1.8, with over 200 detections in the hard X-ray band. This suggests that obscured AGN may play a more significant role in the cosmic growth of supermassive black holes than previously thought, particularly in the early universe. 

Our study sheds light on the potential contribution of obscured AGN to black hole growth and the evolution of galaxies, which are critical issues in modern astrophysics. Our results have significant implications for future studies of AGN and black hole growth and will help to refine our understanding of galaxy evolution.

We analyze the small-scale clustering in "MegaZ-LRG", a large
photometric-redshift catalogue of Luminous Red Galaxies extracted from the
imaging dataset of the Sloan Digital Sky Survey. MegaZ-LRG, presented in a
companion paper, spans the redshift range 0.4 < z < 0.7 with an r.m.s. redshift
error dz ~ 0.03(1+z), covering 5,914 deg^2 to map out a total cosmic volume 2.5
h^-3 Gpc^3. In this study we use 380,000 photometric redshifts to measure
significant deviations from the canonical power-law fit to the angular
correlation function in a series of narrow redshift slices, in which we
construct volume-limited samples. These deviations are direct signatures of the
manner in which these galaxies populate the underlying network of dark matter
haloes. We cleanly delineate the separate contributions of the "1-halo" and
"2-halo" clustering terms and fit our measurements by parameterizing the halo
occupation distribution N(M) of the galaxies. Our results are successfully fit
by a "central" galaxy contribution with a "soft" transition from zero to one
galaxies, combined with a power-law "satellite" galaxy component, the slope of
which is a strong function of galaxy luminosity. The large majority of galaxies
are classified as central objects of their host dark matter haloes rather than
satellites in more massive systems. The effective halo mass of MegaZ-LRG
galaxies lies in the range log_10 (M_eff / h^-1 M_sol) = 13.61 - 13.8
(increasing with redshift, assuming large-scale normalization sigma_8 = 0.8)
for corresponding number densities in the range n_g = 5.03 - 0.56 x 10^-4 h^3
Mpc^-3. Our results confirm the usefulness of the halo model for gaining
physical insight into the patterns of galaxy clustering.

A quantum computer is a machine that can perform certain calculations much
faster than a classical computer by using the laws of quantum mechanics.
Quantum computers do not exist yet, because it is extremely difficult to
control quantum mechanical systems to the necessary degree. What is more, we do
at this moment not know which physical system is the best suited for making a
quantum computer (although we have some ideas). It is likely that a mature
quantum information processing technology will use (among others) light,
because photons are ideal carriers for quantum information. These notes are an
expanded version of the five lectures I gave on the possibility of making a
quantum computer using light, at the Summer School in Theoretical Physics in
Durban, 14-24 January, 2007. There are quite a few proposals using light for
quantum computing, and I can highlight only a few here. I will focus on
photonic qubits, and leave out continuous variables completely. I assume that
the reader is familiar with basic quantum mechanics and introductory quantum
computing.

In this paper, we present our work on web data modeling for integration in data warehouses. The aim of our research is to solve the problem of effectively integrating web data, which is voluminous, heterogeneous, and semi-structured, with traditional data warehouse systems that are designed for structured data. 

To achieve this goal, we propose a data modeling approach that extends the existing star schema model to incorporate web data features such as XML attributes and hierarchical structures. We also propose a method for automatically generating ETL processes that extract, transform, and load web data into the data warehouse.

Our approach has been evaluated on a real-world case study of a large e-commerce website. The results show that our approach is effective in integrating web data with traditional data warehouse systems. We also demonstrate the feasibility of our approach by presenting a prototype implementation.

The motivation for our research is the increasing importance of web data in decision-making processes. As more and more data is generated on the web, it becomes crucial to integrate web data with traditional data warehouse systems to provide comprehensive insights for decision makers. Our work contributes to this area by proposing a data modeling approach that enables effective integration of web data into data warehouses.

In this work, we investigate the fundamental limits of position determination using concentration gradients. The ability to determine the position of cells or particles is important in many areas of biological and physical sciences. However, conventional methods such as fluorescence microscopy or magnetic manipulation may have limitations in terms of spatial resolution, sensitivity, or applicability to certain samples. To overcome these limitations, researchers have explored using concentration gradients of chemical species as a means of detecting positional changes. Previous works have shown that concentration gradients have potential in this regard, but their fundamental limits were unclear. Therefore, we set out to investigate the best possible accuracy and spatial resolution achievable using concentration gradients.

Using a combination of analytical and numerical methods, we established a theoretical framework for calculating the fundamental limits of position determination by concentration gradients. Our analysis revealed that the achievable accuracy and spatial resolution is limited by the diffusion length of the gradient, the signal-to-noise ratio, and the number of particles or cells in the sample. We also demonstrated that optimizing the system parameters and using efficient detection schemes could substantially improve the performance.

Our work sheds new light on the potential of concentration gradients as a tool for high-precision position determination in biological and physical systems. Our findings could guide the development of new techniques for cell tracking, drug delivery, and microscale manipulation.

We extract groups of galaxies from the SDSS Data Release 5 with the purpose
of studying the supercluster-void network and environmental properties of
groups therein. We use a modified friends-of-friends (FoF) method with adopted
variable linking length in transverse and radial direction to eliminate
selection effects and to find reliably as many groups as possible to track the
supercluster network. We take into account various selection effects due to the
use of a magnitude limited sample. To determine linking length scaling we study
the luminosity-density relation in observed groups. We follow the changes in
group sizes and mean galaxy number densities within groups when shifting nearby
groups to larger distances. As a result we show that the linking length should
be a slowly growing function with distance. Our final sample contains 17143
groups in the equatorial, and 33219 groups in the northern part of the DR5
survey with membership N_g >= 2. The group catalogue is available at our
web-site http://www.obs.ee/~erik/index.html.
  Due to a narrow magnitude window in the SDSS the group catalogue based on
this survey has been obtained by moderately growing linking length scaling law
up to redshift z = 0.12. Above this redshift the scaling law turns down. In the
redshift range z=0.12 - 0.2 only the cores are detected. Along with applying
weights when calculating luminosities it is possible to use groups for
determination of the large-scale luminosity-density field.

In this study, we investigate the electronic structure of BaFeO3 using ab initio density functional theory (DFT). The motivation behind our research is due to the fact that BaFeO3 is a promising material for various applications, including catalysis, oxygen sensors, and fuel cells. Thus, a thorough understanding of its electronic structure is crucial.

Our results show that the ground state of BaFeO3 is antiferromagnetic with an indirect bandgap of 1.94 eV. We find that the valence band maximum is mainly composed of O 2p states, while the conduction band minimum is dominated by Fe 3d and O 2p states. Our analysis of the density of states suggests that the presence of Fe-O hybridization leads to a relatively high electronic density at the Fermi level which is associated with the metallic behavior of BaFeO3 under certain conditions.

Our key methodology is based on the use of DFT, which is a state-of-the-art computational tool that allows us to calculate the electronic structure of materials from first principles. Additionally, the ab initio approach provides a more accurate description of the electronic structure compared to empirically fitted models.

In conclusion, our ab initio DFT study provides valuable insights into the electronic structure of BaFeO3. Our results provide a framework for further investigations of the material's electronic and magnetic properties, which can be useful for designing and optimizing its applications in various fields.

Recently, much research has been carried out on Hamiltonians that are not
Hermitian but are symmetric under space-time reflection, that is, Hamiltonians
that exhibit PT symmetry. Investigations of the Sturm-Liouville eigenvalue
problem associated with such Hamiltonians have shown that in many cases the
entire energy spectrum is real and positive and that the eigenfunctions form an
orthogonal and complete basis. Furthermore, the quantum theories determined by
such Hamiltonians have been shown to be consistent in the sense that the
probabilities are positive and the dynamical trajectories are unitary. However,
the geometrical structures that underlie quantum theories formulated in terms
of such Hamiltonians have hitherto not been fully understood. This paper
studies in detail the geometric properties of a Hilbert space endowed with a
parity structure and analyses the characteristics of a PT-symmetric Hamiltonian
and its eigenstates. A canonical relationship between a PT-symmetric operator
and a Hermitian operator is established. It is shown that the quadratic form
corresponding to the parity operator, in particular, gives rise to a natural
partition of the Hilbert space into two halves corresponding to states having
positive and negative PT norm. The indefiniteness of the norm can be
circumvented by introducing a symmetry operator C that defines a positive
definite inner product by means of a CPT conjugation operation.

In this study, we investigate the SW Sextantis stars, which are considered the dominant population of Cataclysmic Variables (CVs) with orbital periods between 3-4 hours. The SW Sextantis stars are characterized by their high-state behaviour and complex light curves that exhibit an intricate array of periodic variations. Our study aims to understand the physical mechanisms behind these unique features of SW Sextantis stars and their relationship with the observed orbital periods.

By analyzing a large sample of SW Sextantis stars using observational data from the Catalina Sky Survey, we found that the majority of these stars exhibit a positive correlation between their outburst intervals and the logarithm of their orbital periods. This observation implies that the dominant mode of mass transfer in these systems is driven by gravitational radiation, which is the dominant mechanism for the majority of short-period CVs. 

Our results shed light on the formation and evolution of CVs, and provide a new perspective in understanding the physical processes that govern their behaviour. This study contributes to the field of observational astronomy and provides critical insights for improving our understanding of the complex dynamics and evolution of CVs.

In this study, we investigate the alignment and signed-intensity anomalies observed in the Wilkinson Microwave Anisotropy Probe (WMAP) data. Our methodology involves analyzing the temperature and polarization maps obtained from WMAP data to determine the significance of these anomalies. Specifically, we examine the alignment of the quadrupole and octupole moments in the temperature and polarization maps, as well as the hemispherical power asymmetry and the dipole modulation in the temperature map.

Our results show that the alignment and signed-intensity anomalies in WMAP data are statistically significant, with the most striking result being the alignment of the quadrupole and octupole moments in both temperature and polarization maps. These findings suggest a possible violation of isotropy in the cosmic microwave background, which has important implications for our understanding of the early Universe and the formation of large-scale structures.

This work attempts to address the problem of understanding the origin and significance of the anomalies observed in WMAP data. By highlighting the statistical significance of these anomalies, we provide evidence for potential violations of the standard cosmological model. Our findings have important implications for the development of alternative models and theories that can better explain these observations.

In this work, we provide evidence for the evolution of early-type galaxies in the GOODS/CDF-S field. Our study focuses on the analysis of the rest-frame colors and masses of galaxies, obtained through deep optical and near-infrared imaging. We investigate the redshift evolution of the population of early-type galaxies, and its dependence on mass and environment. Our methodology involves the use of a large sample of high-quality photometric redshifts and a well-defined selection of early-type systems based on structural parameters.

Our motivation for this study is to shed light on the formation and evolution of massive galaxies, a fundamental question in modern astrophysics. Early-type galaxies represent a crucial piece in this puzzle, as they are typically old, quiescent systems with prominent bulges, believed to have formed through a combination of in-situ and merger-driven processes. By probing the evolution of these systems as a function of redshift and mass, we can test theoretical models and gain insights into the physical mechanisms driving the observed trends.

Our results indicate that the number density of early-type galaxies increases significantly with redshift, and this evolution is more pronounced for lower-mass systems. We also find that the fraction of early-type galaxies in overdense regions is higher than in the field, suggesting that environmental processes may play a role in shaping the morphological properties of galaxies. Our findings are consistent with a scenario where early-type galaxies grow mainly through dry mergers, and their star formation activity is quenched over time.

In this paper, we investigate a problem of Yamabe type on compact spinorial manifolds. Our main objective is to study the conformal geometry of such manifolds by analyzing their spinorial structure and curvature. We establish several important results that shed light on the behavior of the Yamabe constant on these manifolds and the existence of extremal metrics in certain cases.

Our motivation for this research is twofold. On one hand, Yamabe-type problems have been a central topic in geometry for several decades, with applications ranging from topology to physics. On the other hand, compact spinorial manifolds are of great interest in the study of spin geometry, with important implications in theoretical physics.

To tackle this problem, we use a combination of geometric and analytic methods. We first establish some necessary conditions for the existence of extremal metrics on compact spinorial manifolds, based on a careful analysis of their spinorial curvature. We then develop a perturbation argument to construct extremal metrics in certain cases. Our results shed light on the conformal geometry of these manifolds and provide new insights into the interplay between spinor geometry and the Yamabe problem.

Overall, our work represents an important contribution to the study of Yamabe-type problems on compact spinorial manifolds, with potential applications in theoretical physics and other areas of mathematics.

In this work, we present a Dalitz plot analysis of the D+ to K-pi+pi+ decay, which was studied using the FOCUS experiment. The main objective of the study was to extract the phase-space parameters and decay amplitudes, as well as to explore the underlying dynamics of the decay process. Our analysis yielded a number of interesting results that shed light on the nature of the decay and the dynamics involved in the process.

One of the principal challenges in this work was to account for the contributions of higher-order resonances and intermediate states in the decay. To accomplish this, we used the Dalitz plot method, which allowed us to visualize the phase-space distribution of the final-state particles and to identify the presence of any intermediate states. We then employed a statistical analysis to extract the parameters and amplitudes from the data.

Our results showed that the D+ to K-pi+pi+ decay is dominated by the presence of the rho and f0(980) resonances, with some evidence for the presence of other intermediate states. We also found that the decay amplitude varies by a factor of up to two across different regions of the Dalitz plot. These findings provide new insights into the dynamics of this decay and may have implications for the study of other related processes.

Low energy supersymmetric models provide a solution to the hierarchy problem
and also have the necessary ingredients to solve two of the most outstanding
issues in cosmology: the origin of dark matter and baryonic matter. One of the
most attractive features of this framework is that the relevant physical
processes are related to interactions at the weak scale and therefore may be
tested in collider experiments in the near future. This is true for the Minimal
Supersymmetric Standard Model (MSSM) as well as for its extension with the
addition of one singlet chiral superfield, the so-called nMSSM. It has been
recently shown that within the nMSSM an elegant solution to both the problem of
baryogenesis and dark matter may be found, that relies mostly on the mixing of
the singlet sector with the Higgs sector of the theory. In this work we review
the nMSSM model constraints from cosmology and present the associated collider
phenomenology at the LHC and the ILC. We show that the ILC will efficiently
probe the neutralino, chargino and Higgs sectors, allowing to confront
cosmological observations with computations based on collider measurements. We
also investigate the prospects for a direct detection of dark matter and the
constraints imposed by the current bounds of the electron electric dipole
moment in this model.

We present our work on pairing based cooling of Fermi gases. The motivation for this research arises from the need to achieve lower temperatures and higher density in ultracold atomic gases. This field aims to study the behavior of gases at temperatures close to absolute zero, where classical physics can no longer accurately explain their behaviors. Studies have shown that Fermi gases can be cooled below their Fermi temperature by pairing fermions into composite bosons. 

The key idea behind our work is to make use of this pairing mechanism to cool the Fermi gas even further. We propose to control the interaction between the fermions and the composite bosons by tuning external magnetic fields. Our methodology involves using numerical simulations to study the thermalization time scale of the Bose-Fermi mixture, as well as the overall cooling efficiency. 

The problem we attempt to solve is the lack of efficient cooling methods for Fermi gases. Our pairing-based cooling scheme offers a new avenue to achieve this goal. By using composite bosons to reduce the number of fermions, we can increase the density of the gas for a given temperature. Our results show that this method is effective in cooling a Fermi gas to lower temperatures than traditional cooling methods. We believe that our work has the potential to contribute to the development of more efficient cooling methods for ultracold atomic gases.

In this work, we present a novel eigenvalue-based method for detecting high-dimensional signals in white noise using relatively few samples. The motivation behind this research is the challenge posed by high-dimensional signals in many practical applications, such as in biomedical imaging or signal processing. Existing methods often rely on large sample sizes, which can be computationally and financially expensive. 

Our method is based on subspace estimation techniques and utilizes a sample eigenvalue distribution to identify signal components. By exploiting the structure of the eigenvalues, we are able to achieve accurate detection with fewer samples than existing methods, reducing computational complexity and cost. 

We evaluate our method through theoretical analysis and simulations and demonstrate its superior performance compared to several state-of-the-art methods. Furthermore, we show that our method is robust to noise and is able to reliably detect very weak signals. 

Overall, our findings suggest that eigenvalue-based detection methods have significant potential for high-dimensional signal detection in many practical applications. The development of such methods can lead to more efficient and cost-effective detection strategies for complex signals, ultimately improving our ability to analyze and understand a wide range of phenomena.

In this work, we explore the concept of entrelacement d'alg\`ebres de Lie, also known as the wreath product of Lie algebras. Our main goal is to study the algebraic structures that arise from this product and understand its properties. 

Our research shows that the wreath product of Lie algebras provides a powerful tool for constructing new Lie algebras. We demonstrate this by presenting various examples of this product and analyzing their structure using group theory and representation theory. 

We also discuss the relationship between the wreath product of Lie algebras and other algebraic structures. Specifically, we establish connections between wreath products and the semidirect product, as well as the tensor product of Lie algebras. 

Furthermore, we investigate the applications of wreath products in mathematical physics, such as in the study of conformal field theory and vertex algebras. By analyzing these applications, we demonstrate the usefulness of our findings in a broader context. 

Overall, our work contributes to the understanding of the structure and properties of wreath products of Lie algebras, as well as their relationships with other algebraic structures. Our key ideas and methodology are based on rigorous mathematical proofs and calculations, which provide a solid foundation for future research in this area.

We initiate the systematic construction of gauged matter-coupled supergravity
theories in two dimensions. Subgroups of the affine global symmetry group of
toroidally compactified supergravity can be gauged by coupling vector fields
with minimal couplings and a particular topological term. The gauge groups
typically include hidden symmetries that are not among the target-space
isometries of the ungauged theory. The possible gaugings are described
group-theoretically in terms of a constant embedding tensor subject to a number
of constraints which parametrizes the different theories and entirely encodes
the gauged Lagrangian.
  The prime example is the bosonic sector of the maximally supersymmetric
theory whose ungauged version admits an affine E_9 global symmetry algebra. The
various parameters (related to higher-dimensional p-form fluxes, geometric and
non-geometric fluxes, etc.) which characterize the possible gaugings, combine
into an embedding tensor transforming in the basic representation of E_9. This
yields an infinite-dimensional class of maximally supersymmetric theories in
two dimensions. We work out and discuss several examples of higher-dimensional
origin which can be systematically analyzed using the different gradings of
E_9.

In this study, we present the design of parallel kinematic machine tools using kinetostatic performance criteria. Our aim is to improve the performance and accuracy of these machines, which are widely used in manufacturing and CNC machining operations. We adopt a fully analytical methodology, which enables us to optimize the design parameters for maximum performance. 

Our research is motivated by the need to improve the efficiency of parallel kinematic machines for industrial applications. These machines offer superior performance compared to conventional serial kinematic machines, but their design is more complex due to the presence of redundancies. Our approach focuses on identifying the optimal arrangements of these redundancies, while ensuring that the machines meet the kinetostatic performance criteria. 

Our results demonstrate that the proposed methodology is effective in designing high-performance parallel kinematic machines. We successfully identified the optimal kinematic arrangement for different types of machines, including 3RPU, 3RPR, and 3RRR. Moreover, we validated our designs using simulations and experiments, which showed that the machines achieve high accuracy and repeatability. Overall, our research contributes to the development of more advanced and efficient parallel kinematic machines for manufacturing and CNC machining applications.

In our work titled "Spectral Methods for Numerical Relativity", our focus is on developing efficient and accurate numerical methods for solving Einstein's equations. The study of numerical relativity is of significant importance as it enables us to obtain insights into the dynamics of black holes, gravitational waves and other astrophysical phenomena. The motivation behind this research arises from the desire to construct numerical models for the observation of gravitational wave sources and to interpret their signals. The key ideas we explore in this work are the spectral methods that offer high-order accuracy, computational efficiency, and stability for simulating the evolution of gravitational systems. Our methodology involves the use of a pseudo-spectral framework based on Chebyshev polynomials and spectral differentiation matrices. We also employ a numerical time-integration scheme based on the fourth-order Runge-Kutta method. 

The primary challenge our research attempts to solve is the high computational cost of simulating systems that involve the strong gravitational fields and complex topologies characteristic of black holes, neutron stars, and other sources of gravitational waves. Our approach aims to enhance the efficiency and accuracy of numerical calculations without compromising on the quality of the results. Our results with these methods exhibit excellent convergence and stability, demonstrating the effectiveness of spectral methods for numerical relativity. In conclusion, the work presented in this paper can be of interest not only to the numerical relativists but to mathematicians, scientists, and researchers working in other areas of physics, engineering, and applied sciences.

Since the introduction of binomial state as an intermediate state, different
intermediate states have been proposed. Different nonclassical effects have
also been reported in these intermediate states. But till now higher order
antibunching or higher order subpoissonian photon statistics is predicted only
in one type of intermediate state, namely shadowed negative binomial state.
Recently we have shown the existence of higher order antibunching in some
simple nonlinear optical processes to establish that higher order antibunching
is not a rare phenomenon (J. Phys. B 39 (2006) 1137). To establish our earlier
claim further, here we have shown that the higher order antibunching can be
seen in different intermediate states, such as binomial state, reciprocal
binomial state, hypergeometric state, generalized binomial state, negative
binomial state and photon added coherent state. We have studied the possibility
of observing the higher order subpoissonian photon statistics in different
limits of intermediate states. The effect of different control parameters have
also been studied in this connection and it has been shown that the depth of
nonclassicality can be tuned by controlling various physical parameters.

The paper is considering an opportunity for the CERN/GranSasso (CNGS)
neutrino complex, concurrent time-wise with T2K and NOvA, to search for
theta_13 oscillations and CP violation. Compared with large water Cherenkov
(T2K) and fine grained scintillators (NOvA), the LAr-TPC offers a higher
detection efficiency and a lower backgrounds, since virtually all channels may
be unambiguously recognized. The present proposal, called MODULAr, describes a
20 kt fiducial volume LAr-TPC, following very closely the technology developed
for the ICARUS-T60o, and is focused on the following activities, for which we
seek an extended international collaboration:
  (1) the neutrino beam from the CERN 400 GeV proton beam and an optimised horn
focussing, eventually with an increased intensity in the framework of the LHC
accelerator improvement program;
  (2) A new experimental area LNGS-B, of at least 50000 m3 at 10 km off-axis
from the main Laboratory, eventually upgradable to larger sizes. A location is
under consideration at about 1.2 km equivalent water depth;
  (3) A new LAr Imaging detector of at least 20 kt fiducial mass. Such an
increase in the volume over the current ICARUS T600 needs to be carefully
considered. It is concluded that a very large mass is best realised with a set
of many identical, independent units, each of 5 kt, "cloning" the technology of
the T600. Further phases may foresee extensions of MODULAr to meet future
physics goals.
  The experiment might reasonably be operational in about 4/5 years, provided a
new hall is excavated in the vicinity of the Gran Sasso Laboratory and adequate
funding and participation are made available.

Surprisingly, skew derivations rather than ordinary derivations are more
basic (important) object in study of the Grassmann algebras. Let $\L_n =
K\lfloor x_1, ..., x_n\rfloor$ be the Grassmann algebra over a commutative ring
$K$ with ${1/2}\in K$, and $\d$ be a skew $K$-derivation of $\L_n$. It is
proved that $\d$ is a unique sum $\d = \d^{ev} +\d^{od}$ of an even and odd
skew derivation. Explicit formulae are given for $\d^{ev}$ and $\d^{od}$ via
the elements $\d (x_1), ..., \d (x_n)$. It is proved that the set of all even
skew derivations of $\L_n$ coincides with the set of all the inner skew
derivations. Similar results are proved for derivations of $\L_n$. In
particular, $\Der_K(\L_n)$ is a faithful but not simple $\Aut_K(\L_n)$-module
(where $K$ is reduced and $n\geq 2$). All differential and skew differential
ideals of $\L_n$ are found. It is proved that the set of generic normal
elements of $\L_n$ that are not units forms a single $\Aut_K(\L_n)$-orbit
(namely, $\Aut_K(\L_n)x_1$) if $n$ is even and two orbits (namely,
$\Aut_K(\L_n)x_1$ and $\Aut_K(\L_n)(x_1+x_2... x_n)$) if $n$ is odd.

In this study, we investigate the dynamic condensation of water at crack tips in fused silica glass. Our motivation stems from the fact that water plays a crucial role in the behavior of cracks in materials and can often promote their growth and failure. Using molecular dynamics simulations, we show that water molecules condense spontaneously at the surface of silica glass as the temperature drops below the dew point, forming a thin liquid layer that can significantly affect the mechanical behavior of the material. We further investigate the influence of surface tension and contact angle on the water condensation process and find that these factors strongly affect the morphology and stability of the liquid layer. Our results also show that the presence of water can alter the critical stress intensity factor required for crack propagation in fused silica glass by up to 20%, highlighting the importance of considering water effects in fracture mechanics studies. Overall, our findings shed new light on the behavior of water at crack tips in silica glass and provide important insights into the design and optimization of materials for diverse applications.

In this paper, we investigate the normalizers of irreducible subfactors. Normalizers play a crucial role in the study of subfactors, as they provide a way of understanding the structure of the subfactor. Specifically, we study the normalizer of a subfactor M in its principal graph, which is the graph that encodes the irreducible subfactors that form the building blocks of M. Our main result is that the normalizer of an irreducible subfactor in its principal graph is a finite-index subgroup of the automorphism group of the subfactor.

To prove this result, we develop a new approach that combines techniques from operator algebra theory and graph theory. In particular, we use the theory of ternary trees to construct a certain extension of the subfactor, which we call a ternary extension. We then show that the normalizer of the irreducible subfactor in the principal graph can be identified with a certain subgroup of the automorphism group of the ternary extension. Finally, we use the fact that the automorphism group of a finite-index normal subgroup of a group is itself a finite-index subgroup to obtain our result.

Our work has important implications for the study of subfactors, as it sheds light on the structure of the normalizer and provides a new tool for studying the automorphism group of a subfactor. Moreover, our approach may have applications to other areas of mathematics, such as the study of group extensions and graph automorphisms.

In our research, we investigate transcritical bifurcations in non-integrable Hamiltonian systems. The main motivation for this study is to understand the behavior of these systems as they undergo a bifurcation. Specifically, we aim to explore how the dynamics of a system change when a local equilibrium point and a saddle point collide and switch stability. 

The problem we attempt to solve is to provide a comprehensive understanding of the bifurcation phenomenon in non-integrable Hamiltonian systems. This problem is important because many real-world systems are modeled using non-integrable Hamiltonian equations, making it crucial to understand the dynamics of these systems. Our research aims to provide insights into this phenomenon, which may have potential applications in fields such as physics and engineering. 

Our key ideas involve the use of perturbative and numerical techniques to study the bifurcations in various non-integrable Hamiltonian models. We focus on the behavior of the bifurcations as the non-integrable parameter is varied, observing how the dynamics change. We use a combination of analytical and numerical tools to explore the dynamics of the systems for various parameter values. 

In conclusion, our research provides a comprehensive study of transcritical bifurcations in non-integrable Hamiltonian systems, aiming to provide insights into the dynamics of these systems. This work may have potential applications in various fields of science and engineering.

In this work, we investigate the anisotropy and magnetization reversal of chains of submicron-sized Co hollow spheres. The motivation for this research is the need to better understand the magnetic properties of submicron-sized magnetic particles for potential applications in magnetic data storage and spintronics. We fabricate the Co hollow spheres using a template-assisted method and anneal them in a magnetic field to induce magnetic anisotropy. We then form chains of these spheres using an external magnetic field and observe their magnetic behavior using vibrating sample magnetometry and magnetic force microscopy. 

Our results show that the Co hollow spheres exhibit magnetic anisotropy along the axial direction, with the presence of two remanent magnetization states. The chains of hollow spheres exhibit an asymmetric magnetization reversal behavior, which is attributed to the dipolar interaction between the spheres. The dipolar interaction also leads to the formation of domain walls and spin vortices. 

This work aims to provide insights into the magnetic behavior of submicron-sized magnetic particles and to contribute to the development of new magnetic materials with tailored magnetic properties. The problem we attempt to solve is the need to understand the magnetic properties of submicron-sized particles that may have potential applications in magnetic data storage and spintronics. Our findings have implications for the design and optimization of magnetic devices at the nanoscale.

In this work, we investigate the effect of quantum anisotropy and magnetic field on the genuine multipartite entanglement (GME) of multi-qubit Heisenberg XY chains. We aim to explore the interplay between these two parameters and the GME property of the system. The problem we aim to solve is to study the behavior of the GME under different external conditions and to uncover the critical properties that could lead to the emergence of GME. We employ a general measure of GME for N-qubit systems known as the geometric measure of entanglement, which characterizes both the degree and structure of multipartite entanglement. Our analytical and numerical results show that the presence of magnetic field and anisotropy in the system could enhance or suppress GME. Specifically, we find that the critical anisotropic and magnetic field values for the maximum entanglement depend on the size of the chain, the symmetry of the system, and the temperature. Our study provides insights into the crucial role of anisotropy and magnetic field on the GME property of multi-qubit systems and highlights new avenues for investigating the entanglement behavior in quantum many-body systems.

We present a detailed study of soliton compression of ultra-short pulses
based on phase-mismatched second-harmonic generation (\textit{i.e.}, the
cascaded quadratic nonlinearity) in bulk quadratic nonlinear media. The
single-cycle propagation equations in the temporal domain including
higher-order nonlinear terms are presented. The balance between the quadratic
(SHG) and the cubic (Kerr) nonlinearity plays a crucial role: we define an
effective soliton number -- related to the difference between the SHG and the
Kerr soliton numbers -- and show that it has to be larger than unity for
successful pulse compression to take place. This requires that the phase
mismatch be below a critical level, which is high in a material where the
quadratic nonlinearity dominates over the cubic Kerr nonlinearity. Through
extensive numerical simulations we find dimensionless scaling laws, expressed
through the effective soliton number, which control the behaviour of the
compressed pulses. These laws hold in the stationary regime, in which
group-velocity mismatch effects are small, and they are similar to the ones
observed for fiber soliton compressors. The numerical simulations indicate that
clean compressed pulses below two optical cycles can be achieved in a
$\beta$-barium borate crystal at appropriate wavelengths, even for picosecond
input pulses.

In this research work, our aim is to develop a novel method for the semidefinite representation of convex sets. The motivation behind this work is rooted in the need for a more efficient and practical approach to represent large convex sets in optimization problems. Current methods are limited by the difficulty of finding conic representations of such sets. On the other hand, semidefinite programming has shown promising results for optimization problems, and has been widely adopted in many scientific and engineering fields. 

The key idea is to exploit the structure of convex sets and use a small set of constraints to describe them efficiently. We propose a semidefinite formulation that relies on geometric properties of these sets, such as ellipsoids and hyperplanes. In particular, we present a hierarchy of semidefinite relaxations that progressively improves the quality of the representation. We also provide a numerical analysis of the convergence properties of our method, as well as an empirical evaluation of its computational performance. 

Our work contributes to the growing body of research on semidefinite programming and convex optimization. Our method has the potential to be applied in many fields, such as signal processing, machine learning, and control theory. Additionally, it offers a simpler and more scalable alternative to existing methods for convex set representation.

Internet-based personal digital belongings present different vulnerabilities
than locally stored materials. We use responses to a survey of people who have
recovered lost websites, in combination with supplementary interviews, to paint
a fuller picture of current curatorial strategies and practices. We examine the
types of personal, topical, and commercial websites that respondents have lost
and the reasons they have lost this potentially valuable material. We further
explore what they have tried to recover and how the loss influences their
subsequent practices. We found that curation of personal digital materials in
online stores bears some striking similarities to the curation of similar
materials stored locally in that study participants continue to archive
personal assets by relying on a combination of benign neglect, sporadic
backups, and unsystematic file replication. However, we have also identified
issues specific to Internet-based material: how risk is spread by distributing
the files among multiple servers and services; the circular reasoning
participants use when they discuss the safety of their digital assets; and the
types of online material that are particularly vulnerable to loss. The study
reveals ways in which expectations of permanence and notification are violated
and situations in which benign neglect has far greater consequences for the
long-term fate of important digital assets.

We present the full thermodynamics of a fluid confined by an arbitrary
external potential based on the virial expansion of the grand potential. The
fluid may be classical or quantum and it is assumed that interatomic
interactions are pairwise additive. We indicate how the appropriate
"generalized" volume and pressure variables, that replace the usual volume and
hydrostatic pressure, emerge for a given confining potential in the
thermodynamic limit. A discussion of the physical meaning and of the
measurement of these variables is presented. We emphasize that this treatment
yields the correct equation of state of the fluid and we give its virial
expansion. We propose an experiment to measure the heat capacity, so that with
this quantity and the equation of state, the complete thermodynamics of the
system may be extracted. As a corollary, we find that the so-called {\it local
density approximation} for these systems follows in the thermodynamic limit,
although we also point out that it cannot be used indiscriminately for all
local variables. Along the text we discuss the relevance of these findings in
the description of the currently confined ultracold gases.

In this work, we investigate the gauge-Higgs unification (GHU) scenario in the framework of broken flavor symmetry. The main motivation for this research is to understand whether flavor symmetry breaking could affect GHU and to explore whether this scenario could be extended to more realistic models beyond the standard model. Through the use of effective field theory, we analyze the renormalization group (RG) flow of the GHU couplings and investigate the constraints imposed by flavor symmetry breaking. We also perform a phenomenological analysis of the GHU scenario in the context of the large extra dimension (LED) model. Our results show that the breaking of flavor symmetry has a significant impact on the RG flow of GHU couplings, leading to differences in the mass spectrum and mixing patterns of the Higgs and gauge bosons. These results offer insights into how flavor symmetry breaking could be utilized to extend the GHU scenario beyond the standard model. In conclusion, we have presented a formal academic and scientific voice that uses the first-person plural form and active voice to describe our investigation into the GHU scenario in the context of broken flavor symmetry.

We study the cosmological evolution of a tachyon scalar field T with a
Dirac-Born-Infeld type lagrangian and potential V(T) coupled to a canonically
normalized scalar field \phi with an arbitrary interaction term B(T,\phi) in
the presence of a barotropic fluid $\rb$, which can be matter or radiation. The
force between the barotropic fluid and the scalar fields is only gravitational.
We show that the dynamics is completely determine by only three parameters L1 =
- V_T/ V^{3/2}, L2= - B_T /B^{3/2} and L3 =-B_{\phi}/B. We determine
analytically theconditions for $\lm_i$ under which the energy density of T,
\phi and $\rb$ have the same redshift. We study the behavior of T and \phi in
the asymptotic limits for L_i and we show the numerical solution for different
interesting cases.
  The effective equation of state for the tachyon field changes due to the
interaction with the scalar field and we show that it is possible for a tachyon
field to redshift as matter in the absence of an interaction term B and as
radiation when B is turned on. This result solves then the tachyonic matter
problem.

Discrete control systems, as considered here, refer to the control theory of
discrete-time Lagrangian or Hamiltonian systems. These discrete-time models are
based on a discrete variational principle, and are part of the broader field of
geometric integration. Geometric integrators are numerical integration methods
that preserve geometric properties of continuous systems, such as conservation
of the symplectic form, momentum, and energy. They also guarantee that the
discrete flow remains on the manifold on which the continuous system evolves,
an important property in the case of rigid-body dynamics.
  In nonlinear control, one typically relies on differential geometric and
dynamical systems techniques to prove properties such as stability,
controllability, and optimality. More generally, the geometric structure of
such systems plays a critical role in the nonlinear analysis of the
corresponding control problems. Despite the critical role of geometry and
mechanics in the analysis of nonlinear control systems, nonlinear control
algorithms have typically been implemented using numerical schemes that ignore
the underlying geometry.
  The field of discrete control system aims to address this deficiency by
restricting the approximation to choice of a discrete-time model, and
developing an associated control theory that does not introduce any additional
approximation. In particular, this involves the construction of a control
theory for discrete-time models based on geometric integrators that yields
numerical implementations of nonlinear and geometric control algorithms that
preserve the crucial underlying geometric structure.

In this research, we investigate the magnetic evolution and temperature variation in a coronal hole by analyzing data collected from the Atmospheric Imaging Assembly (AIA) on board the Solar Dynamics Observatory (SDO). Coronal holes are regions in the Sun's atmosphere where cooler, denser plasma is present due to a lack of magnetic fields. Therefore, they play an essential role in the study of magnetic fields and solar wind acceleration. Our research is motivated by the need to understand how magnetic fields and temperature evolved in coronal holes.

The problem this work attempts to solve is to better understand the magnetic structure of coronal holes and the temperature evolution of the plasma within them over time. The key ideas in our study involve analyzing the magnetic field topology and temperature variation over different timescales in coronal holes. To achieve these objectives, we developed custom code to extract and analyze the magnetic field and temperature data.

Our methodology involves analyzing the magnetic flux and temperature intensity distribution in the coronal hole. In addition, we computed the radial magnetic field component and the azimuthal averages of temperature values to investigate the magnetic evolution and temperature variation in the coronal hole. Our results revealed that the magnetic structure evolves over time, and there is a strong correlation between the magnetic field and temperature variation. Our findings provide critical insights into the magnetic and thermal properties of coronal holes, which may help to inform future studies of the Sun's activity and space weather.

We consider the evolution of strong (W_r(2796) > 0.3A) MgII absorbers, most
of which are closely related to luminous galaxies. Using 20 high resolution
quasar spectra from the VLT/UVES public archive, we examine 33 strong MgII
absorbers in the redshift range 0.3 < z < 2.5. We compare and supplement this
sample with 23 strong MgII absorbers at 0.4 < z < 1.4 observed previously with
HIRES/Keck. We find that neither equivalent width nor kinematic spread (the
optical depth weighted second moment of velocity) of MgII2796 evolve. However,
the kinematic spread is sensitive to the highest velocity component, and
therefore not as sensitive to additional weak components at intermediate
velocities relative to the profile center. The fraction of absorbing pixels
within the full velocity range of the system does show a trend of decreasing
with decreasing redshift. Most high redshift systems (14/20) exhibit absorption
over the entire system velocity range, which differs from the result for low
redshift systems (18/36) at the 95% level. This leads to a smaller number of
separate subsystems for high redshift systems because weak absorping components
tend to connect the stronger regions of absorption. We hypothesize that low
redshift MgII profiles are more likely to represent well formed galaxies, many
of which have kinematics consistent with a disk/halo structure. High redshift
MgII profiles are more likely to show evidence of complex protogalactic
structures, with multiple accretion or outflow events. Although these results
are derived from measurements of gas kinematics, they are consistent with
hierarchical galaxy formation evidenced by deep galaxy surveys.

In this research, we investigate the capacity of sparse multipath channels in the ultra-wideband (UWB) regime. The motivation for our study is the growing demand for high-capacity wireless communication systems that can support various applications, including high-speed data, multimedia streaming, and internet of things (IoT) devices. However, the UWB channels pose significant challenges due to their multipath propagation, which results in severe signal attenuation and interference.

To solve this problem, we propose a novel approach for estimating the channel capacity of UWB channels with sparse multipath. Our method is based on the principle of compressed sensing, which exploits the sparsity of the multipath channel impulse response. We show that our approach can significantly improve the channel capacity compared to traditional methods.

Our results demonstrate that sparse multipath channels have a significant potential for providing high-capacity wireless communication systems in the UWB regime. Furthermore, our approach can be applied to other wireless communication systems that use sparse multipath channels. Overall, our research provides valuable insights into the capacity analysis of sparse multipath channels, which can contribute to the development of efficient and reliable wireless systems for various applications.

In order to explore the magnetostrophic regime expected for planetary cores,
experiments have been conducted in a rotating sphere filled with liquid sodium,
with an imposed dipolar magnetic field (the DTS setup). The field is produced
by a permanent magnet enclosed in an inner sphere, which can rotate at a
separate rate, producing a spherical Couette flow. The flow properties are
investigated by measuring electric potentials on the outer sphere, the induced
magnetic field in the laboratory frame, and velocity profiles inside the liquid
sodium using ultrasonic Doppler velocimetry. The present article focuses on the
time-averaged axisymmetric part of the flow. The Doppler profiles show that the
angular velocity of the fluid is relatively uniform in most of the fluid shell,
but rises near the inner sphere, revealing the presence of a magnetic wind, and
gently drops towards the outer sphere. The transition from a magnetostrophic
flow near the inner sphere to a geostrophic flow near the outer sphere is
controlled by the local Elsasser number. For Rossby numbers up to order 1, the
observed velocity profiles all show a similar shape. Numerical simulations in
the linear regime are computed, and synthetic velocity profiles are compared
with the measured ones. In the geostrophic region, a torque-balance model
provides very good predictions. We find that the induced magnetic field varies
in a consistent fashion, and displays a peculiar peak in the counter-rotating
regime. This happens when the fluid rotation rate is almost equal and opposite
to the outer sphere rotation rate. The fluid is then almost at rest in the
laboratory frame, and the Proudman-Taylor constraint vanishes, enabling a
strong meridional flow. We suggest that dynamo action might be favored in such
a situation.

(Conext:) Surveys of Wolf-Rayet (WR) populations in nearby galaxies provide
tests of evolutionary models plus Type Ib/c supernova progenitors. This
spectroscopic study complements the recent imaging survey of the spiral galaxy
NGC 300 by Schild et al. (Aims): Revisions to the known WR content of NGC 300
are presented. We investigate the WR nature of candidate #41 from Schild et al.
which is spatially coincident with the bright X-ray point source NGC 300 X-1;
(Methods:) VLT/FORS2 multi-object spectroscopy of WR candidates in NGC 300 is
obtained; (Results:) We establish an early-type WN nature of #41, i.e. similar
to the optical counterpart of IC 10 X-1, which closely resembles NGC 300 X-1.
We confirm 9 new WR stars, bringing the current WR census of the inner disk to
31, with N(WC)/N(WN)~0.9. (Conclusions:) If #41 is the optical counterpart for
NGC 300 X-1, we estimate a WR mass of 38 Msun based upon ground-based
photometry, from which a black hole mass of > 10 Msun results from the 32.8 hr
period of the system and WR wind velocity of 1250 km/s. We estimate an 95%
completeness among WC stars and 70% among WN stars, such that the total WR
content is ~40, with N(WC)/N(WN)~0.7. From the Halpha-derived star formation
rate of the inner galaxy, we infer N(WR)/N(O)~0.04

Let $(M,g,\si)$ be a compact spin manifold of dimension $n \geq 2$. Let
$\lambda_1^+(\tilde{g})$ be the smallest positive eigenvalue of the Dirac
operator in the metric $\tilde{g} \in [g]$ conformal to $g$. We then define
$\lamin(M,[g],\si) = \inf_{\tilde{g} \in [g]} \lambda_1^+(\tilde{g})
\Vol(M,\tilde{g})^{1/n} $. We show that $0< \lamin(M,[g],\si) \leq
\lamin(\mS^n)$. %=\frac{n}{2} \om_n^{{1 \over n}}$ . We find sufficient
conditions for which we obtain strict inequality $\lamin(M,[g],\si) <
\lamin(\mS^n)$. This strict inequality has applications to conformal spin
geometry.
  -----
  Soit $(M,g,\si)$ une vari\'et\'e spinorielle compacte de dimension $n \geq
2$. %Si $\tilde{g} \in [g]$ est une m\'etrique conforme \`a $g$, On note
$\lambda_1^+(\tilde{g})$ la plus petite valeur propre $>0$ de l'op\'erateur de
Dirac dans la m\'etrique $\tilde{g} \in [g]$ conforme \`a $g$. On d\'efinit
$\lamin(M,[g],\si) = \inf_{\tilde{g} \in [g]} \lambda_1^+(\tilde{g})
  \Vol(M,\tilde{g})^{1/n} $. On montre que $0< \lamin(M,[g],\si) \leq
\lamin(\mS^n)$. %= \frac{n}{2} \om_n^{{1 \over n}}$ On trouve des conditions
suffisantes pour lesquelles on obtient l'in\'egalit\'e stricte
$\lamin(M,[g],\si) < \lamin(\mS^n)$. Cette in\'egalit\'e stricte a des
applications en g\'eom\'etrie spinorielle conforme.

In this work, we focus on multivariable generalizations of the Schur class, which are characterized through positive kernel functions. Our main motivation is to develop a framework for the study of systems with multiple inputs and outputs that is more general than classical theories such as the transfer function representation. We adopt a function-theoretic approach based on the theory of reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHS) to study multivariable Schur functions and their properties. 

Our key methodology involves studying the positive kernel representations of multivariable Schur functions, which lead to a rich set of results and applications. We develop a notion of transfer function realization based on these kernel representations, which gives a new perspective on the representation of multivariable systems. In particular, we show that the kernel representations provide a natural setting for the study of algebraic and analytic properties of multivariable Schur functions.

Our main result is a generalization of the classical factorization theorem for scalar Schur functions to the multivariable case. Specifically, we prove that any multivariable Schur function can be factored into a product of an analytic function and its transpose, where the analytic function is characterized by its positive-definite kernel representation. This factorization result has important implications for the study of multivariable systems, including the design and analysis of control systems.

Overall, our work provides a new perspective on the theory of multivariable Schur functions and their applications to systems theory. We expect our results to have broad implications for the study of multivariable systems in a wide range of applications, including control, signal processing, and communications.

In our work titled "On Gene Duplication Models for Evolving Regulatory Networks," we aim to address a fundamental question in evolutionary biology: how do complex regulatory networks evolve from simple ones? The duplication and divergence of genes and their regulatory regions is a key mechanism by which new functions can arise and drive evolutionary innovation. However, the precise evolutionary trajectories and dynamics of these processes are still not well understood, and many open questions remain.

To tackle this problem, we explore various gene duplication models in the context of evolving regulatory networks using computer simulations and statistical analyses. We focus on the role of gene duplication in the evolution of transcriptional regulatory networks, which play important roles in controlling gene expression and cell differentiation.

Our approach allows us to investigate the effects of various parameters such as gene dosage, the topology of the network, and the strength of regulatory interactions. By comparing and contrasting different models, we aim to shed light on the underlying mechanisms and constraints that shape the evolution of regulatory networks.

Overall, our study contributes to a deeper understanding of the complex interplay between gene duplication and regulatory evolution, and may have important implications for fields ranging from evolutionary biology to biotechnology and synthetic biology.

In spite of the efforts made in the latest years, still there is no
comprehensive explanation for the chemical anomalies of globular cluster stars.
Among these, the most striking is oxygen depletion, which reaches values down
to [O/Fe]~-0.4 in most clusters, but in M13 it goes down to less than [O/Fe]~ -
1. In this work we suggest that the anomalies are due to the super position of
two different events: 1) PRIMORDIAL SELF-ENRICHMENT: this is asked to explain
the oxygen depletion down to a minimum value [O/Fe]~ -0.4; 2) EXTRA MIXING IN A
FRACTION OF THE STARS ALREADY BORN WITH ANOMALOUS COMPOSITION: these objects,
starting with already low [O/Fe], will reduce the oxygen abundance down to the
most extreme values. Contrary to other models that invoke extra mixing to
explain the chemical anomalies, we suggest that it is active only if there is a
fraction of the stars in which the primordial composition is not only oxygen
depleted, but also extremely helium rich (Y~ 0.4), as found in a few GCs from
their main sequence multiplicity. We propose that the rotational evolution (and
an associated extra mixing) of extremely helium rich stars may be affected by
the fact that they develop a very small or non existent molecular weight
barrier during the evolution. We show that extra mixing in these stars, having
initial chemistry that has already been CNO processed, affects mainly the
oxygen abundance, and to a much smaller extent if affects the sodium abundance.
The model also predicts a large fluorine depletion concomitant with the oxygen
depletion, and a further enhancement of the surface helium abundance, which
reaches values close to Y=0.5 in the computed models. We stress that, in this
tentative explanation, those stars that are primordially O--depleted, but ARE
NOT extremely helium rich do not suffer deep extra mixing.

The association of long gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) with Type Ib/c supernovae
implies that they explode into the winds of their Wolf-Rayet progenitor stars.
Although the evolution of some GRB afterglows is consistent with expansion into
a free wind, there is also good evidence for expansion into a constant density
medium. The evidence includes the evolution of X-ray afterglows (when X-rays
are below the cooling frequency), the evolution of the pre-jet break optical
and X-ray afterglow, and the sharp turn-on observed for some afterglows. Recent
observations of short bursts, which are expected to be interacting with a
constant density medium, provide a check on the standard afterglow model.
Although radio observations do not support the constant density model for long
bursts in some cases, the evidence for constant density interaction is strong.
The most plausible way to produce such a medium around a massive star is to
shock the progenitor wind. This requires a smaller termination shock than would
be expected, possibly due to a high pressure surroundings, a high progenitor
velocity, or the particular evolution leading to a GRB. However, the need for
the termination shock near the deceleration radius cannot be plausibly
accomodated and may indicate that some long bursts have compact binary
progenitors and explode directly into the interstellar medium.

We present a high-resolution survey of O VI absorption lines in the spectra of 36 low-redshift QSOs. The primary objective of this work is to characterize the physical conditions and statistical properties of O VI absorbers found in the intergalactic medium (IGM). The study aims at addressing the important question of the origin of O VI absorbers - whether they arise solely from photoionization or from a combination of photoionization and collisional ionization. 

Our analysis of the O VI absorbers reveals a complex interplay between various physical processes that shape the properties of the absorbers. We find that the O VI absorbers have a low Doppler parameter and a wide range of ionization fractions, indicative of a mixed origin. We also find that the O VI absorbers are more prevalent in denser environments and that the metallicity of the absorbers is closely linked to the gas density. The findings of this study suggest that the physical conditions of the IGM are highly complex and that the ionization state of the gas cannot be explained solely by photoionization. These results have important implications for understanding the nature of the IGM and its role in shaping the evolution of galaxies.

In this work, we present a novel methodology for quantifying social group evolution, with a focus on understanding the dynamics of emergence and dissolution of these groups. Our approach is grounded in the idea that social groups can be seen as systems of relationships among individuals, and that changes in these relationships can serve as potent indicators of group evolution. Specifically, we use network analysis techniques to extract key features of social group structures, such as connectivity, centrality, and modularity, and use these features to develop a set of quantitative measures that capture the evolution of social groups over time. To test our methodology, we apply it to a large dataset of social network interactions, spanning multiple years, and show that it can accurately track the emergence and dissolution of different social groups, as well as identify important factors that drive these dynamics. Overall, our work represents an important step towards a more systematic and quantitative understanding of social group evolution, and has important implications for a wide range of fields, from social psychology and sociology to political science and economics.

We carried out a wide-field V, I imaging survey of the Local Group dwarf
spheroidal galaxy Leo II using the Subaru Prime Focus Camera on the 8.2-m
Subaru Telescope. The survey covered an area of 26.67 x 26.67 arcmin^2, far
beyond the tidal radius of Leo II (8.63 arcmin), down to the limiting magnitude
of V ~26, which is roughly 1 mag deeper than the turn-off point of the main
sequence stars of Leo II. Radial number density profiles of bright and faint
red giant branch (RGB) stars were found to change their slopes at around the
tidal radius, and extend beyond the tidal radius with shallower slopes. A
smoothed surface brightness map of Leo II suggests the existence of a small
substructure of globular cluster luminosity beyond the tidal radius. We
investigated the properties of the stellar population by means of the
color-magnitude diagram. The horizontal branch (HB) morphology index shows a
radial gradient in which red HB stars are more concentrated than blue HB stars,
which is common to many Local Group dwarf spheroidal galaxies. The color
distribution of RGB stars around the mean RGB sequence shows a larger
dispersion at the center than in the outskirts, indicating a mixture of stellar
populations at the center and a more homogeneous population in the outskirts.
Based on the age estimation using subgiant branch (SGB) stars, we found that
although the major star formation took place ~8 Gyr ago, a considerable stellar
population younger than 8 Gyr is found at the center; such a younger population
is insignificant in the outskirts.

The operator-valued Schur-class is defined to be the set of holomorphic
functions $S$ mapping the unit disk into the space of contraction operators
between two Hilbert spaces. There are a number of alternate characterizations:
the operator of multiplication by $S$ defines a contraction operator between
two Hardy Hilbert spaces, $S$ satisfies a von Neumann inequality, a certain
operator-valued kernel associated with $S$ is positive-definite, and $S$ can be
realized as the transfer function of a dissipative (or even conservative)
discrete-time linear input/state/output linear system. Various multivariable
generalizations of this class have appeared recently,one of the most
encompassing being that of Muhly and Solel where the unit disk is replaced by
the strict unit ball of the elements of a dual correspondence $E^{\sigma}$
associated with a $W^{*}$-correspondence $E$ over a $W^{*}$-algebra $\cA$
together with a $*$-representation $\sigma$ of $\cA$. The main new point which
we add here is the introduction of the notion of reproducing kernel Hilbert
correspondence and identification of the Muhly-Solel Hardy spaces as
reproducing kernel Hilbert correspondences associated with a completely
positive analogue of the classical Szeg\"o kernel. In this way we are able to
make the analogy between the Muhly-Solel Schur class and the classical Schur
class more complete. We also illustrate the theory by specializing it to some
well-studied special cases; in some instances there result new kinds of
realization theorems.

In this research, we propose and analyze a hybrid network architecture that combines traditional wired networks and wireless access points. Our focus is on developing a localized support mechanism for selecting the optimal injection point for data packets in hybrid networks. The problem we aim to solve is the lack of efficient and personalized data delivery in hybrid networks, which are becoming increasingly popular in various applications. Our proposed solution uses the concept of relative distance between the sender and receiver nodes to elect the best injection point for data packets. We optimize this selection by using minimal group communication to preserve bandwidth and minimize network congestion. The key ideas behind our approach are to improve data delivery performance through efficient and localized support and establish personalized communication for individual users in a hybrid network.

We validate our proposed solution through a simulation-based benchmarking approach to evaluate its performance in realistic scenarios. Our simulation results demonstrate that our localized support system is significantly more effective in improving packet delivery success rates, reducing latency, and providing a better quality of experience than existing solutions.
