This work contributes to the ongoing efforts to understand and characterize quantum dynamical phase transitions, and is highly relevant for various fields such as condensed matter, quantum optics, and quantum information theory.

The development of robust human identification protocols has been a consistent challenge in biometrics and security. In this work, we propose using image attributes for identity verification in surveillance systems. The motivation for this research stems from the limitations of traditional biometric methods, which may fail to provide accurate results due to environmental conditions and occlusions. Our approach leverages the frequency and path of a person's movement, captured through surveillance footage, to create a unique set of image attributes that are then used for identification. 

Our key methodology involves training a deep neural network to extract and classify image attributes according to certain parameters such as lighting, clothing, and posture. Subsequently, we leverage the extracted features for identity verification, which involves comparing the attributes extracted from footage with those in an existing database. We demonstrate the efficacy of our approach using a large dataset consisting of footage collected from several surveillance cameras. Through our analysis, we showcase an improvement in the accuracy of identification compared to traditional biometric methods.

In conclusion, our work shows that image attributes can be effective in developing human identification protocols that are both accurate and robust. We believe that our approach has broad implications for surveillance and security in various contexts.

We apply the general principles of effective field theories to the
construction of effective interactions suitable for few- and many-body
calculations in a no-core shell model framework. We calculate the spectrum of
systems with three and four two-component fermions in a harmonic trap. In the
unitary limit, we find that three-particle results are within 10% of known
semi-analytical values even in small model spaces. The method is very general,
and can be readily extended to other regimes, more particles, different species
(e.g., protons and neutrons in nuclear physics), or more-component fermions (as
well as bosons). As an illustration, we present calculations of the
lowest-energy three-fermion states away from the unitary limit and find a
possible inversion of parity in the ground state in the limit of trap size
large compared to the scattering length. Furthermore, we investigate the lowest
positive-parity states for four fermions, although we are limited by the
dimensions we can currently handle in this case.

Aims. We probe the effect of turbulent diffusion on the chemistry at the
interface between a cold neutral medium (CNM) cloudlet and the warm neutral
medium (WNM). Methods. We perform moving grid, multifluid, 1D, hydrodynamical
simulations with chemistry including thermal and chemical diffusion. The
diffusion coefficients are enhanced to account for turbulent diffusion. We
post-process the steady-states of our simulations with a crude model of
radiative transfer to compute line profiles. Results. Turbulent diffusion
spreads out the transition region between the CNM and the WNM. We find that the
CNM slightly expands and heats up: its CH and H$_2$ content decreases due to
the lower density. The change of physical conditions and diffusive transport
increase the H$^+$ content in the CNM which results in increased OH and H$_2$O.
Diffusion transports some CO out of the CNM. It also brings H$_2$ into contact
with the warm gas with enhanced production of CH$^+$, H$_3^+$, OH and H$_2$O at
the interface. O lines are sensitive to the spread of the thermal profile in
the intermediate region between the CNM and the WNM. Enhanced molecular content
at the interface of the cloud broadens the molecular line profiles and helps
exciting transitions of intermediate energy. The relative molecular yield are
found higher for bigger clouds. Conclusions. Turbulent diffusion can be the
source of additional molecular production and should be included in chemical
models of the interstellar medium (ISM). It also is a good candidate for the
interpretation of observational problems such as warm H$_2$, CH$^+$ formation
and presence of H$_3^+$.

In this work, we present a self-consistent density functional description of thermostatted inhomogeneous granular fluids. The study of granular materials has gained a lot of attention due to their relevance in a wide range of natural and industrial processes. Although granular materials have been widely studied at the microscopic and macroscopic level, there is still a fundamental gap in the description of nonequilibrium states.

Our work aims to fill this gap by proposing a theoretical framework based on density functional theory to describe the nonequilibrium behavior of inhomogeneous granular fluids. We focus on thermostatted systems, in which an external energy source is added to balance the energy dissipation due to collisions. 

The main challenge of our work lies in tackling the complex interplay between energy dissipation and flow in non-uniform systems. Our methodology involves the derivation of a set of self-consistent equations to describe the particle density profiles, temperature, and pressure of the system. We additionally investigate the properties of the system, such as phase behavior and transport coefficients, using these equations.

Our results show that the proposed framework is effective in describing the behavior of inhomogeneous granular fluids under the influence of an external thermostat, providing insights into the energy dissipation and transport properties of these materials. Overall, this work contributes to the advancement of the fundamental understanding of granular materials, paving the way for further studies on nonequilibrium phenomena in these systems.

We report a definitive confirmation of a large-scale structure around the
super rich cluster CL0016+1609 at z=0.55. We made spectroscopic follow-up
observations with FOCAS on Subaru along the large filamentary structure
identified in our previous photometric studies. We have confirmed the physical
connection of the huge filament extending over 20 Mpc in the N-S direction, and
another filament extending from the main cluster to the East. Based on a simple
energy argument, we show that it is likely that most of the clumps are bound to
the main CL0016 cluster. This structure is surely one of the most prominent
confirmed structures ever identified in the distant Universe, which then serves
as an ideal laboratory to examine the environmental variation of galaxy
properties. We draw star formation histories of galaxies from the composite
spectra of red galaxies in field, group, and cluster environments. Combining
the results from our previous studies, we find that red galaxies in groups at
z~0.8 and red field galaxies at z~0.5 show strong Hd absorption lines for their
D4000 indices. These are the environments in which we observed the on-going
build-up of the colour-magnitude relation in our previous photometric analyses.
The strong Hd absorption lines imply that their star formation is truncated on
a relatively short time scale. We suggest that a galaxy-galaxy interaction is
the most likely physical driver of the truncation of star formation and thus
responsible for the build-up of the colour-magnitude relation since z~1.
(Abridged)

In our work entitled "Grand minima and maxima of solar activity: New observational constraints," we present the results of our investigation of the behavior of the Sun's magnetic activity over timescales exceeding a century. Our motivation for conducting this study stems from the need to better understand the inherent variability of solar activity, which is crucial for many applications ranging from space weather forecasting to the interpretation of climate records. Specifically, we are interested in identifying the occurrence of "Grand minima" and "Grand maxima" - periods of extreme quiescence and high activity, respectively - which have been observed in the past. However, previous studies have relied on indirect proxies of solar activity, which may not accurately reflect the true behavior of the Sun's magnetic field.

Our work uses direct measurements of solar magnetic activity from the last 150 years to analyze the occurrence of extreme episodes, and we find that the Sun's behavior over this timescale is much more stable than previously thought. We show that the occurrence of Grand minima is highly unlikely, while Grand maxima can occur, but with a low probability. Our results have important implications for future space weather and climate predictions, and provide valuable observational constraints for models of solar dynamo and variability.

In this work, we explore the properties of sparse estimators and investigate their performance using the oracle property framework. The motivation for this research stems from the need to examine the effectiveness of sparse estimators in high-dimensional problems where the number of covariates far exceeds the number of observations. Previous work has shown that sparse estimators can significantly improve prediction accuracy and reduce variance in such problems.

We present our results on the performance of sparse estimators by conducting simulations and analyzing real-world datasets. Our findings indicate that the oracle property holds true for most sparse estimators, which means that they can provide unbiased estimations and have a low variance when the number of irrelevant covariates is sufficiently small.

In addition, we introduce the concept of Hodges' estimator, which has not been widely used in the sparse literature despite its favorable properties in high-dimensional problems. Our analysis shows that Hodges' estimator can provide similar or even better results compared to other popular sparse estimators.

The key ideas of this research are to examine the performance of sparse estimators using the oracle property framework and to introduce Hodges' estimator as a promising alternative. The methodology involves the use of simulations and real-world data analysis to provide empirical evidence of the effectiveness of sparse estimators and Hodges' estimator. The results of this work may have implications for various fields such as machine learning, statistical inference, and bioinformatics.

Fluids in which the interparticle potential has a hard core, is attractive at
moderate separations, and repulsive at greater separations are known to exhibit
novel phase behavior, including stable inhomogeneous phases. Here we report a
joint simulation and theoretical study of such a fluid, focusing on the
relationship between the liquid-vapor transition line and any new phases. The
phase diagram is studied as a function of the amplitude of the attraction for a
certain fixed amplitude of the long ranged repulsion. We find that the effect
of the repulsion is to substitute the liquid-vapor critical point and a portion
of the associated liquid-vapor transition line, by two first order transitions.
One of these transitions separates the vapor from a fluid of spherical
liquidlike clusters; the other separates the liquid from a fluid of spherical
voids. At low temperature, the two transition lines intersect one another and a
vapor-liquid transition line at a triple point. While most integral equation
theories are unable to describe the new phase transitions, the Percus Yevick
approximation does succeed in capturing the vapor-cluster transition, as well
as aspects of the structure of the cluster fluid, in reasonable agreement with
the simulation results.

In this study, we present the analysis of mid-infrared spectra of disks around T Tauri stars using the Spitzer Infrared Spectrograph (IRS), with a focus on gas-phase lines of Neon and Iron ions, as well as molecular hydrogen lines. Our aim is to investigate the properties of the inner disk gas in T Tauri systems, which has a crucial role in planet formation. We analyzed the [Ne II] 12.8 μm, [Fe I] 24.0 μm and several rotational lines of H_2 at 6-17 μm, using a line profile modeling technique, called the COCO code, to extract the physical and chemical properties of the gas in the disk. 

Our results show that the [Ne II] line is a robust tracer of the gas disk, and its intensity correlates with the stellar accretion rate. The [Fe I] line is more intense in the inner disk regions, and its strength links to the warm gas temperature. The rotation diagram of H_2 lines indicates that gas temperatures of around 1000-2000 K are present in the disk inner regions. We compare our results to existing disk models and find that the properties of the gas lines are well reproduced by various models, but the models may have some difficulties in accounting for the presence of the warm gas, likely located near the inner rim of the disk. Our work characterizes the inner gas content of young stellar disks with high accuracy.

In this study, we investigate the properties of the cataclysmic variable SDSS J233325.92+152222.1 and its potential contribution to the evolution of intermediate polars. We first acquired time-resolved spectroscopy and photometry to determine the system's orbital and spin periods. We then used the obtained data to model the accretion geometry of the system and estimate the mass of the white dwarf. Our results suggest that SDSS J233325.92+152222.1 is a magnetic system with a moderately strong magnetic field and an intermediate polar classification.

The motivation for this research is twofold. Firstly, understanding the evolution of intermediate polar systems is crucial for our understanding of the long-term dynamics of interacting binaries. Secondly, the rarity of intermediate polars means that each new discovery is valuable in expanding our knowledge of this class of systems.

The primary problem this study attempts to solve is determining the state and properties of the accretion disk in this intermediate polar system. Our results help to refine the accretion models and provide insight into the transfer of angular momentum between the white dwarf and the accretion disk.

Overall, this study provides a valuable contribution to our understanding of the evolution of intermediate polar systems and highlights the complexity of magnetic cataclysmic variables. Future observations of SDSS J233325.92+152222.1 and similar systems will be vital in confirming these results and expanding our knowledge of these rare objects.

In this study, we compare the particle production in quark and gluon fragmentation at sqrt s ~ 10 GeV. The motivation behind this research is to shed light on the fundamental differences between quarks and gluons and their behavior during fragmentation. Understanding particle production in quark and gluon fragmentation is a crucial aspect of modern physics, as it helps us to better comprehend the nature of strong interactions. 

Using the Monte-Carlo simulation technique, we generate events of quark and gluon fragmentation to study the characteristics of the particles produced. We analyze the distributions of particles based on their transverse momentum and pseudorapidity, and compare the results obtained from quark and gluon fragmentation. Our findings indicate that the number of particles produced in gluon fragmentation is higher compared to quark fragmentation, and the distributions are wider. Moreover, we observe that the ratio of charged and neutral particles is not the same for quark and gluon fragmentation.

The problem this work attempts to solve is to understand the particle production process in quark and gluon fragmentation. The results obtained from this research can aid in the development of new models and theories that better explain the nature of strong interactions in particle physics. This study also provides useful insights for the design of the experiments at current and future colliders, such as the Large Hadron Collider.

The Millennium Galaxy Catalogue (MGC) has been a valuable resource for galaxy studies, providing comprehensive data on over a million galaxies. In this work, we investigate the relationship between close galaxy pairs and asymmetry in the MGC, with implications for the galaxy merger rate. The problem of understanding the mechanisms driving galaxy evolution is a fundamental one in astrophysics, and galaxy mergers are a key process in this context. The rate of galaxy mergers and their impact on galaxy morphology and star formation rates is still a matter of debate. Our goal is to shed light on this issue by investigating the link between close pairs and asymmetry, a common indicator of merger activity. 

We have found a clear correlation between pair separation and asymmetry, with closer pairs showing higher levels of asymmetry. This suggests that galaxy interactions are indeed a main driver of galaxy asymmetry. Furthermore, we have used mock catalogs generated from simulations to estimate the galaxy merger rate implied by our measurements. We find that our results are consistent with previous estimates based on other methods, supporting the notion that mergers are indeed a key process in galaxy evolution. Our work provides new insights into the mechanisms driving galaxy evolution and lays the groundwork for future studies using the MGC.

We consider an unambiguous identification of an unknown coherent state with
one of two unknown coherent reference states. Specifically, we consider two
modes of an electromagnetic field prepared in unknown coherent states alpha_1
and alpha_2, respectively. The third mode is prepared either in the state
alpha_1 or in the state alpha_2. The task is to identify (unambiguously) which
of the two modes are in the same state. We present a scheme consisting of three
beamsplitters capable to perform this task. Although we don't prove the
optimality, we show that the performance of the proposed setup is better than
the generalization of the optimal measurement known for a finite-dimensional
case. We show that a single beamsplitter is capable to perform an unambiguous
quantum state comparison for coherent states optimally. Finally we propose an
experimental setup consisting of 2N-1 beamsplitters for unambiguous
identification among N unknown coherent states. This setup can be considered as
a search in a quantum database. The elements of the database are unknown
coherent states encoded in different modes of an electromagnetic field. The
task is to specify the two modes that are excited in the same, though unknown,
coherent state.

In this work, we investigate the catalytic effect of black hole motion on the resonant dynamics of planetary systems. Resonances between planet orbits play a significant role in shaping the long-term evolution of planetary systems and have important implications for habitability. However, understanding the mechanisms that drive resonance capture and evolution remains a challenge in this field. 

We propose that black hole motion can act as a trigger for resonance capture by stretching and compressing the orbits of the planets, destabilizing some and stabilizing others. We use N-body simulations to test this hypothesis and confirm that the resonance capture probability increases significantly when a black hole is present in the system. Moreover, we find that black holes can also drive resonance jumps, causing significant changes in the semi-major axes of the planets. 

Our findings suggest that black hole motion should be considered as an important factor in the dynamical evolution of planetary systems. This work opens up new avenues for research into the formation and evolution of planetary systems in galactic environments. Furthermore, it highlights the importance of multi-scale studies that incorporate black holes in the context of planet formation and evolution, and broader astrophysical applications.

Experimental studies have shown the ubiquity of altruistic behavior in human
societies. The social structure is a fundamental ingredient to understand the
degree of altruism displayed by the members of a society, in contrast to
individual-based features, like for example age or gender, which have been
shown not to be relevant to determine the level of altruistic behavior. We
explore an evolutionary model aiming to delve how altruistic behavior is
affected by social structure. We investigate the dynamics of interacting
individuals playing the Ultimatum Game with their neighbors given by a social
network of interaction. We show that a population self-organizes in a critical
state where the degree of altruism depends on the topology characterizing the
social structure. In general, individuals offering large shares but in turn
accepting large shares, are removed from the population. In heterogeneous
social networks, individuals offering intermediate shares are strongly selected
in contrast to random homogeneous networks where a broad range of offers, below
a critical one, is similarly present in the population.

In this work, we investigate the stopping effects of U+U collisions with a beam energy of 520 MeV/nucleon. Our motivation stems from the importance of understanding collision dynamics in heavy-ion collisions, which are used to study fundamental properties of nuclear matter. The problem we aim to solve is the lack of a comprehensive understanding of the stopping power and energy deposition in high-energy heavy-ion collisions. 

Using the Heavy Ion Spectrometer at the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, we measured the energy loss of the U projectiles and compared it to calculated values from the Binary Cascade model and the Relativistic Mean-Field theory. Our results show that the energy loss is slightly higher than predicted by the two models, but the difference is within the uncertainties. We also observed an enhanced production of neutrons, which indicates the presence of a high-density zone formed during the collision. 

These findings contribute to the ongoing efforts to develop a better understanding of heavy-ion collisions. By quantifying the energy deposition and stopping power in U+U collisions, our work provides valuable insights for the design and interpretation of future experiments, including those aimed at studying the properties of neutron-rich matter and the equation of state of nuclear matter at high densities.

We consider the simulation of interacting high-dimensional systems using
pairwise interacting qubits. The main tool in this context is the generation of
effective many-body interactions, and we examine a number of different
protocols for obtaining them. These methods include the usage of higher-order
processes (commutator method), unitary conjugation or graph state encoding, as
well as teleportation based approaches. We illustrate and compare these methods
in detail and analyze the time cost for simulation. In the second part of the
article, we investigate the influence of noise on the simulation process. We
concentrate on errors in the interaction Hamiltonians and consider two generic
noise models, (i) timing errors in pairwise interactions and (ii) noisy
pairwise interactions described by Master equations of Lindblad form. We
analyze and compare the effect of noise for the different simulation methods
and propose a way to significantly reduce the influence of noise by making use
of entanglement purification together with a teleportation based protocol.

We present the results of a survey of 45 young (<250 Myr), close (<50 pc)
stars with the Simultaneous Differential Imager (SDI) implemented at the VLT
and the MMT for the direct detection of extrasolar planets. As part of the
survey, we observed 54 objects total, of which 45 were close, young stars, 2
were somewhat more distant (<150 pc), very young (<10 Myr) stars, 3 were stars
with known radial velocity planets, and 4 were older, very nearby (<20 pc)
solar analogues. Our SDI devices use a double Wollaston prism and a quad filter
to take images simultaneously at 3 wavelengths surrounding the 1.62 um methane
absorption bandhead found in the spectrum of cool brown dwarfs and gas giant
planets. In our VLT data, we achieved H band contrasts > 10 mag (5 sigma) at a
separation of 0.5" from the primary star on 45% of our targets and H band
contrasts of > 9 mag at a separation of 0.5'' on 80% of our targets. With this
degree of attenuation, we should be able to image (5sigma detection) a 5
M_{Jup} planet 15 AU from a 70 Myr K1 star at 15 pc or a 5 M_{Jup} planet at 2
AU from a 12 Myr M star at 10 pc. We believe that our SDI images are the
highest contrast astronomical images ever made from ground or space for methane
rich companions <1'' from their star. For the best 20 of our survey stars, we
attained 50% 5 sigma completeness for 6-10 M_Jup planets at semi-major axes of
20-40 AU. Thus, our completeness levels are sufficient to significantly test
theoretical planet distributions. From our survey null result, we can rule out
(at the 98% confidence/2.0sigma level) a model planet population using a planet
distribution where N(a) $\propto$ constant out to a distance of 45 AU (further
model assumptions discussed within).

In this paper we introduce a variant of pushdown dimension called bounded
pushdown (BPD) dimension, that measures the density of information contained in
a sequence, relative to a BPD automata, i.e. a finite state machine equipped
with an extra infinite memory stack, with the additional requirement that every
input symbol only allows a bounded number of stack movements. BPD automata are
a natural real-time restriction of pushdown automata. We show that BPD
dimension is a robust notion by giving an equivalent characterization of BPD
dimension in terms of BPD compressors. We then study the relationships between
BPD compression, and the standard Lempel-Ziv (LZ) compression algorithm, and
show that in contrast to the finite-state compressor case, LZ is not universal
for bounded pushdown compressors in a strong sense: we construct a sequence
that LZ fails to compress signicantly, but that is compressed by at least a
factor 2 by a BPD compressor. As a corollary we obtain a strong separation
between finite-state and BPD dimension.

In 2005, Shen introduced a new invariant, $\mathcal G(N)$, of a diffuse von
Neumann algebra $N$ with a fixed faithful trace, and he used this invariant to
give a unified approach to showing that large classes of ${\mathrm{II}}_1$
factors $M$ are singly generated. This paper focuses on properties of this
invariant. We relate $\mathcal G(M)$ to the number of self-adjoint generators
of a ${\mathrm{II}}_1$ factor $M$: if $\mathcal G(M)<n/2$, then $M$ is
generated by $n+1$ self-adjoint operators, whereas if $M$ is generated by $n+1$
self-adjoint operators, then $\mathcal G(M)\leq n/2$. The invariant $\mathcal
G(\cdot)$ is well-behaved under amplification, satisfying $\mathcal
G(M_t)=t^{-2}\mathcal G(M)$ for all $t>0$. In particular, if $\mathcal
G(\mathcal L\mathbb F_r)>0$ for any particular $r>1$, then the free group
factors are pairwise non-isomorphic and are not singly generated for
sufficiently large values of $r$. Estimates are given for forming free products
and passing to finite index subfactors and the basic construction. We also
examine a version of the invariant $\mathcal G_{\text{sa}}(M)$ defined only
using self-adjoint operators; this is proved to satisfy $\mathcal
G_{\text{sa}}(M)=2\mathcal G(M)$. Finally we give inequalities relating a
quantity involved in the calculation of $\mathcal G(M)$ to the free-entropy
dimension $\delta_0$ of a collection of generators for $M$.

The Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infects more than 90% of the human population,
and is the cause of several both serious and mild diseases. It is a
tumorivirus, and has been widely studied as a model system for gene
(de)regulation in human. A central feature of the EBV life cycle is its ability
to persist in human B cells in states denoted latency I, II and III. In latency
III the host cell is driven to cell proliferation and hence expansion of the
viral population, but does not enter the lytic pathway, and no new virions are
produced, while the latency I state is almost completely dormant. In this paper
we study a physico-chemical model of the switch between latency I and latency
III in EBV. We show that the unusually large number of binding sites of two
competing transcription factors, one viral and one from the host, serves to
make the switch sharper (higher Hill coefficient), either by cooperative
binding between molecules of the same species when they bind, or by competition
between the two species if there is sufficient steric hindrance.

In this work, we aim to exploit the directional sensitivity of the Double Chooz near detector in order to enhance the detection of neutrino signals. Our motivation for undertaking this research stems from the need to accurately determine the neutrino mixing angle θ13, which has implications for a variety of physics topics, including neutrino mass hierarchy and the search for CP violation in the neutrino sector. However, the measurement of θ13 is challenging due to the smallness of the parameter and the fact that neutrino interactions are difficult to detect. 

To overcome these challenges, we propose a novel approach that exploits the directional dependence of the neutrino signals in the Double Chooz near detector. Our key idea is to use the orientation of the detector to distinguish between different types of neutrino interactions, thereby enhancing the signal-to-noise ratio. To accomplish this, we employ a variety of analytical and statistical techniques to extract the relevant information from the detector data. Specifically, we use a maximum-likelihood method to estimate the fraction of events that are due to neutrino-electron scattering versus neutrino-proton scattering.

Our results demonstrate that the directional sensitivity of the Double Chooz near detector can be exploited to significantly enhance the sensitivity of neutrino oscillation experiments. Our methodology can also be applied to other neutrino facilities to improve the accuracy of neutrino mixing angle measurements.

The recent discovery of binary objects in the Kuiper-belt opens an invaluable
window into past and present conditions in the trans-Neptunian part of the
Solar System. For example, knowledge of how these objects formed can be used to
impose constraints on planetary formation theories. We have recently proposed a
binary-object formation model based on the notion of chaos-assisted capture.
Here we present a more detailed analysis with calculations performed in the
spatial (three-dimensional) three- and four-body Hill approximations. It is
assumed that the potential binary partners are initially following heliocentric
Keplerian orbits and that their relative motion becomes perturbed as these
objects undergo close encounters. First, the mass, velocity, and orbital
element distribu- tions which favour binary formation are identified in the
circular and elliptical Hill limits. We then consider intruder scattering in
the circular Hill four-body problem and find that the chaos-assisted capture
mechanism is consistent with observed, apparently randomly distributed, binary
mutual orbit inclinations. It also predicts asymmetric distributions of
retrograde versus prograde orbits. The time-delay induced by chaos on particle
transport through the Hill sphere is analogous to the formation of a resonance
in a chemical reaction. Implications for binary formation rates are considered
and the 'fine-tuning' problem recently identified by Noll et al. (2007) is also
addressed.

In this work, we investigate the kinetic and fluid turbulent dynamics in magnetized weakly collisional plasmas, focusing on astrophysical gyrokinetics. The motivation for our research stems from the fact that weakly collisional plasmas are prevalent in astrophysical systems such as the solar corona, the interstellar medium, and accretion disks around black holes. These plasmas exhibit complex dynamics that cannot be easily described by traditional fluid models. Therefore, we employ a kinetic approach to study the turbulent cascades in these plasmas.

The main problem that our work attempts to solve is to understand the transfer of energy between different spatial and temporal scales in turbulence and the emergence of universal behavior that is independent of the specific astrophysical environment. Our findings have important implications for numerous astrophysical problems, including energy transport in the solar wind and the formation of magnetic fields in galaxies.

Our key ideas involve using analytical and numerical tools to study the kinetic and fluid turbulent cascades in weakly collisional plasmas. We show that the kinetic and fluid cascades are coupled, and their relative strengths depend on the plasma parameters. We also demonstrate the universality of the scaling exponents for the energy spectra of the turbulent fluctuations, which is consistent with observations.

Overall, our methodology entails developing a theoretical framework for astrophysical gyrokinetics and performing numerical simulations to test our predictions. Our work provides fundamental insights into the turbulent dynamics of weakly collisional plasmas, which are ubiquitous in astrophysical systems and play a crucial role in driving the energy transfer in these systems.

In this paper, we investigate Simple shear flow in inelastic Maxwell models. Our motivation is to explore the behavior and properties of viscoelastic fluids under simple shear flow. We aim to identify the underlying mechanisms of inelasticity and its effects on the material properties. To achieve this, we utilize a numerical simulation approach and focus on analyzing the shear viscosity and first normal stress difference of the model. 

Our results show that the first normal stress difference exhibits a non-monotonic behavior, where it initially increases, and then decreases with increasing shear rate. Furthermore, we observe that the shear viscosity decreases with increasing inelasticity, indicating that the materials become less resistant to deformation. We also investigate the effect of the relaxation time on the model behavior and show that it strongly influences the inelasticity effects on the material properties. 

Overall, our work attempts to solve the problem of understanding the underlying mechanisms of inelasticity in viscoelastic fluids under simple shear flow. Our findings provide valuable insights into the behavior of these materials, which can have potential applications in various fields, such as the design of industrial materials and the development of biomedical materials.

In this work, we present a new approach to understand the energy distribution in gravitational systems. We study the energy content of an axially symmetric solution in the teleparallel theory, which is equivalent to General Relativity. The methodology consists of starting from the teleparallel version of Einstein-Hilbert action, computing the field equations, and obtaining the gravitational potential and the energy conditions. We then obtain the energy-momentum complex and the energy density of a charged axially symmetric solution in teleparallel theory, which is a well-known family of exact solutions. This solution can describe the gravitational field outside and inside a highly compact, charged object, such as a black hole or a neutron star. The motivation for this research is to provide a new framework to understand the energy distribution in strongly gravitating objects, which is important for astrophysical applications. The result obtained shows that the energy density is positive everywhere outside the object, which means that the energy is indeed localized around the compact object. Our result can provide useful information for the interpretation of astrophysical observations and for the development of new models to study exotic objects such as black holes and neutron stars.

We conducted a Chandra archival study of the temperature and metal abundance profiles in hot Galaxy Clusters at 0.1 < z < 0.3 to gain a deeper understanding of the physical conditions within these systems. Our analysis focused on ten clusters with high-quality Chandra observations, allowing for detailed measurements of their temperature and metallicity profiles. Our results suggest that for most of the clusters in our sample, the core regions show a quasi-isothermal temperature profile, while the outskirts show signs of a cooling flow. Additionally, we found that the metallicity profiles are generally consistent with a constant value, although there is a slight decrease in some of the outer regions. 

The main goal of this study was to investigate the effects of environment on the physical properties of galaxy clusters at low redshifts, which can provide valuable insights into the formation and evolution of these structures over cosmic time. Our findings contribute to the ongoing efforts to refine our understanding of the baryonic physics within clusters, and to explore the implications of these physical processes on the large-scale structure of the universe. This work highlights the power of Chandra X-ray observations for probing the astrophysical properties of clusters of galaxies, and opens up new avenues for future studies in this field.

In this study, we investigate the dynamical state of the starless dense core FeSt 1-457 by analyzing its line-of-sight velocity and velocity dispersion. Our motivation for this research stems from the need to understand the early stages of star formation, which begins with the collapse of dense cores and the accumulation of matter within them. FeSt 1-457 is of particular interest because its morphology resembles that of a pulsating globule, a type of object that has been suggested to be an early stage of star formation. Using molecular line data from the IRAM 30m telescope, we find that FeSt 1-457 exhibits a complex velocity structure with two distinct velocity components. We also observe evidence of a velocity gradient in the core, which may indicate rotation. Based on our analysis of the velocity dispersion, we find that FeSt 1-457 is in a state of subsonic turbulence with no evidence of significant gravitational collapse. Our results suggest that FeSt 1-457 is not a pulsating globule, but rather a dense core that is evolving toward the formation of protostars. Our methodology involved performing spectral line analysis with the GILDAS software package. These findings contribute to our understanding of the early stages of star formation, and the role that dense cores play in this process.

In the dynamic world of wireless networking, providing seamless and high-quality service to users remains a challenge. The increasing and diverse traffic types and the heterogeneous wireless networks architecture compound service providers' difficulties in ensuring adaptive service provisioning. In light of this, our research explores a communication model that could enhance adaptive service provisioning in hybrid wireless networks.

Our work attempts to solve the problem of poor network performance and limited opportunities for dynamic service adjustment in this type of network. We propose a three-layer model that leverages machine learning to dynamically adapt to the changing network conditions and user service requirements. The model incorporates a feedback and control loop to continuously improve the service as it interacts with the user devices.

The key ideas in our work include the use of multi-layer service provisioning, machine learning-based network optimization, and a feedback responsive communication model. The model's methodology involves collecting data on user interactions with the network and applying machine learning algorithms to optimize the service. The model's output is then communicated back to the network for dynamic adjustment.

In conclusion, our proposed communication model provides a viable solution to the challenges of adaptive service provisioning in hybrid wireless networks. By integrating machine learning with adaptive provisioning, the model provides reliable and responsive service to users while improving wireless network performance.

Young open clusters are likely sites of cosmic ray acceleration as indicated
by recent detections of the TeV gamma-ray sources in the directions of two open
clusters (Cyg OB2 and Westerlund 2) and their directional proximity to some
unidentified EGRET sources. In fact, up to now a few different scenarios for
acceleration of particles inside open clusters have been considered, i.e.
shocks in massive star winds, pulsars and their nebulae, supernova shocks,
massive compact binaries. Here we consider in detail the radiation processes
due to both electrons and hadrons accelerated inside the open cluster. As a
specific scenario, we apply the acceleration process at the shocks arising in
the winds of WR type stars. Particles diffuse through the medium of the open
cluster during the activity time of the acceleration scenario defined by the
age of the WR star. They interact with the matter and radiation, at first
inside the open cluster and, later in the dense surrounding clouds. We
calculate the broad band spectrum in different processes for three example open
clusters (Berk 87, Cyg OB2, Westerlund 2) for which the best observational
constraints on the spectra are at present available. It is assumed that the
high energy phenomena, observed from the X-ray up to the GeV-TeV gamma-ray
energies, are related to each other. We conclude that the most likely
description of the radiation processes in these objects is achieved in the
hybrid (leptonic-hadronic) model in which leptons are responsible for the
observed X-ray and GeV gamma-ray emission and hadrons are responsible for the
TeV gamma-ray emission, which is produced directly inside and in dense clouds
surrounding the open cluster.

In this paper, we investigate the role of multiplicative white noise on the growth suppression dynamics of a parametric resonant system. Our motivation for this study lies in the realm of complex systems, where noise plays a significant role in the behavior of the system. The effect of white noise is especially interesting as it enables us to model the stochasticity of such systems, which is often present in real-world industrial applications and experiments, such as mechanical systems and electronic circuits. 

Our results demonstrate that the presence of multiplicative white noise leads to the suppression of growth in the parametric resonant system. Specifically, we show that the noise-induced growth suppression is more pronounced when the noise intensity is large, the resonant parameters are small, and the nonlinear damping coefficient is large. We provide analytical arguments outlining the key ideas of our approach and demonstrate our methodology using both analytic and numerical simulations. Our study is relevant to a wide range of scientific fields, including nonlinear dynamics, stochastic modeling, and statistical physics. Overall, our findings offer new insights into the dynamics of complex systems and contribute to the understanding of the impact of noise on their behavior.

In this paper, we propose a density tensor hierarchy for analyzing open system dynamics and retrieving the noise processes involved. The motivation behind this research is the need for accurate and efficient methods to model open quantum systems, which are often subject to external disruptions and dissipation. Traditional approaches such as master equations or Lindblad formalisms do not offer a complete view of the underlying noise processes, leading to incomplete or inaccurate descriptions of the system's evolution.

Our approach builds upon recent advances in tensor network methods and quantum information theory, allowing us to construct a hierarchy of density tensors that capture the different levels of correlations between the system and environment degrees of freedom. By explicitly analyzing the higher-order moments of the density tensor, we can extract information about the noise processes that affect the system, such as correlations, coherence, and non-Markovian effects.

As a result, we provide a powerful tool for analyzing the dynamics of open quantum systems, which can be applied in a wide range of applications, from quantum information processing to condensed matter physics. Our work addresses the problem of incomplete or incorrect modeling of open system dynamics and offers a novel perspective that can lead to a better understanding of the underlying noise processes.

Community structure is an important property of complex networks. An
automatic discovery of such structure is a fundamental task in many
disciplines, including sociology, biology, engineering, and computer science.
Recently, several community discovery algorithms have been proposed based on
the optimization of a quantity called modularity (Q). However, the problem of
modularity optimization is NP-hard, and the existing approaches often suffer
from prohibitively long running time or poor quality. Furthermore, it has been
recently pointed out that algorithms based on optimizing Q will have a
resolution limit, i.e., communities below a certain scale may not be detected.
In this research, we first propose an efficient heuristic algorithm, Qcut,
which combines spectral graph partitioning and local search to optimize Q.
Using both synthetic and real networks, we show that Qcut can find higher
modularities and is more scalable than the existing algorithms. Furthermore,
using Qcut as an essential component, we propose a recursive algorithm, HQcut,
to solve the resolution limit problem. We show that HQcut can successfully
detect communities at a much finer scale and with a higher accuracy than the
existing algorithms. Finally, we apply Qcut and HQcut to study a
protein-protein interaction network, and show that the combination of the two
algorithms can reveal interesting biological results that may be otherwise
undetectable.

We present the 2nd version of the Catalogue of Radial Velocities with
Astrometric Data (CRVAD-2). This is the result of the cross-identification of
stars from the All-Sky Compiled Catalogue of 2.5 Million Stars (ASCC-2.5) with
the General Catalogue of Radial Velocities and with other recently published
radial velocity lists and catalogues. The CRVAD-2 includes accurate J2000
equatorial coordinates, proper motions and trigonometric parallaxes in the
Hipparcos system, $B, V$ photometry in the Johnson system, spectral types,
radial velocities (RVs), multiplicity and variability flags for 54907 ASCC-2.5
stars. We have used the CRVAD-2 for a new determination of mean RVs of 363 open
clusters and stellar associations considering their established members from
proper motions and photometry in the ASCC-2.5. For 330 clusters and
associations we compiled previously published mean RVs from the literature,
critically reviewed and partly revised them. The resulting Catalogue of Radial
Velocities of Open Clusters and Associations (CRVOCA) contains about 460 open
clusters and about 60 stellar associations in the Solar neighbourhood. These
numbers still represent less than 30% of the total number of about 1820 objects
currently known in the Galaxy. The mean RVs of young clusters are generally
better known than those of older ones.

In this study, we present a novel virtual sensor based fault detection and classification method for a plasma etch reactor. The main objective of this research was to address the challenges associated with the traditional fault detection and classification methods that require data from physical sensors which can be expensive and maintain. Our proposed virtual sensor-based method uses soft sensors to measure the critical parameters of the plasma etch process, such as the plasma density, temperature, pressure and reaction rate. The method utilizes machine learning techniques, including Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Support Vector Machines (SVM), to classify and detect any faults that may occur in the process.         
 
The results of our study revealed that the virtual sensor-based method provides an accurate and reliable fault detection and classification performance. The proposed method can detect various types of faults, including plasma instability, pump malfunction, and precursor flow rate abnormality with an accuracy rate of over 95%. Additionally, the proposed method provides a fast response to faults, reducing the mean time between failure (MTBF) and increasing the productivity of the plasma etch reactor.   

The key ideas and methodology of this research involve developing a virtual sensor-based fault detection and classification framework for a plasma etch reactor, which integrates data-driven modeling, feature extraction, and machine learning algorithms. We used the Electronic Design Automation (EDA) software COMSOL Multiphysics to build a comprehensive model of the plasma etch reactor and simulate the process under various operating conditions. The data collected from the simulated model was used to train and test the virtual sensors and machine learning algorithms. The obtained results demonstrate the effectiveness and potential of the proposed method for improving the fault detection and classification performance of plasma etch reactors.

In this work, we present a novel approach to reason about a continuum of hypotheses based on finite evidence. Our motivation for this research is rooted in the realization that traditional statistical methods often rely on the assumption that there is a finite set of possible hypotheses. However, in many real-world scenarios, the true hypothesis may lie in a continuum of possibilities. To address this challenge, we propose a framework that allows for consistent reasoning about a continuum of hypotheses using a Bayesian approach. Our methodology is based on the use of a Bayesian functional prior, which characterizes the prior distribution over the space of hypotheses as a function. By applying this framework, we are able to make principled inferences about the true hypothesis using a finite amount of evidence.

We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on both synthetic and real-world data, highlighting the improved accuracy and robustness of our method compared to traditional approaches. Our method also provides a principled way of handling the prior distribution over the space of hypotheses, making it possible to incorporate prior knowledge in a flexible and transparent way. Overall, our work represents a significant step forward in the development of statistical methods capable of handling a continuum of hypotheses, with significant implications for a wide range of applications in science and engineering.

In this study, we present an economical approach for the implementation of a Polymer Optical Fiber (POF) demultiplexer. The key ideas behind this research are to design a low-cost and high-performance POF-based device. The proposed method employs a novel concept of a multi-fiber collimator and a single fiber end facet for the beam coupling and demultiplexing in the POF. The methodology of the study involves simulations and experimental evaluations, which have been carried out by the Finite Difference Time Domain (FDTD) simulation software and the experimentally recorded Optical Time Domain Reflectometry (OTDR) signal. The obtained results verify that the proposed POF demultiplexer works effectively over a wide range of wavelengths with high transmission rates and low insertion losses of less than 1.5 dB for a wavelength range of 650-850 nm. The proposed economical and practical POF demultiplexer can be used in various applications such as high-speed communication and sensing systems. The motivation for this research was prompted by the need for cheaper and efficient POF-based devices that can be used in real-world scenarios where cost and measurement accuracy are critical factors.

In this study, we investigate the mid-infrared (MIR) emission of M87, the brightest galaxy in the Virgo Cluster. We utilized observations from the Infrared Spectrograph (IRS) instrument aboard the Spitzer Space Telescope to generate MIR spectra of M87 that cover the range of 5-38 microns. Our analysis shows that the MIR emission of M87 is mainly composed of contributions from polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), silicates, and dust continuum emission. We found that the PAH emission band features in the MIR spectra of M87 strongly suggest photon-dominated region (PDR) chemistry, a process driven by ultraviolet radiation from young, massive stars. Analysis of the silicate dust emission provides further insights into the presence and structure of warm dust around M87's nucleus. 

The motivation for this research came from the desire to gain a better understanding of the physical and chemical processes that govern the MIR emission from one of the most massive galaxies in the local universe. This study also aimed to investigate the possibility of PDR chemistry in M87, which has not been extensively studied in previous works. 

Our research provides a detailed characterization of the MIR emission of M87 and helps to shed light on the physical processes that give rise to it. Our findings also demonstrate the effectiveness of IRS spectra in studying the MIR properties of nearby galaxies, which can have implications for future studies of other galaxies in the nearby and distant universe. By investigating the MIR properties of M87, we aim to contribute to a broader understanding of the physical and chemical processes that shape the evolution of massive galaxies.

We construct an analytic model for the rate of gas accretion onto a planet
embedded in a protoplanetary disk as a function of planetary mass, disk
viscosity, disk scale height, and unperturbed surface density in order to study
the long-term accretion and final masses of gas giant planets. We first derive
an analytical formula for surface density profile near the planetary orbit from
considerations of the balance of force and the dynamical stability. Using it in
the empirical formula linking surface density with gas accretion rate that is
derived based on hydrodynamic simulations of Tanigawa and Watanabe (2002, ApJ
586, 506), we then simulate the mass evolution of gas giant planets in
viscously-evolving disks. We finally determine the final mass as a function of
semi-major axis of the planet. We find that the disk can be divided into three
regions characterized by different processes by which the final mass is
determined. In the inner region, the planet grows quickly and forms a deep gap
to suppress the growth by itself before disk dissipation. The final mass of the
planet in this region is found to increase with the semi-major axis in a
similar way to the mass given by the viscous condition for gap opening, but the
former is larger by a factor of approximately 10 than the latter. In the
intermediate region, viscous diffusion of the disk gas limits the gas accretion
before the planet form a deep gap. The final mass can be up to the disk mass,
when disk viscous evolution occurs faster than disk evaporation. In the outer
region, planets capture only tiny amounts of gas within the lifetime of the
disk to form Neptune-like planets. We derive analytic formulae for the final
masses in the different regions and the locations of the boundaries, which are
helpful to gain a systematic understanding of the masses of gas giant planets.

We present constraints on the mean matter density, Omega_m, dark energy
density, Omega_de, and the dark energy equation of state parameter, w, using
Chandra measurements of the X-ray gas mass fraction (fgas) in 42 hot (kT>5keV),
X-ray luminous, dynamically relaxed galaxy clusters spanning the redshift range
0.05<z<1.1. Using only the fgas data for the 6 lowest redshift clusters at
z<0.15, for which dark energy has a negligible effect on the measurements, we
measure Omega_m=0.28+-0.06 (68% confidence, using standard priors on the Hubble
Constant, H_0, and mean baryon density, Omega_bh^2). Analyzing the data for all
42 clusters, employing only weak priors on H_0 and Omega_bh^2, we obtain a
similar result on Omega_m and detect the effects of dark energy on the
distances to the clusters at ~99.99% confidence, with Omega_de=0.86+-0.21 for a
non-flat LCDM model. The detection of dark energy is comparable in significance
to recent SNIa studies and represents strong, independent evidence for cosmic
acceleration. Systematic scatter remains undetected in the fgas data, despite a
weighted mean statistical scatter in the distance measurements of only ~5%. For
a flat cosmology with constant w, we measure Omega_m=0.28+-0.06 and
w=-1.14+-0.31. Combining the fgas data with independent constraints from CMB
and SNIa studies removes the need for priors on Omega_bh^2 and H_0 and leads to
tighter constraints: Omega_m=0.253+-0.021 and w=-0.98+-0.07 for the same
constant-w model. More general analyses in which we relax the assumption of
flatness and/or allow evolution in w remain consistent with the cosmological
constant paradigm. Our analysis includes conservative allowances for systematic
uncertainties. The small systematic scatter and tight constraints bode well for
future dark energy studies using the fgas method. (Abridged)

The phenomenon of ordering in quasi one-dimensional (quasi-1D) organic conductors has attracted significant scientific interest due to their rich electronic properties and potential applications in electronic devices. Our research aims to investigate the ordering phenomena in several quasi-1D organic conductors using a combination of experimental and theoretical approaches. Specifically, we focus on examining the role of electron-electron interactions and structural distortions on the electronic properties of these materials. 

We utilize a variety of experimental techniques, including X-ray diffraction and transport measurements, to study the crystal structures and electrical properties of the materials. Additionally, we employ numerical simulations and theoretical calculations to analyze the electronic properties of these materials. 

The motivation for this research stems from the promise of quasi-1D organic conductors to exhibit exotic electronic properties, such as charge density waves and spin density waves, which have potential applications in fields such as energy storage, superconductivity, and spintronics. The key problem that our work attempts to solve is to unravel the complex interplay between electron-electron interactions, lattice vibrations, and ordering phenomena in quasi-1D organic conductors. 

By improving our understanding of the fundamental physics of these materials, we hope to enable the development of novel electronic devices with improved performance characteristics. Our study contributes to the ongoing effort to advance the search for efficient, eco-friendly, and cost-effective materials for next-generation electronics.

In this research, we investigate the stellar population and origin of the high-latitude star-forming cloud CG 12. This cloud is located at a high galactic latitude of ~70° and has puzzled astronomers due to its unusual location and properties. To understand this cloud's nature, we obtained deep optical and infrared imaging data using the CTIO/MOSAIC-II camera and the Spitzer Space Telescope. These observations allowed us to conduct a detailed analysis of the population, age, and distribution of the stars in the region.

Our findings reveal that CG 12 is composed of at least two distinct, young stellar populations, which differ in age and spatial distribution. These populations exhibit a remarkably anomalous mass function, indicating extreme star formation conditions. Additionally, we find that the cloud is surrounded by a large, low-density shell, suggesting that it has undergone a violent encounter with a nearby massive star.

The results of this study provide important insights into the formation and evolution of high-latitude star-forming clouds and highlight the need for further investigations of these enigmatic systems. By shedding light on the origin and properties of CG 12, we can better understand the broader context of star formation in the Milky Way and other galaxies.

Recently the PVLAS collaboration reported the observation of a rotation of
linearly polarized laser light induced by a transverse magnetic field -- a
signal being unexpected within standard QED. In this review, we emphasize two
mechanisms which have been proposed to explain this result: production of a
single light neutral spin-zero particle or pair production of light minicharged
particles. We discuss a class of models, involving, in addition to our familiar
``visible'' photon, further light ``hidden paraphotons'', which mix
kinematically with the visible one, and further light paracharged particles. In
these models, very strong astrophysical and cosmological bounds on the weakly
interacting light particles mentioned above can be evaded. In the upcoming
year, a number of decisive laboratory based tests of the particle
interpretation of the PVLAS anomaly will be done. More generally, such
experiments, exploiting high fluxes of low-energy photons and/or large
electromagnetic fields, will dig into previously unconstrained parameter space
of the above mentioned models.

In this study, we investigate the origin of the work function by analyzing the electronic structure of metallic surfaces. The work function is an essential parameter in studying the physics and chemistry of surfaces, and it characterizes the energy required to remove an electron from the material. Despite its importance, the fundamental origins of the work function are not yet fully understood. 

In our work, we attempt to solve this problem by developing an analytical model based on first-principles calculations. Our approach is based on a combination of density functional theory and thermodynamic principles to explore the electronic structure of metallic surfaces and their corresponding work functions. 

We find that the work function is related to the surface dipole moment and the electronegativity of the atoms involved. Furthermore, we observe that the electronic density near the surface is significantly different from the bulk, leading to the work function’s sensitivity to surface perturbations. 

Overall, our theoretical analysis provides new insights into the mechanisms behind the work function and sheds light on how we can engineer surfaces with specific electronic properties. Our results contribute to the fundamental understanding of surface chemistry and provide valuable guidance for designing surface-based technologies.

In this work, we explore the possibility of generating neutrino Majorana masses from instanton effects in CFT orientifolds with MSSM-like spectra. The elusive nature of these masses has been a long-standing problem in the Standard Model and beyond. By exploiting the non-perturbative effects of instantons, we provide a concrete mechanism for their generation.

Our analysis is carried out in a five-dimensional supersymmetric framework, where the CFT orientifolds are realized as warped compactifications of string theory. We focus on a class of models that exhibit light MSSM-like spectra, which allows us to make contact with experimental data.

In our approach, we introduce a set of sterile neutrinos localized on a distant brane, which mix with the active ones through the Higgs field. The instanton-induced neutrino masses arise from the exchange of heavy Kaluza-Klein modes between the branes and are naturally suppressed by the warped geometry.

Our results suggest that instanton effects can play a crucial role in generating neutrino Majorana masses in realistic string-inspired models. This sheds light on the long-standing problem of neutrino mass generation and opens up new avenues for model building in the context of string theory.

The study of brain connectivity is a crucial step in understanding the network dynamics underlying brain function and behavior. In this paper, we investigate the use of graph theory metrics and anatomical proximity to predict the connectivity patterns of primate cortical networks. By combining a sample of anatomical connections across the primate brain with the topological properties of nodes, we constructed a model that successfully predicted the strength of connectivity between cortical regions with high accuracy. Our findings suggest that the combination of both topological and spatial properties of nodes provides a more nuanced and comprehensive understanding of the primate cortical network.

To achieve our results, we first established a data-driven methodology to collect and curate a comprehensive set of anatomical connection data across seven primate species. We then utilized graph theory techniques to extract topological properties such as degree, clustering coefficient, and betweenness centrality for each node in the network. We found that these topological properties were highly predictive of the connectivity strength between nodes after controlling for anatomical distance. Additionally, we observed that incorporating spatial distance further increased the predictive power of our model, indicating the importance of both topological and spatial features in predicting connectivity.

Our work has important implications for understanding how the primate brain is organized and how it functions. By identifying crucial properties of the primate cortical network, our findings can guide future research and contribute to our overall understanding of brain function and evolution.

Let $S =\{x\in \re^n: g_1(x)\geq 0, ..., g_m(x)\geq 0\}$ be a semialgebraic
set defined by multivariate polynomials $g_i(x)$. Assume $S$ is convex, compact
and has nonempty interior. Let $S_i =\{x\in \re^n: g_i(x)\geq 0\}$, and $\bdS$
(resp. $\bdS_i$) be the boundary of $S$ (resp. $S_i$). This paper discusses
whether $S$ can be represented as the projection of some LMI representable set.
Such $S$ is called semidefinite representable or SDP representable. The
contributions of this paper: {\bf (i)} Assume $g_i(x)$ are all concave on $S$.
If the positive definite Lagrange Hessian (PDLH) condition holds, i.e., the
Hessian of the Lagrange function for optimization problem of minimizing any
nonzero linear function $\ell^Tx$ on $S$ is positive definite at the minimizer,
then $S$ is SDP representable. {\bf (ii)} If each $g_i(x)$ is either
sos-concave ($-\nabla^2g_i(x)=W(x)^TW(x)$ for some possibly nonsquare matrix
polynomial $W(x)$) or strictly quasi-concave on $S$, then $S$ is SDP
representable. {\bf (iii)} If each $S_i$ is either sos-convex or poscurv-convex
($S_i$ is compact convex, whose boundary has positive curvature and is
nonsingular, i.e. $\nabla g_i(x) \not = 0$ on $\bdS_i \cap S$), then $S$ is SDP
representable. This also holds for $S_i$ for which $\bdS_i \cap S$ extends
smoothly to the boundary of a poscurv-convex set containing $S$. {\bf (iv)} We
give the complexity of Schm\"{u}dgen and Putinar's matrix Positivstellensatz,
which are critical to the proofs of (i)-(iii).

We explore the physics behind one of the brightest radio afterglows ever, GRB
030329, at late times when the jet is non-relativistic. We determine the
physical parameters of the blast wave and its surroundings, in particular the
index of the electron energy distribution, the energy of the blast wave, and
the density (structure) of the circumburst medium. We then compare our results
with those from image size measurements. We observed the GRB 030329 radio
afterglow with the Westerbork Synthesis Radio Telescope and the Giant Metrewave
Radio Telescope at frequencies from 325 MHz to 8.4 GHz, spanning a time range
of 268-1128 days after the burst. We modeled all the available radio data and
derived the physical parameters. The index of the electron energy distribution
is p=2.1, the circumburst medium is homogeneous, and the transition to the
non-relativistic phase happens at t_NR ~ 80 days. The energy of the blast wave
and density of the surrounding medium are comparable to previous findings. Our
findings indicate that the blast wave is roughly spherical at t_NR, and they
agree with the implications from the VLBI studies of image size evolution. It
is not clear from the presented dataset whether we have seen emission from the
counter jet or not. We predict that the Low Frequency Array will be able to
observe the afterglow of GRB 030329 and many other radio afterglows,
constraining the physics of the blast wave during its non-relativistic phase
even further.

In this study, we present a novel and efficient technique for monitoring the performance of the Large Area Telescope (LAT) instrument on board the Gamma-ray Large Area Space Telescope (GLAST). The primary aim of this research is to provide real-time monitoring of the LAT instrument's status and performance and detect any anomalies or degradation that may occur. Such a technique is crucial for ensuring the accurate detection and analysis of gamma-ray sources and phenomena.

Our methodology involves collecting and analyzing the data from various sources, including the instrument's control systems and telemetry, and cross-correlating the data with a predefined set of performance parameters. These parameters are continuously monitored and compared against the expected values to assess the instrument's health and functionality.

Our results indicate that the proposed technique is effective in detecting any deviations from the expected performance parameters, thus enabling early identification and diagnosis of potential problems. Furthermore, the technique allows for efficient management of the instrument's resources and optimization of its operations.

The problem that our work attempts to solve is the need for a reliable method of monitoring the performance of the LAT instrument on board the GLAST satellite. The proposed technique addresses this problem by providing a real-time monitoring system that ensures the instrument's continual, optimal functionality in detecting gamma-ray sources and phenomena.

In this study, we investigate the post-oligarchic evolution of protoplanetary embryos and its impact on the stability of planetary systems. Our research aims to solve the problem of understanding the formation and evolution of planetary systems, which are complex and dynamic systems that involve the interactions between small bodies, massive planets, and their host stars. Specifically, we focus on the late stages of the formation process, after the oligarchic growth phase, when the embryos are dynamically excited due to mutual gravitational interactions.

We use N-body simulations that include both the gravitational interactions and the physical collisions between embryos. Our results show that the post-oligarchic evolution leads to significant changes in the size and eccentricity distributions of the embryos, as well as their mutual inclinations. These changes can have a profound impact on the final architecture of the planetary system, including the distribution of planets in different orbits and the presence or absence of gaps in the disc.

Our motivation for this research is to better understand the formation and evolution of planetary systems, which is one of the most fundamental questions in astronomy and astrophysics. The results of this study can have important implications for the search for habitable planets and the understanding of the diversity of planetary systems in the Universe.

Our work titled "Information Diffusion Epidemics in Social Networks" aims to explore and understand the process of information diffusion in social networks. The primary problem that we attempt to solve is to identify the factors that impact the speed and extent of information diffusion in different types of social networks.

The motivation for this research stems from the increasing reliance on social networks for disseminating information and the subsequent impact it has on society. In recent years, social networks have played a crucial role in shaping public opinion and influencing societal behavior. Hence it is essential to comprehend how information spreads in social networks to anticipate the propagation of fake news, rumors, and other forms of information.

We study the diffusion of information in various types of social networks, from small-scale homogeneous networks to large heterogeneous ones. We utilize network analysis and mathematical models to understand the parameters influencing the diffusion process. Our work's outcome indicates that the network's structure significantly affects the speed and extent of information spread, with some types of networks being more susceptible to epidemics.

In conclusion, our research sheds light on the dynamics of information diffusion in social networks, offering essential insights into the underlying principles governing the process. Our study provides a foundation for future research aimed at controlling the spread of misinformation in social networks.

In our experimental study, we investigated the effects of varying optical properties of boundary surfaces on the Casimir force. This force, initially observed as an attractive force between two metal plates, emerges due to quantum fluctuations in the vacuum. The Casimir force plays a significant role in several areas of physics, such as nanotechnology and fundamental physics. 

Our work attempts to address the issue of disparate Casimir forces encountered in various systems that are due to different optical properties of their boundary surfaces. This problem arises because the boundary surface's optical characteristics affect how electromagnetic fields interact with it. To circumvent this issue, we performed experimental studies by creating microfabricated surfaces with varying optical properties. Our results show that by altering the surface's optical properties, we could control and manipulate the Casimir force to decrease or increase its magnitude. 

Our research highlights the importance of the boundary surface's optical properties in determining the Casimir force. This insight can inspire advancements in nanotechnology and also provide further insights into the fundamental aspects of quantum field theory and the vacuum's fluctuations. Our experimental methods provide a potential approach for studying the influence of other surface properties on the Casimir force in future studies.

We obtain new invariants of topological link concordance and homology
cobordism of 3-manifolds from Hirzebruch-type intersection form defects of
towers of iterated p-covers. Our invariants can extract geometric information
from an arbitrary depth of the derived series of the fundamental group, and can
detect torsion which is invisible via signature invariants. Applications
illustrating these features include the following: (1) There are infinitely
many homology equivalent rational 3-spheres which are indistinguishable via
multisignatures, eta-invariants, and L2-signatures but have distinct homology
cobordism types. (2) There is an infinite family of 2-torsion (amphichiral)
knots, including the figure eight knot, with non-slice iterated Bing doubles;
as a special case, we give the first proof of the conjecture that the Bing
double of the figure eight knot is not slice. (3) There exist infinitely many
torsion elements at any depth of the Cochran-Orr-Teichner filtration of link
concordance.

We re-discuss the evolutionary state of upper main sequence magnetic stars
using a sample of Ap and Bp stars with accurate Hipparcos parallaxes and
definitely determined longitudinal magnetic fields. We confirm our previous
results obtained from the study of Ap and Bp stars with accurate measurements
of the mean magnetic field modulus and mean quadratic magnetic fields that
magnetic stars of mass M < 3 M_sun are concentrated towards the centre of the
main-sequence band. In contrast, stars with masses M > 3 M_sun seem to be
concentrated closer to the ZAMS. The study of a few known members of nearby
open clusters with accurate Hipparcos parallaxes confirms these conclusions.
Stronger magnetic fields tend to be found in hotter, younger and more massive
stars, as well as in stars with shorter rotation periods. No evidence is found
for any loss of angular momentum during the main-sequence life. The magnetic
flux remains constant over the stellar life time on the main sequence. An
excess of stars with large obliquities beta is detected in both higher and
lower mass stars. The obliquity angle distribution as inferred from the
distribution of r-values appears random at the time magnetic stars become
observable on the H-R diagram. After quite a short time spent on the main
sequence, the obliquity angle beta tends to reach values close to either 90 deg
or 0 deg for M < 3 M_sun. The evolution of the obliquity angle beta seems to be
somewhat different for low and high mass stars. While we find a strong hint for
an increase of beta with the elapsed time on the main sequence for stars with M
> 3 M_sun, no similar trend is found for stars with M < 3 M_sun. However, the
predominance of high values of beta at advanced ages in these stars is notable.

We have used the very large JVAS/CLASS 8.4-GHz surveys of flat-spectrum radio
sources to test the hypothesis that there is a systematic alignment of
polarization position angle vectors on cosmological scales of the type claimed
by Hutsemekers et al (2005). The polarization position angles of 4290 sources
with polarized flux density >=1 mJy have been examined. They do not reveal
large-scale alignments either as a whole or when split in half into
high-redshift (z >= 1.24) and low-redshift sub-samples. Nor do the radio
sources which lie in the specific areas covered by Hutsemekers et al (2005).
show any significant effect. We have also looked at the position angles of
parsec-scale jets derived from VLBI observations and again find no evidence for
systematic alignments. Finally, we have investigated the correlation between
the polarization position angle and those of the parsec-scale jets. As
expected, we find that there is a tendency for the polarization angles to be
perpendicular to the jet angles. However, the difference in jet and
polarization position angles does not show any systematic trend in different
parts of the sky.

We present a study on two energy release processes for coronal mass ejections (CMEs): the magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) catastrophe and magnetic reconnection. Our research is motivated by the importance of understanding the complex dynamics of CMEs, which can have a significant impact on the Earth's space environment. We investigate these two energy release processes by simulating a CME with a 3D numerical MHD model.

Our results show that the MHD catastrophe plays a significant role in the initial phase of the CME, during which the magnetic field rapidly expands and accelerates, leading to the ejection of plasma into interplanetary space. On the other hand, magnetic reconnection is responsible for the energy release in the later stages of the CME, where it can cause the magnetic field to relax and form a flux rope structure. We also find that the strength and orientation of the magnetic field play a crucial role in determining which energy release process dominates during a CME event.

Our study contributes to the ongoing efforts to improve our understanding of CMEs and their potential impact on our planet.
