By identifying the key factors that drive the two energy release processes, we provide valuable insights for future space weather forecasting. Our methodology utilizes advanced MHD numerical simulations and is based on a rigorous treatment of the underlying physical processes, which enables us to obtain accurate results and quantifiable predictions.

In this work, we present a study of surface plasmon polaritons (SPPs) and surface phonon polaritons (SPhPs) on metallic and semiconducting spheres using exact and semiclassical descriptions. We analyze the coupling between these polaritons modes generated by a dipole emitter located near the surface of spheres of different materials and sizes. Our methodology involves the use of the boundary element method to compute eigenmodes, and Green's functions to calculate the scattering amplitudes. We apply the rigorous coupled wave analysis and the Mie theory to obtain analytical expressions as a benchmark for the numerical results.

Our work aims to solve the problem of the mutual interaction between SPPs and SPhPs in a mixed-metal-semiconductor system. This problem is of interest for various applications, such as sensing, light manipulation, and energy conversion. The motivation behind our research is to investigate the fundamental physics behind SPP-SPhP hybridization and its impact on the optical properties of nanostructured materials. Furthermore, we explore the role of geometric parameters, such as the size and the shape of the particles, on the hybridization process.

Our findings demonstrate that the exact and semiclassical methods complement each other in describing the behavior of nanospheres, especially for small particles and in the presence of loss and dispersion. We highlight the importance of the polaritonic coupling in controlling the scattering spectra and the local field enhancements. Our results provide insights into how to tailor the optical response of hybrid systems, paving the way for developing new devices based on nanophotonics.

In this study, we present the discovery and characterization of five intermediate-period planets from the Next Generation Transit Survey (NGTS) N2K sample. The motivation for this research lies in the need for a better understanding of the planet occurrence rates and how they vary with host star properties, which is crucial for the development of robust planet formation and evolution theories. The N2K sample is particularly valuable for this objective, as it comprises a set of high-quality, high-cadence radial velocity measurements for stars that have been observed at high precision by NGTS photometry. Our analysis employed a Bayesian framework for exoplanet detection and characterization, implemented through nested sampling techniques. We estimate the planet occurrence rate and distribution, as well as the mass-radius relation and orbital eccentricity of each planet. Our results indicate that the occurrence rate of intermediate-period planets around solar-type stars is higher than previously thought. We also find that these planets have a relatively low eccentricity and are consistent with terrestrial planets. Overall, our study contributes to the understanding of the exoplanet population and evolution.

In this study, we investigate the projectile fragmentation of the $^{86}$Kr beam at 64 MeV/nucleon, by using the fragment separator at the Radioactive Ion Beam Factory, Japan. The motivation for this research is to understand the fragmentation mechanism and the production of light and heavy fragments in intermediate energy heavy-ion collisions. This research attempts to provide insight into the production of heavy neutron-rich nuclei, which can be used in various applications in nuclear physics research. By measuring the fragment momenta and charges, we examined the fragmentation pattern and the production cross-sections for various fragments. We found that the production cross-sections for lighter fragments are higher than those for heavier ones, indicating that the light fragments are more readily produced than the heavy ones. We also observed that the isotopic distribution of the fragments is consistent with the statistical model of the fragmentation process. Finally, we compare our results with previous experimental data and theoretical models, highlighting the importance of the projectile-target combination and emphasizing our work's contribution to the understanding of projectile fragmentation at intermediate energies.

Early measurements of SN 1987A indicate a beam/jet (BJ) which hit polar
ejecta (PE) to produce the "Mystery Spot" (MS). The SN flash takes an extra 8 d
to hit the MS, and this was confirmed at 2e39 ergs/s in the optical at day 8. A
ramp in luminosity starting near day 10 indicates particles from the BJ hitting
the PE, with the fastest particles traveling at 0.8 c, and an upper limit for
the optical luminosity of the MS of 5e40 ergs/s at day 20. The details of SN
1987A strongly suggest that it resulted from a merger of 2 stellar cores of a
common envelope (CE) binary, i.e. a "double-degenerate" (DD)-initiated SN, and
is thus the Rosetta Stone for 99% of MSPs in the non-core-collapsed globular
clusters, GRBs, and SNe, including all recent nearby SNe except SN 1986J and
the more distant SN 2006gy. Without having to blast through the CE of Sk -69
202, it is likely that the BJ would have caused a full, long-soft gamma-ray
burst (lGRB) upon hitting the PE, thus DD can produce lGRBs. The typical 0.5
deg collimation of a GRB, over the 22 lt-d from SN 1987A to its PE, produces
~100 s of delay, MATCHING the observed delay of the non-prompt parts of lGRBs.
Because DD must be the dominant SN mechanism in elliptical galaxies, where only
short, hard GRBs (sGRBs) have been observed, DD without CE or PE must also make
sGRBs, thus the initial photon spectrum of 99% of ALL GRBs is KNOWN, & neutron
star (NS)-NS mergers may not make GRBs as we know them. Observations of Ia's
strongly suggest that these are also DD, implying another systematic effect in
Ia Cosmology, as Ia's will appear to be Ic's when viewed from their DD merger
poles, given sufficient matter above that lost to core- collapse (otherwise it
would just beg the question of what ELSE they could possibly be). There is no
need to invent exotica, such as collapsars or hypernovae, to account for GRBs.

New information on short/hard gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) is being gathered
thanks to the discovery of their optical and X-ray afterglows. However, some
key aspects are still poorly understood, including the collimation level of the
outflow, the duration of the central engine activity, and the properties of the
progenitor systems. We want to constrain the physical properties of the short
GRB 050724 and of its host galaxy, and make some inferences on the global short
GRB population. We present optical observations of the afterglow of GRB 050724
and of its host galaxy, significantly expanding the existing dataset for this
event. We compare our results with models, complementing them with available
measurements from the literature. We study the afterglow light curve and
spectrum including X-ray data. We also present observations of the host galaxy.
The observed optical emission was likely related to the large flare observed in
the X-ray light curve. The apparent steep decay was therefore not due to the
jet effect. Available data are indeed consistent with low collimation, in turn
implying a large energy release, comparable to that of long GRBs. The flare
properties also constrain the internal shock mechanism, requiring a large
Lorentz factor contrast between the colliding shells. This implies that the
central engine was active at late times, rather than ejecting all shells
simultaneously. The host galaxy has red colors and no ongoing star formation,
consistent with previous findings on this GRB. However, it is not a pure
elliptical, and has some faint spiral structure. GRB 050724 provides the most
compelling case for association between a short burst and a galaxy with old
stellar population. It thus plays a pivotal role in constraining progenitors
models, which should allow for long delays between birth and explosion.

We present a white paper highlighting the importance of finding solar system analogs using astrometric measurements from the Space Interferometry Mission (SIM) and the Hipparcos mission. The aim of this paper is to guide the ExoPlanet Task Force in identifying and prioritizing potential targets for future missions. We believe that understanding the physical and chemical properties of other solar systems is crucial in order to decipher the origins and evolution of our own solar system. Our strategy revolves around first identifying stars that have properties similar to the Sun, such as mass, age, and metallicity. We then use SIM and Hipparcos data to measure these stars' astrometric signatures, which are indicators of the presence of planets in their orbits. Using this method, we identify stars with planets in the habitable zone, as well as those with planetary companions in different regions of potential habitability. With the ultimate goal of characterizing the atmosphere and habitability of exoplanets, finding analogs to our solar system is a crucial step forward. We recommend the ExoPlanet Task Force to consider the use of SIM and Hipparcos data to prioritize future exoplanet missions, thereby laying the foundation for continued exploration of the cosmos.

We present the results of a pointing survey toward central regions of starless cores conducted using the HCN J = 1-0 hyperfine transition. Our goal was to probe inward motions within these cores and assess their potential role in the early stages of star formation. Starless cores are small, dense regions of gas and dust that represent the earliest stages of star formation. Previous studies have shown that these cores exhibit inward radial motions, which suggest that they are undergoing gravitational collapse. However, the exact nature and properties of these motions are still not well understood. In this work, we report the first comprehensive survey of the inward motions in six starless cores, which were selected based on their well-defined and compact appearance in near-infrared extinction maps. We find that the majority of the observed cores show evidence of inward motions, supporting the notion that gravitational collapse is a dominant mechanism driving early stages of star formation. Our results provide important new insights into the fundamental properties of starless cores and have implications for our understanding of the formation of stars and planetary systems.

We present a study on the dynamics of massive BHs in galaxy mergers, obtained
from a series of high-resolution N-Body/SPH simulations. The presence of a
gaseous component is essential for the rapid formation of an eccentric
(Keplerian) BH binary, that resides at the center of a massive (~10^9 Msun)
turbulent nuclear disc. Using physically and/or numerically motivated recipes,
we follow the accretion history of the BHs during the merger. The mass of the
BHs increases as large central inflows of gas occur inside each galaxy, and
their mass ratio varies with time. Given the encountered strong degeneracy
between numerical resolution and physical assumptions, we suggest here three
possible paths followed by the galaxies and the BHs during a merger in order to
fulfill the M-sigma relation : Adjustment, Symbiosis, and BH Dominance. In an
extremely high resolution run, we resolved the turbulent gas pattern down to
parsec scales, and found that BH feedback is expected to be effective near the
end of the merger. We then trace the BH binary orbit down to a scale of 0.1 pc
modeling the nuclear disc as an equilibrium Mestel disc composed either of gas,
gas and stars, or just stars. Under the action of dynamical friction against
the rotating gaseous and/or stellar background the orbit circularizes. When
this occurs, each BH is endowed with its own small-size (~0.01 pc) accretion
disc comprising a few percent of the BH mass. Double AGN activity is expected
to occur on an estimated timescale of ~10 Myrs, comparable to the inspiral
time. The double nuclear point--like sources that may appear have typical
separations of ~10 pc, and are likely to be embedded in the still ongoing
starburst.

We describe the role of GeV gamma-ray observations with GLAST-LAT (Gamma-ray
Large Area Space Telescope - Large Area Telescope) in identifying interaction
sites of cosmic-ray proton (or hadrons) with interstellar medium (ISM). We
expect to detect gamma rays from neutral pion decays in high-density ISM
regions in the Galaxy, Large Magellanic Cloud, and other satellite galaxies.
These gamma-ray sources have been detected already with EGRET (Energetic Gamma
Ray Experiment Telescope) as extended sources (eg. LMC and Orion clouds) and
GLAST-LAT will detect many more with a higher spatial resolution and in a wider
spectral range. We have developed a novel image restoration technique based on
the Richardson-Lucy algorithm optimized for GLAST-LAT observation of extended
sources. Our algorithm calculates PSF (point spread function) for each event.
This step is very important for GLAST-LAT and EGRET image analysis since PSF
varies more than one order of magnitude from one gamma ray to another depending
on its energy as well as its impact point and angle in the instrument. The
GLAST-LAT and EGRET image analysis has to cope with Poisson fluctuation due to
low number of detected photons for most sources. Our technique incorporates
wavelet filtering to minimize effects due to the fluctuation. Preliminary
studies on some EGRET sources are presented, which shows potential of this
novel image restoration technique for the identification and characterisation
of extended gamma-ray sources.

We develop velocity-dependent models describing the evolution of string
networks that involve several types of interacting strings, each with a
different tension. These incorporate the formation of Y-type junctions with
links stretching between colliding strings, while always ensuring energy
conservation. These models can be used to describe network evolution for
non-abelian strings as well as cosmic superstrings. The application to $Z_{N}$
strings in which interactions are topologically constrained, demonstrates that
a scaling regime is generally reached which involves a hierarchy of string
densities with the lightest most abundant. We also study hybrid networks of
cosmic superstrings, where energetic considerations are more important in
determining interaction outcomes. We again find that networks tend towards
scaling, with the three lightest network components being dominant and having
comparable number densities, while the heavier string states are suppressed. A
more quantitative analysis depends on the precise calculation of the string
interaction matrix using the underlying string or field theory. Nevertheless,
these results provide further evidence that the presence of junctions in a
string network does not obstruct scaling.

It has recently been suggested that exchange spring media offer a way to
increase media density without causing thermal instability
(superparamagnetism), by using a hard and a soft layer coupled by exchange.
Victora has suggested a figure of merit xi = 2 E_b/mu_0 m_s H_sw, the ratio of
the energy barrier to that of a Stoner-Wohlfarth system with the same switching
field, which is 1 for a Stoner-Wohlfarth (coherently switching) particle and 2
for an optimal two-layer composite medium. A number of theoretical approaches
have been used for this problem (e.g., various numbers of coupled
Stoner-Wohlfarth layers and continuum micromagnetics). In this paper we show
that many of these approaches can be regarded as special cases or
approximations to a variational formulation of the problem, in which the energy
is minimized for fixed magnetization. The results can be easily visualized in
terms of a plot of the energy as a function of magnetic moment m_z, in which
both the switching field [the maximum slope of E(m_z)] and the stability
(determined by the energy barrier E_b) are geometrically visible. In this
formulation we can prove a rigorous limit on the figure of merit xi, which can
be no higher than 4. We also show that a quadratic anistropy suggested by Suess
et al comes very close to this limit.

In our research, we aim to evaluate personal archiving strategies for internet-based information. The motivation behind our work is the growing significance of the internet for storing and accessing our personal information, as well as the increasing need to preserve this information for future use. Given the sheer volume of information we generate and store online, it is essential to have effective personal archiving strategies that can help us manage, preserve, and access our digital content.

To this end, our work focuses on identifying the key challenges that individuals face when attempting to archive their online information, including the lack of clear guidelines on how to archive digital materials, the complexity of information storage and retrieval systems, and the limited compatibility of archiving tools. We also highlight the varying needs of different user groups, including digital natives, older adults, and professionals, and the need to design archiving strategies that cater to their specific requirements.

Our methodology involves a systematic literature review of existing personal archiving strategies for internet-based information and an empirical study involving semi-structured interviews with individuals who have experience with personal archiving. We analyze the data obtained from the literature review and interviews, using thematic analysis to identify the most prevalent themes and issues. Our work seeks to contribute to the growing body of research on personal archiving by providing actionable insights into effective archiving strategies for internet-based information and highlighting the importance of catering to the needs of different user groups.

In this work, we investigate the sensing capacity of sensor networks for the class of linear observation, fixed Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) models. The key idea behind our research is that the quality of data gathered by a sensor network depends on the number of sensors deployed and the quality of the sensor signals. To study this, we establish a mathematical model for the observed signals. By calculating the Fisher information, we derive the sensing capacity for the linear observation model. We then compare our results to the theoretical limit and analyze their performance for different scenarios.

Our methodology comprises the formulation of a stochastic model for the network and the statistical analysis of the data to determine the sensing capacity. Our analytical approach uses probabilistic inference and statistical decision theory to analyze the estimation error for different parameters of the SNR model, and to optimize the performance of the sensor network.

Our results show that the sensing capacity of a sensor network is directly proportional to the number of sensors deployed and the SNR of the sensor signals for the linear observation model. Our findings provide useful insights for designing efficient sensor networks and optimizing their performance for different applications. The motivation for this research is to enhance our understanding of the fundamental limits and capabilities of sensor networks, which are essential for several real-world applications such as environmental monitoring, surveillance, and healthcare.

We carry out and analyze new multi-color photometry of the Galactic globular
cluster (GC) M75 in UBVI and focus on the brighter sequences of the color-
magnitude diagram (CMD), with particular emphasis on their location in U-based
CMD. Specifically, we study the level both of the horizontal (HB) and red giant
branches (RGB) relative to the main-sequence turnoff (TO) in the U magnitude.
Along with the presented photometry of M75, we use our collection of
photometric data on GCs belonging to the metal-poor range, [Fe/H]zw<-1.1 dex,
obtained from observations with different equipment, but calibrated by standard
stars situated in the observed cluster fields. We confirm our earlier finding,
and extend it to a larger magnitude range.We demonstrate that DeltaU_{TO}^{BHB}
expressing the difference in U magnitude between the TO point and the level of
the blue HB, near its red boundary, of the metal-poor GCs observed with the
EMMI camera of the NTT/ESO telescope is about 0.4-0.5 mag smaller as compared
to GCs observed with the 100" telescope and 1.3 m Warsaw telescope of the Las
Campanas Observatory. At the same time, Delta U_{TO}^{RGB}, the difference in U
magnitude between the TO and RGB inflection (brightest) points, does not show
such an apparent dependence on the characteristics of U filters used, but it
depends on cluster metallicity. We have shown, for the first time, the
dependence of the parameter DeltaU_{TO}^{RGB} on [Fe/H] and have estimated its
analytical expression, by assuming a linear relation between the parameter and
metallicity. Its slope, Delta U_{TO}^{RGB}/Delta [Fe/H]~1.2 mag/dex, is approx.
a factor of two steeper than that of the dependence of the RGB bump position in
the V magnitude on metallicity. The asymptotic giant branch (AGB) clump and
features of the RGB luminosity function (LF) of M75 are also discussed.

The validity of the DFT models implemented by FIREBALL for CNT electronic
device modeling is assessed. The effective masses, band gaps, and transmission
coefficients of semi-conducting, zigzag, (n,0) carbon nanotubes (CNTs)
resulting from the ab initio tight-binding density functional theory (DFT) code
FIREBALL and the empirical, nearest-neighbor pi-bond model are compared for all
semiconducting n values 5 <(=) n <(=) 35. The DFT values for the effective
masses differ from the pi-bond values by +(-) 9% over the range of n values, 17
<(=) n <(=) 29, most important for electronic device applications. Over the
range 13 <(=) n <(=) 35, the DFT bandgaps are less than the empirical bandgaps
by 20-180 meV depending on the functional and the n value. The pi-bond model
gives results that differ signifcantly from the DFT results when the CNT
diameter goes below 1 nm due to the large curvature of the CNT. The pi-bond
model quickly becomes inaccurate away from the bandedges for a (10, 0) CNT, and
it is completely inaccurate for n <(=) 8.

Single molecule data made of on and off events are ubiquitous. Famous
examples include enzyme turnover, probed via fluorescence, and opening and
closing of ion-channel, probed via the flux of ions. The data reflects the
dynamics in the underlying multi-substate on-off kinetic scheme (KS) of the
process, but the determination of the underlying KS is difficult, and sometimes
even impossible, due to the loss of information in the mapping of the
mutli-dimensional KS onto two dimensions. A way to deal with this problem
considers canonical (unique) forms. (Unique canonical form is constructed from
an infinitely long trajectory, but many KSs.) Here we introduce canonical forms
of reduced dimensions that can handle any KS (i.e. also KSs with symmetry and
irreversible transitions). We give the mapping of KSs into reduced dimensions
forms, which is based on topology of KSs, and the tools for extracting the
reduced dimensions form from finite data. The canonical forms of reduced
dimensions constitute a powerful tool in discriminating between KSs.

Our work on Two-photon transitions in hydrogen and cosmological recombination investigates the intricate details of atomic physics in the early universe. The study is motivated by the need to understand the recombination process that occurred when the Universe was approximately 300,000 years old. During this epoch, the primordial plasma of free electrons combined with atomic nuclei to form neutral atoms, marking the end of the opaque epoch. The recombination era produced the cosmic microwave background radiation that we observe today. 

Our research focuses on the two-photon transitions of hydrogen atoms, which play a significant role in the recombination process. By carefully examining the two-photon processes, we aim to develop a more precise understanding of the recombination era, and consequently, to improve our understanding of the early universe. 

We employ a combination of theoretical and computational methods to simulate the behavior of hydrogen atoms under different environmental conditions. The problem we attempt to solve is the lack of a completely accurate model of hydrogen's two-photon transitions and its impact on the recombination process. 

Through our work, we aim to contribute to the broader field of cosmology by filling in critical knowledge gaps about the early universe's evolution. Our findings will provide a more comprehensive understanding of the recombination epoch and the universe's formation and evolution.

In this work, we present the use of colour pairs as a tool to constrain the age and metallicity of stellar populations. The motivation of this research lies in the need to improve our understanding of the history and evolution of galaxies, which can only be achieved by accurately determining the age and metallicity of their constituent stars.

Our key idea is to utilise colour pairs, defined as the difference in magnitudes between two filters, to obtain a more reliable measure of age and metallicity. We employ this methodology on synthetic spectra from a grid of models, covering a variety of stellar ages and metallicities. By comparing the resulting colours with observational data from a sample of real galaxies, we are able to calibrate the colour pairs to accurately predict the age and metallicity of stars within these galaxies.

Our results show that the use of colour pairs can significantly improve the accuracy of age and metallicity determinations, reducing the potential biases caused by differences in dust extinction and star formation histories. Furthermore, we demonstrate the versatility of this technique, showing that it can be applied to a wide range of galaxies with varying properties.

Overall, our use of colour pairs provides a valuable tool for constraining stellar ages and metallicities, while also improving our understanding of galaxy evolution.

This paper attempts to define a generalisation of the standard Einstein
condition (in conformal/metric geometry) to any parabolic geometry. To do so,
it shows that any preserved involution $\sigma$ of the adjoint bundle $\mc{A}$
gives rise, given certain algebraic conditions, to a unique preferred affine
connection $\nabla$ with covariantly constant rho-tensor $\mathsf{P}$,
compatible with the algebraic bracket on $\mc{A}$. These conditions can
reasonably be considered the generalisations of the Einstein condition, and
recreate the standard Einstein condition in conformal geometry. The existence
of such an involution is implies by some simpler structures: preserved metrics
when the overall algebra $\mf{g}$ is $\mf{sl}(m,\mbb{F})$, preserved complex
structures anti-commuting with the skew-form for $\mf{g}=\mf{sp}(2m,\mbb{F})$,
and preserved subundles of the tangent bundle, of a certain rank, for all the
other non-exceptional simple Lie algebras. Examples of Einstein involutions are
constructed or referenced for several geometries. The existence of cone
constructions for certain Einstein involutions is then demonstrated.

The purpose of this paper is to find optimal estimates for the Green function
of a half-space of {\it the relativistic $\alpha$-stable process} with
parameter $m$ on $\Rd$ space. This process has an infinitesimal generator of
the form $mI-(m^{2/\alpha}I-\Delta)^{\alpha/2},$ where $0<\alpha<2$, $m>0$, and
reduces to the isotropic $\alpha$-stable process for $m=0$. Its potential
theory for open bounded sets has been well developed throughout the recent
years however almost nothing was known about the behaviour of the process on
unbounded sets. The present paper is intended to fill this gap and we provide
two-sided sharp estimates for the Green function for a half-space. As a
byproduct we obtain some improvements of the estimates known for bounded sets
specially for balls. The advantage of these estimates is a clarification of the
relationship between the diameter of the ball and the parameter $m$ of the
process.
  The main result states that the Green function is comparable with the Green
function for the Brownian motion if the points are away from the boundary of a
half-space and their distance is greater than one. On the other hand for the
remaining points the Green function is somehow related the Green function for
the isotropic $\alpha$-stable process. For example, for $d\ge3$, it is
comparable with the Green function for the isotropic $\alpha$-stable process,
provided that the points are close enough.

Because of the intense brightness of the OB-type multiple star system sigma
Ori, the low-mass stellar and substellar populations close to the centre of the
very young sigma Orionis cluster is poorly know. I present an IJHKs survey in
the cluster centre, able to detect from the massive early-type stars down to
cluster members below the deuterium burning mass limit. The near-infrared and
optical data have been complemented with X-ray imaging. Ten objects have been
found for the first time to display high-energy emission. Previously known
stars with clear spectroscopic youth indicators and/or X-ray emission define a
clear sequence in the I vs. I-Ks diagram. I have found six new candidate
cluster members that follow this sequence. One of them, in the magnitude
interval of the brown dwarfs in the cluster, displays X-ray emission and a very
red J-Ks colour, indicative of a disc. Other three low-mass stars have excesses
in the Ks band as well. The frequency of X-ray emitters in the area is 80+/-20
%. The spatial density of stars is very high, of up to 1.6+/-0.1 arcmin-2.
There is no indication of lower abundance of substellar objects in the cluster
centre. Finally, I also report two cluster stars with X-ray emission located at
only 8000-11000 AU to sigma Ori AB, two sources with peculiar colours and an
object with X-ray emission and near-infrared magnitudes similar to those of
previously-known substellar objects in the cluster.

In our work on "The Intrinsic Origin of Spin Echoes in Dipolar Solids Generated by Strong Pi Pulses," we investigate the fundamental mechanism that leads to the generation of spin echoes in nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) experiments. Our study is motivated by the long-standing research interest in developing more accurate and sensitive techniques for studying molecular dynamics and structure in condensed matter systems. We employ a theoretical approach to model the dynamics of spin systems in dipolar solids subjected to strong pi pulses. Our methodology involves solving the spin Hamiltonian using analytical and numerical techniques. We show that spin echoes arise due to the intrinsic dynamics of the spin system and do not require any external or additional manipulations. Instead, they are the result of a coherent time evolution that occurs after the application of a pi pulse. Our results demonstrate that spin echoes can be generated in a variety of dipolar solids, including systems with short-range and long-range dipolar interactions. These findings have important implications for the design of new NMR experiments and for the interpretation of experimental data from dipolar solids. Overall, our work contributes to a deeper understanding of the fundamental physics of spin dynamics in condensed matter systems.

This paper considers the problem of reasoning on massive amounts of (possibly
distributed) data. Presently, existing proposals show some limitations: {\em
(i)} the quantity of data that can be handled contemporarily is limited, due to
the fact that reasoning is generally carried out in main-memory; {\em (ii)} the
interaction with external (and independent) DBMSs is not trivial and, in
several cases, not allowed at all; {\em (iii)} the efficiency of present
implementations is still not sufficient for their utilization in complex
reasoning tasks involving massive amounts of data. This paper provides a
contribution in this setting; it presents a new system, called DLV$^{DB}$,
which aims to solve these problems. Moreover, the paper reports the results of
a thorough experimental analysis we have carried out for comparing our system
with several state-of-the-art systems (both logic and databases) on some
classical deductive problems; the other tested systems are: LDL++, XSB, Smodels
and three top-level commercial DBMSs. DLV$^{DB}$ significantly outperforms even
the commercial Database Systems on recursive queries. To appear in Theory and
Practice of Logic Programming (TPLP)

In this study, we investigate the phase transitions in the coloring of random graphs. The problem of finding a coloring for a given graph is fundamental to computer science and has potential applications in various fields such as scheduling, register allocation, and communication networks. Random graph models are widely used in theoretical computer science to understand the behavior of algorithms on a diverse range of graphs. 

We explore the properties of random graphs with respect to their chromatic number, which is the minimum number of colors required to color the vertices of a given graph such that adjacent vertices have different colors. We use the probabilistic method to analyze the number of colors needed to color random graphs generated by the Erdős–Rényi model and the configuration model.

Our findings suggest the existence of a sharp threshold phenomenon in the chromatic number of random graphs where the minimum number of colors needed to color a graph changes abruptly as the graph size or the edge density increases. This phase transition phenomenon is a fundamental property of random graphs and can be used to design efficient algorithms for coloring random graphs.

Overall, our study provides new insights into the behavior of random graphs and highlights the importance of understanding the phase transitions in combinatorial problems. Further research can explore other graph models and investigate phase transitions in other combinatorial problems.

Unbiased angular diameter measurements are required for accurate distances to
Cepheids using the interferometric Baade Wesselink method (IBWM). The precision
of this technique is currently limited by interferometric measurements at the
1.5% level. At this level, the center-to-limb darkening (CLD) and the presence
of circumstellar envelopes (CSE) seem to be the two main sources of bias. The
observations we performed aim at improving our knowledge of the interferometric
visibility profile of Cepheids. In particular, we assess the systematic
presence of CSE around Cepheids in order determine accurate distances with the
IBWM free from CSE biased angular diameters. We observed a Cepheid (Y Oph) for
which the pulsation is well resolved and a non-pulsating yellow supergiant
(alpha Per) using long-baseline near-infrared interferometry. We interpreted
these data using a simple CSE model we previously developed. We found that our
observations of alpha Per do not provide evidence for a CSE. The measured CLD
is explained by an hydrostatic photospheric model. Our observations of Y Oph,
when compared to smaller baseline measurements, suggest that it is surrounded
by a CSE with similar characteristics to CSE found previously around other
Cepheids. We have determined the distance to Y Oph to be d=491+/-18 pc.
Additional evidence points toward the conclusion that most Cepheids are
surrounded by faint CSE, detected by near infrared interferometry: after
observing four Cepheids, all show evidence for a CSE. Our CSE non-detection
around a non-pulsating supergiant in the instability strip, alpha Per, provides
confidence in the detection technique and suggests a pulsation driven mass-loss
mechanism for the Cepheids.

In this study, we present the results of our investigation into the mass of the candidate exoplanet companion to HD 33636 using high-precision radial velocities and astrometry data acquired from the Hubble Space Telescope. The motivation behind our research lies in the search for exoplanets and their characterization, which enables the determination of their atmospheric compositions, internal structures, and potential habitability. 

We employed a combination of astrometry and radial velocity measurements to determine the mass of the exoplanet candidate. Using the astrometry data, we obtained the position of the star relative to the background stars, while the radial velocity measurements provided us with information about the star's motion in the line of sight. Combining these two sets of data enabled us to estimate the exoplanet mass through the analysis of semi-amplitude variations in the radial velocity curve.

Our findings suggest that the exoplanet candidate has a mass of approximately 1.5 Jupiter masses, indicating that it is a gas giant. This study provides valuable insights into the nature of exoplanets and their potential to support life. Our approach, which merges astrometry and radial velocity measurements, serves as a valuable tool for future exoplanet research. The importance of these discoveries to the field of astronomy and astrophysics cannot be understated, and we believe our work will inspire further research to unlock the mysteries of our Universe.

In this work, we explore the long-term evolution of magnetic turbulence in relativistic collisionless shocks in electron-positron plasmas. The motivation for this research stems from the need to understand the mechanisms underlying the dissipation of kinetic energy and the acceleration of particles in such extreme environments. We utilize state-of-the-art numerical simulations that self-consistently evolve the plasma and magnetic fields. Using these simulations, we demonstrate that the amplification of magnetic turbulence is sustained over long periods of time, leading to the efficient acceleration of particles to very high energies.

Our results reveal that the magnetic turbulence is generated through the Weibel instability, which occurs due to the counter-streaming of particles in the plasma. This instability leads to the growth of strong magnetic fields, which in turn scatter the particles and accelerate them through a process called Fermi acceleration. We find that this process is very efficient in electron-positron plasmas, and can account for the production of high-energy cosmic rays observed in astrophysical settings.

Overall, this work provides new insights into the physics of relativistic collisionless shocks, and sheds light on the mechanisms responsible for the acceleration of particles in such environments. Our research contributes to the broader efforts to understand the origin and nature of high-energy cosmic rays, and has implications for a wide range of astrophysical phenomena.

Protons and antiprotons at collider energies are a source of high energy
Weizs\"acker--Williams photons. This may open a possibility to study exclusive
photoproduction of heavy vector mesons at energies much larger than possible at
the HERA accelerator. Here we present a detailed investigation of the exclusive
$J/\psi$ photoproduction in proton-proton (RHIC, LHC) and proton-antiproton
(Tevatron) collisions. We calculate several differential distributions in $t_1,
t_2, y, \phi$, as well as transverse momentum distributions of $J/\Psi$'s. We
discuss correlations in the azimuthal angle between outgoing protons or proton
and antiproton as well as in the ($t_1, t_2$) space. Differently from
electroproduction experiments, here both colliding beam particles can be a
source of photons, and we find large interference terms in azimuthal angle
distributions in a broad range of rapidities of the produced meson. We also
include the spin--flip parts in the electromagnetic vertices. We discuss the
effect of absorptive corrections on various distributions. Interestingly,
absorption corrections induce a charge asymmetry in rapidity distributions, and
are larger for $p p$ reactions than for the $p \bar p$ case. The reaction
considered here constitutes an important nonreduceable background in recently
proposed searches for odderon exchange.

In this work, we present a complete hydrodynamic calculation to study the relaxation dynamics of a dewetting contact line, which is of great importance in various industrial and biological processes. The motivation for this research is to understand the underlying physical mechanisms governing the dewetting process at the nanoscale, which will help in designing new materials with desired wetting properties. We use the full Navier-Stokes equations coupled with the continuity equation to model the evolution of the thin fluid film near the contact line. To capture the molecular-scale phenomena, we also incorporate the Langmuir molecular adsorption-desorption kinetics as the boundary condition. The key idea of this study is to investigate the role of slip, viscosity variations, and surface tension gradients in the relaxation of the contact line. Our results show that the dynamics of the contact line strongly depend on the initial film thickness, slip length, and the strength of the surface tension gradient near the contact line. We provide a systematic analysis of the interplay between these factors and their collective effects on the relaxation process. These findings offer valuable insights into the dynamics of dewetting contact lines and present opportunities for designing surface coatings with tailored wetting properties.

The 105-ms X-ray pulsar J1852+0040 is the central compact object (CCO) in SNR
Kes 79. We report a sensitive upper limit on its radio flux density of 12 uJy
at 2 GHz using the NRAO GBT. Timing using XMM and Chandra over a 2.4 yr span
reveals no significant change in its spin period. The 2 sigma upper limit on
the period derivative leads, in the dipole spin-down formalism, to an energy
loss rate E-dot < 7e33 ergs/s, surface magnetic field strength B_p < 1.5e11 G,
and characteristic age tau_c = P/2P-dot > 8 Myr. This tau_c exceeds the age of
the SNR by 3 orders of magnitude, implying that the pulsar was born spinning at
its current period. However, the X-ray luminosity of PSR J1852+0040, L(bol) ~
3e33(d/7.1 kpc)^2 ergs/s is a large fraction of E-dot, which challenges the
rotation-powered assumption. Instead, its high blackbody temperature,
0.46+/-0.04 keV, small blackbody radius ~ 0.8 km, and large pulsed fraction, ~
80%, may be evidence of accretion onto a polar cap, possibly from a fallback
disk made of supernova debris. If B_p < 1e10 G, an accretion disk can penetrate
the light cylinder and interact with the magnetosphere while resulting torques
on the neutron star remain within the observed limits. A weak B-field is also
inferred in another CCO, the 424-ms pulsar 1E 1207.4-5209, from its steady spin
and soft X-ray absorption lines. We propose this origin of radio-quiet CCOs:
the B-field, derived from a turbulent dynamo, is weaker if the NS is formed
spinning slowly, which enables it to accrete SN debris. Accretion excludes
neutron stars born with both B_p < 1e11 G and P > 0.1 s from radio pulsar
surveys, where B_p < 1e11 G is not encountered except among very old (tau_c >
40 Myr) or recycled pulsars. Finally, such a CCO, if born in SN 1987A, could
explain the non-detection of a pulsar there.

In this work, we aim to investigate the impact of local inhomogeneity on the measured cosmological constant. Despite being a fundamental parameter in cosmology, the observed value of the cosmological constant appears to be extremely small compared to the theoretical predictions. One possible explanation for this discrepancy is the effect of local inhomogeneities on the cosmic expansion rate. We present a systematic approach to quantify and correct for this effect, which has been shown to potentially contribute a significant bias to the observed value of the cosmological constant.

Our methodology involves modeling the impact of nearby structures on the cosmic background radiation observed by the Planck satellite and applying this correction to the observed value of the cosmological constant. We find that the resulting systematic correction can account for up to 30% of the discrepancy between the theoretical and observed values of the cosmological constant. Our results highlight the importance of accounting for local inhomogeneities in interpreting cosmological observations and provide a step towards resolving one of the longstanding puzzles in modern cosmology.

Overall, this work demonstrates the importance of a careful and systematic approach to understanding the impact of local structures on cosmological observations, and provides a framework for future studies to refine and improve upon our results.

In this work, we explore the concepts of distillable entanglement and area laws in spin and harmonic-oscillator systems. Our main motivation lies in understanding the fundamental quantum mechanics principles that dictate entanglement dynamics and how they relate to the geometry of the underlying physical systems.

To this end, we employ a combination of analytical and numerical methods to investigate the quantum entanglement of bipartite spin systems and harmonic-oscillator-lattice models. Specifically, we examine the properties of distillable entanglement, which refers to the amount of entanglement that can be extracted from a given state by local operations and classical communication. We also explore the connections between distillable entanglement and area laws, which relate the entanglement across a boundary to the geometric features of the system.

Our results show that distillable entanglement is closely connected to the topology of the physical systems, with highly connected lattices exhibiting greater entanglement. Additionally, we find that area laws are valid in the systems we considered, indicating that entanglement is distributed non-uniformly across the boundary of the subsystems.

Overall, our work sheds light on the relationship between entanglement and geometry in quantum systems, with important implications for quantum information theory and quantum computing.

For cellular biochemical reaction systems where the numbers of molecules is
small, significant noise is associated with chemical reaction events. This
molecular noise can give rise to behavior that is very different from the
predictions of deterministic rate equation models. Unfortunately, there are few
analytic methods for examining the qualitative behavior of stochastic systems.
Here we describe such a method that extends deterministic analysis to include
leading-order corrections due to the molecular noise. The method allows the
steady-state behavior of the stochastic model to be easily computed,
facilitates the mapping of stability phase diagrams that include stochastic
effects and reveals how model parameters affect noise susceptibility, in a
manner not accessible to numerical simulation. By way of illustration we
consider two genetic circuits: a bistable positive-feedback loop and a
negative-feedback oscillator. We find in the positive feedback circuit that
translational activation leads to a far more stable system than transcriptional
control. Conversely, in a negative-feedback loop triggered by a
positive-feedback switch, the stochasticity of transcriptional control is
harnessed to generate reproducible oscillations.

We present a study on logarithmic corrections and soft photon phenomenology in the multipole model of the nucleon form factors. The motivation for this research lies in the need to improve our understanding of the internal structure of the nucleon, which is crucial in the interpretation of high-energy experiments. Our work attempts to solve the discrepancy between the experimental measurements of the nucleon form factors and their theoretical predictions, by considering the effects of soft photon radiation.

The key idea behind our research is to incorporate logarithmic corrections, which arise from the summation of infrared divergences in radiative corrections, into the multipole model. We develop a systematic theoretical framework to calculate these corrections and apply it to the proton and neutron form factors. Moreover, we investigate the role of soft photons in the form factor measurements by studying the radiative corrections to the unpolarized and polarized electron-proton scattering cross sections.

Our methodology involves using a combination of analytic and numerical techniques to compute the logarithmic corrections and soft photon radiation effects. We demonstrate that our results account for a significant part of the discrepancy between theory and experiment for the proton form factors, with prospects for further improvement. This research contributes to a deeper understanding of the nucleon's internal structure and paves the way for more accurate predictions of nucleon properties from first principles.

Our research delves into the phenomenon of geometry-induced asymmetric diffusion, where the geometry of a medium can cause particles to exhibit directional preference in their movement. We aim to understand the underlying mechanisms of this phenomenon and explore its potential applications.

The motivation behind our research stems from the ubiquitous presence of geometry in the natural world, ranging from complex biological systems to simple physical environments. The ability to predict and control particle behavior in these geometries can have far-reaching implications in fields such as drug delivery, microfluidics, and environmental science.

The problem we aim to solve is to develop a comprehensive model of asymmetric diffusion in complex geometries. Previous work in this area has focused on simple geometries and has relied on simplifying assumptions, limiting its applicability. We utilize a combination of analytical and computational techniques to derive a generalized model that can account for various geometrical features.

Our key idea is to consider the effects of both geometric confinement and asymmetry simultaneously, rather than treating them as two distinct phenomena. Our model takes into account the non-uniform density of particles in the confined space and the interplay between particle-particle and particle-boundary interactions.

Our methodology involves mathematical analysis and simulations to test our model's predictions against experiments and previous findings. We anticipate that our work will provide insights into the fundamental physics of geometry-induced asymmetric diffusion and pave the way for designing novel applications in various fields.

In this work, we investigate a model of gauge-Higgs unification and radiative electroweak symmetry breaking in warped extra dimensions. The motivation for this research is the lack of a firm theoretical framework explaining the origin of electroweak symmetry breaking in the Standard Model. The work attempts to solve this problem by proposing an extension of the Standard Model in which the Higgs boson emerges from the fifth dimension together with the gauge fields. The compactification of the extra dimension induces a non-trivial background field that breaks the electroweak symmetry, leading to the masses of the W and Z bosons. In this model, the Higgs boson plays a crucial role in the process of radiative electroweak symmetry breaking, which explains the small value of the Higgs mass compared to the scale of new physics.

We derive the effective potential for the Higgs boson in this model and show that electroweak symmetry breaking can be triggered radiatively from the top quark loop. We perform a detailed analysis of the electroweak precision data and obtain constraints on the model parameters, including the size of the extra dimension and the top quark Yukawa coupling. We find that the model is consistent with the data and provides an alternative explanation for the origin of electroweak symmetry breaking. Our results suggest that gauge-Higgs unification and warped extra dimensions may be a promising avenue towards understanding the fundamental nature of the Higgs boson and electroweak symmetry breaking.

Mobile entities with wireless links are able to form a mobile ad-hoc network.
Such an infrastructureless network does not have to be administrated. However,
self-organizing principles have to be applied to deal with upcoming problems,
e.g. information dissemination. These kinds of problems are not easy to tackle,
requiring complex algorithms. Moreover, the usefulness of pure ad-hoc networks
is arguably limited. Hence, enthusiasm for mobile ad-hoc networks, which could
eliminate the need for any fixed infrastructure, has been damped. The goal is
to overcome the limitations of pure ad-hoc networks by augmenting them with
instant Internet access, e.g. via integration of UMTS respectively GSM links.
However, this raises multiple questions at the technical as well as the
organizational level. Motivated by characteristics of small-world networks that
describe an efficient network even without central or organized design, this
paper proposes to combine mobile ad-hoc networks and infrastructured networks
to form hybrid wireless networks. One main objective is to investigate how this
approach can reduce the costs of a permanent backbone link and providing in the
same way the benefits of useful information from Internet connectivity or
service providers. For the purpose of bridging between the different types of
networks, an adequate middleware service is the focus of our investigation.
This paper shows our first steps forward to this middleware by introducing the
Injection Communication paradigm as principal concept.

In this article, we present an analysis of lattice refining loop quantum cosmology with an emphasis on anisotropic models and stability. At the heart of our research lies the motivation to investigate the potential of loop quantum cosmology to solve some of the outstanding problems of modern cosmology, in particular, the singularity problems that appear in the standard models of cosmology. 

We employ a novel methodology in this work, using the refined lattice approach to construct isotropic and anisotropic models. The refined lattice approach allows us to study the evolution of the universe in discrete steps, which is more in line with the fundamental principles of quantum mechanics. Additionally, we use stability analysis techniques to explore the behavior of the models and obtain conditions for stability. 

The results of our analysis provide important insights into the behavior of the universe at its earliest stages, before the onset of classical physics. We find that the singularity problem is resolved in the anisotropic models, where quantum effects dominate the evolution of the universe. Moreover, we demonstrate that the refined lattice approach provides a viable framework for studying cosmology in the context of quantum mechanics.

Overall, our work shows great promise for the future of loop quantum cosmology and highlights the importance of investigating alternative models of cosmology. Through the use of the refined lattice approach and stability analysis, we contribute to the development of a more holistic understanding of the universe and the fundamental laws that govern it.

We construct the integration measure over the moduli space of an arbitrary
number of N kinds of dyons of the pure SU(N) gauge theory at finite
temperatures. The ensemble of dyons governed by the measure is mathematically
described by a (supersymmetric) quantum field theory that is exactly solvable
and is remarkable for a number of striking features: 1) The free energy has the
minimum corresponding to the zero average Polyakov line, as expected in the
confining phase; 2)The correlation function of two Polyakov lines exhibits a
linear potential between static quarks in any N-ality non-zero representation,
with a calculable string tension roughly independent of temperature; 3) The
average spatial Wilson loop falls off exponentially with its area and the same
string tension; 4) At a critical temperature the ensemble of dyons rearranges
and de-confines; 5)The estimated ratio of the critical temperature to the
square root of the string tension is in excellent agreement with the lattice
data.

The LHC will have unprecedented sensitivity to flavor-changing neutral
current (FCNC) top quark decays, whose observation would be a clear sign of
physics beyond the standard model. Although many details of top flavor
violation are model dependent, the standard model gauge symmetries relate top
FCNCs to other processes, which are strongly constrained by existing data. We
study these constraints in a model independent way, using a low energy
effective theory from which the new physics is integrated out. We consider the
most important operators which contribute to top FCNCs and analyze the current
constraints on them. We find that the data rule out top FCNCs at a level
observable at the LHC due to most of the operators comprising left-handed first
or second generation quark fields, while there remains a substantial window for
top decays mediated by operators with right-handed charm or up quarks. If FCNC
top decays are observed at the LHC, such an analysis may help decipher the
underlying physics.

In this work, we present multi-color photometry of the Galactic globular cluster M75 = NGC 6864 using data collected with the 2.6-m Shajn telescope at the Crimean Astrophysical Observatory. The metallicity estimation for M75 is challenging due to its peculiar chemical composition and high reddening. We introduce a new sensitive metallicity indicator based on the differences between the (U-V) and (V-I) colors, which is calibrated using high-resolution spectroscopic data. Additionally, we investigate the position of the horizontal branch (HB) in the ultraviolet (UV) portion of the spectrum using the (U-B) vs. (B-V) color-color diagram. The HB morphology and the presence of a spread in the main sequence turnoff region are discussed. 

Our methodology includes applying color-magnitude diagrams (CMDs) to the multi-band data in order to identify different stellar populations within the cluster. We use both theoretical isochrones and empirical calibrations of the RGB stellar parameters to derive the age and distance of M75 and confirm that it is a relatively old and distant globular cluster. The correlation between photometric and spectroscopic metallicities is examined to validate our proposed metallicity indicator. 

The motivation for this research stems from the need to accurately determine the properties and evolution of globular clusters, which are crucial for our understanding of the Galaxy's formation and evolution. Our results contribute to the study of M75 and provide new insights into the metallicity and HB properties of this cluster.

In this work, we present a study on non-standard neutrino interactions (NSI) using neutrinos emitted by core-collapse supernovae. These interactions, beyond the standard model, can alter the neutrino oscillation parameters and affect the supernova's explosion mechanism. Therefore, it is imperative to investigate these interactions and their potential implications. 

To probe NSI, we use a three-flavor neutrino propagation simulation in which we vary the NSI parameters. We simulate the expected neutrino signals from a distant supernova and compare them to the standard neutrino oscillation scenario. We also investigate the degeneracy of some NSI parameters to better understand their effect on the expected signals. 

The problem this work attempts to solve is to quantify the impact of NSI on the neutrino signal from a core-collapse supernova. Our results show that NSI can significantly impact the neutrino signals, leading to deviations from the standard oscillation scenario. Furthermore, we found that these deviations can be used to place constraints on non-standard neutrino interactions. 

In conclusion, our work demonstrates the importance of considering non-standard neutrino interactions in supernova neutrinos and provides a framework to probe these interactions and place constraints on their parameters.

We study a heavy piston of mass $M$ that moves in one dimension. The piston
separates two gas chambers, each of which contains finitely many ideal, unit
mass gas particles moving in $d$ dimensions, where $ d\geq 1$. Using averaging
techniques, we prove that the actual motions of the piston converge in
probability to the predicted averaged behavior on the time scale $M^ {1/2} $
when $M$ tends to infinity while the total energy of the system is bounded and
the number of gas particles is fixed. Neishtadt and Sinai previously pointed
out that an averaging theorem due to Anosov should extend to this situation.
  When $ d=1$, the gas particles move in just one dimension, and we prove that
the rate of convergence of the actual motions of the piston to its averaged
behavior is $\mathcal{O} (M^ {-1/2}) $ on the time scale $M^ {1/2} $. The
convergence is uniform over all initial conditions in a compact set. We also
investigate the piston system when the particle interactions have been
smoothed. The convergence to the averaged behavior again takes place uniformly,
both over initial conditions and over the amount of smoothing.
  In addition, we prove generalizations of our results to $N$ pistons
separating $N+1$ gas chambers. We also provide a general discussion of
averaging theory and the proofs of a number of previously known averaging
results. In particular, we include a new proof of Anosov's averaging theorem
for smooth systems that is primarily due to Dolgopyat.

In this work, we present evidence of high metallicity in the X-ray gas up to the virial radius of a binary cluster of galaxies, suggesting the presence of galactic superwinds at high-redshift. Our analysis is based on the joint Chandra and XMM-Newton observations of the cluster, which span a total exposure time of 400 ksec. We measured the gas temperature, metallicity, and density profiles using a combination of spectral and imaging techniques. Our results show that the metallicity of the X-ray gas is significantly higher than the solar value, and this excess extends out to the virial radius of the cluster. We interpret this as evidence of galactic superwinds, which can inject metals and energy into the surrounding gas, enriching it and creating a metal-enriched halo.

Our work attempts to solve the problem of understanding the origin and evolution of the diffuse X-ray emission in galaxy clusters, which is believed to arise from a combination of thermal and non-thermal processes. Our observations provide strong evidence that galactic superwinds play a significant role in enriching the intracluster medium with metals and energy, particularly at high-redshift, where cluster formation is still ongoing. Our results have important implications for cosmology and galaxy formation, as they suggest that galactic superwinds can affect the evolution of galaxies and the intergalactic medium over cosmic time.

In this research, we investigate the properties of cosmic rays with GeV gamma rays. Cosmic rays are high-energy charged particles that originate from sources in the universe and reach Earth's atmosphere. One of the ways to study cosmic rays is by detecting gamma rays produced in their interactions with the interstellar medium. The use of GeV gamma rays as a probe provides a unique opportunity to study cosmic rays in greater detail. 

Our work attempts to solve the problem of understanding the origin and acceleration mechanisms of cosmic rays. Despite decades of research, the sources and acceleration processes of cosmic rays remain elusive. By studying GeV gamma rays, we aim to shed light on this long-standing mystery. Specifically, the study focuses on the detection of gamma-rays in the Galactic plane, which is a region of potential sources of cosmic rays. 

The results show that the study of cosmic rays with GeV gamma rays provides complementary information to other cosmic ray detectors. By analyzing the gamma-ray flux and spectral characteristics, we were able to constrain the properties of the cosmic ray sources and acceleration mechanisms. Furthermore, we found that the detection of gamma rays in the Galactic plane is consistent with the prediction of a diffuse gamma-ray emission originating from interactions of cosmic rays with the interstellar medium. 

The motivation for this research comes from the desire to deepen our understanding of the most energetic particles in the universe. The study of cosmic rays with GeV gamma rays has the potential to provide crucial insights into the workings of cosmic accelerators and the physical processes that govern the evolution of the universe.

We consider a simple Newtonian model of a steady accretion disk around a
black hole. The model is based on height-integrated hydrodynamic equations,
alpha-viscosity, and a pseudo-Newtonian potential that results in an innermost
stable circular orbit (ISCO) that closely approximates the one predicted by GR.
We find that the hydrodynamic models exhibit increasing deviations from the
standard disk model of Shakura & Sunyaev as disk thickness H/R or the value of
alpha increases. The latter is an analytical model in which the viscous torque
is assumed to vanish at the ISCO. We consider the implications of the results
for attempts to estimate black hole spin by using the standard disk model to
fit continuum spectra of black hole accretion disks. We find that the error in
the spin estimate is quite modest so long as H/R < 0.1 and alpha < 0.2. At
worst the error in the estimated value of the spin parameter is 0.1 for a
non-spinning black hole; the error is much less for a rapidly spinning hole. We
also consider the density and disk thickness contrast between the gas in the
disk and that inside the ISCO. The contrast needs to be large if black hole
spin is to be successfully estimated by fitting the relativistically-broadened
X-ray line profile of fluorescent iron emission from reflection off an
accretion disk. In our hydrodynamic models, the contrast in density and
thickness is low when H/R>0.1, sugesting that the iron line technique may be
most reliable in extemely thin disks. We caution that these results have been
obtained with a viscous hydrodynamic model and need to be confirmed with MHD
simulations of radiatively cooled thin disks.

We discuss the design of efficient scrip systems and develop tools for
empirically analyzing them. For those interested in the empirical study of
scrip systems, we demonstrate how characteristics of agents in a system can be
inferred from the equilibrium distribution of money. From the perspective of a
system designer, we examine the effect of the money supply on social welfare
and show that social welfare is maximized by increasing the money supply up to
the point that the system experiences a ``monetary crash,'' where money is
sufficiently devalued that no agent is willing to perform a service. We also
examine the implications of the presence of altruists and hoarders on the
performance of the system. While a small number of altruists may improve social
welfare, too many can also cause the system to experience a monetary crash,
which may be bad for social welfare. Hoarders generally decrease social welfare
but, surprisingly, they also promote system stability by helping prevent
monetary crashes. In addition, we provide new technical tools for analyzing and
computing equilibria by showing that our model exhibits strategic
complementarities, which implies that there exist equilibria in pure strategies
that can be computed efficiently.

In this paper, we present the first part of our high resolution radio continuum survey of the nearby galaxy M33. The main motivation behind this work is to investigate the properties of the synchrotron radio emission in this galaxy, which can provide important insights into the physical processes occurring in its interstellar medium. To achieve this goal, we performed a sensitive, high-resolution radio continuum survey of the disk of M33 using the Very Large Array at 6 cm and 20 cm wavelengths. Our survey covers an area of approximately 6.6 square degrees, with an angular resolution of 2.5'' and 7'', respectively. We present the radio maps obtained from our survey, which reveal a rich variety of structures and features, including diffuse emission associated with the disk of M33, as well as compact sources associated with star-forming regions and supernova remnants. These maps will serve as a valuable resource for future studies of the physics and astrophysics of M33, and will enable us to investigate the properties of the synchrotron emission in detail. Overall, our work aims to improve our understanding of the interstellar medium in nearby galaxies, and to shed light on the physical processes that shape the evolution of galaxies.

(abridged abstract) We present an analysis of the stellar mass growth over
the last 10 Gyrs using a large 3.6$\mu$ selected sample. We split our sample
into active (blue) and quiescent (red) galaxies. Our measurements of the K-LFs
and LD evolution support the idea that a large fraction of galaxies is already
assembled at $z\sim 1.2$. Based on the analysis of the evolution of the stellar
mass-to-light ratio (in K-band) for the spectroscopic sub-sample, we derive the
stellar mass density for the entire sample. We find that the global evolution
of the stellar mass density is well reproduced by the star formation rate
derived from UV dust corrected measurements. Over the last 8Gyrs, we observe
that the stellar mass density of the active population remains approximately
constant while it gradually increases for the quiescent population over the
same timescale. As a consequence, the growth of the stellar mass in the
quiescent population must be due to the shutoff of star formation in active
galaxies that migrate into the quiescent population. From $z=2$ to $z=1.2$, we
observe a major build-up of the quiescent population with an increase by a
factor of 10 in stellar mass, suggesting that we are observing the epoch when
an increasing fraction of galaxies are ending their star formation activity and
start to build up the red sequence.

The magnetic structure of the intermetallic antiferromagnet Sm2IrIn8 was
determined using x-ray resonant magnetic scattering (XRMS). Below TN = 14.2,
Sm2IrIn8 has a commensurate antiferromagnetic structure with a propagation
vector (1/2,0,0). The Sm magnetic moments lie in the ab plane and are rotated
roughly 18 degrees away from the a axis. The magnetic structure of this
compound was obtained by measuring the strong dipolar resonant peak whose
enhancement was of over two orders of magnitude at the L2 edge. At the L3 edge
both quadrupolar and dipolar features were observed in the energy line shape.
The magnetic structure and properties of Sm2IrIn8 are found to be consistent
with the general trend already seen for the Nd-, Tb- and the Ce-based compounds
from the RmMnIn3m+2n family (R = rare earth; M=Rh or Ir, m = 1, 2; n = 0, 1),
where the crystalline electrical field (CEF) effects determine the direction of
magnetic moments and the TN evolution in the series. The measured Neel
temperature for Sm2IrIn8 is slightly suppressed when compared to the TN of the
parent cubic compound SmIn3.

Dusty hyperluminous galaxies in the early universe provide unique
environments for studying the role of massive stars in the formation and
destruction of dust. At redshifts above ~ 6, when the universe was less than ~
1 Gyr old, dust could have only condensed in the explosive ejecta of Type II
supernovae (SNe), since most of the progenitors of the AGB stars, the major
alternative source of interstellar dust, did not have time to evolve off the
main sequence since the onset of star formation. In this paper we present
analytical models for the evolution of the gas, dust, and metals in high
redshift galaxies, with a special application to SDSS J1148+5251, a
hyperluminous quasar at z = 6.4. We find that an average supernova must
condense at least 1 Msun of dust to account for the observed dust mass in this
quasar. Observationally, it is in excess of the largest dust yield of ~0.02
Msun found thus far in the ejecta of any SN. If future observations find this
to be a typical supernova dust yield, then additional processes, such as
accretion onto preexisting grains, or condensation around the AGN will need to
be invoked to account for the large amount of dust in this and similar objects.
The galaxy's star formation history is still uncertain, and current
observations of the gas, metal, and dust contents of J1148 can be reproduced by
either an intensive and short burst of star formation (~ 1000 Msun/yr) with a
duration of ~ 100 Myr, or a much lower star formation rate (~ 100 Msun/yr)
occurring over the lifetime of the galaxy.

We present our work on the resummation of large logarithms in the process of $\gamma^* \pi^0 \to \gamma$. Our motivation is to achieve a better understanding of the physics underlying this process, which has important implications for the study of hadron structure and the determination of parton distribution functions (PDFs). Specifically, we aim to develop a new theoretical framework that can accurately describe the behavior of the perturbative QCD contribution to this process at high energies.

Our approach is based on the factorization theorem, which allows us to separate the short-distance perturbative contribution from the long-distance non-perturbative one. We use renormalization group techniques to resum the large logarithms arising from the evolution of the PDFs and the strong coupling constant. Our main results consist of explicit expressions for the resummed cross section and a study of their perturbative and non-perturbative uncertainties.

Overall, we find that our framework provides an improved treatment of the perturbative contribution to $\gamma^* \pi^0 \to \gamma$ at high energies, allowing for more precise measurements of the PDFs and a better understanding of hadron structure. Our work contributes to the ongoing efforts to deepen our understanding of QCD at high energies and to improve the accuracy of theoretical predictions in hadron physics.

In this study, we investigate the diffusion of optical probes in polymer solutions using fluorescence correlation spectroscopy (FCS). Our goal is to understand the mechanisms governing the interaction between the probes and the polymer matrix, and to explore the effects of polymer concentration, temperature, and probe size and charge on diffusivity. 

Our results show that the diffusion of probes is hindered by the crowded and viscoelastic polymer environment, resulting in slowed-down and anomalous diffusion behaviors. We found that the increase in polymer concentration leads to the reduction in the hydrodynamic radius of the probe due to entanglement effects, and that the presence of charges on the probes enhances their interactions with the polymer chains, reducing their mobility. 

Our study provides insights into the dynamic behavior of polymer solutions at the microscopic level and the effects of probe-polymer interactions on diffusivity, which have implications for a wide range of applications such as drug delivery, sensing, and biomaterials engineering. The methodology we used involves combining FCS with theories of diffusion in complex media and simulations, enabling us to obtain a deeper understanding of the key processes involved. Our work contributes to the ongoing efforts in the polymer research community to design novel materials with tailored properties by providing a fundamental understanding of diffusion dynamics under complex conditions.

In this study, we investigate the evolution of supernovae in circumstellar wind bubbles, focusing on the particular case of a Wolf-Rayet star. Our motivation for this research is to gain a deeper understanding of the physical processes that drive supernovae in these complex environments. To accomplish this, we have employed a numerical simulation to model the evolution of the circumstellar wind bubble prior to the explosion of the supernova. 

Our results reveal that as the supernova shockwave propagates through the wind bubble, it encounters regions of varying densities and pressures, which leads to a complex and dynamic explosion. In particular, we find that the presence of high-density clumps in the wind bubble can significantly affect the evolution of the supernova, causing it to become more energetic and to produce a larger amount of dust. Additionally, our simulation shows that the interaction between the supernova and the clumps produces a range of different morphologies, including bright knots and filaments of emission.

Overall, our work provides new insights into the dynamics of supernovae in circumstellar wind bubbles and the role that clumps can play in shaping their evolution. This research is important for advancing our understanding of the final stages of stellar evolution and the physical processes that govern the formation of supernovae.

We present the results of a visible spectroscopic and photometric survey of
Jupiter Trojans belonging to different dynamical families carried out at the
ESO-NTT telescope. We obtained data on 47 objects, 23 belonging to the L5 swarm
and 24 to the L4 one. These data together with those already published by
Fornasier et al. (2004a) and Dotto et al. (2006), constitute a total sample of
visible spectra for 80 objects. The survey allows us to investigate six
families (Aneas, Anchises, Misenus, Phereclos, Sarpedon, Panthoos) in the L5
cloud and four L4 families (Eurybates, Menelaus, 1986 WD and 1986 TS6). The
sample that we measured is dominated by D--type asteroids, with the exception
of the Eurybates family in the L4 swarm, where there is a dominance of C- and
P-type asteroids. All the spectra that we obtained are featureless with the
exception of some Eurybates members, where a drop--off of the reflectance is
detected shortward of 5200 A. Similar features are seen in main belt C-type
asteroids and commonly attributed to the intervalence charge transfer
transition in oxidized iron. Our sample comprises fainter and smaller Trojans
as compared to the literature's data and allows us to investigate the
properties of objects with estimated diameter smaller than 40--50 km. The
analysis of the spectral slopes and colors versus the estimated diameters shows
that the blue and red objects have indistinguishable size distribution. We
perform a statistical investigation of the Trojans's spectra property
distributions as a function of their orbital and physical parameters, and in
comparison with other classes of minor bodies in the outer Solar System.
Trojans at lower inclination appear significantly bluer than those at higher
inclination, but this effect is strongly driven by the Eurybates family.

We have measured the absolute proper motions of globular clusters NGC 2808,
3201, 4372, 4833, 5927 and 5986. The proper motions are on the Hipparcos system
and they are the first determinations ever made for these low Galactic latitude
clusters. The proper motion uncertainties range from 0.3 to 0.5 mas/yr. The
inferred orbits indicate that 1) the single metal rich cluster in our sample,
NGC 5927, dynamically belongs to the thick disk, 2) the remaining metal poor
