clusters have rather low-energy orbits of high eccentricity; among these, there
appear to be two "pairs" of dynamically associated clusters, 3) the most
energetic cluster in our sample, NGC 3201 is on a highly retrograde orbit --
which had already been surmised from its radial velocity alone -- with an
apocentric distance of 22 kpc, and 4) none of the metal poor clusters appear to
be associated with the recently detected SDSS streams, or with the Monoceros
structure.
  These are the first results of the Southern Proper-Motion Program (SPM) where
the second-epoch observations are taken with the CCD camera system recently
installed on the double astrograph at El Leoncito, Argentina.

In this work, we explore 2-frame flow dynamics and hyperbolic rank rigidity in nonpositive curvature. The main problem we attempt to solve is the study of nonpositive curvature spaces and how the hyperbolic rank rigidity, which is a well-known combinatorial condition, affects the dynamics of the 2-frame flow. We aim to provide a deeper understanding of the relationship between the two by exploring how the 2-frame flow behaves in terms of its trajectories and dynamics as we vary the hyperbolic rank. 

Our key idea is to use the dynamical behavior of the 2-frame flow as a tool to characterize the geometric structure of the nonpositive curvature space. We use a combination of techniques, including graph theory and combinatorics, to analyze the behavior of the flow. Through our methodology, we prove that the hyperbolic rank rigidity plays a crucial role in determining the behavior of the flow and its trajectories. Specifically, we show that the flow dynamics exhibit a significant difference depending on the hyperbolic rank. 

In conclusion, this work sheds light on the 2-frame flow dynamics and hyperbolic rank rigidity in nonpositive curvature spaces. Our findings show that the dynamics of the flow can provide insights into the geometric structure of the space and that the hyperbolic rank rigidity plays a crucial role in determining the behavior of the flow. Our work contributes to the understanding of these mathematical concepts and opens up new avenues for further exploration.

In this work, we present the preliminary results of our research into evolutionary mesh numbering. Our motivation for this study was to explore the potential benefits of using evolutionary algorithms to optimize mesh numbering processes in computational simulations. The numeric labels assigned to the nodes in a mesh provide critical input data for many simulations, and the quality of these labels can have a significant impact on their accuracy and performance. However, manual optimization of mesh numbering can be time-consuming, and traditional automated algorithms have limitations in addressing complex and variable meshes.

To address this problem, we propose an evolutionary algorithm approach to mesh numbering that learns from and adapts to the mesh structure over time. Our methodology relies on the use of a genetic algorithm to evolve labeling schemes, with objective functions based on measures such as localization error and numerical stability. Our preliminary experiments on simple two-dimensional grids showed improved performance compared to standard mesh-of-staircases numbering. We anticipate that this approach may be particularly useful for more complex mesh geometries encountered in practical simulation scenarios.

In conclusion, this work contributes to the ongoing effort to develop more efficient and effective numerical methods for scientific and engineering simulation, and we look forward to further investigations on more complex meshes and higher-dimensional problems.

In this work, we present an analysis of the properties of sums over geometries, and we propose an improved mean field approximation method to tackle the difficulty of calculating such quantities in condensed matter physics. Our study is motivated by the relevance of these sums in the calculation of partition functions and correlation functions of various physical systems on a lattice. 

We consider the simplest case of a scalar field theory on a random graph, and we show that the sum over all graphs can be expressed as a Gaussian integral. However, the global structure of the graphs can modify the Gaussian due to the non-locality of the interaction. To address this issue, we develop a mean field approximation that accounts for these global structures by including a self-consistent term in the Hamiltonian. 

We test our approach on a specific example, the Ising model on a random graph, and we compare it to traditional mean field theory. Our results show that our modified mean field approach improves the accuracy of the partition function and the correlation functions by taking into account the effects of the global structure. 

Overall, our work contributes to the development of techniques for calculating quantities of physical interest on random graphs, and we demonstrate the importance of including global effects in mean field approximations for improved accuracy.

In this work, we explore the phenomenology of Light Heavy (LH) Minimal Supersymmetric Standard Model (MSSM) Higgs bosons at large tanβ. Large tanβ can significantly alter the production and decay of the MSSM Higgs bosons. We investigate the existing constraints on the LH MSSM Higgs bosons from various experiments, such as LEP, Tevatron, and LHC. We carry out a comprehensive analysis of the parameter space of LH MSSM Higgs bosons and investigate their possible signatures, such as the production cross-sections, branching ratios, and the decay modes, under the constraints from the existing experimental data. 

Our results show that the LH MSSM Higgs bosons could provide an interesting target for the experimental searches at the LHC. In particular, for the large values of tanβ, we observed that the decay modes of LH MSSM Higgs bosons into the final state of a τ-lepton pair can significantly enhance the sensitivity of the search for these bosons. We also found that the production cross-section for the LH MSSM Higgs bosons in the decay processes of heavier Higgses can be greatly enhanced by the large value of tanβ. Therefore, the results of our study offer concrete predictions for the experimental searches of LH MSSM Higgs bosons at the LHC, which can be important for the ongoing efforts to detect new physics beyond the Standard Model.

An optical cavity enhances the interaction between atoms and light, and the
rate of coherent atom-photon coupling can be made larger than all decoherence
rates of the system. For single atoms, this strong coupling regime of cavity
quantum electrodynamics (cQED) has been the subject of spectacular experimental
advances, and great efforts have been made to control the coupling rate by
trapping and cooling the atom towards the motional ground state, which has been
achieved in one dimension so far. For N atoms, the three-dimensional ground
state of motion is routinely achieved in atomic Bose-Einstein condensates
(BECs), but although first experiments combining BECs and optical cavities have
been reported recently, coupling BECs to strong-coupling cavities has remained
an elusive goal. Here we report such an experiment, which is made possible by
combining a new type of fibre-based cavity with atom chip technology. This
allows single-atom cQED experiments with a simplified setup and realizes the
new situation of N atoms in a cavity each of which is identically and strongly
coupled to the cavity mode. Moreover, the BEC can be positioned
deterministically anywhere within the cavity and localized entirely within a
single antinode of the standing-wave cavity field. This gives rise to a
controlled, tunable coupling rate, as we confirm experimentally. We study the
heating rate caused by a cavity transmission measurement as a function of the
coupling rate and find no measurable heating for strongly coupled BECs. The
spectrum of the coupled atoms-cavity system, which we map out over a wide range
of atom numbers and cavity-atom detunings, shows vacuum Rabi splittings
exceeding 20 gigahertz, as well as an unpredicted additional splitting which we
attribute to the atomic hyperfine structure.

In line with the local philicity concept proposed by Chattaraj et al.
(Chattaraj, P. K.; Maiti, B.; Sarkar, U. J. Phys. Chem. A. 2003, 107, 4973) and
a dual descriptor derived by Toro-Labbe and coworkers (Morell, C.; Grand, A.;
Toro-Labbe, A. J. Phys. Chem. A. 2005, 109, 205), we propose a multiphilic
descriptor. It is defined as the difference between nucleophilic (Wk+) and
electrophilic (Wk-) condensed philicity functions. This descriptor is capable
of simultaneously explaining the nucleophilicity and electrophilicity of the
given atomic sites in the molecule. Variation of these quantities along the
path of a soft reaction is also analyzed. Predictive ability of this descriptor
has been successfully tested on the selected systems and reactions.
Corresponding force profiles are also analyzed in some representative cases.
Also, to study the intra- and intermolecular reactivities another related
descriptor namely, the nucleophilicity excess (DelW-+) for a nucleophile, over
the electrophilicity in it has been defined and tested on all-metal aromatic
compounds.

In this study, we present the results of the X-ray timing analysis of PSR J1852+0040 - a neutron star located in the supernova remnant Kesteven 79. Using observations from the XMM-Newton satellite, we obtained high-precision timing information that enabled us to study the pulsar characteristics and its environment in detail. Our goal was to understand the birth of neutron stars and their magnetization properties. 

Our analysis revealed that PSR J1852+0040 has a weak magnetic field, which is highly unusual for a young pulsar. Such a weak magnetic field at birth has been predicted by theoretical models, but it was never observed before. The data also indicated that the pulsar's rotational energy is dissipated mainly through magnetic-dipole radiation. Additionally, we found evidence of X-ray emission from the supernova shell around the pulsar, which signifies that the interaction between the pulsar and its environment is complex.

Our findings provide insight into the formation and early evolution of neutron stars and their magnetic fields. This study opens up new avenues to explore the diversity of the neutron star population. The results obtained from this study can have implications for our understanding of the universe's evolution and the physics that governs it.

In this work, we present the measurement of the baryon acoustic oscillation (BAO) scale using the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) and the Two-degree Field Galaxy Redshift Survey (2dFGRS) data. The BAO scale is a characteristic length scale imprinted on the large-scale structure of the universe by acoustic waves in the early universe. It is an essential tool for understanding the expansion history of the universe and testing cosmological models. 

We use the correlation function of galaxies to estimate the BAO scale in both surveys. The correlation function measures the excess probability of finding two galaxies at a certain distance compared to a random distribution. By taking the ratio of the observed correlation function to a reference model, we obtain the BAO scale. 

The motivation for this research is to improve our understanding of the universe's expansion history. The accurate measurement of the BAO scale allows us to constrain the parameters of dark energy and the curvature of the universe. 

Our results show consistent measurements of the BAO scale in both surveys, with an accuracy of around 3%. Our measurements are consistent with the standard cosmological model, providing further support for the idea that the universe is undergoing accelerating expansion. 

In summary, we have presented the measurement of the BAO scale using the SDSS and 2dFGRS data. Our results improve our understanding of the universe's expansion history and provide further support for the standard cosmological model.

We study linear series on a general curve of genus $g$, whose images are
exceptional with regard to their secant planes. Working in the framework of an
extension of Brill-Noether theory to pairs of linear series, we prove that a
general curve has no linear series with exceptional secant planes, in a very
precise sense, whenever the total number of series is finite. Next, we
partially solve the problem of computing the number of linear series with
exceptional secant planes in a one-parameter family in terms of tautological
classes associated with the family, by evaluating our hypothetical formula
along judiciously-chosen test families. As an application, we compute the
number of linear series with exceptional secant planes on a general curve
equipped with a one-dimensional family of linear series. We pay special
attention to the extremal case of $d$-secant $(d-2)$-planes to
$(2d-1)$-dimensional series, which appears in the study of Hilbert schemes of
points on surfaces. In that case, our formula may be rewritten in terms of
hypergeometric series, which allows us both to prove that it is nonzero and to
deduce its asymptotics in $d$.

An extensive literature exists describing various techniques for the
evaluation of Hankel determinants. The prevailing methods such as Dodgson
condensation, continued fraction expansion, LU decomposition, all produce
product formulas when they are applicable. We mention the classic case of the
Hankel determinants with binomial entries ${3k+2 \choose k}$ and those with
entries ${3k \choose k}$; both of these classes of Hankel determinants have
product form evaluations. The intermediate case, ${3k+1 \choose k}$ has not
been evaluated. There is a good reason for this: these latter determinants do
not have product form evaluations.
  In this paper we evaluate the Hankel determinant of ${3k+1 \choose k}$. The
evaluation is a sum of a small number of products, an almost product.
  The method actually provides more, and as applications, we present the
salient points for the evaluation of a number of other Hankel determinants with
polynomial entries, along with product and almost product form evaluations at
special points.

We report on our investigation into the production of trans-Neptunian binaries through chaos-assisted capture in the Kuiper Belt. Our study is motivated by the peculiar properties observed in binary minor planets beyond Neptune and the lack of a widely accepted formation mechanism to explain their origin. To address this problem, we use computational simulations to explore the dynamical evolution of a system of three bodies: a primary body, a secondary body, and a test particle. We simulate the motion of these bodies under the gravitational influence of each other in a three-dimensional space over a period of 4.5 billion years. Our results show that the chaos-assisted capture mechanism can efficiently produce trans-Neptunian binaries through three-body encounters. Specifically, the capture occurs through the Kozai-Lidov mechanism, in which the test particle's inclination angle oscillates chaotically due to the perturbations from the secondary and primary bodies. Such a mechanism works best when the masses of the primary and secondary bodies are roughly equal. Our findings suggest that chaos-assisted capture is a viable formation mechanism that can explain the formation of trans-Neptunian binaries in the Kuiper Belt, a significant contribution to the ongoing astronomical debate on the origin of these exotic objects.

Year 2005 was the World Year of Physics, and it was commemorated worldwide as
the 100th anniversary of Albert Einstein's legendary works in which the
foundations of at least three areas of modern physics were laid: statistical
mechanics, quantum mechanics and special relativity. These works turned year
1905 in what deservedly was called the "miraculous year" (annus mirabilis) of
Einstein. However, the influence of this great mind was not limited to science.
As it is well known, his fingerprint remains engraved in many aspects of our
daily life. Because of that, in this work we have tried to cover almost all the
subjects in which Einstein was involved during the last century and, of course,
try and reveal the future projection his actions will have in this new century
that has just begun. Among the subjects covered in the volume one can find the
possible connection between Einstein and the artistic vanguard movement, his
religiosity, his days in Argentina, the Nobel Prize, the Einstein-Freud
correspondence, his pacifism and the nuclear energy, science and politics under
the Second Empire and the Weimar Republic, and even a description of his
patents and experiments, the studies performed to search for "built-in"
features in his brain and the doubts many had regarding whether Einstein was a
genius or a crank. Also included are essays on Einstein and Borges, philosophy
and relativity, Mach's principle, the relation among science, arts and
humanities, and, closer to Einstein's own theoretical research, the twin
paradox, chance and quantum mechanics, the arrow of time, gravitational lenses,
string theory, holography and black holes, gravitomagnetism, superconductivity,
gravitational waves, the photoelectric effect and the alternative theories for
gravitation.

In this paper, we derive a hypercontractive inequality for matrix-valued functions and apply it to problems in quantum computing and locally decodable codes (LDCs). The hypercontractive inequality we derive generalizes the well-known hypercontractivity theorem for real-valued functions, which states that the L2 norm of a function raised to a power is greater than or equal to the Lp norm of the function for p strictly between 1 and 2. Our hypercontractive inequality for matrix-valued functions provides a similar bound in terms of the operator norm of the function and an exponent that is again strictly between 1 and 2. 

The main motivation for this research comes from the fact that matrix-valued functions arise naturally in many quantum computing and LDC problems. For example, in quantum computing, the unitary operators that implement quantum gates are matrix-valued functions. In LDCs, the decoding algorithms involve evaluating matrix-valued functions at various points in the code. By establishing a hypercontractive inequality for matrix-valued functions, we can obtain new results and bounds for these problems that were not previously possible with the real-valued hypercontractivity theorem.

Overall, our work contributes to the growing body of research in harmonic analysis and its applications to quantum computing and coding theory. The hypercontractive inequality we derive provides a powerful tool for analyzing complex functions and systems, and we believe it will have many future applications in a variety of fields.

We consider the general problem of determining the steady state of stochastic
nonequilibrium systems such as those that have been used to model (among other
things) biological transport and traffic flow. We begin with a broad overview
of this class of driven diffusive systems - which includes exclusion processes
- focusing on interesting physical properties, such as shocks and phase
transitions. We then turn our attention specifically to those models for which
the exact distribution of microstates in the steady state can be expressed in a
matrix product form. In addition to a gentle introduction to this matrix
product approach, how it works and how it relates to similar constructions that
arise in other physical contexts, we present a unified, pedagogical account of
the various means by which the statistical mechanical calculations of
macroscopic physical quantities are actually performed. We also review a number
of more advanced topics, including nonequilibrium free energy functionals, the
classification of exclusion processes involving multiple particle species,
existence proofs of a matrix product state for a given model and more
complicated variants of the matrix product state that allow various types of
parallel dynamics to be handled. We conclude with a brief discussion of open
problems for future research.

In this study, we present a photometric analysis of the intermediate-age star cluster BS 90 in the star-forming region NGC 346 in the Small Magellanic Cloud. Our data were obtained using the Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS) on board the Hubble Space Telescope. The cluster BS 90 is located in the outskirts of NGC 346 and has a large population of intermediate-mass stars. We conducted star counts and determined the cluster radius to be ~9.4 pc. We also used the color-magnitude diagram (CMD) to estimate the distance modulus, reddening, and age of the cluster. We found that the cluster has a distance modulus of 18.84 ± 0.08 mag, a reddening of E(B-V) = 0.14 ± 0.02 mag, and an age of ~65 Myr. The CMD shows that there is a spread in the age of stars in the cluster, indicating that it may have undergone multiple episodes of star formation.

The motivation for this research is to understand the age distribution and properties of intermediate-age clusters in NGC 346, which will help us to better understand the formation and evolution of star clusters in general. Our results suggest that BS 90 is a relatively young and metal-poor intermediate-age cluster, consistent with the properties of NGC 346 as a star-forming region. Our study highlights the importance of high-resolution, multi-band photometry for characterizing and understanding the properties of star clusters.

In this work, we explore the potential of the Gravity Probe B (GP-B) mission to provide meaningful constraints on torsion gravity theories. Despite the tremendous success of general relativity, it remains an incomplete description of gravity and is inconsistent with quantum mechanics. Torsion gravity theories represent one class of alternative models that attempt to address this discrepancy. To test these theories, we investigated the effect of torsion on the GP-B experiment. We found that torsion induces a coupling between the spin of the GP-B gyroscope and the background torsion field, which modifies the expected precession rate. In particular, we obtained explicit expressions for the torsion-induced precession and the corresponding bounds on the effective torsion parameters. Our results suggest that, while GP-B alone cannot definitively rule out torsion gravity theories, it can nevertheless provide complementary restrictions and help to discriminate among different models. Overall, our work contributes to the ongoing efforts to identify and test alternative theories of gravity, which are crucial for advancing our understanding of fundamental physics and cosmology.

We consider normalizers of an irreducible inclusion $N\subseteq M$ of
$\mathrm{II}_1$ factors. In the infinite index setting an inclusion
$uNu^*\subseteq N$ can be strict, forcing us to also investigate the semigroup
of one-sided normalizers. We relate these normalizers of $N$ in $M$ to
projections in the basic construction and show that every trace one projection
in the relative commutant $N'\cap < M,e_N>$ is of the form $u^*e_Nu$ for some
unitary $u\in M$ with $uNu^*\subseteq N$. This enables us to identify the
normalizers and the algebras they generate in several situations. In particular
each normalizer of a tensor product of irreducible subfactors is a tensor
product of normalizers modulo a unitary. We also examine normalizers of
irreducible subfactors arising from subgroup--group inclusions $H\subseteq G$.
Here the normalizers are the normalizing group elements modulo a unitary from
$L(H)$. We are also able to identify the finite trace $L(H)$-bimodules in
$\ell^2(G)$ as double cosets which are also finite unions of left cosets.

In this work, we present our research on linear tabling strategies and optimizations. The main objective of our study is to enhance the performance of Prolog's tabling mechanism, which is an essential feature of many logic programming languages. Specifically, we aim to optimize the control flow of tabled predicates by introducing linear strategies that avoid unnecessary tabled calls and redundant suspensions. 

Our motivation for this research stems from the fact that tabling is commonly used to compute recursive and mutually recursive predicates, but its efficiency can be significantly impacted by the size and complexity of the table. Therefore, we seek to optimize the tabling mechanism by reducing the number of calls made to the table, while preserving the correctness of the computations.

We present our linear tabling strategies along with a series of performance evaluations that demonstrate their effectiveness in various scenarios. Our experiment results show that our approach can significantly reduce the time and memory overhead of tabling, improving the efficiency of logic programming languages. Moreover, we compare our work with existing tabling optimization methods and demonstrate that our approach outperforms them in most scenarios. 

In conclusion, our work presents a novel approach to optimize tabling in logic programming languages that can improve the performance of the programs that use it. Our linear strategies offer an efficient and scalable solution, and we expect to see their adoption in various logic programming systems in the future.

We present a study of planetary embryos and planetesimals residing in thin debris disks with the aim of understanding the formation and evolution of planetary systems. Our work seeks to solve the problem of understanding the formation of small bodies in debris disks and their role in the planet formation process. Such systems host a variety of small bodies that range in size from micron-sized dust grains to planetary embryos. Our motivation for this research is to understand the physical and dynamical processes that govern the growth and evolution of these small bodies as they move through the disk. 

Our study is based on numerical simulations using a hybrid N-body/ Monte Carlo method. We investigate the dynamics of a system of small bodies evolving in a disk of planetesimals and microscopic dust grains. Additionally, we investigate the effects of different physical processes, such as gas drag and gravitational interactions, on the growth and evolution of planetesimal systems. Based on our simulations, we find that the growth of planets and planetesimals is strongly influenced by the radial velocity of the disk, and by the amount of gas available. Further, we find that the typical size of planetesimals, and their growth rate, will vary significantly across different regions of the disk. 

In conclusion, our research sheds light on the formation and evolution of small bodies in planetary systems, and provides important insights into the conditions that are necessary for the formation of planets.

We present observations of probable line-of-sight acceleration of a broad
absorption trough of C IV in the quasar SDSS J024221.87+004912.6. We also
discuss how the velocity overlap of two other outflowing systems in the same
object constrains the properties of the outflows. The Si IV doublet in each
system has one unblended transition and one transition which overlaps with
absorption from the other system. The residual flux in the overlapping trough
is well fit by the product of the residual fluxes in the unblended troughs. For
these optically thick systems to yield such a result, at least one of them must
consist of individual subunits rather than being a single structure with
velocity-dependent coverage of the source. If these subunits are identical,
opaque, spherical clouds, we estimate the cloud radius to be r = 3.9 10^15 cm.
If they are identical, opaque, linear filaments, we estimate their width to be
w = 6.5 10^14 cm. These subunits are observed to cover the Mg II broad emission
line region of the quasar, at which distance from the black hole the above
filament width is equal to the predicted scale height of the outer atmosphere
of a thin accretion disk. Insofar as that scale height is a natural size scale
for structures originating in an accretion disk, these observations are
evidence that the accretion disk can be a source of quasar absorption systems.
Based on data from ESO program 075.B-0190(A).

In this research, we investigate the production of cosmic rays from trans-relativistic supernovae, motivated by the need to understand the acceleration and propagation mechanisms of high-energy particles in the universe. We carried out a series of simulations using the particle-in-cell code, which allowed us to study the dynamics of the shock waves that occur during supernova explosions and the subsequent particle acceleration. Our simulations show that trans-relativistic supernovae can produce cosmic rays with energies up to 10^16 electronvolts, consistent with observations of high-energy cosmic rays in the Milky Way Galaxy. Furthermore, we found that the magnetic fields generated during the shock evolution play a crucial role in the acceleration of particles, which is a key open problem in the field.

Our study contributes to the ongoing effort to understand the nature of cosmic rays, which have important implications for astrophysics and particle physics. We also highlight the importance of considering the properties of trans-relativistic supernovae, which have been overlooked in previous works, in the study of cosmic ray origins. Our results have significant implications for the interpretation of observational data and for the development of theoretical models of cosmic ray acceleration. In conclusion, our study provides valuable insights into the processes that drive the production of cosmic rays from trans-relativistic supernovae, advancing our understanding of the most energetic phenomena in the universe.

In this paper, we present an update on Cryptoplanet, a software platform for the simulation of extrasolar planets and the analysis of their habitability potential. Our work aims to tackle the challenge of characterizing exoplanets with diverse physical and atmospheric conditions, which requires complex models and high-performance computing resources. The key contribution of Cryptoplanet is the integration of a suite of tools for simulating atmospheric chemistry, radiative transfer, climate dynamics, and planetary interior structures, which enable the generation of synthetic spectra and transmission curves from a wide range of planetary configurations.

Our methodology relies on a modular, object-oriented architecture that allows the user to customize the simulation setup and generate a self-consistent set of results. Moreover, we implemented a Bayesian inference framework that allows the fitting of simulated spectra to observational data and the estimation of relevant planetary parameters such as mass, radius, and atmospheric composition.

We present the results of our simulations on a number of known exoplanets, which demonstrate the capabilities of Cryptoplanet in reproducing observational data and constraining the habitability potential of exoplanets. We also discuss the potential applications of our platform in the context of upcoming space missions such as JWST and ARIEL, which will revolutionize our understanding of the diversity of planetary systems. In summary, Cryptoplanet is a powerful tool for exoplanet science that can provide crucial insights into the formation, evolution, and habitability of exoplanets.

We study the effects of lepton flavour violation (LFV) on the production
processes e+e- --> \chi+_i \chi-_j at a linear collider with longitudinal e+
and e- beam polarizations. In the case of LFV the sneutrino mass eigenstates
have no definite flavour, therefore, in the t-channel more than one sneutrino
mass eigenstate can contribute to the chargino production cross sections. Our
framework is the Minimal Supersymmetric Standard Model (MSSM) including LFV
terms. We show that in spite of the restrictions on the LFV parameters due to
the current limits on rare lepton decays, the cross section \sigma(e+e- -->
\chi+_1 \chi-_1) can change by a factor of 2 or more when varying the LFV
mixing angles. We point out that even if the present bound on BR(tau- --> e-
gamma) improves by a factor of thousand the influence of LFV on the chargino
production cross section can be significant. These results could have an
important impact on the strategies for determining the underlying model
parameters at the linear collider.

In this research, we present an effective conservation of energy and momentum algorithm that utilizes switching potentials for molecular dynamics simulation of thermodynamical systems. The primary motivation for this study is to develop an efficient method for performing large-scale simulations of complex molecular systems, such as biological molecules and polymers. Such simulations are important in understanding the molecular mechanisms that underlie biological processes and in developing new materials for industrial applications.

Our algorithm generates switching potentials that smoothly transition from one potential function to another, which allows for more accurate simulations of molecular dynamics. We tested our implementation against other commonly used conservation algorithms and found that our method resulted in significantly improved energy conservation and stability in long-term simulations. Additionally, our algorithm demonstrated excellent parallel performance, making it a valuable tool for running simulations at scale.

This work enhances our understanding of molecular simulation techniques and provides a more accurate method for modeling the behavior of complex systems. By solving the problem of energy conservation and stability, we can more accurately model the behavior of molecules and material systems, which is crucial for developing new materials and optimizing industrial processes. Our results demonstrate the efficacy of switching potentials in molecular simulations and lay the groundwork for further research in this area.

We investigate the mass function in the substellar domain down to a few
Jupiter masses in the young sigma Orionis open cluster (3+/-2 Ma, d =
360^+70_-60 pc). We have performed a deep IJ-band search, covering an area of
790 arcmin^2 close to the cluster centre. This survey was complemented with an
infrared follow-up in the HKs- and Spitzer 3.6-8.0 mum-bands. Using
colour-magnitude diagrams, we have selected 49 candidate cluster members in the
magnitude interval 16.1 mag < I < 23.0 mag. Accounting for flux excesses at 8.0
mum and previously known spectral features of youth, 30 objects are bona fide
cluster members. Four are first identified from our optical-near infrared data.
Eleven have most probable masses below the deuterium burning limit and are
classified as planetary-mass object candidates. The slope of the substellar
mass spectrum (Delta N / Delta M = a M^-alpha) in the mass interval 0.11 Msol M
< 0.006 Msol is alpha = +0.6+/-0.2. Any opacity mass-limit, if these objects
form via fragmentation, may lie below 0.006 Msol. The frequency of sigma
Orionis brown dwarfs with circumsubstellar discs is 47+/-15 %. The continuity
in the mass function and in the frequency of discs suggests that very low-mass
stars and substellar objects, even below the deuterium-burning mass limit, may
share the same formation mechanism.

In this work, we explore the use of abstract factorials in the context of mathematical analysis. Specifically, our aim is to address the challenge of generalizing factorial functions beyond their conventional domain of natural numbers. We propose a novel approach based on abstract algebra, where we define factorials as a special case of a more general transformation that applies to arbitrary classes of abstract structures. 

Our methodology involves the definition of a novel algebraic framework, which we call abstract factorial algebra, capable of capturing the essential properties of factorials in a more abstract and general form. We use this framework to derive a variety of properties about factorials that extend the classic results from the natural numbers to other structures, such as finite groups, semirings, and hypergraphs. 

Our work demonstrates the power and versatility of abstract algebra as a tool for generalizing mathematical concepts. We provide examples of applications of our approach in fields such as discrete mathematics, combinatorics, and number theory, showcasing how abstract factorials can be used to solve a wide range of problems that involve counting, enumeration, and optimization. 

Overall, our results offer a novel perspective on factorials that highlights their intrinsic abstract nature and their relevance for modern mathematics. We hope that our work will inspire further research on abstract factorials and their applications in different areas of mathematical and computational sciences.

We present an experimental and theoretical study of the polarized
photoluminescence spectrum of single semiconductor quantum dots in various
charge states. We compare our high resolution polarization sensitive spectral
measurements with a new many-carrier theoretical model, which was developed for
this purpose. The model considers both the isotropic and anisotropic exchange
interactions between all participating electron-hole pairs. With this addition,
we calculate both the energies and polarizations of all optical transitions
between collective, quantum dot confined charge carrier states. We succeed in
identifying most of the measured spectral lines. In particular, the lines
resulting from singly-, doubly- and triply- negatively charged excitons and
biexcitons. We demonstrate that lines emanating from evenly charged states are
linearly polarized. Their polarization direction does not necessarily coincide
with the traditional crystallographic direction. It depends on the shells of
the single carriers, which participate in the recombination process.

Given a finite irreducible Coxeter group $W$, a positive integer $d$, and
types $T_1,T_2,...,T_d$ (in the sense of the classification of finite Coxeter
groups), we compute the number of decompositions $c=\si_1\si_2 cdots\si_d$ of a
Coxeter element $c$ of $W$, such that $\si_i$ is a Coxeter element in a
subgroup of type $T_i$ in $W$, $i=1,2,...,d$, and such that the factorisation
is "minimal" in the sense that the sum of the ranks of the $T_i$'s,
$i=1,2,...,d$, equals the rank of $W$. For the exceptional types, these
decomposition numbers have been computed by the first author. The type $A_n$
decomposition numbers have been computed by Goulden and Jackson, albeit using a
somewhat different language. We explain how to extract the type $B_n$
decomposition numbers from results of B\'ona, Bousquet, Labelle and Leroux on
map enumeration. Our formula for the type $D_n$ decomposition numbers is new.
These results are then used to determine, for a fixed positive integer $l$ and
fixed integers $r_1\le r_2\le ...\le r_l$, the number of multi-chains $\pi_1\le
\pi_2\le ...\le \pi_l$ in Armstrong's generalised non-crossing partitions
poset, where the poset rank of $\pi_i$ equals $r_i$, and where the "block
structure" of $\pi_1$ is prescribed. We demonstrate that this result implies
all known enumerative results on ordinary and generalised non-crossing
partitions via appropriate summations. Surprisingly, this result on multi-chain
enumeration is new even for the original non-crossing partitions of Kreweras.
Moreover, the result allows one to solve the problem of rank-selected chain
enumeration in the type $D_n$ generalised non-crossing partitions poset, which,
in turn, leads to a proof of Armstrong's $F=M$ Conjecture in type $D_n$.

In this work, we present polarization observations of the supernova remnant G156.2+5.7 at a wavelength of 6 cm. Our aim is to understand the morphology and magnetic field structure of this remnant. We used the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array to obtain high sensitivity polarization observations, and we compared the results with previous measurements made at different wavelengths. 

Our analysis reveals that the magnetic field orientation in the western part of the remnant is perpendicular to the remnant's shell, while in the eastern part, it is parallel. These results suggest that the magnetic field in the western part is strong and has managed to push away the plasma, while in the eastern part, the magnetic field is weak and tangled. Furthermore, we observe a clear polarization shell surrounding the remnant's shell, which indicates the presence of an ordered magnetic field.

The motivation for this research is to gain a better understanding of supernova remnants and their magnetic field structures. Such knowledge will not only help us understand the evolution of supernova remnants but also provide valuable information for studying other astrophysical phenomena such as cosmic ray acceleration and the interstellar medium. Overall, our observations contribute to the growing body of knowledge regarding the magnetic field structures of supernova remnants and their role in shaping their evolution.

In this study, we investigate the impact of radio feedback from active galactic nuclei (AGN) in cosmological simulations on the formation of disk galaxies. The formation of disk galaxies is a complex process that requires the cooling and condensation of gas in dark matter halos. It has been suggested that feedback from AGN plays a crucial role in regulating this process, but the specifics of this interaction are not well understood.

Our research aims to address this knowledge gap by running cosmological simulations of galaxy formation with varying levels of AGN feedback. We use the state-of-the-art IllustrisTNG simulation suite, which includes a comprehensive subgrid model for AGN feedback that accounts for both thermal and kinetic energy injection.

We find that AGN feedback has a profound effect on the formation and morphology of disk galaxies. In simulations with strong feedback, massive black holes actively suppress the formation of extended disks by heating and disrupting gas in the surrounding halo. However, in simulations with weaker feedback, the growth of disk galaxies is less inhibited, leading to the formation of more extended and rotationally supported structures.

Overall, our results demonstrate the importance of AGN feedback in shaping the properties of disk galaxies, providing a valuable insight into the physics of galaxy formation and evolution.

The Universe contains approximately 6 times more dark matter than normal
baryonic matter, and a directly observed fundamental difference between dark
matter and baryons would both be significant for our understanding of dark
matter structures and provide us with information about the basic
characteristics of the dark matter particle. We discuss one distinctive feature
of dark matter structures in equilibrium, namely the property that a local dark
matter temperature may depend on direction. This is in stark contrast to
baryonic gases. We used X-ray observations of two nearby, relaxed galaxy
clusters, under the assumptions of hydrostatic equilibrium and identical dark
matter and gas temperatures in the outer cluster region, to measure this dark
matter temperature anisotropy beta_dm, with non-parametric Monte Carlo methods.
We find that beta_dm is greater than the value predicted for baryonic gases,
beta_gas=0, at more than 3 sigma confidence. The observed value of the
temperature anisotropy is in fair agreement with the results of cosmological
N-body simulations and shows that the equilibration of the dark matter
particles is not governed by local point-like interactions in contrast to
baryonic gases.

The authors present a study of the non equilibrium statistical properties of
a one dimensional hard-rod fluid dissipating energy via inelastic collisions
and subject to the action of a Gaussian heat bath, simulating an external
driving mechanism. They show that the description of the fluid based on the
one-particle phase-space reduced distribution function, in principle necessary
because of the presence of velocity dependent collisional dissipation, can be
contracted to a simpler description in configurational space. Indeed, by means
of a multiple-time scale method the authors derive a self-consistent governing
equation for the particle density distribution function. This equation is
similar to the dynamic density functional equation employed in the study of
colloids, but contains additional terms taking into account the inelastic
nature of the fluid. Such terms cannot be derived from a Liapunov generating
functional and contribute not only to the relaxational properties, but also to
the non equilibrium steady state properties. A validation of the theory against
molecular dynamics simulations is presented in a series of cases, and good
agreement is found.

In this work, we present a detailed analysis of the technical issues involved in the numerical propagation of high energy cosmic rays in the Galaxy. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that high energy cosmic rays are an important source of information about the origin and evolution of the universe. However, accurately simulating the propagation of these cosmic rays has proven to be a challenging task due to the complex nature of the galactic magnetic field and interactions with plasma and radiation fields.

Our computational framework is based on the Green's function method, which allows us to accurately simulate cosmic ray propagation in a broad range of astrophysical environments. We present a detailed evaluation of the numerical accuracy and stability of our method, including discussions on grid resolution, time stepping schemes, and boundary conditions.

Our results show that our method is capable of accurately simulating high energy cosmic ray propagation in a variety of astrophysical environments. We also identify several technical issues that need to be addressed in future work, such as improving the accuracy of the numerical integration and the selection of appropriate boundary conditions.

In summary, this work aims to provide a solid foundation for future numerical studies of high energy cosmic ray propagation in the Galaxy. We believe that our method will be an important tool for investigating the origin and evolution of cosmic rays and their relevance to astrophysical phenomena.

We analyzed the presently available experimental data on nucleon
electromagnetic form factors within a multipole model based on dispersion
relations. A good fit of the data is achieved by considering the coefficients
of the multipole expansions as logarithmic functions of the momentum transfer
squared. The superconvergence relations, applied to this coefficients, makes
the model agree with unitary constraints and pQCD asymptotics for the Dirac and
Pauli form factors. The soft photon emission is proposed as a mechanism
responsible for the difference between the Rosenbluth, polarization and
beam--target--asymmetry data. It is shown, that the experimentally measured
cross sections depend not only on the Dirac and Pauli form factors, but also on
the average number of the photons emitted. For proton this number is shown to
be different for different types of experimental measurements and then
estimated phenomenologically. For neutron the same mechanism predicts, that the
data form different types of experiments must coincide with high accuracy. A
joint fit of all the experimental data reproduce the $Q^2-$dependence with the
accuracy $\chi^2/dof=0.86$. Predictions of the model, that 1) the ratios of the
proton form factors $G_E/G_M$ are different for Rosenbluth, polarization and
beam--target--asymmetry experiments and 2) similar ratios are nearly the same
for neutron, can be used for experimental verification of the model.

In this work, we explore the optical implementation and entanglement distribution of Gaussian valence bond states. The problem that this study attempts to solve is how to generate and manipulate entangled photon pairs in a way that can be easily implemented using optical circuits. Entanglement is a crucial resource in quantum information processing, and Gaussian valence bond states have been proposed as a promising candidate for practical quantum communication. 

The key ideas of our work are based on the decomposition of the Fock space into subspaces with a definite number of photons. By taking advantage of the particular properties of the Gaussian valence bond states, we design a protocol that allows us to generate and distribute entangled pairs with high fidelity and efficiency. To achieve this, we use a combination of linear and nonlinear optical elements, such as beam splitters, phase shifters, and Pockels cells. 

The motivation for this research is to provide a practical method for the implementation of photon-based quantum communication systems, which will be essential for future advances in secure communication and quantum computing. In summary, the optical implementation and entanglement distribution of Gaussian valence bond states can pave the way for the development of practical quantum communication technologies.

The theory of the dislocation motion in the periodic potential relief of the
crystal lattice (the Peierls-Nabarro barriers) is reviewed. On the basis of the
kink mechanism the temperature dependence of the flow stress is described for a
wide class of materials. The theory of quantum mechanical dislocation
tunnelling through the Peierls-Nabarro barriers is extended and compared with
experimental data on the plasticity of alkali halides, BCC and HCP metals at
low temperatures. The behavior of the flow stress at the range of athermic
anomalies is modeled by changing the mechanism of the dislocation motion from
the thermally activated hopping over the barriers to the quantum tunnelling
through them. Some results of previous calculations are represented in a more
explicit convenient for applications form. The pronounced effect of the
switching between the normal and the superconducting states on the flow stress
of metals is explained on the basis of the change in the dissipative properties
of the electron subsystem affecting the dislocation motion.

In this work, we demonstrate the potential benefits of combining two commonly employed probes of dark energy: weak lensing tomography and halo clustering. The former method measures the distortions of images of distant galaxies due to the gravitational influence of intervening matter, while the latter involves the statistical analysis of the spatial distribution of massive dark matter halos hosting galaxy clusters. We show that the joint analysis of these two probes has the potential to significantly enhance our understanding of dark energy and its impact on the large-scale structure of the universe.

Motivated by the pressing need to better understand the nature of dark energy, we employ a theoretical framework that accounts for various measurement biases and uncertainties. Our methodology involves combining data from multiple cosmological surveys, including the Dark Energy Survey and the ongoing Hyper Suprime-Cam Survey.

The key advantage of our approach lies in its ability to mitigate various sources of systematics and thereby improve the robustness of our conclusions. Specifically, by combining the complementary information from both probes, we can more accurately determine the properties of dark energy, such as its equation of state, and probe any potential deviations from general relativity. Overall, our work highlights the potential for combining different probes of cosmology to unlock new insights into the fundamental nature of the universe.

In this work, we investigate the possible non-thermal nature of the soft-excess emission in the Sersic 159-03 galaxy cluster. The soft-excess emission is a puzzling phenomenon where an excess of soft X-ray radiation is observed in the spectra of several galaxy clusters, including Sersic 159-03. This emission is believed to arise from a thermal component, but its physical origin remains unclear. We aim to test an alternative hypothesis that proposes a non-thermal mechanism to explain the soft-excess emission in Sersic 159-03.

Our approach is based on analyzing the X-ray data of Sersic 159-03 obtained from the XMM-Newton telescope and combining it with radio data from the VLA observatory. We modeled the X-ray spectra of the cluster with a thermal and non-thermal component and compared them to the observed data. We found that the non-thermal component provides a better fit to the data than the thermal component, suggesting that the soft-excess emission in Sersic 159-03 may indeed have a non-thermal origin.

Our motivation for conducting this research is to understand the nature of the soft-excess emission, which has been a long-standing challenge in the study of galaxy clusters. Our findings have important implications for the current understanding of the physical processes that occur in these cosmic structures and may provide new insights into the production of non-thermal radiation.

In this work, we present recent results on the thermal Casimir force between dielectrics and related problems. Our research is motivated by the growing interest in the field of quantum vacuum fluctuations and their effects on macroscopic objects, especially at the nanoscale. The Casimir effect is a manifestation of these quantum fluctuations, where two parallel plates experience an attractive force due to the suppression of virtual photons between them. However, the inclusion of thermal effects complicates the situation and leads to a novel thermal Casimir force. 

We approach the study of the thermal Casimir force using a modified version of the Lifshitz theory. Our methodology involves a detailed calculation of the dielectric function and the dispersion relation of the materials involved. Moreover, we account for the temperature dependence of the dielectric function for a more accurate model. 

Our results indicate that the thermal Casimir force dominates at larger plate separations, and its behavior depends on the temperature and dielectric properties of the materials. Additionally, we investigate the effect of surface roughness on the thermal Casimir force and demonstrate that it can mitigate its strength. Our research presents a fundamental understanding of the thermal Casimir force within the context of dielectric materials and enhances our comprehension of quantum vacuum fluctuations.

In this study, we present the discovery of 11 new T dwarfs in the Two Micron All-Sky Survey (2MASS), including a possible L/T transition binary. Our motivation for this research is to expand our knowledge of the coolest and faintest members of the stellar population, with particular interest in the transition between L and T spectral types. This elusive transition marks the shift between objects dominated by dust clouds and those dominated by gas in their atmospheres. Such a transition is important because it allows us to better understand the atmospheric physics of brown dwarfs and their connection with the giant planets. 

The problem we aim to solve is identifying these transition objects, which are rare and elusive in nature. We use a variety of well-tested tools and techniques to identify and characterize our targets, including color-magnitude diagrams, spectral fitting, and photometric variability analysis of multi-epoch observations. 

Our key findings include the discovery of 11 new T dwarfs, whose spectroscopic and photometric properties are discussed in detail, as well as the identification of a possible L/T transition binary. The methodology employed in this research provides a framework for identifying and characterizing the coolest and dimmest objects in the universe, and contributes to our understanding of their physical properties and behavior.

In this work, we present the results obtained from the analysis of blazar surveys using two different instruments: the Wilkinson Microwave Anisotropy Probe (WMAP) and the Swift Gamma-Ray Burst Explorer. Blazars are a particular type of active galactic nuclei with a jet of particles moving at nearly the speed of light, which emits radiation across the electromagnetic spectrum. These objects are considered to be the most luminous and persistent emitters of high-energy radiation in the universe.

The problem that this work attempts to solve is that blazars exhibit flux variability over a wide range of timescales, which makes their detection and characterization challenging. Our primary objective was to identify and study the properties of blazars using a combination of radio, infrared, optical, X-ray, and gamma-ray wavelengths, with the ultimate goal of gaining a better understanding of these enigmatic objects.

Our methodology involved cross-instrument analyses of the same regions of the sky surveyed by WMAP and Swift, followed by the selection of candidate blazars based on their spectral and brightness properties. We also investigated the correlation between the gamma-ray flux and the radio spectral index for these objects.

The results of our study provide valuable insights into the nature of blazars and their physical properties, as well as their evolution over cosmic time. Our work contributes to advancing our understanding of the most extreme astrophysical phenomena in the universe and lays the groundwork for future studies and observations of blazars.

Inflationary flow analysis has been used to probe the nature of the primordial power spectrum, with the aim of identifying the limits of achievable resolution in its measurement. In this study, we explore the possibility of using the inflationary flow to map the primordial power spectrum with high resolution, while considering the observational constraints. We used a model-independent approach, which allowed us to analyze some of the key features of the inflationary trajectory and the resulting primordial power spectrum. Our results demonstrate that there are some fundamental limits on the resolution of the primordial power spectrum that can be obtained with the inflationary flow analysis. We argue that these limitations arise due to the exponential expansion of the universe during inflation and the subsequent smoothing of the spectrum on large scales. 

The methodology we employed in our analysis involved applying the inflationary flow formalism to a large number of mock datasets, to determine the resolution limits of the primordial power spectrum that can be achieved with this method. Our results provide theoretical guidance for future observational efforts aimed at improving constraints on the primordial power spectrum. Ultimately, this study contributes to the ongoing efforts to understand the earliest moments of our universe, and to measure the properties of inflation with the utmost precision.

In this study, we investigated the consistency of the thick brane model with recent astronomical observations. The motivation for this research is to examine the possibility of explaining dark matter and dark energy as effects of modifications to standard gravity. The thick brane model is a modification of the standard cosmological model, which proposes that the universe consists of a four-dimensional spacetime and a higher-dimensional space. This model can predict modifications to the standard gravity, which can explain some of the observed astronomical phenomena, including the accelerated expansion of the universe.

To evaluate the consistency of the thick brane model with observations, we compared its predictions with the recent observations of dark matter and dark energy. Our methodology involved investigating the modified gravity solutions in the thick brane model and comparing them with the observed mass density of galaxies and cosmic microwave background radiation measurements.

Our results show that, while the thick brane model can describe the modified gravity, it struggles to provide a consistent explanation for the observed dark matter and dark energy phenomena. Additionally, the model struggles to explain the cosmic microwave background radiation temperature fluctuations. Therefore, while the thick brane model is an interesting modification of the standard cosmological model, significant modifications or extensions are required to provide a consistent explanation of the observed universe.

A general class of loop quantizations for anisotropic models is introduced
and discussed, which enhances loop quantum cosmology by relevant features seen
in inhomogeneous situations. The main new effect is an underlying lattice which
is being refined during dynamical changes of the volume. In general, this leads
to a new feature of dynamical difference equations which may not have constant
step-size, posing new mathematical problems. It is discussed how such models
can be evaluated and what lattice refinements imply for semiclassical behavior.
Two detailed examples illustrate that stability conditions can put strong
constraints on suitable refinement models, even in the absence of a fundamental
Hamiltonian which defines changes of the underlying lattice. Thus, a large
class of consistency tests of loop quantum gravity becomes available. In this
context, it will also be seen that quantum corrections due to inverse powers of
metric components in a constraint are much larger than they appeared recently
in more special treatments of isotropic, free scalar models where they were
artificially suppressed.

In this research work, we present a novel approach for the recursive calculation of effective resistances in distance-regular networks based on the Bose-Mesner algebra and Christoffel-Darboux identity. The motivation behind this research is the necessity to efficiently calculate effective resistances, as they are fundamental in a variety of fields such as network design, control, and information theory. Current methods for effective resistance calculation are based on matrix inversion or Monte Carlo methods, which can be computationally expensive for large networks.

The problem we attempt to solve is to provide a faster and more efficient method for calculating effective resistances in distance-regular networks. Our proposed approach combines the spectral properties of distance-regular graphs with the recursive relation obtained using the Bose-Mesner algebra and Christoffel-Darboux identity to obtain a compact and efficient algorithm for effective resistance computation.

We apply our algorithm to a set of distance-regular graphs and compare our results with those obtained using matrix inversion and Monte Carlo methods. Our results show that our approach is significantly faster than existing methods for effective resistance computation and provides accurate results for various distance-regular graphs.

In conclusion, the proposed algorithm based on Bose-Mesner algebra and Christoffel-Darboux identity provides an efficient and accurate method for calculating effective resistances in distance-regular networks. This research work contributes to the development of faster and more efficient algorithms for network analysis and will be useful in various fields where network analysis is applied.

In this work, we investigate the concept of the Plasma Puddle (PP) as a perturbative black hole. The PP is a confined plasma system, where gravity is negligible, and the fluid dynamics is the dominant force. We demonstrate that, when the PP is compressed, a density gradient is established that creates a gravitational field. This field is capable of trapping light, and therefore, the PP acts as a black hole. 

The motivation for this research comes from the need to understand the fundamental properties of the PP and its relation to astrophysical black holes. Our key idea is that the PP can act as a laboratory model to understand the behavior of black holes in the universe. Specifically, we demonstrate that the PP black hole exhibits Hawking radiation, which is a well-known phenomenon in astrophysical black holes. 

Our methodology involves theoretical analysis and numerical simulations of the PP dynamics. We use fluid dynamics equations and Einstein's field equations to model the PP as a black hole. Our simulation results show that the PP black hole emits radiation as predicted by Hawking's theory. 

In summary, we propose the PP as a perturbative black hole and demonstrate that it exhibits Hawking radiation. Our work provides insights into the behavior of black holes and offers a new perspective for laboratory experiments.

In this work, we present an investigation on the Kalman-Yakubovich-Popov (KYP) inequality for passive discrete time-invariant systems. The motivation of this research stems from the importance of the KYP inequality in the design and analysis of various control systems. The KYP inequality is a powerful tool for verifying stability and performance of control systems, and its application to passive systems is particularly significant as it provides insights into the properties of these systems.

Our work attempts to solve the problem of deriving a generalized KYP inequality for passive discrete systems. Prior research has formulated the KYP inequality for certain classes of systems, but a generalized inequality has not yet been developed for passive discrete systems. We seek to fill this gap in the literature by proposing a new KYP inequality that is applicable to any passive discrete time-invariant system. 

Our approach involves using the structure of passive systems to derive new bounds that characterize the system's behavior. We then use the derived bounds to formulate a new KYP inequality that can be applied to passive discrete systems. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed inequality through numerical examples, which confirm its validity and usefulness in the analysis and design of passive systems.

In conclusion, our work presents a novel approach to deriving a KYP inequality for passive discrete systems, which can significantly contribute to the design and analysis of control systems. The proposed inequality is applicable to any passive discrete time-invariant system, and its effectiveness has been demonstrated through numerical examples.

INTEGRAL monitoring of the Galactic Plane is revealing a growing number of
recurrent X-ray transients, characterised by short outbursts with very fast
rise times (~ tens of minutes) and typical durations of a few hours. A
substantial fraction of these sources are associated with OB supergiants and
hence define a new class of massive X-ray binaries, which we call Supergiant
Fast X-ray Transients. Characterisation of the astrophysical parameters of
their counterparts is underway. So far, we have found a number of late O and
early B supergiants of different luminosities at a large range of distances.
Nothing in their optical properties sets them apart from classical Supergiant
X-ray Binaries. On the other hand, there is now rather concluding evidence that
persistent supergiant X-ray binaries also show fast outbursts. This suggests a
continuum of behaviours between typical persistent supergiant systems and
purely transient systems, but offers very little information about the physical
causes of the outbursts.

We present observations with the new 11 GHz radiometer of the COSMOSOMAS
experiment at the Teide Observatory (Tenerife). The sky region between 0 deg <=
RA <= 360 deg and 26 deg <= DEC 49 deg (ca. 6500 square degrees) was observed
with an angular resolution of 0.9 deg. Two orthogonal independent channels in
the receiving system measured total power signals from linear polarizations
with a 2 GHz bandwidth. Maps with an average sensitivity of 50 microK per beam
have been obtained for each channel. At high Galactic latitude (|b|>30deg) the
11 GHz data are found to contain the expected cosmic microwave background as
well as extragalactic radiosources, galactic synchrotron and free-free
emission, and a dust-correlated component which is very likely of galactic
origin. At the angular scales allowed by the window function of the experiment,
the dust-correlated component presents an amplitude \Delta T aprox. 9-13 microK
while the CMB signal is of order 27 microK. The spectral behaviour of the
dust-correlated signal is examined in the light of previous COSMOSOMAS data at
13-17 GHz and WMAP data at 22-94 GHz in the same sky region. We detect a
flattening in the spectral index of this signal below 20 GHz which rules out
synchrotron radiation as being responsible for the emission. This anomalous
dust emission can be described by a combination of free-free emission and
spinning dust models with a flux density peaking around 20 GHz.

In this work, we explore the dynamics of high-speed collision of two perfect fluids in cylindrical symmetry. The phenomenon of fluid collapse is of great interest in astrophysical scenarios, where it may lead to the formation of black holes. Through numerical simulations, we investigate the conditions necessary for the formation of trapped surfaces and eventual black hole formation, by reporting on the effect of different initial data and the equation of state of the fluids. Our results show that for certain parameter regimes, the fluids can violently collapse and form a black hole horizon, with properties consistent with the cosmic censorship conjecture. We also identify the emergence of self-similar solutions and gravitational wave signals in the late-time behavior of the collapse.

The key idea behind our investigation is the use of a general framework for the description of perfect fluid dynamics in cylindrical symmetry, together with the construction of a numerical scheme that can handle the high-speed dynamics of the collision. Our methodology involves solving for the Einstein field equations together with the fluid equations, as well as analyzing the behavior of certain geometric quantities and energy conditions.

This research is motivated by the need to understand the fundamental properties of the universe, particularly in the context of extreme gravitational fields and high-energy density regimes. Our work contributes to the ongoing effort of exploring the nature of black holes, their formation, and their observational signatures.

We study the quantum Hall effect in graphene at filling factors \nu = 0 and
\nu = \pm, concentrating on the quantum Hall ferromagnetic regime, within a
non-perturbative bosonization formalism. We start by developing a bosonization
scheme for electrons with two discrete degrees of freedom (spin-1/2 and
pseudospin-1/2) restricted to the lowest Landau level. Three distinct phases
are considered, namely the so-called spin-pseudospin, spin, and pseudospin
phases. The first corresponds to a quarter-filled (\nu =-1) while the others to
a half-filled (\nu = 0) lowest Landau level. In each case, we show that the
elementary neutral excitations can be treated approximately as a set of
n-independent kinds of boson excitations. The boson representation of the
projected electron density, the spin, pseudospin, and mixed spin-pseudospin
density operators are derived. We then apply the developed formalism to the
effective continuous model, which includes SU(4) symmetry breaking terms,
recently proposed by Alicea and Fisher. For each quantum Hall state, an
effective interacting boson model is derived and the dispersion relations of
the elementary excitations are analytically calculated. We propose that the
charged excitations (quantum Hall skyrmions) can be described as a coherent
state of bosons. We calculate the semiclassical limit of the boson model
derived from the SU(4) invariant part of the original fermionic Hamiltonian and
show that it agrees with the results of Arovas and co-workers for SU(N) quantum
Hall skyrmions. We briefly discuss the influence of the SU(4) symmetry breaking
terms in the skyrmion energy.

In this paper, we explore the generators of II_1 factors and their relationships with other factors. The key idea behind our work is to understand the internal structure of II_1 factors by finding generators that generate them. These generators play a crucial role in understanding how various factors can be decomposed and classified. 

Our motivation for this research comes from the fact that II_1 factors are fundamental objects in the theory of operator algebras, with many interesting properties. We aim to provide a better understanding of their structures and to uncover important connections between them. 

Our methodology involves using operator algebras, group theory, and subfactors to study and classify these factors. We also employ various techniques, such as modular theory and intertwining operators, to analyze the generators and their actions on the factor. 

Our results include new insights into the structure and classification of II_1 factors, as well as a better understanding of their generators. In particular, we establish the existence of certain generators for certain factors and demonstrate their essential role in various decompositions and classifications. Our work sheds light on the internal structure of II_1 factors and provides a foundation for future research in this area.

The knowledge of the nuclear symmetry energy of hot neutron-rich matter is
important for understanding the dynamical evolution of massive stars and the
supernova explosion mechanisms. In particular, the electron capture rate on
nuclei and/or free protons in presupernova explosions is especially sensitive
to the symmetry energy at finite temperature. In view of the above, in the
present work we calculate the symmetry energy as a function of the temperature
for various values of the baryon density, by applying a momentum-dependent
effective interaction. In addition to a previous work, the thermal effects are
studied separately both in the kinetic part and the interaction part of the
symmetry energy. We focus also on the calculations of the mean field potential,
employed extensively in heavy ion reaction research, both for nuclear and pure
neutron matter. The proton fraction and the electron chemical potential, which
are crucial quantities for representing the thermal evolution of supernova and
neutron stars, are calculated for various values of the temperature. Finally,
we construct a temperature dependent equation of state of $\beta$-stable
nuclear matter, the basic ingredient for the evaluation of the neutron star
properties.

In this work, we present a group-finding algorithm and a catalogue of groups of galaxies in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey Data Release 5. Our aim is to identify and characterize groups of galaxies to better understand the large-scale structure of the universe.

The group-finder algorithm is based on the Friends-of-Friends (FoF) algorithm, with modifications to optimize the grouping of galaxies across a range of separation scales. We apply the algorithm to a sample of galaxies from the SDSS DR5 and generate a catalogue of groups containing at least three members.

Our catalogue contains 79,991 groups of galaxies, with a median group size of 16 members and a maximum size of over 500 members. We also provide information on the properties of each group, including the distribution of galaxy luminosities, velocities, and colors.

The motivation for this research is to improve our understanding of the nature and evolution of galaxies by studying groups as a whole, rather than as individual objects. The grouping of galaxies can reveal important information about the large-scale structure and dynamics of the universe, such as the clustering of galaxies and the formation of galaxy clusters.

Overall, our work provides a valuable resource for future studies on the properties and evolution of galaxy groups, and helps to shed light on the mysteries of the universe.

We present new results from our ongoing multiplicity study of exoplanet host
stars, carried out with the infrared camera SofI at ESO-NTT. We have identified
new low mass companions to the planet host stars HD101930 and HD65216.
HD101930AB is a wide binary systems composed of the planet host star HD101930A
and its companion HD101930B which is a M0 to M1 dwarf with a mass of about
0.7Msun separated from the primary by ~73arcsec (2200AU projected separation).
HD65216 forms a hierarchical triple system, with a projected separation of
253AU (angular separation of about 7arcsec) between the planet host star
HD65216A and its close binary companion HD65216BC, whose two components are
separated by only ~0.17arcsec (6AU of projected separation). Two VLT-NACO
images separated by 3 years confirm that this system is co-moving to the planet
host star. The infrared photometry of HD65216B and C is consistent with a M7 to
M8 (0.089Msun), and a L2 to L3 dwarf (0.078Msun), respectively, both close to
the sub-stellar limit. An infrared spectrum with VLT-ISAAC of the pair
HD65216BC, even though not resolved spatially, confirms this late spectral
type. Furthermore, we present H- and K-band ISAAC infrared spectra of HD16141B,
the recently detected co-moving companion of the planet host star HD16141A. The
infrared spectroscopy as well as the apparent infrared photometry of HD16141B
are both fully consistent with a M2 to M3 dwarf located at the distance of the
planet host star.

The propagation of damage in a confined magnetic Ising film, with short range
competing magnetic fields ($h$) acting at opposite walls, is studied by means
of Monte Carlo simulations. Due to the presence of the fields, the film
undergoes a wetting transition at a well defined critical temperature $T_w(h)$.
In fact, the competing fields causes the occurrence of an interface between
magnetic domains of different orientation. For $T < T_w(h)$ ($T > T_w(h)$) such
interface is bounded (unbounded) to the walls, while right at $T_w(h)$ the
interface is essentially located at the center of the film.
  It is found that the spatio-temporal spreading of the damage becomes
considerably enhanced by the presence of the interface, which act as a
''catalyst'' of the damage causing an enhancement of the total damaged area.
The critical points for damage spreading are evaluated by extrapolation to the
thermodynamic limit using a finite-size scaling approach. Furthermore, the
wetting transition effectively shifts the location of the damage spreading
critical points, as compared with the well known critical temperature of the
order-disorder transition characteristic of the Ising model. Such a critical
points are found to be placed within the non-wet phase.

In this work, we present a multilevel deconstruction of the in vivo behavior of DNA-protein complexes that form loops. These complexes play a crucial role in regulating genetic processes, but their behavior in vivo is poorly understood. Our goal is to understand the dynamics of these complexes, their interactions with other molecules, and their role in gene regulation.

Our methodology combines high-resolution imaging and computational modeling to investigate the behavior of DNA-protein complexes at multiple levels. We begin with single-molecule experiments to study the behavior of individual complexes, then move to whole-cell imaging to observe their behavior in vivo. Finally, we use computational modeling to integrate our experimental data with existing knowledge of gene regulation.

Our results reveal that DNA-protein complexes form highly dynamic and diverse structures in vivo, and that their behavior is influenced by a wide range of factors, including DNA topology, protein concentration, and the presence of other molecules.
