cal/mole, respectively, for rate increases associated with time-specific
irradiation of the crystalline substrates prior to dissolution and
incorporation in the reaction solutions. These energies, presumably, correspond
to conformational or vibrational changes in the reactants or the activated
complex. For the lactic dehydrogenase / pyruvate reaction, it is estimated that
on the order of 10% of the irradiation energy (546 nm, 400 footcandles for 5
seconds) would be required to produce the observed reaction rate increase if a
presumed photoproduct is consumed stoichiometrically with the pyruvate
substrate. These findings are consistent with the proposition that the observed
reaction rate enhancement involves photoproducts derived from oscillatory
atmospheric gas reactions at the crystalline enzyme substrate surfaces rather
than photo-excitations of the substrate molecules, per se.

In this work, we investigate the dual computations of non-abelian Yang-Mills on the lattice. Our motivation for this research stems from the need to provide a deeper understanding of non-abelian Yang-Mills theory and its lattice formulation.

Our key idea is to study the duality of the lattice formulation of non-abelian Yang-Mills theory. We employ a methodology that involves the use of the lattice regularization to obtain a dual formulation of the gauge theory. We then use this dual formulation to study the properties of the gauge theory and the possible interplay between the electromagnetic and chromodynamic fields.

The problem that our work attempts to solve is the question of whether the dual formulation of non-abelian Yang-Mills theory can provide insights into the properties of the gauge theory and its interactions with other fields. To this end, we study the lattice formulation of the theory and its duality properties. We then use the results obtained from the dual formulation to analyze the gauge theory and its interactions.

In conclusion, our work provides a new perspective on the lattice formulation of non-abelian Yang-Mills theory. By studying its dual computations, we hope to shed light on the nature of the gauge theory and its interactions. Our results may have implications for the study of fundamental interactions at high energies and the development of more complete theories of particle physics.

According to Two-Time Physics, there is more to space-time than can be
garnered with the ordinary formulation of physics. Two-Time Physics has shown
that the Standard Model of Particles and Forces is successfully reproduced by a
two-time field theory in 4 space and 2 time dimensions projected as a
holographic image on an emergent spacetime in 3+1 dimensions. Among the
successes of this approach is the resolution of the strong CP problem of QCD as
an outcome of the restrictions imposed by the higher symmetry structures in 4+2
dimensions. In this paper we launch a program to construct the duals of the
Standard Model as other holographic images of the same 4+2 dimensional theory
on a variety of emergent spacetimes in 3+1 dimensions. These dual field
theories are obtained as a family of gauge choices in the master 4+2 field
theory. In the present paper we deal with some of the simpler gauge choices
which lead to interacting Klein-Gordon field theories for the conformal scalar
with a predicted SO(d,2) symmetry in a variety of interesting curved spacetimes
in (d-1)+1 dimensions. More challenging and more interesting gauge choices
(including some that relate to mass) which are left to future work are also
outlined. Through this approach we discover a new realm of previously
unexplored dualities and hidden symmetries that exist both in the macroscopic
and microscopic worlds, at the classical and quantum levels. Such phenomena
predicted by 2T-physics can in principle be confirmed both by theory and
experiment. 1T-physics can be used to analyze the predictions but in most
instances gives no clue that the predicted phenomena exist in the first place.
This point of view suggests a new paradigm for the construction of a
fundamental theory that is likely to impact on the quest for unification.

Recently [J. Haro and E. Elizalde, Phys. Rev. Lett. {\bf 97}, 130401 (2006)],
a Hamiltonian formulation has been introduced in order to address some
longstanding severe problems associated with the physical description of the
dynamical Casimir effect at all times while the mirrors are moving. Here we
present the complete calculation providing precise details, in particular, of
the regularization procedure, which is decisive for the correct derivation of
physically meaningful quantities. A basic difference when comparing with the
results previously obtained by other authors is the fact that the motion force
derived in our approach contains a reactive term --proportional to the mirrors'
acceleration. This is of the essence in order to obtain particles with a
positive energy all the time during the oscillation of the mirrors --while
always satisfying the energy conservation law. A careful analysis of the
interrelations among the different results previously obtained in the
literature is then carried out. For simplicity, the specific case of a neutral
scalar field in one dimension, with one or two partially transmitting mirrors
(a fundamental proviso for the regularization issue) is considered in more
detail, but our general method is shown to be generalizable, without essential
problems (Sect. 2 of this paper), to fields of any kind in two and higher
dimensions.

Magnetic properties with chains of hcp Co hollow spheres have been studied.
The diameter of the spheres ranges from 500 to 800 nm, with a typical shell
thickness of about 60 nm. The shell is polycrystalline with an average
crystallite size of 20 to 35 nm. The blocking temperature determined by the
zero-field-cooling MZFC(T) measurement at H = 90 Oe is about 325 K. The
corresponding effective anisotropy is determined as, Keff = 4.6*10^4 J/m^3. In
addition, the blocking temperature and the effective anisotropy determined by
the analysis on HC(T) are 395 K and 5.7*10^4 J/m^3, respectively. The
experimentally determined anisotropy is smaller by one order of magnitude than
the magnetocrystalline anisotropy of the bulk hcp Co, which is about 3 to
5*10^5 J/m^3. A further analysis on HC(T) shows that the magnetization reversal
follows a nucleation rotational mode with an effective switching volume, V* =
2.3*10^3 nm^3. The corresponding effective diameter is calculated as 16.4 nm.
It is slightly larger than the coherence length of Co, about 15 nm. The
possible reason for the much reduced magnetic anisotropy is discussed briefly.

Motivated by a class of orbit problems in astrophysics, this paper considers
solutions to Hill's equation with forcing strength parameters that vary from
cycle to cycle. The results are generalized to include period variations from
cycle to cycle. The development of the solutions to the differential equation
is governed by a discrete map. For the general case of Hill's equation in the
unstable limit, we consider separately the case of purely positive matrix
elements and those with mixed signs; we then find exact expressions, bounds,
and working estimates for the growth rates. We also find exact expressions,
estimates, and bounds for the infinite products of several $2 \times 2$
matrices with random variables in the matrix elements. In the limit of sharply
spiked forcing terms (the delta function limit), we find analytic solutions for
each cycle and for the discrete map that matches solutions from cycle to cycle;
for this case we find the growth rates and the condition for instability in the
limit of large forcing strength, as well as the widths of the stable/unstable
zones.

We examine the properties of a morphologically-selected sample of 0.4<z<1.0
spheroidal galaxies in the GOODS fields in order to ascertain whether their
increase in abundance with time arises primarily from mergers. To address this
question we determine scaling relations between the dynamical mass determined
from stellar velocity dispersions, and the stellar mass determined from optical
and infrared photometry. We exploit these relations across the larger sample
for which we have stellar masses in order to construct the first statistically
robust estimate of the evolving dynamical mass function over 0<z<1. The trends
observed match those seen in the stellar mass functions of Bundy et al. 2005
regarding the top-down growth in the abundance of spheroidal galaxies. By
referencing our dynamical masses to the halo virial mass we compare the growth
rate in the abundance of spheroidals to that predicted by the assembly of dark
matter halos. Our comparisons demonstrate that major mergers do not fully
account for the appearance of new spheroidals since z~1 and that additional
mechanisms, such as morphological transformations, are required to drive the
observed evolution.

In this paper, we present our work on Exploded Fibrations, a new direction in the study of algebraic geometry and topology. Our motivation for this research stems from the desire to develop a more refined understanding of the relationship between geometry and topology in the context of modern algebraic geometry. Specifically, we seek to investigate the ways in which the geometry of a space is related to its homotopy type, and how this relationship can help us to better understand both the topology of the space and its algebraic structure.

Our approach involves the use of a new technical framework that we call the "exploded category" of spaces. This framework allows us to construct new types of fibrations that capture the subtle interplay between geometry and topology that is often overlooked in traditional algebraic geometry. Through a careful analysis of these fibrations, we are able to derive new results about the topology of spaces, as well as new tools for working with algebraic structures.

In addition to providing a new perspective on the relationship between geometry and topology, our work also addresses a number of important open problems in the field of algebraic geometry. Specifically, we aim to solve previously unsolved problems related to the homotopy theory of algebraic varieties, as well as provide new insights into the geometric structures that underlie many important algebraic structures. Overall, we believe that our work has the potential to open up a new frontier in algebraic geometry and topology, and we look forward to continued progress in this area.

Most industrial machine tools have a serial kinematic architecture, which
means that each axis has to carry the following one, including its actuators
and joints. High Speed Machining highlights some drawbacks of such
architectures: heavy moving parts require from the machine structure high
stiffness to limit bending problems that lower the machine accuracy, and limit
the dynamic performances of the feed axes. That is why PKMs attract more and
more researchers and companies, because they are claimed to offer several
advantages over their serial counterparts, like high structural rigidity and
high dynamic capacities. Indeed, the parallel kinematic arrangement of the
links provides higher stiffness and lower moving masses that reduce inertia
effects. Thus, PKMs have better dynamic performances. However, the design of a
parallel kinematic machine tool (PKMT) is a hard task that requires further
research studies before wide industrial use can be expected. Many criteria need
to be taken into account in the design of a PKMT. We pay special attention to
the description of kinetostatic criteria that rely on the conditioning of the
Jacobian matrix of the mechanism. The organisation of this paper is as follows:
next section introduces general remarks about PKMs, then is explained why PKMs
can be interesting alternative machine tool designs. Then are presented
existing PKMTs. An application to the design of a small-scale machine tool
prototype developed at IRCCyN is presented at the end of this paper.

In this work, we aimed to investigate the uncertainty surrounding mass loss on the asymptotic giant branch (AGB). While the occurrence of mass loss is well established, the precise mechanism driving this process remains unknown. Our investigation was centered on collating and analyzing observational data from a range of sources to gain a more detailed understanding of the underlying mechanisms driving mass loss. We also evaluated a number of theoretical models for their compatibility with the available data.

Our methodological approach included undertaking a comprehensive review of existing literature and observational data. We analyzed the data using advanced statistical and machine learning techniques to identify patterns and correlations. We also used sophisticated models to better understand the impact of various physical processes, such as radiation pressure and dust formation, on mass loss.

The problem this work attempts to solve is the lack of a clear understanding of the mechanisms driving mass loss on the AGB. By conducting a thorough analysis of existing data, we aimed to identify potential drivers behind the complex and poorly understood process of mass loss.

Our results suggest that the drivers of mass loss on the AGB are vast and complex, and further observations and modeling are needed to better understand them. Nonetheless, our findings provide significant insights into the fundamental processes regulating mass loss on the AGB.

In recent years, the study of Josephson junction arrays has attracted significant attention due to their potential applications as qubits in quantum computation. In this work, we investigate the superconductor-insulator duality for an array of Josephson wires. The motivation for this research is to understand the fundamental properties of the system, including the possible existence of a quantum phase transition between a superconducting phase and an insulating phase. 

We consider an array of Josephson wires coupled via tunneling junctions and investigate the system’s behavior using a renormalization group method. Our analysis suggests that the superconductor-insulator duality holds in the low-energy regime, with the superconducting phase corresponding to a fixed point of the renormalization group flow. We also find that the insulating phase is associated with a different fixed point.

Our results demonstrate the existence of a quantum phase transition between the superconducting and insulating phases of the Josephson wire array. The critical behavior at this transition point is found to be characterized by universal scaling exponents, providing insight into the critical behavior of more complex systems. Our investigation contributes to the understanding of the Josephson effect in condensed matter physics and sheds light on the possible application of Josephson junction arrays in quantum computation.

In this study, we investigate "hidden" Seyfert 2 galaxies, i.e., those that are not easily detected using conventional methods, in the Chandra Deep Field North. We employ an X-ray stacking analysis to reveal these elusive objects, using data from the Chandra X-ray Observatory, optical/near-infrared spectroscopy, and multi-wavelength observations. Our objective is to understand the role of these obscured active galactic nuclei (AGN) in galaxy evolution and their contribution to the cosmic X-ray background radiation. 

Motivated by the fact that more than half of all AGN activity is obscured by gas and dust, we aim to identify these hidden Seyfert 2 galaxies and study their properties. We utilize X-ray stacking analysis since it helps to increase the signal-to-noise ratio of faint sources and recover information from undetected sources. We confirm the existence of "hidden" Seyfert 2 galaxies in the Chandra Deep Field North by detecting their high-energy emission, which is diagnostic of AGN activity.

This work attempts to contribute to a better understanding of the AGN population and their significance in galaxy evolution, as well as to provide insight into the origin of the cosmic X-ray background. Our results highlight the importance of X-ray observations in detecting obscured AGN activity, and contribute to the ongoing debate regarding the nature of the observed cosmic X-ray background radiation.

In this paper we present a novel framework for sequence to shape maps. These
combinatorial maps realize exponentially many shapes, and have preimages which
contain extended connected subgraphs of diameter n (neutral networks). We prove
that all basic properties of RNA folding maps also hold for combinatorial maps.
Our construction is as follows: suppose we are given a graph $H$ over the $\{1
>...,n\}$ and an alphabet of nucleotides together with a symmetric relation
$\mathcal{R}$, implied by base pairing rules. Then the shape of a sequence of
length n is the maximal H subgraph in which all pairs of nucleotides incident
to H-edges satisfy $\mathcal{R}$. Our main result is to prove the existence of
at least $\sqrt{2}^{n-1}$ shapes with extended neutral networks, i.e. shapes
that have a preimage with diameter $n$ and a connected component of size at
least $(\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2})^n+(\frac{1-\sqrt{5}}{2})^n$. Furthermore, we show
that there exists a certain subset of shapes which carries a natural graph
structure. In this graph any two shapes are connected by a path of shapes with
respective neutral networks of distance one. We finally discuss our results and
provide a comparison with RNA folding maps.

We discuss the emission of gravitational radiation produced in encounters of
dark matter galactic halos. To this aim we perform a number of numerical
simulations of typical galaxy mergers, computing the associated gravitational
radiation waveforms as well as the energy released in the processes. Our
simulations yield dimensionless gravitational wave amplitudes of the order of
$10^{-13}$ and gravitational wave frequencies of the order of $10^{-16}$ Hz,
when the galaxies are located at a distance of 10 Mpc. These values are of the
same order as those arising in the gravitational radiation originated by strong
variations of the gravitational field in the early Universe, and therefore,
such gravitational waves cannot be directly observed by ground-based detectors.
We discuss the feasibility of an indirect detection by means of the B-mode
polarization of the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) induced by such waves.
Our results show that the gravitational waves from encounters of dark matter
galactic halos leave much too small an imprint on the CMB polarization to be
actually observed with ongoing and future missions.

The rings observed around supernova remnant 1987A are emitted by a plasma
mainly made of ionized and neutral hydrogen atoms. With a density of 10 power
10 atoms per cubic metre, and at least a dimension of plasma of 0.01
light-year, the column density is 10 power 24 atoms per square metre, much more
than needed for an optically thick gas at Lyman frequencies (Case B). While, at
10000 K, the bulky gas would absorb Lyman lines fully, at 50000K it emits
superradiant lines. As superradiance de-excites the atoms strongly, nearly all
available energy is emitted in a few competing modes: Superradiance appears
only for beams which cross the largest column densities; for an observation
from Earth, these beams generate three elliptical hollow cylinders whose bases
are the observed rings; admitting that the Earth is not in a privileged
direction, these cylinders envelope ellipsoidal shells observed, for the
external rings, by photon echoes. For the equatorial ring, the brightness of
the superradiant beams is multiplied by a quasi-resonant induced scattering of
the rays emitted by the star. The de-excitation of atoms by emitted beams cools
the gas strongly, so that ionization decreases fast, the process self
accelerates. The energy of the high radiance rays from the star is strongly
scattered to the ring while the low radiance of the glow which surrounds the
star is rather amplified. The fast absorption of the radial beams produces the
radial decrease of radiance of the ring while a competition of modes produces
the pearls.

In this work, we investigate the shear-rate dependent transport coefficients for inelastic Maxwell models. Our study aims to address the problem of understanding the rheological behavior of soft-matter systems and improving their design and engineering. We employ non-equilibrium molecular dynamics simulations to derive the transport coefficients that characterize the material properties of the inelastic Maxwell model. Our results show that these transport coefficients are sensitive to the shear rate, indicating non-Newtonian flow behavior. This dependence is attributed to the shear-induced particle alignment and the consequent changes in the stress tensor components. In comparison with the elastic Maxwell model, the inelastic model demonstrates a higher shear viscosity, a larger Navier slip length, and a smaller thermal conductivity. We further explore the effect of different collision rules and boundary conditions on the transport coefficients, finding that they play an important role in defining the flow characteristics of inelastic Maxwell models. Our study provides new insights into the complex shear-rate dependence of inelastic soft materials and highlights the role of conservation laws and boundary conditions in material design and processing.

In this paper, we present a hydrodynamical simulation of detonations in superbursts. The motivation for this research is to enhance our understanding of superbursts, which are unusually intense and energetic stellar explosions that occur on the surface of neutron stars. These explosions release large amounts of energy and light, which can potentially help astronomers better understand the nature of matter in the extreme conditions present in neutron stars.

Our simulation employs a high-resolution numerical method for solving the equations of ideal gas hydrodynamics. We also use a specialized algorithm that allows us to properly resolve the detonation front and track its propagation in time and space. The preliminary one-dimensional results we obtained suggest that the detonation propagation consists of two phases: a slower deflagration phase followed by a faster detonation phase. The detonation velocities we obtain are consistent with observational data for superbursts.

The problem this work attempts to solve is the lack of a comprehensive theoretical model for superbursts. Our simulation provides a valuable tool for exploring the physics of superbursts and obtaining insights into the mechanisms responsible for their occurrence. The results obtained can guide future observational studies and help to better understand the properties of neutron stars.

In this study, we present the use of Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) as an ideal probe to study the solar chromosphere. Our research aims to address the limitations of previous observations of the chromosphere that were restricted to visible and ultraviolet wavelengths. The ALMA telescope allows us to observe the chromosphere's thermal structure in high precision by detecting the emission from CO and other diatomic molecules.

Our work has shown that ALMA provides valuable information about the chromosphere's temperature, density, and magnetic field structure. The data produced allows us to investigate the physical processes that occur in the chromosphere, such as the heating of the corona, and the source of high-energy particles. Additionally, the observations provide essential information to improve our understanding of the solar atmosphere's dynamics.

The methodology used in this work involves analyzing data obtained with ALMA under different conditions and comparing it to solar models. Observations during the solar eclipse of 2017 provided a unique opportunity to capture and compare data at different wavelengths with those from ALMA. We also conducted simulations to identify the factors that influence the results of our observations.

Overall, our research highlights ALMA's potential as an essential tool in investigating the chromosphere's structure and how it relates to the wider solar atmosphere. The results will enable a better understanding of the Sun's behavior and its impact on Earth's climate and space weather.

In this work, we investigate the scaling properties of surfaces composed of pyrex and silicon that have been subjected to blasting by sharp particles. The motivation for this research is to gain a deeper understanding of the behavior of surfaces subjected to impact by sharp objects, and to provide insights that can be used to improve manufacturing processes that use such impacts. In particular, we focus on understanding how the surface roughness of these materials changes as a function of impact energy and angle.

Our results suggest that there is a clear scaling regime for both pyrex and silicon surfaces that have been blasted by sharp particles. We find that the surface roughness of these materials exhibits a power law dependence on the impact energy, with a scaling exponent of approximately 0.5. Additionally, we observe that the scaling exponent is sensitive to the angle of impact, with different exponents being observed for normal and oblique impacts.

In summary, our work sheds new light on the behavior of surfaces subjected to sharp impacts and provides valuable information that can be used to optimize manufacturing processes that rely on such impacts. Our findings also suggest that the scaling properties of the roughness of these surfaces can be used to design coatings that are optimized for specific applications.

We present new continuum VLA observations of the nearby Sy 1.5 galaxy NGC
5033, made at 4.9 and 8.4 GHz on 8 April 2003. Combined with VLA archival
observations at 1.4 and 4.9 GHz made on 7 August 1993, 29 August 1999, and 31
October 1999, we sample the galaxy radio emission at scales ranging from the
nuclear regions (<~ 100 pc) to the outer regions of the disk (~ 40 kpc). The
high-resolution VLA images show a core-jet structure for the Sy 1.5 nucleus.
While the core has a moderately steep non-thermal radio spectrum (S_\nu ~
\nu^\alpha; \alpha_{1.5}^{4.9} \approx -0.4), the inner kpc region shows a
steeper spectrum (\alpha_{1.5}^{8.4} \approx -0.9). This latter spectrum is
typical of galaxies where energy losses are high, indicating that the escape
rate of cosmic ray electrons in NGC 5033 is low. The nucleus contributes little
to the total 1.4 GHz radio power of NGC 5033 and, based on the radio to
far-infrared (FIR) relation, it appears that the radio and far-infrared
emission from NGC 5033 are dominated by a starburst that during the last 10 Myr
produced stars at a rate of ~2.8 M_sun/yr, yielding a supernova (type Ib/c and
II) rate of 0.045 #/yr. This supernova rate corresponds to about 1 SN event
every 22 yr. Finally, from our deep 8.4 GHz VLA-D image, we suggest the
existence of a radio spur in NGC 5033, which could have been due to a hot
superbubble formed as a consequence of sequential supernova explosions
occurring during the lifetime of a giant molecular cloud.

Our research aims to investigate the behaviour of superconducting material MgB2 when confined to magnetic field tuned cylinders by means of critical fluctuations. The motivation for this research is to gain a deeper understanding of the behaviour of superconducting materials under confinement, which can have significant implications for the design and construction of superconducting devices.

Our methodology involves preparing MgB2 samples and confining them to magnetic field-tuned cylinders. We will then measure the critical temperature and critical current density, which are critical parameters that describe the behaviour of superconducting materials. We will use these measurements to study the critical fluctuations that occur in the superconducting material under confinement.

By probing the superconductivity of MgB2 in this way, we hope to shed light on the effects of confinement on the superconducting properties of materials, and to gain a better understanding of the underlying physics. This research has the potential to contribute significant insights into the fundamental behaviour of superconducting materials under confinement. Knowing how to manipulate and control superconductors could eventually lead to the development of more efficient and advanced technologies, which could benefit society in many ways.

The observation of neutrino oscillations requires new physics beyond the
standard model (SM). A SM-like gauge theory with p lepton families can be
extended by introducing q heavy right-handed Majorana neutrinos but preserving
its SU(2)_L x U(1)_Y gauge symmetry. The overall neutrino mass matrix M turns
out to be a symmetric (p+q) x (p+q) matrix. Given p>q, the rank of M is in
general equal to 2q, corresponding to 2q non-zero mass eigenvalues. The
existence of (p-q) massless left-handed Majorana neutrinos is an exact
consequence of the model, independent of the usual approximation made in
deriving the Type-I seesaw relation between the effective p x p light Majorana
neutrino mass matrix M_\nu and the q x q heavy Majorana neutrino mass matrix
M_R. In other words, the numbers of massive left- and right-handed neutrinos
are fairly matched. A good example to illustrate this seesaw fair play rule is
the minimal seesaw model with p=3 and q=2, in which one massless neutrino sits
on the unbalanced seesaw.

We report on the development and implementation of a new 60-cm radio survey telescope equipped with a sideband-separating superconductor-insulator-superconductor (SIS) receiver for the 200 GHz band. This project was motivated by the need to study the high-frequency component of the radio spectrum, where many astronomical phenomena occur, such as star formation and the early universe's evolution. The 200 GHz band, in particular, is a window into the molecular universe where probing the gas content of galaxies is possible. The installation of a sensitive and high-resolution telescope, specifically optimized for this band, is a significant contribution to the current state of observational radio astronomy.

The primary problem this project aimed to solve was the lack of suitable telescopes within the 200 GHz band. The previous instruments had either limited sensitivity or poor spectral resolution, thereby making it hard to study faint and small-scale features. In contrast, the new telescope we developed has achieved high sensitivity and excellent spectral resolution, enabling us to study various astronomical objects in unprecedented detail.

We present the initial commissioning results of the telescope, including pointing accuracy, beam profile, and system temperature measurements. The telescope's sensitivity and spectral resolution are qualitatively shown by observations of a nearby molecular cloud and a Galactic star-forming region. The 60-cm Radio Survey Telescope with Sideband-Separating SIS receiver for the 200 GHz Band is now open for regular observing programs and will undoubtedly yield a wealth of science.

Our research aims to compare the large-scale properties of star formation in three different molecular cloud complexes: Serpens, Perseus, and Ophiuchus. We use data from the Bolocam 1.1mm dust continuum surveys from the Spitzer Legacy program "From Molecular Cores to Planet-Forming Disks" (c2d) to construct maps of the dust column density and temperature distributions.

The primary motivation for this work is to investigate the differences and similarities in the physical conditions of the three clouds and how they influence the star formation process. By comparing the observed star formation activity with the predicted rate based on the gas properties, we identify regions of enhanced or suppressed star formation. Additionally, we explore the variations in the dense gas mass distribution, the temperature structure, and the fraction of emission that originates from dust grains.

Our findings show clear differences in the distribution of the dense gas across the three clouds, which suggest variations in star formation efficiency. Moreover, we discover evidence of star formation within the low-density envelopes of the molecular clouds, with half of the protostars discovered lying outside of regions with high column densities. Our results provide insights into the diversity of the star formation process in molecular clouds and may help to improve our understanding of how stars and planets form in the Milky Way and beyond.

We have studied the statistics of giant pulses from the Crab pulsar for the
first time with particular reference to their widths. We have analyzed data
collected during 3.5 hours of observations conducted with the Westerbork
Synthesis Radio Telescope operated in a tied-array mode at a frequency of 1200
MHz. The PuMa pulsar backend provided voltage recording of X and Y linear
polarization states in two conjugate 10 MHz bands. We restricted the time
resolution to 4 microseconds to match the scattering on the interstellar
inhomogeneities. In total about 18000 giant pulses (GP) were detected in full
intensity with a threshold level of 6 sigma. Cumulative probability
distributions (CPD) of giant pulse energies were analyzed for groups of GPs
with different effective widths in the range 4 to 65 microseconds. The CPDs
were found to manifest notable differences for the different GP width groups.
The slope of a power-law fit to the high-energy portion of the CPDs evolves
from -1.7 to -3.2 when going from the shortest to the longest GPs. There are
breaks in the CPD power-law fits indicating flattening at low energies with
indices varying from -1.0 to -1.9 for the short and long GPs respectively. The
GPs with a stronger peak flux density were found to be of shorter duration. We
compare our results with previously published data and discuss the importance
of these peculiarities in the statistical properties of GPs for the heoretical
understanding of the emission mechanism responsible for GP generation.

We present ACS/HST coronagraphic observations of HD 100546, a B9.5 star, 103
pc away from the sun, taken in the F435W, F606W, and F814W bands. Scattered
light is detected up to 14'' from the star. The observations are consistent
with the presence of an extended flattened nebula with the same inclination as
the inner disk. The well-known ``spiral arms'' are clearly observed and they
trail the rotating disk material. Weaker arms never before reported are also
seen. The inter-arm space becomes brighter, but the structures become more
neutral in color at longer wavelengths, which is not consistent with models
that assume that they are due to the effects of a warped disk. Along the major
disk axis, the colors of the scattered-light relative to the star are \Delta
(F435W-F606W) ~ 0.0--0.2 mags and \Delta (F435W-F814W)~0.5--1 mags. To explain
these colors, we explore the role of asymmetric scattering, reddening, and
large minimum sizes on ISM-like grains. We conclude each of these hypotheses by
itself cannot explain the colors. The disk colors are similar to those derived
for Kuiper Belt objects, suggesting that the same processes responsible for
their colors may be at work here. We argue that we are observing only the
geometrically thick, optically thin envelope of the disk, while the optically
thick disk responsible for the far-IR emission is undetected. The observed
spiral arms are then structures on this envelope. The colors indicate that the
extended nebulosity is not a remnant of the infalling envelope but reprocessed
disk material.

In this note, we consider possible constraints from $t \bar t$ production on
the gauge bosons of theories with an extended strong interaction sector such as
axigluons or flavour universal colorons. Such constraints are found to be
competitive with those obtained from the dijet data. The current $t \bar t$
data from the Tevatron rule out axigluon masses ($m_A$) up to 900 GeV and 850
GeV at 2 $\sigma$ and 4 $\sigma$ levels respectively. For the case of flavour
universal colorons the data rule out a mass ($m_C$) below 800 GeV (780 GeV) at
the $2 (4) \sigma $ level and also the mass range between 900 GeV to 2.1 TeV at
2 $\sigma$ level, for $\cot \xi = 1$, where $\xi$ is the mixing angle. For
$\cot \xi =2$ on the other hand, the excluded range is $m_C \lsim 950 (920)$
GeV and $m_C \gsim 1.02 (1.15 \lsim m_C \lsim 1.8)$ TeV at $2 \sigma$ ($4
\sigma $) level. We point out that for higher axigluon/coloron masses, even for
the dijet channel, the limits on the coloron mass, for $\cot \xi = 1$, may be
different than those for the axigluon. We also compute the expected
forward-backward asymmetry for the case of the axigluons which would allow it
to be discriminated against the SM as also the colorons. We further find that
at the LHC, the signal should be visible in the $t \bar t$ invariant mass
spectrum for a wide range of axigluon and coloron masses that are still
allowed. We point out how top polarisation may be used to further discriminate
the axigluon and coloron case from the SM as well as from each other.

Using the microscopic theory of the conserved spin current [Phys. Rev. Lett.
\textbf{96}, 076604 (2006)], we investigate the spin Hall effect in the two
dimensional electron gas system with a perpendicular magnetic field. The spin
Hall conductance $\sigma_{\mu\nu}^{s}$ as a response to the electric field
consists of two parts, i.e., the conventional part $\sigma_{\mu\nu}^{s0}$ and
the spin torque dipole correction $\sigma_{\mu\nu}^{s\tau}$. It is shown that
the spin-orbit coupling competes with Zeeman splitting by introducing
additional degeneracies between different Landau levels at certain values of
magnetic field. These degeneracies, if occurring at the Fermi level, turn to
give rise to resonances in both $\sigma_{\mu\nu}^{s0}$ and $\sigma_{\mu\nu
}^{s\tau}$ in spin Hall conductance. Remarkably, both of these two components
have the same sign in the wide range of variation in the magnetic field, which
result in an overall enhancement of the total spin Hall current. In particular,
the magnitude of $\sigma_{\mu\nu}^{s\tau}$ is much larger than that of
$\sigma_{\mu\nu}^{s0}$ around the resonance.

Interest in development of brain prostheses, which might be proposed to
recover mental functions lost due to neuron-degenerative disease or trauma,
requires new methods in molecular engineering and nanotechnology to build
artificial brain tissues. We develop a Dynamic Core model to analyze complexity
of damaged biological neural network as well as transition and recovery of the
system functionality due to changes in the system environment. We provide a
method to model complexity of physical systems which might be proposed as an
artificial tissue or prosthesis. Delocalization of Dynamic Core model is
developed to analyze migration of mental functions in dynamic bio-systems which
undergo architecture transition induced by trauma. Term Dynamic Core is used to
define a set of causally related functions and Delocalization is used to
describe the process of migration. Information geometry and topological
formalisms are proposed to analyze information processes. A holographic model
is proposed to construct dynamic environment with self-poetic Dynamic Core
which preserve functional properties under transition from one host to another.
We found statistical constraints for complex systems which conserve a Dynamic
Core under environment transition. Also we suggest those constraints might
provide recommendations for nanotechnologies and tissue engineering used in
development of an artificial brain tissue.

In this article, we explore the r-process of stellar nucleosynthesis from both an astrophysics and nuclear physics perspective. The r-process is a crucial mechanism for the creation of heavy elements, but its exact details remain a mystery. We begin by discussing the key theoretical concepts and models that have been developed to understand the r-process, including neutron star mergers and supernovae. 

We then delve into the experimental techniques used in nuclear physics to study the r-process, including beta-decay and high-energy proton scattering. The results of these experiments have yielded valuable insights into the properties of neutron-rich nuclei and their role in the r-process. 

Moving forward, we outline some of the remaining mysteries surrounding the r-process, such as the origin of short-lived radioactive isotopes observed in meteorites and the precise conditions necessary for r-process nucleosynthesis. We also discuss future directions for research in this field, including the use of next-generation nuclear facilities and improved detection methods. 

Overall, this article highlights the significant progress that has been made in understanding the r-process, as well as the intriguing questions that remain unanswered. Our hope is that by continuing to study the r-process, we can gain a deeper understanding of the origins of heavy elements and the evolution of the universe itself.

In this work, we investigate the structure and dynamics of the Sun's open magnetic field using observations obtained over a 12-year period. Our methodology involves analyzing data from the European Space Agency's Solar and Heliospheric Observatory (SOHO) mission, specifically the Michelson Doppler Imager and the Extreme-ultraviolet Imaging Telescope. 

We find that the open magnetic field of the Sun's corona exhibits dynamic behavior, characterized by constant changes in its orientation and expansion. We observe a strong connection between the dynamics of the magnetic field and the Sun's activity cycle, which undergoes fluctuations that can span several years. 

Our studies reveal that the Sun's open magnetic field displays an asymmetric behavior, with the south pole experiencing stronger activity than the north. Moreover, we uncover a correlation between the asymmetry of open magnetic flux, the polarity of the Sun's polar magnetic fields, and their temporal evolution. 

Our research is motivated by the need to understand the dynamics of the Sun's magnetic field, a critical component of space weather that affects Earth and other planets in the solar system. By studying the structure and dynamics of the Sun's open magnetic field, we advance our knowledge of the processes that drive space weather and the potential impacts on our technological infrastructure. Overall, our findings provide new insights into the complex behavior of the Sun's magnetic field and contribute to a better understanding of the solar cycle.

Coherence and phase synchronization between time series corresponding to
different spatial locations are usually interpreted as indicators of the
connectivity between locations. In neurophysiology, time series of electric
neuronal activity are essential for studying brain interconnectivity. Such
signals can either be invasively measured from depth electrodes, or computed
from very high time resolution, non-invasive, extracranial recordings of scalp
electric potential differences (EEG: electroencephalogram) and magnetic fields
(MEG: magnetoencephalogram) by means of a tomography such as sLORETA
(standardized low resolution brain electromagnetic tomography). There are two
problems in this case. First, in the usual situation of unknown cortical
geometry, the estimated signal at each brain location is a vector with three
components (i.e. a current density vector), which means that coherence and
phase synchronization must be generalized to pairs of multivariate time series.
Second, the inherent low spatial resolution of the EEG/MEG tomography
introduces artificially high zero-lag coherence and phase synchronization. In
this report, solutions to both problems are presented. Two additional
generalizations are briefly mentioned: (1) conditional coherence and phase
synchronization; and (2) non-stationary time-frequency analysis. Finally, a
non-parametric randomization method for connectivity significance testing is
outlined. The new connectivity measures proposed here can be applied to pairs
of univariate EEG/MEG signals, as is traditional in the published literature.
However, these calculations cannot be interpreted as connectivity, since it is
in general incorrect to associate an extracranial electrode or sensor to the
underlying cortex.

In a data warehousing process, the data preparation phase is crucial.
Mastering this phase allows substantial gains in terms of time and performance
when performing a multidimensional analysis or using data mining algorithms.
Furthermore, a data warehouse can require external data. The web is a prevalent
data source in this context, but the data broadcasted on this medium are very
heterogeneous. We propose in this paper a UML conceptual model for a complex
object representing a superclass of any useful data source (databases, plain
texts, HTML and XML documents, images, sounds, video clips...). The translation
into a logical model is achieved with XML, which helps integrating all these
diverse, heterogeneous data into a unified format, and whose schema definition
provides first-rate metadata in our data warehousing context. Moreover, we
benefit from XML's flexibility, extensibility and from the richness of the
semi-structured data model, but we are still able to later map XML documents
into a database if more structuring is needed.

In this research work, we study the scaling laws of strategic behavior and size heterogeneity in agent dynamics. In many complex systems, such as markets, social networks, and ecosystems, agents display strategic behavior to optimize their payoffs. Moreover, these systems often exhibit size heterogeneity, i.e., agents are characterized by different levels of resources, populations, and influence. Despite their importance, the joint effects of strategic behavior and size heterogeneity on system dynamics remain poorly understood. 

To address these issues, we develop a general framework based on scaling laws, which capture the relationships between system-level properties and agent-level features. We apply this framework to a wide range of agent-based models, including prisoner's dilemma games, herding behavior, and epidemiological spreading. Our results show that strategic behavior and size heterogeneity can have profound and often counterintuitive effects on system behavior, including phase transitions, scaling regimes, and emergent patterns. Moreover, we find that our framework is robust to variations in model assumptions and can provide insights into the underlying mechanisms of diverse complex systems. 

Overall, this research work contributes to a better understanding of the interplay between strategic behavior and size heterogeneity in complex systems and may have implications for various fields, such as economics, sociology, ecology, and public health.

We present the results of a uniform analysis of the soft X-ray spectra of
fifteen type I AGN observed with the high resolution X-ray gratings on board
\emph{Chandra}. We found that ten of the fifteen AGN exhibit signatures of an
intrinsic ionized absorber. The absorbers are photoionized and outflowing, with
velocities in the range $\sim 10^{1}-10^{3}$ km $\rm{s}^{-1}$. The column
density of the warm absorbing gas is $\sim 10^{20-23} \rm{cm}^{-2}$. Nine of
the ten AGN exhibiting warm absorption are best--fit by multiple ionization
components and three of the ten AGN \emph{require} multiple kinematic
components. The warm absorbing gas in our AGN sample has a wide range of
ionization parameter, spanning roughly four orders of magnitude ($\xi \sim
10^{0-4}$ ergs cm $\rm{s}^{-1}$) in total, and often spanning three orders of
magnitude in the same gas. Warm absorber components with ionization parameter
$\xi<10$ generate an unresolved transition array due to Fe in seven of the ten
AGN exhibiting warm absorption. These low ionization state absorbers may also
carry away the largest mass outflows from the AGN. The mass outflow rate
depends critically on the volume filling factor of the gas, which cannot yet be
directly measured. However, upper limits on the mass outflow ratesfor filling
factors of unity can be much greater than the expected accretion rate onto the
central supermassive black hole and filling factors as small as 1% can give
outflow rates comparable to the accretion rate. There appears to be a gap in
the outflow velocities in our sample between $\sim 300-500$ km $\rm{s}^{-1}$,
the origin of which is not clear. The outflow components with velocities below
this gap tend to be associated with lower column densities than those with
velocities above the gap.

In this work, we present a new chaotic attractor in a basic multi-strain epidemiological model, which considers temporary cross-immunity. The aim of this study is to investigate the dynamics of this model and explore the possible outcomes of disease prevalence in a population with multiple strains.

We show that, depending on the parameter values, the system exhibits a variety of different dynamic behaviors, including periodic oscillations, steady states, and most importantly, chaotic behavior. This chaotic attractor arises due to the interplay of the various strains and the temporary cross-immunity between them. We analyze the impact of different parameters on the stability and coexistence of the strains in the population.

The results of our simulations suggest that the presence of chaos may lead to unpredictable outbreaks of one or more strains within the population. This study highlights the potential risk of chaotic behavior in epidemiological models and emphasizes the importance of predicting and preventing large-scale disease outbreaks.

The motivation for this research is to improve our understanding of infectious disease dynamics, which is crucial for developing effective public health policies and controlling infectious disease outbreaks. The findings of this study can inform future research into the dynamics of multispecies epidemiological models and shed light on the mechanisms underlying the emergence and spread of infectious diseases.

We present our findings on the line and continuum variability analysis of two intermediate-redshift, high-luminosity quasars, PG 0953+414 and PG 2112+059, which were monitored over a period of six years using multi-epoch spectroscopy. The motivation for our research stemmed from the significance of understanding the physical processes that govern the evolution and behavior of quasars, which are some of the most luminous and extreme objects in the universe. By analyzing the variability of both the broad emission lines and underlying continuum emission, we sought to investigate the accretion disk dynamics around the central black hole of these quasars. 

Our methodology involved a careful selection of spectral lines to analyze and the construction of a model to remove host galaxy contamination from the spectra. We employed various statistical tools to study the variability properties of the quasars, including cross-correlation analysis, power spectral density estimation, and structure function analysis. Our analysis revealed that both quasars exhibit significant variability in both the continuum and broad emission lines on various timescales. Using the velocity-resolved emission line profiles, we were able to identify kinematic signatures of the accretion disk, including both inflowing and outflowing components. Our results highlight the importance of long-term monitoring campaigns for studying the variability of quasars and provide insight into the physical mechanisms that drive their behavior.

In this work, we investigate the behavior of the contour of a random splitting tree, focusing on its increments. The main question we address is whether the contour can be characterized as a L\'evy process. 

The splitting tree model comprises a random binary tree that is built from the splitting of line segments. The contour can be interpreted as a sequence of excursions above and below the spine of the tree. By considering the increments of these excursions, we obtain a sequence of random variables that can be analyzed using the theory of L\'evy processes. 

Our motivation for this research comes from the fundamental role that L\'evy processes play in probability theory and their relevance in diverse fields such as finance, physics, and biology. Moreover, the study of the contour of splitting trees has applications in population genetics and phylogenetics. 

Our main result is to prove that the contour of splitting trees is indeed a L\'evy process. We establish this by showing that its characteristic exponent satisfies the properties of a L\'evy exponent. Furthermore, we provide explicit expressions for the corresponding L\'evy-Khintchine formula. Overall, our findings lead to a better understanding of the contour of splitting trees and open new avenues for its applications in probability theory and related fields.

In our work titled "Stellar Evolution in NGC 6791: Mass Loss on the Red Giant Branch and the Formation of Low Mass White Dwarfs," we investigated the mass loss of stars on the red giant branch (RGB) and the formation of low mass white dwarfs (WDs) in NGC 6791. This open cluster is ideal for studying stellar evolution due to its advanced age of ~8 Gyr and high metallicity.

We characterized the mass loss rates of 18 RGB stars in NGC 6791 through spectral analysis of molecular bands. We found that the mass loss rates were significantly higher than those predicted by theoretical models. Additionally, we identified eight low mass white dwarfs (WDs) in the cluster. Based on our analysis, we suggest that these WDs were formed through direct accretion of RGB winds onto low mass companion stars. This contradicts previous studies that suggested mass transfer in binary systems as the primary mechanism for the formation of low mass WDs.

Our findings have important implications for our understanding of stellar evolution and the formation of WDs in open clusters. Our methodology, which combined spectroscopic analysis and theoretical modeling, can be applied to other clusters to improve our understanding of mass loss and WD formation mechanisms.

In this work, we investigate the formation of cosmic strings by flux trapping in a superconducting phase transition. The motivation for this research is twofold: first, cosmic strings are hypothetical one-dimensional topological defects that are predicted by theoretical models of the early universe, and their observation could provide valuable insights into the physics of the early universe. Second, flux trapping is a well-known phenomenon in superconductors, and its potential role in the formation of cosmic strings has not been fully explored.

Our key idea is that during a superconducting phase transition, quantized magnetic flux tubes can become trapped in the superconducting domain walls, which can subsequently collapse into cosmic strings. To investigate this possibility, we numerically simulate the dynamics of a scalar field that undergoes a Kibble-Zurek phase transition in the presence of a magnetic field. Our results show that the magnetic field can indeed lead to the formation of cosmic strings by trapping flux tubes in the domain walls, and that the resulting cosmic strings have the characteristic properties of topological defects.

Overall, our work provides a new mechanism for the formation of cosmic strings and demonstrates the potential role of flux trapping in the early universe. Our methodology combines numerical simulations of the scalar field dynamics with analytical calculations of the associated magnetic fluxes, which we use to establish the conditions for cosmic string formation. The results obtained in this work provide a basis for further investigations of the role of flux trapping in the formation of cosmic strings, and could have important implications for our understanding of the early universe.

(abridged) The accretion disk in AGN is expected to produce strong outflows,
in particular a UV-line driven wind. Despite providing a good fit to the data,
current spectral models of the X-ray spectrum of AGN observed through an
accretion disk wind are ad-hoc in their treatment of the properties of the wind
material. In order to address these limitations we adopt a numerical
computation method that links a series of radiative transfer calculations,
incorporating the effect of a global velocity field in a self-consistent manner
(XSCORT). We present a series of example spectra from the XSCORT code that
allow us to examine the shape of AGN X-ray spectra seen through a wind, for a
range of velocity and density distributions, total column densities and initial
ionization parameters. These detailed spectral models clearly show considerable
complexity and structure that is strongly affected by all these factors. The
presence of sharp features in the XSCORT spectra contrasts strongly with both
the previous models and with the smooth nature of the observed X-ray spectra of
AGN with soft X-ray excesses, demonstrating that accretion disk winds are
unlikely to be the origin of this mysterious spectral feature. The most
significant parameter affecting the presence of the sharp features in the
models is the terminal velocity of the wind. Increasing the terminal velocity
of the absorbing material to ~c, and hence dramatically increasing the velocity
dispersion across the wind, could potentially remove these features resulting
in a spectrum similar to the previous models. Such a fast moving outflow cannot
be associated with a radiatively driven accretion disk wind, however the
presence of a highly relativistic jet may provide an origin for such material.

We present Lie group integrators for nonlinear stochastic differential
equations with non-commutative vector fields whose solution evolves on a smooth
finite dimensional manifold. Given a Lie group action that generates transport
along the manifold, we pull back the stochastic flow on the manifold to the Lie
group via the action, and subsequently pull back the flow to the corresponding
Lie algebra via the exponential map. We construct an approximation to the
stochastic flow in the Lie algebra via closed operations and then push back to
the Lie group and then to the manifold, thus ensuring our approximation lies in
the manifold. We call such schemes stochastic Munthe-Kaas methods after their
deterministic counterparts. We also present stochastic Lie group integration
schemes based on Castell--Gaines methods. These involve using an underlying
ordinary differential integrator to approximate the flow generated by a
truncated stochastic exponential Lie series. They become stochastic Lie group
integrator schemes if we use Munthe-Kaas methods as the underlying ordinary
differential integrator. Further, we show that some Castell--Gaines methods are
uniformly more accurate than the corresponding stochastic Taylor schemes.
Lastly we demonstrate our methods by simulating the dynamics of a free rigid
body such as a satellite and an autonomous underwater vehicle both perturbed by
two independent multiplicative stochastic noise processes.

In this study, we investigate the topology of neutral atomic hydrogen (HI) in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) through the use of position-position-velocity (PPV) data cubes. The motivation behind our research is the importance of understanding the three-dimensional structure of these gas clouds as they are key to the formation of stars and galaxies. Our work attempts to address the problem of characterizing the topology of the HI gas in the LMC, which is crucial for providing a deeper understanding of the physical processes involved in star formation. 

To achieve this objective, we employ computational techniques such as contouring, extracting filamentary structures, and topology-based analysis. Specifically, we utilize the Morse-Smale complex, a topological method for analyzing scalar and vector fields, to identify and classify critical points, ridges, and holes. Our analysis reveals a rich and complex topology of the HI gas in the LMC, consisting of filaments, loops, and voids, which interact in intricate ways. 

In conclusion, this work provides valuable insights into the structure and morphology of the HI gas in the LMC and helps to shed light on the physical mechanisms involved in star formation. Our methodology and results hold significant implications for future studies of the LMC and other galaxies, as well as the broader field of astrophysics.

In this study, we investigate the generic cosmological evolution for a tachyon and a scalar field, which we refer to as interacting tachyon. The research aims to solve the problem of understanding the dynamics of interacting tachyons in the context of cosmology. The motivation for this study derives from the cosmological implications of tachyons as a possible explanation for the accelerated expansion of the universe.

Through a detailed analysis of the equations of motion, we demonstrate that the interacting tachyon exhibits specific features in its evolution, depending on the initial conditions. Specifically, we find that the evolution of the scalar field is driven by the tachyon behavior, whereas the tachyon field leads to the accelerated expansion of the universe.

Our analysis revealed that the initial conditions of the interacting tachyon significantly affect its evolution, including the range of values for the tachyon speed. We also discuss the implications of our findings for the emergence of dark energy, as tachyons can naturally serve as an origin for it.

Overall, this work sheds light on the cosmological evolution of the interacting tachyon and has the potential to open new avenues for research into the accelerating universe's nature.

Recent numerical simulations of binary black hole mergers show the
possibility of producing very large recoil velocities (> 3000 km/s). Kicks of
this magnitude should be sufficient to eject the final black hole from
virtually any galactic potential. This result has been seen as a potential
contradiction with observations of supermassive black holes residing in the
centers of most galaxies in the local universe. Using an extremely simplified
merger tree model, we show that, even in the limit of very large ejection
probability, after a small number of merger generations there should still be
an appreciable fraction (>50%) of galaxies with supermassive black holes today.
We go on to argue that the inclusion of more realistic physics ingredients in
the merger model should systematically increase this retention fraction,
helping to resolve a potential conflict between theory and observation. Lastly,
we develop a more realistic Monte Carlo model to confirm the qualitative
arguments and estimate occupation fractions as a function of the central
galactic velocity dispersion.

In this research, we investigate the Supernova channel of Super-Asymptotic Giant Branch (SAGB) stars, specifically focusing on their evolutionary models and what prompts their supernova explosion. Our study aims to identify the conditions under which Super-AGB stars undergo explosive nucleosynthesis in their cores, leading to a supernova phenomenon. 

The motivation for this research stems from the previously overlooked role that Super-AGB stars might play as potential progenitors for Type Ia supernovae. Theories in the field have long supposed that white dwarfs were the most relevant candidates as supernova progenitors, but recent studies suggest that Super-AGB stars may also be viable candidates. 

Our results show that Super-AGB stars with extreme helium shell burning, as well as the most massive single stars born, experience explosive oxygen burning in their cores. This leads to an oxygen-depleted core with leftover fuel that ignites, ultimately initiating a supernova. We also highlight the importance of using modern nuclear data in calculating Super-AGB models to accurately depict core properties and therefore inform our understanding of supernova explosions. 

Overall, our research underscores the relevance of Super-AGB stars as possible contributors to the supernova phenomenon. While white dwarfs remain a prevalent pathway for supernova explosions, our research suggests there may be other routes worthy of future exploration and research.

The approach exposed in the recent paper (A. Shebeko, P. Papakonstantinou, E.
Mavrommatis, Eur. Phys. J. A 27, 143 (2006)) has been applied in studying
center-of-mass motion effects on the nucleon density and momentum distributions
in nuclei. We are focused upon effects due to the center-of-mass and
short-range nucleon correlations embedded in translationally invariant
ground-state wavefunctions. The latter are constructed in the so-called fixed
center-of-mass approximation, starting with a Slater determinant wave function
modified by some correlator (e.g., after Jastrow or Villars). It is shown how
one can simplify evaluation of the corresponding expectation values that
determine the distributions. The analytic expressions derived here involve the
own "Tassie-Barker" factors for each distribution. As an illustration,
numerical calculations have been carried out for the nucleus ^{4}He with the
Slater determinant to describe the nucleon (1s)^4 configuration composed of
single-particle orbitals which differ from harmonic oscillator ones at small
distances. Such orbitals simulate somewhat short-range repulsion between
nucleons. Special attention is paid to a simultaneous shrinking of the
center--of--mass corrected density and momentum distributions compared to the
purely (1s)^4 shell nontranslationally invariant ones.

An integral PBW-basis of type $A_1^{(1)}$ has been constructed by Zhang [Z]
and Chen [C] using the Auslander-Reiten quiver of the Kronecker quiver. We
associate a geometric order to elements in this basis following an idea of
Lusztig [L1] in the case of finite type. This leads to an algebraic realization
of a bar-invariant basis of $\uq2$. For any affine symmetric type, we obtain an
integral PBW-basis of the generic composition algebra, by using an algebraic
construction of the integral basis for a tube in [DDX], an embedding of the
module category of the Kronecker quiver into the module category of the tame
quiver, and a list of the root vectors of indecomposable modules according to
the preprojective, regular, and preinjective components of the Auslander-Reiten
quiver of the tame quiver. When the basis elements are ordered to be compatible
with the geometric order given by the dimensions of the orbit varieties and the
extension varieties, we can show that the transition matrix between the
PBW-basis and a monomial basis is triangular with diagonal entries equal to 1.
Therefore we obtain a bar-invariant basis. By a orthogonalization for the
PBW-basis with the inner product, we finally give an algebraic way to realize
the canonical bases of the quantized enveloping algebras of all symmetric
affine Kac-Moody Lie algebras.

We present a study on the possible progenitors of long gamma-ray bursts (GRBs). These enigmatic phenomena are thought to be associated with the collapse of massive stars and the formation of black holes. However, the exact mechanism of long GRB formation remains elusive, as does the identification of the underlying stellar progenitor. In this work, we investigate the potential link between long GRBs and the mass-loss history of their progenitors.

Our study involves a detailed analysis of the properties of the host galaxies of a sample of long GRBs, including their star-forming rates and metallicity. We also consider the effects of binary interactions on the evolution of massive stars and their ultimate fate. Our methodology consists of computing a grid of stellar evolutionary models that take into account different mass-loss prescriptions and binary evolution scenarios.

Our results reveal that low-metallicity and rapidly rotating stars are the most likely progenitors of long GRBs. In addition, binary interactions can significantly enhance the mass-loss rates of massive stars, thereby increasing the likelihood of GRB formation. These findings provide important insights into the origins of these powerful cosmic explosions and shed light on the crucial role of massive stars in shaping the universe as we know it.

The experimental literature on the motion of mesoscopic probe particles
through polymer solutions is systematically reviewed. The primary focus is the
study of diffusive motion of small probe particles. Comparison is made with
measurements of solution viscosities. A coherent description was obtained,
namely that the probe diffusion coefficient generally depends on polymer
concentration as D_p = D_0 exp(-alpha c^nu). One finds that alpha depends on
polymer molecular weights as alpha ~ M^0.8, and \nu appears to have large-M and
small-M values with a crossover linking them. The probe diffusion coefficient
does not simply track the solution viscosity; instead, D_p eta typically
increases markedly with increasing polymer concentration and molecular weight.
In some systems, e.g., hydroxypropylcellulose:water, the observed probe spectra
are bi- or tri-modal. Extended analysis of the full probe phenomenology implies
that hydroxypropylcellulose solutions are characterized by a single,
concentration-independent, length scale that is approximately the size of a
polymer coil. In a very few systems, one sees re-entrant or
low-concentration-plateau behaviors of uncertain interpretation; from their
rarity, these behaviors are reasonably interpreted as corresponding to specific
chemical effects. True microrheological studies examining the motion of
mesoscopic particles under the influence of a known external force are also
examined. Viscosity from true microrheological measurements is in many cases
substantially smaller than the viscosity measured with a macroscopic
instrument.

In this study, we investigate the formation and survival of dust in supernova ejecta, motivated by the need to better understand the role of supernovae in the evolution of galaxies and the formation of planets. Our research employs a multi-wavelength observational approach, using infrared, radio, and optical observations to trace the presence and evolution of dust in supernova remnants. We also use theoretical models to simulate the physical processes involved in dust formation and destruction.

One key idea of our work is that supernova explosions are a major source of dust in the universe, but the precise mechanisms and conditions for dust formation and survival in supernova ejecta are not well understood. Our approach is designed to provide insights into these processes by analyzing the properties and evolution of dust in different types of supernova remnants.

Our methodology involves observing and analyzing the spectra and morphology of supernova remnants at various wavelengths, from the radio to the infrared, and modeling the emission and absorption of dust in these systems using radiative transfer codes. We also compare our observations to theoretical models of supernova explosions and dust formation to test and refine our understanding of the physics involved.

The problem we aim to solve is to improve our understanding of how dust forms and evolves in supernova remnants, and how this process contributes to the overall enrichment of galaxies with heavy elements and the formation of planets. Our research has important implications for the study of galaxy formation and evolution, as well as for the interpretation of astronomical observations of supernovae and their remnants.

We present a study on the use of the CIV 1549 line as an Eigenvector 1 (EV1) parameter for Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN). The CIV 1549 line is a well-known emission line in AGN that is commonly used to derive physical and kinematic properties of the nuclear region. In this work, we analyze a sample of 74 type 1 AGN with available spectroscopic data to investigate the relationship between the CIV 1549 line and the EV1 parameter. Our aim is to determine whether the CIV 1549 line can be used as a reliable measure of AGN continuum luminosity and Black Hole mass.

We find a strong correlation between the CIV 1549 line and the EV1 parameter, implying that the CIV 1549 line is a robust indicator of the AGN physical properties. Furthermore, we use our results to revisit the relationship between AGN luminosity and Black Hole mass, finding that the CIV 1549 line is capable of providing accurate estimates for both parameters.

Our research motivates future studies on the utilization of the CIV 1549 line as a probe for the physical processes governing AGN emission and evolution. Our findings have implications for the development of AGN models and the use of AGN as probes of the large-scale structure and evolution of the Universe.

In this work, we investigate the phase behavior of a fluid with competing attractive and repulsive interactions. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that fluids with complex interactions can exhibit a wide range of phase behavior, including unexpected phase transitions and non-equilibrium phenomena. Understanding such behavior is critical for many industrial, biological, and environmental processes, including the design of new materials, drug delivery, and water treatment.

Through molecular simulations and theory, we find that the interplay of repulsive and attractive interactions leads to the formation of multiple fluid phases, including gas-like, liquid-like, and gel-like phases. Interestingly, we observe that the nature and stability of these phases depend critically on the relative strength and range of the attractive and repulsive potentials. In particular, we find that increasing the range of either interaction leads to more stable liquid and gel phases.

Our results shed new light on the complex phase behavior of fluids with competing interactions and have important implications for the design of new materials and industrial processes. We expect that our findings will stimulate further experimental and theoretical studies in this field and help pave the way for novel applications of soft matter physics.

We derive constraints that must be satisfied by the sources of ~10^{15} to
~10^{18} eV cosmic rays, under the assumption that the sources are Galactic. We
show that while these constraints are not satisfied by ordinary supernovae
(SNe), which are believed to be the sources of <10^{15} eV cosmic rays, they
may be satisfied by the recently discovered class of trans-relativistic
supernovae (TRSNe), which were observed in association with gamma-ray bursts.
We define TRSNe as SNe that deposit a large fraction, f_R>10^{-2}, of their
kinetic energy in mildly relativistic, \gamma\beta>1, ejecta. The high velocity
ejecta enable particle acceleration to ~10^{18} eV, and the large value of f_R
(compared to f_R~10^{-7} for ordinary SNe) ensures that if TRSNe produce the
observed ~10^{18} eV cosmic ray flux, they do not overproduce the flux at lower
energies. This, combined with the estimated rate and energy production of
TRSNe, imply that Galactic TRSNe may be the sources of cosmic rays with
energies up to ~10^{18}eV .

In this work, we investigate the relationship between the bounded pushdown dimension and the Lempel Ziv information density of formal languages. Our objective is to establish a connection between computability theory and information theory by exploring the interplay between these two measures of complexity. We start by introducing the bounded pushdown dimension and Lempel Ziv information density and provide a comprehensive overview of existing research in these areas. 

We then present our results, which show that there exists a direct correlation between the bounded pushdown dimension and Lempel Ziv information density for regular languages, but not for context-free or context-sensitive languages. We further investigate this relationship by developing a methodology that allows us to obtain the Lempel Ziv information density of languages with non-regular behavior. Our findings provide new insights into the complexity of formal languages and have implications for the design and analysis of algorithms for processing natural language data. 

Our approach is grounded in formal definitions and rigorous analysis, making use of mathematical tools from both computability theory and information theory. We also provide experimental evidence to support our theoretical claims. Overall, our work aims to address an important problem in the study of formal languages and contributes to the ongoing dialogue between computability theory and information theory.

The specific band structure of graphene, with its unique valley structure and
Dirac neutrality point separating hole states from electron states has led to
the observation of new electronic transport phenomena such as anomalously
quantized Hall effects, absence of weak localization and the existence of a
minimum conductivity. In addition to dissipative transport also supercurrent
transport has already been observed. It has also been suggested that graphene
might be a promising material for spintronics and related applications, such as
the realization of spin qubits, due to the low intrinsic spin orbit
interaction, as well as the low hyperfine interaction of the electron spins
with the carbon nuclei. As a first step in the direction of graphene
spintronics and spin qubits we report the observation of spin transport, as
well as Larmor spin precession over micrometer long distances using single
graphene layer based field effect transistors. The non-local spin valve
geometry was used, employing four terminal contact geometries with
ferromagnetic cobalt electrodes, which make contact to the graphene sheet
through a thin oxide layer. We observe clear bipolar (changing from positive to
negative sign) spin signals which reflect the magnetization direction of all 4
electrodes, indicating that spin coherence extends underneath all 4 contacts.
No significant changes in the spin signals occur between 4.2K, 77K and room
temperature. From Hanle type spin precession measurements we extract a spin
relaxation length between 1.5 and 2 micron at room temperature, only weakly
dependent on charge density, which is varied from n~0 at the Dirac neutrality
point to n = 3.6 10^16/m^2. The spin polarization of the ferromagnetic contacts
is calculated from the measurements to be around 10%.

The temporal evolution of step-edge fluctuations under electromigration
conditions is analysed using a continuum Langevin model. If the
electromigration driving force acts in the step up/down direction, and
step-edge diffusion is the dominant mass-transport mechanism, we find that
significant deviations from the usual $t^{1/4}$ scaling of the terrace-width
correlation function occurs for a critical time $\tau$ which is dependent upon
the three energy scales in the problem: $k_{B}T$, the step stiffness, $\gamma$,
and the bias associated with adatom hopping under the influence of an
electromigration force, $\pm \Delta U$. For ($t < \tau$), the correlation
function evolves as a superposition of $t^{1/4}$ and $t^{3/4}$ power laws. For
$t \ge \tau$ a closed form expression can be derived. This behavior is
confirmed by a Monte-Carlo simulation using a discrete model of the step
dynamics. It is proposed that the magnitude of the electromigration force
acting upon an atom at a step-edge can by estimated by a careful analysis of
the statistical properties of step-edge fluctuations on the appropriate
time-scale.
