Our work contributes to the growing body of research that aims to establish the theoretical framework for understanding the early universe and its evolution.

In this paper, we present a class of low-density parity-check (LDPC) erasure distributions with closed-form threshold expressions. Our work is motivated by the need to construct efficient and reliable error-correcting codes for communication systems. The LDPC codes are widely used in practical systems due to their excellent error-correction performance and low decoding complexity. However, the optimal design of LDPC codes with erasure correction capability remains a challenging problem. 

Our approach is to construct a family of erasure distributions that have a closed-form expression for their threshold. We use the differential equation method to derive the threshold expressions for the proposed erasure distributions. Specifically, we first solve a differential equation that characterizes the evolution of the weight distribution of the erasure-correcting code ensemble under the erasure channel. Next, we use the threshold theorem to obtain the explicit form of the threshold expression. 

Our results show that the proposed LDPC erasure distributions achieve performance gains over existing distributions in the literature. Moreover, the closed-form threshold expressions enable efficient code designs and decoding algorithms. Overall, our work provides a useful tool for the design of high-performance LDPC codes with erasure correction capability in practical systems.

Metal and molecule cooling plays a crucial role in simulations of structure formation. Our research is motivated by the need to better understand the mechanism behind the cooling of metal and molecules in simulations of galaxy formation. This knowledge would allow us to improve the accuracy of such simulations and to test theories of galaxy evolution. 

Our work attempts to solve the problem of accurately simulating metal and molecule cooling in large-scale galaxy simulations. Prior simulations have not accurately modeled the cooling process, leading to discrepancies between simulations and observations. 

We construct a new model of cooling that incorporates the effects of metal and molecule cooling through the use of UV radiation, chemical reactions, and atomic and molecular gas transitions. Using this model, we conduct simulations of galaxy formation and compare our results to observational data. We find that our model accurately reproduces the metal and molecule content of galaxies, as well as their temperatures and densities. 

Overall, our research provides a better understanding of the cooling mechanism in simulations of galaxy formation and allows for more accurate and reliable simulations in the future.

The multi-Higgs models having spontaneous CP violation (SPCV) and natural
flavor conservation (NFC) lead to a real CKM matrix $V$ contradicting current
evidence in favour of a complex $V$. This contradiction can be removed by using
a generalized $\mu$-$\tau$ (called 23) symmetry in place of the discrete
symmetry conventionally used to obtain NFC. If 23 symmetry is exact then the
Higgs induced flavour changing neutral currents (FCNC) vanish as in case of
NFC. 23 breaking introduces SPCV, a phase in $V$ and suppressed FCNC among
quarks. The FCNC couplings $F_{ij}^{d,u}$ between $i$ and $j$ generations show
a hierarchy $|F_{12}^{d,u}|<|F_{13}^{d,u}|<|F_{23}^{d,u}|$ with the result that
the FCNC can have observable consequences in $B$ mixing without conflicting
with the $K^0-\bar{K}^0$ mixing. Detailed fits to the quark masses and the CKM
matrix are used to obtain the (complex) couplings $F_{ij}^d$ and $F_{ij}^u$.
Combined constraints from flavour and CP violations in the $K,B_d,B_s,D$ mesons
are analyzed within the model. They allow ($i$) relatively light Higgs, 100-150
GeV ($ii$) measurable extra contributions to the magnitudes and phases of the
$B^0_{d,s}-\bar{B}^0_{d,s}$ mixing amplitudes and ($iii$) the $D^0-\bar{D}^0$
mixing at the current sensitivity level.

Infrared spectra from the Spitzer Space Telescope (SSC) of many debris disks
are well fit with a single black body temperature which suggest clearings
within the disk. We assume that inside the clearing orbital instability due to
planets removes dust generating planetesimal belts and dust generated by the
outer disk that is scattered or drifts into the clearing. From numerical
integrations we estimate a minimum planet spacing required for orbital
instability (and so planetesimal and dust removal) as a function of system age
and planet mass. We estimate that a 10^8 year old debris disk with a dust disk
edge at a radius of 50 AU hosted by an A star must contain approximately 5
Neptune mass planets between the clearing radius and the iceline in order to
remove all primordial objects within it. We infer that known debris disk
systems contain at least a fifth of a Jupiter mass in massive planets. The
number of planets and spacing required is insensitive to the assumed planet
mass. However an order of magnitude higher total mass in planets could reside
in these systems if the planets are more massive.

(Abridged) Galaxies form in hierarchically assembling dark matter halos. With
cosmological three dimensional adaptive mesh refinement simulations, we explore
in detail the virialization of baryons in the concordance cosmology, including
optically thin primordial gas cooling. We focus on early protogalaxies with
virial temperatures of 10^4 K and their progenitors. Without cooling, virial
heating occurs in shocks close to the virial radius for material falling in
from voids. Material in dense filaments penetrates deeper to about half that
radius. With cooling the virial shock position shrinks and also the filaments
reach scales as small as a third the virial radius. The temperatures in
protogalaxies found in adiabatic simulations decrease by a factor of two from
the center and show flat entropy cores. In cooling halos the gas reaches virial
equilibrium with the dark matter potential through its turbulent velocities. We
observe turbulent Mach numbers ranging from one to three in the cooling cases.
This turbulence is driven by the large scale merging and interestingly remains
supersonic in the centers of these early galaxies even in the absence of any
feedback processes. The virial theorem is shown to approximately hold over 3
orders of magnitude in length scale with the turbulent pressure prevailing over
the thermal energy. The turbulent velocity distributions are Maxwellian and by
far dominate the small rotation velocities associated with the total angular
momentum of the galaxies. Decomposing the velocity field using the
Cauchy-Stokes theorem, we show that ample amounts of vorticity are present
around shocks even at the very centers of these objects.

We present a detailed analysis of the Galaxy Stellar Mass Function of
galaxies up to z=2.5 as obtained from the VVDS. We estimate the stellar mass
from broad-band photometry using 2 different assumptions on the galaxy star
formation history and show that the addition of secondary bursts to a
continuous star formation history produces systematically higher (up to 40%)
stellar masses. At low redshift (z=0.2) we find a substantial population of
low-mass galaxies (<10^9 Msun) composed by faint blue galaxies (M_I-M_K=0.3).
In general the stellar mass function evolves slowly up to z=0.9 and more
significantly above this redshift. Conversely, a massive tail is present up to
z=2.5 and have extremely red colours (M_I-M_K=0.7-0.8). We find a decline with
redshift of the overall number density of galaxies for all masses (59+-5% for
M>10^8 Msun at z=1), and a mild mass-dependent average evolution
(`mass-downsizing'). In particular our data are consistent with mild/negligible
(<30%) evolution up to z=0.7 for massive galaxies (>6x10^10 Msun). For less
massive systems the no-evolution scenario is excluded. A large fraction (>=50%)
of massive galaxies have been already assembled and converted most of their gas
into stars at z=1, ruling out the `dry mergers' as the major mechanism of their
assembly history below z=1. This fraction decreases to 33% at z=2. Low-mass
systems have decreased continuously in number and mass density (by a factor up
to 4) from the present age to z=2, consistently with a prolonged mass assembly
also at z<1.

In this study, we present a high-resolution investigation of a star-forming cluster in the Cep-A HW2 region. The main aim of our work is to understand the formation mechanisms and the evolution of young stellar clusters. The high-resolution observations were conducted using the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) at 1.3 mm wavelength. Based on the ALMA observations, we identified several compact sources within the cluster. We estimated their masses and luminosities and found that most of them are protostars in various stages of evolution. Our findings suggest that this star-forming region is in a very active stage of star formation and provides an ideal laboratory to study the processes involved in the formation of massive stars.
We also investigated the distribution of gas and dust in the cluster region and found that the dust emission is concentrated around the protostars. We inferred the temperature and density profiles of the gas and estimated the total gas mass within the cluster to be around 300 M⊙. Our observations and analysis offer valuable insights towards understanding the early stages of the formation of stars and can contribute to our knowledge of star formation in general.

In this work, we investigate the temperature dependence of the tensile properties of single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) through O(N) tight binding molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. SWCNTs exhibit exceptional mechanical properties such as high tensile strength and stiffness, and are therefore considered as potential building blocks for future nanoscale devices. However, it is crucial to understand how these properties vary with temperature as well. Our research attempts to fill this knowledge gap by exploring the influence of temperature on the mechanical properties of SWCNTs.

Our comprehensive simulations reveal that the tensile properties of SWCNTs are highly sensitive to temperature. The ultimate tensile strength (UTS) and Young's modulus decrease with increasing temperature, whereas the failure strain increases. This trend is observed for all chiralities of SWCNTs in our simulations. Our results suggest that temperature-induced disorder and thermal vibrations play a critical role in the mechanical properties of SWCNTs. Our study provides relevant insight into the mechanical performance of SWCNTs under different temperature conditions, essential for the design and optimization of nanoscale devices.

In this study, we explore the potential of using the difference power spectrum of 21-cm fluctuations as a powerful statistical tool for investigating the epoch of cosmic reionization. The problem at hand is that the process of reionization, which marks the transition of the intergalactic medium from a neutral to an ionized state, is one of the least understood phenomena in the history of the universe. Hence, it is crucial to explore new avenues of probing this epoch to uncover new insights into the physics of the early universe.

We propose a method of analyzing the difference power spectrum of 21-cm fluctuations, which is the statistical measure of the difference in power between the foreground and reionization signal. This approach mitigates the challenging issue of foreground contamination in cosmological observations. Using simulations, we demonstrate that this technique can constrain the duration and topology of the reionization process.

Our results indicate that the difference power spectrum is a promising tool for probing the epoch of reionization, providing new avenues for understanding the physics of the early universe. By providing a novel statistical framework to explore reionization, we believe this work will open up new avenues of research in the study of the early universe.

In this work, we present the results of a Fourier resolved spectroscopy analysis of the low-mass X-ray binary 4U 1728-34. Our investigation focused on deciphering the spectral and temporal properties of the binary system using high-resolution X-ray spectra obtained by the Reflection Grating Spectrometer on board the XMM-Newton satellite.

The primary aim of our study was to investigate the possible presence of a high-frequency QPO (HFQPO) in the power spectrum of 4U 1728-34. Our methodology involved the use of Fourier analysis to resolve the X-ray spectrum into narrow frequency bands and investigate the properties of the corresponding power spectra.

Our analysis revealed the presence of a broad bump centered around 30 Hz in the power spectral density distribution. Contrary to our expectations, we did not find evidence for the existence of a coherent HFQPO in the frequency range of 30-100 Hz. Furthermore, our results suggest that the observed variability is likely due to the combination of several incoherent frequency components.

Overall, this study provides new insights into the spectral and temporal properties of low-mass X-ray binaries and reveals that the HFQPO phenomenon might be more elusive than previously thought. Our findings contribute to advancing the field of X-ray astronomy and have implications for the interpretation of similar systems.

In this study, we present a modeling of the temporal and azimuthal variability of the Io plasma torus through Cassini UVIS observations. The rings of Jupiter are a unique natural laboratory to study plasma physics, and the Io plasma torus, powered by the gravitational interaction between Jupiter and Io, exhibits a highly dynamic behavior. In this context, the UVIS instrument onboard the Cassini spacecraft has provided a long-term dataset with unprecedented spatial and temporal resolution of the torus emissions. The complex variability of the torus, including changes in its shape, brightness, and composition, poses a challenge for theoretical modeling. We address this challenge by improving a previously developed statistical model that combines a ring current simulation with the UVIS observational constraints. The new model can reproduce the observations of multiple emission lines over a wide range of azimuthal and temporal scales. Our results demonstrate that the torus variations are driven by a combination of upstream conditions, plasma dynamics, and the interplay between different emission processes. Overall, this work contributes to a better understanding of space weather and plasma dynamics in planetary environments.

In this work, we explore the use of neutral networks to map DNA sequences to three-dimensional shapes. This is an important problem in structural biology, as the shape of a molecule largely determines its function. However, determining the three-dimensional structure of a protein is difficult and time-consuming, and traditional experimental methods can only provide limited information. Therefore, computational methods that can accurately predict protein structures are highly desirable.

The key idea behind our approach is to use neutral networks to address the immense complexity of sequence-to-structure mapping. Neutral networks are sets of sequences that have similar folding energies, allowing for the exploration of alternative folding pathways. We trained a neural network on a dataset of sequence-structure pairs and then used it to generate a large number of alternative structures for each sequence. We then clustered these structures to identify distinct folding paths and used them to refine our predictions.

Our methodology resulted in accurate predictions of protein structure, even for sequences with little sequence similarity to known structures. We believe the use of neutral networks has great potential for the development of improved computational methods for predicting protein structure. This work has important implications for the design of new drugs and the understanding of biological systems, and it represents an exciting direction for future research in structural biology.

We find that there is no supersymmetric flavor/CP problem, mu-problem,
cosmological moduli/gravitino problem or dimension four/five proton decay
problem in a class of supersymmetric theories with O(1) GeV gravitino mass. The
cosmic abundance of the non-thermally produced gravitinos naturally explains
the dark matter component of the universe. A mild hierarchy between the mass
scale of supersymmetric particles and electroweak scale is predicted,
consistent with the null result of a search for the Higgs boson at the LEP-II
experiments. A relation to the strong CP problem is addressed. We propose a
parametrization of the model for the purpose of collider studies. The scalar
tau lepton is the next to lightest supersymmetric particle in a theoretically
favored region of the parameter space. The lifetime of the scalar tau is of
O(1000) seconds with which it is regarded as a charged stable particle in
collider experiments. We discuss characteristic signatures and a strategy for
confirmation of this class of theories at the LHC experiments.

We present simulation results of $\gamma$ + Jet analysis using CMS (Compact
Muon Solenoid) Object-Oriented software at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC)
center of mass energy $\sqrt{s}$=14 TeV. The study of direct photon production
helps in validating the perturbative Quantum Chromodynamics (pQCD) and
providing information on the gluon distribution in the nucleons. Direct photon
processes also constitute a major background to several other Standard Model
(SM) processes and signals of new physics. Thus these processes need to be
understood precisely in the new energy regime. In this work, we have done a
detailed study of the GEANT4 simulated $\gamma$ + jet events generated with
Pythia, and the related background processes. Isolation cuts have been
optimized for direct photon which improves the signal over background ratio by
$\sim25%$ as compared to previous studies done in CMS. The inclusion of a large
$\Delta\phi$ cut between the photon and the leading jet at $40^0$ in the
analysis leads to a further increase of $\sim15%$ in S/B, thus giving an
overall gain of $\sim42%$ in S/B ratio.

We present the results of our study of the lower mass function of the young open cluster Blanco 1, based on optical and near-infrared photometry obtained with the Hubble Space Telescope and the Spitzer Space Telescope. The main aim of this work is to explore the low-mass end of the stellar initial mass function (IMF) and to determine the slope of the mass function for the cluster. The Blanco 1 cluster is an ideal target for this study since it is young and nearby, with an estimated age of about 100 million years and a distance of about 250 pc.

We use the photometric data to obtain mass estimates for the cluster members from 30 MJup to 3 Mo. We find a power-law slope of α = 0.63 ± 0.07 for the mass function in the range from 0.03 Mo to 0.5 Mo, which is consistent with previous studies of other young clusters. Our results suggest that the IMF may be universal for the low-mass stars, with a power-law slope of α ~ 0.5 for the whole mass range. 

In conclusion, our study of the lower mass function of Blanco 1 contributes to the understanding of the formation and evolution of low-mass stars and brown dwarfs in young open clusters. The results obtained here will provide useful constraints for future theoretical models of star formation.

In this study, we present sub-arcsecond observations of the CO(1-0) and CO(2-1) emission lines in the ultraluminous infrared galaxy (ULIRG) IRAS 10190+1322, using the Submillimeter Array (SMA). The main goal is to investigate the molecular gas properties and kinematics in the central region of IRAS 10190+1322. The intense starburst activity and the corresponding high gas density and temperature in the central regions of ULIRGs lead to non-uniform and complex gas kinematics. Molecular line observations allow us to trace the cold molecular gas and to study the structure and dynamics of the gas in the nuclei of ULIRGs.

Our sub-arcsecond SMA observations reveal the presence of two CO(1-0) and CO(2-1) components in the central region of IRAS 10190+1322. The high-resolution maps show that the molecular gas is distributed in a compact core with a size of about 1 kpc, and that the bulk of the gas emission is concentrated in the northern component. The position-velocity diagrams along the major and minor axes of the core reveal different gas kinematics in the two components, which may suggest different origins of the molecular gas.

Our results demonstrate the wide range of molecular gas properties and kinematics in ULIRGs, and provide valuable insights into the central starburst activities in these extreme systems. The high-resolution observations presented in this work will help constrain the physical properties and structure of the molecular gas reservoirs in the nuclei of ULIRGs.

We investigate the magnetocaloric properties of the two-dimensional
frustrated J1-J2 model on a square lattice. This model describes well the
magnetic behavior of two classes of quasi-two-dimensional S = 1/2 vanadates,
namely the Li2VOXO4 (X = Si, Ge) and AA'VO(PO4)2 (A, A' = Pb, Zn, Sr, Ba)
compounds. The magnetocaloric effect (MCE) consists in the adiabatic
temperature change upon changing the external magnetic field. In frustrated
systems, the MCE can be enhanced close to the saturation field because of
massive degeneracies among low lying excitations. We discuss results for the
MCE in the two distinct antiferromagnetic regimes of the phase diagram.
Numerical finite temperature Lanczos as well as analytical methods based on the
spin wave expansion are employed and results are compared. We give explicit
values for the saturation fields of the vanadium compounds. We predict that at
subcritical fields there is first a (positive) maximum followed by sign change
of the MCE, characteristic of all magnetically ordered phases.

The X-ray transient XMMU J174716.1-281048 was serendipitously discovered with
XMM-Newton in 2003. It lies about 0.9 degrees off the Galactic Centre and its
spectrum shows a high absorption (~8 x 10E22 cm^(-2)). Previous X-ray
observations of the source field performed in 2000 and 2001 did not detect the
source, indicative of a quiescent emission at least two orders of magnitude
fainter. The low luminosity during the outburst (~5 x 10E34 erg/s at 8 kpc)
indicates that the source is a member of the ``very faint X-ray transients''
class. On 2005 March 22nd the INTEGRAL satellite caught a possible type-I X-ray
burst from the new INTEGRAL source IGR J17464-2811, classified as fast X-ray
transient. This source was soon found to be positionally coincident, within the
uncertainties, with XMMU J174716.1-281048. Here we report data analysis of the
X-ray burst observed with the IBIS and JEM-X telescopes and confirm the type-I
burst nature. We also re-analysed XMM-Newton and Chandra archival observations
of the source field. We discuss the implications of these new findings,
particularly related to the source distance as well as the source
classification.

In this paper, we investigate the generalization of circles over algebraic extensions. Our primary motivation for this research stems from the inability to fully describe the geometry of certain shapes in a Euclidean space. Specifically, we aim to provide a more comprehensive understanding of the geometric properties of circles over algebraic extensions. While this problem has been studied before, our research provides a more general approach to the problem. 

We start by extending the definition of a circle to an algebraic space, and then we explore the geometrical properties of these circles. One of our notable findings is that over certain algebraic extensions, a circle can turn into an ellipse. This observation is critical since it implies that the properties of some circles over algebraic extensions are only discerned when studied in the context of the algebraic extension.

Moreover, we establish that a circle in an algebraic space is always a smooth curve, which can be described by a polynomial of degree two with coefficients in that algebraic extension. Finally, we construct a field extension of degree greater than two that admits no circle. This finding suggests that the properties of circles over algebraic extensions are more complicated than initially anticipated. In summary, our study provides a broad perspective on the properties of circles in algebraic spaces, providing an essential foundation for further mathematical and geometric investigations in this area.

Although powers of the Young-Jucys-Murphya elements X_i = (1 i) + ... +(i-1
i), i = 1, ..., n, in the symmetric group S_n acting on {1, ...,n} do not lie
in the centre of the group algebra of S_n, we show that transitive powers,
namely the sum of the contributions from elements that act transitively on {1,
>...,n}, are central. We determine the coefficients, which we call star
factorization numbers, that occur in the resolution of transitive powers with
respect to the class basis of the centre of S_n, and show that they have a
polynomiality property. These centrality and polynomiality properties have
seemingly unrelated consequences. First, they answer a question raised by Pak
about reduced decompositions; second, they explain and extend the beautiful
symmetry result discovered by Irving and Rattan; and thirdly, we relate the
polynomiality to an existing polynomiality result for a class of double Hurwitz
numbers associated with branched covers of the sphere, which therefore suggests
that there may be an ELSV-type formula associated with the star factorization
numbers.

In this study, we investigate the presence of exoplanets orbiting retired A stars and their companions, specifically focusing on three intermediate-mass subgiants: HD 120084, HD 96127, and HD 81817. Our motivation for this research stems from the importance of understanding the relationship between a star and its planetary system, as well as the role of intermediate-mass subgiants in the evolution of stars and their planets. 

Using radial velocity measurements obtained from the HARPS-N spectrograph, we perform a detailed analysis to detect potential exoplanets in these systems. Through careful analysis of the data, we are able to confirm the presence of exoplanets orbiting HD 120084 and HD 81817, with HD 120084b being a gas giant planet and HD 81817c being a low-mass Neptune-like planet. We also find a strong hint of a planetary companion orbiting HD 96127. 

Our results provide valuable insights into the formation and evolution of planetary systems, as well as the behavior of intermediate-mass subgiants. We utilize our methodology, consisting of precise radial velocity measurements and rigorous data analysis techniques, to accurately detect planets in these systems, contributing to the growing body of research on exoplanetary systems. The implications of our findings are significant, underscoring the need for continued exploration of planetary systems in order to fully understand the dynamic relationship between a star and its planets.

In this work, we investigate the exclusive photoproduction of $J/\psi$ in proton-proton and proton-antiproton scattering. The study is motivated by the fact that the production of $J/\psi$ mesons in high-energy collisions is an important process in understanding the properties of hadrons and their interactions with photons. However, there is very little experimental data available in this regard, and theoretical predictions are limited. Hence, the aim of our study is to provide a better understanding of this process by calculating the cross sections for exclusive $J/\psi$ production in $pp$ and $p\bar{p}$ scattering, making use of various theoretical models. 

Our results show that the cross sections are significantly different in the two cases, with $pp$ scattering yielding a much larger signal than $p\bar{p}$ scattering. Moreover, we observe that the predictions from the different models vary widely, indicating the need for further experimental data to constrain the theoretical uncertainties. The key ideas in our approach include the use of helicity amplitudes, the incorporation of higher-order perturbative QCD effects, and the inclusion of spin-dependent contributions from the proton and antiproton. Our work provides important insights into the exclusive photoproduction of $J/\psi$ mesons, and we hope that our findings will stimulate further research in this area.

Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) ejected by the massive black hole at the Galactic
center have unique kinematic properties compared to other halo stars. Their
trajectories will deviate from being exactly radial because of the asymmetry of
the Milky Way potential produced by the flattened disk and the triaxial dark
matter halo, causing a change of angular momentum that can be much larger than
the initial small value at injection. We study the kinematics of HVSs and
propose an estimator of dark halo triaxiality that is determined only by
instantaneous position and velocity vectors of HVSs at large Galactocentric
distances (r>~50kpc). We show that, in the case of a substantially triaxial
halo, the distribution of deflection angles (the angle between the stellar
position and velocity vector) for HVSs on bound orbits is spread uniformly over
the range 10--180deg. Future astrometric and deep wide-field surveys should
measure the positions and velocities of a significant number of HVSs, and
provide useful constraints on the shape of the Galactic dark matter halo.

In this work, we investigate the performance of a multiple-antenna fading channel with a quantized precoding matrix. We consider a setup where a transmitter equipped with multiple antennas communicates with a receiver through a fading channel. We assume that the transmitter has limited feedback capacity and can only convey a quantized version of the precoding matrix. Our goal is to study the impact of this quantization on the channel capacity and to derive useful insights that can inform the design of practical communication systems.

Our motivation for this research is the increasing demand for high-speed data transmission in wireless communication systems. Multi-antenna systems have been shown to significantly improve the capacity of wireless channels, but they require complex signal processing and channel feedback mechanisms. In practice, the feedback capacity is limited, and the quantization of the feedback signals can introduce errors that degrade the system performance.

To address this challenge, we derive analytical expressions for the capacity of a multiple-antenna fading channel with a quantized precoding matrix. We consider both the case of perfect and imperfect channel state information, and we provide numerical results that illustrate the impact of the quantization on the system performance. Our results highlight the trade-off between feedback capacity and channel capacity and can serve as a guide for the design of practical multi-antenna communication systems.

We present recent results obtained with the Wide Field Spectrograph (WiFeS), a powerful and versatile instrument installed on the Australian National University (ANU) 2.3m telescope, located in Siding Springs Observatory. WiFeS combines a wide spectral coverage, high spatial resolution, and excellent sensitivity, allowing detailed spectroscopic studies of various astrophysical objects, such as galaxies, stars, and supernovae. Our observations, made in different modes and wavelength ranges, have provided unique insights into the physical and chemical properties of various astrophysical phenomena. For instance, we have revealed the complex kinematic and chemical structure of galaxies, shedding light on their formation and evolution, and identified the signature of the elusive Population III stars, which formed in the early universe and are crucial to understanding cosmic reionization. 

The motivation for our research with WiFeS stems from the pressing need to understand the complex processes that govern the formation and evolution of galaxies and stars, as they are essential to uncovering the mysteries of the universe's origin. The observations we present in this work aim to address these fundamental questions and contribute to advancing our knowledge of the cosmos. Our work also aims to solve the problem of the limited spectral coverage and low spatial resolution of traditionally used spectrographs in astronomy, hindering the exploration of the complex physical and chemical properties of astrophysical objects. WiFeS, with its fantastic capabilities, has overcome these limitations, and our results demonstrate its immense potential for further discoveries in astrophysics.

Protein-DNA complexes with loops play a fundamental role in a wide variety of
cellular processes, ranging from the regulation of DNA transcription to
telomere maintenance. As ubiquitous as they are, their precise in vivo
properties and their integration into the cellular function still remain
largely unexplored. Here, we present a multilevel approach that efficiently
connects in both directions molecular properties with cell physiology and use
it to characterize the molecular properties of the looped DNA-lac repressor
complex while functioning in vivo. The properties we uncover include the
presence of two representative conformations of the complex, the stabilization
of one conformation by DNA architectural proteins, and precise values of the
underlying twisting elastic constants and bending free energies. Incorporation
of all this molecular information into gene-regulation models reveals an
unprecedented versatility of looped DNA-protein complexes at shaping the
properties of gene expression.

In this study, we investigate the effect of transport coefficients on the magnetorotational instability (MRI) in a shearing box with zero net flux through magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) simulations. The MRI is a fundamental mechanism for angular momentum transport in accretion discs and plays a crucial role in astrophysical phenomena such as star formation and black hole accretion. Despite its importance, the dependence of MRI on various transport coefficients and their interplay with the MRI remains poorly understood. 

Our simulations show that the α-parameter, a measure of the Shakura-Sunyaev viscosity parameter in a turbulent flow, significantly affects the growth rate and saturation amplitude of the MRI. We find that the MRI is largely suppressed at low values of the α-parameter and exhibits a delayed onset at high values. We also investigate the effects of the magnetic Reynolds number and Prandtl number (ratio of viscosity to magnetic diffusivity) and find that they do not significantly alter the MRI behavior in the shearing box with zero net flux.

The motivation for this research is to gain a better understanding of the role of transport coefficients in MRI and hence improve our knowledge of accretion disc physics. Our findings highlight the importance of considering transport coefficients when modeling MRI-dominated flows. Furthermore, our work attempts to solve the problem of incomplete understanding of MRI behavior by examining the effect of transport coefficients in a well-known MRI setup in MHD simulations.

Chandra and VLA observations of the symbiotic star R Aqr in 2004 reveal
significant changes over the three to four year interval between these
observations and previous observations taken with the VLA in 1999 and with
Chandra in 2000. This paper reports on the evolution of the outer thermal X-ray
lobe-jets and radio jets. The emission from the outer X-ray lobe-jets lies
farther away from the central binary than the outer radio jets, and comes from
material interpreted as being shock heated to ~10^6 K, a likely result of
collision between high speed material ejected from the central binary and
regions of enhanced gas density. Between 2000 and 2004, the Northeast (NE)
outer X-ray lobe-jet moved out away from the central binary, with an apparent
projected motion of ~580 km s^-1. The Southwest (SW) outer X-ray lobe-jet
almost disappeared between 2000 and 2004, presumably due to adiabatic expansion
and cooling. The NE radio bright spot also moved away from the central binary
between 2000 and 2004, but with a smaller apparent velocity than of the NE
X-ray bright spot. The SW outer lobe-jet was not detected in the radio in
either 1999 or 2004. The density and mass of the X-ray emitting material is
estimated. Cooling times, shock speeds, pressure and confinement are discussed.

The monotonicity and stability of difference schemes for, in general,
hyperbolic systems of conservation laws with source terms are studied. The
basic approach is to investigate the stability and monotonicity of a non-linear
scheme in terms of its corresponding scheme in variations. Such an approach
leads to application of the stability theory for linear equation systems to
establish stability of the corresponding non-linear scheme. The main
methodological innovation is the theorems establishing the notion that a
non-linear scheme is stable (and monotone) if the corresponding scheme in
variations is stable (and, respectively, monotone). Criteria are developed for
monotonicity and stability of difference schemes associated with the numerical
analysis of systems of partial differential equations. The theorem of
Friedrichs (1954) is generalized to be applicable to variational schemes with
non-symmetric matrices. A new modification of the central Lax-Friedrichs (LxF)
scheme is developed to be of the second order accuracy. A monotone piecewise
cubic interpolation is used in the central schemes to give an accurate
approximation for the model in question. The stability and monotonicity of the
modified scheme are investigated. Some versions of the modified scheme are
tested on several conservation laws, and the scheme is found to be accurate and
robust. As applied to hyperbolic conservation laws with, in general, stiff
source terms, it is constructed a second order scheme based on
operator-splitting techniques.

In this work, we investigate the spin effects in Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) and its connection with recurrence lattices with multi-site exchanges. QCD is a theory that describes the interactions between quarks and gluons, which are the building blocks of matter. The spin of these particles is an important property that can affect the way they interact with each other. However, the study of spin effects in QCD is a challenging problem due to the complex nature of the theory.

To address this problem, we develop a new approach based on the concept of recurrence lattices with multi-site exchanges. Our methodology involves using lattice QCD simulations to compute the relevant observables and analyzing the data using a statistical technique known as Principal Component Analysis (PCA). Our results show that the spin effects in QCD can be explained in terms of the recurrence properties of the lattice, and that multi-site exchanges play a crucial role in this process.

The key ideas behind our work are rooted in the general principles of QCD and the mathematical properties of recurrence lattices. The methodology we employ is based on a combination of numerical simulations and statistical analysis, which allows us to explore complex phenomena in a rigorous and quantitative way. Our results shed new light on the spin effects in QCD and provide a deeper understanding of the underlying physics.

In this work, we present our study on the fairness provision implementation in the IEEE 802.11e Infrastructure Basic Service Set (IBSS). The basic idea behind this study is to ensure that each user in the network has equal access to the shared network resources, regardless of how many users are there. We have used a simulation-based approach to evaluate the performance of the existing fairness provision mechanism in the IEEE 802.11e IBSS. 

Our results reveal that the current fairness provision implementation in the IEEE 802.11e IBSS is insufficient to ensure fair resource allocation among users. We have also proposed a new fairness provision mechanism, which outperforms the existing mechanism in terms of fairness, throughput, and delay. Our proposed mechanism introduces a novel backoff strategy and a dynamic weight assignment scheme, which takes into account the number of users currently contending for network resources. 

The motivation behind this work is to solve the problem of unfair resource allocation and to improve the overall quality of service for users in the IEEE 802.11e IBSS. Our findings have significant implications for the design and implementation of fairness provision mechanisms in wireless networks. Future works can examine how our proposed mechanism performs in different types of network topologies and under varying loads.

The Stanley lattice, Tamari lattice and Kreweras lattice are three remarkable
orders defined on the set of Catalan objects of a given size. These lattices
are ordered by inclusion: the Stanley lattice is an extension of the Tamari
lattice which is an extension of the Kreweras lattice. The Stanley order can be
defined on the set of Dyck paths of size $n$ as the relation of \emph{being
above}. Hence, intervals in the Stanley lattice are pairs of non-crossing Dyck
paths. In a former article, the second author defined a bijection $\Phi$
between pairs of non-crossing Dyck paths and the realizers of triangulations
(or Schnyder woods). We give a simpler description of the bijection $\Phi$.
Then, we study the restriction of $\Phi$ to Tamari's and Kreweras' intervals.
We prove that $\Phi$ induces a bijection between Tamari intervals and minimal
realizers. This gives a bijection between Tamari intervals and triangulations.
We also prove that $\Phi$ induces a bijection between Kreweras intervals and
the (unique) realizers of stack triangulations. Thus, $\Phi$ induces a
bijection between Kreweras intervals and stack triangulations which are known
to be in bijection with ternary trees.

In this work, we present a generalisation of the Einstein condition and cone construction for parabolic geometries. Our motivation for the research is to extend the Riemannian notion of Einstein manifolds to the wider class of parabolic geometries. Parabolic geometries are differential geometric structures that allow for a uniform description of various important geometric objects, such as conformal and projective structures. However, the study of Einstein structures in parabolic geometry has been relatively unexplored. 

Our main result is the proof of a cone construction that produces parabolic Einstein structures from a given non-Einstein structure. This cone construction provides a powerful tool for investigating Einstein structures in parabolic geometries. We demonstrate the utility of this construction by applying it to several concrete examples in detail. Furthermore, we establish a dichotomy for parabolic structures with non-negative Einstein constant, which distinguishes between those that admit an Einstein structure and those that do not. 

Our work provides new insight into the nature of parabolic Einstein structures and lays the groundwork for future research in this area. The results obtained have significant implications for various fields of mathematics, including algebraic geometry and theoretical physics.

Trafficking of AMPA receptors in and out of synapses is crucial for synaptic
plasticity. Previous studies have focused on the role of endo/exocytosis
processes or that of lateral diffusion of extra-synaptic receptors. We have now
directly imaged AMPAR movements inside and outside synapses of live neurons
using single-molecule fluorescence microscopy. Inside individual synapses, we
found immobile and mobile receptors, which display restricted diffusion.
Extra-synaptic receptors display free diffusion. Receptors could also exchange
between these membrane compartments through lateral diffusion. Glutamate
application increased both receptor mobility inside synapses and the fraction
of mobile receptors present in a juxtasynaptic region. Block of inhibitory
transmission to favor excitatory synaptic activity induced a transient increase
in the fraction of mobile receptors and a decrease in the proportion of
juxtasynaptic receptors. Altogether, our data show that rapid exchange of
receptors between a synaptic and extra-synaptic localization occurs through
regulation of receptor diffusion inside synapses.

In our research, we aim to investigate the soft excess phenomenon observed in Seyfert 1 active galactic nuclei (AGN) and propose an absorption origin that has not been considered before. The motivation behind this work is the long-standing mystery of the soft excess in these objects, which refers to the apparent excess of soft X-ray emission that cannot be explained by the standard AGN models. Previous studies have tried to explain it by means of thermal comptonization or reflection from the accretion disk, but these models have been unable to fully resolve this issue.

Our proposed absorption origin arises from the existence of a warm absorber, a region of highly ionized gas located in the vicinity of the AGN's central engine. This gas can absorb X-ray photons in the soft energy range, leading to a deficit in X-ray flux and creating the soft excess. We studied a sample of Seyfert 1 AGN observed by the XMM-Newton and Suzaku telescopes, and performed spectral analysis to test our hypothesis. Our results show that the warm absorber provides a good fit to the observations and can explain the soft excess.

To conclude, our work proposes a new explanation for the soft excess in Seyfert 1 AGN, which challenges the current models and offers a fresh perspective in the study of these objects. Our approach is based on state-of-the-art X-ray spectral analysis and provides a robust framework for future investigations on this topic.

We present a study aimed at constraining Warm Dark Matter (WDM) using Quasi-Stellar Object (QSO) gravitational lensing. WDM is a candidate for dark matter composed of relatively light particles which form structures on smaller scales than Cold Dark Matter (CDM). This discrepancy has led to an observational tension, wherein the predicted number of dwarf galaxies in CDM is not observed. We seek to investigate the physical consequences of WDM, and its effect on QSO gravitational lensing. Our methodology involves the fitting of gravitational lens models to QSO systems, where the background source is lensed by a foreground galaxy. We use Markov Chain Monte Carlo methods to sample the parameter space of the lens models, where the WDM energy density is a free parameter. We analyze the resulting parameter space, to measure the constraints placed on the WDM energy density, and compare these constraints with those placed by other observations. Our results indicate that QSO gravitational lensing offers a complementary probe of WDM in the universe, and provides further insight into the nature of dark matter.

We present the first detailed study of the properties (temperatures,
gravities, and masses) of the NGC 6791 white dwarf population. This unique
stellar system is both one of the oldest (8 Gyr) and most metal-rich ([Fe/H] ~
0.4) open clusters in our Galaxy, and has a color-magnitude diagram (CMD) that
exhibits both a red giant clump and a much hotter extreme horizontal branch.
Fitting the Balmer lines of the white dwarfs in the cluster, using Keck/LRIS
spectra, suggests that most of these stars are undermassive, <M> = 0.43 +/-
0.06 Msun, and therefore could not have formed from canonical stellar evolution
involving the helium flash at the tip of the red giant branch. We show that at
least 40% of NGC 6791's evolved stars must have lost enough mass on the red
giant branch to avoid the flash, and therefore did not convert helium into
carbon-oxygen in their core. Such increased mass loss in the evolution of the
progenitors of these stars is consistent with the presence of the extreme
horizontal branch in the CMD. This unique stellar evolutionary channel also
naturally explains the recent finding of a very young age (2.4 Gyr) for NGC
6791 from white dwarf cooling theory; helium core white dwarfs in this cluster
will cool ~3 times slower than carbon-oxygen core stars and therefore the
corrected white dwarf cooling age is in fact ~7 Gyr, consistent with the well
measured main-sequence turnoff age. These results provide direct empirical
evidence that mass loss is much more efficient in high metallicity environments
and therefore may be critical in interpreting the ultraviolet upturn in
elliptical galaxies.

In this paper, we explore the contributions of Issai Schur to Analysis. Schur, a mathematician of Jewish origin, made significant contributions in various branches of analysis, including linear algebra, group theory, and number theory. Our research attempts to solve the problem of underrepresentation of mathematicians from marginalized communities in the history of mathematics by shedding light on the works of Schur, who was a victim of anti-Semitic discrimination in his time.

The motivation behind this paper is to recognize the mathematical genius of Issai Schur and to highlight the importance of diversity and inclusivity in the field of mathematics. We also aim to provide insights into the key ideas and methodology that Schur used in his groundbreaking work. His fundamental contributions to analysis include the Schur decomposition and the Schur complement, which have had broad applications in diverse fields such as quantum mechanics, control theory, and computer science.

The methodology we use in this paper involves a detailed analysis of Schur's works and the impact they have had in the field of analysis. We also examine the historical context of anti-Semitic discrimination that Schur faced, which may have hindered the recognition of his work. By acknowledging the contributions of Issai Schur and other marginalized mathematicians, we aim to promote a more diverse and inclusive environment in the field of mathematics.

We introduce VErsatile SPectral Analysis (VESPA): a new method which aims to
recover robust star formation and metallicity histories from galactic spectra.
VESPA uses the full spectral range to construct a galaxy history from synthetic
models. We investigate the use of an adaptative parametrization grid to recover
reliable star formation histories on a galaxy-by-galaxy basis. Our goal is
robustness as opposed to high resolution histories, and the method is designed
to return high time resolution only where the data demand it. In this paper we
detail the method and we present our findings when we apply VESPA to synthetic
and real Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) spectroscopic data. We show that the
number of parameters that can be recovered from a spectrum depends strongly on
the signal-to-noise, wavelength coverage and presence or absence of a young
population. For a typical SDSS sample of galaxies, we can normally recover
between 2 to 5 stellar populations. We find very good agreement between VESPA
and our previous analysis of the SDSS sample with MOPED.

In this work, we propose a universal source coding strategy for monotonic distributions, which can be applied to fast decaying monotonic distributions. Our motivation is to provide an efficient and precise coding method that can handle diverse forms of data, including audio, image, and text signals. We leverage the quantization process to achieve a codebook that can handle a large range of distribution types with low distortion rates.

We present our approach based on a novel theorem, which establishes that the entropy rate of such monotonic distributions is monotonically decreasing. This property allows us to design a scheme that is asymptotically optimal and has a complexity rate of O(log N) bits per sample, where N is the length of the monotonic sequence.

We validate our methodology through numerical experiments that show its effectiveness for both synthetic test cases and real-world data sets. The results obtained demonstrate the superior performance of our universal source coding strategy compared to existing methods for fast decaying monotonic distributions.

Overall, our work contributes to the development of source coding theory by providing a universal and efficient approach to handle monotonic distributions with various rates of decay. This methodology can have practical applications in data compression, reduction, and transmission, benefiting numerous scientific and engineering fields.

In this work, we investigate the properties of quantum and classical spins on the spatially distorted Kagome lattice, with a focus on their applications to volborthite. We begin by introducing the Kagome lattice, a two-dimensional network of corner-sharing triangles that possesses a number of exotic properties and has been the subject of much recent research. Specifically, we consider a modified form known as the spatially distorted Kagome lattice, and study the behavior of both quantum and classical spins on this lattice.

Our motivation for this research is to better understand the unique properties of volborthite, a mineral which contains a distorted Kagome lattice of copper ions, and which exhibits a number of interesting magnetic phenomena. By studying the behavior of spins on this lattice using quantum and classical models, we hope to shed light on the nature of these phenomena, and to gain insight into the underlying physics of the material.

To achieve this goal, we employ a variety of computational techniques, including Monte Carlo simulations and the density matrix renormalization group method. Through these methods, we are able to investigate the behavior of both classical and quantum spins on the distorted Kagome lattice, and to compare our results to experimental data on volborthite.

Overall, our work seeks to elucidate the fundamental physics of volborthite and other materials with similar Kagome lattice structures, and to contribute to the growing body of research on these fascinating systems.

In this paper, we present a cross-disciplinary framework that serves to describe contextually mediated change based on findings across diverse fields such as psychology, sociology, and artificial intelligence. Our work has been motivated by the challenge of reconciling the different perspectives on contextually mediated change that arise from different disciplines, which has thus far impeded a unified understanding of these phenomena. 

Key to our framework is the recognition of the importance of context in understanding change. The framework proposes four interrelated dimensions of context ubiquitous across domains of change: temporal, spatial, social and technical. These dimensions highlight the interplay between human and technological agents and the environmental factors that affect the dynamics of change. 

To generate this framework, we utilized a mixed-methods approach, including insights from grounded theory, systematic review, and case studies. These methods allowed us to map out the patterns of interaction between different contextual dimensions, which suggested that change emerges dynamically and depends on a complex system of interconnected factors.

The results of our work offer an integrative approach to contextual mediation, developed through a cross-disciplinary lens, which highlights the interplay of context with change phenomena. Furthermore, our framework can serve to inform research and practice across various disciplines and facilitate the design of more effective interventions and policies to promote positive change.

In this work, we focus on conditional independence testing. More specifically, our aim is to determine full conditional independence by low-order conditioning. The main challenge in this context is that existing methods are limited in their ability to deal with high-dimensional data. To address this issue, we propose a novel algorithm that measures full conditional independence for arbitrary high-dimensional variables. Our approach is based on the use of low-order conditioning methods, which allow us to focus on the relevant aspects of the data and eliminate redundant information. By doing so, we can accurately determine the conditionally independent variables without having to resort to computationally expensive methods. 
Our algorithm is validated using simulated data as well as real-world datasets, and the results demonstrate that it outperforms existing methods in terms of accuracy and computational efficiency. Furthermore, we demonstrate the practical utility of our method by applying it to the problem of network inference, where we show that our approach can improve the accuracy of network reconstruction. The key ideas and methodology underlying our approach are based on the use of statistical independence measures and low-order conditioning techniques. Overall, our work provides a practical and effective solution to the problem of determining full conditional independence by low-order conditioning.

A unified analytical framework for optimum power allocation in the unordered
V-BLAST algorithm and its comparative performance analysis are presented.
Compact closed-form approximations for the optimum power allocation are
derived, based on average total and block error rates. The choice of the
criterion has little impact on the power allocation and, overall, the optimum
strategy is to allocate more power to lower step transmitters and less to
higher ones. High-SNR approximations for optimized average block and total
error rates are given. The SNR gain of optimization is rigorously defined and
studied using analytical tools, including lower and upper bounds, high and low
SNR approximations. The gain is upper bounded by the number of transmitters,
for any modulation format and type of fading channel. While the average
optimization is less complex than the instantaneous one, its performance is
almost as good at high SNR. A measure of robustness of the optimized algorithm
is introduced and evaluated. The optimized algorithm is shown to be robust to
perturbations in individual and total transmit powers. Based on the algorithm
robustness, a pre-set power allocation is suggested as a low-complexity
alternative to the other optimization strategies, which exhibits only a minor
loss in performance over the practical SNR range.

The recent parity-violating $ep$ forward-scattering elastic asymmetry data
from Jefferson Lab (HAPPEx and G0), when combined with the $\nu p$ elastic
cross section data from Brookhaven (E734), permit an extraction of the
strangeness contribution to the vector and axial nucleon form factors for
momentum transfers in the range $0.45 < Q^2 < 1.0$ GeV$^2$. These results,
combined with the recent determination of the strange vector form factors at
$Q^2 = 0.1$ GeV$^2$ (SAMPLE, HAPPEx, PVA4, G0) have been interpreted in terms
of $uuds\bar{s}$ configurations very different from the kaon-loop
configurations usually associated with strangeness in the nucleon. New
experiments are being proposed to improve the state of our knowledge of the
$\nu p$ elastic cross section -- these new experiments will push the range of
$Q^2$ to much lower values, and greatly increase the precision of the $\nu p$
elastic data. One outcome of this can be a measurement of the strangeness
contribution to the nucleon spin, $\Delta s$. Nuclear targets (e.g. C or Ar)
are to be used in these neutrino experiments, and so a deep understanding of
the nuclear physics, particularly in regard to final state effects, is needed
before the potential of these precision experiments can be fully realized.

We compute the stationary in-degree probability, $P_{in}(k)$, for a growing
network model with directed edges and arbitrary out-degree probability. In
particular, under preferential linking, we find that if the nodes have a light
tail (finite variance) out-degree distribution, then the corresponding
in-degree one behaves as $k^{-3}$. Moreover, for an out-degree distribution
with a scale invariant tail, $P_{out}(k)\sim k^{-\alpha}$, the corresponding
in-degree distribution has exactly the same asymptotic behavior only if
$2<\alpha<3$ (infinite variance). Similar results are obtained when
attractiveness is included. We also present some results on descriptive
statistics measures %descriptive statistics such as the correlation between the
number of in-going links, $D_{in}$, and outgoing links, $D_{out}$, and the
conditional expectation of $D_{in}$ given $D_{out}$, and we calculate these
measures for the WWW network. Finally, we present an application to the
scientific publications network. The results presented here can explain the
tail behavior of in/out-degree distribution observed in many real networks.

We present a morphological study of galaxies in the A901/902 supercluster
from the COMBO-17 survey. A total of 570 galaxies with photometric redshifts in
the range 0.155 < z_phot < 0.185 are visually classified by three independent
classifiers to M_V=-18. These morphological classifications are compared to
local galaxy density, distance from the nearest cluster centre, local surface
mass density from weak lensing, and photometric classification. At high local
galaxy densities, log(Sigma_10 /Mpc^2) > 1.5, a classical morphology-density
relation is found. A correlation is also found between morphology and local
projected surface mass density, but no trend is observed with distance to the
nearest cluster. This supports the finding that local environment is more
important to galaxy morphology than global cluster properties. The breakdown of
the morphological catalogue by colour shows a dominance of blue galaxies in the
galaxies displaying late-type morphologies and a corresponding dominance of red
galaxies in the early-type population. Using the 17-band photometry from
COMBO-17, we further split the supercluster red sequence into old passive
galaxies and galaxies with young stars and dust according to the prescription
of Wolf et al. (2005). We find that the dusty star-forming population describes
an intermediate morphological group between late-type and early-type galaxies,
supporting the hypothesis that field and group spiral galaxies are transformed
into S0s and, perhaps, ellipticals during cluster infall.

We conducted a multi-band study of the optically dark gamma-ray burst (GRB) 051028 in an attempt to unveil its properties and provide insight into the nature of this peculiar event. GRB 051028 was detected by the Swift Observatory and classified as optically dark due to the lack of optical afterglow emission. To overcome this limitation, we utilized data from Swift/UVOT, Swift/XRT, and the Chandra X-ray Observatory, allowing us to study the afterglow emission in the ultraviolet and X-ray bands. 

Our analysis revealed a complex temporal and spectral evolution of the afterglow emission. The X-ray light curve displayed a steep decay, while the ultraviolet data showed a shallow decay followed by a possible bump. Additionally, the X-ray spectrum indicated a cooling break, while the ultraviolet spectrum displayed a significant excess of flux above the expected power-law emission. We interpreted these findings as evidence of a long-lasting energy injection in the afterglow.

The optically dark nature of GRB 051028 presents a significant challenge to our understanding of the physics governing gamma-ray bursts. By conducting a detailed multi-band study of this event, we aimed to shed light on its unique properties and contribute to the ongoing effort to comprehend the most energetic explosions in the universe. Our findings provide valuable insights into the emission mechanisms underlying this class of events, and we expect them to inform and motivate future observational and theoretical studies.

The solar magnetic field is the primary agent that drives solar activity and
couples the Sun to the Heliosphere. Although the details of this coupling
depend on the quantitative properties of the field, many important aspects of
the corona - solar wind connection can be understood by considering only the
general topological properties of those regions on the Sun where the field
extends from the photosphere out to interplanetary space, the so-called open
field regions that are usually observed as coronal holes. From the simple
assumptions that underlie the standard quasi-steady corona-wind theoretical
models, and that are likely to hold for the Sun, as well, we derive two
conjectures on the possible structure and dynamics of coronal holes: (1)
Coronal holes are unique in that every unipolar region on the photosphere can
contain at most one coronal hole. (2) Coronal holes of nested polarity regions
must themselves be nested. Magnetic reconnection plays the central role in
enforcing these constraints on the field topology. From these conjectures we
derive additional properties for the topology of open field regions, and
propose several observational predictions for both the slowly varying and
transient corona/solar wind.

We present a study on the X-ray properties of the most-luminous quasars from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS). Quasars with high luminosity provide an opportunity to study the physics of the accretion disk around a supermassive black hole, and their X-ray properties are essential for understanding the emission mechanisms and the interaction between the disk and the surrounding environment.

Using the archival data from Chandra and XMM-Newton, we analyzed the X-ray spectra of 36 most-luminous quasars at z>2.5. We found that all the quasars in our sample have X-ray luminosities exceeding 10^45 ergs s^-1, with a peak at ~10^46 ergs s^-1. We also found that the majority of the quasars show a power-law spectrum, with a photon index ranging from 1.5 to 2.5.

Our study attempts to address the following questions: How does the accretion disk produce the high X-ray luminosity in the most-luminous quasars? What is the spectral shape of the X-ray emission? Are there any differences between the X-ray properties of the most-luminous quasars and those of less-luminous ones?

Our key methodology involved X-ray spectral fitting using XSPEC, a widely used X-ray spectral analysis package. Our results show that the X-ray properties of the most-luminous quasars are consistent with the standard accretion disk model, implying that the disk is the dominant source of X-ray emission. The power-law spectrum suggests that the Comptonization process is important in the X-ray production. Our findings provide important constraints on the physical processes in the accretion disk of the most-luminous quasars and give insights into the evolution of supermassive black holes in the early universe.

Admission control as a mechanism for providing QoS requires an accurate
description of the requested flow as well as already admitted flows. Since
802.11 WLAN capacity is shared between flows belonging to all stations,
admission control requires knowledge of all flows in the WLAN. Further,
estimation of the load-dependent WLAN capacity through analytical model
requires inputs about channel data rate, payload size and the number of
stations. These factors combined point to a centralized admission control
whereas for 802.11 DCF it is ideally performed in a distributed manner. The use
of measurements from the channel avoids explicit inputs about the state of the
channel described above. BUFFET, a model based measurement-assisted distributed
admission control scheme for DCF proposed in this paper relies on measurements
to derive model inputs and predict WLAN saturation, thereby maintaining average
delay within acceptable limits. Being measurement based, it adapts to a
combination of data rates and payload sizes, making it completely autonomous
and distributed. Performance analysis using OPNET simulations suggests that
BUFFET is able to ensure average delay under 7ms at a near-optimal throughput.

In this study, we investigate the hierarchical star-formation in M33 and provide a detailed analysis on the fundamental properties of the star-forming regions. The motivation for this research is derived from the need to understand the formation of stars, their evolution, and the role they play in the galaxy's overall structure. The problem this work attempts to solve is to identify how star-formation proceeds in the large-scale environment of M33. Our aim is to provide a comprehensive view of the physical properties of the star-forming regions, such as their sizes, masses, luminosities, and the rate at which stars are forming.

We used data from high-resolution observations of M33 obtained with the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) to generate a catalog of star-forming regions. We analyzed the catalog to identify the hierarchical structure of the star-forming regions and to estimate their properties. Our results show that the star-forming regions in M33 are organized in a hierarchical structure, with subregions forming embedded within larger-scale structures. The properties of the star-forming regions, such as their sizes, masses, and luminosities, increase as the hierarchy of the structure increases. We conclude that hierarchical star-formation in M33 plays a crucial role in shaping the overall structure of the galaxy and the formation of stars.

We present a novel model for exploring the Globular Cluster extreme anomalies. Our motivation for this research was drawn from the growing interest in understanding the behavior of stars in massive star clusters. These clusters are home to a large number of stars, all of which are expected to follow clear evolutionary paths. However, the presence of extreme anomalies in some of these clusters has so far defied explanation. Our work aims to fill this gap by developing a new mathematical model that takes into account the various factors that could contribute to these anomalies.

To build our model, we started by analyzing the observed properties of several globular clusters and investigating the factors that could lead to deviations from the expected evolution. We then conducted a series of simulations to test our model against known cases of extreme anomalies and observed its predictive power. Our model proved to be successful in reproducing the observed behavior in several clusters.

Our work represents a significant step towards understanding the extreme anomalies present in globular clusters. Our model can be extended to other clusters, providing a foundation for future studies in the field. Ultimately, our research aims to contribute to our understanding of stellar evolution and the formation of dense star clusters.

In this study, we investigate the accretion state of the Seyfert galaxy NGC 3783 using timing analysis. The motivation for this research originates from the fact that the accretion process is the primary energy source for the active galactic nuclei (AGN). Therefore, understanding the accretion state is essential in order to comprehend the AGN's physical properties.

We aim to solve the problem of accurately determining the accretion state of NGC 3783, which can be misinterpreted based on the emission properties of the AGN due to underlying complex physical processes. To achieve this, we analyze X-ray observations of NGC 3783 spanning over thirteen years. We implement Fourier analysis techniques to extract the periodic variability present in the X-ray light curve of the AGN.

Our results show a strong correlation between the X-ray variability and the accretion rate. This indicates that attribute was in its high accretion state during the observations. Moreover, the power density spectrum showed a monotonic decrease in the power-law index between 2004 and 2014, which is an indication of a significant change in the accretion structure of NGC 3783.

Overall, our analysis of the X-ray light curve of NGC 3783 provides valuable insights into the accretion state of the AGN. This study also demonstrates the significance of timing analysis, which provides unique information about the physical properties of AGNs.

In this research, we aim to explore the optimal investment strategy in the presence of an unbounded random endowment and utility-based pricing. Our motivation comes from the fact that the existing literature on investment management assumes the endowment to be deterministic and bounded. However, this assumption may not always hold, particularly in the case of investors dealing with alternative investments such as private equity and hedge funds, where endowments can be unbounded and uncertain.

Our key idea is to consider a dynamic investment strategy that maximizes the expected utility of the investor's wealth, subject to the endowment's uncertainty. We propose a new framework that incorporates the random endowment, along with investment opportunities and pricing models. We apply the theory of stochastic calculus to derive the optimal investment policy.

Our results show that the optimal investment strategy is a combination of two components, the myopic component, which is insensitive to the endowment's size and the strategic component that adapts to the endowment's randomness. We also find that the optimal policy involves rebalancing the portfolio frequently to maintain a suitable risk level.

Overall, our research contributes to the growing literature on dynamic investment strategies, providing insights into the optimal management of unbounded endowments for investors dealing with alternative investments. Our results have practical implications for investors and investment managers in developing investment strategies based on their tolerance for risk, return expectations, and other relevant criteria.

The search for elementary excitations with fractional quantum numbers is a
central challenge in modern condensed matter physics. We explore the
possibility in a realistic model for several materials, the spin-1/2 spatially
anisotropic frustrated Heisenberg antiferromagnet in two dimensions. By
restricting the Hilbert space to that expressed by exact eigenstates of the
Heisenberg chain, we derive an effective Schr\"odinger equation valid in the
weak interchain-coupling regime. The dynamical spin correlations from this
approach agree quantitatively with inelastic neutron measurements on the
triangular antiferromagnet Cs_2CuCl_4. The spectral features in such
antiferromagnets can be attributed to two types of excitations: descendents of
one-dimensional spinons of individual chains, and coherently propagating
"triplon" bound states of spinon pairs. We argue that triplons are generic
features of spatially anisotropic frustrated antiferromagnets, and arise
because the bound spinon pair lowers its kinetic energy by propagating between
chains.

In this study, we analyze the mass-to-light ratio of Ly-alpha emitters at redshifts z=5.7, 6.5, 7, and 8.8 and the implications of Ly-alpha surveys. Our primary aim is to address the critical issue of the formation and evolution of galaxies in the early universe. Using  lenses to magnify the light from galaxies, we analyze the shapes of galaxies and their offset from the dark matter halo. We find that the mass-to-light ratios of typical Ly-alpha galaxies at z=5.7 and 6.5 are higher than those of nearby galaxies, indicating that these early galaxies are less evolved. At redshifts z=7 and 8.8, however, the mass-to-light ratio is lower than expected, indicating that these galaxies may have already completed their star-forming process. We also show that the results of Ly-alpha surveys may be affected by galactic winds and ionizing radiation. This study provides insights into the formation and evolution of galaxies in the early universe and emphasizes the importance of multifaceted studies, including simulations and observations, to understand this complex process.

In this work, we propose a new method for generating optimal cache-oblivious mesh layouts that can greatly improve the performance of algorithms that operate on meshes. The motivation for this research is the fact that cache-oblivious algorithms can provide significant performance gains over traditional algorithms by minimizing the number of cache misses. However, achieving optimal performance requires careful management of the memory hierarchy, which can be difficult and time-consuming. Our approach leverages the properties of cache-oblivious algorithms to automatically optimize mesh layouts for maximum performance without the need for manual tuning.

To achieve this, we use a combination of graph-based representations of meshes and mathematical optimization techniques to find layouts that minimize the number of cache misses for a given memory hierarchy. We evaluate our approach using both synthetic and real-world meshes and show that it consistently outperforms existing approaches to cache-oblivious mesh layout generation. Our results demonstrate that our method can provide significant performance improvements for a variety of mesh-based algorithms, including finite element simulations, surface reconstructions, and ray-tracing applications.

Overall, our work represents an important step towards the development of more efficient and effective cache-oblivious algorithms for mesh-based computations. By automating the process of mesh layout generation, we believe that our approach can help researchers and practitioners to quickly and easily optimize their algorithms for specific hardware architectures, without sacrificing performance or efficiency.

AIMS: We investigate whether the morphology of bilateral supernova remnants
(BSNRs) observed in the radio band is determined mainly either by a non-uniform
interstellar medium (ISM) or by a non-uniform ambient magnetic field.
  METHODS: We perform 3-D MHD simulations of a spherical SNR shock propagating
through a magnetized ISM. Two cases of shock propagation are considered: 1)
through a gradient of ambient density with a uniform ambient magnetic field; 2)
through a homogeneous medium with a gradient of ambient magnetic field
strength. From the simulations, we synthesize the synchrotron radio emission,
making different assumptions about the details of acceleration and injection of
relativistic electrons.
  RESULTS: We find that asymmetric BSNRs are produced if the line-of-sight is
not aligned with the gradient of ambient plasma density or with the gradient of
ambient magnetic field strength. We derive useful parameters to quantify the
degree of asymmetry of the remnants that may provide a powerful diagnostic of
the microphysics of strong shock waves through the comparison between models
and observations.
  CONCLUSIONS: BSNRs with two radio limbs of different brightness can be
explained if a gradient of ambient density or, most likely, of ambient magnetic
field strength is perpendicular to the radio limbs. BSNRs with converging
similar radio arcs can be explained if the gradient runs between the two arcs.
